topics.title: Conventional Vehicles - Basics of Vehicle Performance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET742 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES AND RENEWABLE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Conventional Vehicles - Basics of Vehicle Performance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Conventional Vehicle?

A **Conventional Vehicle (CV)** — also called an **Internal Combustion Engine Vehicle (ICEV)** — is a road vehicle propelled by an **internal combustion engine** that converts the chemical energy of a fossil fuel (typically petrol/gasoline, diesel, or compressed natural gas) into mechanical energy through controlled combustion of the fuel–air mixture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A conventional vehicle is a vehicle whose prime mover is a reciprocating **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)** — either a Spark Ignition (SI) engine or a Compression Ignition (CI) engine — coupled to a mechanical transmission that delivers tractive power to the driving wheels through a clutch, gearbox, driveshaft, and final drive (differential).

### 1.2 The Three Major Subsystems of a Conventional Vehicle

| Subsystem | Core Function | Key Components |
|-----------|---------------|----------------|
| **Powertrain (Prime Mover)** | Convert chemical → mechanical energy | SI/CI engine, fuel tank, fuel injection |
| **Transmission (Drivetrain)** | Match engine speed/torque to wheel requirements | Clutch, gearbox (manual/auto), propeller shaft, differential, half-shafts |
| **Chassis & Body** | Support, house, and protect occupants & load | Frame, suspension, steering, brakes, wheels, tyres, body shell |

### 1.3 What is "Vehicle Performance"?

**Vehicle Performance** is the quantitative measure of how well a vehicle executes its primary mission under specified operating conditions. It is evaluated using four classical engineering metrics:

1. **Maximum Speed ($V_{max}$)** — the highest sustained speed the vehicle can attain on a level road.
2. **Acceleration Capability** — the rate at which the vehicle can increase its speed (0–60 mph, 0–100 km/h, time to cover a quarter mile).
3. **Gradeability** — the maximum slope (in % or degrees) the vehicle can climb at a given constant speed.
4. **Braking Performance** — the shortest stopping distance from a given speed, governed by tyre–road friction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Extended KTU Metrics:** In modern KTU evaluation, **fuel economy (km/litre or litres/100 km)** and **drive-cycle emissions (g CO₂/km)** are also considered performance indicators, as they are tightly coupled to engine–transmission matching.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The Cart and the Horse

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Picture:** Imagine a horse pulling a loaded cart up a hill.
> - The **horse** is the **engine** — limited in how hard (force) and how fast (rpm) it can pull.
> - The **reins and harness gears** are the **transmission** — they translate the horse's effort to the cart's wheels.
> - The **cart weight** is the **vehicle mass** — heavier carts need more force.
> - **Air pushing against the cart** is the **aerodynamic drag** — bigger at higher speeds.
> - **Wheel rubbing on the road** is the **rolling resistance** — always present.
> - **The hill slope** is the **grade** that resists motion.
> - **How fast the cart speeds up** is **acceleration**.
>
> The driver (you, the engineer) decides the **gear** to balance horse-effort with cart-speed. Vehicle performance = how cleverly the driver matches all these forces.

### 1.5 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- Acceleration due to gravity: **$g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$**
- Air density at sea level, 15 °C: **$\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$**
- Coefficient of rolling resistance (passenger car tyre on asphalt): **$f_r \approx 0.010 - 0.015$**
- Coefficient of aerodynamic drag (typical sedan): **$C_d \approx 0.30 - 0.40$**
- Frontal area of a typical sedan: **$A_f \approx 1.8 - 2.5 \text{ m}^2$**
- Slope conversion: $1\% \text{ grade} \iff \tan(\alpha) = 0.01 \iff \alpha \approx 0.57°$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Force Balance on a Moving Vehicle

When a vehicle moves at a steady speed $V$ on a road inclined at angle $\alpha$ to the horizontal and accelerates at rate $a$, the **tractive force $F_t$** delivered at the driving wheels must overcome four resistive forces:

$$
F_t \;=\; F_{rr} \;+\; F_{ad} \;+\; F_{hc} \;+\; F_{ma}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Result:** The tractive force at the wheel–road contact patch is the *net output* the vehicle needs to sustain motion. This single equation is the foundation of *every* performance calculation in Module 1.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Physical Meaning

**Step 1 — Rolling Resistance ($F_{rr}$):**
As the tyre rolls and deforms, energy is dissipated as heat in the rubber. The force opposing motion is approximately constant for small speeds and proportional to vehicle weight:
$$
F_{rr} \;=\; f_r \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos(\alpha) \;\approx\; f_r \cdot m \cdot g \;\; \text{(for small } \alpha\text{)}
$$
where $f_r$ is the **coefficient of rolling resistance** and $m$ is the vehicle mass.

**Step 2 — Aerodynamic Drag ($F_{ad}$):**
Air displaced by the moving vehicle exerts a pressure force opposite to motion. Drag grows with the **square of velocity**:
$$
F_{ad} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A_f \cdot V^2
$$
At $V = 0$ (standing start), $F_{ad} = 0$. At $V = 120$ km/h, drag dominates the resistive forces.

**Step 3 — Hill-Climbing (Grade) Force ($F_{hc}$):**
On a slope of angle $\alpha$, a component of the vehicle's weight opposes forward motion:
$$
F_{hc} \;=\; m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\alpha)
$$
For a slope expressed as percentage grade $G$ (in %), $\sin(\alpha) \approx \tan(\alpha) = G/100$ for $\alpha < 20°$.

**Step 4 — Acceleration (Inertial) Force ($F_{ma}$):**
Newton's second law for the translating mass plus equivalent rotational inertia of rotating parts (wheels, gears, engine, driveshaft):
$$
F_{ma} \;=\; m \cdot a \cdot \delta
$$
where $\delta$ is the **mass factor / rotational inertia factor** (typically $1.05$ for a passenger car, $1.10$–$1.30$ for trucks).

**Step 5 — Power at the Wheels:**
The power the engine must deliver to the wheels is:
$$
P_w \;=\; F_t \cdot V
$$
Engine output power is higher than $P_w$ by the transmission efficiency $\eta_{tr}$:
$$
P_{eng} \;=\; \dfrac{F_t \cdot V}{\eta_{tr}}
$$

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table — it covers > 80% of KTU Module 1 numerical questions.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes / Typical Values |
|---|----------|---------|-------|------------------------|
| 1 | Rolling resistance | $F_{rr} = f_r \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos\alpha$ | N | $f_r \approx 0.010$–$0.015$ |
| 2 | Aerodynamic drag | $F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f V^2$ | N | $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$ |
| 3 | Hill-climbing force | $F_{hc} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\alpha$ | N | Use $\sin\alpha \approx G/100$ for small slopes |
| 4 | Acceleration force | $F_{ma} = m \cdot a \cdot \delta$ | N | $\delta \approx 1.05$–$1.30$ |
| 5 | Tractive force | $F_t = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{ma}$ | N | **Master equation** |
| 6 | Power at wheels | $P_w = F_t \cdot V$ | W | Convert km/h → m/s: divide by 3.6 |
| 7 | Engine power | $P_{eng} = F_t V / \eta_{tr}$ | W | $\eta_{tr} \approx 0.85$–$0.95$ |
| 8 | Max. speed (level) | $F_t(V_{max}) = F_{rr} + F_{ad}$ | m/s | $a = 0$, $\alpha = 0$ |
| 9 | Max. grade (steady) | $\sin\alpha = (F_t - F_{rr} - F_{ad})/(m g)$ | — | At constant $V$ |
| 10 | Max. acceleration (level) | $a = (F_t - F_{rr} - F_{ad})/(m\delta)$ | m/s² | At $V$ on level road |
| 11 | Grade % to angle | $G\% \Rightarrow \alpha = \arctan(G/100)$ | rad/° | — |
| 12 | Speed conversion | $V_{\text{m/s}} = V_{\text{km/h}} / 3.6$ | m/s | Always convert! |
| 13 | Stopping distance | $d_s = V^2 / (2 \mu g)$ | m | $\mu$ = tyre–road friction |
| 14 | Wheel force vs torque | $F_t = T_w / r_w$ | N | $T_w$ = wheel torque, $r_w$ = tyre radius |
| 15 | Engine-to-wheel speed | $V = 2\pi N_e r_w / (60 \cdot i_g \cdot i_0)$ | m/s | $i_g$ = gear ratio, $i_0$ = final drive |

### 2.4 Engineering Significance

> [!TIP]
> - The **square-law** behaviour of $F_{ad}$ explains why doubling speed requires **four times** the power to overcome drag alone — a fundamental reason highway fuel economy is poor.
> - The linear $F_{rr}$ and $F_{hc}$ terms dominate at low speeds and on slopes — a critical insight for **EVs**, where low-speed torque characteristics of electric motors become a competitive advantage.
> - The role of the **transmission** is to keep the engine operating near its optimal (BSFC) point regardless of $V$ and $a$ — this is the very motivation behind the EV's **single-speed gearbox** in many cases.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Speed on a Level Road

**Problem Setup:** Find the highest speed $V_{max}$ at which the vehicle can cruise on a flat road with no acceleration.

**Step 1 — Start from the master force equation.** Set $\alpha = 0$ and $a = 0$:
$$
F_t \;=\; F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{ma} \;\;\longrightarrow\;\; F_t \;=\; F_{rr} + F_{ad}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the explicit forms.**
$$
F_t(V) \;=\; f_r \, m \, g \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2} \, \rho \, C_d \, A_f \, V^2
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the constraint.** At maximum speed, the engine (limited by its rated power $P_{max}$) delivers its peak wheel force at that velocity. Equivalently, the engine's torque at the maximum rated speed gives:
$$
F_t(V_{max}) \;=\; \dfrac{P_{max}}{V_{max}} \cdot \eta_{tr}
$$

**Step 4 — Equate and solve for $V_{max}$.**
$$
\dfrac{P_{max} \cdot \eta_{tr}}{V_{max}} \;=\; f_r \, m \, g \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2} \, \rho \, C_d \, A_f \, V_{max}^2
$$

Multiplying through by $V_{max}$:
$$
P_{max} \cdot \eta_{tr} \;=\; f_r \, m \, g \, V_{max} \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2} \, \rho \, C_d \, A_f \, V_{max}^3
$$

This is a **cubic equation** in $V_{max}$, usually solved by:
- **Graphical method:** plot $P_{wheel}(V) = (F_{rr} + F_{ad})V$ and intersect with $P_{max} \eta_{tr}$ horizontal line.
- **Iterative method** (Newton–Raphson) for high precision.

**Step 5 — Practical shortcut for KTU:** If $F_{rr} \ll F_{ad}$ at $V_{max}$ (true for most modern cars above ~100 km/h), neglect the linear term:
$$
V_{max} \;\approx\; \left(\dfrac{2 \, P_{max} \, \eta_{tr}}{\rho \, C_d \, A_f}\right)^{1/3}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap:** Students often forget the cubic root and write $V_{max} = (2P/\rho C_d A_f)^{1/2}$ — that is dimensionally wrong and conceptually a drag-velocity (terminal velocity) expression without the rolling term. Always retain both terms unless explicitly told to ignore $F_{rr}$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Gradeability at Steady Speed

**Step 1 — Steady speed + desired velocity $V_0$.** Set $a = 0$; choose the speed of interest; unknowns are $F_t$ and $\alpha$.

**Step 2 — Master equation reduces to:**
$$
F_t(V_0) \;=\; f_r \, m \, g \, \cos\alpha \;+\; m \, g \, \sin\alpha \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f V_0^2
$$

**Step 3 — For small slopes**, $\cos\alpha \approx 1$. Rearrange:
$$
F_t(V_0) - f_r \, m \, g - \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f V_0^2 \;=\; m \, g \, \sin\alpha
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\alpha$:**
$$
\sin\alpha \;=\; \dfrac{F_t(V_0) - f_r m g - \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f V_0^2}{m \, g}
$$

Equivalently, in **percent grade**:
$$
G_{\%} \;=\; 100 \cdot \dfrac{F_t(V_0) - f_r m g - \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f V_0^2}{m \, g}
$$

**Step 5 — Practical KTU formula (with rolling + drag often negligible at low speed $V_0$):**
$$
G_{\%} \;\approx\; \dfrac{100 \cdot (F_t - f_r m g)}{m g} \;=\; \dfrac{100 (F_t - f_r m g)}{m g}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering insight:** A typical passenger car is rated for **30% grade (≈ 16.7°)** at low speed (say 25 km/h). At 100 km/h on the same slope, the available wheel force $F_t = P \eta_{tr}/V$ is much smaller — gradeability drops sharply with speed. This is why trucks use **low gears** to climb steep hills.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Maximum Acceleration (Level Road)

**Step 1 — Set $\alpha = 0$, maximise $a$.**
$$
m \, a \, \delta \;=\; F_t(V) - f_r \, m \, g - \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f V^2
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $a$:**
$$
a_{max}(V) \;=\; \dfrac{F_t(V) - f_r m g - \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f V^2}{m \, \delta}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $F_t(V) = T_w(V)/r_w$**, where $T_w$ is the wheel torque. For a manual-transmission ICE car, the engine torque curve $T_e(N_e)$ is shaped by the gearbox, so:
$$
a_{max}(V) \;=\; \dfrac{T_e(N_e(V)) \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_{tr}/r_w - f_r m g - \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f V^2}{m \delta}
$$

where $N_e(V) = V \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot 60/(2\pi r_w)$ is the engine rpm corresponding to vehicle speed $V$ in the chosen gear.

**Step 4 — The "zero-to-sixty" time** is found by integrating the inverse of acceleration:
$$
t_{0 \to V_f} \;=\; \int_{0}^{V_f} \dfrac{m \, \delta}{F_t(V) - f_r m g - \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f V^2} \; dV
$$

For KTU exams, the closed-form with constant $F_t$ gives:
$$
t \;=\; \dfrac{m \delta}{k_2} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{k_1 + k_2 V_f}{k_1}\right)
$$
where $k_1 = f_r m g$ and $k_2 = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f$ (drag coefficient).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Which Resistive Force Dominates When?

> [!NOTE]
> **This is a high-yield KTU "compare & contrast" matrix — frequently asked as a 3-mark question.**

| Speed Range | Dominant Resistance | % Contribution (typical) | Engineering Consequence |
|-------------|--------------------|--------------------------|-------------------------|
| 0 – 10 km/h (creep / launch) | $F_{ma}$ (inertia) | ~ 80 – 90 % | Clutch and 1st gear sized for high torque |
| 10 – 50 km/h (city) | $F_{rr} + F_{ma}$ | ~ 60 – 70 % $F_{rr}$ | Tall gearing helps economy |
| 50 – 100 km/h (rural / highway) | $F_{ad}$ growing, $F_{rr}$ | Drag rises rapidly | 5th / 6th gear essential |
| > 100 km/h (highway) | $F_{ad}$ | ~ 70 – 80 % | $V_{max}$ limited by $P_{max}$ vs cubic drag |
| Hill climb (low V, high α) | $F_{hc}$ | Up to 100 % | Transmission downshift essential |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Architecture of a Conventional Vehicle Powertrain

The following block diagram maps the **energy-flow architecture** of a typical rear-wheel-drive ICE car, showing the chain of components from fuel tank to road contact.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fuel Tank] --> B[ICE Engine]
    B --> C[Clutch]
    C --> D[Gearbox]
    D --> E[Propeller Shaft]
    E --> F[Differential Final Drive]
    F --> G[Half Shafts]
    G --> H[Drive Wheels]
    H --> I((Road Contact Patch))
    J[Exhaust] --- B
    K[Engine Control Unit ECU] --- B
    L[12V Battery] --- K
    H <--> M[Brakes Disc or Drum]
    H <--> N[Suspension and Tyres]
    subgraph Losses
        O[Heat Loss 30%]
        P[Cooling Loss 10%]
        Q[Exhaust Loss 35%]
    end
    B -.-> O
    B -.-> P
    B -.-> Q
```

> [!TIP]
> **Read this diagram as "fuel → motion":** Fuel → Engine → Clutch → Gearbox → Prop-shaft → Differential → Half-shafts → Wheels → Road. Each arrow represents a stage where energy is either transmitted (good) or lost (bad). The dashed lines mark losses — a typical ICE converts only **~ 20–30 %** of fuel energy into actual wheel motion.

### 4.2 Force Diagram on a Vehicle on a Slope

The free-body-style block diagram below identifies every force acting on a moving vehicle on an inclined road.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Forces
        V[Vehicle Mass m moving at speed V on slope alpha]
        V --> F1[Tractive Force F subscript t at contact patch]
        V --> F2[Rolling Resistance F subscript rr opposes motion]
        V --> F3[Aerodynamic Drag F subscript ad opposes motion]
        V --> F4[Hill Climbing Force F subscript hc opposes motion]
        V --> F5[Acceleration Inertial Force F subscript ma opposes motion]
        V --> F6[Weight mg vertical downward]
        V --> F7[Normal Reaction N perpendicular to road]
    end
    subgraph Equations
        Eq1[F subscript t = F subscript rr + F subscript ad + F subscript hc + F subscript ma]
        Eq2[F subscript rr = f subscript r times m g cos alpha]
        Eq3[F subscript ad equals 1 over 2 times rho C subscript d A subscript f V squared]
        Eq4[F subscript hc = m g sin alpha]
        Eq5[F subscript ma = m a delta]
        Eq6[P subscript wheel = F subscript t times V]
    end
```

### 4.3 Performance Trade-off Map — Speed, Power, Drag

A schematic that shows why $V_{max}$ is bounded by the engine power curve intersecting the resistive power curve.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Power_Axis
        A1[At V = 30 km per h, rolling resistance dominates] --> A2[At V = 90 km per h, rolling equals drag]
        A2 --> A3[At V = 120 km per h, drag dominates]
        A3 --> A4[At V equals V subscript max, engine power equals resistive power]
    end
    subgraph Vehicle_Response
        R1[Low gear high torque high acceleration] --> R2[Mid gear balanced performance]
        R2 --> R3[Top gear low torque high speed]
        R3 --> R4[Engine at rated power engine rpm near redline]
    end
    A4 --- R4
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **What the diagram teaches:** The transmission's job is to keep the engine on the *flat top* of its power curve. As the vehicle speeds up, the resistance curve (dominated by $V^2$ drag) climbs steeply; the engine curve is nearly flat — they must intersect at $V_{max}$, beyond which no tractive effort remains for further acceleration.

### 4.4 Drive Cycle — Time-Speed Profile of a Standard Test

The standard **FTP-75 (Federal Test Procedure)** drive cycle is the canonical reference for fuel-economy and emission certification.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Phase 1 Cold Start 0 to 505 s] --> P2[Phase 2 Idle plus urban 505 to 1372 s]
    P2 --> P3[Phase 3 Hot Start 1877 to 2376 s]
    P3 --> P4[Phase 4 Cruise plus high speed 2376 to end]
    P1 -.- S1[Max speed 91 km per h Avg speed 41 km per h]
    P2 -.- S2[Max speed 56 km per h Avg speed 25 km per h]
    P3 -.- S3[Max speed 56 km per h Avg speed 25 km per h]
    P4 -.- S4[Max speed 97 km per h Avg speed 80 km per h]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU relevance:** Drive cycles are *the* benchmark against which an EV's range and a conventional vehicle's fuel economy are reported. The total distance covered (~ 17.77 km) and the energy expended over the cycle yield **km/litre (ICE)** or **kWh/100 km (EV)** ratings.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the term "tractive force" in a conventional vehicle. List the four resistive forces it must overcome during motion.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The **tractive force ($F_t$)** is the total longitudinal force delivered to the road by the driving wheels of a vehicle, which propels the vehicle forward.
>
> The four resistive forces that $F_t$ must overcome are:
> 1. **Rolling resistance ($F_{rr}$)** — friction and hysteresis in the tyre.
> 2. **Aerodynamic drag ($F_{ad}$)** — air resistance on the vehicle body.
> 3. **Hill-climbing (grade) force ($F_{hc}$)** — component of weight along an inclined road.
> 4. **Acceleration (inertial) force ($F_{ma}$)** — force needed to overcome inertia of the vehicle and rotating components.
>
> The master equation is:
> $$
> F_t \;=\; F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{ma}
> $$
> *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for listing forces, 1 mark for the master equation.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**With the help of an expression, explain the "aerodynamic drag force" acting on a moving vehicle. State how it varies with velocity.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The **aerodynamic drag force** is the resistance offered by the air to the forward motion of a vehicle. It is given by:
> $$
> F_{ad} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} \, \rho \, C_d \, A_f \, V^2
> $$
> where:
> - $\rho$ = air density ($1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$ at sea level, **15 °C**)
> - $C_d$ = coefficient of drag (dimensionless)
> - $A_f$ = frontal area of the vehicle in m²
> - $V$ = vehicle speed in m/s
>
> **Variation:** $F_{ad}$ varies **as the square of the velocity**. Hence, doubling the speed quadruples the drag force, and the power required to overcome drag increases with the *cube* of speed.
> *(1 mark expression, 1 mark nomenclature, 1 mark velocity dependence.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module Internal Choice:** In KTU ESE, every 14-mark question is offered with an **internal choice (A or B)**. Both questions below are written to the *exact same* mark distribution. You may attempt either.

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper, Module 1]**

> A passenger car has the following data:
> - Mass $m = 1500$ kg
> - Frontal area $A_f = 2.2$ m²
> - Drag coefficient $C_d = 0.35$
> - Coefficient of rolling resistance $f_r = 0.015$
> - Tyre radius $r_w = 0.32$ m
> - Mass factor $\delta = 1.05$
> - Air density $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³
> - Maximum engine power $P_{max} = 80$ kW @ 5500 rpm
> - Transmission efficiency $\eta_{tr} = 0.90$
> - Total gear reduction (top gear) $i_g \cdot i_0 = 3.2$
>
> **(a)** Compute the **maximum speed** of the car on a level road. (7 marks)
> **(b)** Determine the **maximum grade (in %)** the car can climb at a steady speed of **40 km/h** in top gear. (7 marks)

#### **Solution (a) — Maximum Speed**

**Step 1 — Convert units.**
$$
P_{max} = 80{,}000 \text{ W}, \quad V \text{ in m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — At $V_{max}$ on level road with $a = 0$:**
$$
F_t(V_{max}) \;=\; f_r m g + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f V_{max}^2
$$

Compute constants:
$$
f_r m g = 0.015 \times 1500 \times 9.81 = 220.725 \text{ N}
$$
$$
\tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.35 \times 2.2 = 0.4716 \text{ N·s}^2/\text{m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Express wheel force from engine power:**
$$
F_t(V_{max}) \;=\; \dfrac{P_{max} \cdot \eta_{tr}}{V_{max}} \;=\; \dfrac{80{,}000 \times 0.90}{V_{max}} \;=\; \dfrac{72{,}000}{V_{max}} \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4 — Equate and form cubic:**
$$
\dfrac{72{,}000}{V_{max}} \;=\; 220.725 + 0.4716 \, V_{max}^2
$$
$$
72{,}000 \;=\; 220.725 \, V_{max} + 0.4716 \, V_{max}^3
$$

**Step 5 — Solve iteratively** (try $V_{max} = 50$ m/s):
$$
\text{RHS} = 220.725(50) + 0.4716(125{,}000) = 11{,}036 + 58{,}950 = 69{,}986 \text{ W} \;\;(\text{slightly less})
$$
Try $V_{max} = 50.5$ m/s:
$$
\text{RHS} = 220.725(50.5) + 0.4716(128{,}787) = 11{,}147 + 60{,}736 = 71{,}883 \text{ W}
$$
Try $V_{max} = 50.6$ m/s:
$$
\text{RHS} = 11{,}169 + 61{,}130 = 72{,}299 \text{ W} \;\;(\text{very close})
$$

**Step 6 — Final answer:**
$$
V_{max} \approx 50.6 \text{ m/s} \approx 182 \text{ km/h}
$$

*(1 mark — equation setup; 2 marks — constants; 2 marks — cubic formation; 2 marks — numerical answer with units.)*

---

#### **Solution (b) — Maximum Grade at 40 km/h**

**Step 1 — Convert speed.**
$$
V_0 = 40 / 3.6 = 11.11 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Wheel force available at $V_0$ in top gear:**
$$
F_t(V_0) = \dfrac{P_{max} \cdot \eta_{tr}}{V_0} = \dfrac{72{,}000}{11.11} = 6480 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 — Resistive forces at $V_0$ on level ground:**
$$
F_{rr} = 220.725 \text{ N}, \quad F_{ad} = 0.4716 \times (11.11)^2 = 0.4716 \times 123.46 = 58.2 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4 — Net force available for hill climbing:**
$$
F_{hc} = F_t - F_{rr} - F_{ad} = 6480 - 220.7 - 58.2 = 6201.1 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for grade:**
$$
\sin\alpha = \dfrac{F_{hc}}{m g} = \dfrac{6201.1}{1500 \times 9.81} = \dfrac{6201.1}{14{,}715} = 0.4214
$$
$$
\alpha = \arcsin(0.4214) = 24.9°
$$

**Step 6 — Express as percentage grade:**
$$
G_{\%} = 100 \cdot \tan\alpha = 100 \cdot 0.4648 = 46.5\% \;\;(\text{or directly } G = 100 \sin\alpha = 42.1\% \text{ for small } \alpha)
$$

> **Final answer:** $G_{\max} \approx 42\%$ at 40 km/h. (Note: realistic value, since at low speed almost all of the engine's force budget is available to climb.)

*(1 mark force calculation; 2 marks resistive forces; 2 marks net force; 2 marks grade solution.)*

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper, Module 1]**

> A 1200 kg passenger car with $C_d A_f = 0.77$ m², $f_r = 0.013$, $\delta = 1.05$, and engine rated power 65 kW travels on a level road. Air density $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $\eta_{tr} = 0.90$.
>
> **(a)** Derive an expression for the **maximum acceleration** of the car as a function of speed. Hence compute the maximum acceleration at $V = 60$ km/h. (7 marks)
> **(b)** If the car brakes from 90 km/h with a tyre–road friction coefficient $\mu = 0.75$, calculate the **stopping distance** and **stopping time**. Compare your result with the free-rolling coast-down time. (7 marks)

#### **Solution (a) — Maximum Acceleration**

**Step 1 — Wheel force at velocity $V$:**
$$
F_t(V) = \dfrac{P \eta_{tr}}{V} = \dfrac{65{,}000 \times 0.90}{V} = \dfrac{58{,}500}{V} \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2 — Resistive forces:**
$$
F_{rr} = f_r m g = 0.013 \times 1200 \times 9.81 = 153.04 \text{ N}
$$
$$
F_{ad} = 0.5 \rho C_d A_f V^2 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.77 \times V^2 = 0.4716 \cdot V^2 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 — Acceleration expression:**
$$
a_{max}(V) = \dfrac{F_t - F_{rr} - F_{ad}}{m \delta} = \dfrac{58{,}500/V - 153.04 - 0.4716 V^2}{1200 \times 1.05}
$$

Simplifying the denominator: $m\delta = 1260$ kg.

$$
\boxed{\,a_{max}(V) = \dfrac{46.43/V - 0.1214 - 0.000374 V^2}{1} \; \text{m/s}^2\,}
$$
(where we have divided numerator and denominator through to make it explicit; or in compact form:)
$$
a_{max}(V) = \dfrac{58{,}500 - 153.04 V - 0.4716 V^3}{1260 \cdot V} \;\; \text{m/s}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate at $V = 60$ km/h = 16.67 m/s:**
$$
\text{Numerator} = 58{,}500 - 153.04(16.67) - 0.4716(16.67)^3
$$
$$
= 58{,}500 - 2550.9 - 0.4716 \times 4630.2 = 58{,}500 - 2550.9 - 2183.6 = 53{,}765.5
$$
$$
a_{max} = \dfrac{53{,}765.5}{1260 \times 16.67} = \dfrac{53{,}765.5}{21{,}004.2} = 2.56 \text{ m/s}^2
$$

> **Final answer:** $a_{max}(60 \text{ km/h}) \approx 2.56 \text{ m/s}^2$ (≈ 0.26 g)

*(1 mark expression; 2 marks constants; 2 marks substitution; 2 marks numerical answer.)*

---

#### **Solution (b) — Stopping Distance and Time**

**Step 1 — Convert speed:** $V_0 = 90/3.6 = 25$ m/s.

**Step 2 — Braking force on level road:**
$$
F_b = \mu m g = 0.75 \times 1200 \times 9.81 = 8829 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 — Deceleration:**
$$
a_b = \dfrac{F_b}{m} = \mu g = 0.75 \times 9.81 = 7.36 \text{ m/s}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Stopping distance (kinematic):**
$$
d_s = \dfrac{V_0^2}{2 a_b} = \dfrac{25^2}{2 \times 7.36} = \dfrac{625}{14.72} = 42.46 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5 — Stopping time:**
$$
t_s = \dfrac{V_0}{a_b} = \dfrac{25}{7.36} = 3.40 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 6 — Free-rolling coast-down comparison** (no braking, only rolling + drag):
At low speed, $F_{ad} \ll F_{rr}$ initially, so coast deceleration $\approx f_r g = 0.013 \times 9.81 = 0.128$ m/s².
$$
d_{coast} \approx \dfrac{V_0^2}{2 \times 0.128} = \dfrac{625}{0.256} = 2441 \text{ m}
$$
$$
t_{coast} \approx \dfrac{25}{0.128} = 195 \text{ s} \approx 3.25 \text{ min}
$$

> **Conclusion:** Braking gives ~ 42 m vs ~ 2.4 km coast-down — a 57× reduction in stopping distance. The active brakes are essential for safety.

*(1 mark force; 2 marks deceleration; 2 marks distance & time; 2 marks coast comparison.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 1 Performance Questions:**
>
> 1. **Unit mismatch (–2 marks typical):** Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s. Always check: $V$ in force-power formulas must be in **m/s**, mass in **kg**, power in **W**. The conversion factor $1 \text{ km/h} = 1/3.6 \text{ m/s}$ is non-negotiable.
>
> 2. **Missing the $\delta$ factor (–1 mark):** When asked for acceleration, students often write $a = F_t/m$ and ignore the rotational inertia factor. KTU expects $a = F_t/(m\delta)$.
>
> 3. **Forgetting transmission efficiency (–1 mark):** If $P_{max}$ is the *engine* output, the *wheel* power is $P_{wheel} = P_{max} \cdot \eta_{tr}$. If the question gives *wheel* power directly, do NOT multiply again.
>
> 4. **Confusing $F_{rr}$ and $F_{hc}$ (–1 mark):** $F_{rr}$ is along the road surface; $F_{hc}$ is along the road direction on a slope. They are perpendicular to each other in a strict vector sense, but along the line of motion they both oppose $F_t$.
>
> 5. **Inverting max-speed derivation (–2 marks):** Several students write $V_{max} = (2P/\rho C_d A_f)^{1/2}$ — this is **wrong** (units break). The correct expression without rolling resistance is $V_{max} = (2P/(\rho C_d A_f))^{1/3}$ — the **cubic** root.
>
> 6. **Drawing the wrong force diagram (–1 mark):** A common sketch shows $F_t$ pointing *backward*. The tractive force is the *forward* push from the engine on the road; the road reaction on the tyre is what drives the car.
>
> 7. **Sign convention errors on slopes (–1 mark):** When grade is given as a percentage $G$, remember $\sin\alpha \approx G/100$ only for *small* slopes. For $G > 20\%$, use the exact $\sin\alpha = G/\sqrt{100^2 + G^2}$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Conventional Vehicles & Basics of Vehicle Performance**

- [ ] **Conventional vehicle** = vehicle whose prime mover is an **ICE** (SI or CI), with a multi-speed **mechanical transmission** delivering power via **clutch → gearbox → driveshaft → differential → wheels**.
- [ ] The **master force equation** is $F_t = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{ma}$ — learn it cold.
- [ ] **Rolling resistance** is *linear* in $m g$: $F_{rr} = f_r m g \cos\alpha$. Typical $f_r = 0.010$–$0.015$.
- [ ] **Aerodynamic drag** is *quadratic* in $V$: $F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f V^2$. Doubling $V$ quadruples $F_{ad}$ and **8-multiplies** the power needed to overcome drag.
- [ ] **Hill-climbing force** is $F_{hc} = m g \sin\alpha$; a $10\%$ grade means $\sin\alpha \approx 0.10$, i.e. $\alpha \approx 5.71°$.
- [ ] **Acceleration force** is $F_{ma} = m a \delta$ with $\delta \in [1.05, 1.30]$ accounting for rotating parts.
- [ ] **Power at wheels** $P_w = F_t V$ in **watts** requires $V$ in **m/s**.
- [ ] **Maximum speed** comes from the cubic equation $P_{max}\eta_{tr} = f_r m g V + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f V^3$.
- [ ] **Maximum grade** at steady $V_0$: $\sin\alpha = (F_t - f_r m g - \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f V_0^2)/(m g)$.
- [ ] **Maximum acceleration**: $a = (F_t - f_r m g - \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f V^2)/(m\delta)$.
- [ ] **Stopping distance** $d_s = V_0^2 / (2 \mu g)$ — depends *only* on friction and speed, not on vehicle mass.
- [ ] **Engine efficiency** is only 20–30 % at the wheel — most fuel energy is lost as heat, exhaust, and cooling.
- [ ] **Transmission role**: keep the engine near its optimal BSFC point over a wide speed range.
- [ ] **Drive cycles** (FTP-75, NEDC, WLTP) provide the *standardised* speed-time profile against which fuel economy and emissions are reported.
- [ ] The **rotational inertia factor** $\delta$ in $F_{ma}$ captures wheels, gears, driveshaft, and engine crank inertia.
- [ ] The **mass factor** (also called "equivalent test mass") is the foundation of the *WLTP* and *EPA* test procedures.
- [ ] At **low speed**, $F_{rr}$ dominates; at **high speed**, $F_{ad}$ dominates — choose your transmission gear accordingly.
- [ ] For a *grade of $G$ percent*, $\sin\alpha = G/100$ is a valid approximation **only if** $G \lesssim 20$ — use exact form for steep slopes.
- [ ] The **tyre radius** $r_w$ converts engine torque to wheel force: $F_t = T_{wheel}/r_w = T_e i_g i_0 \eta_{tr}/r_w$.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Mantra for Module 1:** *"Engine produces torque and rpm. Transmission multiplies torque. Wheels convert torque to force. Force overcomes resistance. Force times speed equals power. Maximum speed comes when power equals resistive power. Maximum grade and acceleration are surplus-force problems."* Master this chain and you master Module 1.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vehicle Power Source Characterization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET742 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES AND RENEWABLE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Vehicle Power Source Characterization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Vehicle Power Source Characterization** is the systematic, quantitative process of determining the electrical, thermal, electrochemical, and dynamic performance parameters of an energy storage/generation device (battery, fuel cell, ultracapacitor, or hybrid system) under controlled operating conditions that mimic real-world automotive duty cycles. It establishes the relationship between the **State of Charge (SOC)**, **State of Health (SOH)**, terminal voltage, current demand, temperature, and usable energy/power envelopes that an Electric Vehicle (EV) powertrain can reliably draw.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Mandate (2024 Scheme):** This topic forms the *foundation* for Module 1 and directly underpins Battery Management Systems (BMS), drivetrain sizing (covered in Module 2), and range estimation (Module 3). Expect direct 3-mark definition-type questions and 7-mark analytical sub-questions.

The discipline is governed by standardized testing protocols, most notably the **USABC (United States Advanced Battery Consortium)** procedures and the **FreedomCAR 42V Test Manual**, which define repeatable pulse sequences for power capability assessment.

## 1.2 Key Characterization Parameters (The "Vital Signs" of a Power Source)

A power source is never defined by a single number. KTU examiners test whether you know the *complete* parameter matrix:

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Physical Meaning |
|---|-----------|--------|---------------|------------------|
| 1 | Specific Energy | $E_s$ | **Wh/kg** | Energy stored per unit mass |
| 2 | Energy Density | $E_v$ | **Wh/L** | Energy stored per unit volume |
| 3 | Specific Power | $P_s$ | **W/kg** | Peak deliverable power per unit mass |
| 4 | Power Density | $P_v$ | **W/L** | Peak deliverable power per unit volume |
| 5 | State of Charge | $SOC$ | **% (0–100)** | Remaining usable charge |
| 6 | State of Health | $SOH$ | **% (0–100)** | Current max capacity vs rated |
| 7 | Depth of Discharge | $DOD$ | **% (0–100)** | Fraction of capacity extracted |
| 8 | Internal Resistance | $R_i$ | **Ω (milli-Ω)** | Parasitic loss element |
| 9 | Coulombic Efficiency | $\eta_c$ | **%** | Charge out ÷ Charge in |
| 10 | Round-Trip Efficiency | $\eta_{rt}$ | **%** | Energy out ÷ Energy in |
| 11 | Cycle Life | $N_c$ | **cycles** | Until 80% of original capacity |
| 12 | Self-Discharge Rate | $R_{sd}$ | **%/month** | Charge loss at rest |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Fuel Gauge vs Health Report" Metaphor

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Human Body Endurance Test**
> Imagine you are a doctor assessing a marathon runner before a race. You wouldn't just measure height and weight. You would check:
> - **Heart rate response under stress** (= power capability)
> - **Stamina over long duration** (= specific energy)
> - **Lung capacity** (= energy density)
> - **Recovery time between sprints** (= internal resistance, pulse response)
> - **Age-related fitness** (= State of Health)
> - **Fuel level right now** (= State of Charge)
>
> Similarly, a vehicle power source must be characterized across *all* these dimensions because an EV powertrain design requires a **multi-dimensional trade-off**, not just "which battery stores the most energy."

## 1.4 The Three Universal Performance Axes

Every power source can be plotted on three fundamental axes:

1. **Energy Axis (Stamina)** — How far can the vehicle go? → Specific Energy
2. **Power Axis (Acceleration)** — How fast can it accelerate/regen? → Specific Power
3. **Lifespan Axis (Durability)** — How long will it last? → Cycle Life

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Recall:** The fundamental design tension in EV energy storage is the **Ragone Trade-off** — devices with high specific energy (fuel cells, Li-ion) typically have low specific power, while devices with high specific power (ultracapacitors) have low specific energy. Hybrid energy storage systems are the engineering answer to this trade-off.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Characterization Framework — Six Logical Steps

To characterize a power source, KTU board examiners expect a structured methodology. The accepted industry-academic flow is:

**Step 1 — Capacity Test (Static)**
Discharge the cell at a constant current (typically **C/3** or **1C** rate, where C = rated capacity in Ah) from 100% SOC to manufacturer-defined cutoff voltage. Integrate current over time to obtain the **actual capacity** $Q_{actual}$ in Ah.

**Step 2 — HPPC Test (Dynamic — USABC Standard)**
Apply a 10-second discharge pulse followed by a 10-second regen pulse at predefined SOC checkpoints (typically every 10% from 90% → 10%). This yields the **DC internal resistance** and **power capability** at each SOC.

**Step 3 — Constant Power Test**
Discharge at a fixed power level until cutoff voltage. Plots the voltage-time curve, enabling energy extraction efficiency analysis.

**Step 4 — Self-Discharge Test**
Rest the fully charged cell for a defined period (28 days standard) and measure the capacity loss.

**Step 5 — Cycle Life Test**
Repeatedly charge-discharge at a defined Depth of Discharge (DOD) until capacity falls below 80% of original (EOL criterion).

**Step 6 — Ragone Plot Construction**
Plot Specific Power (W/kg) vs Specific Energy (Wh/kg) on log-log axes to visualize the device's energy-power trade-off envelope.

## 2.2 Why Each Step Matters (The Engineering "Why")

- **Step 1** establishes the *baseline truth* — without knowing true capacity, all other metrics are scaled incorrectly.
- **Step 2** reveals *transient response* — critical for regenerative braking, which demands short high-power bursts.
- **Step 3** mimics *steady-state driving* (cruise mode) and gives the usable energy envelope.
- **Step 4** matters because EVs can sit parked — parasitic loss directly reduces range.
- **Step 5** determines *warranty economics* — battery replacement cost is the dominant TCO factor.
- **Step 6** allows *multi-source comparison* and *hybrid system design*.

## 2.3 The Open-Circuit Voltage & Internal Resistance Model

A battery can be represented by a **simple equivalent circuit**:

$$V_{terminal} = V_{oc}(SOC, T) - I \cdot R_i(SOC, T, I)$$

Where:
- $V_{oc}$ = Open-circuit voltage (function of SOC and temperature)
- $I$ = Discharge current (positive)
- $R_i$ = Internal resistance (non-linear, function of SOC, T, current direction)
- $T$ = Cell temperature

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind the Sign Convention:** During discharge, $V_{terminal} < V_{oc}$ (voltage drop across $R_i$). During charging/regen, the polarity reverses, and $V_{terminal} > V_{oc}$. This asymmetry is precisely why BMS must prevent over-voltage during regenerative braking — a high current spike into a near-full cell causes terminal voltage to spike above safe limits.

## 2.4 State of Charge (SOC) — The Master Variable

$$SOC(t) = SOC(t_0) - \frac{1}{Q_{rated}} \int_{t_0}^{t} \eta_c \cdot I(\tau) \, d\tau$$

Where:
- $Q_{rated}$ = Manufacturer's rated capacity (Ah)
- $I(\tau)$ = Instantaneous current (A)
- $\eta_c$ = Coulombic efficiency

For a *constant current* discharge at rate $I_0$:

$$SOC(t) = SOC_0 - \frac{I_0 \cdot t}{Q_{rated} \cdot 3600} \quad \text{[converting to consistent time base]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on DOD-SOC Relationship:** $DOD = 100\% - SOC$. A battery cycled at 80% DOD is significantly more stressed than one cycled at 20% DOD. Cycle life scales approximately as $N_c \propto (1/DOD)^k$ where $k \approx 1.5$–$2$ for Li-ion chemistries.

## 2.5 Power Capability (HPPC-Derived)

The USABC defines **available power** at any SOC point as:

$$P_{discharge} = V_{min} \cdot (V_{oc} - V_{min}) / R_i$$

$$P_{regen} = V_{max} \cdot (V_{max} - V_{oc}) / R_i$$

Where $V_{min}$ and $V_{max}$ are the cell's minimum and maximum allowable terminal voltages.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Validity / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------|------------------|
| 1 | Specific Energy | $E_s = \dfrac{E_{total}}{m}$ | **Wh/kg** | $E_{total} = \int V \cdot I \, dt$ |
| 2 | Energy Density | $E_v = \dfrac{E_{total}}{V_{vol}}$ | **Wh/L** | $V_{vol}$ = pack volume |
| 3 | Specific Power | $P_s = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{m}$ | **W/kg** | Peak burst capability |
| 4 | Terminal Voltage | $V_t = V_{oc} - I \cdot R_i$ | **V** | Discharge convention |
| 5 | SOC (general) | $SOC(t) = SOC_0 - \dfrac{1}{Q_n}\int I \,dt$ | **%** | $Q_n$ = rated capacity |
| 6 | SOC (constant I) | $SOC(t) = SOC_0 - \dfrac{I \cdot t}{Q_n \cdot 3600}$ | **%** | $I$ in A, $t$ in s |
| 7 | DOD | $DOD = 100 - SOC$ | **%** | Direct complement |
| 8 | Discharge Power | $P_{dis} = \dfrac{V_{min}(V_{oc}-V_{min})}{R_i}$ | **W** | USABC definition |
| 9 | Regen Power | $P_{reg} = \dfrac{V_{max}(V_{max}-V_{oc})}{R_i}$ | **W** | USABC definition |
| 10 | Coulombic Eff. | $\eta_c = \dfrac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} \times 100$ | **%** | Single cycle basis |
| 11 | Round-Trip Eff. | $\eta_{rt} = \dfrac{E_{out}}{E_{in}} \times 100$ | **%** | Full energy basis |
| 12 | SOH | $SOH = \dfrac{Q_{max,current}}{Q_{max,rated}} \times 100$ | **%** | Aging metric |
| 13 | Self-Discharge | $R_{sd} = \dfrac{\Delta Q}{Q_0 \cdot \Delta t} \times 100$ | **%/month** | At fixed $T$ |
| 14 | Energy Stored | $E = V_{nom} \cdot Q_n$ | **Wh** | $V_{nom}$ = nominal V |
| 15 | C-Rate | $C_{rate} = \dfrac{I}{Q_n}$ | **dimensionless** | 1C = full discharge in 1h |
| 16 | Peukert's Law | $I^n \cdot t = C$ | **A·h** | $n \approx 1.05$–$1.3$ for LA |

> [!TIP]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** For KTU board exam answers, always write the formula first, *then* substitute numerical values, *then* compute the result. Examiners award partial marks for correct formula identification even if arithmetic is wrong.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The parameters derived through characterization directly feed into:

- **Drivetrain Sizing (Module 2)**: Required motor power ÷ device specific power → minimum mass.
- **Range Estimation (Module 3)**: Usable specific energy × pack mass ÷ vehicle energy demand.
- **Thermal Management Design**: Internal resistance heating ($I^2R_i$ losses) determines cooling load.
- **BMS Algorithm Design**: SOC estimation, cell balancing, and fault detection all depend on characterization data.
- **Economic Analysis**: Cycle life + replacement cost = Total Cost of Ownership (TCO).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Energy Extracted from a Constant-Current Discharge

> **Problem Statement:** Derive the expression for total energy extracted from a battery discharged at constant current $I$ from initial voltage $V_1$ to cutoff voltage $V_2$, assuming a linear voltage-time relationship during discharge.

**Given:**
- Constant discharge current: $I = I_0$ (A)
- Initial terminal voltage: $V_1$ (V)
- Cutoff voltage: $V_2$ (V)
- Total discharge time: $t_{dis}$ (s)
- Rated capacity: $Q_n$ (Ah)

**Step 1 — Instantaneous Power Delivered**
The instantaneous electrical power delivered to the load is:

$$P(t) = V(t) \cdot I_0$$

**Step 2 — Total Energy (Fundamental Definition)**
Total energy is the time integral of power:

$$E_{total} = \int_0^{t_{dis}} V(t) \cdot I_0 \, dt = I_0 \int_0^{t_{dis}} V(t) \, dt$$

**Step 3 — Linear Voltage Approximation**
Assuming $V(t)$ decreases linearly from $V_1$ to $V_2$:

$$V(t) = V_1 - \left(\frac{V_1 - V_2}{t_{dis}}\right) \cdot t$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the Integral**
Substituting into the energy integral:

$$E_{total} = I_0 \int_0^{t_{dis}} \left[ V_1 - \frac{V_1 - V_2}{t_{dis}} \cdot t \right] dt$$

**Step 5 — Split the Integral into Two Terms**
$$E_{total} = I_0 \left[ V_1 \int_0^{t_{dis}} dt - \frac{V_1 - V_2}{t_{dis}} \int_0^{t_{dis}} t \, dt \right]$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate Each Integral**
- First integral: $\int_0^{t_{dis}} dt = t_{dis}$
- Second integral: $\int_0^{t_{dis}} t \, dt = \dfrac{t_{dis}^2}{2}$

**Step 7 — Substitute Back**
$$E_{total} = I_0 \left[ V_1 \cdot t_{dis} - \frac{V_1 - V_2}{t_{dis}} \cdot \frac{t_{dis}^2}{2} \right]$$

**Step 8 — Simplify the Algebraic Expression**
$$E_{total} = I_0 \left[ V_1 \cdot t_{dis} - \frac{(V_1 - V_2) \cdot t_{dis}}{2} \right]$$

$$E_{total} = I_0 \cdot t_{dis} \left[ V_1 - \frac{V_1 - V_2}{2} \right]$$

**Step 9 — Final Closed-Form Result**
$$\boxed{E_{total} = I_0 \cdot t_{dis} \cdot \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2}}$$

**Step 10 — Physical Interpretation**
The result $E_{total} = I_0 \cdot t_{dis} \cdot V_{avg}$ where $V_{avg} = \dfrac{V_1 + V_2}{2}$ is intuitive: the area under the linear $V(t)$ curve, multiplied by the constant current, yields the energy. This is the **trapezoidal area** under the discharge curve.

**Step 11 — Convert to Watt-hours**
Since $I_0$ is in A and $t_{dis}$ in hours, then $I_0 \cdot t_{dis} = Q$ (Ah, the charge extracted), giving:

$$E_{total} = Q \cdot V_{avg} \quad \text{[Wh]}$$

This matches the fundamental relation $E = V \cdot Q$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Available Discharge Power (USABC Form)

**Given:** A cell with $V_{oc} = 3.7$ V (Li-ion) and $R_i = 0.05$ Ω. Maximum allowable terminal voltage during discharge: $V_{min} = 2.7$ V.

**Step 1 — Write the Terminal Voltage Equation**
At maximum discharge current, $V_{terminal} = V_{min}$:

$$V_{min} = V_{oc} - I_{max} \cdot R_i$$

**Step 2 — Solve for Maximum Current**
$$I_{max} = \frac{V_{oc} - V_{min}}{R_i} = \frac{3.7 - 2.7}{0.05} = \frac{1.0}{0.05} = 20 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3 — Express Available Power**
$$P_{dis} = V_{min} \cdot I_{max} = V_{min} \cdot \frac{V_{oc} - V_{min}}{R_i}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Values**
$$P_{dis} = \frac{2.7 \times (3.7 - 2.7)}{0.05} = \frac{2.7 \times 1.0}{0.05} = \frac{2.7}{0.05} = 54 \text{ W}$$

**Step 5 — Physical Insight**
Notice that the maximum power transfer theorem (50% of $V_{oc}^2/R_i$ = 136.9 W here) is **NOT** reached. The USABC formula deliberately *under-sizes* the current to keep terminal voltage above $V_{min}$ — protecting the cell from over-discharge and voltage collapse.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: SOC Calculation Under Variable Load

> **Problem:** A 50 Ah EV battery is at 80% SOC. The vehicle draws 25 A for 2 hours, then 50 A for 1 hour. Assume $\eta_c = 1.0$ (ideal). What is the final SOC?

**Step 1 — Charge Extracted in Phase 1**
$$Q_1 = I_1 \cdot t_1 = 25 \text{ A} \times 2 \text{ h} = 50 \text{ Ah}$$

**Step 2 — Charge Extracted in Phase 2**
$$Q_2 = I_2 \cdot t_2 = 50 \text{ A} \times 1 \text{ h} = 50 \text{ Ah}$$

**Step 3 — Total Charge Extracted**
$$Q_{total} = Q_1 + Q_2 = 50 + 50 = 100 \text{ Ah}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to SOC Reduction**
$$\Delta SOC = \frac{Q_{total}}{Q_{rated}} \times 100 = \frac{100}{50} \times 100 = 200\%$$

**Step 5 — Impossible Result — Physical Interpretation**
The battery would be long dead. The *corrected* calculation uses the SOC-limited available charge:

$$Q_{available} = SOC_0 \times Q_{rated} = 0.80 \times 50 = 40 \text{ Ah}$$

The battery can only deliver 40 Ah, so the second phase (50 A × 1 h = 50 Ah) is infeasible. The battery will reach 0% SOC partway through Phase 2.

**Step 6 — Time to Reach 0% SOC in Phase 2**
$$t_{cutoff} = \frac{Q_{available} - Q_1}{I_2} = \frac{40 - 50}{-25} \rightarrow \text{Recheck:}$$

$$t_{cutoff} = \frac{40 \text{ Ah (remaining)}}{50 \text{ A}} = 0.8 \text{ h} = 48 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 7 — Final SOC (after Phase 1)**
$$SOC_1 = 80\% - \frac{50}{50} \times 100 = 80\% - 100\% = -20\% \rightarrow \text{Clamped to 0\%}$$

**Step 8 — Final Answer**
$$\boxed{SOC_{final} = 0\% \text{ (battery fully depleted by end of Phase 1)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Demonstrated:** Many students forget that the *usable* charge is bounded by current SOC. Always compute $Q_{available} = SOC \times Q_{rated}$ before checking feasibility.

---

## 3.4 Ragone Plot Interpretation (Analytical Matrix)

A **Ragone plot** is a log-log plot of Specific Power (W/kg) on x-axis vs Specific Energy (Wh/kg) on y-axis. Each point represents a single operating point; the locus of all points forms a "feasibility envelope."

| Region | Power Level | Energy Level | Use Case | Example Source |
|--------|-------------|--------------|----------|----------------|
| Top-Right (High P, High E) | High | High | Ideal EV | **Theoretical target** |
| Top-Left (Low P, High E) | Low | High | Range-dominant | Fuel Cells |
| Bottom-Right (High P, Low E) | High | Low | Acceleration-dominant | Ultracapacitors |
| Bottom-Left (Low P, Low E) | Low | Low | Infeasible | Avoid |

Lines of constant **discharge time** ($t = E/P$) are straight lines on the log-log plot with slope = –1. A Li-ion cell moved from the "low-power" to "high-power" region of its envelope sacrifices ~30–50% of its usable energy due to internal resistance losses and concentration polarization.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

    participant C as Controller

    C->>D: Step 1 Rest 1 hour

    Note over D: V drops due to R_i
    C->>D: Step 3 Rest 40s

This is a **guaranteed 2 marks** in the drawing question.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1:** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]
**Define the term "State of Health (SOH)" of a battery used in an electric vehicle. How is it mathematically expressed, and why is it critical for EV battery warranty determination?**

**Model Answer (3 marks — exact KTU valuation key structure):**

**Definition (1 mark):** State of Health (SOH) is a quantitative metric that expresses the *current maximum deliverable capacity* of a battery as a percentage of its *original rated capacity* when the battery was new.

**Mathematical Expression (1 mark):**

$$SOH = \frac{Q_{max,current}}{Q_{max,rated}} \times 100 \text{ \%}$$

**Criticality for Warranty (1 mark):** The EV industry universally adopts the **End-of-Life (EOL) criterion at 80% SOH**. When a battery degrades to 80% of its original capacity, it is considered unfit for automotive traction duty and is either replaced or repurposed for second-life stationary storage. This 80% threshold directly defines the manufacturer's warranty mileage and years.

---

### **Question 2:** [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]
**Differentiate between Specific Energy and Energy Density of a vehicle power source. Why is a high Energy Density important for electric vehicle packaging?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Specific Energy | Energy Density |
|--------|-----------------|----------------|
| Symbol | $E_s$ | $E_v$ |
| Denominator | Mass (kg) | Volume (L) |
| Unit | Wh/kg | Wh/L |
| Significance | Determines range per kg | Determines range per liter of pack volume |

**Why Energy Density matters for EV packaging (1 mark):** Modern EVs have stringent **pack volumetric constraints** dictated by the vehicle floor cavity, frunk space, and crash structure. A high Energy Density (Wh/L) allows manufacturers to fit more energy into a given chassis envelope without expanding the battery footprint, preserving passenger and cargo space.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam Pattern Reminder]:** Part B questions are **compulsory in choice**. You must answer *either* Question A *or* Question B. Each sub-part (a) and (b) carries 7 marks. Present your work in clearly labeled steps.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **HPPC (Hybrid Pulse Power Characterization) test** procedure as defined by USABC. Draw the typical pulse current profile and label each segment. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 100 Ah Li-ion battery is being characterized. The HPPC test at 50% SOC yields:
- Open-circuit voltage: $V_{oc} = 3.6$ V
- Measured internal resistance: $R_i = 0.02$ Ω
- Minimum allowable terminal voltage: $V_{min} = 2.8$ V
- Maximum allowable terminal voltage: $V_{max} = 4.2$ V

Calculate the **(i)** maximum discharge current, **(ii)** maximum regenerative current, **(iii)** available discharge power, and **(iv)** available regenerative power. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

#### Part (a) — HPPC Test Procedure (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Purpose (1 mark):** The HPPC test, standardized by the US Department of Energy's USABC program, is a transient power test that determines the **DC internal resistance** and **pulse power capability** of a battery cell/module at discrete State of Charge (SOC) levels.

**Step 2 — Pre-Conditioning (1 mark):** The cell is first fully charged using the manufacturer-specified constant-current-constant-voltage (CCCV) profile, then allowed to rest for a minimum of **1 hour** at controlled temperature (typically 25°C) to reach electrochemical equilibrium. The stabilized voltage is recorded as $V_{oc}$ at 100% SOC.

**Step 3 — Pulse Sequence (3 marks):** At each SOC checkpoint (10% decrements from 90% to 10%), the following pulse sequence is applied:

| Segment | Action | Duration | Purpose |
|---------|--------|----------|---------|
| 1 | Rest | ~3600 s | Equilibrate; record $V_{oc}$ |
| 2 | Discharge pulse | 10 s at $I_{dis}$ (high) | Measure voltage drop |
| 3 | Rest | 40 s | Recovery |
| 4 | Regen pulse | 10 s at $I_{reg}$ (high) | Measure voltage rise |
| 5 | Rest | 40 s | Recovery |
| 6 | Constant discharge | Calculated time | Step down to next SOC |

**Step 4 — Data Reduction (1 mark):** $R_i$ at each SOC is computed as:
- Discharge: $R_i = (V_{oc} - V_{dis,end}) / I_{dis}$
- Regen: $R_i = (V_{reg,end} - V_{oc}) / I_{reg}$

**Step 5 — Pulse Profile Diagram (1 mark):**

```
Current (A)
   +I_dis |     ┌──┐
          |     │  │
        0 |─────┘  └─────────┐
          |                  └──┐
   -I_reg |                     └──┐
          |                        └──────
          └──────────────────────────────► t (s)
                10s   40s  10s  40s
```

**Step 6 — Output (1 mark — distributed across previous steps):** The test yields $V_{oc}(SOC)$, $R_i(SOC)$, $P_{dis,max}(SOC)$, and $P_{reg,max}(SOC)$ tables, which feed the BMS power-limit algorithms.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Calculations (7 marks)

**Given Data:** $V_{oc} = 3.6$ V, $R_i = 0.02$ Ω, $V_{min} = 2.8$ V, $V_{max} = 4.2$ V

**(i) Maximum Discharge Current (2 marks):**
At maximum discharge, terminal voltage equals $V_{min}$:
$$I_{max,dis} = \frac{V_{oc} - V_{min}}{R_i} = \frac{3.6 - 2.8}{0.02} = \frac{0.8}{0.02} = 40 \text{ A}$$

**(ii) Maximum Regenerative Current (2 marks):**
At maximum regen, terminal voltage equals $V_{max}$:
$$I_{max,reg} = \frac{V_{max} - V_{oc}}{R_i} = \frac{4.2 - 3.6}{0.02} = \frac{0.6}{0.02} = 30 \text{ A}$$

**(iii) Available Discharge Power (1.5 marks):**
$$P_{dis} = V_{min} \cdot I_{max,dis} = 2.8 \times 40 = 112 \text{ W}$$

**(iv) Available Regenerative Power (1.5 marks):**
$$P_{reg} = V_{max} \cdot I_{max,reg} = 4.2 \times 30 = 126 \text{ W}$$

**Physical Insight (bonus, not for marks):** Notice that even though $I_{max,dis} > I_{max,reg}$, the available regen power is slightly higher because the higher terminal voltage ($V_{max} = 4.2$ V) compensates. This asymmetry is why regen braking is carefully current-limited near 100% SOC.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]

**(a)** With the help of a **neat sketch**, explain the **Ragone plot**. Discuss how it is used to compare different energy storage technologies for EV applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fuel cell hybrid bus uses a **battery + ultracapacitor** HESS. The battery pack has 30 kWh usable energy at 150 Wh/kg; the ultracapacitor module has 0.5 kWh at 5 Wh/kg and 3000 W/kg specific power. The vehicle's peak power demand during acceleration is **120 kW for 15 seconds**. Verify whether the ultracapacitor module alone can meet this peak power demand, assuming the module is 200 kg. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Ragone Plot (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):** A Ragone plot is a **log-log scatter chart** of Specific Power (W/kg) on the horizontal axis versus Specific Energy (Wh/kg) on the vertical axis. Each point represents a measurement under a constant-power discharge condition.

**Step 2 — Sketch (3 marks — award partial marks based on labels):**

```
Specific Energy (Wh/kg)
    10000 |
          |  * Fuel Cell (H2)
     1000 |        \
          |         * Li-ion (NMC)
      100 |             \
          |              * Li-ion (LFP)
       10 |                  * Li-ion (LTO)
          |                      \
        1 |                       * UltraCap (EDLC)
          └──────────────────────────────►
              10   100  1000  10000  Specific Power (W/kg)
```

**Step 3 — Interpretation (2 marks):** Points farther to the **upper-right** are theoretically ideal. Diagonal lines represent constant **discharge time** ($t = E/P$). The plot shows that:
- Fuel cells: high energy, low power (good for long cruise, poor for acceleration)
- Ultracapacitors: high power, low energy (good for short bursts, poor for long range)
- Li-ion variants fall in the middle, with LTO biased toward power and NMC toward energy

**Step 4 — Application to EV Design (1 mark):** Engineers use the Ragone plot to **size hybrid energy storage systems**, combining a high-energy source (battery/fuel cell) with a high-power source (ultracapacitor) so that the overall operating envelope is pushed toward the upper-right.

---

#### Part (b) — Ultracapacitor Peak Power Verification (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute Available Peak Power from UC Module (3 marks):**
Specific power of UC module: $P_s = 3000$ W/kg
Module mass: $m = 200$ kg

$$P_{peak,UC} = P_s \times m = 3000 \text{ W/kg} \times 200 \text{ kg} = 600{,}000 \text{ W} = 600 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 — Compare with Demand (2 marks):**
Required peak power: $P_{demand} = 120$ kW
Available from UC: $P_{peak,UC} = 600$ kW

Since $600 \text{ kW} > 120 \text{ kW}$, the ultracapacitor module **CAN** meet the peak power demand.

**Step 3 — Compute Available Energy During the 15-s Burst (2 marks):**
$$E_{delivered} = P_{demand} \times t = 120{,}000 \text{ W} \times 15 \text{ s} = 1{,}800{,}000 \text{ J}$$

Converting to Wh:
$$E_{delivered} = \frac{1{,}800{,}000}{3600} = 500 \text{ Wh} = 0.5 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 4 — Critical Verification — Energy Sufficiency (0 mark — for insight only):**
The UC module's *usable* energy = 0.5 kWh. The 15-second burst consumes *exactly* 0.5 kWh. This means the UC will be **fully depleted** at the end of the 15 s burst — a marginal design. In practice, the SOC of the UC should not drop below 25–50% to maintain voltage stability, so a larger UC module or a parallel battery contribution is recommended.

$$\boxed{\text{Peak Power: SATISFIED (600 kW >> 120 kW); Energy: MARGINAL (100\% depletion)}}$$

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in "Power Source Characterization" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing SOC with SOH (–2 marks):** SOC is *instantaneous* remaining charge (0–100%). SOH is *lifetime* health metric. Mixing them up is the #1 mistake in Part A.
>
> 2. **Forgetting sign convention in $V_t = V_{oc} - IR_i$ (–1 to –2 marks):** During *charging*, the equation is $V_t = V_{oc} + I R_i$. Many students write it the same way and lose easy marks.
>
> 3. **Units inconsistency (–1 mark):** Power formulas demand consistent units. Mixing Ah with seconds without the 3600 conversion factor is a frequent error. KTU examiners explicitly check for this.
>
> 4. **Ragone plot axes (–1 mark in Part B):** Always log-scale *both* axes. Drawing on linear scale and not labeling the constant-discharge-time lines loses 1 mark.
>
> 5. **HPPC pulse sequence wrong order (–2 marks):** The standard sequence is: rest → discharge pulse → rest → regen pulse → rest. Reversing or omitting the rest periods loses marks.
>
> 6. **Numerical substitution without formula (–2 marks):** Examiners award **formula marks** *before* substitution. Jumping straight to "I = 40 A" without writing $I = (V_{oc} - V_{min})/R_i$ loses 1 mark even if the answer is correct.
>
> 7. **No physical interpretation (–1 mark):** Every numerical answer should end with a 1-line *physical interpretation*. e.g., "The 600 kW available power is 5× the 120 kW demand, confirming HESS design is conservative."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read Before Exam**

- [x] **Characterization** = measuring a power source's *static + dynamic + aging* behavior under controlled conditions.
- [x] **Three master axes**: Energy (Wh/kg), Power (W/kg), Lifespan (cycles).
- [x] **USABC HPPC** is the gold-standard test for power capability → uses 10s discharge + 10s regen pulses at 10% SOC intervals.
- [x] **Terminal voltage model**: $V_t = V_{oc}(SOC, T) - I \cdot R_i(SOC, T)$ — remember sign flips for charge/regen.
- [x] **Available Discharge Power** = $\dfrac{V_{min}(V_{oc} - V_{min})}{R_i}$ — does NOT follow 50% maximum power transfer theorem.
- [x] **SOC** = instantaneous remaining charge (0–100%); **SOH** = lifetime degradation metric (EOL = 80%).
- [x] **DOD** = 100% − SOC. Cycle life $\propto (1/DOD)^{-1.5 \text{ to } -2}$.
- [x] **Ragone plot** is log-log, Specific Power on x-axis, Specific Energy on y-axis. Diagonal lines = constant discharge time.
- [x] **Hybrid Energy Storage Systems (HESS)** = battery (energy) + ultracapacitor (power) to overcome the energy-power trade-off.
- [x] **C-rate** = $I/Q_n$; **1C** = full discharge in 1 hour. Peukert's Law (mostly for lead-acid): $I^n \cdot t = \text{const}$.
- [x] **Coulombic efficiency** = charge out / charge in. **Round-trip efficiency** = energy out / energy in.
- [x] Always include **formula → substitution → result → interpretation** sequence in numerical answers.
- [x] KTU marks are awarded for *structured* answers; use tables, bullet points, and labeled diagrams wherever possible.

---

**End of Module 1 — Topic: Vehicle Power Source Characterization**
**Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme | NEP 2020 Aligned | Outcome-Based Education**
