topics.title: Transmission Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET742 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES AND RENEWABLE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Transmission Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Transmission Characteristics** in the context of Electric Vehicles (EVs) refer to the quantitative and qualitative behavior of the power transmission unit that mechanically couples the electric traction motor to the driving wheels. They describe how torque, speed, efficiency, gear ratio, and load are transmuted between the motor shaft and the wheel hub, dictating the overall dynamic performance, energy economy, and drivability of the vehicle.

In KTU 2024 scheme parlance, transmission characteristics encompass:

- **Gear Ratio ($i_g$)** – Ratio of motor speed ($\omega_m$) to wheel speed ($\omega_w$)
- **Torque Multiplication ($T_m \rightarrow T_w$)** – Mechanical advantage offered
- **Transmission Efficiency ($\eta_t$)** – Power losses across gears, bearings, and lubricants
- **Speed Reduction Factor** – Decoupling of high motor RPM from low wheel RPM
- **Tractive Force Capability ($F_t$)** – Force delivered at the tire contact patch
- **Backward Propagated Load Inertia** – Reflected inertia and damping at motor shaft

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> Unlike Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) vehicles that *require* a multi-speed transmission to keep the engine within its narrow optimal power band, EV traction motors inherently possess a wide constant-power speed range. This fundamentally alters the design philosophy of EV transmissions — often reducing it to a **single-speed reducer (fixed gear)**. The transmission must therefore be analyzed in light of this unique EV advantage.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are riding a **bicycle**:

- The **electric motor** is like your powerful leg muscles.
- The **transmission (gear system)** is like the bicycle's chainring and sprocket — a small front gear with a large rear gear multiplies your pedaling force (torque) but reduces how fast the wheel turns.
- The **wheels** are the actual contact with the road, providing the final motion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Explanation**
> Think of the EV transmission as a *force-amplifier* and *speed-reducer*. The motor spins very fast (often 10,000–20,000 RPM) but produces moderate torque. The transmission takes this rapid spin and converts it into slower, more forceful rotation at the wheels — exactly what is needed to push a 1,500 kg car up a hill. The **"characteristics"** of this conversion (how much it multiplies, how efficiently, and at what cost) define the transmission's signature.

---

## 1.3 Why Transmission Characteristics Matter in EVs

| Aspect | ICE Vehicle | EV (with single-speed transmission) |
|--------|-------------|--------------------------------------|
| Motor power band | Narrow (~2,000–5,000 RPM) | Wide (~0–15,000 RPM) |
| Number of gears | 5–7 + reverse | Typically 1 + reverse |
| Transmission complexity | High (synchromesh, clutches) | Low (planetary or helical gear) |
| Efficiency at peak | ~95% | ~96–98% |
| Shift shock | Noticeable | None (seamless torque) |
| Cost | High | Significantly lower |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Takeaway:** The transmission in an EV is often *simpler* than in an ICE car, but the *mathematical modeling* of its characteristics is *equally critical* because it directly determines the vehicle's **gradeability, top speed, acceleration, and range**.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Rolling resistance coefficient ($C_{rr}$)** ≈ 0.008 to 0.015 (for passenger car tires on asphalt)
- **Aerodynamic drag coefficient ($C_d$)** ≈ 0.25 to 0.35 (typical sedan EV)
- **Air density ($\rho$)** = **1.225 kg/m³** (at sea level, 15°C)
- **Gravitational acceleration ($g$)** = **9.81 m/s²**
- **Typical EV gear ratio ($i_g$)** = 8:1 to 12:1
- **Wheel radius ($r_w$)** ≈ 0.30 to 0.35 m

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle

The EV drivetrain functions as a **mechanical impedance transformer** between two rotating masses:

1. **Source side** – Electric motor (high speed, moderate torque, low inertia)
2. **Load side** – Vehicle wheels (low speed, high tractive force, high inertia)

The transmission bridges these by:

- **Reducing speed** by factor $i_g$
- **Multiplying torque** by factor $i_g \cdot \eta_t$
- **Reflecting inertia** by factor $i_g^2$

> [!NOTE]
> **Impedance Matching Principle**
> Just as a transformer matches impedance between a source and load in electrical circuits, a mechanical transmission matches the "rotational impedance" between motor and wheels. Without it, the motor would spin uselessly fast while the car barely creeps forward.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Flow of Transmission

1. **Motor delivers torque ($T_m$) and rotates at $\omega_m$**
   - Power input: $P_m = T_m \cdot \omega_m$
2. **Transmission couples motor to differential**
   - Gear ratio $i_g = \dfrac{N_m}{N_w}$ where $N_m$ is motor pinion teeth, $N_w$ is wheel gear teeth
3. **Torque at wheels ($T_w$) increases**
   - $T_w = T_m \cdot i_g \cdot \eta_t$
4. **Wheel speed ($\omega_w$) reduces**
   - $\omega_w = \dfrac{\omega_m}{i_g}$
5. **Tractive force at tire contact patch**
   - $F_t = \dfrac{T_w}{r_w} = \dfrac{T_m \cdot i_g \cdot \eta_t}{r_w}$
6. **Vehicle linear velocity**
   - $v_m = \omega_w \cdot r_w = \dfrac{\omega_m \cdot r_w}{i_g}$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Description |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Gear ratio | $i_g = \dfrac{\omega_m}{\omega_w} = \dfrac{N_m}{N_w}$ | dimensionless | Speed reduction factor |
| 2 | Wheel torque | $T_w = T_m \cdot i_g \cdot \eta_t$ | N·m | Torque at wheel hub |
| 3 | Wheel speed | $\omega_w = \dfrac{\omega_m}{i_g}$ | rad/s | Angular speed at wheel |
| 4 | Tractive force | $F_t = \dfrac{T_m \cdot i_g \cdot \eta_t}{r_w}$ | N | Force at tire-road interface |
| 5 | Vehicle speed | $v = \dfrac{\omega_m \cdot r_w}{i_g}$ | m/s | Linear velocity |
| 6 | Reflected inertia | $J_{ref} = J_m + \dfrac{J_w}{i_g^2}$ | kg·m² | Inertia at motor shaft |
| 7 | Tractive effort eq. | $F_t = m \cdot a + m g \sin\alpha + m g C_{rr} \cos\alpha + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2$ | N | Net driving force |
| 8 | Gradeability | $\sin\alpha_{max} = \dfrac{F_{t,max} - m g C_{rr}}{m g}$ | dimensionless | Maximum climb angle |
| 9 | Top speed | $v_{max} = \dfrac{\omega_{m,max} \cdot r_w}{i_g}$ | m/s | Limited by motor max RPM |
| 10 | Accel. time (0–v) | $t = \int_0^v \dfrac{m \cdot v'}{F_t(v') - F_{res}(v')} \, dv'$ | s | Time integral for acceleration |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vertical Pipe Substitution Mandate**
> In all rendered math, the symbol $\vert$ or $\mid$ must be used in place of `|` to prevent markdown table corruption. Example: $\vert v \vert$ instead of $|v|$.

---

## 2.4 Categories of EV Transmissions

### A. Single-Speed Transmissions
- **Architecture:** Fixed gear ratio (typically 8:1 to 12:1) using a helical or planetary gear set
- **Use case:** Most modern passenger EVs (Tesla Model 3, Nissan Leaf, Tata Nexon EV)
- **Advantages:** Simple, compact, lightweight, low cost, near-zero maintenance
- **Limitations:** Compromised efficiency at very low and very high speeds; suboptimal for high-performance EVs

### B. Multi-Speed Transmissions
- **Architecture:** 2-speed (Porsche Taycan) or even 3-speed (some concept EVs)
- **Use case:** Performance EVs, heavy-duty EVs (e.g., Tesla Cybertruck tri-motor, Audi e-tron GT)
- **Advantages:** Optimized efficiency across full speed range; better acceleration
- **Limitations:** Added complexity, weight, cost, and need for shift actuators

### C. Continuously Variable Transmission (CVT)
- **Architecture:** Variable-diameter pulleys
- **Use case:** Hybrid EVs (Toyota Prius, Honda Insight)
- **Advantages:** Infinite ratio selection, optimized efficiency
- **Limitations:** Torque capacity limits, NVH (noise-vibration-harshness) issues

### D. In-Wheel Direct Drive
- **Architecture:** No transmission; motor placed inside the wheel
- **Use case:** Niche, concept vehicles (Protean Electric, Lordstown)
- **Advantages:** Eliminates drivetrain losses, frees cabin space
- **Limitations:** Unsprung mass, thermal management, cost

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Fact for KTU Viva:** The Tata Nexon EV uses a single-speed reduction gear with a ratio of approximately **9.3:1**, while the Tesla Model 3 Long Range uses approximately **9.0:1**.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Gradeability design:** Determining whether a vehicle can climb a 30% gradient
- **Range optimization:** Selecting gear ratio to maximize Wh/km efficiency
- **Top speed vs. acceleration trade-off:** Lower $i_g$ → higher top speed but slower 0–100 km/h
- **Torque vectoring:** Multiple motors with differential transmission strategies
- **Regenerative braking tuning:** Transmission ratio affects regen braking torque at wheels

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Tractive Force Equation from Newton's Second Law

Consider an EV of mass $m$ moving on a road inclined at angle $\alpha$ with acceleration $a$. The forces opposing motion are:

1. **Aerodynamic drag** $F_a = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2$
2. **Rolling resistance** $F_r = m g C_{rr} \cos\alpha$
3. **Gravitational component** $F_g = m g \sin\alpha$

**Step 1:** Apply Newton's second law in the direction of motion:

$$F_t - F_a - F_r - F_g = m \cdot a$$

**Step 2:** Substitute all resistive force expressions:

$$F_t = m \cdot a + m g \sin\alpha + m g C_{rr} \cos\alpha + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2$$

**Step 3:** For small angles (typical EV design uses $\alpha$ in radians, small enough that $\cos\alpha \approx 1$):

$$F_t = m \cdot a + m g \sin\alpha + m g C_{rr} + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2$$

**Step 4:** Express tractive force in terms of motor torque and transmission:

$$F_t = \dfrac{T_m \cdot i_g \cdot \eta_t}{r_w}$$

**Step 5:** Equate both expressions to obtain the **vehicle dynamics equation**:

$$\dfrac{T_m \cdot i_g \cdot \eta_t}{r_w} = m \cdot a + m g \sin\alpha + m g C_{rr} + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is the *master equation* of EV dynamics. Every performance metric — acceleration, top speed, gradeability, range — derives from solving this equation for different operating conditions.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Optimal Gear Ratio for Maximum Gradeability

**Objective:** Find $i_g$ that allows the EV to climb the steepest possible gradient.

**Step 1:** At maximum gradeability, vehicle is at low speed ($v \to 0$), so aerodynamic drag is negligible ($F_a \approx 0$). Also, acceleration is zero (steady-state climbing):

$$F_{t,max} = m g \sin\alpha_{max} + m g C_{rr} \cos\alpha_{max}$$

**Step 2:** At maximum motor torque $T_{m,max}$:

$$F_{t,max} = \dfrac{T_{m,max} \cdot i_g \cdot \eta_t}{r_w}$$

**Step 3:** Equate and solve for $\sin\alpha_{max}$:

$$\sin\alpha_{max} = \dfrac{T_{m,max} \cdot i_g \cdot \eta_t}{m g \cdot r_w} - C_{rr} \cos\alpha_{max}$$

**Step 4:** For moderate angles where $\cos\alpha_{max} \approx 1$:

$$\sin\alpha_{max} \approx \dfrac{T_{m,max} \cdot i_g \cdot \eta_t}{m g \cdot r_w} - C_{rr}$$

**Step 5:** **Design rule:** Higher $i_g$ improves gradeability, but $i_g$ is also limited by top-speed requirement:

$$i_g \leq \dfrac{\omega_{m,max} \cdot r_w}{v_{max}}$$

**Step 6:** Therefore, **optimal gear ratio** is a compromise:

$$i_{g,opt} = \sqrt{i_{g,accel} \cdot i_{g,top-speed}} \quad \text{(geometric mean heuristic)}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Top Speed and Acceleration Time (0–100 km/h)

**Top speed derivation:**

At top speed, acceleration $a = 0$ and gradient is zero ($\alpha = 0$):

$$F_{t,max} = m g C_{rr} + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v_{max}^2$$

Solve the quadratic for $v_{max}$:

$$v_{max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{m g C_{rr} + \dfrac{T_{m,max} \cdot i_g \cdot \eta_t}{r_w} - \text{...}}{\dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A}}$$

Simpler form (ignoring small terms):

$$v_{max} \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{\dfrac{T_{m,max} \cdot i_g \cdot \eta_t}{r_w} - m g C_{rr}}{\dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A}}$$

**Acceleration time (numerical integration):**

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{0 \to v_f} &= \int_0^{v_f} \dfrac{m \cdot v}{F_t(v) - F_{res}(v)} \, dv \\
&= \int_0^{v_f} \dfrac{m \cdot v}{\dfrac{T_m(v) \cdot i_g \cdot \eta_t}{r_w} - m g C_{rr} - \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2} \, dv
\end{aligned}$$

For a constant-torque region (low speeds), $T_m = T_{m,max}$, and integration yields:

$$t_{0 \to v_f} = \dfrac{m \cdot r_w}{T_{m,max} \cdot i_g \cdot \eta_t} \cdot v_f \quad \text{(low-speed approximation)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This linear approximation works well below 30–40 km/h. Above that, the quadratic drag term $\rho C_d A v^2$ becomes dominant and the integral becomes non-linear.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Example (Worked-Out)

**Given:** EV with $m = 1500$ kg, $T_{m,max} = 280$ N·m, $i_g = 9$, $\eta_t = 0.96$, $r_w = 0.32$ m, $C_{rr} = 0.012$, $C_d = 0.29$, $A = 2.2$ m², $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³.

**Find:** Maximum tractive force and gradeability.

**Step 1:** Tractive force at wheels:

$$F_{t,max} = \dfrac{280 \cdot 9 \cdot 0.96}{0.32} = \dfrac{2419.2}{0.32} = 7560 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2:** Rolling resistance force:

$$F_r = 1500 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.012 = 176.58 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3:** Net available climbing force:

$$F_{climb} = 7560 - 176.58 = 7383.42 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4:** Maximum gradient:

$$\sin\alpha_{max} = \dfrac{7383.42}{1500 \cdot 9.81} = \dfrac{7383.42}{14715} = 0.5018$$

$$\alpha_{max} = \arcsin(0.5018) \approx 30.1°$$

**Step 5:** Equivalent percentage grade:

$$\text{Grade} = \tan(30.1°) \cdot 100\% = 58\%$$

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world interpretation:** This means the EV can theoretically climb a 58% grade (i.e., rise 58 m for every 100 m horizontal) in steady-state. In practice, manufacturers limit this to ~30% for safety.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 EV Transmission System — Block Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    BAT[Battery Pack] --> INV[Motor Inverter VCU]
    INV --> MTR[PMSM Traction Motor]
    MTR --> TM[Motor Torque Tm]
    MTR --> WM[Motor Speed omega_m]
    TM --> GR[Single Speed Reducer Gear Ratio ig]
    WM --> GR
    GR --> WT[Wheel Torque Tw]
    GR --> WW[Wheel Speed omega_w]
    WT --> DIFF[Differential Unit]
    DIFF --> WHLS[Wheel Hub Assembly]
    WW --> WHLS
    WHLS --> FT[Tractive Force Ft]
    FT --> VEH[Vehicle Dynamics m,a,v]
    VEH --> RES[Resistive Forces Fr Fa Fg]
    RES --> LOAD[Load Demand on Motor]
    LOAD --> MTR
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Energy and information flow counter-clockwise: Battery → Inverter → Motor → Transmission → Wheels → Vehicle Motion. The motor and vehicle are coupled through a feedback loop where the load (resistive forces) determines the new torque demand.

---

## 4.2 Transmission Type Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[Design an EV Transmission] --> Q1{Performance Requirement}
    Q1 -->|Economy Commuter| S1[Single Speed Reducer 8:1 to 10:1]
    Q1 -->|Performance Sport| M1[Multi Speed 2 or 3 Gear]
    Q1 -->|Heavy Duty Truck| M2[Multi Speed 4 to 6 Gear]
    Q1 -->|Urban Compact| S2[Single Speed Planetary 9:1]
    S1 --> EFF1[Efficiency 96 percent Compact Lightweight]
    M1 --> EFF2[High efficiency across range Added cost]
    M2 --> EFF3[Max torque capacity Higher complexity]
    S2 --> EFF4[Best Wh per km Minimum cost]
```

---

## 4.3 Torque–Speed Characteristic Map (Qualitative)

```
Motor Torque (N·m)
│
│   ████████
│   █      █
│   █      █
│   █      █  CONSTANT TORQUE
│   █      █
│   █      █
│   █      █
│   █      █
│   █      █
│   █      █░░░░░░░░
│   █      █         ███████
│   █      █         █  CONSTANT POWER
│   █      █         █
│   █      █         █
│   █      █         █
└──────────────────────────────── Motor Speed (RPM)
    0     ω_base              ω_max
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** EV motors have two distinct regions — *constant torque* (low speeds) and *constant power* (high speeds). The transmission ratio $i_g$ shifts this entire curve along the speed axis at the wheel.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**List any three key differences between the transmission requirements of an Electric Vehicle and a conventional Internal Combustion Engine vehicle.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | ICE Transmission | EV Transmission |
|---|------------------|------------------|
| 1 | Multi-speed (5–7 gears) needed to keep engine in narrow power band | Usually single-speed because EV motors have wide constant-power range |
| 2 | Requires clutch and synchromesh mechanism for smooth gear engagement | No clutch needed; direct mesh gear reducer suffices |
| 3 | Gear shifts cause torque interruption; NVH issues | Seamless torque delivery; no shift shock |
| 4 | Heavier and more complex | Lighter and simpler |
| 5 | Efficiency ~95% in best gear | Efficiency ~96–98% in single-speed |

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define gear ratio in an EV transmission and state its typical range for a passenger electric car.**

**Model Answer:**

The **gear ratio ($i_g$)** of an EV transmission is the ratio of the motor's rotational speed to the wheel's rotational speed:

$$i_g = \dfrac{\omega_m}{\omega_w} = \dfrac{N_m}{N_w}$$

where $N_m$ and $N_w$ are the number of teeth on the motor pinion and wheel gear, respectively.

For a typical passenger EV, $i_g$ ranges from **8:1 to 12:1**, with 9:1 being most common.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the complete **tractive force equation** for an electric vehicle moving up a road gradient, including all resistive forces. Clearly state the significance of each term. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An electric vehicle has the following specifications:
- Mass $m = 1800$ kg (including battery)
- Wheel radius $r_w = 0.33$ m
- Gear ratio $i_g = 9.5$
- Transmission efficiency $\eta_t = 0.97$
- Maximum motor torque $T_{m,max} = 320$ N·m
- Rolling resistance coefficient $C_{rr} = 0.011$
- $C_d = 0.28$, $A = 2.3$ m², $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³

Calculate the **maximum gradient** the vehicle can climb at constant low speed. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation** — (7 marks)

Apply Newton's second law in the direction of motion on a vehicle of mass $m$ on a road inclined at angle $\alpha$:

$$\sum F = m \cdot a$$

Forces acting:
- Tractive force $F_t$ (forward)
- Aerodynamic drag $F_a = \dfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2$ (backward)
- Rolling resistance $F_r = m g C_{rr} \cos\alpha$ (backward)
- Gravitational component $F_g = m g \sin\alpha$ (backward)

Equation of motion:

$$F_t - F_a - F_r - F_g = m \cdot a$$

Substituting:

$$F_t = m \cdot a + m g \sin\alpha + m g C_{rr} \cos\alpha + \dfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2$$

Also:

$$F_t = \dfrac{T_m \cdot i_g \cdot \eta_t}{r_w}$$

Equating both:

$$\dfrac{T_m \cdot i_g \cdot \eta_t}{r_w} = m \cdot a + m g \sin\alpha + m g C_{rr} \cos\alpha + \dfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2$$

Significance of each term:
- $m \cdot a$ — Force needed to accelerate
- $m g \sin\alpha$ — Force to overcome gravity on slope
- $m g C_{rr} \cos\alpha$ — Rolling friction
- $\dfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2$ — Air drag (dominant at high speeds)

**(b) Numerical Solution** — (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Tractive force at max motor torque:

$$F_{t,max} = \dfrac{T_{m,max} \cdot i_g \cdot \eta_t}{r_w} = \dfrac{320 \cdot 9.5 \cdot 0.97}{0.33}$$

$$F_{t,max} = \dfrac{2948.8}{0.33} = 8935.76 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2:** At constant low speed, $v \approx 0$ → no aerodynamic drag.

**Step 3:** At maximum gradient (steady-state climb), $a = 0$:

$$F_{t,max} = m g \sin\alpha_{max} + m g C_{rr} \cos\alpha_{max}$$

$$8935.76 = 1800 \cdot 9.81 \cdot \sin\alpha_{max} + 1800 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.011 \cdot \cos\alpha_{max}$$

$$8935.76 = 17658 \sin\alpha_{max} + 194.24 \cos\alpha_{max}$$

**Step 4:** Assume $\alpha_{max} = 25°$ as initial guess:

$$17658 \cdot \sin(25°) + 194.24 \cdot \cos(25°) = 17658 \cdot 0.4226 + 194.24 \cdot 0.9063 = 7462.7 + 176.04 = 7638.7 \text{ N}$$

Not enough — try $\alpha_{max} = 30°$:

$$17658 \cdot 0.5 + 194.24 \cdot 0.866 = 8829 + 168.2 = 8997.2 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5:** Linear interpolation:

$$\alpha_{max} = 25° + \dfrac{8935.76 - 7638.7}{8997.2 - 7638.7} \cdot 5° = 25° + \dfrac{1297.06}{1358.5} \cdot 5°$$

$$\alpha_{max} = 25° + 4.77° = 29.77° \approx 29.8°$$

**Step 6:** Convert to percentage grade:

$$\text{Grade} = \tan(29.8°) \cdot 100\% = 0.572 \cdot 100\% = 57.2\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common mistake 1:** Forgetting the $\cos\alpha$ factor in the rolling resistance term. Marks lost: **2/7**
> - **Common mistake 2:** Using $g = 10$ m/s² instead of $9.81$ m/s² — KTU strictly expects $9.81$ m/s². Marks lost: **0.5/7**
> - **Common mistake 3:** Ignoring aerodynamic drag in the wrong direction. At very low speeds, it IS negligible — but state the assumption explicitly. Marks lost: **1/7**
> - **Common mistake 4:** Not specifying units in the final answer. Always include "N" for force, "m" for radius, and "°" or "%" for gradient.

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **torque–speed characteristics of a typical EV traction motor** and discuss how the transmission ratio $i_g$ modifies these characteristics at the wheel. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A two-wheeler electric scooter uses a single-speed transmission with $i_g = 4.5$ and a hub motor of maximum speed 600 RPM. The wheel diameter is 0.5 m. Calculate: (i) the maximum vehicle speed in km/h, and (ii) the wheel torque if the motor delivers 60 N·m at 50% transmission efficiency. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a)** — (7 marks)

The torque-speed characteristic of a PMSM (Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor) used in EVs has two distinct regions:

**Region 1: Constant Torque Region ($0 \le \omega \le \omega_{base}$)**
- Motor delivers constant maximum torque $T_{m,max}$
- Power increases linearly with speed
- $P_m = T_{m,max} \cdot \omega$

**Region 2: Constant Power Region ($\omega_{base} \le \omega \le \omega_{max}$)**
- Torque decreases inversely with speed
- Power remains at $P_{m,max}$
- $T_m = \dfrac{P_{m,max}}{\omega}$

**Effect of transmission ratio $i_g$ on wheel characteristics:**

Wheel torque:

$$T_w = T_m \cdot i_g \cdot \eta_t$$

Wheel speed:

$$\omega_w = \dfrac{\omega_m}{i_g}$$

Thus, the entire torque-speed curve is **shifted and scaled** at the wheel:
- Torque values are multiplied by $i_g \cdot \eta_t$
- Speed values are divided by $i_g$

> [!NOTE]
> For a single-speed transmission, the *shape* of the curve is preserved, but the operating point is shifted to a region that better matches the vehicle's required wheel-speed range (0–1500 RPM typically).

**(b) Numerical Solution** — (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Compute wheel radius:

$$r_w = \dfrac{D}{2} = \dfrac{0.5}{2} = 0.25 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate maximum wheel speed:

$$\omega_w = \dfrac{\omega_m}{i_g} = \dfrac{600}{4.5} = 133.33 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 3:** Convert to linear speed:

$$v_{max} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{60} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot 0.5 \cdot 133.33}{60} = \dfrac{209.44}{60} = 3.49 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to km/h:

$$v_{max} = 3.49 \cdot 3.6 = 12.57 \text{ km/h}$$

**Step 5:** Calculate wheel torque:

$$T_w = T_m \cdot i_g \cdot \eta_t = 60 \cdot 4.5 \cdot 0.5 = 135 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 6:** Verify with tractive force:

$$F_t = \dfrac{T_w}{r_w} = \dfrac{135}{0.25} = 540 \text{ N}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common mistake 1:** Mixing up gear ratio definition (writing $i_g = \omega_w / \omega_m$ instead of correct direction). Marks lost: **2/7**
> - **Common mistake 2:** Forgetting to convert RPM → rad/s or RPM → m/s using the factor $\pi D / 60$. Marks lost: **1.5/7**
> - **Common mistake 3:** Confusing wheel diameter with radius. Marks lost: **1/7**
> - **Common mistake 4:** Not showing units throughout the solution. KTU deducts 0.5 marks for missing units.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Transmission Characteristics**

### ✅ Core Concepts
- [ ] Transmission acts as a **mechanical impedance transformer** between motor and wheels
- [ ] **Single-speed reducer** is the dominant topology in modern passenger EVs
- [ ] Wide constant-power range of EV motors eliminates the need for multi-gear boxes
- [ ] **Gear ratio $i_g$** = motor speed ÷ wheel speed (dimensionless)
- [ ] **Wheel torque** = motor torque × $i_g$ × $\eta_t$
- [ ] **Tractive force** = wheel torque ÷ wheel radius

### ✅ Critical Equations (MUST memorize)
- [ ] Tractive force equation: $F_t = m a + m g \sin\alpha + m g C_{rr} \cos\alpha + \dfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2$
- [ ] Optimal gear ratio: $i_{g,opt} = \sqrt{i_{g,accel} \cdot i_{g,top-speed}}$
- [ ] Top speed: $v_{max} = \dfrac{\omega_{m,max} \cdot r_w}{i_g}$
- [ ] Reflected inertia: $J_{ref} = J_m + \dfrac{J_w}{i_g^2}$
- [ ] Gradeability: $\sin\alpha_{max} = \dfrac{T_{m,max} \cdot i_g \cdot \eta_t}{m g \cdot r_w} - C_{rr}$

### ✅ Typical Values to Remember
- [ ] Gear ratio range for EV: **8:1 to 12:1**
- [ ] Air density: **$\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³**
- [ ] Rolling resistance: **$C_{rr} \approx 0.01$**
- [ ] Drag coefficient of EV sedan: **$C_d \approx 0.25$–$0.30$**
- [ ] Transmission efficiency: **$\eta_t = 0.95$–$0.98$**
- [ ] Gravitational acceleration: **$g = 9.81$ m/s²**

### ✅ Common Exam Traps
- [ ] Forgetting $\cos\alpha$ in rolling resistance
- [ ] Using $g = 10$ instead of $9.81$ (KTU penalty)
- [ ] Mixing up radius and diameter
- [ ] Ignoring aerodynamic drag in low-speed gradeability problems (state assumption explicitly)
- [ ] Not converting units (RPM → m/s, km/h → m/s)

### ✅ One-Line Memory Aid
> *"The transmission in an EV is not a gearbox — it is a torque magnifier and speed reducer rolled into one."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Electric Vehicles - History of Electric Vehicles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET742 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES AND RENEWABLE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Electric Vehicles - History of Electric Vehicles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electric Vehicle (EV):** A vehicle whose principal propulsion energy is derived from an **electric motor** drawing current from a **rechargeable onboard energy storage device** (such as a battery, supercapacitor, or fuel cell), instead of an internal combustion engine (ICE) that burns fossil fuel.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEAET742, Module 1)**, the "Introduction to Electric Vehicles" segment establishes the conceptual foundation of EV technology, beginning with its **historical evolution** to help students appreciate how societal, economic, and technological forces have shaped modern electric mobility.

### 1.2 Classification of EVs (KTU Terminology)

For board-level clarity, EVs are broadly classified as:

- **BEV (Battery Electric Vehicle):** Pure electric, no ICE. Example: Tata Nexon EV, Tesla Model 3.
- **HEV (Hybrid Electric Vehicle):** Combines ICE + Electric motor + small battery. Cannot be externally charged. Example: Toyota Prius (early gen).
- **PHEV (Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicle):** Larger battery that can be externally charged; ICE acts as range extender. Example: Toyota Prius Prime.
- **FCEV (Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle):** Uses **hydrogen** fuel cell to generate electricity onboard. Example: Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Reluctant Revolution":**
> Think of the EV as a brilliant invention that was "born too early." Imagine you invent a brilliant smartphone in **1850** — the technology is right, but the supporting ecosystem (charging stations, lithium batteries, renewable power grids) is missing. The EV story is similar. Inventors in the 1830s–1900s built working electric cars, but cheap petrol and the maturing ICE technology pushed them aside. They have now "returned home" because the ecosystem (batteries, charging, climate policy) is finally ready.

### 1.4 Why Study EV History?

> [!TIP]
> **Three Board-Exam Reasons:**
> 1. **Pattern Recognition** — Past booms and busts of EV technology reveal *why* battery breakthroughs (lithium-ion) and *why* oil shocks revived interest.
> 2. **Policy & Economy** — Modern EV adoption is driven by the same forces (oil prices, emissions) that triggered the **1970s EV revival**.
> 3. **Engineering Continuity** — Concepts like **regenerative braking** were demonstrated in the **1960s lunar rovers** and are now mainstream in every Tesla.

### 1.5 Key Historical Constants and Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics Used Throughout EV History:**
> - **Specific Energy (Wh/kg):** Energy stored per unit mass of battery. Lead-acid ≈ **30–40 Wh/kg**, Lithium-ion ≈ **150–250 Wh/kg**.
> - **Energy Density (Wh/L):** Energy stored per unit volume.
> - **Range (km):** Distance on a single charge.
> - **State of Charge (SoC):** Percentage of usable energy remaining in the battery.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Distinct Eras of EV History

A structured analytical breakdown of the **historical evolution** is essential for KTU answers. The timeline can be divided into **five clear eras**:

#### **Era 1 — Invention Dawn (1828–1899)**
- The very first electric motor was invented by **Hungarian physicist Ányos Jedlik (1828)**.
- **Robert Anderson (Scotland, 1832–1839)** built one of the first known electric carriages using non-rechargeable galvanic cells.
- **Thomas Davenport (USA, 1834)** built the first DC electric motor.
- By the **1890s**, EVs were practical — France and Britain had working electric taxis and busses.

#### **Era 2 — The Golden Age (1900–1920)**
- At the dawn of the 20th century, **electric cars represented around one-third of all vehicles on the road** in the United States.
- Women preferred EVs because they did not require a hand-cranked starter (a dangerous task).
- Vehicles like the **Detroit Electric (1907)** offered a range of approximately **130 km per charge** and were marketed to affluent urban buyers.

#### **Era 3 — The ICE Takeover (1920–1970)**
- **Henry Ford's Model T (1908)** was cheap, reliable, and mass-produced using the moving assembly line.
- Discovery of **cheap Texas crude oil (1920s)** made petrol inexpensive.
- **Charles Kettering's electric starter (1912)** eliminated the dangerous crank-starting of ICE vehicles.
- EVs became commercially irrelevant for nearly **five decades**.

#### **Era 4 — The Oil Crisis Revival (1970–1990)**
- The **1973 OPEC Oil Embargo** and **1979 Iran Oil Crisis** caused petrol prices to quadruple.
- **NASA's Lunar Roving Vehicle (1971)** — designed by Boeing and GM with **AC induction motors** — proved that EVs could operate in extreme environments.
- **Sebring-Vanguard CitiCar (1974)** became the **6th most-produced American car** by 1976.

#### **Era 5 — The Modern Lithium Revolution (1990–Present)**
- **GM EV1 (1996)** — first mass-produced modern EV with lead-acid then NiMH batteries.
- **Tesla Roadster (2008)** — first highway-legal serial-production EV to use **lithium-ion battery cells**.
- **Nissan Leaf (2010)** — first mass-market BEV.
- **India Context:** Reva (now Mahindra e2o, 2001) was one of the earliest modern Indian EVs; **FAME-I (2015) and FAME-II (2019)** policies accelerated adoption.

### 2.2 The Underlying Engineering "Why"

- **Why did EVs lose to ICE in 1920?** Because the **specific energy of lead-acid batteries (~30 Wh/kg)** is roughly **1/50th of petrol (~13,000 Wh/kg equivalent)**. Without a battery breakthrough, range was the killer constraint.
- **Why did EVs return after 2008?** Because the **specific energy of lithium-ion cells reached ~150 Wh/kg**, roughly **5× that of lead-acid**, finally solving the range problem.
- **Why is history on the board exam?** Because the KTU examiner tests your **analytical reasoning** — connecting **energy density, range, charging infrastructure, and policy** across time.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Equations and Reference Table for KTU Board Exams**

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula / Definition | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Specific Energy of Battery | $E_{sp}$ | $E_{sp} = \dfrac{E_{stored}}{m_{battery}}$ | $\text{Wh/kg}$ |
| Energy Stored in Pack | $E_{stored}$ | $E_{stored} = V_{pack} \times C_{pack}$ | $\text{Wh}$ |
| Vehicle Range (estimated) | $R$ | $R = \dfrac{E_{stored} \times \eta_{drivetrain}}{E_{c}}$ | $\text{km}$ |
| Electric Motor Power | $P_{m}$ | $P_{m} = V_{batt} \times I_{batt}$ | $\text{W}$ |
| Energy Consumption | $E_{c}$ | $E_{c} = \dfrac{\text{kWh consumed}}{\text{km travelled}}$ | $\text{kWh/km}$ |
| Torque of Motor | $T$ | $T = \dfrac{P_{m}}{\omega_{m}}$ | $\text{Nm}$ |
| Battery Round-Trip Efficiency | $\eta_{bat}$ | $\eta_{bat} = \dfrac{E_{discharged}}{E_{charged}} \times 100$ | $\%$ |

> **Quick Reference — Specific Energy Across Battery Generations**

| Battery Chemistry | Specific Energy (Wh/kg) | Era Dominant |
|---|---|---|
| Lead-Acid | $30 - 40$ | 1880s – 1990s |
| Nickel-Cadmium (NiCd) | $40 - 60$ | 1990s |
| Nickel-Metal Hydride (NiMH) | $60 - 120$ | 1996 – 2010 |
| Lithium-Ion (Li-ion) | $150 - 250$ | 2008 – Present |
| Solid-State (emerging) | $350 - 500$ | Future |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The history of EVs is not merely academic — it provides **engineering lessons** that guide modern design:
- The **failure of EV1 due to lack of charging infrastructure** taught modern automakers to **build charging networks alongside vehicles** (Tesla Supercharger, CCS2 in India).
- The **thermal management lessons from Boeing/Lunar Rover** directly inform today's **liquid-cooled battery packs**.
- India's **faster adoption curve** is informed by **grid readiness (renewable energy + solar charging)**, which earlier eras lacked.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Historical Derivation

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Why the 1900 EV Peak Crashed by 1930

Although this topic is historical in nature, a **rigorous logical derivation** is required for KTU answers. We will derive the **commercial collapse of EVs** between 1920 and 1930 using engineering economics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Assumption:** A vehicle is commercially viable if its **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** per km is lower than its competitor.

Let us compare an EV of 1910 vs. an ICE of 1910.

**Step 1 — Cost of Energy per km for an EV (1910)**

Cost of electricity in 1910 (USA urban): $\approx \$0.10 \text{ per kWh}$ (in 1910 dollars).

Energy consumption of a 1910 EV: $\approx 0.25 \text{ kWh/km}$.

$$
C_{EV} = E_c \times \text{Price}_{elec} = 0.25 \text{ kWh/km} \times \$0.10/\text{kWh}
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{EV} = \$0.025 \text{ per km}}
$$

**Step 2 — Cost of Energy per km for an ICE (1910)**

Petrol price in 1910 (USA): $\approx \$0.05 \text{ per litre}$.

Fuel consumption of a Ford Model T: $\approx 10 \text{ L per 100 km} = 0.1 \text{ L/km}$.

$$
C_{ICE} = \text{Fuel}_{cons} \times \text{Price}_{petrol} = 0.1 \text{ L/km} \times \$0.05/\text{L}
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{ICE} = \$0.005 \text{ per km}}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation**

$$
\frac{C_{EV}}{C_{ICE}} = \frac{\$0.025}{\$0.005} = 5
$$

> **Logical Conclusion:** In 1910, the EV was **5× more expensive to operate per km** than the Model T. Combined with the EV's **limited range (≈80–130 km)** and **lack of charging infrastructure**, the commercial collapse was inevitable. This is a classic KTU-style analytical question — the answer connects **energy cost, range, and infrastructure** rather than blaming a single factor.

**Step 4 — Connecting back to History**

The same economic relationship, in **inverse**, explains the modern EV revival:
- Lithium-ion specific energy ($E_{sp} \approx 200 \text{ Wh/kg}$) **dramatically extends range**.
- Solar/wind electricity is **cheaper per kWh** than petrol per km in most countries by 2020.
- Public charging networks (BPCL, Tata Power, Ather Grid in India) replicate the **petrol-station ubiquity** of 1920.

$$
\text{Hence, } C_{EV,2024} \leq C_{ICE,2024} \implies \text{EV commercially viable}
$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Era-by-Era Comparison

| Era | Period | Dominant Battery | Specific Energy (Wh/kg) | Typical Range (km) | Key Constraint | Outcome |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Invention | 1828 – 1899 | Non-rechargeable, then Lead-Acid | $25 - 35$ | $20 - 50$ | Battery cost | Experimental |
| 2. Golden Age | 1900 – 1920 | Lead-Acid | $30 - 40$ | $80 - 130$ | Charging network | Peak adoption |
| 3. ICE Takeover | 1920 – 1970 | Lead-Acid | $30 - 40$ | $50 - 80$ | Petrol cheap, range | Commercial exit |
| 4. Oil Crisis | 1970 – 1990 | NiCd, Early NiMH | $40 - 80$ | $60 - 100$ | Cost, weight | Niche revival |
| 5. Lithium Era | 1990 – Present | NiMH → Li-ion → Solid-State | $80 \rightarrow 250$ | $150 \rightarrow 600+$ | Infrastructure (solved) | Mass adoption |

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation: Range Equation for Historical EVs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Range Equation Used in KTU Numerical Problems**

**Step 1 — Energy stored in the battery pack**

$$
E_{stored} = V_{pack} \times C_{pack}
$$

Where $V_{pack}$ is the pack voltage (V) and $C_{pack}$ is the pack capacity (Ah).

**Step 2 — Usable energy considering Depth of Discharge (DoD)**

$$
E_{usable} = E_{stored} \times \text{DoD}
$$

For lead-acid, $\text{DoD} \approx 0.5$ to protect battery life.
For lithium-ion, $\text{DoD} \approx 0.8$ to $0.9$.

**Step 3 — Energy delivered to the wheels**

$$
E_{wheel} = E_{usable} \times \eta_{overall}
$$

Where $\eta_{overall}$ is drivetrain efficiency.
For early EVs: $\eta_{overall} \approx 0.5$ to $0.6$.
For modern EVs: $\eta_{overall} \approx 0.85$ to $0.90$.

**Step 4 — Range of the vehicle**

$$
R = \frac{E_{wheel}}{E_c}
$$

**Step 5 — Worked Example (KTU Style)**

> **Problem:** A 1910 Detroit Electric had a battery pack rated at **84 V, 100 Ah** with lead-acid chemistry. Drivetrain efficiency is **60%**, and energy consumption is **0.18 kWh/km**. Compute the range.

**Solution:**

$$
E_{stored} = V_{pack} \times C_{pack} = 84 \text{ V} \times 100 \text{ Ah} = 8400 \text{ Wh} = 8.4 \text{ kWh}
$$

$$
E_{usable} = E_{stored} \times \text{DoD} = 8.4 \times 0.5 = 4.2 \text{ kWh}
$$

$$
E_{wheel} = E_{usable} \times \eta_{overall} = 4.2 \times 0.6 = 2.52 \text{ kWh}
$$

$$
R = \frac{E_{wheel}}{E_c} = \frac{2.52 \text{ kWh}}{0.18 \text{ kWh/km}}
$$

$$
\boxed{R \approx 14 \text{ km}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Insight:** This computed value is conservative. Real-world Detroit Electric cars achieved **80–130 km** with larger battery packs (~150 V, 200 Ah) and lower consumption. The KTU examiner may ask you to **state the formula**, **identify the limiting parameter** (in this case, the DoD of lead-acid), and **comment on how lithium-ion changed the calculation**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**

**Q1. [3 Marks]** *Define an Electric Vehicle. Mention any two historical milestones in the development of EVs before 1920.*

**Model Answer:**
An Electric Vehicle (EV) is a vehicle that uses one or more electric motors for propulsion, drawing energy from an **onboard rechargeable energy storage system** such as a battery or fuel cell, instead of an internal combustion engine.

**Two historical milestones before 1920:**
1. **1832–1839** — Robert Anderson (Scotland) built one of the first non-rechargeable electric carriages.
2. **1907** — The **Detroit Electric** was produced and sold in the USA, becoming one of the most successful early electric cars with a range of approximately **130 km per charge**.

> [!TIP]
> **Mark Allocation Tip:** Definition = 1 mark, Milestone 1 = 1 mark, Milestone 2 = 1 mark.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**

**Q2. [3 Marks]** *List the three main reasons for the commercial decline of electric vehicles between 1920 and 1970.*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Mass production of ICE vehicles:** Henry Ford's Model T (1908) assembly-line production made petrol cars **cheap and affordable**.
2. **Abundant cheap petrol:** Discovery of Texas crude oil in the 1920s reduced petrol prices drastically, making ICE running costs lower than EVs.
3. **Range and infrastructure advantage:** The ICE vehicle offered longer range and quick refuelling, supported by a growing petrol-station network that EVs lacked.

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 / Model Question, Module 1]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain the five distinct eras in the historical evolution of electric vehicles. List at least one key invention or vehicle in each era.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *With the help of the standard range equation, derive the range of a 1910 electric car given the following data: Battery = 84 V, 100 Ah, Lead-Acid, Depth of Discharge = 50%, Drivetrain Efficiency = 60%, Energy Consumption = 0.18 kWh/km. Comment on how the range would change if lithium-ion cells of the same pack dimensions were used.*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Five Eras of EV Evolution**

| Era | Period | Key Innovation |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Invention | 1828 – 1899 | Ányos Jedlik's first electric motor (1828) |
| 2. Golden Age | 1900 – 1920 | Detroit Electric (1907), 130 km range |
| 3. ICE Takeover | 1920 – 1970 | Ford Model T (1908), Kettering Starter (1912) |
| 4. Oil Crisis Revival | 1970 – 1990 | NASA Lunar Rover (1971), CitiCar (1974) |
| 5. Lithium Revolution | 1990 – Present | Tesla Roadster (2008), Nissan Leaf (2010) |

*Conclusion:* The evolution of EVs is driven by **energy-density breakthroughs** in battery chemistry and **economic/policy factors** such as oil prices and emission norms.

**(b) Range Derivation**

Step 1 — Energy stored:
$$
E_{stored} = V \times C = 84 \times 100 = 8400 \text{ Wh} = 8.4 \text{ kWh}
$$

Step 2 — Usable energy (with DoD = 50%):
$$
E_{usable} = 8.4 \times 0.5 = 4.2 \text{ kWh}
$$

Step 3 — Energy at wheels:
$$
E_{wheel} = 4.2 \times 0.6 = 2.52 \text{ kWh}
$$

Step 4 — Range:
$$
R = \frac{2.52}{0.18} = 14 \text{ km}
$$

**Comment on Lithium-ion:**

Lithium-ion cells have:
- **DoD = 80 to 90%** (vs 50% for lead-acid).
- **Higher drivetrain efficiency (~85 to 90%)** (vs 60% for early EVs).
- **Same pack dimensions can store ~3–5× more energy** due to higher specific energy.

If we keep the same 84 V, 100 Ah pack, but with Li-ion chemistry:

$$
E_{stored} = 8.4 \text{ kWh}, \quad E_{usable} = 8.4 \times 0.85 = 7.14 \text{ kWh}
$$

$$
E_{wheel} = 7.14 \times 0.85 = 6.069 \text{ kWh}
$$

$$
R_{Li-ion} = \frac{6.069}{0.18} \approx 33.7 \text{ km}
$$

> **Conclusion:** Range increases from **14 km → 33.7 km**, a **~2.4× improvement** purely from chemistry change. With higher-capacity packs, modern EVs achieve **300–600 km** ranges.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Compare lead-acid and lithium-ion battery technologies in terms of specific energy, depth of discharge, cycle life, and environmental impact. Relate this comparison to the historical evolution of EVs.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Discuss the role of the 1973 OPEC oil crisis in reviving interest in electric vehicles. Mention at least three EV models that emerged during the 1970–1990 era.*

**Model Solution (Outline):**

**(a) Lead-Acid vs. Lithium-Ion Comparison Table**

| Parameter | Lead-Acid | Lithium-Ion |
|---|---|---|
| Specific Energy | $30 - 40 \text{ Wh/kg}$ | $150 - 250 \text{ Wh/kg}$ |
| Depth of Discharge | $\approx 50\%$ | $\approx 80 - 90\%$ |
| Cycle Life | $300 - 500$ cycles | $1000 - 3000$ cycles |
| Energy Efficiency | $70 - 80\%$ | $90 - 95\%$ |
| Environmental Impact | Lead is toxic; recycling is established | Less toxic; recycling infrastructure still maturing |
| Cost per kWh (2024) | $\approx \$130$ | $\approx \$115$ |

*Relation to History:* The **Gold Standard of Lead-Acid** (1900–1990) limited EV range to **80–130 km**, while the **Lithium Breakthrough (2008–Present)** enabled **400+ km** ranges, triggering mass adoption.

**(b) Role of 1973 OPEC Crisis**

The OPEC embargo caused petrol prices to **quadruple** in the USA, exposing **fuel-security vulnerabilities**. This led to:
- **US Department of Energy funding** for EV research.
- **NASA Lunar Roving Vehicle (1971)** demonstrating EV viability in space.
- **Sebring-Vanguard CitiCar (1974)** — became the 6th most-produced American car.
- **General Motors' EV1 prototype (early 1990s)** — precursor to the modern EV.

> **Conclusion:** The 1973 crisis created **policy-driven demand** for EV alternatives, but the technology was not yet ready. It took the **lithium-ion breakthrough of the 2000s** to make EVs commercially competitive.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in EV History Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing dates:** Many students write "Tesla was founded in 2003" when the **Roadster launched in 2008**. Be precise: **Tesla Motors founded 2003, Roadster released 2008, Model S 2012, Model 3 2017**.
> 2. **Ignoring Indian context:** KTU is a Kerala university — the examiner may give **bonus credit** for mentioning **Reva Electric (2001)**, **FAME-I (2015)**, **FAME-II (2019)**, or **Kerala State EV Policy (2019)**.
> 3. **Memorizing without analysis:** Do not just list events — explain the **why**. (e.g., "EVs declined because lead-acid specific energy was only 30 Wh/kg, 1/50th of petrol.")
> 4. **Skipping units:** When writing the range equation, always mention **Wh, kWh, Wh/kg, V, Ah**.
> 5. **Writing 'invention was in 1900':** The first electric motor was by Jedlik in **1828**, not 1900.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — EV History (Module 1)**

- ✅ **First electric motor:** Ányos Jedlik, **1828** (Hungary).
- ✅ **First electric carriage:** Robert Anderson, **1832–1839** (Scotland).
- ✅ **Golden Age peak:** 1900–1920; **~1/3 of US vehicles** were electric.
- ✅ **Detroit Electric (1907):** Range **~130 km**, lead-acid battery.
- ✅ **Ford Model T (1908):** Made ICE vehicles affordable and mass-produced.
- ✅ **Kettering Electric Starter (1912):** Removed the dangerous crank-start, favouring ICE.
- ✅ **OPEC Crisis (1973):** Quadrupled petrol prices, triggered EV research.
- ✅ **NASA Lunar Rover (1971):** First EV on the Moon, AC induction motor.
- ✅ **GM EV1 (1996):** First modern mass-produced BEV (NiMH, then lead-acid).
- ✅ **Tesla Roadster (2008):** First production EV with **lithium-ion cells**.
- ✅ **Specific Energy milestones:** Lead-Acid **30–40 Wh/kg** → Li-ion **150–250 Wh/kg**.
- ✅ **Indian milestones:** Reva Electric (2001), FAME-I (2015), FAME-II (2019), Kerala EV Policy (2019).
- ✅ **Range Equation:** $R = (V \times C \times \text{DoD} \times \eta_{overall}) \div E_c$.
- ✅ **Classification to remember:** BEV, HEV, PHEV, FCEV.
- ✅ **Modern context:** Li-ion price fell below **\$100/kWh** in 2023, triggering global mass adoption.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Electric Vehicles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET742 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES AND RENEWABLE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification of Electric Vehicles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for *PEAET742 - Electric Vehicles and Renewable Technologies*, the **Classification of Electric Vehicles (EVs)** refers to the systematic categorization of road-going vehicles based on the **degree of electrification of the powertrain**, the **source of tractive energy**, the **degree of reliance on the external charging infrastructure**, and the **presence/absence of an Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)** as a range extender.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> The classification forms the foundational basis for understanding EV architectures (Series, Parallel, Series-Parallel), energy storage sizing, and the role of renewable energy integration. Students must be able to **draw, label, and differentiate** the architecture of at least **BEV, HEV, PHEV, and FCEV** as per KTU valuation expectations.

The standard KTU classification framework, aligned with **SAE J1772** and **IEC 61851** standards, places every road vehicle into one of the following broad categories:

| S.No. | Category | Full Form | ICE Present? | Grid Chargeable? |
|-------|----------|-----------|--------------|------------------|
| 1 | **BEV** | Battery Electric Vehicle | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| 2 | **HEV** | Hybrid Electric Vehicle | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| 3 | **PHEV** | Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicle | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| 4 | **FCEV** | Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle | ❌ No | ❌ No (uses H₂) |
| 5 | **ICE** | Internal Combustion Engine Vehicle | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (reference baseline) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metric for KTU:**
> The **All-Electric Range (AER)** measured in **kilometers (km)** is the standard benchmark parameter used to distinguish PHEVs from HEVs. A vehicle with **AER > 10 km** and an on-board charger is classified as a PHEV under most international norms.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the EV classification like a **diet spectrum** for vehicles:

- A **BEV** is like a **strict vegan car** — it eats only electricity from the plug and refuses fossil fuels completely.
- A **HEV** is like a **flexitarian car** — it has both a petrol stomach (ICE) and an electric stomach (battery), but the battery is small and recharges only from the engine or regen braking, never from a plug.
- A **PHEV** is like a **flexitarian car with a packed lunch** — it can eat petrol *and* be plugged in for a full electric meal, giving it the best of both worlds for short and long trips.
- A **FCEV** is like a **hydrogen-breathing car** — instead of charging, it "drinks" hydrogen and exhales only water vapor, but it needs a special hydrogen refueling station instead of a plug.

This spectrum is governed by a single key parameter: **how much of the energy comes from the grid vs. fossil fuels**. The greater the grid-electricity fraction, the cleaner the vehicle (assuming a renewable-fed grid).

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Takeaway for Exams:** If a question gives you a vehicle and asks you to classify it, check three things in this order:
> 1. Does it have an **ICE**? → If *No*, it's either BEV or FCEV (check the energy source).
> 2. Does it have a **charging port**? → If *Yes*, it's a BEV or PHEV.
> 3. Does it have a **hydrogen tank + fuel cell stack**? → If *Yes*, it's an FCEV.
> 4. Default with ICE + small battery + no plug = **HEV**.

---

## 1.3 Sub-Classification of HEVs (KTU High-Priority Topic)

The HEV category is further sub-divided based on the **hybridization factor (HF)** — the ratio of electric motor power to the sum of ICE + electric motor power:

$$\text{HF} = \frac{P_{electric}}{P_{ICE} + P_{electric}}$$

| Sub-Type | Hybridization Factor (HF) | Voltage Level | Electric Drive Function |
|----------|--------------------------|---------------|--------------------------|
| **Micro Hybrid** | $\text{HF} < 0.1$ | **12 V** | Start-Stop only |
| **Mild Hybrid** | $0.1 \leq \text{HF} < 0.25$ | **48 V** | Start-Stop + Torque Assist |
| **Full Hybrid** | $0.25 \leq \text{HF} < 0.5$ | **200–500 V** | Full Electric Drive + Assist |
| **Plug-in Hybrid** | $\text{HF} \geq 0.5$ | **300–800 V** | Long AER + ICE range extender |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **48 V mild-hybrid** architecture is currently the industry sweet-spot (e.g., Mercedes-Benz EQ Boost, Audi BSG) because it stays below the **60 V DC safety threshold** defined by **IEC 60950**, allowing the use of cheaper, non-high-voltage-rated components.

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Architectures in Detail

### 2.1.1 Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV)

A **BEV** is a **purely electric** vehicle in which the **sole source of tractive energy** is a rechargeable electrochemical battery pack. The powertrain consists of:

- **Energy Source:** Li-ion / LFP / Solid-state battery pack (typical **350–800 V** architecture)
- **Energy Converter:** Traction Inverter (DC → 3-phase AC)
- **Mechanical Output:** Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor (PMSM) or Induction Motor
- **Auxiliaries:** On-board Charger (OBC), DC-DC converter (12 V), Battery Management System (BMS)

**Why it exists:** Zero tailpipe emissions, highest well-to-wheel efficiency (**~70–80%**), simplest drivetrain (single-speed transmission possible).

**How it works (operational logic):**
1. Grid AC → OBC → DC Battery (charging phase)
2. Battery DC → Inverter → 3-phase AC → Traction Motor (driving phase)
3. Motor (acting as generator) → Inverter → DC Battery (regenerative braking)

---

### 2.1.2 Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV)

A **HEV** combines an **ICE** (typically petrol/diesel) with an **electric motor/generator (M/G)** and a **small high-voltage battery** (≈ 1–2 kWh). The battery is **never grid-charged**; it is replenished exclusively through **regenerative braking** and **engine-off-coast** strategies.

**Three operational modes of any HEV:**

| Mode | Power Flow | When It Activates |
|------|------------|-------------------|
| **EV Mode** | Battery → Motor → Wheels | Low-speed cruising, parking maneuvers |
| **Hybrid Mode** | ICE + Motor → Wheels | Hard acceleration, highway climbing |
| **Regen Mode** | Wheels → Motor (gen) → Battery | Braking, deceleration |

**KTU's three architectural variants of HEV:**

1. **Series Hybrid:** ICE acts only as a **range extender (APU — Auxiliary Power Unit)**. It drives a generator that charges the battery; **no mechanical link between ICE and wheels**. Best for city duty cycles.
2. **Parallel Hybrid:** ICE and Motor are **mechanically coupled** to the wheels via a clutch/gearbox. Both can drive the wheels **simultaneously or independently**. Best for highway driving.
3. **Series-Parallel (Power-Split) Hybrid:** Uses a **planetary gear set (Toyota Hybrid System - THS)** to blend series and parallel paths. Used in Toyota Prius — the industry gold standard.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Point:**
> The **Planetary Gear Set** in a Toyota THS has three ports: **Sun gear** (MG1), **Planet carrier** (ICE), and **Ring gear** (MG2 + wheels). The Willis equation for a planetary gearset is:
>
> $$(\omega_s - \omega_c) \cdot N_s + (\omega_r - \omega_c) \cdot N_r = 0$$
>
> where $N_s$, $N_r$ are sun and ring tooth counts, and $\omega_s$, $\omega_c$, $\omega_r$ are sun, carrier, and ring angular velocities.

---

### 2.1.3 Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicle (PHEV)

A **PHEV** is essentially an **HEV + a larger battery (10–20 kWh) + an on-board charger**. It is the **bridge technology** between HEV and BEV, offering:
- **AER of 30–80 km** on pure electric mode
- Unlimited range using ICE for long trips
- Grid chargeable (Level 1 or Level 2)

The **Utility Factor (UF)** — the fraction of total distance driven on electricity — is the key KTU metric for PHEVs:

$$\text{UF} = \frac{d_{electric}}{d_{electric} + d_{ICE}}$$

A PHEV with **UF > 0.5** is considered to deliver majority of its real-world miles on electricity (e.g., Mitsubishi Outlander PHEV, BYD Song Plus DM-i).

---

### 2.1.4 Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle (FCEV)

A **FCEV** generates electricity **on-board** via a **Proton Exchange Membrane (PEM) fuel cell stack** that electrochemically combines compressed **hydrogen (H₂)** with **oxygen (O₂)** from ambient air. The only by-product is **water vapor** and heat.

**Core components:**
- **H₂ Storage Tank:** Type IV composite cylinders at **350 bar** or **700 bar**
- **Fuel Cell Stack:** Typically 80–250 kW PEMFC
- **Power Buffer Battery:** A small Li-ion pack (1–2 kWh) for transient load leveling
- **Traction Motor + Inverter:** Same as BEV

**The half-reactions in a PEMFC:**

$$\text{Anode:} \quad H_2 \rightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^-$$

$$\text{Cathode:} \quad \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2O$$

$$\text{Overall:} \quad H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O + \text{Electrical Energy} + \text{Heat}$$

The **theoretical open-circuit voltage** is **E⁰ = 1.229 V** per cell, but in practice drops to **~0.6–0.7 V** per cell under load, requiring many cells in series for a usable high-voltage stack.

---

### 2.1.5 Range-Extended Electric Vehicle (REEV) — Bonus KTU Topic

A **REEV** (also called **EREV — Extended Range EV**) is a sub-type of BEV with a small ICE generator. The ICE **never drives the wheels** mechanically — it only generates electricity to charge the battery. Examples: **BMW i3 REx, Chevrolet Volt (1st gen), Mahindra eVerito**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A REEV is **NOT** a parallel hybrid because there is no mechanical path from ICE to wheels. It is a **BEV with an on-board DC generator (APU)**.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is the **consolidated KTU 2024 formula reference** for the Classification of Electric Vehicles module. Memorize these for the Part-A 3-mark derivations.

| # | Formula / Equation | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
|---|-------------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | $F_t = F_r + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{la}$ | $F_t$ = Total tractive force (N), $F_r$ = rolling resistance, $F_{ad}$ = aerodynamic drag, $F_{hc}$ = hill climbing, $F_{la}$ = linear acceleration | **Vehicle Dynamics** — sizing motor and battery |
| 2 | $F_r = m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr} \cdot \cos(\alpha)$ | $m$ = mass (kg), $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$, $C_{rr}$ = rolling resistance coeff. (≈ 0.015 for car tires), $\alpha$ = slope angle | Tractive force on flat/graded road |
| 3 | $F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2$ | $\rho$ = air density **1.225 kg/m³**, $C_d$ = drag coefficient (≈ 0.25 for modern EV), $A$ = frontal area (m²), $v$ = velocity (m/s) | Aerodynamic drag force |
| 4 | $F_{hc} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\alpha)$ | Same as above, $\alpha$ = road gradient | Force needed to climb a slope |
| 5 | $F_{la} = m \cdot a$ | $m$ = vehicle mass, $a$ = linear acceleration (m/s²) | Force to accelerate the vehicle |
| 6 | $P_{tractive} = F_t \cdot v$ | Power in Watts, $v$ in m/s | Power demanded from motor |
| 7 | $P_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{tractive}}{\eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{transmission}}$ | $\eta$ = efficiency of each stage (0.9–0.97 typical) | Sizing the electric motor |
| 8 | $E_{battery} = \dfrac{V_{batt} \cdot C_{Ah}}{1000}$ in kWh | $V_{batt}$ in V, $C_{Ah}$ in Ah | Battery pack energy sizing |
| 9 | $\text{Range} = \dfrac{E_{battery}}{E_{consumption\_per\_km}}$ | Energy in kWh, consumption in kWh/km | Driving range estimation |
| 10 | $\text{HF} = \dfrac{P_{electric}}{P_{ICE} + P_{electric}}$ | Power in kW | **Hybridization Factor** — classifies HEV sub-type |
| 11 | $\text{UF} = \dfrac{d_{electric}}{d_{total}}$ | Distances in km | **Utility Factor** — quantifies PHEV electric usage |
| 12 | $V_{stack} = N_{cells} \cdot V_{cell}$ | $N$ = number of cells, $V_{cell}$ = voltage per cell | **FCEV** stack voltage sizing |
| 13 | $\eta_{PEMFC} = \dfrac{V_{cell}}{1.482}$ | 1.482 V is the thermoneutral voltage of H₂ | FCEV cell efficiency |
| 14 | $\text{Energy stored in H₂} = n_{H_2} \cdot \text{LHV}_{H_2}$ | $\text{LHV}_{H_2} = 33.33 \text{ kWh/kg}$ | FCEV range calculation |
| 15 | $v_{\omega} \cdot r = v_{vehicle}$ | $\omega$ = motor angular speed (rad/s), $r$ = wheel radius (m) | Motor speed to vehicle speed conversion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Trick:** Whenever a question gives a vehicle's $C_d$ and frontal area $A$, **the aerodynamic drag term $F_{ad}$ always uses SI units** — i.e., velocity in **m/s**, not km/h. Convert km/h to m/s by dividing by **3.6**.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The classification of EVs is not an academic exercise — it is the **fundamental decision matrix** used by:

- **Automotive OEMs** (e.g., Tata, Mahindra, Tesla, BYD) to position their products
- **Power Grid Operators** to forecast the additional load from charging
- **Policy Makers** to design FAME-II subsidies (India), Inflation Reduction Act credits (USA)
- **Renewable Energy Planners** to size solar/wind farms for EV charging demand

For example, if 30% of Indian vehicles become BEVs by 2030, the additional electricity demand will be approximately:

$$\Delta E_{demand} = N_{vehicles} \times \text{Avg. daily km} \times \text{Consumption (kWh/km)}$$

$$= 3 \times 10^7 \times 30 \text{ km/day} \times 0.15 \text{ kWh/km} \approx 135 \text{ GWh/day}$$

This is a **massive load** for India's grid, and is the reason KTU 2024 has dedicated modules on **Renewable Energy Integration** with EV charging (covered in Modules 3 & 4 of the syllabus).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Tractive Force Equation for a Generic EV

The total tractive force $F_t$ required at the wheel-road contact patch is the sum of all resistive forces. We derive it step by step, as expected in a KTU 14-mark question.

### Step 1: Identify all resistive forces acting on a moving vehicle

A vehicle moving on a road with a slope $\alpha$ experiences four fundamental forces:

1. **Rolling resistance** $F_r$ — due to tire deformation
2. **Aerodynamic drag** $F_{ad}$ — due to air pushing against the front face
3. **Hill climbing force** $F_{hc}$ — due to gravity component along the slope
4. **Linear acceleration force** $F_{la}$ — due to Newton's Second Law ($F = ma$)

### Step 2: Derive Rolling Resistance

The tire deforms as it rolls, creating an effective offset between the normal force $N$ and the contact patch center. The coefficient of rolling resistance $C_{rr}$ is defined experimentally as the ratio of the tangential force to the normal force. On a flat road, $N = mg$, so:

$$F_r = C_{rr} \cdot N = m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr} \quad \text{(on flat road, } \alpha = 0\text{)}$$

On a slope with angle $\alpha$, the normal force reduces to $mg \cos(\alpha)$ because gravity now has a component along the slope. Therefore:

$$F_r = m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr} \cdot \cos(\alpha)$$

### Step 3: Derive Aerodynamic Drag

The drag force is proportional to the dynamic pressure of the air, the frontal area, and a shape factor $C_d$:

$$F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2$$

**Step-by-step explanation:**
- $\rho$ = density of air (**1.225 kg/m³** at sea level, $15^\circ C$)
- $v$ = velocity of the vehicle relative to the air (in m/s)
- $A$ = frontal cross-sectional area
- $C_d$ = drag coefficient (dimensionless)

### Step 4: Derive Hill Climbing Force

When the road is inclined at angle $\alpha$ from horizontal, gravity $mg$ has two components:
- Perpendicular to slope: $mg \cos(\alpha)$ — balanced by normal force
- Parallel to slope: $mg \sin(\alpha)$ — opposes forward motion when going uphill

$$F_{hc} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\alpha)$$

### Step 5: Derive Linear Acceleration Force

By Newton's Second Law of Motion, to accelerate a mass $m$ at rate $a$ m/s² requires:

$$F_{la} = m \cdot a$$

For a rotating mass (e.g., wheels, motor rotor), an **equivalent rotational inertia factor** $\delta$ (delta) is included, where $\delta \approx 1.05$ to $1.3$ for a typical passenger EV:

$$F_{la} = m \cdot (1 + \delta) \cdot a$$

### Step 6: Combine all forces

By **D'Alembert's principle** (force balance on a particle in dynamic equilibrium):

$$F_t = F_r + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{la}$$

Substituting all derived expressions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_t &= m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr} \cdot \cos(\alpha) \\
&\quad + \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2 \\
&\quad + m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\alpha) \\
&\quad + m \cdot (1 + \delta) \cdot a
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Master Tractive Force Equation** of an EV — the single most important equation in the KTU Electric Vehicles syllabus.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Driving Range of a BEV

The range $R$ of a Battery Electric Vehicle is the **total distance the vehicle can travel on a fully charged battery** under a defined drive cycle (e.g., WLTP, NEDC, or Indian MIDC).

### Step 1: Define Battery Energy

The stored energy in a battery pack is:

$$E_{batt} = V_{nominal} \times C_{Ah} \quad \text{(in Wh)}$$

To express in kWh (the unit used on dashboards):

$$E_{batt}(\text{kWh}) = \frac{V_{nominal} \times C_{Ah}}{1000}$$

### Step 2: Define Usable Energy

The battery is **never fully discharged** to 0% to prevent damage. A typical Depth of Discharge (DoD) limit is **80%** for LFP and **90%** for NMC chemistries:

$$E_{usable} = E_{batt} \times \text{DoD}$$

### Step 3: Define Energy Consumption per km

The energy consumed per kilometer is the integral of the tractive power over distance:

$$E_{consumption} = \int_0^{R} \frac{P_{tractive}(v)}{v} \, dv$$

For a simplified **constant-velocity cruise** at speed $v_0$ on a flat road ($\alpha = 0$, $a = 0$):

$$P_{tractive} = F_r \cdot v_0 + F_{ad} \cdot v_0 = \left[ m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr} + \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v_0^2 \right] \cdot v_0$$

So the per-km energy is:

$$e_{km} = \frac{P_{tractive}}{v_0} = m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr} + \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v_0^2$$

### Step 4: Range Formula

$$R = \frac{E_{usable}}{e_{km}} = \frac{V_{nominal} \times C_{Ah} \times \text{DoD}}{1000 \times e_{km}}$$

### Step 5: Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A BEV has a **60 kWh** usable battery, mass **1800 kg**, $C_{rr} = 0.012$, $C_d = 0.27$, $A = 2.3 \, m^2$. Find the cruising range at a constant $v_0 = 90 \, km/h = 25 \, m/s$ on a flat road.

**Solution:**

**Step 5a:** Calculate rolling resistance term

$$F_r = 1800 \times 9.81 \times 0.012 = 211.9 \, N$$

**Step 5b:** Calculate aerodynamic drag term

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{ad} &= \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 0.27 \times 2.3 \times 25^2 \\
&= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.27 \times 2.3 \times 625 \\
&= 237.5 \, N
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5c:** Total tractive force

$$F_t = 211.9 + 237.5 = 449.4 \, N$$

**Step 5d:** Tractive power

$$P_{tractive} = F_t \times v_0 = 449.4 \times 25 = 11{,}235 \, W \approx 11.24 \, kW$$

**Step 5e:** Per-km energy consumption

$$e_{km} = \frac{11.24}{90} = 0.125 \, kWh/km$$

**Step 5f:** Range

$$R = \frac{60}{0.125} = 480 \, km$$

✅ **Answer: 480 km** — this matches the WLTP range of a typical mid-size BEV (e.g., Tata Nexon EV LR ≈ 465 km).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Power-Split Hybrid (Toyota THS) Kinematics

For a **planetary gear set** in a Toyota Hybrid System, the kinematic relation (Willis equation) is derived from the constraint that all gears share the same angular velocity at the mesh point.

### Step 1: Define the geometry

Let $N_s$ = number of teeth on sun gear, $N_r$ = number of teeth on ring gear. The carrier rotates with the planet centers. From gear geometry, when the carrier is fixed:

$$\frac{\omega_s}{\omega_r} = -\frac{N_r}{N_s}$$

The negative sign indicates **opposite rotation direction** of sun and ring.

### Step 2: Apply the general Willis equation

For a planetary gearset with carrier rotating at $\omega_c$, the relation becomes:

$$(\omega_s - \omega_c) \cdot N_s + (\omega_r - \omega_c) \cdot N_r = 0$$

### Step 3: Rearrange for the THS

In the THS, **MG1 is on the sun gear**, **ICE is on the carrier**, and **MG2 + wheels are on the ring gear**. Solving for the ICE speed (carrier speed):

$$\omega_{ICE} = \frac{N_s \cdot \omega_{MG1} + N_r \cdot \omega_{ring}}{N_s + N_r}$$

This is the equation that the **Hybrid Control Unit (HCU)** uses **continuously** to determine the ICE operating point that delivers the requested wheel torque at maximum efficiency.

### Step 4: Significance for KTU

- By varying $\omega_{MG1}$ (using MG1 as a generator), the system can make the ICE operate at any speed-independent of vehicle speed.
- This is called **ECVT (Electronic Continuously Variable Transmission)** — a mechanical CVT has limits, but the THS does not.

---
