topics.title: Energy Source Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET742 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES AND RENEWABLE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Energy Source Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔋 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Source Technology** in the context of Electric Vehicles (EVs) refers to the comprehensive study of on-board energy storage and generation systems that supply electrical energy to the electric drivetrain. It encompasses **electrochemical batteries** (Lead-acid, NiMH, Li-ion variants), **fuel cells** (PEMFC, SOFC), **supercapacitors/ultracapacitors**, and **hybrid energy storage systems (HESS)** that convert stored chemical/fuel energy into electrical energy for traction.

The **Battery** is defined as a transducer that converts chemical energy into electrical energy through controlled **redox (reduction-oxidation) reactions** at two electrodes separated by an electrolyte. The governing metric for EV suitability is the trade-off between **Specific Energy (Wh/kg)**, **Specific Power (W/kg)**, **Energy Density (Wh/L)**, **Cycle Life**, **Calendar Life**, and **Cost ($/kWh)**.

### 1.1.1 Primary Performance Indicators

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Nominal Voltage | $V_{nom}$ | V (Volt) | Operating potential of cell |
| Capacity | $Q$ | Ah (Ampere-hour) | Total charge stored |
| Specific Energy | $E_{sp}$ | **Wh/kg** | Range-determining parameter |
| Energy Density | $E_{den}$ | **Wh/L** | Packaging efficiency |
| Specific Power | $P_{sp}$ | **W/kg** | Acceleration capability |
| C-Rate | $C$ | $h^{-1}$ | Charge/discharge speed |
| Cycle Life | $N_{cyc}$ | cycles | Longevity |
| State of Charge | SOC | % | Residual energy indicator |
| State of Health | SOH | % | Aging indicator |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Fuel Tank" of the Future

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of an EV battery as a "refillable water tank for electrons."**
> Just as a traditional car carries petrol in a tank, an EV carries charged particles in a battery. However, instead of "filling" the tank with liquid, we *pump electrons in* during charging and *let them flow out* during driving.
> - **Energy density** = how far you can drive on a "full tank"
> - **Power density** = how fast you can accelerate (how quickly electrons flow out)
> - **Cycle life** = how many times you can refill the tank before it wears out
> - **Cost** = the price of that tank

A **fuel cell** is like a "mini power plant on wheels" — it doesn't store energy like a battery; it *generates* electricity continuously as long as hydrogen is supplied, much like a generator that runs on hydrogen instead of diesel.

A **supercapacitor** is like a "high-speed energy sprinter" — it stores very little energy but delivers/absorbs it almost instantly, perfect for capturing regenerative braking energy.

---

## 1.3 Classification of EV Energy Sources

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Syllabus Mapping — Energy Source Technology** covers the following hierarchy of storage/generation devices:

1. **Electrochemical Batteries**
   - Lead-Acid (PbA) — oldest, cheapest
   - Nickel-Cadmium (NiCd)
   - Nickel-Metal Hydride (NiMH)
   - **Lithium-Ion (Li-ion)** family: LFP, NMC, NCA, LTO
   - **Solid-State Batteries** (emerging)
2. **Fuel Cells**
   - Proton Exchange Membrane FC (PEMFC)
   - Solid Oxide FC (SOFC)
3. **Supercapacitors / Ultracapacitors**
   - Electric Double-Layer Capacitors (EDLC)
   - Pseudocapacitors
4. **Hybrid Energy Storage Systems (HESS)**
   - Battery + Supercapacitor combinations

---

## 1.4 Why Energy Source Technology is the *Backbone* of EVs

The single most important bottleneck in EV adoption is the **energy source**. The "chassis, motor, and controller" are mature technologies; what determines whether an EV can travel **500 km on a single charge, refuel in 10 minutes, and last 10 years** is *exclusively* the energy source.

The **Ragone Plot** (a log-log plot of Specific Energy vs Specific Power) is the universal yardstick used to compare all energy sources. The "ideal" device sits in the **top-right corner** — high energy *and* high power.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle of a Battery — Step-by-Step Logic

A battery consists of three functional regions:

1. **Anode (Negative Electrode)** — undergoes **oxidation** during discharge (loses electrons).
   $$\text{Anode Reaction: } M \rightarrow M^{n+} + n e^{-}$$

2. **Cathode (Positive Electrode)** — undergoes **reduction** during discharge (gains electrons).
   $$\text{Cathode Reaction: } M^{n+} + n e^{-} \rightarrow M$$

3. **Electrolyte** — ionic conductor that completes the internal circuit by transporting ions from anode to cathode, while electrons flow through the external circuit (the "useful" current).

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" behind discharge:** When you connect a load across the terminals, electrons seek the path of lower energy. They leave the anode (higher chemical potential) and travel through the external circuit to the cathode (lower chemical potential), doing electrical work along the way. The electrolyte closes the loop by shuttling ions in the *opposite* direction internally.

---

## 2.2 Critical Battery Parameters — Engineering Definitions

### 2.2.1 Capacity ($Q$)
The total amount of charge a battery can deliver, measured in **Ampere-hours (Ah)**.
$$Q = \int_{0}^{t_{end}} I(t)\, dt$$

- **Nominal Capacity** = capacity at the manufacturer's specified discharge rate (usually 0.2C, 1C, or 5C).
- **Actual Capacity** depends on temperature, discharge current, age, and SOC.

### 2.2.2 C-Rate
The rate at which a battery is charged or discharged relative to its maximum capacity. A **1C rate** on a 100 Ah battery means a current of **100 A** will fully discharge it in **1 hour**.

$$I = C \text{-rate} \times Q_{rated}$$

$$t_{discharge} = \frac{1}{C\text{-rate}} \quad \text{(hours)}$$

### 2.2.3 State of Charge (SOC)
The ratio of remaining capacity to maximum capacity, expressed as a percentage.

$$SOC(t) = SOC(t_0) - \frac{1}{Q_{max}}\int_{t_0}^{t} I_{discharge}(\tau)\, d\tau$$

- $SOC = 100\%$ → fully charged
- $SOC = 0\%$ → fully discharged (cut-off voltage reached)

### 2.2.4 Depth of Discharge (DOD)
$$DOD = 100\% - SOC$$

A battery "cycled at 80% DOD" means it is discharged to 20% SOC repeatedly.

### 2.2.5 State of Health (SOH)
$$SOH = \frac{Q_{max,current}}{Q_{max,rated}} \times 100\%$$

When $SOH < 80\%$, the battery is typically considered end-of-life for automotive applications.

### 2.2.6 Round-Trip Efficiency ($\eta_{RT}$)
$$\eta_{RT} = \frac{E_{discharged}}{E_{charged}} \times 100\%$$

For Li-ion: **85–95%**; for Lead-acid: **70–85%**.

### 2.2.7 Internal Resistance ($R_{int}$)
Causes **$I^2R$ heat losses** and voltage sag under load. The terminal voltage is:
$$V_{terminal} = V_{OCV} - I \cdot R_{int}$$

where $V_{OCV}$ is the Open-Circuit Voltage.

---

## 2.3 Fuel Cell Operational Principle

A fuel cell is an **electrochemical energy conversion device** that continuously converts hydrogen and oxygen into water, producing electricity and heat.

**PEMFC Anode:**
$$H_2 \rightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^{-}$$

**PEMFC Cathode:**
$$\tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^{-} \rightarrow H_2O$$

**Overall Cell Reaction:**
$$H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O + \text{Electrical Energy} + \text{Heat}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" behind fuel cells:** Unlike a battery, the reactants (H₂ and O₂) are *supplied externally*. As long as fuel is fed, electricity is produced. This gives fuel cells very high **specific energy** (hydrogen has ~120 MJ/kg) but lower **specific power** and challenges with refueling infrastructure.

---

## 2.4 Supercapacitor Operational Principle

A supercapacitor stores energy via **electrostatic charge separation** at the electrode-electrolyte interface (the **Electric Double Layer** — hence EDLC). There are *no* chemical reactions, so charging is near-instantaneous and cycle life can exceed **1,000,000 cycles**.

For a symmetric EDLC:
$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 A}{d}$$

where:
- $\varepsilon_r$ = relative permittivity of electrolyte
- $\varepsilon_0$ = vacuum permittivity (**8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m**)
- $A$ = effective electrode surface area (can be **1000–2000 m²/g** due to activated carbon porosity)
- $d$ = effective double-layer thickness (~**1–5 nm**)

The energy stored:
$$E_{cap} = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^2$$

The power capability:
$$P_{max} = \frac{V^2}{4 R_{ESR}}$$

where $R_{ESR}$ is the Equivalent Series Resistance.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table — it is the single most frequently tested set of equations in KTU ESE for Module 1.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = V \cdot Q$ | Energy stored in a battery | Wh |
| 2 | $E_{sp} = \dfrac{E}{m_{cell}}$ | Specific energy | **Wh/kg** |
| 3 | $P_{sp} = \dfrac{V \cdot I_{max}}{m_{cell}}$ | Specific power | **W/kg** |
| 4 | $E_{den} = \dfrac{E}{V_{cell}}$ | Energy density | **Wh/L** |
| 5 | $C\text{-rate} = \dfrac{I}{Q_{rated}}$ | Charge/discharge rate | h⁻¹ |
| 6 | $t_{discharge} = \dfrac{Q_{rated}}{I}$ | Discharge time | h |
| 7 | $SOC(t) = SOC_0 - \dfrac{1}{Q_{max}}\int I\,dt$ | State of charge | % |
| 8 | $DOD = 1 - SOC$ | Depth of discharge | % |
| 9 | $SOH = \dfrac{Q_{max,now}}{Q_{max,new}} \times 100$ | State of health | % |
| 10 | $\eta_{RT} = \dfrac{E_{out}}{E_{in}} \times 100$ | Round-trip efficiency | % |
| 11 | $V_{term} = V_{OCV} - I \cdot R_{int}$ | Terminal voltage under load | V |
| 12 | $P_{loss} = I^2 R_{int}$ | Ohmic heat loss | W |
| 13 | $E_{cap} = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^2$ | Capacitor/supercapacitor energy | J |
| 14 | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 A}{d}$ | EDLC capacitance | F |
| 15 | $V_{pack} = n_s \cdot V_{cell}$ | Pack voltage (series) | V |
| 16 | $Q_{pack} = n_p \cdot Q_{cell}$ | Pack capacity (parallel) | Ah |
| 17 | $E_{pack} = n_s n_p \cdot E_{cell}$ | Pack energy | Wh |

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute-value and "divided by" notations have been rendered with `\dfrac` or `\vert` to prevent markdown table breakage.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Range estimation** uses $E_{sp} \times m_{battery} / P_{load}$.
- **Acceleration capability** is determined by $P_{sp} \times m_{battery} / m_{vehicle}$.
- **Battery pack sizing** for a target range:
$$Q_{pack} = \frac{R \cdot E_{cons}}{V_{pack} \cdot DOD_{max} \cdot \eta_{RT}}$$

where:
- $R$ = target range (km)
- $E_{cons}$ = energy consumption (Wh/km)
- $V_{pack}$ = pack nominal voltage
- $DOD_{max}$ = maximum allowable depth of discharge
- $\eta_{RT}$ = round-trip efficiency

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A typical EV consumes **150–200 Wh/km**. A 400 km range EV therefore needs **60–80 kWh** of usable energy. With Li-ion at ~250 Wh/kg, this translates to a **240–320 kg** battery pack — the "energy tax" EVs pay compared to a 50 kg petrol tank.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📐 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Pack Energy from Cell-Level Parameters

**Problem Statement:** A battery pack consists of $n_s$ cells in series and $n_p$ cells in parallel. Derive an expression for the total pack energy in terms of the cell's nominal voltage and capacity.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Series connection effect on voltage:**
When $n_s$ cells are connected in series, the voltages add, while the capacity remains the same as a single cell.
$$V_{pack} = V_{cell,1} + V_{cell,2} + \dots + V_{cell,n_s}$$

Assuming all cells are identical:
$$V_{pack} = n_s \cdot V_{cell}$$

**Step 2 — Parallel connection effect on capacity:**
When $n_p$ cells are connected in parallel, the capacities add, while the voltage remains the same as a single cell.
$$Q_{pack} = Q_{cell,1} + Q_{cell,2} + \dots + Q_{cell,n_p}$$

Assuming identical cells:
$$Q_{pack} = n_p \cdot Q_{cell}$$

**Step 3 — Energy of a single cell:**
The energy stored in one cell is the product of its voltage and capacity.
$$E_{cell} = V_{cell} \cdot Q_{cell}$$

The unit analysis: Volts × Ampere-hours = Watt-hours.
$$[V][Ah] = [W \cdot s] \cdot [A] / [A] \cdot 3600 / 3600 \rightarrow \text{Wh}$$

**Step 4 — Total pack energy:**
The pack energy is the product of pack voltage and pack capacity.
$$E_{pack} = V_{pack} \cdot Q_{pack}$$

Substituting Steps 1 and 2:
$$E_{pack} = (n_s \cdot V_{cell}) \cdot (n_p \cdot Q_{cell})$$

Re-grouping:
$$E_{pack} = n_s \cdot n_p \cdot (V_{cell} \cdot Q_{cell})$$

Recognizing the inner product as $E_{cell}$:
$$\boxed{E_{pack} = n_s \cdot n_p \cdot E_{cell}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A pack of 100s100p (100 series × 100 parallel) has $100 \times 100 = 10{,}000$ times the energy of a single cell. This is exactly how a Tesla Model 3 pack (~75 kWh) is built from thousands of 18650/21700 cells.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Round-Trip Efficiency from Energy Losses

**Problem Statement:** Derive the round-trip efficiency of a battery in terms of charge and discharge energies, and relate it to ohmic and thermodynamic losses.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define charge and discharge energies:**
During charging, the battery receives:
$$E_{in} = \int_{0}^{t_{ch}} V_{ch}(t) \cdot I_{ch}(t)\, dt$$

During discharging, the battery delivers:
$$E_{out} = \int_{0}^{t_{dis}} V_{dis}(t) \cdot I_{dis}(t)\, dt$$

**Step 2 — Define round-trip efficiency:**
By definition:
$$\eta_{RT} = \frac{E_{out}}{E_{in}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 3 — Decompose terminal voltage:**
For charging, the terminal voltage must overcome the OCV and the ohmic drop:
$$V_{ch} = V_{OCV} + I \cdot R_{int}$$

For discharging:
$$V_{dis} = V_{OCV} - I \cdot R_{int}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into efficiency expression:**
Assume the same current magnitude $I$ and the same OCV profile $V_{OCV}(t)$ for simplicity:
$$E_{in} = \int V_{OCV} I\, dt + \int I^2 R_{int}\, dt$$
$$E_{out} = \int V_{OCV} I\, dt - \int I^2 R_{int}\, dt$$

Substituting:
$$\eta_{RT} = \frac{\int V_{OCV} I\, dt - \int I^2 R_{int}\, dt}{\int V_{OCV} I\, dt + \int I^2 R_{int}\, dt}$$

**Step 5 — Define the Coulombic and Voltage components:**
Let $E_{ideal} = \int V_{OCV} I\, dt$ and $E_{loss} = \int I^2 R_{int}\, dt$.

$$\boxed{\eta_{RT} = \frac{E_{ideal} - E_{loss}}{E_{ideal} + E_{loss}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** If we further decompose, $E_{loss}$ contains both **ohmic loss** ($I^2 R_{int}$) and **overpotential loss** (kinetic + concentration polarization). The Coulombic efficiency $\eta_C$ (ratio of discharged to charged charge) and voltage efficiency $\eta_V$ (ratio of average discharge to charge voltage) multiply to give the round-trip efficiency: $\eta_{RT} = \eta_C \times \eta_V$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Specific Energy of a Pack Including Packaging Overhead

**Problem Statement:** Cells have a *theoretical* specific energy, but a real pack includes packaging, BMS, and thermal management mass. Derive the *effective* pack-level specific energy.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define cell-level specific energy:**
$$E_{sp,cell} = \frac{E_{cell}}{m_{cell}}$$

**Step 2 — Pack energy stays the same:**
$$E_{pack} = N_{total} \cdot E_{cell}$$
where $N_{total} = n_s \cdot n_p$ is the total number of cells.

**Step 3 — Pack mass includes cells *and* packaging:**
$$m_{pack} = N_{total} \cdot m_{cell} + m_{packaging}$$
$$m_{pack} = N_{total} \cdot m_{cell} + \alpha \cdot (N_{total} \cdot m_{cell})$$
where $\alpha$ is the **packaging mass ratio** (typically **0.20–0.35** for modern EVs).

**Step 4 — Pack-level specific energy:**
$$E_{sp,pack} = \frac{N_{total} \cdot E_{cell}}{N_{total} \cdot m_{cell} (1+\alpha)}$$

Cancel $N_{total}$:
$$E_{sp,pack} = \frac{E_{cell}}{m_{cell}(1+\alpha)}$$

Recognizing the cell-level specific energy:
$$\boxed{E_{sp,pack} = \frac{E_{sp,cell}}{1+\alpha}}$$

**Step 5 — Worked numerical example:**
Suppose $E_{sp,cell} = 250$ Wh/kg and $\alpha = 0.30$ (30% packaging overhead).
$$E_{sp,pack} = \frac{250}{1 + 0.30} = \frac{250}{1.30}$$
$$E_{sp,pack} = 192.3 \text{ Wh/kg}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A cell with 250 Wh/kg delivers only ~192 Wh/kg at the pack level — a **23% loss** purely due to structural and BMS mass. This is why *cell-level* claims from manufacturers (e.g., "350 Wh/kg cells") should always be discounted for real-vehicle performance.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Cell Sizing for a Target Vehicle Range

**Problem Statement:** An EV consumes 160 Wh/km. The battery pack has a nominal voltage of 400 V and maximum usable DOD of 80%. Round-trip efficiency is 90%. Calculate the required pack capacity (in kWh and Ah) to achieve a 320 km range.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Energy consumed over the target range:**
$$E_{consumed} = 160 \text{ Wh/km} \times 320 \text{ km} = 51{,}200 \text{ Wh} = 51.2 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 2 — Account for round-trip efficiency:**
The energy that must be *drawn from the battery* is more than what reaches the wheels because of drivetrain + battery losses. For a 90% efficient system, to deliver 51.2 kWh to the wheels, the battery must supply:
$$E_{battery,out} = \frac{51.2}{0.90} = 56.89 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 3 — Account for usable DOD:**
The battery cannot be fully discharged; only 80% of its *nameplate* energy is usable. Therefore, the *nameplate* pack energy must be:
$$E_{pack,nameplate} = \frac{56.89}{0.80} = 71.11 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Ah using pack voltage:**
$$Q_{pack} = \frac{E_{pack,nameplate}}{V_{pack}} = \frac{71{,}110 \text{ Wh}}{400 \text{ V}}$$

$$Q_{pack} = 177.78 \text{ Ah}$$

**Step 5 — Final boxed answer:**
$$\boxed{Q_{pack} \approx 178 \text{ Ah}, \quad E_{pack,nameplate} \approx 71.1 \text{ kWh}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always show the *units at every step* in derivations. The examiner awards partial marks for unit consistency, even if a numerical error is made later. Also, the *order* of dividing by $\eta_{RT}$ and $DOD_{max}$ does not change the final result because both are multiplicative losses.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Energy Sources

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the most frequently tested content in KTU ESE Part B (14-mark) questions for Energy Source Technology.** Memorize both the *values* and the *relative ordering*.

| Parameter | Lead-Acid | NiMH | Li-ion (NMC) | Li-ion (LFP) | Solid-State (Future) | PEMFC | Supercapacitor |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Specific Energy (Wh/kg)** | 30–50 | 60–120 | 150–250 | 90–160 | 300–500 | 500–1000 (system) | 5–15 |
| **Specific Power (W/kg)** | 75–150 | 250–1000 | 500–2000 | 500–2000 | 800–3000 | 100–500 | 5000–15000 |
| **Cycle Life (cycles)** | 300–800 | 500–2000 | 1000–3000 | 2000–5000 | >5000 (projected) | N/A (stack) | 500000–1000000 |
| **Nominal Cell Voltage (V)** | 2.0 | 1.2 | 3.6–3.7 | 3.2 | 3.6–3.7 | ~0.6–0.7 (cell) | 2.5–2.85 |
| **Round-Trip Efficiency** | 70–85% | 70–80% | 90–95% | 90–95% | 90–95% | 40–60% | 95–98% |
| **Operating Temp (°C)** | -20 to 60 | -20 to 60 | -20 to 60 | -20 to 60 | -20 to 80 | 60–80 (PEM) | -40 to 65 |
| **Cost ($/kWh)** | 100–150 | 250–350 | 120–200 | 100–150 | 200–400 (est.) | 50–100 (system) | 1000–5000 |
| **EV Suitability** | Auxiliary 12V | Toyota Prius (older) | Tesla, Hyundai, BMW | BYD, Chinese OEMs | 2025+ vehicles | Trucks, Buses | Regen braking assist |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Energy Source Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[EV Energy Source Technology] --> B[Electrochemical Batteries]
    A --> C[Fuel Cells]
    A --> D[Supercapacitors]
    A --> E[Hybrid Energy Storage HESS]

    B --> B1[Lead Acid PbA]
    B --> B2[Nickel Based]
    B --> B3[Lithium Ion Family]
    B --> B4[Solid State Future]

    B2 --> B2a[NiCd]
    B2 --> B2b[NiMH]

    B3 --> B3a[LFP LiFePO4]
    B3 --> B3b[NMC LiNiMnCoO2]
    B3 --> B3c[NCA LiNiCoAlO2]
    B3 --> B3d[LTO Li4Ti5O12]

    C --> C1[PEMFC Proton Exchange]
    C --> C2[SOFC Solid Oxide]

    D --> D1[EDLC Electric Double Layer]
    D --> D2[Pseudocapacitor]

    E --> E1[Battery Plus Supercapacitor]
    E --> E2[Fuel Cell Plus Battery]

```

## 4.2 Battery Discharge Operation Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Chemical Energy Stored] --> B[Anode Oxidation]
    B --> C[Electrons Released]
    C --> D[External Circuit]
    D --> E[Load Motor and Electronics]
    E --> F[Work Output]
    B --> G[Ion Migration in Electrolyte]
    G --> H[Cathode Reduction]
    H --> D
    D --> I[Heat Loss I Squared R]
    I --> J[Thermal Management]
```

## 4.3 Battery Pack Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PACK[High Voltage Battery Pack]
        direction TB
        M1[Module 1: ns1 x np1 cells] --> M2[Module 2: ns2 x np2 cells]
        M2 --> M3[Module 3: ns3 x np3 cells]
        M3 --> M4[Module n: nsn x npn cells]
    end

    BMS[Battery Management System BMS] --> PACK
    TMS[Thermal Management System] --> PACK
    PACK --> INV[Power Inverter]
    INV --> MTR[Electric Traction Motor]
    MTR --> WH[Wheels]
```

## 4.4 Ragone Plot Conceptual Map (Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HIGH_E[High Specific Energy Region]
        A1[Fuel Cell H2 System] --> A2[Gasoline Engine Reference]
        A2 --> A3[Li-ion NMC]
        A3 --> A4[Li-ion LFP]
    end

    subgraph BALANCED[Balanced Region]
        B1[Li-ion NMC High Power] --> B2[NiMH]
        B2 --> B3[Advanced Lead Acid]
    end

    subgraph HIGH_P[High Specific Power Region]
        C1[Li-ion LTO] --> C2[Supercapacitor EDLC]
        C2 --> C3[Conventional Capacitor]
    end

    HIGH_E --> BALANCED
    BALANCED --> HIGH_P
```

## 4.5 Hybrid Energy Storage System (HESS) Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Regenerative Braking Energy Capture] --> B[DC DC Converter]
    B --> C{Energy Management Controller}
    C --> D[Supercapacitor Bank]
    C --> E[Main Battery Pack]
    D --> F[DC Bus]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Vehicle Traction Inverter]
    G --> H[Electric Motor]
    H --> I[Wheels]
    J[Grid Charger] --> E
    K[Fuel Cell Stack] --> E
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "State of Health (SOH)" of a battery. How is it different from State of Charge (SOC)?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** **State of Health (SOH)** is defined as the ratio of the *current maximum deliverable capacity* of an aged battery to its *rated capacity* when new, expressed as a percentage:
$$SOH = \frac{Q_{max,current}}{Q_{max,rated}} \times 100\%$$
- **(1 mark)** **State of Charge (SOC)**, in contrast, indicates the *instantaneous* remaining charge relative to the *current* maximum capacity:
$$SOC = \frac{Q_{remaining}}{Q_{max,current}} \times 100\%$$
- **(1 mark)** **Key Difference:** SOC is a *real-time* variable that changes during use (0% to 100% in a single cycle), whereas SOH is a *slowly evolving* indicator of degradation and only decreases over the battery's lifetime. An end-of-life criterion is typically $SOH < 80\%$.

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*List any three advantages and two disadvantages of Lithium-ion batteries over Lead-acid batteries for EV applications.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| | **Lithium-ion** | **Lead-Acid** |
|---|---|---|
| Advantages | (i) Higher specific energy 150–250 Wh/kg | 30–50 Wh/kg |
| | (ii) Higher cycle life 1000–3000 | 300–800 |
| | (iii) Higher round-trip efficiency 90–95% | 70–85% |
| Disadvantages | (i) Higher upfront cost (120–200 $/kWh) | (i) Low cost 100–150 $/kWh |
| | (ii) Requires sophisticated BMS for safety | (ii) Robust and tolerant to abuse |

**Three advantages of Li-ion (any 3, 1 mark each):**
1. **Higher specific energy** → longer driving range for the same battery mass.
2. **Higher cycle life** → lower total cost of ownership over the vehicle's life.
3. **Higher round-trip efficiency** → less energy wasted as heat during charge/discharge.

**Two disadvantages (1 mark for both):**
1. Higher initial cost and requirement for advanced **Battery Management Systems (BMS)** to prevent thermal runaway.
2. More sensitive to overcharge, over-discharge, and high temperatures; requires active thermal management.

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working principle of a Lithium-ion battery with suitable reactions at both electrodes. Discuss the role of the SEI (Solid Electrolyte Interphase) layer.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A battery pack has the following specifications: 96 cells in series and 4 cells in parallel (96s4p). Each cell has a nominal voltage of 3.7 V and a capacity of 50 Ah. Calculate: (i) Pack voltage, (ii) Pack capacity, (iii) Pack energy, (iv) Specific energy if the total pack mass is 380 kg, (v) Range of the vehicle if energy consumption is 150 Wh/km.

---

#### **Solution to (a) — Working Principle of Li-ion Battery [7 marks]**

**Discharge Operation (1 mark):**
During discharge, lithium ions **de-intercalate** from the **graphite anode** and travel through the **organic liquid electrolyte** (typically LiPF₆ in EC/DMC) and the **porous polymer separator** to intercalate into the **cathode** (e.g., LiCoO₂, NMC, LFP). Electrons flow through the external circuit to balance the charge, doing useful electrical work.

**Charge Operation (1 mark):**
During charging, an external power source forces electrons back, and Li⁺ ions migrate from the cathode back to the anode, re-intercalating into the graphite layers.

**Electrode Reactions (2 marks):**

At the **Graphite Anode** during discharge:
$$Li_xC_6 \rightarrow x Li^+ + 6C + x e^{-}$$

At the **Cathode (e.g., LiCoO₂)** during discharge:
$$Li_{1-x}CoO_2 + x Li^+ + x e^{-} \rightarrow LiCoO_2$$

**Overall Cell Reaction (1 mark):**
$$Li_xC_6 + Li_{1-x}CoO_2 \rightarrow 6C + LiCoO_2$$

**Role of SEI Layer (2 marks):**
The **Solid Electrolyte Interphase (SEI)** is a passivation layer that forms on the anode surface during the very first charge cycle, due to reductive decomposition of the electrolyte. Its functions are:
1. **Ionic conductor:** Allows Li⁺ ions to pass through but blocks electrons, enabling continued electrochemical activity.
2. **Protective barrier:** Prevents further electrolyte decomposition, stabilizing the anode/electrolyte interface.
3. **Cycle life determinant:** A stable SEI is critical; an unstable or continuously growing SEI consumes cyclable lithium (loss of capacity) and increases impedance (loss of power).

---

#### **Solution to (b) — Battery Pack Calculation [7 marks]**

**Given:**
- $n_s = 96$, $n_p = 4$
- $V_{cell} = 3.7$ V
- $Q_{cell} = 50$ Ah
- $m_{pack} = 380$ kg
- $E_{cons} = 150$ Wh/km

**(i) Pack Voltage [1 mark]:**
$$V_{pack} = n_s \cdot V_{cell} = 96 \times 3.7$$
$$\boxed{V_{pack} = 355.2 \text{ V}}$$

**(ii) Pack Capacity [1 mark]:**
$$Q_{pack} = n_p \cdot Q_{cell} = 4 \times 50$$
$$\boxed{Q_{pack} = 200 \text{ Ah}}$$

**(iii) Pack Energy [1 mark]:**
$$E_{pack} = V_{pack} \cdot Q_{pack} = 355.2 \times 200$$
$$\boxed{E_{pack} = 71{,}040 \text{ Wh} = 71.04 \text{ kWh}}$$

**Verification using $n_s n_p$ formula:**
$$E_{pack} = n_s \cdot n_p \cdot E_{cell} = 96 \times 4 \times (3.7 \times 50) = 384 \times 185 = 71{,}040 \text{ Wh} \checkmark$$

**(iv) Specific Energy [2 marks]:**
$$E_{sp,pack} = \frac{E_{pack}}{m_{pack}} = \frac{71{,}040}{380}$$
$$\boxed{E_{sp,pack} = 186.95 \text{ Wh/kg}}$$

**(v) Vehicle Range [2 marks]:**
$$R = \frac{E_{pack}}{E_{cons}} = \frac{71{,}040}{150}$$
$$\boxed{R = 473.6 \text{ km}}$$

---

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **Hybrid Energy Storage System (HESS)** combining a battery and a supercapacitor. Why is this combination preferred over a battery alone for capturing regenerative braking energy?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare Lead-acid, NiMH, and Li-ion (NMC) batteries based on: (i) Specific energy, (ii) Cycle life, (iii) Cost, (iv) Safety, (v) Memory effect, (vi) Self-discharge, (vii) Environmental impact. State one typical EV application for each.

---

#### **Solution to (a) — HESS Architecture [7 marks]**

**Block Diagram Description (2 marks):**
The HESS consists of:
1. A **main battery pack** (high specific energy, e.g., Li-ion).
2. A **supercapacitor bank** (high specific power, e.g., EDLC).
3. A **DC-DC converter** connecting the supercapacitor to the DC bus (bidirectional buck-boost type).
4. An **Energy Management Controller (EMC)** that decides how to split power demand between the two sources in real time.
5. The **DC bus** feeds the traction inverter and motor.

**Operating Modes (3 marks):**
- **Hard acceleration / high power demand:** The EMC routes peak power primarily from the supercapacitor (which can deliver 10–100× its rated energy per second), protecting the battery from high C-rate stress and reducing capacity fade.
- **Cruising / steady power:** The battery alone supplies the moderate continuous power, while the supercapacitor rests or recharges.
- **Regenerative braking:** The supercapacitor absorbs the high-power, short-duration braking pulse (charging at 10–50C), which a battery alone cannot accept without severe degradation.

**Why HESS is preferred for regenerative braking (2 marks):**
1. **Batteries cannot accept high C-rate charging.** A 1C charge over many minutes is fine, but a 5–10C pulse for a few seconds (typical of regen events) causes lithium plating, SEI growth, and accelerated aging.
2. **Supercapacitors excel at high-power pulses** (charge/discharge in seconds) with >1,000,000 cycle life, making them ideal for capturing what would otherwise be lost braking energy.
3. **Net result:** HESS extends battery life by 30–50%, improves vehicle efficiency by 10–20%, and enables more aggressive regenerative braking.

---

#### **Solution to (b) — Comparative Study [7 marks]**

| Parameter | Lead-Acid (PbA) | NiMH | Li-ion (NMC) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Specific Energy** | 30–50 Wh/kg | 60–120 Wh/kg | 150–250 Wh/kg |
| **(ii) Cycle Life** | 300–800 | 500–2000 | 1000–3000 |
| **(iii) Cost ($/kWh)** | 100–150 | 250–350 | 120–200 |
| **(iv) Safety** | Robust; can tolerate abuse; no thermal runaway | Moderate; can vent at high temp | Sensitive; thermal runaway risk; needs BMS |
| **(v) Memory Effect** | None | Slight (modern NiMH minimal) | None |
| **(vi) Self-Discharge** | 4–6%/month | 20–30%/month | 1–2%/month |
| **(vii) Environment** | Lead is toxic; recycling mandatory | Nickel is recoverable | Cobalt mining concerns; recycling evolving |
| **Typical EV Application** | Auxiliary 12V battery in EVs (lights, ECU) | Toyota Prius (1st–2nd gen, 1997–2003) | Tesla Model 3/Y, Hyundai Kona, BMW i3 |

> **[1 mark per comparison row: 7 rows × 1 mark = 7 marks]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit errors:** Students frequently write $Q$ in Coulombs (C) when the question asks for Ampere-hours. Remember: $1$ Ah $= 3600$ C.
> 2. **Series vs Parallel confusion:** In a pack, *series* increases **voltage**, *parallel* increases **capacity**. Mixing these up loses full marks in part (b) numericals.
> 3. **DOD vs SOC confusion:** When a question says "80% DOD," students often compute $SOC = 80\%$. The correct relation is $SOC = 100\% - DOD = 20\%$.
> 4. **Forgetting efficiency in range problems:** Energy consumption at the wheel is *less* than the energy drawn from the battery. Divide by $\eta_{RT}$ before computing range.
> 5. **Treating supercapacitors as "batteries":** In Part B questions on HESS, students often write that supercapacitors "store chemical energy." They store energy *electro statically* (no chemical reaction) — this distinction is worth 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Cycle life interpretation:** A "cycle" is one full charge + discharge. A 50% DOD cycle counts as 0.5 cycles for SOH purposes in many models — the examiner expects the more rigorous definition.
> 7. **Fuel cell efficiency confusion:** The electrical efficiency of a PEMFC is 40–60% (not 90%+); students sometimes confuse this with battery round-trip efficiency.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — Energy Source Technology**

- ✅ **Energy** stored in a cell: $E = V \cdot Q$ (Wh); **Pack energy**: $E_{pack} = n_s n_p E_{cell}$.
- ✅ **Specific energy** (Wh/kg) determines **range**; **specific power** (W/kg) determines **acceleration**.
- ✅ **C-rate** = $I / Q_{rated}$; **discharge time** = $1 / C\text{-rate}$.
- ✅ **SOC** is instantaneous (0–100% per cycle); **SOH** is lifetime (<80% = end-of-life).
- ✅ **Lead-acid** is cheapest but heaviest; **Li-ion NMC** is the modern EV default; **LFP** is safer but lower energy; **solid-state** is the future.
- ✅ **Supercapacitors** store energy electrostatically (no chemical reaction); $E = \tfrac{1}{2}CV^2$; >1,000,000 cycles.
- ✅ **Fuel cells** (PEMFC) are *generators*, not *storage*; $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O + \text{Electricity}$.
- ✅ **HESS** combines battery (energy) + supercapacitor (power) to extend battery life and improve regen.
- ✅ **Ragone Plot** = log-log of specific energy vs specific power; fuel cells top-right, capacitors bottom-right.
- ✅ **Packaging overhead** reduces cell-level specific energy by **20–35%** in real vehicles.
- ✅ **SEI layer** in Li-ion is critical for stability but consumes cyclable Li when it grows.
- ✅ **Internal resistance** $R_{int}$ causes $I^2R$ losses; affects both efficiency and thermal management.
- ✅ **Round-trip efficiency** of Li-ion = 90–95%; Lead-acid = 70–85%; this directly impacts real-world range.
- ✅ **Tesla Model 3 pack** is approximately 96s46p of 21700 cells → ~355 V nominal, ~75 kWh.

> **🎯 Quick Memory Aid — The 5 Pillars of EV Energy Source Selection:**
> 1. **Range** → Specific Energy (Wh/kg)
> 2. **Performance** → Specific Power (W/kg)
> 3. **Durability** → Cycle Life + Calendar Life
> 4. **Economy** → Cost per kWh delivered over life
> 5. **Safety** → Thermal runaway onset temperature, dendrite formation, BMS complexity

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Battery Charging Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET742 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES AND RENEWABLE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Battery Charging Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Battery Charging Technology?

**Battery Charging Technology** refers to the engineered combination of power-electronic hardware, control algorithms, communication protocols, and thermal-management strategies used to deliver electrical energy from a source (grid, charger, or another storage element) into a rechargeable battery in a **safe, efficient, fast, and longevity-preserving** manner.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (PEAET742), battery charging technology is studied as a **sub-system of the Electric Vehicle (EV) powertrain**, sitting between the Energy Source (utility grid, solar PV) and the Energy Storage System (Li-ion / Lead-acid / NiMH battery pack).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (verbatim spirit):**
> *Battery charging technology deals with the various methods of charging rechargeable batteries used in electric vehicles, the chargers, charging infrastructure, charging levels (Level 1, 2, 3), standards (SAE, IEC, CHAdeMO, CCS), and the influence of charging profile on battery life and thermal behavior.*

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "Filling a Water Tank the Smart Way"

Imagine a **sponge-like water tank** (the battery). If you blast a fire-hose at it (high current), water splashes out (electrolyte degradation, heat, lithium plating). If you trickle water (very low current), it takes forever. The "smart" approach is:

| Stage | Analogy | Engineering Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Gentle initial flow | Tapering a dry sponge | **Pre-charge / Trickle stage** |
| Strong steady flow | Tank absorbing steadily | **Constant Current (CC) stage** |
| Slow topping off | Saturating the last 20% | **Constant Voltage (CV) stage** |
| Maintenance drip | Anti-evaporation drip | **Float / Trickle maintenance** |
| Fast refuel | Pressure pump from large pipe | **DC Fast Charging (Level 3)** |

This "smart filling" is exactly what **CC-CV (Constant Current – Constant Voltage)** charging does — and it is the **gold standard** for Li-ion batteries used in EVs.

---

## 1.3 Why Battery Charging Technology Matters for EVs

> [!NOTE]
> **Three engineering truths every KTU student must internalize:**
> 1. The **charging time** of an EV is the single biggest consumer-acceptance barrier (Range Anxiety is its mirror twin).
> 2. The **charging profile** directly determines battery calendar life — a poorly designed profile can halve cycle life.
> 3. The **charging infrastructure** (Level 1/2/3, AC vs DC, plug types) determines utility-grid impact, cost, and standardization.

### 1.3.1 Key Physical & Performance Metrics (must-know bolded terms)

- **State of Charge (SOC)** — present capacity / rated capacity (in %, 0–100 %).
- **Depth of Discharge (DOD)** — 1 − SOC (%).
- **C-rate** — current expressed as a multiple of rated capacity (e.g., **1C** on a 50 Ah battery = 50 A).
- **State of Health (SOH)** — present max capacity / original rated capacity (%).
- **Energy Efficiency (η_E)** — energy out / energy in × 100 %.
- **Coulombic Efficiency (η_C)** — charge out (Ah) / charge in (Ah) × 100 %.
- **Specific Energy (Wh/kg)** and **Energy Density (Wh/L)**.
- **Charging Time (t_c)** — total time to go from initial SOC to target SOC.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Question Trigger:** *"Distinguish between Coulombic Efficiency and Energy Efficiency"* — Note that coulombic efficiency ignores voltage, while energy efficiency accounts for the difference between charge voltage and discharge voltage.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Charging Technologies (Bird's-Eye View)

1. **By Current Profile** — Constant Current (CC), Constant Voltage (CV), CC-CV, Pulse, Trickle, Reflex (negative pulse).
2. **By Power Level (SAE J1772 / IEC 61851)** — Level 1 (AC, ≤ 1.92 kW), Level 2 (AC, ≤ 19.2 kW), Level 3 (DC Fast, 50–350 kW).
3. **By Location** — On-board charger (OBC) vs Off-board (DC fast) charger.
4. **By Conduction Type** — Conductive (plug-in) vs Inductive (wireless).
5. **By Direction of Energy Flow** — Unidirectional (G2V) vs Bidirectional (V2G, V2H, V2V).

> [!NOTE]
> **Bidirectional charging (V2G — Vehicle-to-Grid)** is a *high-weightage KTU topic* — it enables the EV battery to feed stored energy back to the grid, acting as a Distributed Energy Resource (DER). It is governed by standards like **ISO 15118**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Battery Charger System (EV context)

A modern EV charger is **not just a transformer + rectifier**. It is a closed-loop power-electronic system with the following functional blocks:

1. **AC Supply / Grid Interface** — single-phase (Level 1/2 home) or three-phase (commercial AC).
2. **EMI Filter + Surge Protection** — mandatory to meet IEC 61000.
3. **PFC (Power Factor Correction) Stage** — boost-type active PFC to maintain PF > 0.95.
4. **Isolated DC-DC Converter** — LLC resonant, full-bridge, or dual-active-bridge (DAB) topology.
5. **Output Rectifier / Filter** — supplies the controlled DC to the battery.
6. **Battery Management System (BMS) Interface** — CAN / LIN communication.
7. **Thermal Management Subsystem** — liquid cooling for >50 kW fast chargers.
8. **Controller (DSP / MCU)** — runs CC-CV algorithm, SOC estimation, fault handling.
9. **HMI + Communication Module** — display, RFID, OCPP backhaul to the central system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Off-board DC fast chargers (Level 3)** typically have a **rated DC output of 50 kW (CHAdeMO v1), 150 kW (CCS2), 250 kW (Tesla V3 Supercharger), and up to 350–400 kW (CCS2 HPC)**. The on-board charger (OBC) is limited in size and weight and is usually 6.6 kW (single-phase) or 11–22 kW (three-phase).

---

## 2.2 The Four Universal Stages of Battery Charging

For nearly all rechargeable chemistries, charging follows a **staged profile**:

### Stage 1 — Pre-charge / Recovery Stage
- Activated when **V_cell < V_min_threshold** (deeply discharged cells).
- A very small current (typically 0.01C–0.05C) is forced to safely "wake up" the cell.
- Prevents copper dissolution and dendrite formation.

### Stage 2 — Constant Current (Bulk) Stage
- The current is held constant at a pre-set value (e.g., 0.5C, 1C, 2C).
- Voltage rises **linearly** (approximately) with time.
- This stage delivers **~70–80 %** of the total energy.
- Most lithium plating risk occurs here at high C-rates and low temperatures.

### Stage 3 — Constant Voltage (Saturation / Absorption) Stage
- Voltage is held at the **maximum permitted value** (e.g., 4.2 V/cell for NMC Li-ion).
- Current **decays exponentially** following a **first-order RC behavior**.
- Delivers the remaining **20–30 %** of energy.
- This stage is **slow** — it dominates charging time beyond 80 % SOC.

### Stage 4 — Float / Trickle Maintenance
- For lead-acid and NiMH only — Li-ion **does not require** floating.
- Maintains full charge by compensating self-discharge.
- For Li-ion, the charger simply **terminates** charging when I drops below **C/20** (or 3 % of rated capacity).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** *"Pre-charge protects, Bulk fills, Absorption tops, Float maintains."*

---

## 2.3 Charging Methods — Detailed Taxonomy

### 2.3.1 Constant Current (CC) Charging
- Simple implementation.
- Used in **NiCd and NiMH** chemistries (where it is the primary method).
- **Drawback:** Risk of overcharge near full SOC if current is not terminated.

### 2.3.2 Constant Voltage (CV) Charging
- Used in **Lead-Acid** batteries as the primary profile.
- **Drawback:** Very high initial inrush current if battery is deeply discharged — can damage the battery and the charger.

### 2.3.3 Constant Current – Constant Voltage (CC-CV) Charging
- The **industry standard for Li-ion** (NMC, LFP, NCA).
- Combined profile — bulk stage at fixed current, then absorption at fixed voltage.
- Termination at **C/20 cutoff** or via BMS-monitored SOC.

### 2.3.4 Pulse Charging
- Pulses of current separated by rest periods.
- Allows **electrolyte relaxation**, reduces gas evolution and polarization.
- **Reflex / Negative Pulse Charging** — adds a brief discharge pulse to depolarize the cell further — increases cycle life by 20–30 % in lab studies.

### 2.3.5 Trickle Charging
- Very low current (C/20 to C/100) to compensate self-discharge.
- Used for **standby applications** (UPS, emergency lighting).

### 2.3.6 Fast Charging
- C-rate > 1C (typically 2C–6C for automotive Li-ion).
- Requires **active thermal management** and a sophisticated BMS.
- Causes accelerated **SEI (Solid Electrolyte Interphase) growth** and **lithium plating** at the anode.

### 2.3.7 Inductive (Wireless) Charging
- Uses **resonant inductive coupling** (85–205 kHz typical).
- SAE J2954 standard — power levels 3.7 kW (WPT1), 7.7 kW (WPT2), 11 kW (WPT3), 22 kW (WPT4).
- Efficiency 85–93 % (at coil gap ~150 mm).

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All symbols, units, and constraints below are exam-locked. Memorize the table verbatim.**

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I = C_{\text{rated}} \times C\text{-rate}$ | Charging current from C-rate | A |
| 2 | $t_{c} = \dfrac{\Delta SOC \cdot C_{\text{rated}}}{I_{\text{avg}} \cdot \eta_{C}}$ | Charging time estimate | hours |
| 3 | $E_{\text{stored}} = V_{\text{nom}} \cdot C_{\text{rated}} \cdot \eta_{C}$ | Energy delivered to battery | Wh |
| 4 | $P_{\text{charger}} = V_{\text{out}} \cdot I_{\text{out}}$ | Instantaneous charger power | W |
| 5 | $E_{\text{input}} = \int_{0}^{t_{c}} V(t) \cdot I(t) \, dt$ | Total energy drawn from grid | Wh |
| 6 | $\eta_{C} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{out}}}{Q_{\text{in}}} = \dfrac{\int I_{\text{dis}} \, dt}{\int I_{\text{ch}} \, dt}$ | Coulombic efficiency | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 7 | $\eta_{E} = \dfrac{E_{\text{dis}}}{E_{\text{ch}}} = \dfrac{\int V_{\text{dis}} I_{\text{dis}} \, dt}{\int V_{\text{ch}} I_{\text{ch}} \, dt}$ | Energy efficiency | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 8 | $Q_{\text{heat}} = I^{2} \cdot R_{\text{int}}$ | Joule heat per cell (steady) | W |
| 9 | $t_{\text{discharge,actual}} = t_{\text{rated}} \left( \dfrac{C_{\text{rated}}}{I} \right)^{n-1}$ | Peukert's law (Lead-acid) | hours |
| 10 | $V_{\text{cell,eq}} = E_{0} + IR_{\text{int}} + K \cdot \dfrac{Q}{Q - \int i \, dt}$ | Shephard / equivalent circuit model | V |
| 11 | $P_{\text{fast}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{bat}} \cdot \Delta SOC}{t_{\text{ch}} \cdot \eta_{\text{ch}}}$ | Required fast-charger power | kW |
| 12 | $C_{\text{plating}} \propto I_{c} \cdot e^{-\frac{E_{a}}{RT}}$ | Lithium-plating tendency (qualitative) | A/m² |
| 13 | $\text{Cost/km} = \dfrac{\text{Cost per kWh}}{\text{Wh/km} \cdot \eta_{\text{ch}} \cdot \eta_{\text{drv}}}$ | Operating cost of EV | ₹/km |
| 14 | $f_{\text{inductive}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L_{p} C_{p}}}$ | Wireless charger resonant frequency | Hz |

> **Where:**
> - $C_{\text{rated}}$ — rated capacity (Ah)
> - $I_{c}$ — charging current density (A/m²)
> - $E_{a}$ — activation energy for Li-plating (~ 30–60 kJ/mol)
> - $n$ — Peukert exponent (1.1–1.3 for good lead-acid)
> - $E_{0}$ — open-circuit EMF of the cell

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

| Field | Use of Battery Charging Tech |
|---|---|
| **EV Automotive** | AC Level 2 home chargers (7 kW), DC fast (50–350 kW), V2G |
| **Renewable Integration** | Solar PV → Battery → Grid (smooths intermittency) |
| **Telecom Towers** | Diesel-battery hybrid with smart charging |
| **Spacecraft** | CC-CV with MPPT, trickle maintenance from solar arrays |
| **Medical Implants** | Inductive transcutaneous charging (Li-ion in pacemakers) |
| **Grid Storage** | Second-life EV batteries with controlled C-rate (0.2C–0.5C) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Charging Time for a CC-CV Profile

**Problem Statement:**
A Li-ion cell has rated capacity $C_{\text{rated}} = 50 \text{ Ah}$, nominal voltage $V_{\text{nom}} = 3.7 \text{ V}$, cut-off voltage $V_{\text{min}} = 3.0 \text{ V}$, full-charge voltage $V_{\text{max}} = 4.2 \text{ V}$, and is charged using a CC-CV profile with constant current $I_{CC} = 0.5C$ until $V_{\text{max}}$ is reached, followed by CV at $4.2 \text{ V}$ until the current decays to $I_{\text{cut}} = C/20 = 2.5 \text{ A}$. Estimate the **total charging time** assuming coulombic efficiency $\eta_{C} = 0.998$.

---

### Step 1 — Express the constant current numerically

The CC stage current is given by the C-rate formula:

$$I_{CC} = 0.5 \cdot C_{\text{rated}} = 0.5 \times 50 = 25 \text{ A}$$

The cutoff current for the CV stage is:

$$I_{\text{cut}} = \frac{C_{\text{rated}}}{20} = \frac{50}{20} = 2.5 \text{ A}$$

---

### Step 2 — Find the SOC at the end of the CC stage

For a Li-ion cell, the CC stage typically reaches **~75–80 % SOC** when voltage hits $V_{\text{max}}$. Assuming (typical industry value) the CC stage brings the cell to $\text{SOC}_{CC} = 0.80$ (i.e., 80 %).

The amount of charge delivered during CC is:

$$\Delta Q_{CC} = C_{\text{rated}} \cdot \Delta SOC = 50 \cdot 0.80 = 40 \text{ Ah}$$

---

### Step 3 — Compute the time spent in the CC stage

$$t_{CC} = \frac{\Delta Q_{CC}}{I_{CC}} = \frac{40 \text{ Ah}}{25 \text{ A}} = 1.6 \text{ h}$$

**Conversion logic:** Charge in Ah divided by current in A gives time in hours (this is a direct application of $Q = I \cdot t$).

---

### Step 4 — Model the CV stage current decay

The CV stage is governed by the cell's equivalent circuit: an RC relaxation where current decays exponentially. The first-order model is:

$$I_{CV}(t) = I_{0} \cdot e^{-t / \tau}$$

Here, $I_{0}$ is the current at the start of the CV stage (≈ $I_{CC} = 25 \text{ A}$), and $\tau$ is the cell's polarization time constant (assume $\tau = 0.4$ h for a typical 50 Ah Li-ion cell).

---

### Step 5 — Compute the charge delivered during CV

The charge added during CV is the integral of current over the CV period:

$$\Delta Q_{CV} = \int_{0}^{t_{CV}} I_{0} e^{-t/\tau} \, dt = I_{0} \cdot \tau \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t_{CV}/\tau}\right)$$

We require the current to decay from 25 A to 2.5 A, so:

$$I_{0} \cdot e^{-t_{CV}/\tau} = I_{\text{cut}}$$

$$25 \cdot e^{-t_{CV}/0.4} = 2.5$$

$$e^{-t_{CV}/0.4} = 0.1$$

$$-\frac{t_{CV}}{0.4} = \ln(0.1) = -2.3026$$

$$t_{CV} = 0.4 \times 2.3026 = 0.921 \text{ h}$$

---

### Step 6 — Account for coulombic efficiency

Coulombic efficiency is applied to the energy actually stored:

$$Q_{\text{stored}} = \eta_{C} \cdot \Delta Q_{CC} + \eta_{C} \cdot \Delta Q_{CV}$$

But for the **time** calculation, the wall-clock time depends on the current profile only — efficiency affects the SOC achieved, not the time directly. For this problem, $\eta_{C}$ being close to 1 changes the result by < 0.5 %, so:

$$t_{\text{total}} \approx t_{CC} + t_{CV} = 1.6 + 0.921 = 2.521 \text{ h}$$

---

### Step 7 — Final compact result

$$\boxed{t_{\text{total}} = 2.52 \text{ hours}}$$

**Interpretation:** Out of 2.52 h, the CC stage contributes 1.6 h (~63 %) and the CV stage contributes 0.92 h (~37 %) — even though the CV stage delivers only ~20 % of the charge. **This is why "fast charging" beyond 80 % SOC is intrinsically slow.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Heat Generation in a Battery During Charging

**Statement:** During charging at constant current $I$, a battery with internal resistance $R_{\text{int}}$ generates **Joule heat**. Derive the total heat produced over a charging period $t_{c}$.

---

### Step 1 — Instantaneous power dissipation

By Joule's law, the instantaneous power dissipated as heat in the cell's internal resistance is:

$$P_{\text{heat}}(t) = I^{2}(t) \cdot R_{\text{int}}(t)$$

For a constant-current profile, $I(t) = I_{CC}$ is constant, but $R_{\text{int}}$ varies with SOC and temperature.

---

### Step 2 — Average resistance approximation

If we approximate $R_{\text{int}}$ as a constant average value $\bar{R}$ over the charging window, the total heat becomes:

$$Q_{\text{heat}} = \int_{0}^{t_{c}} I_{CC}^{2} \cdot \bar{R} \, dt = I_{CC}^{2} \cdot \bar{R} \cdot t_{c}$$

---

### Step 3 — Express in terms of stored energy

Since the stored energy is $E_{\text{stored}} = V_{\text{nom}} \cdot C_{\text{rated}} \cdot \Delta SOC$, the heat-to-energy ratio is:

$$\frac{Q_{\text{heat}}}{E_{\text{stored}}} = \frac{I_{CC}^{2} \cdot \bar{R} \cdot t_{c}}{V_{\text{nom}} \cdot C_{\text{rated}} \cdot \Delta SOC}$$

But $I_{CC} \cdot t_{c} \approx C_{\text{rated}} \cdot \Delta SOC / \eta_{C}$, so:

$$\frac{Q_{\text{heat}}}{E_{\text{stored}}} = \frac{I_{CC} \cdot \bar{R}}{V_{\text{nom}} \cdot \eta_{C}}$$

---

### Step 4 — Numerical illustration

Take $I_{CC} = 25 \text{ A}$, $\bar{R} = 25 \text{ m}\Omega = 0.025 \text{ }\Omega$, $V_{\text{nom}} = 3.7 \text{ V}$, $\eta_{C} \approx 1$:

$$Q_{\text{heat}} = (25)^{2} \times 0.025 \times 2.52 = 39.4 \text{ Wh per cell}$$

The energy stored is approximately:

$$E_{\text{stored}} = 3.7 \times 50 \times 1.0 = 185 \text{ Wh}$$

Therefore, the heat-to-stored-energy ratio is:

$$\frac{Q_{\text{heat}}}{E_{\text{stored}}} = \frac{39.4}{185} \approx 21.3 \text{ \%}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This 21 % heat loss is exactly why fast-charging stations need sophisticated liquid cooling — for a 100 kWh EV battery, the heat to remove during a 20-minute fast-charge is in the order of 20 kW!**

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Charger Power Rating for a Fast-Charge Target

**Problem:** An EV has a usable battery capacity of $E_{\text{bat}} = 60 \text{ kWh}$. The user wants to charge from 20 % to 80 % SOC in $t_{\text{ch}} = 20 \text{ minutes}$. The end-to-end charging efficiency is $\eta_{\text{ch}} = 0.90$. Determine the minimum charger power rating.

---

### Step 1 — Energy to be delivered to the battery

$$\Delta E_{\text{bat}} = E_{\text{bat}} \cdot (\text{SOC}_{f} - \text{SOC}_{i}) = 60 \times (0.80 - 0.20) = 36 \text{ kWh}$$

---

### Step 2 — Account for charging efficiency

The energy that must be drawn from the grid is larger because of conversion losses:

$$\Delta E_{\text{grid}} = \frac{\Delta E_{\text{bat}}}{\eta_{\text{ch}}} = \frac{36}{0.90} = 40 \text{ kWh}$$

---

### Step 3 — Required average charger power

Time in hours: $t_{\text{ch}} = 20 \text{ min} = \dfrac{1}{3} \text{ h}$

$$P_{\text{charger,avg}} = \frac{\Delta E_{\text{grid}}}{t_{\text{ch}}} = \frac{40 \text{ kWh}}{1/3 \text{ h}} = 120 \text{ kW}$$

---

### Step 4 — Account for non-constant profile (CC-CV taper)

In practice, the CC-CV profile means the average power is lower than the peak. Empirically, multiply by a profile factor $k_{p} \approx 1.15$:

$$P_{\text{charger,peak}} = k_{p} \cdot P_{\text{charger,avg}} = 1.15 \times 120 = 138 \text{ kW}$$

---

### Step 5 — Final Result

$$\boxed{P_{\text{charger,peak}} \approx 140 \text{ kW}}$$

**Interpretation:** To charge 60 kWh from 20 % → 80 % in 20 minutes, a DC fast charger of at least **140 kW** is required — consistent with a CCS2 HPC station.

---

## 3.4 KTU Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Charging Method | Best For | SOC Reach in 30 min | Cycle Life Impact | Complexity | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Trickle (C/20)** | UPS, standby | ~5 % | Best (≥ 3000) | Low | Very low |
| **CC (0.5C)** | NiMH, NiCd | ~25 % | Good (1500–2500) | Low | Low |
| **CC-CV (0.5C)** | EV Li-ion (home) | ~25 % | Very good (2000) | Medium | Medium |
| **CC-CV (1C)** | EV Li-ion (work) | ~50 % | Good (1500) | Medium-high | Medium |
| **CC-CV (2C)** | DC fast (50 kW) | ~75 % | Fair (1000) | High | High |
| **CC-CV (4C)** | Tesla supercharger | ~90 % | Reduced (700) | Very high | Very high |
| **Pulse (with reflex)** | Long-cycle Li-ion | ~30 % | Best (2500+) | High | Medium |
| **Inductive (WPT2)** | Garage wireless | ~15 % | Very good | High | High |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Explain the Constant Current — Constant Voltage (CC-CV) charging method used for Lithium-ion batteries. State the typical cut-off current used to terminate the CV stage.**

**Model Answer:**

In the **CC-CV (Constant Current – Constant Voltage)** method, the battery is first charged at a **fixed current** (typically 0.5C to 1C) until the cell voltage reaches its **maximum allowable value** (e.g., 4.2 V for NMC, 3.65 V for LFP). This is the **CC stage** and delivers roughly 70–80 % of the total charge.

The charger then switches to the **CV stage**, holding the voltage constant at $V_{\text{max}}$ and allowing the current to **decay exponentially** as the polarization in the cell relaxes. Charging is **terminated** when the current drops to a predefined cut-off value, conventionally:

$$I_{\text{cut}} = \frac{C_{\text{rated}}}{20} = 0.05C$$

**Why CC-CV is universal for Li-ion:**
- Prevents over-voltage (avoids electrolyte oxidation & thermal runaway).
- Prevents excessive current at high SOC (avoids lithium plating).
- Maximizes cycle life while delivering fastest possible charge.

> A typical 50 Ah NMC cell charges from 0 to 100 % SOC in 2.0–2.5 hours using a 0.5C CC-CV profile.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Compare Level 1, Level 2, and Level 3 EV charging. Mention the typical power range and charging time for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Level 1 (AC) | Level 2 (AC) | Level 3 (DC Fast) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Voltage** | 120 V (US) / 230 V (India) | 240 V (US) / 400 V 3φ (EU/India) | 200–1000 V DC |
| **Power** | ≤ 1.92 kW | 3.3–19.2 kW | 50–350 kW |
| **Charger Location** | On-board | On-board | Off-board |
| **Typical Connector** | Type 1 (J1772) | Type 2 (Mennekes) | CCS2 / CHAdeMO / GB/T |
| **Time to 80 %** | 8–12 h (40 kWh) | 3–6 h | 15–45 min |
| **Use Case** | Home, slow | Home/Work | Highway, public |

> **Key distinction:** Level 1 and Level 2 use the on-board charger (OBC) and supply AC; the rectifier is inside the car. Level 3 supplies DC directly to the battery, bypassing the OBC entirely.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of an **on-board EV charger (OBC)**. Identify the role of the **PFC stage**, the **isolated DC-DC stage**, and the **BMS interface** in ensuring efficient and safe battery charging. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **60 kWh** EV battery is to be charged from **20 % to 90 % SOC** using a **CC-CV profile**. The CC current is **1C (i.e., 150 A)**, the coulombic efficiency is 0.99, and the polarization time constant during the CV stage is **τ = 0.5 h**. The CV stage begins at **75 % SOC** and the cut-off current is **C/20 = 7.5 A**. Compute the total charging time and the energy drawn from the grid assuming the charger efficiency is 92 %. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution (a) — On-Board EV Charger Architecture (7 Marks)**

The on-board charger (OBC) is a power-electronic converter that takes AC mains supply and delivers regulated DC to the high-voltage traction battery. Its functional blocks are:

1. **AC Input + EMI Filter:** Suppresses conducted EMI, surge protection (varistor + fuse), inrush current limiter (NTC thermistor).

2. **PFC Stage (Active Power Factor Correction):** A boost converter operating in **Critical Conduction Mode (CrCM)** or **Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM)** with average-current-mode control. It shapes the input current to be sinusoidal and in-phase with the input voltage, achieving **PF ≥ 0.99** and **THD < 5 %** (meeting IEC 61000-3-2). Output: regulated **400 V DC link**.

3. **Isolated DC-DC Stage:** Either a **Full-Bridge (FB) + rectifier** or an **LLC resonant** converter, or a **Dual-Active-Bridge (DAB)** for bidirectional operation. Provides:
   - Galvanic isolation (safety — IEC 61851-1).
   - High-frequency transformer for step-down.
   - Output voltage range (200–450 V for 400 V systems; 500–900 V for 800 V systems).

4. **Output Filter + Sensor Suite:** LC filter for ripple (< 2 % of $V_{\text{bat}}$), current shunt or Hall-effect sensor, voltage divider, temperature sensors (NTC on busbars and cells).

5. **BMS Interface (CAN / SPI / UART):** Bidirectional communication:
   - BMS → Charger: real-time SOC, voltage limits, current limits, temperature.
   - Charger → BMS: actual voltage, current, fault codes, end-of-charge flag.

6. **Controller (DSP / MCU):** Implements the **CC-CV algorithm**, OBC startup sequencing, fault management (over-voltage, over-current, over-temperature, communication loss).

> **Key role summary:**
> - **PFC stage** → grid-friendly, low harmonic distortion.
> - **Isolated DC-DC** → safety isolation and voltage adaptation.
> - **BMS interface** → adaptive charge profile based on cell health.

*(7 marks distributed: 2 marks block diagram, 2 marks PFC role, 1.5 marks isolated DC-DC role, 1.5 marks BMS role.)*

---

#### **Solution (b) — Charging Time and Energy Computation (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- $E_{\text{bat}} = 60 \text{ kWh}$
- $\text{SOC}_{i} = 0.20$, $\text{SOC}_{f} = 0.90$ → $\Delta \text{SOC} = 0.70$
- $C_{\text{rated}} = E_{\text{bat}} / V_{\text{nom}} = 60000 / 400 = 150 \text{ Ah}$ (assume 400 V nominal)
- $I_{CC} = 1C = 150 \text{ A}$
- $\eta_{C} = 0.99$
- CV starts at $\text{SOC}_{CC} = 0.75$, $\tau = 0.5$ h, $I_{\text{cut}} = 7.5 \text{ A}$

**Step 1 — Energy stored in battery during charging:**

$$\Delta E_{\text{bat}} = 60 \times 0.70 = 42 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 2 — Energy drawn from the grid (charger efficiency η = 0.92):**

$$\Delta E_{\text{grid}} = \frac{\Delta E_{\text{bat}}}{\eta_{\text{ch}}} = \frac{42}{0.92} = 45.65 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 3 — Time in the CC stage:**

Charge delivered in CC: $\Delta Q_{CC} = 150 \times (0.75 - 0.20) = 82.5 \text{ Ah}$

$$t_{CC} = \frac{\Delta Q_{CC}}{I_{CC}} = \frac{82.5}{150} = 0.55 \text{ h} = 33 \text{ min}$$

**Step 4 — Time in the CV stage (exponential decay):**

$$I(t) = I_{0} \cdot e^{-t/\tau}, \quad I_{0} = I_{CC} = 150 \text{ A}$$

Setting $I(t_{CV}) = 7.5 \text{ A}$:

$$7.5 = 150 \cdot e^{-t_{CV}/0.5}$$

$$e^{-t_{CV}/0.5} = 0.05$$

$$-\frac{t_{CV}}{0.5} = \ln(0.05) = -2.9957$$

$$t_{CV} = 0.5 \times 2.9957 = 1.498 \text{ h} \approx 1.5 \text{ h} = 90 \text{ min}$$

**Step 5 — Total charging time:**

$$t_{\text{total}} = t_{CC} + t_{CV} = 0.55 + 1.498 = 2.048 \text{ h} \approx 2 \text{ h } 3 \text{ min}$$

**Step 6 — Sanity check on SOC reached in CV:**

The CV stage delivers:

$$\Delta Q_{CV} = I_{0} \cdot \tau \cdot (1 - e^{-t_{CV}/\tau}) = 150 \times 0.5 \times (1 - 0.05) = 71.25 \text{ Ah}$$

This corresponds to an SOC rise of $71.25 / 150 = 0.475$. Starting from 0.75 SOC, the cell reaches $0.75 + 0.475 = 1.225$ which exceeds 100 % — meaning the **CV stage actually terminates early** (around 0.90 SOC).

**Refined CV time** to go from 0.75 to 0.90 SOC, with $\eta_{C} = 0.99$:

$$\Delta Q_{CV,actual} = 150 \times (0.90 - 0.75) / 0.99 = 22.73 \text{ Ah}$$

$$I_{0} \cdot \tau \cdot (1 - e^{-t_{CV}/\tau}) = 22.73$$

$$75 \cdot (1 - e^{-t_{CV}/0.5}) = 22.73 \Rightarrow e^{-t_{CV}/0.5} = 0.6969$$

$$t_{CV} = 0.5 \times 0.3610 = 0.180 \text{ h} = 10.8 \text{ min}$$

**Step 7 — Final answers:**

$$\boxed{t_{\text{total}} = t_{CC} + t_{CV} = 0.55 + 0.18 = 0.73 \text{ h} = 43.8 \text{ min}}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta E_{\text{grid}} = 45.65 \text{ kWh}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` — **ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

**(a)** Explain the **Constant Current (CC)**, **Constant Voltage (CV)**, and **Pulse Charging** methods. Compare their relative impact on **battery cycle life**, **charging time**, and **energy efficiency**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An electric two-wheeler uses a **48 V, 30 Ah** Lithium-ion battery pack. It is charged at a **0.5C rate** using a CC-CV profile. The internal resistance of the pack is **$R_{\text{int}} = 60 \text{ m}\Omega$**. The charging is from **10 % to 100 % SOC**. Compute:
   (i) The total charge delivered (in Ah).
   (ii) The charging current.
   (iii) The total heat generated during charging (in Wh) assuming $\bar{R} = 60 \text{ m}\Omega$ throughout.
   (iv) The percentage of the stored energy that is converted to heat. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution (a) — Charging Methods Comparison (7 Marks)**

**Constant Current (CC) Charging:**
- A regulated current is forced through the battery.
- Voltage rises linearly (for Li-ion, steeply at the end).
- Widely used in **NiMH and NiCd** batteries.
- **Drawback:** Without CV taper, the cell can be overcharged and damaged.

**Constant Voltage (CV) Charging:**
- A regulated voltage is applied; the battery draws whatever current its state allows.
- Current is **high initially** (limited only by cable resistance) and **decays exponentially**.
- Used in **Lead-Acid** batteries (typically set at 2.30–2.45 V/cell).
- **Drawback:** Very high initial inrush current in deeply discharged cells.

**Pulse Charging:**
- Current is delivered in **pulses** with rest periods (and optionally negative "reflex" pulses).
- Rest periods allow **electrolyte diffusion**, ion redistribution, and thermal relaxation.
- Reduces concentration polarization and gas evolution.
- Reflex pulses actively **depolarize** the cell, increasing cycle life by 20–30 %.
- Best suited for **high-power Li-ion** applications.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | CC | CV | Pulse |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cycle Life Impact** | Moderate | High (Pb) | Best (20–30 % gain) |
| **Charging Time** | Medium | Slow (tapering) | Fastest for equal life |
| **Energy Efficiency** | 85–90 % | 75–85 % | 90–95 % |
| **Complexity** | Low | Low | High |
| **Heat Generation** | Medium | High initially | Lowest |

---

#### **Solution (b) — Two-Wheeler Battery Charging Calculation (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- $V_{\text{bat}} = 48 \text{ V}$, $C_{\text{rated}} = 30 \text{ Ah}$, $R_{\text{int}} = 60 \text{ m}\Omega = 0.060 \text{ }\Omega$
- C-rate = 0.5C, $\text{SOC}_{i} = 10$ %, $\text{SOC}_{f} = 100$ %

**(i) Total charge delivered:**

$$\Delta Q = C_{\text{rated}} \times (\text{SOC}_{f} - \text{SOC}_{i}) = 30 \times (1.00 - 0.10) = 27 \text{ Ah}$$

**(ii) Charging current:**

$$I = 0.5C = 0.5 \times 30 = 15 \text{ A}$$

**(iii) Total heat generated:**

Time to deliver 27 Ah at 15 A (ignoring CV taper for simplicity):

$$t_{c} = \frac{\Delta Q}{I} = \frac{27}{15} = 1.8 \text{ h} = 6480 \text{ s}$$

$$Q_{\text{heat}} = I^{2} \cdot R_{\text{int}} \cdot t_{c} = (15)^{2} \times 0.060 \times 6480$$

$$Q_{\text{heat}} = 225 \times 0.060 \times 6480 = 87,480 \text{ J}$$

Converting to Wh:

$$Q_{\text{heat}} = \frac{87,480}{3600} = 24.3 \text{ Wh}$$

**(iv) Percentage of stored energy converted to heat:**

Energy stored in battery:

$$E_{\text{stored}} = V_{\text{bat}} \cdot \Delta Q = 48 \times 27 = 1296 \text{ Wh}$$

Percentage of energy lost as heat:

$$\%\text{ Heat loss} = \frac{Q_{\text{heat}}}{E_{\text{stored}}} \times 100 = \frac{24.3}{1296} \times 100 = 1.875 \text{ \%}$$

> **Note:** This 1.9 % loss is for **internal resistance alone**. In practice, additional losses occur in the charger electronics (PFC + DC-DC losses ~ 8–10 %), cables, and contactors. Total end-to-end charging loss is typically 10–15 %.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes KTU examiners penalize heavily:**
> 1. **Mixing C-rate and current units** — Writing "0.5C" without converting to actual Amperes loses 1 mark immediately.
> 2. **Ignoring the CV taper time** — Students who compute only the CC stage and ignore the CV decay lose 2–3 marks in 14-mark questions.
> 3. **Confusing energy efficiency with coulombic efficiency** — Coulombic efficiency is unitless and ~99 % for Li-ion, while energy efficiency is ~85–92 % due to voltage differences.
> 4. **Forgetting charger efficiency** — If the question asks for "energy drawn from grid," you **must** divide by $\eta_{\text{ch}}$, not just multiply the battery's stored energy.
> 5. **Not stating the cut-off current** — The C/20 termination criterion is a KTU favorite — write it explicitly.
> 6. **Confusing AC Level 1/2 with DC Level 1/2** — Some textbooks (older Indian ones) refer to AC only; the KTU 2024 scheme uses the **SAE/IEC nomenclature** where Level 3 = DC Fast.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Battery Charging Technology**

**Definitions (verbatim memory lines):**
- ☐ **CC-CV** is the standard Li-ion charging profile: constant current → constant voltage → terminate at C/20.
- ☐ **Level 1 AC** ≤ 1.92 kW | **Level 2 AC** ≤ 19.2 kW | **Level 3 DC** 50–350 kW.
- ☐ **OBC** is on-board (AC, limited); **DCFC** is off-board (DC, high power).
- ☐ **V2G** = Vehicle-to-Grid; bidirectional flow enabled by **ISO 15118** protocol.
- ☐ **C-rate** = $I / C_{\text{rated}}$; **SOC** = $Q_{\text{present}} / Q_{\text{rated}}$; **DOD** = 1 − SOC.
- ☐ **Pulse charging** improves cycle life by 20–30 % via reflex (depolarization) pulses.
- ☐ **Inductive (wireless) charging** uses resonant coupling at 85–205 kHz per **SAE J2954**.
- ☐ **Laptop** Lithium polymer: 4.2 V/cell NMC, 3.0 V cut-off; **LFP**: 3.65 V max, 2.5 V cut-off.

**Key Formulas (must memorize):**
- ☐ $I = C_{\text{rated}} \times \text{C-rate}$
- ☐ $t_{c} = \dfrac{\Delta Q}{I \cdot \eta_{C}}$
- ☐ $\eta_{E} = \dfrac{\int V_{\text{dis}} I_{\text{dis}} \, dt}{\int V_{\text{ch}} I_{\text{ch}} \, dt}$
- ☐ $P_{\text{charger}} = \dfrac{\Delta E_{\text{bat}}}{\eta_{\text{ch}} \cdot t_{c}}$
- ☐ $Q_{\text{heat}} = I^{2} R_{\text{int}} t_{c}$

**Standards to know by acronym:**
- ☐ **SAE J1772** (AC Level 1/2 plug, US) | **IEC 61851** (conductive charging)
- ☐ **CCS2** (Combined Charging System, EU/India) | **CHAdeMO** (Japan)
- ☐ **GB/T** (China) | **Tesla NACS** (now adopted as SAE J3400)
- ☐ **ISO 15118** (V2G communication) | **OCPP 1.6/2.0.1** (back-office protocol)
- ☐ **SAE J2954** (wireless charging)

**Numerical thumb rules:**
- ☐ EV battery 1C charge ≈ 1 hour to 80 % SOC; 2C ≈ 30 min; 4C ≈ 15 min.
- ☐ The CV stage contributes ~37 % of the **time** but only ~20 % of the **charge**.
- ☐ Fast charging beyond 80 % SOC is **inherently slow** — it is not a charger limitation.
- ☐ Energy efficiency of a typical OBC is 90–95 %; total end-to-end (grid → battery) is 85–90 %.

**Conceptual hooks (use these in 1-mark / 2-mark answers):**
- ☐ "Why CC-CV?" → prevents over-voltage and lithium plating.
- ☐ "Why pulse?" → electrolyte relaxation and depolarization.
- ☐ "Why bidirectional?" → V2G, grid balancing, renewable integration.
- ☐ "Why liquid cooling in DC fast charger?" → 20 kW+ heat to remove per 100 kWh battery.
- ☐ "Why no float for Li-ion?" → corrosion of anode current collector at high SOC.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vehicle-to-Grid Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET742 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES AND RENEWABLE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Vehicle-to-Grid Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G)** technology is a smart energy management framework that enables the **bidirectional flow of electrical energy** between the high-voltage battery pack of an Electric Vehicle (EV) and the external power grid. Under this paradigm, the EV is no longer treated as a passive, end-point load; instead, it functions as a **Distributed Energy Resource (DER)** capable of both drawing power from the grid (Grid-to-Vehicle, G2V) and injecting stored energy back into the grid (Vehicle-to-Grid, V2G).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Examiner-Approved Wording):**
> "Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G) is a system in which plug-in electric vehicles, including Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs) and Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicles (PHEVs), communicate with the power grid to perform demand response services by either drawing electricity from the grid during off-peak hours or delivering stored energy back to the grid during peak demand periods, thereby acting as mobile, distributed energy storage units."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

Think of your **mobile phone power bank**. Normally, you only *charge* the power bank from a wall socket and then *discharge* it into your phone — that is a unidirectional flow. Now imagine your power bank is so massive (say, 60 kWh) that instead of merely charging your phone, you could *sell* the surplus stored electricity back to the electricity company during a hot summer afternoon when everyone is running their ACs. The power company pays you, the grid gets relief, and the car owner earns money while parked. That is precisely what V2G does — your parked EV becomes a **"rolling battery on wheels"** that can be both *charged* and *discharged* under the grid operator's instructions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Implementation Snapshot:**
> - The **Nissan Leaf** was the first mass-produced EV to publicly demonstrate V2G capability (2017, UK).
> - **Denmark, Netherlands, Japan, and California (USA)** are global V2G pilot leaders.
> - The communication standard governing V2G is the **ISO 15118** protocol, paired with **CCS-2 (Combined Charging System Type 2)** connectors.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value | KTU Notation |
|---|---|---|
| Nominal grid frequency (India) | **50 Hz** | $f_{grid}$ |
| Standard Indian grid voltage (LV) | **415 V (3-φ), 230 V (1-φ)** | $V_{LL}, V_{LN}$ |
| Typical EV battery capacity | **20 – 100 kWh** | $C_{bat}$ |
| Standard DC fast charging level | **50 kW – 350 kW** | $P_{ch,DC}$ |
| V2G round-trip efficiency benchmark | **85 % – 92 %** | $\eta_{V2G}$ |
| Target Depth of Discharge limit | **70 % – 80 %** | DoD |
| State of Charge operating window | **20 % – 80 %** | SoC |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Favourite Term to Remember:** "Bidirectional smart charging with grid services export" — this single phrase, used correctly in a 14-mark answer, signals strong conceptual clarity.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of V2G — Stepwise Breakdown

V2G is not a single device; it is a **layered ecosystem** comprising four interacting subsystems. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to identify and explain each layer.

- **Layer 1 — Physical (Power Hardware):**
  A **bidirectional onboard charger (BOC)** or **off-board DC bidirectional charger** replaces the conventional unidirectional AC-DC rectifier. It uses an **Active Front-End (AFE)** converter (typically a Voltage Source Converter, VSC) that can invert the power flow using IGBT/MOSFET switches in synchronous PWM mode.

- **Layer 2 — Communication (Information Layer):**
  The EV, the charger, and the **Aggregator / Utility** exchange data using the **ISO 15118** standard over **Power Line Communication (PLC)** or OCPP 2.0.1. Data exchanged includes SoC, departure time, target SoC, and dynamic price signals.

- **Layer 3 — Control & Decision (Energy Management Layer):**
  An **Aggregator** (a virtual power plant operator) pools hundreds of EVs to behave as a single dispatchable unit. It receives grid frequency/voltage signals from the System Operator (SO) and decides the dispatch setpoint $P_{disp}(t)$ for each EV.

- **Layer 4 — Market & Economic (Settlement Layer):**
  V2G participants are remunerated through **ancillary service markets** — frequency regulation, spinning reserve, capacity payments, and Time-of-Use (ToU) arbitrage.

### 2.2 Power Flow Topology and the "V2X" Family

The "X" in **V2X** refers to the *destination* of the discharged energy:

- **G2V (Grid-to-Vehicle):** Conventional unidirectional charging. EV acts as a load.
- **V2G (Vehicle-to-Grid):** EV injects power back into the public distribution grid.
- **V2H (Vehicle-to-Home):** EV powers a residential home during outage or peak tariff hours (acts as a home UPS).
- **V2B (Vehicle-to-Building):** EV supplies a commercial building's load, often coupled with rooftop PV.
- **V2L (Vehicle-to-Load):** The EV's 230 V AC outlet powers appliances directly (e.g., Hyundai Ioniq 5, BYD Atto 3).
- **V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything):** Umbrella term encompassing all the above.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the distinction matters:** A KTU 14-mark question may ask *"Compare V2G, V2H and V2B with suitable applications."* Each differs in the **Point of Common Coupling (PCC)** and the **grid code** applicable — students who blur these lose 2-3 marks easily.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{bat} = V_{bat} \times Q_{bat} \times 3600$ | Stored battery energy from voltage and Ah capacity | Wh |
| 2 | $SoC(t) = SoC_0 + \dfrac{\int_0^t P_{bat}(\tau)\,d\tau}{C_{bat}}$ | Instantaneous State of Charge, where $P_{bat}>0$ = charging | % or p.u. |
| 3 | $P_{V2G}(t) = V_{dc}(t) \times I_{dc}(t) \times \eta_{inv}$ | Active power injected into the grid via inverter | W or kW |
| 4 | $\eta_{V2G,round} = \eta_{ch} \times \eta_{dis} = \eta_{ch} \times \eta_{inv} \times \eta_{dis,bat}$ | Combined round-trip (charge + discharge) efficiency | dimensionless |
| 5 | $f_{grid} = f_{nom} - K_{droop} \times (P - P_{sched})$ | Active power–frequency droop characteristic for regulation | Hz |
| 6 | $C_{reg} = \Delta P_{V2G} / \Delta f$ | Frequency regulation capacity offered by V2G fleet | MW/Hz |
| 7 | $B_{degr} = \alpha \times DoD + \beta \times N_{eq,cyc}$ | Battery degradation cost per kWh cycled | ₹/kWh |
| 8 | $R_{pay} = (E_{inj} \times R_{inj}) - (E_{draw} \times R_{draw}) - C_{degr}$ | Net V2G daily revenue | ₹/day |
| 9 | $P_{agg} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} P_{V2G,i}$ | Aggregated power from a fleet of N vehicles | MW |
| 10 | $E_{loss,line} = I^2 R_{line}$ | Distribution line copper loss during V2G export | W |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL LATEX RULE FOR KTU VALUATION:** In the table above, notice how all absolute values and conditional expressions are written using commas or words (e.g., "where $P_{bat}>0$ = charging") instead of vertical pipes `|...|`. This avoids breaking the markdown table parser — a habit that mirrors good LaTeX typesetting in your university lab reports.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Grid Stability:** Frequency Regulation (FCAS in India) and Voltage Support through reactive power ($Q$) injection via the V2G inverter's VAR control loop.
- **Renewable Integration:** Solar and wind are intermittent; a fleet of 1 million V2G-enabled EVs in Kerala could absorb ~3-5 GWh of surplus midday solar.
- **Demand-Side Management (DSM):** Peak shaving during Kerala's 7 PM–11 PM residential demand window.
- **Disaster Resilience:** Islanded microgrid operation during floods (highly relevant to Kerala's recurring monsoon crisis).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — V2G State of Charge (SoC) Dynamics

**Statement:** Show that the State of Charge of an EV battery engaged in V2G service evolves as a first-order differential equation in time.

**Starting Point (Given):** The coulomb-counting form of SoC is

$$
SoC(t) = SoC(0) + \frac{1}{C_{bat}}\int_{0}^{t} I_{bat}(\tau)\,d\tau
$$

**Step 1 — Replace current with power.**
Using the fundamental electrical relation $P_{bat}(t) = V_{bat}(t) \cdot I_{bat}(t)$, we solve for current as

$$
I_{bat}(t) = \frac{P_{bat}(t)}{V_{bat}(t)}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the SoC integral.**

$$
SoC(t) = SoC(0) + \frac{1}{C_{bat}}\int_{0}^{t} \frac{P_{bat}(\tau)}{V_{bat}(\tau)}\,d\tau
$$

**Step 3 — Assume a tightly regulated DC bus.**
In a properly designed V2G charger, the DC-link voltage is held within a tight band by the bidirectional DC-DC converter (typically a 1-2 % ripple). We can therefore approximate $V_{bat}(\tau) \approx V_{bat,nom}$, a constant.

$$
SoC(t) \approx SoC(0) + \frac{1}{C_{bat}\,V_{bat,nom}}\int_{0}^{t} P_{bat}(\tau)\,d\tau
$$

**Step 4 — Define usable energy capacity.**
The product $E_{bat} = C_{bat}\,V_{bat,nom}$ is the nominal stored energy in watt-hours. Hence

$$
SoC(t) = SoC(0) + \frac{1}{E_{bat}}\int_{0}^{t} P_{bat}(\tau)\,d\tau
$$

**Step 5 — Differentiate to obtain the differential form.**

$$
\frac{d\,SoC(t)}{dt} = \frac{P_{bat}(t)}{E_{bat}}
$$

**Step 6 — Sign convention for V2G mode.**
- **Charging (G2V):** $P_{bat}(t) > 0$ → SoC increases.
- **Discharging (V2G):** $P_{bat}(t) < 0$ → SoC decreases.

**Step 7 — Include efficiency to account for losses.**

$$
\frac{d\,SoC(t)}{dt} = \frac{\eta_{ch}\,P_{grid \to bat}(t) - P_{bat \to grid}(t)/\eta_{dis}}{E_{bat}}
$$

This is the **master SoC dynamic equation** for any KTU numerical problem on V2G dispatch.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — V2G Round-Trip Energy and Revenue
