**Problem Setup:** A V2G-capable EV with $C_{bat} = 60\text{ kWh}$ is parked from 9 AM to 6 PM. It discharges between 6 PM and 10 PM at a rate of 7 kW (peak tariff window), and charges between 12 AM and 4 AM at the same 7 kW (off-peak window). The off-peak tariff is ₹6/kWh, peak tariff is ₹12/kWh, and battery round-trip efficiency is 90 %. Compute the daily arbitrage revenue, neglecting degradation.

**Step 1 — Energy drawn from the grid during off-peak charging.**

$$
E_{draw} = P_{ch} \times \Delta t_{ch} = 7\text{ kW} \times 4\text{ h} = 28\text{ kWh}
$$

**Step 2 — Energy injected into the grid during peak discharging (after losses).**

$$
E_{inj} = P_{dis} \times \Delta t_{dis} \times \eta_{V2G,round}
$$

$$
E_{inj} = 7\text{ kW} \times 4\text{ h} \times 0.90 = 25.2\text{ kWh}
$$

**Step 3 — Daily cost of purchasing energy.**

$$
C_{buy} = E_{draw} \times R_{draw} = 28 \times 6 = \text{₹}168
$$

**Step 4 — Daily revenue from selling energy.**

$$
R_{sell} = E_{inj} \times R_{inj} = 25.2 \times 12 = \text{₹}302.4
$$

**Step 5 — Net daily arbitrage revenue.**

$$
R_{net} = R_{sell} - C_{buy} = 302.4 - 168 = \text{₹}134.4
$$

**Step 6 — Annualised revenue (assuming 300 working days).**

$$
R_{year} = 134.4 \times 300 = \text{₹}40{,}320
$$

**Step 7 — Comparative revenue per kWh of battery throughput.**

$$
\frac{R_{net}}{E_{draw}} = \frac{134.4}{28} \approx \text{₹}4.8\text{ per kWh}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner Insight:** Notice that even with a 10 % loss penalty, the **price differential (₹12 − ₹6 = ₹6)** is large enough to yield a net profit. If asked *"Is V2G profitable in India?"*, your answer should reference the **Time-of-Use (ToU) tariff spread** — not the technology cost — as the primary economic driver.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — V2G Contribution to Frequency Regulation

**Given:** A 500-EV aggregator, each providing $P_{V2G,i} = 10\text{ kW}$ maximum V2G capacity. The grid frequency drops by $\Delta f = 0.1\text{ Hz}$ from nominal 50 Hz. The droop coefficient is $K_{droop} = 0.05\text{ Hz/MW}$.

**Step 1 — Total V2G regulating capacity.**

$$
P_{agg} = N \times P_{V2G,i} = 500 \times 10\text{ kW} = 5{,}000\text{ kW} = 5\text{ MW}
$$

**Step 2 — Frequency regulation capacity offered (from Formula 6).**

$$
C_{reg} = \frac{\Delta P_{V2G}}{\Delta f} = \frac{5\text{ MW}}{0.1\text{ Hz}} = 50\text{ MW/Hz}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with conventional governor response.**
A typical thermal unit in Kerala (e.g., NTPC Kayamkulam) provides $\sim 20$ MW/Hz — meaning the EV fleet can outperform a 250 MW thermal generator in this metric.

> [!TIP]
> **Why KTU loves this question:** It directly links EV technology with **power system stability** — a perfect illustration of interdisciplinary integration (Module 1 EV × Power Systems).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a V2G System

Because V2G is a system-level concept with power flow, control, communication, and market layers, a **block diagram** is more appropriate than a physical drawing. The Mermaid block below shows the full closed-loop interaction.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Grid[AC Power Grid 415V 50Hz]:::source
    PCC[Point of Common Coupling]:::node
    BDC[Bidirectional AC-DC Charger AFE Converter]:::convert
    DCBus[DC Link Bus 400-800V]:::node
    DCDC[DC-DC Converter Step Up or Down]:::convert
    BMS[Battery Management System BMS]:::ctrl
    BAT[EV Battery Pack 60 kWh Li-ion]:::storage
    Meter[Smart Energy Meter]:::node
    PLC[Power Line Communication ISO 15118]:::comm
    AGG[Aggregator Virtual Power Plant]:::ctrl
    SO[System Operator State Load Despatch Centre]:::ctrl
    MKT[Ancillary Services Market FCAS]:::node

    Grid --- PCC
    PCC --- BDC
    BDC --- DCBus
    DCBus --- DCDC
    DCDC --- BMS
    BMS --- BAT
    PCC --- Meter
    Meter --- PLC
    PLC --- AGG
    AGG --- SO
    AGG --- MKT
    SO -.Dispatch Signal.-> AGG
    AGG -.Setpoint.-> PLC
    PLC -.Charge or Discharge.-> BDC

    classDef source fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef storage fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef convert fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef node fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#BDB76B,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef comm fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Power Flow Direction Diagram (G2V vs V2G)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G[GRID]:::source
    EV1[EV: G2V Mode CHARGING]:::storage1
    EV2[EV: V2G Mode DISCHARGING]:::storage2
    H[HOME V2H]:::node
    B[BUILDING V2B]:::node
    L[LOAD V2L]:::node

    G -- AC power --> EV1
    EV1 -- DC stored --> BAT1[(Battery SoC rising)]
    EV2 -- DC stored --> BAT2[(Battery SoC falling)]
    EV2 -- AC power --> G
    EV2 -- AC power --> H
    EV2 -- AC power --> B
    EV2 -- AC power --> L

    classDef source fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px
    classDef storage1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    classDef storage2 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef node fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#483D8B,stroke-width:1px
```

### 4.3 Communication Protocol Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L5[Application Layer: Demand Response, Tariff]:::layer
    L4[Transport Layer: OCPP 2.0.1, ISO 15118]:::layer
    L3[Network Layer: TCP/IP over PLC]:::layer
    L2[Data Link Layer: HomePlug Green PHY]:::layer
    L1[Physical Layer: Control Pilot Pin of CCS-2]:::layer

    L5 --> L4 --> L3 --> L2 --> L1

    classDef layer fill:#F0F8FF,stroke:#1E90FF,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Hint for Students:** When the examiner asks *"Draw the block diagram of a V2G system,"* the minimum acceptable figure MUST contain: Grid, Bidirectional Charger, Battery + BMS, Communication link, and Aggregator. Omitting any one of these results in a -2 mark deduction under KTU 2024 valuation norms.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*Define Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G) technology. Mention any two grid services that an EV can provide under V2G operation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G) is a smart energy management scheme in which an Electric Vehicle's onboard battery exchanges power bidirectionally with the electrical grid through a bidirectional charger and a communication interface (typically ISO 15118).

**Two grid services:**
1. **Peak shaving / load shifting:** EVs discharge during peak demand (e.g., 6 PM–10 PM in Kerala) to flatten the load curve.
2. **Frequency regulation:** EVs modulate their charge/discharge power in response to grid frequency deviations ($\Delta f$), providing fast-acting primary response much faster than conventional thermal generators.

*(1 mark for definition + 0.5 mark per service × 2 = 1 mark for services + 0.5 mark for communication mention = 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
*Distinguish between G2V and V2G modes of operation in an EV. State the role of a Bidirectional AC-DC converter in this context.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | G2V (Grid-to-Vehicle) | V2G (Vehicle-to-Grid) |
|---|---|---|
| Power flow | Grid → EV battery | EV battery → Grid |
| EV role | Controllable load | Distributed energy source |
| Charger mode | Rectifier (AC → DC) | Inverter (DC → AC) |
| Typical time | Off-peak hours (12 AM – 4 AM) | Peak hours (6 PM – 10 PM) |
| Grid benefit | Load levelling, valley filling | Peak shaving, ancillary services |

**Role of Bidirectional AC-DC Converter:** It enables the **reversal of active power flow** without changing the hardware topology. In G2V mode it operates as a **PWM rectifier (Active Front End)** with near-unity power factor; in V2G mode the same IGBT bridge operates as a **grid-tie inverter** with low Total Harmonic Distortion (THD < 5 %, IEEE 519 compliant).

*(1.5 marks for comparison table + 1.5 marks for converter role explanation.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Answer **ONE** full question from a choice of TWO. Each 14-mark question typically has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of a Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G) system with a neat block diagram. Describe the functions of the Bidirectional Charger, Battery Management System (BMS), Aggregator, and Communication module. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A V2G-enabled EV has a battery of capacity 50 kWh. It charges from the grid during 11 PM to 3 AM at a constant rate of 11 kW, and discharges to the grid during 6 PM to 8 PM at a constant rate of 12 kW. The charging efficiency is 92 %, and the discharging efficiency is 95 %. The off-peak tariff is ₹5/kWh and the peak tariff is ₹11/kWh. Calculate:
   (i) The SoC at the end of the charging window, assuming initial SoC is 20 %.
   (ii) The energy fed back to the grid.
   (iii) The net daily revenue from this V2G transaction.
   *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution to Q-A (a) — 7 marks:**

The V2G architecture consists of **five functional layers**:

1. **Power Conversion Layer:** A bidirectional AC-DC converter (Active Front End, AFE) connected to the grid at the Point of Common Coupling (PCC). It uses IGBTs in a voltage-source configuration, with an LCL filter to limit harmonic injection. A bidirectional DC-DC converter stages the voltage to match the EV battery (typically 400 V or 800 V architecture). *(1.5 marks)*

2. **Energy Storage Layer:** The high-voltage Li-ion traction battery, monitored continuously by a Battery Management System (BMS). The BMS enforces safety limits — cell voltage window (e.g., 2.5 V – 4.2 V per cell), temperature window (15 °C – 45 °C optimal), and SoC window (typically 20 %–80 % for V2G to limit degradation). *(1.5 marks)*

3. **Communication Layer:** Implements **ISO 15118** over **Power Line Communication (PLC)** on the Control Pilot pin of the CCS-2 connector. Bidirectional data includes SoC, departure time, target SoC, dynamic price, and authentication. *(1 mark)*

4. **Aggregation Layer:** A **Virtual Power Plant (VPP)** operator pools the dispatchable capacity of hundreds to thousands of EVs. It acts as the market-facing entity, bidding into the Ancillary Services market (e.g., India's FCAS framework). *(1.5 marks)*

5. **Market & Grid Operator Layer:** The System Operator (in India: NLDC/RLDC/SLDC) issues Automatic Generation Control (AGC) signals to the aggregator, which translates them into individual EV setpoints. *(1.5 marks)*

*(Diagrammatic representation mandatory — a minimum block diagram with these five blocks fetches full 7 marks.)*

---

**Model Solution to Q-A (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given Data:**
- Battery capacity $C_{bat} = 50$ kWh
- Initial SoC = 20 % → Initial stored energy = $0.20 \times 50 = 10$ kWh
- Charging rate $P_{ch} = 11$ kW, duration $\Delta t_{ch} = 4$ h
- Discharging rate $P_{dis} = 12$ kW, duration $\Delta t_{dis} = 2$ h
- $\eta_{ch} = 0.92$, $\eta_{dis} = 0.95$
- $R_{draw} = \text{₹}5$/kWh, $R_{inj} = \text{₹}11$/kWh

**(i) SoC at end of charging window (3 AM):**

Energy drawn from grid during 4 hours:

$$
E_{draw} = P_{ch} \times \Delta t_{ch} = 11 \times 4 = 44 \text{ kWh}
$$

Energy actually stored in battery (after charging losses):

$$
E_{stored} = E_{draw} \times \eta_{ch} = 44 \times 0.92 = 40.48 \text{ kWh}
$$

Final stored energy:

$$
E_{final} = E_{initial} + E_{stored} = 10 + 40.48 = 50.48 \text{ kWh}
$$

Final SoC:

$$
SoC_{final} = \frac{E_{final}}{C_{bat}} = \frac{50.48}{50} = 1.0096 = 100.96\%
$$

> Since the battery is **fully charged** (capped at 100 %), the EV stops accepting charge once $SoC = 100\%$. So the practical SoC is 100 %.

**Effective charging time to reach 100 % SoC:**

$$
\Delta t_{full} = \frac{(C_{bat} - E_{initial})}{P_{ch} \times \eta_{ch}} = \frac{50 - 10}{11 \times 0.92} = \frac{40}{10.12} = 3.95 \text{ h}
$$

So the EV reaches 100 % SoC in approximately **3.95 hours** (just before 3 AM). The remaining ~0.05 h is spent in idle/standby. *(2.5 marks)*

**(ii) Energy fed back to the grid during 6 PM – 8 PM discharge:**

Energy delivered by battery to the inverter (DC side):

$$
E_{bat,out} = P_{dis} \times \Delta t_{dis} = 12 \times 2 = 24 \text{ kWh}
$$

Energy injected to grid (AC side) after inverter losses:

$$
E_{inj} = E_{bat,out} \times \eta_{dis} = 24 \times 0.95 = 22.8 \text{ kWh}
$$

$$
\boxed{E_{inj} = 22.8 \text{ kWh}}
$$

*(2 marks)*

**(iii) Net daily revenue:**

Cost of energy purchased:

$$
C_{buy} = E_{draw} \times R_{draw} = 44 \times 5 = \text{₹}220
$$

Revenue from energy sold:

$$
R_{sell} = E_{inj} \times R_{inj} = 22.8 \times 11 = \text{₹}250.8
$$

Net daily revenue:

$$
R_{net} = R_{sell} - C_{buy} = 250.8 - 220 = \text{₹}30.8
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{net} = \text{₹}30.8 \text{ per day}}
$$

*(2.5 marks)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the various **grid services** that a V2G-enabled EV fleet can provide. Compare V2G with V2H and V2B modes of operation. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With reference to bidirectional EV chargers, explain the working of an **Active Front-End (AFE) rectifier** and its role in unity power factor correction and regenerative inversion during V2G mode. Include the relevant phasor diagram and harmonic spectrum considerations. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution to Q-B (a) — 7 marks:**

A V2G fleet can monetise its flexibility through **four categories of grid services**:

1. **Frequency Regulation (Primary Response):** V2G can modulate power in <1 second — orders of magnitude faster than thermal generators (typical response 4–10 seconds). This makes it ideal for **FCAS (Frequency Control Ancillary Services)** markets. *(1.5 marks)*

2. **Spinning / Non-spinning Reserve:** EVs parked for >6 hours/day are pre-qualified as reserve capacity. They can be dispatched within 10–15 minutes. *(1 mark)*

3. **Peak Shaving / Load Shifting:** Discharging during peak tariff hours (6 PM–10 PM) reduces the evening ramp — a critical issue in India where the 7 PM peak often strains generation. *(1.5 marks)*

4. **Voltage & Reactive Power Support:** The bidirectional inverter can absorb/inject reactive power (Q) through VAR control, helping regulate PCC voltage within ±5 % statutory band. *(1 mark)*

**Comparison Table (V2G vs V2H vs V2B):** *(2 marks)*

| Feature | V2G | V2H | V2B |
|---|---|---|---|
| Recipient | Public grid | Residential home | Commercial building |
| Aggregation needed | Yes (large fleet) | No (single EV) | No/Optional |
| Revenue stream | FCAS market, ToU | Self-consumption savings | Demand charge reduction |
| Grid code compliance | Strict (CEA + DISCOM) | Relaxed (behind-the-meter) | Medium |
| Power flow | Bidirectional w/ grid | Bidirectional w/ home bus | Bidirectional w/ building |

---

**Model Solution to Q-B (b) — 7 marks:**

The **Active Front-End (AFE) rectifier** is a **bidirectional, self-commutated voltage source converter** using IGBTs with sinusoidal PWM (SVPWM). It replaces the conventional diode bridge + boost PFC front-end.

**In G2V (Rectifier) Mode:**
- The IGBT bridge draws current from the grid at near-unity power factor (PF > 0.99).
- Active and reactive currents are independently controlled via **d-q axis current control** (synchronous reference frame, SRF-PLL).
- DC-link voltage is regulated to a fixed reference (e.g., 700 V for 400 V battery systems). *(2 marks)*

**In V2G (Inverter) Mode:**
- The same IGBTs operate in anti-parallel PWM mode, **synthesising a 50 Hz sine wave** that is phase-locked to the grid voltage via a PLL.
- Power flows from DC bus → AC grid, exporting stored energy. *(2 marks)*

**Phasor Considerations:**
- Unity PF means the current phasor $\bar{I}$ is in-phase with $\bar{V}$ in G2V; in V2G the current is **180° out of phase** with voltage (active power reversal), or shifted by 90° for reactive power injection.
- LCL filter at the AC side attenuates switching-frequency harmonics to meet **IEEE 519 / IEEE 1547** standards (THD < 5 %). *(1.5 marks)*

**Harmonic Spectrum:**
- Switching frequency $f_{sw} = 10$–20 kHz (above audible band).
- Harmonic clusters appear at $f_{sw} \pm n \cdot f_0$ where $f_0 = 50$ Hz.
- SVPWM achieves 15 % lower switching loss than SPWM at the same harmonic profile. *(1.5 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing SoC and DoD:** DoD = 1 − SoC. Mixing them in a derivation costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Ignoring efficiency in revenue calculations:** Many students compute $R_{net} = (E_{draw} \times R_{sell}) - (E_{draw} \times R_{buy})$ — this is WRONG. You must apply charging AND discharging efficiencies separately.
> 3. **Forgetting to cap SoC at 100 %:** A battery cannot exceed 100 % SoC. Always clamp the result.
> 4. **Calling V2H and V2G the same:** V2H is behind-the-meter (no grid interaction), V2G is grid-facing. Examiners deduct 1-2 marks for this conflation.
> 5. **Missing the communication standard:** Any block diagram answer without **ISO 15118** or **OCPP** is considered incomplete under 2024 scheme rubric.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **V2G = Bidirectional power flow** between EV battery and grid through an AFE bidirectional converter.
- **Three pillars** of V2G: **Hardware (bidi charger) + Software (ISO 15118/OCPP) + Market (Aggregator/FCAS).**
- **Communication Protocol:** ISO 15118 (top layer), OCPP 2.0.1 (charger-to-backend), HomePlug Green PHY (physical layer over PLC).
- **Five V2X flavours to remember:** G2V, V2G, V2H, V2B, V2L.
- **Round-trip efficiency** $\eta_{V2G,round} = \eta_{ch} \times \eta_{dis}$ — typically 85-92 %.
- **SoC dynamics:** $\frac{d(SoC)}{dt} = \frac{P_{bat}(t)}{E_{bat}}$ — sign convention is critical.
- **Operating window:** Most OEMs restrict V2G to **20 % – 80 % SoC** to minimise calendar + cyclic degradation.
- **Economic driver:** Time-of-Use (ToU) tariff spread in India (e.g., ₹5 vs ₹12) is the primary profit source.
- **Grid services:** Frequency regulation, peak shaving, spinning reserve, voltage/VAR support.
- **Aggregator:** The essential intermediary that enables individual EVs to participate in wholesale markets.
- **Standards & codes to cite:** ISO 15118, IEEE 1547, IEEE 519, CEA (Central Electricity Authority) Regulations 2019, CERC (Central Electricity Regulatory Commission) Anciliary Services Rules 2022.
- **Key EVs in V2G (global):** Nissan Leaf, Mitsubishi Outlander PHEV, Hyundai Ioniq 5 (V2L), BYD Atto 3.
- **Indian context:** Kerala State Electricity Board (KSEB) launched India's first **EV charging tariff** in 2022 — the foundation for future V2G tariffs.
- **Future trend:** Kerala's **Kochi Metro Rail Limited** is exploring V2G for regenerative braking energy storage via metro-train lithium batteries — a real-world KTU-relevant case study.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "V2G-CASH":**
> **V**oltage support, **A**ggregator, **S**oC window, **H**ardware (bidirectional converter), **C**ommunication (ISO 15118), **A**ncillary services, **S**ettlement (FCAS market), **H**ome (V2H mode). — Use this 8-letter acronym to structure any 7-mark descriptive answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vehicle Dynamics & Load Forces - Mathematical Models to Describe Vehicle Performance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET742 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES AND RENEWABLE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Vehicle Dynamics & Load Forces - Mathematical Models to Describe Vehicle Performance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1: Vehicle Dynamics & Load Forces

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Vehicle Dynamics (KTU 2024 Definition):** Vehicle dynamics is the study of the forces and moments acting on a ground vehicle and the resulting motion of the vehicle, encompassing longitudinal, lateral, and vertical dynamics. In the context of Electric Vehicles (EVs), it is the analytical framework used to mathematically model tractive effort, resistive forces, and energy consumption to predict acceleration, grade-climbing ability, and range performance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Load Forces (Resistive Forces):** The aggregate of all opposing forces a vehicle must overcome to maintain or change its velocity. They are: **(i) Rolling Resistance, (ii) Aerodynamic Drag, (iii) Grade/Climbing Resistance, and (iv) Inertial (Acceleration) Force.** The propulsive (tractive) force from the motor MUST balance these to satisfy the **Fundamental Equation of Vehicle Performance**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are pushing a loaded shopping trolley up a slight incline on a windy day. To move it forward at a constant speed, you must push hard enough to overcome **three** things at once:

1. **The friction of the wheels on the floor** → this is **Rolling Resistance**.
2. **The wind pushing against the trolley from the front** → this is **Aerodynamic Drag**.
3. **The slope of the ground trying to pull the trolley backward** → this is **Grade Resistance**.

If you want to **speed up** (accelerate), you need an *extra* push proportional to the trolley's mass — this is **Inertial Force** (Newton's Second Law, $F=ma$).

> [!TIP]
> **The Golden Rule of Vehicle Design:** "The motor tractive force is the BANK ACCOUNT; load forces are the BILLS. Performance is determined by which is bigger at any given moment." This is exactly what the **tractive force equation** quantifies.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $\rho$ | Air density (sea level, 25°C) | **1.225 kg/m³** |
| $g$ | Acceleration due to gravity | **9.81 m/s²** |
| $C_d$ | Drag coefficient (typical sedan) | **0.25 – 0.35** |
| $A_f$ | Frontal area (typical car) | **2.0 – 2.5 m²** |
| $f_r$ | Rolling resistance coefficient | **0.008 – 0.015** (car tire on asphalt) |
| $\eta_{dr}$ | Drivetrain efficiency | **0.85 – 0.95** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Load Forces — Structured Logical Breakdown

### 🔹 Force 1: Rolling Resistance ($F_{rr}$)
- **Why it exists:** Tires deform as they roll, dissipating energy as heat (hysteresis). On soft surfaces, the tire sinks in and the front half is constantly being pushed up.
- **How to model:** Empirically proportional to vehicle weight (normal force on tires) and independent of velocity at low speeds.
- **Key insight:** At highway speeds, the hysteresis loss actually grows with velocity, so an improved model includes a linear velocity term.

### 🔹 Force 2: Aerodynamic Drag ($F_{ad}$)
- **Why it exists:** Air has mass. To push through it, the vehicle must accelerate air out of the way, creating pressure differentials and turbulent wake.
- **How to model:** Proportional to the **square of velocity** (kinetic energy rate of displaced air). This makes drag the *dominant* force at high speeds.
- **Key insight:** Drag power is proportional to $V^3$ — doubling speed **multiplies power required to overcome drag by 8**.

### 🔹 Force 3: Grade/Climbing Resistance ($F_{hc}$)
- **Why it exists:** Gravity pulls the vehicle straight down. On an incline, only a component along the road acts to resist motion.
- **How to model:** Direct projection of weight vector along the slope angle $\alpha$.
- **Key insight:** A 10% grade means 10 m rise per 100 m road. For a 1500 kg car, this is roughly **1470 N** of extra demand.

### 🔹 Force 4: Inertial/Acceleration Force ($F_{ia}$)
- **Why it exists:** Mass resists changes in velocity (Newton's First Law). For EVs, rotating masses (rotors, gears, wheels) also store kinetic energy.
- **How to model:** $F = m \cdot a$ for translation, with an equivalent mass factor for rotating parts.
- **Key insight:** During rapid acceleration in an EV, this is often the **largest** instantaneous load.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rolling Resistance (basic) | $F_{rr} = f_r \cdot m \cdot g \cos\alpha$ | N | $\alpha$ is grade angle |
| 2 | Rolling Resistance (velocity-dep) | $F_{rr} = f_0 \cdot m \cdot g + f_1 \cdot m \cdot g \cdot V$ | N | More accurate at $V > 60$ km/h |
| 3 | Aerodynamic Drag | $F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f V^2$ | N | $V$ in **m/s** |
| 4 | Grade Resistance | $F_{hc} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\alpha \approx m \cdot g \cdot G$ | N | $G$ = grade in fraction (e.g. 0.10) |
| 5 | Acceleration Force | $F_{ia} = m \cdot \delta \cdot a = m \cdot (1+\gamma) \cdot a$ | N | $\delta$ = mass factor |
| 6 | Total Tractive Force | $F_t = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{ia}$ | N | The **master equation** |
| 7 | Tractive Power | $P_t = F_t \cdot V$ | W | Mechanical at wheels |
| 8 | Mass Factor | $\delta = 1 + \dfrac{I_w}{m \cdot r^2} + \dfrac{I_m \cdot i_g^2 \eta_g}{m \cdot r^2}$ | — | Rotational inertia lumped into translation |
| 9 | Vehicle Kinetic Energy | $KE = \tfrac{1}{2} m \delta V^2$ | J | Used for regenerative braking |
| 10 | Energy per distance | $E_{sp} = \dfrac{F_t}{m \cdot g \cdot \eta_{dr}} \cdot 3600$ | Wh/km | Specific energy consumption |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Emphasis:** The **tractive force equation** ($F_t = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{ia}$) is a **favourite 14-mark question**. Memorize the direction of each term and whether the angle is $\sin$ (grade) or $\cos$ (rolling). This single equation is the spine of EV performance modeling.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **EV Powertrain Sizing:** Motor kW rating = Peak $F_t \times V_{max}$ (with 20–30% safety margin).
- **Range Estimation (EPA/WLTP):** Energy per km from $F_t$ integrated over a *standardized driving cycle* (UDDS, NEDC, WLTP).
- **Battery Pack Sizing:** $\text{Energy} = \int F_t \cdot V \, dt / \eta_{drivetrain}$.
- **Regenerative Braking Design:** Kinetic energy $\tfrac{1}{2} m \delta V^2$ determines max regen energy that can be recovered.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Grade Resistance from Free Body Diagram

**Starting premise:** A vehicle of mass $m$ is on an incline of angle $\alpha$ from horizontal. The weight $W = mg$ acts vertically downward.

**Step 1 — Resolve the weight vector along the slope axes.**

The weight vector $mg$ can be decomposed into two components:
- Perpendicular to road surface: $W_{\perp} = m g \cos\alpha$ (this is balanced by the normal force $N$ from the road).
- Parallel to road surface, directed down the slope: $W_{\parallel} = m g \sin\alpha$.

**Step 2 — Identify the grade force.**

The component of gravity acting along the road surface, pulling the vehicle backward when going uphill, is the grade resistance. We write:

$$F_{hc} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\alpha$$

**Step 3 — Approximation for small angles.**

For typical road grades, $\alpha < 15°$. Using the small-angle approximation $\sin\alpha \approx \tan\alpha$, and noting that road grade is conventionally defined as $G = \tan\alpha$ (rise/run):

$$F_{hc} \approx m \cdot g \cdot G$$

**Worked numerical check:** A 1500 kg car on a 10% grade ($\alpha = 5.71°$):

$$F_{hc} = 1500 \times 9.81 \times \sin(5.71°)$$

$$F_{hc} = 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.0995 \approx 1464 \text{ N}$$

Using the approximation: $F_{hc} \approx 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.10 = 1471.5 \text{ N}$. Error is only **0.5%** — perfectly acceptable for engineering.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Mass Factor for Rotating Inertia

**Starting premise:** When a vehicle accelerates linearly with acceleration $a$, the wheels and motor rotors also have angular acceleration $\dot\omega = a/r$ (assuming no-slip rolling).

**Step 1 — Write kinetic energy of all moving parts.**

$$KE_{total} = \underbrace{\tfrac{1}{2} m V^2}_{\text{translation}} + \underbrace{\tfrac{1}{2} I_w \omega^2}_{\text{wheels}} + \underbrace{\tfrac{1}{2} I_m \omega_m^2}_{\text{motor rotor}}$$

**Step 2 — Express angular velocities in terms of linear velocity $V$.**

For a wheel of radius $r$: $\omega = V/r$. For the motor, with gearbox ratio $i_g$: $\omega_m = i_g \cdot \omega = i_g \cdot V/r$.

**Step 3 — Substitute.**

$$KE_{total} = \tfrac{1}{2} m V^2 + \tfrac{1}{2} I_w \left(\tfrac{V}{r}\right)^2 + \tfrac{1}{2} I_m \left(\tfrac{i_g V}{r}\right)^2$$

$$KE_{total} = \tfrac{1}{2} V^2 \left[ m + \tfrac{I_w}{r^2} + \tfrac{I_m \cdot i_g^2}{r^2} \right]$$

**Step 4 — Define the mass factor $\delta$ so that $KE = \tfrac{1}{2} \delta m V^2$.**

$$\delta = 1 + \dfrac{I_w}{m \cdot r^2} + \dfrac{I_m \cdot i_g^2}{m \cdot r^2}$$

**Step 5 — Account for drivetrain efficiency during traction.**

During acceleration, power flows from motor to wheels *with* gear losses. Energy is *removed* by the inertia term (it accelerates the rotor), so the **effective** mass factor becomes:

$$\delta_{accel} = 1 + \dfrac{I_w}{m \cdot r^2} + \dfrac{I_m \cdot i_g^2 \cdot \eta_g}{m \cdot r^2}$$

**Step 6 — For regenerative braking (energy flows the other way), the efficiency term inverts:**

$$\delta_{brake} = 1 + \dfrac{I_w}{m \cdot r^2} + \dfrac{I_m \cdot i_g^2 / \eta_g}{m \cdot r^2}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Trick Question:** "Why is the mass factor different during acceleration vs. braking?" The above derivation gives the rigorous answer. Examiners love this — it shows you understand that efficiency and inertia are **multiplicative** in the energy domain.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: The Master Tractive Force Equation

**Step 1 — Free Body Diagram of a vehicle on an inclined road accelerating forward.**

The forces acting on the vehicle along the direction of travel are:
- Propulsive force $F_t$ at the wheel-ground contact (from motor torque).
- Rolling resistance $F_{rr}$ opposing motion.
- Aerodynamic drag $F_{ad}$ opposing motion.
- Grade component $F_{hc} = m g \sin\alpha$ opposing motion (uphill).

**Step 2 — Apply Newton's Second Law along the road.**

$$F_t - F_{rr} - F_{ad} - F_{hc} = m \cdot \delta \cdot a$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to obtain the tractive force equation:**

$$\boxed{F_t = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + m \cdot \delta \cdot a}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the component equations (for a car on a flat road, $a=0$, $\alpha=0$):**

$$F_t = f_r \cdot m \cdot g + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f V^2 + m \cdot g \cdot G + m \cdot \delta \cdot a$$

**Step 5 — Compute the corresponding tractive power.**

Since $P = F \cdot V$ at the wheel:

$$P_t = V \left[ f_r \cdot m g + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f V^2 + m g G + m \delta a \right]$$

This is the **mechanical power demand** that the battery + motor + drivetrain must supply.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Performance Calculation Strategy

| Performance Question | Required Equations | What to Solve For |
|---|---|---|
| Maximum grade at constant speed | Set $a=0$, solve for $G$ | $\sin\alpha_{max} = (F_t - F_{rr} - F_{ad})/(mg)$ |
| Maximum acceleration on flat road | Set $G=0$, solve for $a$ | $a = (F_t - F_{rr} - F_{ad})/(m\delta)$ |
| Top speed on flat road | Set $a=0$, $G=0$ | Solve $F_t(V) = F_{rr}(V) + F_{ad}(V)$ |
| Range estimation | Integrate $P_t/V$ over driving cycle | $E_{consumed} = \int F_t \, ds$ |
| Time to accelerate 0–100 km/h | Integrate $1/a \, dV$ | $t = \int_0^{V_f} (m\delta / (F_t - F_{loads})) dV$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block: Tractive Force Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Battery Pack Energy Source] --> B[Power Electronics Inverter]
    B --> C[Electric Motor Torque Generation]
    C --> D[Transmission and Gearbox iG]
    D --> E[Drivetrain Efficiency eta]
    E --> F[Wheel Torque at Road Interface]
    F --> G[Tractive Force Ft at Contact Patch]
    G --> H{Vehicle Dynamics Equation}
    H --> I[Rolling Resistance Frr]
    H --> J[Aerodynamic Drag Fad]
    H --> K[Grade Resistance Fhc]
    H --> L[Inertial Force Fia]
    I --> M[Net Vehicle Acceleration Output]
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Performance Metrics Vmax Grade Acceleration Range]
    N --> O[Driver Feedback and Control Loop]
    O --> B
    style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style M fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block: Force Decomposition on an Incline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V[Vehicle Mass m] --> W[Weight Vector W = mg Downward]
    W --> P[Perpendicular Component mg cos alpha]
    W --> G[Parallel Component mg sin alpha Grade Resistance]
    P --> N[Normal Force N from Road]
    G --> FT[Tractive Force Required Ft]
    RR[Rolling Resistance fr mg cos alpha] --> FT
    AD[Aerodynamic Drag 0.5 rho Cd Af V squared] --> FT
    IA[Inertial Force m delta a] --> FT
    FT --> OUT[Net Performance]
    style W fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style FT fill:#82e0aa,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style OUT fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block: Energy Flow in an EV (Longitudinal Dynamics View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    E[Chemical Energy in Battery kWh] --> E1[Electrical Energy to Inverter]
    E1 --> E2[AC Electrical Energy to Motor]
    E2 --> E3[Mechanical Rotational Energy]
    E3 --> E4[Mechanical Translational Energy at Wheels]
    E4 --> E5[Kinetic and Potential Energy of Vehicle]
    E5 --> R1[Useful Work Moving Vehicle]
    E5 --> R2[Rolling Resistance Losses]
    E5 --> R3[Aerodynamic Drag Losses]
    E5 --> R4[Climbing Energy Stored as PE]
    E5 --> R5[Brake Thermal Losses]
    style E fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E5 fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style R1 fill:#82e0aa,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style R2 fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style R3 fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style R4 fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style R5 fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define the four resistive forces acting on a moving vehicle. State the tractive force equation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 each for naming, formula, and 1 for units/direction):**

The four resistive forces opposing vehicle motion are:

1. **Rolling Resistance ($F_{rr}$):** Force due to tire deformation and road friction. $F_{rr} = f_r \cdot m g \cos\alpha$.
2. **Aerodynamic Drag ($F_{ad}$):** Force due to air resistance, proportional to $V^2$. $F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f V^2$.
3. **Grade/Climbing Resistance ($F_{hc}$):** Component of gravity along the slope. $F_{hc} = m g \sin\alpha$.
4. **Inertial Force ($F_{ia}$):** Force to overcome mass during acceleration. $F_{ia} = m \delta a$.

The **tractive force equation** is:

$$F_t = f_r m g \cos\alpha + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f V^2 + m g \sin\alpha + m \delta a$$

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024] What is the "mass factor" in vehicle dynamics? Why is it greater than 1?**

**Model Answer:**

The **mass factor** $\delta$ is a dimensionless multiplier (≥1) that accounts for the kinetic energy of the **rotating parts** (wheels, motor rotor, driveline) in addition to the vehicle's translational mass during acceleration.

It is defined as:

$$\delta = 1 + \dfrac{I_w}{m r^2} + \dfrac{I_m i_g^2 \eta_g}{m r^2}$$

It is greater than 1 because accelerating the wheels and motor rotors requires **additional torque** beyond what is needed to accelerate the vehicle body. For a typical car, $\delta \approx 1.05$ to $1.15$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set A]**

**(a)** Derive the tractive force equation of a road vehicle from Newton's second law, considering all resistive forces on a road with grade $\alpha$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A passenger EV has the following specifications: $m = 1500$ kg, $C_d = 0.30$, $A_f = 2.2$ m², $f_r = 0.012$, drivetrain efficiency $\eta = 0.90$, $\delta = 1.10$. Calculate the power required at the wheels and at the battery to cruise at 90 km/h on a level road. Take $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):** See **Section 3.3** of these notes. The full derivation is the model answer: free body diagram, Newton's second law, identification of all four force terms, and final rearranged equation.

**(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):**

Convert velocity: $V = 90$ km/h $= 90/3.6 = 25$ m/s.

**Step 1 — Rolling Resistance:**

$$F_{rr} = f_r \cdot m \cdot g = 0.012 \times 1500 \times 9.81 = 176.58 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Aerodynamic Drag:**

$$F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f V^2 = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times (25)^2$$

$$F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times 625 = 247.5 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Grade Resistance:** Flat road, $\alpha = 0$, so $F_{hc} = 0$.

**Step 4 — Acceleration Force:** Constant speed, $a = 0$, so $F_{ia} = 0$.

**Step 5 — Total Tractive Force:**

$$F_t = 176.58 + 247.5 + 0 + 0 = 424.08 \text{ N}$$

**Step 6 — Power at the wheels:**

$$P_{wheel} = F_t \cdot V = 424.08 \times 25 = 10602 \text{ W} \approx 10.6 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 7 — Power at the battery (input):**

$$P_{batt} = \dfrac{P_{wheel}}{\eta_{drivetrain}} = \dfrac{10602}{0.90} = 11780 \text{ W} \approx 11.78 \text{ kW}$$

**Final Answer:** $P_{wheel} \approx 10.6$ kW, $P_{battery} \approx 11.78$ kW.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Set B]**

**(a)** Explain with a free body diagram the four resistive forces acting on a road vehicle. How does each force vary with vehicle velocity? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An electric bus of mass 12,000 kg is to operate on a road with 8% grade. The rolling resistance coefficient is 0.015, $C_d A_f = 5.5$ m², $\delta = 1.08$, $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³. If the motor can deliver a maximum tractive force of 25 kN, calculate the maximum constant speed the bus can maintain up the grade. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Explanation (7 marks):** See **Section 2.1** of these notes. Award 1.5 marks each for naming, formula, and velocity dependence of the four forces.

| Force | Velocity Dependence |
|---|---|
| Rolling resistance $F_{rr}$ | Approximately **constant** (basic model); linear in $V$ (advanced model) |
| Aerodynamic drag $F_{ad}$ | **Quadratic** in $V$ ($\propto V^2$) |
| Grade resistance $F_{hc}$ | **Independent** of $V$ |
| Acceleration force $F_{ia}$ | Independent of $V$ (depends on $a$) |

**(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):**

Given: $m = 12000$ kg, $G = 0.08$, $f_r = 0.015$, $C_d A_f = 5.5$ m², $\delta = 1.08$, $F_t = 25000$ N, $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³, constant speed means $a = 0$.

**Step 1 — Rolling Resistance:**

$$F_{rr} = f_r \cdot m g = 0.015 \times 12000 \times 9.81 = 1765.8 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Grade Resistance (using $\sin\alpha \approx G$ for small angles):**

$$F_{hc} = m \cdot g \cdot G = 12000 \times 9.81 \times 0.08 = 9417.6 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Tractive force available for overcoming drag:**

$$F_{ad,max} = F_t - F_{rr} - F_{hc} = 25000 - 1765.8 - 9417.6 = 13816.6 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Aerodynamic drag equation:**

$$F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f V^2 = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 5.5 \times V^2 = 3.3 V^2$$

**Step 5 — Solve for maximum velocity:**

$$3.3 V^2 = 13816.6$$

$$V^2 = \dfrac{13816.6}{3.3} = 4186.85$$

$$V = \sqrt{4186.85} \approx 64.7 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to km/h:**

$$V_{max} = 64.7 \times 3.6 \approx 233 \text{ km/h}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common mistake 1 (–2 marks):** Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s. The drag formula uses $V$ in **m/s**. Mixing units is the #1 reason students lose marks.
> 2. **Common mistake 2 (–2 marks):** Using $\sin\alpha$ for grade resistance but providing $\alpha$ in degrees *and* mixing it with $\cos$ for rolling resistance. Always be consistent: use $G = \tan\alpha$ for both.
> 3. **Common mistake 3 (–1 mark):** Not specifying the assumption "$\sin\alpha \approx \tan\alpha = G$ for small $\alpha$". Examiners reward this statement.
> 4. **Common mistake 4 (–1 mark):** Forgetting the mass factor $\delta$ even though it is given in the problem. If $\delta$ is provided, you must use it.
> 5. **Common mistake 5 (–1 mark):** Writing $C_d \cdot A_f$ as a single product $C_d A_f$ — if the data sheet gives it as a single number (e.g., 5.5 m²), use that directly. Do **not** invent a separate $C_d$ value.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Vehicle Dynamics & Load Forces**

- ✅ The **master equation** is $F_t = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{ia}$. Memorize this in order.
- ✅ **Rolling resistance** uses $\cos\alpha$; **grade resistance** uses $\sin\alpha$. This is the easiest place to lose 2 marks.
- ✅ **Aerodynamic drag** is $\tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f V^2$ — and $V$ MUST be in m/s.
- ✅ **Drag power** scales as $V^3$: a car at 120 km/h needs 8× the drag power of the same car at 60 km/h.
- ✅ The **mass factor** $\delta > 1$ accounts for rotating inertia. It is different for traction vs. braking because of efficiency placement.
- ✅ For small grades, $\sin\alpha \approx \tan\alpha = G$ (grade fraction).
- ✅ **Maximum grade at constant speed:** $G_{max} = (F_t - F_{rr} - F_{ad})/(mg)$.
- ✅ **Maximum acceleration on flat road:** $a_{max} = (F_t - F_{rr} - F_{ad})/(m\delta)$.
- ✅ **Top speed:** Solve $F_t(V) = F_{rr}(V) + F_{ad}(V)$ for $V$.
- ✅ **Energy for a trip:** Integrate tractive power over distance: $E = \int F_t \, ds$.
- ✅ **Specific energy consumption** in Wh/km is the standard metric for EV range comparison.
- ✅ Standard values to remember: $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s².
- ✅ Typical sedan: $C_d A_f \approx 0.7$ m², truck: $C_d A_f \approx 4$–$6$ m².
- ✅ The **tractive force equation** is symmetric: in cruising, $F_t = F_{loads}$; in accelerating, $F_t = F_{loads} + ma$.

**End of Module 1 Topic Notes — Vehicle Dynamics & Load Forces**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vehicle Load Forces: Aerodynamic Drag
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET742 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES AND RENEWABLE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Vehicle Load Forces: Aerodynamic Drag

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In vehicle dynamics and EV powertrain engineering, **Aerodynamic Drag** is defined as the resistive force exerted on a body (vehicle) moving through a fluid medium (air) due to the relative motion between the body and the fluid. It acts parallel to the direction of flow and opposes the vehicle's forward motion, thereby acting as a critical **load force** that the traction motor must overcome.

Mathematically, it is the resultant of distributed pressure and viscous shear stresses integrated over the entire wetted surface of the vehicle:

$$F_d = \oint (p\,\hat{n} + \tau_w)\,dA$$

where the integral is taken over the projected frontal area exposed to the airflow. For engineering estimation, this complex surface integral is reduced to a single scalar force using empirically derived coefficients.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Aerodynamic drag is classified as a **speed-dependent load force** (∝ v²), as opposed to rolling resistance (∝ v) and gravitational force (∝ constant). This distinction is critical for energy management in EVs.

## 1.2 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard values are used universally in KTU reference problems:

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value (Sea Level, 15 °C) | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Air density (ISA) | $\rho$ | **1.225** | kg/m³ |
| Dynamic viscosity of air | $\mu$ | **1.81 × 10⁻⁵** | Pa·s |
| Kinematic viscosity of air | $\nu$ | **1.48 × 10⁻⁵** | m²/s |
| Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | **9.81** | m/s² |
| Speed of sound in air | $c$ | **340.3** | m/s |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedaling Analogy — "Riding into the Wind"**
>
> Imagine you are cycling at 25 km/h on a calm day. You feel a modest resistance. Now imagine cycling at 50 km/h on the same road. The resistance on your palms, face, and chest is **four times greater** — not twice. This is because the air molecules you are pushing aside every second increase in proportion to speed, AND the energy of disturbance increases with the square of speed. The wind you create is the *wake*, and the energy lost to it is the **aerodynamic drag**.

A typical family sedan with a drag coefficient of 0.30 and frontal area of 2.2 m² cruising at 100 km/h experiences approximately **520 N of drag** — equivalent to lifting a 53 kg bag of cement. At 150 km/h, this jumps to nearly **1170 N**.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Sanity Check (KTU Pattern):** If you double the speed, drag force quadruples (2² = 4). If you halve the drag coefficient, the drag force halves. These are the most-tested relationships in board examinations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of Total Aerodynamic Drag

The total aerodynamic drag of a road vehicle is the sum of **four** distinct physical mechanisms. KTU expects students to be able to name and qualitatively rank them.

1. **Pressure Drag (Form Drag / Shape Drag)**
   - Caused by flow separation at the rear of the vehicle.
   - The low-pressure wake region behind the vehicle "sucks" the vehicle backward.
   - For bluff bodies (trucks, vans): this is the dominant component (~60–80%).
   - Reduced by **streamlining** (tapered tail, fastback roofline).

2. **Skin Friction Drag (Viscous Drag)**
   - Caused by the no-slip condition of fluid at the body surface.
   - The thin **boundary layer** near the body surface has a velocity gradient; viscous shear in this layer dissipates energy.
   - Depends on surface roughness, Reynolds number, and whether the boundary layer is laminar or turbulent.
   - Reduced by smooth surface finishes and flush-mounted glass.

3. **Induced Drag (Lift-Induced Drag)**
   - Associated with the generation of lift (or downforce) on a body in motion.
   - For road vehicles, this is usually small unless aerodynamic aids (spoilers, wings) are added.
   - Governed by an aerodynamic efficiency factor $L/D$ (lift-to-drag ratio).

4. **Internal / Parasitic Drag**
   - Airflow through the radiator, engine bay, cabin ventilation, brake cooling ducts, and underbody.
   - Accounts for 10–15% of total drag in modern sedans.

## 2.2 The Drag Equation — Engineering Form

For board-level calculations, the surface integral is replaced by the following algebraic drag equation:

$$F_d = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_d\,A\,v^2$$

where:
- $F_d$ = Aerodynamic drag force [N]
- $\rho$ = Mass density of the fluid (air) [kg/m³]
- $C_d$ = Drag coefficient [dimensionless]
- $A$ = Frontal (projected) area [m²]
- $v$ = Relative velocity between vehicle and air [m/s]

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU 2024-exam-tested. Memorize the units.**

| # | Formula | Description | Typical KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_d = \frac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2$ | Drag force | Direct numerical problems |
| 2 | $P_d = F_d \cdot v = \frac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^3$ | Power to overcome drag | Energy consumption analysis |
| 3 | $E_d = F_d \cdot d = \frac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2 d$ | Energy vs distance | EV range estimation |
| 4 | $E_d = \int_0^T P_d\,dt$ | Energy over time interval | Variable-speed driving cycles |
| 5 | $P_d = \frac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^3 \eta_m^{-1}$ | Motor output (with efficiency $\eta_m$) | Powertrain sizing |
| 6 | $C_d = \frac{2F_d}{\rho A v^2}$ | Drag coefficient from experiment | Wind tunnel calculations |
| 7 | $Re = \frac{\rho v L}{\mu} = \frac{v L}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number | Boundary layer classification |
| 8 | $F_d \propto v^2$, $P_d \propto v^3$ | Scaling laws | "What-if" problems |
| 9 | $F_d$ (headwind) $= \frac{1}{2}\rho C_d A (v + v_w)^2$ | Effective speed with headwind $v_w$ | Real-world driving |
| 10 | $F_d \text{ reduction} = 1 - (C_{d,\text{new}}/C_{d,\text{old}})$ | % reduction in drag | Design optimization |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the electric vehicle industry, **aerodynamic efficiency is the single most important design lever for highway range extension**.

- **Tesla Model S**: $C_d = 0.208$ (one of the lowest production cars ever; verified by wind tunnel and on-road tests).
- **Mercedes EQS**: $C_d = 0.20$ (current record holder for production sedans).
- **Toyota Prius (4th Gen)**: $C_d = 0.24$.
- **Typical Indian SUVs**: $C_d \approx 0.35$–$0.42$.

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Insight:** A reduction of $C_d$ from 0.30 to 0.24 (a 20% drop) at a constant 100 km/h cruise extends EV range by roughly **6–8%** under standard NEDC/WLTP cycles. This is why EV manufacturers obsess over flush door handles, underbody panels, and active grille shutters.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Drag Force Equation (Dimensional Approach)

We begin with the assumption that the drag force $F_d$ depends on the fluid density $\rho$, the relative velocity $v$, and a characteristic length scale $L$ of the body (or the frontal area $A = L^2$).

**Step 1 — Set up the functional dependence.**

$$F_d = f(\rho,\,v,\,A) = k\,\rho^{a}\,v^{b}\,A^{c}$$

where $k$ is a dimensionless constant, later absorbed into the experimentally measured $C_d$.

**Step 2 — Enforce dimensional homogeneity.**

Using $[M, L, T]$ notation:

$$[F_d] = M L T^{-2} = M^{a} (L T^{-1})^{b} (L^2)^{c}$$

Equating exponents of $M$, $L$, and $T$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Mass:}&\quad 1 = a \quad \Rightarrow a = 1 \\
\text{Time:}&\quad -2 = -b \quad \Rightarrow b = 2 \\
\text{Length:}&\quad 1 = b + 2c \quad \Rightarrow 1 = 2 + 2c \quad \Rightarrow c = -\tfrac{1}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back.**

$$F_d = k\,\rho^{1}\,v^{2}\,A^{-1/2}$$

Hmm — this yields $A^{-1/2}$, not $A^{+1}$. The dimensional approach on $A$ alone fails because the body shape (encoded by $C_d$) is not scale-invariant. Empirically, it is observed that $F_d \propto A$ for geometrically similar bodies at the same Reynolds number. **This is the empirical scaling** that produces the working engineering equation:

$$\boxed{F_d = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_d\,A\,v^2}$$

The factor $\tfrac{1}{2}$ is a historical convention (introduced so that $C_d$ is reported as a clean number near 1.0 for spheres).

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Power Required to Overcome Drag

**Step 1 — Recall mechanical work rate.**

The instantaneous mechanical power needed to push the vehicle against the drag force at constant speed $v$ is:

$$P_d = F_d \cdot v$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the drag equation.**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_d &= \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2\right) \cdot v \\
&= \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^3
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Account for drivetrain efficiency.**

The electrical power drawn from the battery must be larger to compensate for losses in motor, inverter, and transmission:

$$P_{\text{batt}} = \frac{P_d}{\eta_{\text{mt}}}$$

where $\eta_{\text{mt}}$ is the combined motor-transmission efficiency (typically 0.85–0.92 for modern EVs).

**Step 4 — Energy over a distance.**

The energy consumed to travel a distance $d$ at constant speed $v$ is:

$$E_d = F_d \cdot d = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2 d$$

If the trip lasts time $T$ at constant speed, then $d = vT$ and:

$$E_d = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^3 T$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (KTU Board Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Problem (14-Mark Style)**
>
> An electric sedan has the following specifications:
> - Drag coefficient: $C_d = 0.28$
> - Frontal area: $A = 2.20\ \text{m}^2$
> - Mass (with driver): $m = 1650\ \text{kg}$
> - Rolling resistance coefficient: $C_{rr} = 0.012$
> - Drivetrain efficiency: $\eta_{mt} = 0.90$
> - Air density: $\rho = 1.225\ \text{kg/m}^3$
> - Cruise speed: $v = 110\ \text{km/h}$

**Question:** Compute (a) the drag force, (b) the power required to overcome drag, and (c) the battery energy consumed in a 200 km highway cruise.

### Solution — Sub-part (a): Drag Force

Convert speed: $v = 110\ \text{km/h} = 110 / 3.6 = 30.56\ \text{m/s}$.

$$\begin{aligned}
F_d &= \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2 \\
&= \tfrac{1}{2}(1.225)(0.28)(2.20)(30.56)^2 \\
&= \tfrac{1}{2}(1.225)(0.28)(2.20)(933.91) \\
&= 352.4\ \text{N}
\end{aligned}$$

### Solution — Sub-part (b): Power to Overcome Drag

$$\begin{aligned}
P_d &= F_d \cdot v = (352.4)(30.56) \\
&= 10{,}770\ \text{W} \approx 10.77\ \text{kW}
\end{aligned}$$

Battery-side power: $P_{\text{batt}} = 10.77 / 0.90 = 11.97\ \text{kW}$.

### Solution — Sub-part (c): Energy for 200 km

$$\begin{aligned}
E_d &= F_d \cdot d = (352.4)(200{,}000) \\
&= 7.048 \times 10^7\ \text{J} = 70.48\ \text{MJ}
\end{aligned}$$

In kWh: $E_d = 70.48 \times 10^6 / 3.6 \times 10^6 = 19.58\ \text{kWh}$.

Battery-side: $E_{\text{batt}} = 19.58 / 0.90 = 21.76\ \text{kWh}$.

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity Check:** A typical 75 kWh EV battery would therefore consume **29% of its capacity** for a 200 km highway cruise against drag alone (excluding rolling resistance, hill climbing, and auxiliary loads). This single fact explains why EVs are dramatically more efficient in city driving (low speed → low drag power) than highway driving.

---

## 3.4 The Critical Insight: $F_d \propto v^2$ and $P_d \propto v^3$

This is the **single most important scaling law** in vehicle energy analysis.

| Speed $v$ | Drag Force $F_d$ | Power $P_d$ | Relative Energy (same distance) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 50 km/h | $F_0$ | $P_0$ | 1.00× |
| 100 km/h | $4 F_0$ | $8 P_0$ | 2.00× (energy per km is doubled) |
| 150 km/h | $9 F_0$ | $27 P_0$ | 3.00× (energy per km is tripled) |
| 200 km/h | $16 F_0$ | $64 P_0$ | 4.00× (energy per km is quadrupled) |

**Engineering Implication:** Aerodynamic drag is the *dominant* energy consumer above approximately 70 km/h. Below that, rolling resistance and acceleration events dominate.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Decomposition of Aerodynamic Drag — Mermaid Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the *constituents of total aerodynamic drag*, their *causal mechanisms*, and the *design levers* used by EV engineers to mitigate them. This is the KTU-recommended way to answer "Explain the types of aerodynamic drag in road vehicles" type questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Aerodynamic Drag Fd] --> B[Pressure Drag]
    A --> C[Skin Friction Drag]
    A --> D[Induced Drag]
    A --> E[Internal Parasitic Drag]

    B --> B1[Flow separation at vehicle rear]
    B1 --> B2[Low pressure wake region]
    B2 --> B3[Net rearward suction force]

    C --> C1[No slip condition at body surface]
    C1 --> C2[Velocity gradient in boundary layer]
    C2 --> C3[Viscous shear stress dissipation]

    D --> D1[Lift generation over body]
    D1 --> D2[3D wing effect at finite span]
    D2 --> D3[Downwash induced drag component]

    E --> E1[Radiator and engine bay airflow]
    E --> E2[Cabin HVAC intake and exhaust]
    E --> E3[Brake cooling ducts]
    E --> E4[Underbody turbulent flow]

    B3 --> R1[Design Lever: Streamlined rear taper]
    C3 --> R2[Design Lever: Smooth surface finish]
    D3 --> R3[Design Lever: Low lift body shaping]
    E4 --> R4[Design Lever: Flat underbody panels]

    R1 --> OUT[Reduced Cd and extended EV range]
    R2 --> OUT
    R3 --> OUT
    R4 --> OUT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Each branch shows a *cause → mechanism → force* chain. The four redrawn "Design Lever" nodes at the bottom represent the four canonical countermeasures that EV manufacturers apply during aerodynamic refinement.

## 4.2 Energy Flow Architecture — From Battery to Air

The following block-level functional architecture shows how **battery energy** is progressively converted and partially lost to aerodynamic drag during highway cruise. This is the canonical answer scaffold for "Discuss the energy consumption due to aerodynamic drag in EVs."

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BATT[Battery Pack] --> INV[Traction Inverter]
    INV --> MTR[Electric Motor]
    MTR --> GEAR[Single Speed Gearbox]
    GEAR --> WHL[Drive Wheels]
    WHL --> VEH[Vehicle Body in Motion]
    VEH --> DRAG[Aerodynamic Drag Force Fd]
    DRAG --> WAKE[Pressure Wake plus Boundary Layer Loss]
    WAKE --> AIR[Atmospheric Heat Sink]
    INV -. 5 to 10 percent loss .-> AIR
    MTR -. 3 to 7 percent loss .-> AIR
    GEAR -. 2 to 4 percent loss .-> AIR
```

> [!TIP]
> **Note the dotted loss arrows:** The aerodynamic loss is the *terminal* loss — once energy enters the wake, it cannot be recovered. Compare this with regenerative braking, which partially recovers kinetic energy. This asymmetry is why EV manufacturers prioritise *minimising* drag in the first place, rather than recovering the lost energy.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PEAET742]`

**Define aerodynamic drag in a road vehicle. List any four design parameters that influence the drag coefficient $C_d$ of a passenger car.**

**Model Answer:**

Aerodynamic drag is the resistive force exerted by air on a vehicle moving through it, acting opposite to the direction of motion and arising from the combined effects of pressure differentials, viscous shear, and induced flow. It is given by:

$$F_d = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2$$

Four design parameters that influence $C_d$:
1. **Body shape** — streamlined profiles (e.g., fastback, teardrop) reduce $C_d$ to 0.20–0.25; bluff shapes (boxy vans) push $C_d$ above 0.40.
2. **Rear taper angle** — gradual rear taper reduces flow separation; abrupt rear ends create large low-pressure wakes.
3. **Surface smoothness** — flush glass, hidden door handles, and smooth panel gaps reduce skin friction and interference drag.
4. **Underbody design** — flat underbody panels and covered wheel wells reduce turbulent underbody flow.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PEAET742]`

**State the relationship between aerodynamic drag power and vehicle speed. Why is aerodynamic drag considered a critical parameter for highway range in electric vehicles?**

**Model Answer:**

The power required to overcome aerodynamic drag varies with the **cube of the speed**:

$$P_d = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^3$$

This cubic relationship means that doubling the speed increases the drag power eight-fold. Consequently, aerodynamic drag is the *dominant* energy loss mechanism during highway cruise. In an electric vehicle, where range is directly tied to battery energy, an inefficient aerodynamic design sharply reduces highway range. Reducing $C_d$ from 0.30 to 0.24 at 100 km/h can extend range by 6–8%, which is why EV manufacturers invest heavily in aerodynamic refinement.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PEAET742]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the power required to overcome the aerodynamic drag of a road vehicle. Clearly state all assumptions and define each term.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An electric bus has the following data: $C_d = 0.45$, $A = 7.5\ \text{m}^2$, $\rho = 1.2\ \text{kg/m}^3$, drivetrain efficiency $\eta_{mt} = 0.88$, cruise speed $v = 80\ \text{km/h}$. Calculate: (i) the drag force, (ii) the drag power at the wheels, and (iii) the battery energy consumed in a 100 km trip.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Define drag force.**

The drag force opposing the motion of a vehicle moving at velocity $v$ through air of density $\rho$ is given by:

$$F_d = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2$$

where $A$ is the projected frontal area and $C_d$ is the dimensionless drag coefficient that captures the body's shape-dependent flow physics.

**Step 2 — Mechanical power is force times velocity.**

The instantaneous mechanical power that the drivetrain must deliver to sustain constant speed against drag alone is:

$$P_{\text{wheel}} = F_d \cdot v$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the drag force.**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{wheel}} &= \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2\right) \cdot v \\
&= \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^3
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Account for drivetrain losses.**

The battery must supply:

$$P_{\text{batt}} = \frac{P_{\text{wheel}}}{\eta_{mt}}$$

**Assumptions:** (i) Steady, uniform, horizontal flow; (ii) No cross-wind component; (iii) Constant $C_d$ (no flow separation transition in speed range); (iv) Flat, level road (no grade); (v) Constant vehicle speed.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

Convert speed: $v = 80\ \text{km/h} = 22.22\ \text{m/s}$.

**(i) Drag force:**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_d &= \tfrac{1}{2}(1.2)(0.45)(7.5)(22.22)^2 \\
&= \tfrac{1}{2}(1.2)(0.45)(7.5)(493.83) \\
&= 999.5\ \text{N} \approx 1000\ \text{N}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Drag power at wheels:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{wheel}} &= F_d \cdot v = (999.5)(22.22) \\
&= 22{,}210\ \text{W} \approx 22.2\ \text{kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Battery energy for 100 km:**

Energy at wheels: $E_w = F_d \cdot d = (999.5)(100{,}000) = 9.995 \times 10^7\ \text{J} = 27.76\ \text{kWh}$.

Battery energy: $E_{\text{batt}} = 27.76 / 0.88 = 31.55\ \text{kWh}$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PEAET742]` (Alternative Choice)

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the different types of aerodynamic drag experienced by a road vehicle. How does each component arise physically?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Two electric vehicles travel at the same constant speed of 90 km/h. Vehicle X has $C_d = 0.30$, $A = 2.0\ \text{m}^2$, and $\eta_{mt} = 0.90$. Vehicle Y has $C_d = 0.24$, $A = 2.2\ \text{m}^2$, and $\eta_{mt} = 0.92$. Take $\rho = 1.225\ \text{kg/m}^3$. Calculate the percentage reduction in battery power consumption achieved by Vehicle Y.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

*(For the sketch, students must draw a side-profile of a car with annotations for: (i) flow separation at the rear, (ii) boundary layer along the body, (iii) lift force on a curved roof, and (iv) underbody airflow.)*

**1. Pressure (Form) Drag:**
Arises from the **pressure difference** between the front (high pressure, stagnation region) and rear (low pressure, separated wake) of the vehicle. When flow cannot follow the body's curvature at the rear, it separates, leaving a low-pressure turbulent wake. The resulting net pressure imbalance produces a force in the direction opposing motion. For bluff bodies (vans, trucks), pressure drag is 60–80% of total drag. **Mitigation:** gradual rear taper, fastback or notchback profiles, smooth underbody.

**2. Skin Friction Drag:**
Arises from **viscous shear stress** in the thin boundary layer adjacent to the body's surface. The fluid velocity varies from zero at the surface (no-slip condition) to free-stream value at the edge of the boundary layer, creating a velocity gradient that dissipates energy. **Mitigation:** smoother surface finishes, flush glass, sealed panel gaps.

**3. Induced Drag:**
Arises from the **three-dimensional lift** generated when a finite-span body moves through the air. The pressure difference between the upper and lower surfaces produces lift, and the resulting spanwise flow at the wingtips creates a trailing vortex system that induces additional drag. **Mitigation:** body shapes with minimal lift, addition of small integrated lips or spoilers.

**4. Internal (Parasitic) Drag:**
Arises from **air entering and exiting the vehicle** through the radiator, engine bay, HVAC system, brake cooling, and the underbody cavities. These flows create additional internal turbulence. **Mitigation:** active grille shutters, sealed underbody, optimised HVAC inlet design.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

Convert speed: $v = 90\ \text{km/h} = 25.0\ \text{m/s}$.

**Power at wheels (Vehicle X):**

$$P_{d,X} = \tfrac{1}{2}(1.225)(0.30)(2.0)(25.0)^3 = \tfrac{1}{2}(1.225)(0.30)(2.0)(15{,}625) = 5742\ \text{W}$$

Battery power (Vehicle X): $P_{\text{batt},X} = 5742 / 0.90 = 6380\ \text{W}$.

**Power at wheels (Vehicle Y):**

$$P_{d,Y} = \tfrac{1}{2}(1.225)(0.24)(2.2)(15{,}625) = 5053\ \text{W}$$

Battery power (Vehicle Y): $P_{\text{batt},Y} = 5053 / 0.92 = 5492\ \text{W}$.

**Percentage reduction:**

$$\Delta P\% = \frac{P_{\text{batt},X} - P_{\text{batt},Y}}{P_{\text{batt},X}} \times 100 = \frac{6380 - 5492}{6380} \times 100 = 13.92\%$$

Vehicle Y consumes approximately **13.9% less battery power** at 90 km/h cruise, despite having a slightly larger frontal area — a direct consequence of its lower $C_d$ and higher drivetrain efficiency.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (ESE Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Unit mismatch on velocity** — The drag equation requires $v$ in **m/s**, not km/h. Students who plug in 90 directly lose 1 mark immediately. Always convert: $v(\text{m/s}) = v(\text{km/h}) / 3.6$.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the $\tfrac{1}{2}$ factor** — The factor $\tfrac{1}{2}$ in the drag equation is a *convention*, not a constant. The $C_d$ value is defined to absorb this convention, so omitting $\tfrac{1}{2}$ gives a numerically wrong (and unjustifiable) answer. Lose 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Mixing up $F_d \propto v^2$ vs. $P_d \propto v^3$** — Many students write "drag is proportional to $v^3$" which is **false**. Force is $\propto v^2$; power is $\propto v^3$. This is a 2-mark conceptual loss.
>
> 4. **Not separating wheel-side vs. battery-side power** — When drivetrain efficiency is given, students often compute wheel-side power and present it as the final answer. The question specifically asks for battery energy. Deduct 1 mark per sub-part.
>
> 5. **Confusing frontal area $A$ with wetted surface area** — $A$ is the *orthogonal projection* of the body onto a plane perpendicular to the flow (i.e., what you see from the front). The wetted area is larger. Using wetted area is conceptually wrong and loses 1 mark.
>
> 6. **Neglecting headwind** — If the problem says "20 km/h headwind", the *effective* speed becomes $v + v_w$, not $v$. Missing this loses 2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — Aerodynamic Drag**

- **Master Formula:** $F_d = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2$ — drag is **quadratic in speed**.
- **Power Formula:** $P_d = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^3$ — drag power is **cubic in speed**.
- **Energy per unit distance:** $E_d/d = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2$ — this is the "drag cost per km".
- **Unit hygiene:** $v$ in m/s, $A$ in m², $\rho$ in kg/m³, $F_d$ in N, $P_d$ in W.
- **Standard air density (sea level, ISA):** $\rho = \mathbf{1.225\ \text{kg/m}^3}$.
- **Typical $C_d$ range:** Sedan 0.24–0.30, SUV 0.32–0.40, Truck 0.60–0.80, EV (best) 0.20–0.24.
- **Four drag components:** Pressure drag, Skin friction drag, Induced drag, Internal parasitic drag.
- **Dominant component for cars:** Pressure drag (~55–65%) and Internal parasitic drag (~10–15%).
- **Energy scaling:** Doubling speed → 4× force, 8× power, 2× energy per km.
- **Headwind adjustment:** $v_{\text{eff}} = v_{\text{vehicle}} + v_{\text{wind}}$ (headwind adds, tailwind subtracts).
- **Drivetrain correction:** $P_{\text{batt}} = P_{\text{wheel}} / \eta_{mt}$; $E_{\text{batt}} = E_{\text{wheel}} / \eta_{mt}$.
- **Time-domain form:** $E = \int_0^T \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^3\,dt$ (for variable-speed driving cycles).
- **Design levers for low $C_d$:** Streamlined rear taper, smooth surface finish, low-lift body, flat underbody, flush door handles, active grille shutters.
- **EV industry benchmark:** Tesla Model S ($C_d = 0.208$), Mercedes EQS ($C_d = 0.20$) — current production leaders.
- **Rule of thumb:** Aerodynamic drag dominates above ~70 km/h; rolling resistance dominates below that.
- **KTU-recommended structure for any drag problem:** (1) State the formula, (2) Convert all units, (3) Plug in values, (4) Show intermediate arithmetic, (5) State final answer with correct unit, (6) Sanity-check order of magnitude.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rolling Resistance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET742 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES AND RENEWABLE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Rolling Resistance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

**Rolling Resistance** (also known as **rolling friction** or **rolling drag**) is defined as the resistive force that opposes the motion of a body (such as a wheel or tire) when it rolls along a deformable surface, caused primarily by energy dissipation due to the non-elastic deformation of the rolling object and the supporting surface.

Mathematically expressed as:

$$F_{rr} = C_{rr} \cdot N = \mu_{rr} \cdot W$$

where:
- $F_{rr}$ = Rolling resistance force (in Newtons, **N**)
- $C_{rr}$ or $\mu_{rr}$ = Coefficient of Rolling Resistance (dimensionless)
- $N$ or $W$ = Normal load (in **N**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Rolling resistance is one of the **three primary resistive forces** acting on a moving vehicle — the other two being **Aerodynamic Drag** ($F_{ad}$) and **Grading/Climbing Resistance** ($F_{gc}$). For an Electric Vehicle (EV), minimizing rolling resistance is critical to **maximize driving range per kWh of battery energy**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine pushing a **heavy rubber ball across three different surfaces**:

1. **Smooth polished marble floor** — The ball rolls almost effortlessly, covering a long distance with minimal push.
2. **Soft gym mat** — The ball sinks slightly, and you can see the mat deform. You need to push harder, and the ball stops sooner.
3. **Deep sand** — The ball barely rolls because sand keeps shifting; energy is lost in rearranging the sand grains.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind Rolling Resistance:** A tire is not perfectly rigid. As it rolls, the section in contact with the ground **flattens** (this is called the **contact patch**). The flattening is *not* fully recovered — energy is lost as heat inside the rubber (called **hysteresis**). This energy loss manifests as a force opposing motion, which we call **rolling resistance**.

### Standard Engineering Metrics (Bold for Emphasis)

- Typical **Crr for car tires** on asphalt: **0.007 to 0.012**
- Typical **Crr for bicycle tires** on smooth road: **0.001 to 0.004**
- Typical **Crr for truck tires**: **0.005 to 0.010**
- Standard reference temperature: **25 °C**
- Standard inflation pressure for testing: **manufacturer-specified kPa**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Primary Causes of Rolling Resistance

Rolling resistance is not a single phenomenon — it is the **resultant of multiple energy-dissipation mechanisms**. The KTU syllabus expects students to identify each:

1. **Hysteresis Loss in Tire Material** (Most significant contributor, ~90% of total)
   - As the tire deforms, the rubber's polymer chains undergo internal friction.
   - The energy input during compression is **greater** than the energy recovered during rebound.
   - This difference is dissipated as **heat**.

2. **Surface Deformation Energy**
   - Both the tire and the road deform.
   - On soft roads (sand, mud, gravel), the energy lost in deforming the surface is substantial.

3. **Micro-Slip at Contact Patch**
   - The tire does not roll perfectly — slight slipping occurs at the contact area.
   - This causes frictional losses.

4. **Air Drag Inside Tire Cavity** (Minor)
   - For tubeless and pneumatic tires, internal air circulation dissipates some energy.

5. **Wheel Bearing Friction** (Sometimes grouped with rolling resistance)
   - Mechanical friction in the hub bearings.

### 2.2 Factors Affecting the Coefficient of Rolling Resistance ($C_{rr}$)

| **Factor** | **Effect on $C_{rr}$** | **Physical Reason** |
|---|---|---|
| Tire inflation pressure ↑ | $C_{rr}$ ↓ (decreases) | Less deformation = less hysteresis |
| Vehicle speed ↑ | $C_{rr}$ ↑ (slightly increases) | More rapid deformation cycles |
| Tire temperature ↑ | $C_{rr}$ ↓ initially, then ↑ | Rubber gets softer, then over-softens |
| Load on tire ↑ | $C_{rr}$ may ↓ slightly | Greater contact area, lower unit pressure |
| Road surface roughness ↑ | $C_{rr}$ ↑ | More vibration and micro-impact losses |
| Tire material (silica vs carbon black) | $C_{rr}$ varies | Different viscoelastic properties |
| Tire width ↑ | $C_{rr}$ ↓ | Lower contact pressure per unit area |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical for KTU Board Exam:** Master these formulas — they appear in almost every numerical problem involving vehicle propulsion force, range estimation, and energy consumption.

| **#** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_{rr} = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g$ | Rolling resistance force (on flat surface) | N |
| 2 | $P_{rr} = F_{rr} \cdot v$ | Power consumed to overcome rolling resistance | W |
| 3 | $E_{rr} = F_{rr} \cdot d$ | Energy lost to rolling resistance over distance $d$ | J |
| 4 | $F_{total} = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{gc}$ | Total tractive force required | N |
| 5 | $F_{ad} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^{2}$ | Aerodynamic drag force | N |
| 6 | $F_{gc} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\theta)$ | Grading (climbing) resistance | N |
| 7 | $C_{rr} = \dfrac{W_{loss}}{W \cdot d}$ | Definition of $C_{rr}$ as energy ratio | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\eta_{rr} = \dfrac{F_{rr}}{N}$ | Rolling resistance coefficient (alternative notation) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $P_{total} = v \cdot (F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{gc})$ | Total propulsion power demand | W |
| 10 | $E_{per km} = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot 1000$ | Energy per km against rolling resistance | J/km |

> **Notation Note:** Throughout KTU textbooks, $\vert x \vert$ for absolute value is written using `\vert x \vert` in LaTeX, **never** the bare pipe `|x|`, to prevent markdown table breakage.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **EV Range Estimation:** A reduction of $C_{rr}$ from **0.012 to 0.008** can improve EV range by **8–15%** on city roads.
- **Tire Industry:** Manufacturers like Michelin, Bridgestone invest billions in low-rolling-resistance tires (e.g., "Energy Saver" series).
- **Solar Car Racing (e.g., World Solar Challenge):** Teams use ultra-thin, high-pressure tires with $C_{rr} \approx 0.0015$.
- **Train Design:** Steel wheels on steel rails have $C_{rr} \approx 0.0005$ to 0.0020 — one of the most efficient transport modes.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Rolling Resistance from Tire Deflection Geometry

**Assumption:** The tire deforms symmetrically under load, and the resultant normal force shifts forward by a small distance $a$ from the geometric center of the contact patch.

Let:
- $W$ = Normal load on tire (N)
- $a$ = Horizontal offset of normal reaction from the wheel center (m)
- $r$ = Effective rolling radius (m)

**Step 1: Free Body Analysis of a Stationary Wheel**

Taking moments about the contact point to balance the wheel:

$$W \cdot a = F_{rr} \cdot r$$

**Explanation of Step 1:** The vertical load $W$ acts at a horizontal distance $a$ ahead of the wheel center (this is the key insight — the resultant shifts forward due to asymmetric deformation). The rolling resistance force $F_{rr}$ acts at the contact point and opposes motion. For the wheel in equilibrium, these two moments must balance.

**Step 2: Solving for Rolling Resistance Force**

$$F_{rr} = \frac{W \cdot a}{r}$$

**Explanation:** This is the most fundamental form of the rolling resistance equation. Notice that $F_{rr}$ depends on the **lever arm** $a$, which is the horizontal shift of the pressure distribution centroid.

**Step 3: Definition of the Coefficient of Rolling Resistance**

By definition:

$$C_{rr} = \frac{a}{r}$$

**Explanation:** The dimensionless coefficient $C_{rr}$ is simply the ratio of the offset distance to the wheel radius. Substituting this into the equation from Step 2 yields the standard form.

**Step 4: Final Standard Equation**

$$\boxed{F_{rr} = C_{rr} \cdot W}$$

This is the **board-exam-ready form** of the rolling resistance equation.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Power Required to Overcome Rolling Resistance

**Given:** A vehicle of mass $m$ moving at constant velocity $v$ on a flat road.

**Step 1: Force Balance at Constant Velocity**

At constant speed, propulsion force equals total resistance:

$$F_{prop} = F_{rr} = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g$$

**Step 2: Power is Force times Velocity**

$$P_{rr} = F_{rr} \cdot v$$

**Step 3: Substituting the Expression for $F_{rr}$**

$$P_{rr} = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot v$$

**Final Result (Boxed):**

$$\boxed{P_{rr} = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot v}$$

**Key Insight for KTU:** This shows that **power required is linearly proportional to speed** for rolling resistance (unlike aerodynamic drag, which is proportional to $v^{3}$). At low city speeds, rolling resistance dominates energy consumption; at highway speeds, aerodynamic drag dominates.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Energy Consumed Per Kilometer

**Step 1: Energy = Force × Distance**

$$E_{rr} = F_{rr} \cdot d = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot d$$

**Step 2: Set $d = 1$ km = 1000 m**

$$E_{rr,\,per\,km} = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot 1000$$

**Step 3: Convert to Watt-hours (1 kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J)**

$$E_{rr,\,per\,km} = \frac{C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot 1000}{3.6 \times 10^{6}} \quad \text{(in kWh)}$$

**Numerical Example for KTU Practice:**
- $m = 1500$ kg, $C_{rr} = 0.010$, $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- $E_{rr} = 0.010 \times 1500 \times 9.81 \times 1000 = 147{,}150$ J/km $\approx 0.0409$ kWh/km

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Favorite Question Type)

| **Property** | **Rolling Resistance ($F_{rr}$)** | **Aerodynamic Drag ($F_{ad}$)** | **Grading Resistance ($F_{gc}$)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Depends on Speed** | Linearly (mild) | Cubically ($v^{2}$ in force, $v^{3}$ in power) | No (only on slope angle) |
| **Direction** | Opposes motion (always) | Opposes motion (always) | Opposes motion when climbing |
| **Dominant at** | Low / city speeds | High / highway speeds | Hilly terrain |
| **Vehicle Mass Effect** | Directly proportional | Independent | Directly proportional |
| **Reducible by** | Tire pressure, tire material | Vehicle shape, frontal area | Route planning |
| **Power Law** | $P \propto v$ | $P \propto v^{3}$ | $P \propto v$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Rolling Resistance Generation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputA[Vehicle Mass m kg] --> loadNorm[Normal Load N on Tire]
    inputB[Gravity g m per s squared] --> loadNorm
    loadNorm --> contactPatch[Contact Patch Formation]
```
