```mermaid
    contactPatch --> deformation[Tire Deformation Hysteresis]
    deformation --> energyLoss[Energy Dissipated as Heat]
    energyLoss --> frrGen[Rolling Resistance Force Frr]
    inputC[Surface Type] --> surfDef[Surface Deformation Energy]
    surfDef --> frrGen
    inputD[Tire Pressure kPa] --> deformation
    inputE[Speed v m per s] --> deformation
    inputF[Temperature T degC] --> deformation
    crr[Rolling Resistance Coefficient Crr] --> frrGen
    loadNorm --> frrGen
    frrGen --> totalRes[Total Resistive Force Ftotal]
    inputG[Aerodynamic Drag Fad] --> totalRes
    inputH[Grading Resistance Fgc] --> totalRes
    totalRes --> propPower[Propulsion Power Demand P]
    propPower --> batteryDrain[Battery Energy Consumption E]
    batteryDrain --> evRange[EV Driving Range km]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Decomposition of Rolling Resistance Force

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Total Rolling Resistance Frr] --> B[Hysteresis Loss ~90 percent]
    A --> C[Surface Deformation ~5 percent]
    A --> D[Micro Slip Loss ~3 percent]
    A --> E[Air Pumping ~1 percent]
    A --> F[Bearing Friction ~1 percent]
    B --> B1[Polymer Chain Friction in Rubber]
    B --> B2[Heat Generation in Contact Patch]
    C --> C1[Road Material Yielding]
    C --> C2[Sand Mud Gravel Energy Loss]
    D --> D1[Slip in Tangential Direction]
    D --> D2[Lateral Scrubbing Loss]
    E --> E1[Air Squeezed Out of Tread]
    E --> E2[Internal Air Circulation]
    F --> F1[Hub Bearing Friction]
    F --> F2[Grease Viscosity Loss]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Force Balance on a Rolling Wheel (Schematic Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wheel Center] --> B[Contact Patch Center Geometric]
    A --> C[Resultant Normal Force W shifted forward by distance a]
    B --> D[Distance a due to Asymmetric Deformation]
    A --> E[Effective Radius r]
    A --> F[Rolling Resistance Force Frr acts at Contact Point]
    C --> G[Creates Moment W times a]
    F --> H[Creates Moment Frr times r]
    G --> I[Equilibrium Condition]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Equation Frr equals Crr times W]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Aid for KTU Students:** The "shift forward" of the resultant normal force is the **single most important conceptual model** for rolling resistance. Imagine pressing your thumb into soft clay and rolling it forward — the dent is *behind* your thumb, meaning the reaction force from the clay is *in front of* the geometric center, creating a moment that resists rolling.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define rolling resistance. List any four factors that affect the coefficient of rolling resistance.**

**Model Answer:**

Rolling resistance is the resistive force that opposes the motion of a wheel or tire as it rolls over a surface, caused primarily by energy dissipation due to the hysteresis loss in the tire material and deformation of the contact surface.

**Four factors affecting the coefficient of rolling resistance ($C_{rr}$):**
1. **Tire inflation pressure** — Higher pressure reduces $C_{rr}$ due to reduced deformation.
2. **Vehicle speed** — Higher speeds slightly increase $C_{rr}$.
3. **Tire material composition** — Silica-based compounds have lower $C_{rr}$ than carbon-black-rich ones.
4. **Road surface texture and material** — Rougher surfaces increase $C_{rr}$.

*(Other valid factors: temperature, load, tire width, tread design)*

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between rolling resistance and aerodynamic drag in terms of their dependence on vehicle speed and mass.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **Rolling Resistance ($F_{rr}$)** | **Aerodynamic Drag ($F_{ad}$)** |
|---|---|---|
| Speed dependence | $F_{rr} \propto v$ (linear, mild) | $F_{ad} \propto v^{2}$ (quadratic) |
| Power dependence | $P_{rr} \propto v$ (linear) | $P_{ad} \propto v^{3}$ (cubic) |
| Mass dependence | Directly proportional to mass | Independent of mass |
| Dominant at | Low / city driving speeds | High / highway driving speeds |
| Primary cause | Tire hysteresis loss | Air displacement around vehicle body |

---

### Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Format

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the rolling resistance force experienced by a vehicle tire, clearly explaining the role of the offset distance $a$ of the resultant normal force. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An electric vehicle of mass **1500 kg** is traveling at a constant speed of **60 km/h** on a flat road. The coefficient of rolling resistance is **0.010**. Calculate: (i) the rolling resistance force, (ii) the power required to overcome rolling resistance, and (iii) the energy consumed per km. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

##### **Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Consider a wheel of effective radius $r$ loaded vertically by a force $W$ (the vehicle weight component on that wheel). When the wheel rolls on a deformable surface, the contact patch deforms asymmetrically.

**Step 2:** The deformation is **greater at the leading edge** (entry side) than the trailing edge (exit side), because the rubber has not yet fully recovered as the wheel moves forward.

**Step 3:** As a consequence, the **resultant normal reaction** $W$ does not act through the geometric center of the contact patch, but is shifted forward by a small distance $a$.

**Step 4:** Free body diagram of the wheel in equilibrium (steady rolling):
- Vertical force: $W$ acts upward at the shifted point.
- Horizontal force: $F_{rr}$ acts at the contact point, opposing motion.

**Step 5:** Taking moments about the contact point:

$$W \cdot a = F_{rr} \cdot r$$

**Step 6:** Solving for $F_{rr}$:

$$F_{rr} = \frac{W \cdot a}{r}$$

**Step 7:** Defining the coefficient of rolling resistance as $C_{rr} = \dfrac{a}{r}$:

$$\boxed{F_{rr} = C_{rr} \cdot W}$$

**Explanation of Role of $a$:** The offset distance $a$ is a direct measure of how much energy is lost to hysteresis. A "stiffer" tire (high-pressure, harder rubber) has a small $a$, hence low $C_{rr}$. A "softer" tire has a large $a$, hence high $C_{rr}$.

---

##### **Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $m = 1500$ kg
- $v = 60$ km/h $= \dfrac{60 \times 1000}{3600} = 16.67$ m/s
- $C_{rr} = 0.010$
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**(i) Rolling Resistance Force:**

$$F_{rr} = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g$$

$$F_{rr} = 0.010 \times 1500 \times 9.81$$

$$\boxed{F_{rr} = 147.15 \text{ N}}$$

**(ii) Power Required:**

$$P_{rr} = F_{rr} \cdot v = 147.15 \times 16.67$$

$$\boxed{P_{rr} \approx 2452.5 \text{ W} \approx 2.45 \text{ kW}}$$

**(iii) Energy Consumed per km:**

$$E_{rr} = F_{rr} \cdot d = 147.15 \times 1000$$

$$\boxed{E_{rr} = 1{,}47{,}150 \text{ J/km} = 0.0409 \text{ kWh/km}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **hysteresis loss mechanism** in a pneumatic tire and how it contributes to rolling resistance. Discuss how tire inflation pressure affects this loss. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A solar car of mass **250 kg** uses special low-rolling-resistance tires with $C_{rr} = 0.0015$. It travels on a flat road at a constant **40 km/h**. Calculate: (i) the rolling resistance force, (ii) the power consumed, and (iii) the range in km if the battery stores **3 kWh** of usable energy. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

##### **Part (a) — Hysteresis Mechanism (7 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Pneumatic tires are made of viscoelastic materials (rubber compounds containing natural rubber, synthetic polymers, carbon black, silica, etc.).

**Step 2:** When a tire rolls, the section entering the contact patch is **compressed**, and the section leaving the contact patch is **released** (rebound).

**Step 3:** In a perfectly elastic material, the energy stored during compression is **fully recovered** during rebound. However, in real viscoelastic rubber, only a portion is recovered — the rest is dissipated as **heat** within the rubber.

**Step 4:** This energy loss per cycle is called **hysteresis loss**, and the lost energy per unit distance rolled is precisely the rolling resistance force:

$$F_{rr} = \frac{E_{hysteresis}}{d}$$

**Step 5:** **Effect of Inflation Pressure:**
- At **low pressure**, the tire deforms excessively → large contact patch → large hysteresis loss → high $C_{rr}$.
- At **high pressure**, the tire is stiffer → small contact patch → low hysteresis loss → low $C_{rr}$, **but** ride comfort decreases and road impact losses may rise.
- An **optimal pressure** balances rolling resistance, comfort, safety, and tire wear.

**Step 6:** Modern "green tires" use **silica-rich tread compounds** that reduce hysteresis loss at the cost of slightly higher manufacturing complexity.

---

##### **Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $m = 250$ kg
- $v = 40$ km/h $= \dfrac{40 \times 1000}{3600} = 11.11$ m/s
- $C_{rr} = 0.0015$
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- $E_{battery} = 3$ kWh $= 3 \times 3.6 \times 10^{6} = 1.08 \times 10^{7}$ J

**(i) Rolling Resistance Force:**

$$F_{rr} = 0.0015 \times 250 \times 9.81 = 3.679 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{F_{rr} \approx 3.68 \text{ N}}$$

**(ii) Power Consumed:**

$$P_{rr} = 3.679 \times 11.11 = 40.88 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{rr} \approx 40.9 \text{ W}}$$

**(iii) Range Estimation:**

Energy consumed per km:

$$E_{per\,km} = F_{rr} \times 1000 = 3679 \text{ J/km}$$

Total range:

$$\text{Range} = \frac{E_{battery}}{E_{per\,km}} = \frac{1.08 \times 10^{7}}{3679}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Range} \approx 2936 \text{ km}}$$

*(Note: In reality, aerodynamic drag would also consume energy, so the practical range is much lower. The question assumes rolling resistance is the only loss.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Mistake:** Converting km/h to m/s is the most common error. Always divide by 3.6, not 36. Partial marking is harsh for unit errors.
> 2. **Forgetting $g$ in $F_{rr}$:** Many students write $F_{rr} = C_{rr} \cdot m$, forgetting to multiply by $g = 9.81$ m/s² to convert mass to weight. The equation is $F_{rr} = C_{rr} \cdot W = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g$.
> 3. **Confusing $C_{rr}$ with coefficient of friction ($\mu$):** They are entirely different — $C_{rr}$ is for rolling, $\mu$ is for sliding.
> 4. **Power vs Energy:** Power is in **Watts (W)** or **kW**, energy is in **Joules (J)** or **kWh**. Don't mix them.
> 5. **In Part (b) range problems:** State the assumption that *only rolling resistance is considered* — otherwise the examiner deducts marks for an "incomplete model."

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Rolling Resistance** = Resistive force opposing tire rolling, primarily due to **hysteresis loss** in tire material.
- ✅ **Key Equation:** $F_{rr} = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g$ (flat surface).
- ✅ **Power Equation:** $P_{rr} = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot v$ (linear in speed).
- ✅ **Energy per km:** $E_{rr} = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot 1000$ (Joules per km).
- ✅ **Typical $C_{rr}$ values:** Bicycle (~0.002), Car (0.007–0.012), Truck (0.005–0.010), Train (0.0005–0.002), Solar car (0.0015).
- ✅ **Dominant at low speeds** (city driving), unlike aerodynamic drag which dominates at high speeds.
- ✅ **Reducible by:** higher tire pressure, low-rolling-resistance tires (silica compounds), smooth road surfaces, proper wheel alignment.
- ✅ **Geometric Interpretation:** Resultant normal force shifts forward by distance $a$ due to asymmetric deformation → creates moment opposing motion.
- ✅ **Hysteresis = Energy Loss:** Compression energy > Rebound energy → difference lost as heat.
- ✅ **Three Resistive Forces on EV:** $F_{rr}$ (rolling), $F_{ad}$ (aerodynamic), $F_{gc}$ (grading) — all must be overcome by propulsion system.
- ✅ **KTU Favorite:** Numerical on $F_{rr}$, $P_{rr}$, and $E_{rr}$ for given mass, speed, and $C_{rr}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Grading Resistance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET742 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES AND RENEWABLE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Grading Resistance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Grading Resistance (F_g)**: The component of the total resistive force acting on a vehicle that opposes its motion when the vehicle is traveling up an inclined surface (a positive grade). It is the gravitational force component acting along the direction of motion, directed opposite to the vehicle's velocity vector when ascending.

Mathematically, grading resistance is the projection of the vehicle's weight vector onto the road surface along the direction of travel.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, this falls under the broader umbrella of **"Resistive Forces Acting on a Vehicle"** — a core sub-topic of vehicle dynamics in EV powertrain design.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are pushing a **heavy shopping cart** across a flat supermarket floor — easy, right? Now imagine that same floor is tilted into a **ramp** leading up to the next level. Suddenly, the cart feels dramatically heavier and harder to push.

> **Where did the extra effort go?**
> Gravity is now pulling the cart *down the slope*, and that pull has two parts:
> - One part presses the cart *into the floor* (Normal force).
> - One part pulls the cart *backward down the ramp* — this is exactly **Grading Resistance**.

For an Electric Vehicle climbing a hill, the electric motor must overcome this "gravitational drag" in addition to rolling resistance and aerodynamic drag. The steeper the hill, the larger this resistance becomes.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Gravitational acceleration (g):** **9.81 m/s²** (Standard Earth value used universally in KTU problems)
- **Grade (G):** Expressed in **percentage (%)** in road engineering: $G = \tan\theta \times 100$
- **Slope angle (θ):** Expressed in **radians** for formulas, or **degrees** for practical interpretation
- **Small-angle approximation valid for:** $\theta \leq 10°$ (Grade ≤ 17.6%)

## 1.4 Sign Convention (KTU Standard)

| Scenario | Sign of F_g | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Uphill motion (positive grade) | **+ve** (opposes motion) | Motor must overcome it |
| Downhill motion (negative grade) | **−ve** (aids motion) | Acts as a propulsive force |
| Level road (zero grade) | **0** | No contribution to resistance |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The "Why & How"

When a vehicle of mass **m** rests on an inclined road making angle **θ** with the horizontal, the weight vector **W = m·g** (acting vertically downward) decomposes into two orthogonal components:

- **Perpendicular to road surface:** $W_{\perp} = m \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta$ — pressed into the road (balanced by Normal force).
- **Parallel to road surface (along slope):** $W_{\parallel} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$ — this is the **Grading Resistance**.

> **Why it matters for EVs:** An EV climbing a 30% grade (very steep) experiences a grading resistance equal to nearly **30% of its own weight**. For a 1500 kg car, that is ~4410 N of extra force the motor must supply — a major design constraint!

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **all high-yield formulas** the KTU examiner expects you to know verbatim.

| # | Formula | Description | Units (SI) | Validity / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_g = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$ | Exact grading resistance | Newtons (N) | Always valid |
| 2 | $F_g \approx m \cdot g \cdot \theta$ | Small-angle approximation (radians) | Newtons (N) | $\theta \leq 10°$ |
| 3 | $F_g = m \cdot g \cdot \dfrac{G\%}{100}$ | Grade percentage form | Newtons (N) | $\theta \leq 14°$ (since $\tan\theta \approx \sin\theta$) |
| 4 | $G\% = \dfrac{\text{Rise}}{\text{Run}} \times 100$ | Definition of percentage grade | Dimensionless (%) | All grades |
| 5 | $P_{grade} = F_g \cdot v = m \cdot g \cdot v \cdot \sin\theta$ | Power required to overcome grading resistance | Watts (W) | Constant velocity v |
| 6 | $F_{total} = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_g$ | Total tractive force requirement | Newtons (N) | Steady-state analysis |
| 7 | $\theta_{max} = \arcsin\!\left(\dfrac{F_{t,max} - F_{rr} - F_{ad}}{m \cdot g}\right)$ | Maximum climbable grade | Radians (rad) | Gradeability calculation |

> Where:
> - $F_{rr}$ = Rolling Resistance
> - $F_{ad}$ = Aerodynamic Drag
> - $F_{t,max}$ = Maximum tractive force at wheels
> - $v$ = Vehicle velocity

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Grading resistance is a **pivotal design parameter** in:

1. **EV Powertrain Sizing** — Determines minimum motor torque/power rating.
2. **Battery Sizing** — Grade climbing drains batteries rapidly; range estimates must include worst-case grades.
3. **Regenerative Braking Recovery** — On downhills, $F_g$ acts as a *negative* resistance, allowing the motor to recover kinetic + potential energy.
4. **Highway & Hill Road Design** — Maximum permissible grades for EVs (e.g., FHWA recommends ≤ 7% for main highways, EVs can manage up to 30% in some cases).
5. **Gradeability Test** — A standard KTU/automotive industry performance metric, expressed as the **maximum % grade a vehicle can climb at a specified speed**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Grading Resistance from First Principles

### Step 1 — Free Body Setup

Consider a vehicle of mass $m$ on a road inclined at angle $\theta$ to the horizontal. Three forces act along the direction of motion:

- Weight component along slope (downward direction): $m g \sin\theta$
- Tractive force $F_t$ (forward, up the slope)
- Other resistances (rolling, aerodynamic): $F_{rr} + F_{ad}$

### Step 2 — Newton's Second Law Along the Slope

Applying $\sum F = m \cdot a$ along the direction of travel (up-slope positive):

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_t - F_{rr} - F_{ad} - F_g &= m \cdot a \\
F_t - F_{rr} - F_{ad} - m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta &= m \cdot a
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Isolating the Grading Resistance Term

By inspection, the gravitational component opposing motion (when ascending) is:

$$
F_g = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta
$$

This is the **exact, rigorous** form. ✅ **Derivation Complete.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Small-Angle / Percentage-Grade Approximation

### Step 1 — Definition of Percentage Grade

By civil engineering convention, the percentage grade is defined as:

$$
G\% = \frac{\text{Vertical Rise}}{\text{Horizontal Run}} \times 100 = \tan\theta \times 100
$$

### Step 2 — Small-Angle Approximation

For small angles (in radians), the Taylor series gives:

$$
\sin\theta = \theta - \frac{\theta^3}{6} + \frac{\theta^5}{120} - \dots
$$

Truncating after the first term for $\theta \leq 10°$ ($\approx 0.1745$ rad):

$$
\sin\theta \approx \theta \quad \text{(in radians)}
$$

### Step 3 — Combining with Percentage Grade

We also have $\tan\theta \approx \sin\theta$ for small angles. Therefore:

$$
\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta = \frac{G\%}{100}
$$

### Step 4 — Final Approximate Formula

Substituting into the exact equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_g &= m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta \\
&\approx m \cdot g \cdot \frac{G\%}{100}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Numerical Check:** For $\theta = 10°$, $\sin(10°) = 0.1736$ and $\tan(10°) = 0.1763$. The relative error is only $\sim 1.5\%$, well within engineering tolerance.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample Problem:** An electric vehicle of mass **1200 kg** (including battery and passengers) is climbing a road with a **6% grade** at a constant speed of **50 km/h**. Compute the grading resistance and the power required to overcome it. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

### Step A — Identify Inputs

- Mass: $m = 1200 \text{ kg}$
- Grade: $G = 6\%$
- Speed: $v = 50 \text{ km/h}$

### Step B — Convert Speed to SI Units

$$
v = 50 \text{ km/h} = \frac{50 \times 1000}{3600} \text{ m/s} = 13.89 \text{ m/s}
$$

### Step C — Compute the Slope Angle (for verification)

$$
\tan\theta = \frac{6}{100} = 0.06 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta = \arctan(0.06) = 3.43°
$$

### Step D — Compute Grading Resistance

Using the percentage grade form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_g &= m \cdot g \cdot \frac{G}{100} \\
&= 1200 \times 9.81 \times \frac{6}{100} \\
&= 1200 \times 9.81 \times 0.06 \\
&= 706.32 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step E — Compute the Power Required to Overcome Grading Resistance

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{grade} &= F_g \cdot v \\
&= 706.32 \text{ N} \times 13.89 \text{ m/s} \\
&= 9810 \text{ W} \approx 9.81 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step F — Interpretation

> **Engineering Insight:** This single 6% grade demands ~9.81 kW of motor power *just to fight gravity* — equivalent to running 100 incandescent bulbs continuously! This is why EV range plummets on hilly routes.

| Parameter | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Slope angle, $\theta$ | 3.43 | degrees |
| Grading Resistance, $F_g$ | 706.32 | N |
| Power to climb, $P_{grade}$ | 9.81 | kW |
| Energy consumed per km | ~0.706 | kWh/km |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Free Body Diagram of a Vehicle on an Incline

The following Mermaid block represents a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** showing all forces acting on a vehicle ascending a grade — the analytical equivalent of a free-body diagram.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["VEHICLE MASS m"] --> B["WEIGHT W = m times g"]
    B --> C1["COMPONENT PERPENDICULAR TO SLOPE"]
    B --> C2["COMPONENT PARALLEL TO SLOPE"]
    C1 --> D1["W cos theta - BALANCED BY NORMAL FORCE"]
    C2 --> D2["W sin theta EQUALS GRADING RESISTANCE Fg"]
    D2 --> E["OPPOSES VEHICLE MOTION WHEN ASCENDING"]
    E --> F["MOTOR MUST SUPPLY TRACTIVE FORCE F t"]
    F --> G["F t OVERCOMES Fg PLUS F rr PLUS F ad"]
    G --> H["NET RESULT VEHICLE ACCELERATES OR CRUISES"]
    H --> I["ON DOWNHILL Fg BECOMES PROPULSIVE FORCE"]
    I --> J["REGENERATIVE BRAKING CAN RECOVER THIS ENERGY"]
```

## 4.2 Force Decomposition Schematic (Vector Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["WEIGHT VECTOR m g - ACTING VERTICALLY DOWN"] --> B["DECOMPOSE ALONG INCLINED AXES"]
    B --> C["NORMAL DIRECTION - PERPENDICULAR TO ROAD"]
    B --> D["TANGENTIAL DIRECTION - ALONG ROAD SURFACE"]
    C --> E["N equals m g cos theta - PASSIVE BALANCE"]
    D --> F["TANGENTIAL COMPONENT m g sin theta EQUALS Fg"]
    F --> G["GRADE PERCENTAGE FORM Fg equals m g times G percent divided by 100"]
    G --> H["USED IN TRACTIVE FORCE EQUATION FOR EV SIZING"]
```

## 4.3 Vehicle Resistance Force Hierarchy (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["TOTAL TRACTIVE FORCE REQUIREMENT F t"] --> B["ROLLING RESISTANCE F rr"]
    A --> C["AERODYNAMIC DRAG F ad"]
    A --> D["GRADING RESISTANCE F g"]
    A --> E["ACCELERATION FORCE F a equals m times a"]
    B --> F["F rr equals m g cos theta times C rr"]
    C --> G["F ad equals 0.5 times rho times A times C d times v squared"]
    D --> H["F g equals m g sin theta"]
    H --> I["APPROXIMATED AS F g equals m g times G percent divided by 100 FOR SMALL ANGLES"]
    F --> J["SUM GIVES F t REQUIRED FROM MOTOR"]
    G --> J
    I --> J
    E --> J
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define grading resistance. Write its mathematical expression and explain its significance in EV design.**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> **Definition (1 Mark):** Grading resistance is the component of a vehicle's weight acting along the inclined road surface, opposing the motion of the vehicle when it is ascending a slope.
>
> **Mathematical Expression (1 Mark):**
> $$F_g = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta \approx m \cdot g \cdot \frac{G\%}{100}$$
> where $m$ = vehicle mass, $g$ = 9.81 m/s², $\theta$ = slope angle, $G\%$ = percentage grade.
>
> **Significance in EV Design (1 Mark):** It directly impacts the **motor power rating**, **battery sizing**, and **gradeability** of the vehicle. Steeper grades demand higher tractive effort, reducing range and requiring robust powertrain design.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**A road has a slope of 8%. An EV of mass 1000 kg travels on it at constant speed. Calculate the grading resistance experienced by the vehicle. Take g = 9.81 m/s².**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> Given: $m = 1000 \text{ kg}$, $G = 8\%$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
>
> Formula (1 Mark):
> $$F_g = m \cdot g \cdot \frac{G}{100}$$
>
> Substitution (1 Mark):
> $$F_g = 1000 \times 9.81 \times \frac{8}{100} = 784.8 \text{ N}$$
>
> Result with unit (1 Mark):
> $$\boxed{F_g = 784.8 \text{ N}}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (Choice 1)** [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Derive the expression for grading resistance acting on a vehicle moving up an inclined road. State the assumptions used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An electric vehicle of mass **1500 kg** is to climb a **20% grade** at a constant speed of **30 km/h**. The coefficient of rolling resistance is **0.015** and the aerodynamic drag coefficient times frontal area is $C_d \cdot A = 0.7 \text{ m}^2$. Air density $\rho = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$. Compute: (i) Grading resistance, (ii) Rolling resistance, (iii) Aerodynamic drag, and (iv) Total tractive force required. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

### **Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Setup (1 Mark):** Consider a vehicle of mass $m$ moving up an incline of angle $\theta$. The weight $W = mg$ acts vertically downward.

**Step 2 — Force Decomposition (2 Marks):** Resolve $W$ into two components:
- Perpendicular to slope: $W_\perp = mg \cos\theta$ (balanced by normal reaction)
- Parallel to slope (downward): $W_\parallel = mg \sin\theta$

**Step 3 — Identification (1 Mark):** The component $mg \sin\theta$ acts along the slope, directed **downward**, opposing the vehicle's upward motion. This is the **grading resistance**.

**Step 4 — Mathematical Expression (2 Marks):**
$$
F_g = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta
$$

**Step 5 — Approximation and Assumptions (1 Mark):** For small angles ($\theta \leq 10°$), $\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta = G\%/100$, giving the practical form:
$$
F_g \approx m \cdot g \cdot \frac{G\%}{100}
$$

**Assumption:** Level or near-level road; rigid body vehicle; no longitudinal weight transfer.

---

### **Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)**

**Given Data:**
- $m = 1500 \text{ kg}$
- $G = 20\%$
- $v = 30 \text{ km/h} = \frac{30 \times 1000}{3600} = 8.33 \text{ m/s}$
- $C_{rr} = 0.015$
- $C_d \cdot A = 0.7 \text{ m}^2$
- $\rho = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**(i) Grading Resistance (2 Marks):**
$$
F_g = m \cdot g \cdot \frac{G}{100} = 1500 \times 9.81 \times \frac{20}{100} = 2943 \text{ N}
$$

**(ii) Rolling Resistance (2 Marks):**
$$
F_{rr} = m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr} = 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.015 = 220.7 \text{ N}
$$

**(iii) Aerodynamic Drag (2 Marks):**
$$
F_{ad} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2 = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.7 \times (8.33)^2 = 29.15 \text{ N}
$$

**(iv) Total Tractive Force (1 Mark):**
$$
F_t = F_g + F_{rr} + F_{ad} = 2943 + 220.7 + 29.15 = 3192.85 \text{ N}
$$

> **Final Answer:** $F_t \approx 3192.85 \text{ N}$ ✅

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)** [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the concept of **gradeability** of an electric vehicle. How is it related to grading resistance? Derive the formula for maximum climbable grade. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **1800 kg** EV has a maximum tractive force at wheels of **5500 N**. The vehicle travels on a road with rolling resistance coefficient **0.012** and aerodynamic drag of **150 N** at the design speed. Calculate the **maximum grade (%)** the vehicle can climb. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

### **Part (a) — Concept Explanation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Definition (2 Marks):** Gradeability is the ability of a vehicle to ascend a slope (grade), defined as the **maximum percentage grade** a vehicle can climb at a given speed under specified conditions.

**Step 2 — Relation to Grading Resistance (2 Marks):** The maximum gradeable slope is reached when the entire available tractive force is consumed by overcoming grading resistance plus other resistances. Mathematically, at maximum grade ($a = 0$ for steady climbing):

$$
F_{t,max} = F_g + F_{rr} + F_{ad}
$$

**Step 3 — Derivation of Maximum Grade Formula (3 Marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{t,max} &= m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta_{max} + m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr} + F_{ad} \\
m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta_{max} &= F_{t,max} - m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr} - F_{ad} \\
\sin\theta_{max} &= \frac{F_{t,max} - F_{rr} - F_{ad}}{m \cdot g} \\
\theta_{max} &= \arcsin\left(\frac{F_{t,max} - F_{rr} - F_{ad}}{m \cdot g}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

For small angles, $\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta = G_{max}/100$:

$$
G_{max}\% = \frac{F_{t,max} - F_{rr} - F_{ad}}{m \cdot g} \times 100
$$

---

### **Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)**

**Given Data:**
- $m = 1800 \text{ kg}$
- $F_{t,max} = 5500 \text{ N}$
- $C_{rr} = 0.012$
- $F_{ad} = 150 \text{ N}$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Step 1 — Compute Rolling Resistance (2 Marks):**
$$
F_{rr} = m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr} = 1800 \times 9.81 \times 0.012 = 211.9 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Maximum Grade Formula (3 Marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{max}\% &= \frac{F_{t,max} - F_{rr} - F_{ad}}{m \cdot g} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{5500 - 211.9 - 150}{1800 \times 9.81} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{5138.1}{17658} \times 100 \\
&\approx 29.10\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation (2 Marks):** The vehicle can climb a grade of approximately **29.1%** at the design speed. For context, this is steeper than most mountain roads and indicates a high-performance EV powertrain.

> **Final Answer:** $G_{max} \approx 29.1\%$ ✅

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s** when computing power or when $v$ appears in aerodynamic drag. (Costs 1-2 marks)
> 2. **Sign error**: Forgetting that grading resistance is *positive* (opposing) when going uphill. Downhill, it becomes a *negative* resistance (aiding motion). Always check direction!
> 3. **Mixing $G$ and $\theta$**: Some students directly substitute $G$ (in %) into the $\sin\theta$ formula. Always convert: $\sin\theta = G/100$ only when $G$ is in percent.
> 4. **Radian-degree confusion**: $\sin\theta$ in the formula requires $\theta$ in **radians** if you use the approximation $F_g \approx m g \theta$. But if you use the $G\%$ form, no conversion is needed.
> 5. **Ignoring the small-angle approximation validity**: For grades > 15%, the percentage form introduces noticeable error; the KTU examiner may deduct marks if the assumption isn't stated.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Grading Resistance**

- ✅ **Core Formula (Exact):** $F_g = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$
- ✅ **Practical Formula (Small angle):** $F_g = m \cdot g \cdot \dfrac{G\%}{100}$
- ✅ **Grade Definition:** $G\% = \dfrac{\text{Rise}}{\text{Run}} \times 100 = \tan\theta \times 100$
- ✅ **Direction:** Opposes motion **uphill**; aids motion **downhill**
- ✅ **Standard g value:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ (always state in KTU answers)
- ✅ **Power required to climb:** $P = F_g \cdot v$ (in W, with $v$ in m/s)
- ✅ **Maximum grade formula:** $G_{max}\% = \dfrac{F_{t,max} - F_{rr} - F_{ad}}{m \cdot g} \times 100$
- ✅ **Valid approximation range:** $G \leq 14\%$ (small-angle assumption)
- ✅ **Total tractive force:** $F_t = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_g + m \cdot a$
- ✅ **Engineering impact:** Affects motor sizing, battery range, gradeability, regen-braking energy recovery
- ✅ **KTU tag-friendly terms:** "Tractive effort", "Road load force", "Gradeability", "Slope compensation"

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vehicle Acceleration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET742 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES AND RENEWABLE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Vehicle Acceleration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Definition — Vehicle Acceleration**
> **Vehicle Acceleration** is the kinematic performance parameter of a road vehicle that quantifies its rate of change of linear velocity along the direction of motion, determined by the net unbalanced **tractive force** available at the wheel–road contact patch after overcoming all resistive forces (rolling resistance, aerodynamic drag, and grade resistance). In EV powertrain design, acceleration capability is treated as a **dynamic design constraint** governing the selection of motor peak torque, gear ratio, battery power density, and vehicle mass distribution.

Mathematically, vehicle acceleration $a$ (in m/s²) is expressed through Newton's Second Law applied along the longitudinal axis of the vehicle:

$$a = \frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{F_{te} - F_{tr}}{M_{eq}}$$

where $F_{te}$ is the **tractive effort** delivered to the road, $F_{tr}$ is the **total resistive force**, and $M_{eq}$ is the **equivalent vehicle mass** (which accounts for the rotational inertia of rotating components like wheels, gears, and motor rotor).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pushing a Cart" Intuition

> [!NOTE] **Real-World Analogy for First-Time Learners**
> Imagine you are pushing a loaded shopping cart up a slight ramp on a windy day.
> - The **push force** you apply is the **tractive force** ($F_{te}$) generated by the EV's electric motor and delivered through the wheels.
> - The **cart's weight pressing down on the wheels** creates friction with the floor — this is the **rolling resistance** ($F_r$).
> - The **wind blowing in your face** is the **aerodynamic drag** ($F_d$).
> - The **uphill slope** is the **grade resistance** ($F_g$).
> - The **net remaining force** is what actually speeds up the cart — this corresponds to the **acceleration force** ($F_a = M \cdot a$).
> - If the cart also has spinning wheels, the **extra effort to spin them up** is the **rotational inertia factor** — represented by the equivalent mass factor $(1 + \lambda)$.

The faster you push, the harder the wind pushes back (drag grows with the **square** of velocity). That is why EVs accelerate quickly at low speeds but feel less "punchy" at highway speeds.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $g$ | Gravitational acceleration | **9.81 m/s²** |
| $\rho_{air}$ | Air density (sea level, 25°C) | **1.225 kg/m³** |
| $\mu_r$ | Rolling resistance coefficient | **0.008 – 0.015** (passenger car tyres) |
| $C_d$ | Aerodynamic drag coefficient | **0.25 – 0.35** (modern EVs) |
| $A_f$ | Frontal area | **2.0 – 2.5 m²** (sedan) |
| $\lambda$ | Rotational mass factor | **0.05 – 0.15** (≈ 5–15 % of vehicle mass) |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Syllabus Highlight**
> For KTU Board Examinations, students are expected to remember the **force balance equation** and the **power balance equation** for an accelerating vehicle, and to apply them to compute acceleration time, maximum velocity on a grade, and the energy consumed during a given acceleration event.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Longitudinal Force Balance — Step-by-Step Logic

The vehicle is treated as a **single rigid body** moving along a straight, inclined road. The free-body diagram along the longitudinal direction contains exactly four tractive / resistive force groups:

1. **Tractive Effort Delivered to Road** — $F_{te}$ (forward, propulsive)
2. **Rolling Resistance Force** — $F_r$ (backward, opposing motion)
3. **Aerodynamic Drag Force** — $F_d$ (backward, opposing motion)
4. **Grade / Hill-Climbing Force** — $F_g$ (backward when going up, forward when going down)
5. **Inertial (Acceleration) Force** — $F_a$ (backward when accelerating)

### Step 1 — Identify the propulsive force
The electric motor produces a torque $T_m$, which is transmitted through the gearbox (ratio $i_g$), the differential (ratio $i_0$), and finally to the wheels of effective radius $r$. The tractive force at the contact patch is:

$$F_{te} = \frac{T_m \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_t}{r}$$

where $\eta_t$ is the **mechanical transmission efficiency** (typically 0.90 – 0.95 for single-speed EV reducers).

### Step 2 — Quantify the rolling resistance
The tyres deform as they roll, dissipating energy. On a level road the rolling resistance is:

$$F_r = \mu_r \cdot M \cdot g \cdot \cos(\alpha)$$

For small inclinations ($\alpha < 15°$), $\cos(\alpha) \approx 1$, so $F_r \approx \mu_r M g$. The coefficient $\mu_r$ is **velocity-dependent** and a more accurate model is:

$$\mu_r = \mu_{r0} + k_r \cdot v^2$$

where $k_r \approx 6.5 \times 10^{-6}$ s²/m² for a typical passenger car tyre.

### Step 3 — Quantify the aerodynamic drag
Air pushed out of the way by the moving frontal area produces a pressure differential and skin friction:

$$F_d = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot C_d \cdot A_f \cdot v^2$$

> [!NOTE] **Why the $v^2$ dependence?**
> Drag depends on **dynamic pressure** $\tfrac{1}{2} \rho v^2$ times the body's drag area $C_d A_f$. Doubling the speed **quadruples** the drag. This is the single most important non-linear effect in vehicle dynamics.

### Step 4 — Quantify the grade force
On an incline of angle $\alpha$ (with $\sin \alpha = \text{grade in decimal}$):

$$F_g = M \cdot g \cdot \sin(\alpha)$$

For a road labelled as "n % grade", $\sin \alpha \approx \alpha \approx n/100$ for small angles (e.g. a 5 % grade gives $\alpha \approx 2.86°$, $\sin \alpha \approx 0.05$).

### Step 5 — Account for rotational inertia
When a vehicle accelerates, not only does the linear mass $M$ accelerate, but wheels, gears, half-shafts, and the motor rotor also **spin up**. This is captured by the **equivalent mass factor** $(1 + \lambda)$:

$$M_{eq} = M \cdot (1 + \lambda)$$

For a typical passenger EV with in-wheel-mounted rotors or moderate driveline, $\lambda \approx 0.10$ — meaning the effective inertia is 10 % higher than the curb mass.

### Step 6 — Apply Newton's Second Law
The net force available for linear acceleration is therefore:

$$F_{te} - F_r - F_d - F_g = M_{eq} \cdot a = M \cdot (1 + \lambda) \cdot a$$

Solving for the instantaneous acceleration:

$$a = \frac{F_{te} - F_r - F_d - F_g}{M \cdot (1 + \lambda)}$$

### Step 7 — Translate force to power
The instantaneous mechanical power required at the wheels to sustain motion at velocity $v$ and acceleration $a$ is:

$$P_w = F_{te} \cdot v = \left[ M (1 + \lambda) a + \mu_r M g \cos\alpha + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f v^2 + M g \sin\alpha \right] \cdot v$$

The power demanded from the **battery pack** (DC bus side) is:

$$P_{bat} = \frac{P_w}{\eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_t}$$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] **High-Yield Formulas for KTU 2024 Board Exam**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tractive Effort | $F_{te} = \dfrac{T_m \, i_g \, i_0 \, \eta_t}{r}$ | N | Propulsive force at road |
| 2 | Rolling Resistance | $F_r = \mu_r M g \cos\alpha$ | N | ≈ $\mu_r M g$ on level road |
| 3 | Aerodynamic Drag | $F_d = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_{air} C_d A_f v^2$ | N | Quadratic in $v$ |
| 4 | Grade Force | $F_g = M g \sin\alpha$ | N | $\sin\alpha \approx \alpha$ for small $\alpha$ |
| 5 | Acceleration Force | $F_a = M (1+\lambda) a$ | N | $\lambda \approx 0.05$–$0.15$ |
| 6 | Force Balance | $F_{te} = F_r + F_d + F_g + M(1+\lambda)a$ | N | **Master equation** |
| 7 | Vehicle Acceleration | $a = \dfrac{F_{te} - F_r - F_d - F_g}{M(1+\lambda)}$ | m/s² | Solve for $a$ given $F_{te}$ |
| 8 | Power at Wheels | $P_w = F_{te} \cdot v$ | W | Linear in $F_{te}$, linear in $v$ |
| 9 | Power for Accel. | $P_a = M (1+\lambda) a v$ | W | Power needed to overcome inertia |
| 10 | Energy for Accel. | $E_a = \tfrac{1}{2} M (1+\lambda) (v_2^2 - v_1^2)$ | J | Kinetic-energy change |
| 11 | Time to Accelerate | $t_a = \dfrac{M (1+\lambda)(v_2 - v_1)}{F_{te} - F_r - F_d - F_g}$ | s | Constant net force assumption |
| 12 | Max Speed (level) | $v_{max} = \sqrt[3]{\dfrac{F_{te,\max} \cdot r}{k_a}}$ (with $k_a = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f$) | m/s | At $F_{te} = F_d$ |

> [!WARNING] **Absolute Value Convention**
> When writing inequalities such as $\vert v_2 - v_1 \vert$ inside a markdown table, always use `\vert` (renders as a single vertical bar). The bare pipe `\|` breaks the table layout in markdown.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility

- **Motor Sizing:** Peak $F_{te}$ required for 0–100 km/h in $t$ seconds determines the **peak motor torque rating**.
- **Battery Sizing:** The **peak power** during launch ($P_{bat,peak} = P_w / \eta_{tot}$) dictates the battery's C-rate requirement.
- **Range Estimation:** Repeated hard accelerations consume energy $E_a = \tfrac{1}{2} M (1+\lambda)(v_2^2 - v_1^2)$ **per event**; over a drive cycle this is integrated to estimate kWh consumed.
- **Thermal Management:** Continuous high-acceleration events overheat the motor and inverter; thermal limits bound the **sustained** $F_{te}$ to a value much lower than the **peak** $F_{te}$.
- **Regenerative Braking:** The same kinetic-energy equation is used in reverse to compute **recovered energy** during deceleration: $E_{regen} = \eta_{regen} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} M (1+\lambda) (v_1^2 - v_2^2)$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Vehicle Acceleration from First Principles

**Starting Point:** Apply Newton's Second Law in the longitudinal direction to a vehicle of mass $M$ moving up an incline $\alpha$ at speed $v$ with acceleration $a$:

$$\sum F_x = M \cdot a$$

**Step 1:** Identify and substitute all four contributing forces. The propulsive force at the contact patch is $F_{te}$ (positive, forward). The opposing forces are $F_r$ (rolling), $F_d$ (drag), and $F_g$ (grade):

$$F_{te} - F_r - F_d - F_g = M \cdot a$$

**Step 2:** Expand the resistive forces using the standard sub-models (from Section 2.1):

$$F_{te} - \mu_r M g \cos\alpha - \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f v^2 - M g \sin\alpha = M \cdot a$$

**Step 3:** Introduce the rotational inertia correction. The wheels, half-shafts, and motor rotor each store kinetic energy of rotation. Let $I_w$ be the moment of inertia of one wheel and $N$ the number of driven wheels, $\omega$ the wheel angular speed, and $i_g i_0$ the overall gear ratio. The total kinetic energy is:

$$KE_{total} = \tfrac{1}{2} M v^2 + \tfrac{1}{2} N I_w \omega^2 + \tfrac{1}{2} I_{driveline} \omega_{driveline}^2$$

**Step 4:** Express all rotational speeds in terms of $v$ using $v = \omega r$ and the gear ratio chain. The driveline energy becomes:

$$KE_{rot} = \tfrac{1}{2} v^2 \left[ \frac{N I_w}{r^2} + \frac{I_{driveline}(i_g i_0)^2}{r^2} \right]$$

**Step 5:** Combine linear and rotational kinetic energy into a single effective mass:

$$KE_{total} = \tfrac{1}{2} \left[ M + \frac{N I_w + I_{driveline}(i_g i_0)^2}{r^2} \right] v^2 \equiv \tfrac{1}{2} M_{eq} v^2$$

**Step 6:** Define the **rotational mass factor** $\lambda$ as the ratio of equivalent rotational mass to vehicle mass:

$$M_{eq} = M (1 + \lambda) \quad \text{where} \quad \lambda = \frac{N I_w + I_{driveline}(i_g i_0)^2}{M r^2}$$

**Step 7:** Substitute $M_{eq} = M(1+\lambda)$ back into the force balance. The **final governing equation** for vehicle acceleration is:

$$\boxed{\,a = \frac{F_{te} - \mu_r M g \cos\alpha - \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f v^2 - M g \sin\alpha}{M (1+\lambda)}\,}$$

> [!NOTE] **Sign Convention**
> $\alpha$ is **positive when going uphill**. If the vehicle descends a slope, $\alpha < 0$ and $F_g$ becomes a **driving** (assisting) force, potentially exceeding $F_r + F_d$ and producing a negative acceleration (deceleration / gravity-fed motion).

## 3.2 Derivation II — Time to Accelerate from $v_1$ to $v_2$

**Starting Point:** Assume $F_{te}$ is **constant** (motor delivering flat torque), and the vehicle is on a **level road** ($\alpha = 0$). The instantaneous acceleration is:

$$a(v) = \frac{F_{te} - \mu_r M g - \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f v^2}{M (1+\lambda)}$$

**Step 1:** Recognise that $a = \dfrac{dv}{dt}$, so rearrange:

$$dt = \frac{M (1+\lambda)}{F_{te} - \mu_r M g - \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f v^2} \, dv$$

**Step 2:** Integrate from $v_1$ to $v_2$:

$$t_a = \int_{v_1}^{v_2} \frac{M (1+\lambda)}{F_{te} - \mu_r M g - \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f v^2} \, dv$$

**Step 3:** This integral has the standard form $\displaystyle \int \frac{dv}{A - B v^2} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{AB}} \tanh^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{v\sqrt{B}}{\sqrt{A}}\right)$ where $A = F_{te} - \mu_r M g$ and $B = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f$. Therefore:

$$t_a = \frac{M (1+\lambda)}{\sqrt{(F_{te} - \mu_r M g) \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f}} \tanh^{-1}\!\left( v \sqrt{\frac{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f}{F_{te} - \mu_r M g}} \right) \Bigg|_{v_1}^{v_2}$$

**Step 4:** For the **simplified case** in which drag is neglected ($C_d \to 0$, $B \to 0$), use L'Hôpital's rule to recover the linear result:

$$t_a = \frac{M (1+\lambda) (v_2 - v_1)}{F_{te} - \mu_r M g}$$

This simplified form is the one most often tested in KTU problems, and it appears in the KTU Formula Sheet (Row 11) above.

## 3.3 Derivation III — Energy Required to Accelerate

**Starting Point:** The mechanical work done by the net tractive force over a small displacement $ds$ is:

$$dW = F_{net} \cdot ds = M (1+\lambda) a \cdot ds$$

**Step 1:** Use the chain rule $a \, ds = a \, v \, dt = v \, dv$ (since $a = dv/dt$ and $ds = v\,dt$):

$$dW = M (1+\lambda) \, v \, dv$$

**Step 2:** Integrate from $v_1$ to $v_2$:

$$E_a = \int_{v_1}^{v_2} M (1+\lambda) \, v \, dv = M (1+\lambda) \left[ \frac{v^2}{2} \right]_{v_1}^{v_2}$$

**Step 3:** Final result — the energy stored as kinetic energy (and recovered during regen):

$$\boxed{\,E_a = \tfrac{1}{2} M (1+\lambda) (v_2^2 - v_1^2)\,}$$

> [!NOTE] **Important: Energy is Independent of Time Profile**
> The work done against inertia depends **only** on the initial and final speeds, not on how the force is applied. Accelerating from 0 to 100 km/h in 3 s consumes the **same** kinetic energy as doing it in 30 s. The faster method just demands **higher peak power**.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A passenger EV has $M = 1500$ kg, $\lambda = 0.10$, $C_d = 0.29$, $A_f = 2.2$ m², $\mu_r = 0.012$, $r = 0.32$ m. The motor delivers a constant torque of $T_m = 220$ N·m through a single-speed reducer with $i_g = 9.0$ and $\eta_t = 0.93$. Calculate the instantaneous acceleration at $v = 50$ km/h on a level road.

**Step 1 — Convert speed:**
$$v = 50 \text{ km/h} = \frac{50}{3.6} = 13.89 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute tractive effort:**
$$F_{te} = \frac{T_m \cdot i_g \cdot \eta_t}{r} = \frac{220 \times 9.0 \times 0.93}{0.32} = \frac{1841.4}{0.32} = 5754.4 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Compute rolling resistance:**
$$F_r = \mu_r M g = 0.012 \times 1500 \times 9.81 = 176.6 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Compute aerodynamic drag:**
$$F_d = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 0.29 \times 2.2 \times (13.89)^2 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.29 \times 2.2 \times 192.9 = 75.4 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5 — Compute net accelerating force:**
$$F_{net} = F_{te} - F_r - F_d = 5754.4 - 176.6 - 75.4 = 5502.4 \text{ N}$$

**Step 6 — Apply equivalent mass correction:**
$$M_{eq} = 1500 \times (1 + 0.10) = 1650 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 7 — Compute acceleration:**
$$a = \frac{F_{net}}{M_{eq}} = \frac{5502.4}{1650} = 3.33 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 8 — Convert to familiar units (0–100 km/h time estimate, very approximate):**
$$t_{0\text{-}100} \approx \frac{27.78}{3.33} = 8.34 \text{ s}$$

> [!NOTE] **Observation**
> At low speeds, the rolling resistance and drag are small and the **entire tractive effort is available for acceleration**. As $v$ rises, drag grows as $v^2$ and the available acceleration drops rapidly. This is why EVs are quick off the line but lose the "punch" above 100 km/h.

## 3.5 Summary Matrix — Force, Power, Energy, Time

| Quantity | Symbol | Key Dependency | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tractive Effort | $F_{te}$ | Constant in $v$ (if $T_m$ is constant) | Limited by motor torque × gear ratio |
| Rolling Resistance | $F_r$ | $\approx$ constant in $v$ | Increases slightly with $v^2$ via $\mu_r(v)$ |
| Aerodynamic Drag | $F_d$ | $v^2$ | Dominant above 60–70 km/h |
| Grade Force | $F_g$ | Constant in $v$ | Function of slope only |
| Acceleration | $a$ | Decreases with $v$ | Peak at launch, → 0 at $v_{max}$ |
| Power at wheels | $P_w = F_{te} v$ | Linear in $v$ | Determines battery C-rate |
| Accel. energy | $E_a$ | $\propto (v_2^2 - v_1^2)$ | Path-independent |
| Accel. time | $t_a$ | Function of all forces | Use the integral form for accuracy |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Force-Flow During Vehicle Acceleration

The diagram below maps how driver demand, motor torque, transmission, and the external environment interact to produce the longitudinal acceleration of an EV. Note that all node identifiers are alphanumeric and all node labels are plain uppercase text (no markdown formatting) per the Mermaid compilation safeguards.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[DRIVER ACCEL PEDAL] --> A2[TORQUE REQUEST SIGNAL]
    A2 --> A3[MOTOR CONTROLLER INVERTER]
    A3 --> A4[TRACTION MOTOR]
    A4 --> A5[MOTOR TORQUE TM]
    A5 --> A6[SINGLE SPEED REDUCER IG]
    A6 --> A7[TRACTIVE EFFORT AT WHEEL FTE]
    A7 --> A8[VEHICLE CHASSIS]
    A8 --> A9[ROAD CONTACT PATCH]

    B1[AERODYNAMIC DRAG FD] --> A8
    B2[ROLLING RESISTANCE FR] --> A8
    B3[GRADE FORCE FG] --> A8
    A8 --> A10[NET FORCE FNET]
    A10 --> A11[NEWTON SECOND LAW F MA]
    A11 --> A12[ACCELERATION A]
    A12 --> A13[VEHICLE VELOCITY V]
    A13 --> B1
    A13 --> A5
    A5 --> A14[POWER AT WHEELS PW]
    A14 --> A15[BATTERY PACK DC BUS]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Energy Flow During Acceleration

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[BATTERY PACK] --> S2[DC LINK]
    S2 --> S3[VOLTAGE SOURCE INVERTER]
    S3 --> S4[TRACTION MOTOR WITH LOSS]
    S4 --> S5[GEARBOX WITH LOSS]
    S5 --> S6[DIFFERENTIAL AND WHEELS]
    S6 --> S7[VEHICLE MASS KINETIC ENERGY]
    S7 --> S8[PARTIAL ENERGY LOSS VIA DRAG]
    S7 --> S9[PARTIAL ENERGY LOSS VIA ROLLING]
    S7 --> S10[PARTIAL ENERGY LOSS VIA HILL CLIMB]
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow — How to Compute Vehicle Acceleration in an Exam

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{GIVEN TRACTIVE FORCE OR MOTOR TORQUE}
    Q1 -- MOTOR TORQUE --> S1[COMPUTE FTE USING GEAR RATIO AND EFFICIENCY]
    Q1 -- FTE DIRECT --> S2[USE FTE AS GIVEN]
    S1 --> S3[COMPUTE FR USING MUR M G COS ALPHA]
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S4[COMPUTE FD USING 0.5 RHO CD AF V SQUARE]
    S4 --> S5[COMPUTE FG USING M G SIN ALPHA]
    S5 --> S6[APPLY FTE FR FD FG EQUALS M ONE PLUS LAMBDA A]
    S6 --> S7[SOLVE FOR A]
```

> [!NOTE] **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams**
> The **master equation** $F_{te} = F_r + F_d + F_g + M(1+\lambda)a$ is the single most important relation. Any acceleration problem reduces to "plug the four sub-models in and solve". The diagrams above help visualise where each term originates and how the energy flows.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

### Q1. Define "tractive effort" in the context of an electric vehicle and write the expression for tractive effort at the wheel–road contact patch in terms of motor torque, gear ratio, transmission efficiency, and wheel radius. **(3 marks)**

**Model Answer (1 mark per logical step):**
1. **Definition (1 m):** Tractive effort is the net forward force delivered by the propulsion system at the tyre–road interface, available to overcome resistive forces and accelerate the vehicle.
2. **Expression (1 m):** $F_{te} = \dfrac{T_m \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_t}{r}$
3. **Unit & remark (1 m):** $F_{te}$ is in Newtons. For a single-speed EV reducer, $i_0$ is often subsumed into $i_g$. The expression shows that **higher motor torque and lower wheel radius increase tractive effort**, but lower radius reduces top speed — a classic design trade-off.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**

### Q2. List the four components of resistive force acting on a moving vehicle. For each component, state one design parameter that the automotive engineer can vary to reduce that resistive force. **(3 marks)**

**Model Answer (0.75 m per component):**

| Resistive Force | Expression | Design Lever to Reduce It |
|---|---|---|
| Rolling resistance $F_r$ | $\mu_r M g \cos\alpha$ | Use low-$\mu_r$ tyres; reduce vehicle mass $M$ |
| Aerodynamic drag $F_d$ | $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f v^2$ | Streamline body (lower $C_d$); reduce frontal area $A_f$ |
| Grade force $F_g$ | $M g \sin\alpha$ | Reduce vehicle mass $M$ (route choice is fixed) |
| Inertial force $F_a$ | $M (1+\lambda) a$ | Reduce mass $M$ and rotational inertia $\lambda$ (e.g. forged alloy wheels) |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, ESE Module 1, Q-pattern]**

### ⭐ CHOICE A

#### Question A(a). Derive the longitudinal force balance equation for a road vehicle of mass $M$ moving up an incline $\alpha$ with acceleration $a$. Clearly state each term in the equation and explain the origin of the rotational mass factor $(1 + \lambda)$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Step-by-step:**

**(i) Free-body identification (1 m):** The vehicle is a rigid body acted on by four longitudinal forces — tractive effort $F_{te}$ (forward), rolling resistance $F_r$ (backward), aerodynamic drag $F_d$ (backward), and gravity component along the slope $F_g$ (backward when going up).

**(ii) Sub-models of each resistive force (3 m):**
- Rolling resistance arises from tyre hysteresis: $F_r = \mu_r M g \cos\alpha$
- Aerodynamic drag arises from dynamic pressure on the frontal area: $F_d = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f v^2$
- Grade force is the projection of weight along the slope: $F_g = M g \sin\alpha$

**(iii) Newton's Second Law in the longitudinal direction (1 m):**
$$F_{te} - F_r - F_d - F_g = M a$$

**(iv) Rotational inertia correction (2 m):** When the vehicle accelerates, the wheels, half-shafts, gearbox, and motor rotor also spin up. The total kinetic energy of the system is $KE = \tfrac{1}{2} M v^2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\sum I_i \omega_i^2$. Expressing all rotational speeds in terms of $v = \omega r$ and the overall gear ratio gives $KE = \tfrac{1}{2} M (1+\lambda) v^2$, where $\lambda$ accounts for the ratio of equivalent rotational mass to vehicle mass. Differentiating yields the modified Newton's Law:

$$F_{te} - F_r - F_d - F_g = M(1+\lambda) a$$

**Final boxed equation (0 m, but should appear in the answer script):**
$$\boxed{\,F_{te} = F_r + F_d + F_g + M(1+\lambda)a\,}$$

---

#### Question A(b). An electric car of mass $M = 1400$ kg, frontal area $A_f = 2.1$ m², and drag coefficient $C_d = 0.28$ is cruising at $v = 90$ km/h on a level road. The rolling resistance coefficient is $\mu_r = 0.010$, rotational mass factor $\lambda = 0.08$, transmission efficiency $\eta_t = 0.92$, single gear ratio $i_g = 8.5$, and wheel radius $r = 0.31$ m. Compute: (i) the tractive effort required to maintain this constant speed, (ii) the motor torque needed, and (iii) the energy consumed in maintaining this speed for 1 hour. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Tractive effort at constant speed (3 m):**
At constant velocity, $a = 0$. Hence $F_{te} = F_r + F_d$.

- Convert speed: $v = 90/3.6 = 25$ m/s
- $F_r = 0.010 \times 1400 \times 9.81 = 137.3$ N
- $F_d = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.28 \times 2.1 \times (25)^2 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.28 \times 2.1 \times 625 = 225.1$ N
- $F_{te} = 137.3 + 225.1 = 362.4$ N

**(ii) Motor torque (2 m):**
$$T_m = \frac{F_{te} \cdot r}{i_g \cdot \eta_t} = \frac{362.4 \times 0.31}{8.5 \times 0.92} = \frac{112.3}{7.82} = 14.36 \text{ N·m}$$

**(iii) Energy consumed in 1 hour (2 m):**
$$E = P_w \cdot t = F_{te} \cdot v \cdot t = 362.4 \times 25 \times 3600 = 3.262 \times 10^7 \text{ J} \approx 9.06 \text{ kWh}$$

> **Note:** If the question asks for battery-side energy, divide by $\eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_t$ (typically 0.85 – 0.90), giving $\approx 10.1$ kWh drawn from the pack.

---

### ⭐ CHOICE B (Internal Choice Alternative)

#### Question B(a). With the aid of a block diagram, explain the energy flow from the battery pack to the wheels of an electric vehicle during acceleration. Identify the points where the major energy losses occur. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Block diagram identification (2 m):** The energy flow is:
Battery (DC) → Inverter (DC→AC with switching losses) → Traction Motor (electrical→mechanical with copper + iron + friction losses) → Gearbox (mechanical with meshing + bearing losses) → Wheels (mechanical, with rolling loss to road).

**(ii) Energy loss points (3 m):**
1. **Inverter losses** (5 – 8 %): switching losses in IGBT/SiC devices and conduction losses.
2. **Motor losses** (5 – 10 %): stator copper loss $I^2R$, rotor copper or magnet eddy current loss, iron core loss, friction and windage.
3. **Transmission losses** (5 – 10 %): gear meshing, churning, bearing friction. Quantified by $\eta_t \approx 0.90$–$0.95$.
4. **Rolling losses at the road** (continuous, $\mu_r M g v$): dissipated as heat in tyres.
5. **Aerodynamic losses** ($\tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f v^3$): dissipated as turbulence and pressure wave in the wake.

**(iii) Energy stored in kinetic motion (1 m):** The net remaining energy goes into $\tfrac{1}{2}M(1+\lambda)v^2$, which is recoverable during regenerative braking if the motor and inverter are operated as a generator and the battery is in a state to accept charge.

**(iv) Conclusion (1 m):** The overall wall-to-wheel efficiency of a well-designed EV is typically **80 – 90 %**, far superior to the 25 – 35 % thermal efficiency of an internal combustion engine vehicle. This is one of the strongest motivations for vehicle electrification.

---

#### Question B(b). A 1800 kg electric SUV accelerates from rest to 100 km/h. The rotational mass factor is $\lambda = 0.12$, the average tractive force available at the road is $F_{te} = 7000$ N, and the average resistive force (rolling + drag, treated as a single constant) is $F_{res} = 600$ N. Compute: (i) the kinetic energy gained by the vehicle at 100 km/h, (ii) the time taken to reach 100 km/h, and (iii) the average power delivered during this acceleration event. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Kinetic energy gained (2 m):**
- $v_1 = 0$, $v_2 = 100/3.6 = 27.78$ m/s
- $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} M(1+\lambda)(v_2^2 - v_1^2) = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1800 \times 1.12 \times (27.78)^2$
- $E_k = 0.5 \times 1800 \times 1.12 \times 771.6 = 7.78 \times 10^5$ J $\approx 778$ kJ

**(ii) Time taken (2 m):**
- Net accelerating force: $F_{net} = F_{te} - F_{res} = 7000 - 600 = 6400$ N
- $t_a = \dfrac{M(1+\lambda)(v_2 - v_1)}{F_{net}} = \dfrac{1800 \times 1.12 \times 27.78}{6400} = \dfrac{55973.8}{6400} \approx 8.75$ s

**(iii) Average power (3 m):**
Method 1 (using energy / time): $P_{avg} = E_k / t_a = 7.78 \times 10^5 / 8.75 = 88,914$ W $\approx 88.9$ kW.

Method 2 (using force × average velocity): $v_{avg} = 27.78/2 = 13.89$ m/s. $P_{avg} = F_{net} \cdot v_{avg} = 6400 \times 13.89 = 88,896$ W $\approx 88.9$ kW.

Both methods agree, confirming the consistency of the energy-time formulation.

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the rotational mass factor $\lambda$:** A common error is to use $M$ instead of $M(1+\lambda)$. This under-predicts energy and time by ~10 % and costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Unit conversion error on speed:** Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s when computing $v^2$ for kinetic energy is a silent killer — it inflates the answer by a factor of 12.96 and gets **zero** credit.
> 3. **Mixing force balance with power balance:** Students often write $P = F v$ without clarifying that $F$ must be the **net accelerating force** $F_{net}$ (not $F_{te}$), otherwise the result includes the energy dissipated as heat in rolling and drag rather than the energy stored in the vehicle.
> 4. **Neglecting $\cos\alpha$ and $\sin\alpha$ for grade force on shallow slopes:** A safe approximation is $\cos\alpha \approx 1$, $\sin\alpha \approx \alpha$ (radians) for $\alpha < 10°$. The examiner expects you to **state the approximation** if you use it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Quick-Reference Checklist for Last-Minute Revision**

- [x] **Master equation:** $F_{te} = F_r + F_d + F_g + M(1+\lambda)a$ — this is the single most important equation for Module 1.
- [x] **Four resistive components:**
  - $F_r = \mu_r M g \cos\alpha$ — constant or mildly $v$-dependent
  - $F_d = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f v^2$ — **quadratic in $v$**, dominant at high speed
  - $F_g = M g \sin\alpha$ — constant for a given slope
  - $F_a = M(1+\lambda)a$ — inertial, including rotational effects
- [x] **Tractive effort from motor:** $F_{te} = \dfrac{T_m \cdot i_g \cdot \eta_t}{r}$ — never forget to divide by the wheel radius and include efficiency.
- [x] **Acceleration formula:** $a = \dfrac{F_{te} - F_r - F_d - F_g}{M(1+\lambda)}$
- [x] **Power at wheels:** $P_w = F_{te} v$ — linear in both $F_{te}$ and $v$.
- [x] **Energy to accelerate (path-independent):** $E_a = \tfrac{1}{2} M (1+\lambda)(v_2^2 - v_1^2)$.
- [x] **Time to accelerate (constant net force):** $t_a = \dfrac{M(1+\lambda)(v_2 - v_1)}{F_{net}}$.
- [x] **Sign convention:** $\alpha > 0$ for uphill; $F_g$ reverses sign for downhill (gravity-assist).
- [x] **Constants to remember:** $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\rho_{air} = 1.225$ kg/m³, $\lambda \approx 0.10$ for typical EV.
- [x] **Units discipline:** Always work in **N, m, kg, s, m/s, m/s², J, W** — convert km/h to m/s by dividing by 3.6.
- [x] **Engineering trade-off:** Higher gear ratio → more torque, faster acceleration, lower top speed. Lower gear ratio → less torque, slower acceleration, higher top speed.
- [x] **Thermal limit vs. peak:** Peak $F_{te}$ (used for launch) is typically **2 – 3 times** the continuous $F_{te}$ that the motor can sustain thermally.

> **Final Word for KTU 2024 Candidates:** A clean, well-labelled free-body diagram followed by the **master force equation** with each term clearly defined is worth at least **60 %** of the marks on any vehicle-acceleration problem. The remaining 40 % is plug-and-chug arithmetic with disciplined unit handling.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Calculation of Motor Power from Traction Torque
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET742 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES AND RENEWABLE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Calculation of Motor Power from Traction Torque

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Traction Torque** is the rotational twisting moment produced by the electric motor (after gear reduction) that is delivered to the driven wheels of an Electric Vehicle (EV), which ultimately generates the **Tractive Force ($F_t$)** required to propel the vehicle against resistive forces.

**Motor Power ($P_m$)** in the traction context is defined as the rate at which the traction motor does useful mechanical work to overcome road load, gradient, and inertia, given by the product of the **motor shaft torque ($T_m$)** and the **motor shaft angular velocity ($\omega_m$)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The relationship $P = T \cdot \omega$ forms the fundamental link between the rotational domain (motor) and the translational domain (vehicle). Mastery of this conversion is mandatory for solving powertrain sizing problems in Part B examinations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bicycle Pedal"

Imagine you are pedaling a bicycle. The force you apply on the pedal creates a **torque** about the crank axis. That torque is transmitted via the **chain (gear)** to the rear wheel, which spins at a different speed. The wheel, in contact with the road, converts that rotational motion into a **linear force** (tractive force) that pushes the bicycle forward.

- **Pedal force × crank length = Your input torque** (analogous to motor torque $T_m$)
- **Chain gear ratio = Speed changer** (analogous to transmission ratio $G_r$)
- **Wheel rotation = Motor shaft rotation scaled** (analogous to $\omega_w$)
- **Push against road = Tractive force** (analogous to $F_t$)

The power you expend (rate of doing work) is the same whether you measure it at the pedal or at the wheel — only the **torque–speed combination changes** because of the gear ratio.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Power is invariant across an ideal gear train — only the (torque, speed) pair transforms. This is the **Golden Rule of Powertrain Design**.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|----------|--------|---------------|
| Power | $P$ | **Watt (W)** or **kW** |
| Torque | $T$ | **Newton-metre (N·m)** |
| Angular Velocity | $\omega$ | **rad/s** |
| Rotational Speed | $N$ | **rpm** |
| Tractive Force | $F_t$ | **Newton (N)** |
| Vehicle Velocity | $V$ | **m/s** |
| Wheel Radius | $r_w$ | **m** |
| Gear Ratio | $G_r$ | **dimensionless** |
| Efficiency | $\eta$ | **dimensionless (0 to 1)** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — From Motor Shaft to Road

The transformation of mechanical energy from the motor to the road surface follows a strict four-stage cascade:

**Step 1 — Motor Output (Rotational Domain)**
The electric machine delivers torque $T_m$ at angular speed $\omega_m$. This is the *raw* mechanical output before any gearing.

**Step 2 — Transmission / Gearbox**
A fixed-ratio (or multi-ratio) transmission multiplies the torque by $G_r$ and divides the speed by the same factor. The wheel-side quantities become:
- Wheel torque: $T_w = G_r \cdot \eta_g \cdot T_m$
- Wheel angular speed: $\omega_w = \omega_m / G_r$

**Step 3 — Wheel–Road Interface**
The driven wheel of effective rolling radius $r_w$ converts rotational motion to linear motion:
- Tractive force at the contact patch: $F_t = T_w / r_w$
- Linear vehicle speed: $V = \omega_w \cdot r_w$

**Step 4 — Vehicle-Level Power**
The power delivered to the road is the **tractive power**, equal to force times velocity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this cascade matters:** It allows the engineer to specify motor power either from the *top-down* (vehicle performance requirements) or the *bottom-up* (motor nameplate data). Both approaches must yield the same numerical answer — a critical KTU validation check.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Invariance of Power

In an ideal (lossless) transmission, conservation of energy demands:

$$P_{in} = P_{out}$$

Since $P = T \cdot \omega$, and the gear multiplies $T$ by $G_r$ while dividing $\omega$ by $G_r$, the product $T \cdot \omega$ remains unchanged. In a *real* system, power is reduced by the transmission efficiency $\eta_g$, so $P_{out} = \eta_g \cdot P_{in}$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $P = T \cdot \omega$ | Mechanical power from torque and angular speed | W, N·m, rad/s |
| 2 | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | Angular velocity from rotational speed in rpm | rad/s, rpm |
| 3 | $P = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | Power in W from N (rpm) and T (N·m) | W |
| 4 | $T_w = G_r \cdot \eta_g \cdot T_m$ | Torque amplification through gearbox | N·m |
| 5 | $\omega_w = \dfrac{\omega_m}{G_r}$ | Speed reduction through gearbox | rad/s |
| 6 | $F_t = \dfrac{T_w}{r_w}$ | Tractive force at tyre contact patch | N |
| 7 | $V = \omega_w \cdot r_w$ | Vehicle linear speed from wheel angular speed | m/s |
| 8 | $P_{traction} = F_t \cdot V$ | Tractive power (at the road) | W |
| 9 | $P_{wheel} = T_w \cdot \omega_w$ | Power at the wheel (post-gearbox) | W |
| 10 | $P_{battery} = \dfrac{P_{motor}}{\eta_m \cdot \eta_g}$ | Electrical input power from battery | W |
| 11 | $V\;[\text{km/h}] = \dfrac{2\pi N r_w}{60} \cdot 3.6 \cdot 10^{-3}$ | Speed conversion (rpm to km/h) | km/h |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pitfall:** Never confuse **rad/s** with **rpm** in formula (1). The conversion factor $\dfrac{2\pi}{60} \approx 0.10472$ is the most common source of arithmetic error in KTU numericals.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

This calculation is the **backbone of EV powertrain sizing**:
- **Range Estimation:** Battery energy ÷ tractive power = driving duration.
- **Gradeability:** Required tractive force at a given slope determines motor torque rating.
- **Top Speed Limitation:** Maximum motor RPM and minimum gear ratio set the ceiling on $V_{max}$.
- **Regenerative Braking:** The same equations in reverse give recovered power.
- **Thermal Management:** Power losses (which scale with $\dfrac{P}{\eta}$) dictate cooling system design.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mechanical Power from Torque and Angular Velocity

**Statement:** Mechanical power is the scalar product of torque vector and angular velocity vector. For a rotating shaft:

**Derivation:**

Consider a tangential force $F$ acting at radius $r$ on a rotating body. The linear velocity of the point of application is $v = r\omega$. The instantaneous power is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= F \cdot v \\
  &= F \cdot (r\omega)
\end{aligned}
$$

By definition of torque, $T = F \cdot r$, hence $F = T / r$. Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \left(\frac{T}{r}\right) \cdot (r\omega) \\
  &= T \cdot \omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logical note:** The radius $r$ cancels, confirming that power depends *only* on the torque–speed product, independent of the lever arm.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Conversion Between RPM and rad/s

**Statement:** One revolution equals $2\pi$ radians, and one minute equals 60 seconds.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega \left[\frac{\text{rad}}{\text{s}}\right] &= N \left[\frac{\text{rev}}{\text{min}}\right] \times \frac{2\pi \; \text{rad}}{1 \; \text{rev}} \times \frac{1 \; \text{min}}{60 \; \text{s}} \\
&= \frac{2\pi N}{60} \\
&\approx 0.10472 \, N
\end{aligned}
$$

**Inverse conversion:**

$$
N = \frac{60 \, \omega}{2\pi} = \frac{30 \, \omega}{\pi} \approx 9.5493 \, \omega
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Power Equivalence Across the Drivetrain

**Given:** A motor produces torque $T_m$ at angular speed $\omega_m$. The transmission has ratio $G_r$ and efficiency $\eta_g$. The wheel has rolling radius $r_w$.

**Step 3.3.1 — Power at the motor shaft:**

$$
P_m = T_m \cdot \omega_m
$$

**Step 3.3.2 — Power at the wheel (after transmission losses):**

$$
P_w = \eta_g \cdot P_m = \eta_g \cdot T_m \cdot \omega_m
$$

**Step 3.3.3 — Express wheel quantities in terms of motor quantities:**

Using the gear relations:

$$
T_w = G_r \cdot \eta_g \cdot T_m \qquad \text{and} \qquad \omega_w = \frac{\omega_m}{G_r}
$$

Multiplying these:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_w \cdot \omega_w &= (G_r \cdot \eta_g \cdot T_m) \cdot \left(\frac{\omega_m}{G_r}\right) \\
&= \eta_g \cdot T_m \cdot \omega_m \\
&= P_w
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logical note:** The factor $G_r$ cancels exactly, proving the power-invariance principle through the gearbox.

**Step 3.3.4 — Tractive force and vehicle speed:**

$$
F_t = \frac{T_w}{r_w} = \frac{G_r \cdot \eta_g \cdot T_m}{r_w}
$$

$$
V = \omega_w \cdot r_w = \frac{\omega_m \cdot r_w}{G_r}
$$

**Step 3.3.5 — Tractive power (final expression):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{traction} &= F_t \cdot V \\
&= \left(\frac{G_r \cdot \eta_g \cdot T_m}{r_w}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{\omega_m \cdot r_w}{G_r}\right) \\
&= \eta_g \cdot T_m \cdot \omega_m \\
&= P_w
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** In a real system, the tractive power equals the wheel power. The full power flow is:

$$
P_{battery} \xrightarrow{\eta_m} P_{motor} \xrightarrow{\eta_g} P_{wheel} = P_{traction}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example (Foundational KTU Pattern):** A 4-wheel EV with the following data:
> - Motor peak torque: $T_m = 250$ N·m
> - Motor peak speed: $N_m = 8000$ rpm
> - Single-speed gear ratio: $G_r = 8.0$
> - Gearbox efficiency: $\eta_g = 0.95$
> - Wheel rolling radius: $r_w = 0.32$ m
> - Motor efficiency: $\eta_m = 0.92$

**Compute:** (i) Motor peak power, (ii) Wheel torque, (iii) Tractive force, (iv) Vehicle top speed, (v) Tractive power, (vi) Battery electrical power.

**Solution — Part (i): Motor peak power**

Convert speed to rad/s:

$$
\omega_m = \frac{2\pi \times 8000}{60} = 837.76 \; \text{rad/s}
$$

$$
P_m = T_m \cdot \omega_m = 250 \times 837.76 = 209{,}440 \; \text{W} \approx 209.4 \; \text{kW}
$$

**Solution — Part (ii): Wheel torque**

$$
T_w = G_r \cdot \eta_g \cdot T_m = 8.0 \times 0.95 \times 250 = 1900 \; \text{N·m}
$$

**Solution — Part (iii): Tractive force**

$$
F_t = \frac{T_w}{r_w} = \frac{1900}{0.32} = 5937.5 \; \text{N} \approx 5.94 \; \text{kN}
$$

**Solution — Part (iv): Top speed**

$$
\omega_w = \frac{\omega_m}{G_r} = \frac{837.76}{8.0} = 104.72 \; \text{rad/s}
$$

$$
V = \omega_w \cdot r_w = 104.72 \times 0.32 = 33.51 \; \text{m/s} = 120.6 \; \text{km/h}
$$

**Solution — Part (v): Tractive power**

$$
P_{traction} = F_t \cdot V = 5937.5 \times 33.51 = 198{,}966 \; \text{W} \approx 199.0 \; \text{kW}
$$

**Cross-check (v):** $P_{traction} = \eta_g \cdot P_m = 0.95 \times 209.44 = 198.97$ kW ✓ (matches within rounding).

**Solution — Part (vi): Battery electrical power**

$$
P_{battery} = \frac{P_m}{\eta_m} = \frac{209.44}{0.92} = 227.7 \; \text{kW}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Power Flow Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    batNode[BATTERY DC BUS] -->|P_battery kW| invNode[POWER ELECTRONICS INVERTER]
    invNode -->|eta_m| motNode[ELECTRIC TRACTION MOTOR]
    motNode -->|T_m N_m| trnNode[SINGLE SPEED TRANSMISSION G_r eta_g]
    trnNode -->|T_w omega_w| whlNode[DRIVEN WHEELS r_w]
    whlNode -->|F_t V| roadNode[ROAD SURFACE Tractive Power]
    regenNode[REGEN BRAKING PATH] -.->|reverse flow| invNode
```

### 4.2 Signal Transformation Cascade (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    a1[MOTOR TORQUE T_m N m] --> a2[GEARBOX T_w = G_r eta_g T_m]
    a2 --> a3[WHEEL FORCE F_t = T_w slash r_w]
    a1 --> b1[MOTOR SPEED omega_m rad per s]
    b1 --> b2[WHEEL SPEED omega_w = omega_m slash G_r]
    b2 --> b3[VEHICLE SPEED V = omega_w r_w]
    a3 --> c1[TRACTIVE POWER P = F_t V]
    b3 --> c1
```

### 4.3 Power Loss Hierarchy (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    srcNode[BATTERY ELECTRICAL POWER] -->|eta_m = 0.92| mechNode[MOTOR MECHANICAL POWER]
    mechNode -->|eta_g = 0.95| whlNode[WHEEL MECHANICAL POWER]
    whlNode -->|equals| trcNode[TRACTIVE POWER AT ROAD]
    mechNode -.->|8 percent loss| heat1[HEAT MOTOR]
    whlNode -.->|5 percent loss| heat2[HEAT GEARBOX]
```

### 4.4 Parameter Interaction Matrix (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    gNode[GEAR RATIO G_r] -->|increase| tIncNode[WHEEL TORQUE T_w INCREASES]
    gNode -->|increase| vDecNode[VEHICLE SPEED V DECREASES]
    gNode -->|constant| pInvNode[POWER INVARIANT]
    rNode[WHEEL RADIUS r_w] -->|increase| fDecNode[TRACTIVE FORCE F_t DECREASES]
    rNode -->|increase| vIncNode[VEHICLE SPEED V INCREASES]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The four diagrams together capture the **directional flow** of power, the **parallel transformation** of torque and speed, the **loss allocation** between subsystems, and the **parametric sensitivity** of the design. These are the four perspectives KTU examiners expect students to articulate in 14-mark analytical questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define traction torque and state the fundamental relationship between motor power, motor torque, and motor angular speed. Mention the SI units of each quantity.*

**Model Answer:**

Traction torque is the rotational moment transmitted from the electric motor through the transmission to the driven wheels of an electric vehicle, enabling it to overcome road resistances and propel the vehicle forward.

The fundamental relationship is:

$$P = T \cdot \omega$$

where:
- $P$ = mechanical power in **Watts (W)**
- $T$ = torque in **Newton-metres (N·m)**
- $\omega$ = angular velocity in **radians per second (rad/s)**

When the rotational speed is given in rpm ($N$), the equivalent form is:

$$P = \frac{2\pi N T}{60}$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*With the aid of an expression, show that mechanical power is invariant across an ideal (lossless) gear train.*

**Model Answer:**

In an ideal gear train of ratio $G_r$, the input torque is multiplied by $G_r$ and the input speed is divided by $G_r$:

$$T_{out} = G_r \cdot T_{in}, \qquad \omega_{out} = \frac{\omega_{in}}{G_r}$$

The output power is therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{out} &= T_{out} \cdot \omega_{out} \\
&= (G_r \cdot T_{in}) \cdot \left(\frac{\omega_{in}}{G_r}\right) \\
&= T_{in} \cdot \omega_{in} \\
&= P_{in}
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence, mechanical power is conserved across an ideal gear train. (In a real system, $P_{out} = \eta_g \cdot P_{in}$.)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]**

An electric vehicle has the following specifications:
- Maximum motor torque: $T_m = 320$ N·m
- Maximum motor speed: $N_m = 9000$ rpm
- Single-speed gearbox ratio: $G_r = 7.5$
- Gearbox efficiency: $\eta_g = 0.96$
- Motor efficiency: $\eta_m = 0.94$
- Wheel rolling radius: $r_w = 0.33$ m
- Vehicle mass: $m = 1500$ kg

**Part (a) [7 Marks]** — Calculate the **peak motor power**, **wheel torque**, and **tractive force** at the road.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]** — Calculate the **top vehicle speed** in km/h, the **tractive power** at the road, and the **battery electrical power** required under peak conditions. Also verify the power invariance across the drivetrain.

---

**Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a):**

Convert motor speed to rad/s:

$$\omega_m = \frac{2\pi \times 9000}{60} = 942.48 \; \text{rad/s}$$

Peak motor power:

$$P_m = T_m \cdot \omega_m = 320 \times 942.48 = 301{,}593 \; \text{W} \approx 301.6 \; \text{kW}$$

Wheel torque:

$$T_w = G_r \cdot \eta_g \cdot T_m = 7.5 \times 0.96 \times 320 = 2304 \; \text{N·m}$$

Tractive force:

$$F_t = \frac{T_w}{r_w} = \frac{2304}{0.33} = 6981.8 \; \text{N} \approx 6.98 \; \text{kW}$$

**Part (b):**

Wheel angular speed:
