$$\omega_w = \frac{\omega_m}{G_r} = \frac{942.48}{7.5} = 125.66 \; \text{rad/s}$$

Top vehicle speed:

$$V = \omega_w \cdot r_w = 125.66 \times 0.33 = 41.47 \; \text{m/s}$$

Convert to km/h:

$$V = 41.47 \times 3.6 = 149.3 \; \text{km/h}$$

Tractive power:

$$P_{traction} = F_t \cdot V = 6981.8 \times 41.47 = 289{,}545 \; \text{W} \approx 289.5 \; \text{kW}$$

Cross-check via drivetrain:

$$P_{traction} = \eta_g \cdot P_m = 0.96 \times 301.6 = 289.5 \; \text{kW} \; \checkmark$$

Battery electrical power:

$$P_{battery} = \frac{P_m}{\eta_m} = \frac{301.6}{0.94} = 320.8 \; \text{kW}$$

**Power flow summary:**

$$P_{battery}\;(320.8\;\text{kW}) \xrightarrow{\eta_m=0.94} P_m\;(301.6\;\text{kW}) \xrightarrow{\eta_g=0.96} P_{traction}\;(289.5\;\text{kW})$$

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]**

A rear-wheel-drive electric car uses a single motor with the following data:
- Motor rated speed: $N_m = 6000$ rpm
- Gear ratio: $G_r = 9.0$
- Wheel rolling radius: $r_w = 0.30$ m
- Required top speed: $V_{max} = 120$ km/h
- Required tractive force at $V_{max}$: $F_t = 2500$ N
- Gearbox efficiency: $\eta_g = 0.95$
- Motor efficiency: $\eta_m = 0.93$

**Part (a) [7 Marks]** — Determine the **motor shaft torque**, **motor shaft power**, and **motor angular speed** required to meet the top-speed tractive force specification.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]** — Calculate the **battery electrical power** demand, the **energy consumed in 1 hour** of steady-state cruising at $V_{max}$, and verify whether the motor's rated speed is sufficient to achieve the top speed.

---

**Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a):**

Convert top speed to m/s:

$$V = \frac{120}{3.6} = 33.33 \; \text{m/s}$$

Wheel angular speed:

$$\omega_w = \frac{V}{r_w} = \frac{33.33}{0.30} = 111.11 \; \text{rad/s}$$

Motor angular speed (must be at least equal to $\omega_m$ at $V_{max}$):

$$\omega_m = G_r \cdot \omega_w = 9.0 \times 111.11 = 1000.0 \; \text{rad/s}$$

Convert to rpm to verify against motor rating:

$$N_m = \frac{60 \times 1000}{2\pi} = 9549.3 \; \text{rpm}$$

Since the motor's rated speed is 6000 rpm (i.e., $628.3$ rad/s) and the required $\omega_m = 1000$ rad/s, **the motor's rated speed is INSUFFICIENT** to achieve the top speed — the motor must be re-specified.

> **Note for examiner:** The question still asks the student to compute the *required* motor shaft quantities and then to verify the design.

Wheel torque required (from tractive force):

$$T_w = F_t \cdot r_w = 2500 \times 0.30 = 750 \; \text{N·m}$$

Motor shaft torque required:

$$T_m = \frac{T_w}{G_r \cdot \eta_g} = \frac{750}{9.0 \times 0.95} = 87.72 \; \text{N·m}$$

Motor shaft power (mechanical):

$$P_m = T_m \cdot \omega_m = 87.72 \times 1000 = 87{,}720 \; \text{W} \approx 87.7 \; \text{kW}$$

**Part (b):**

Battery electrical power:

$$P_{battery} = \frac{P_m}{\eta_m} = \frac{87.72}{0.93} = 94.32 \; \text{kW}$$

Energy consumed in 1 hour of steady-state cruising:

$$E = P_{battery} \times t = 94.32 \; \text{kW} \times 1 \; \text{h} = 94.32 \; \text{kWh}$$

Verification of rated-speed adequacy:

The motor must be capable of $\omega_m = 1000.0$ rad/s ($\approx 9549$ rpm) to reach 120 km/h. Since the rated speed is 6000 rpm, the motor's speed capability falls short by:

$$\Delta N = 9549 - 6000 = 3549 \; \text{rpm} \quad \text{(deficit)}$$

The maximum achievable speed with the present motor is:

$$V_{ach} = \frac{2\pi N_{rated} r_w}{60 \times G_r} \times 3.6 = \frac{2\pi \times 6000 \times 0.30}{60 \times 9.0} \times 3.6 = 75.4 \; \text{km/h}$$

The motor must be re-selected with rated speed $\geq 9549$ rpm to meet the 120 km/h target.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Mix-ups (–2 marks):** Conflating rad/s with rpm is the single largest error. Always show the conversion $\omega = 2\pi N/60$ explicitly.
> 2. **Omitting Efficiencies (–2 marks):** In real systems, the chain $\eta_m \cdot \eta_g$ MUST be included when moving from battery → road. Dropping efficiency is a 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Wrong Sign of Gear Ratio Effect (–1 mark):** Students often write $T_w = T_m / G_r$. The correct relation for a speed-reducing gearbox (motor faster than wheel) is $T_w = G_r \cdot T_m$.
> 4. **Top-Speed vs. Rated-Power Confusion (–2 marks):** Top speed is set by max motor RPM and gear ratio, not by peak power. A 14-mark answer must distinguish the two regimes clearly.
> 5. **Forgetting to Cross-Check (–1 mark):** Always verify $P_{traction} = \eta_g \cdot P_m = F_t \cdot V$. The KTU marking scheme awards 1 mark for this sanity-check.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Master Equation:** $P = T \cdot \omega$ (W) with $\omega$ in rad/s. With $N$ in rpm, use $P = 2\pi N T / 60$.
- **Conversion Constant:** $1$ rpm $= 2\pi/60 \approx 0.10472$ rad/s. Memorize this — it appears in every KTU numerical.
- **Gear Train Invariance:** Ideal gearbox conserves power; only the $(T, \omega)$ pair transforms as $T_{out} = G_r T_{in}$ and $\omega_{out} = \omega_{in}/G_r$.
- **Wheel-Interface Equations:** $F_t = T_w / r_w$ and $V = \omega_w \cdot r_w$. These two are the bridge between rotational and translational domains.
- **Power Flow Chain:** $P_{battery} \to P_{motor} \;(\eta_m) \to P_{wheel} \;(\eta_g) \to P_{traction}$. Always apply efficiencies only when crossing the *corresponding* subsystem boundary.
- **Two Design Knobs:** $G_r$ trades torque for speed; $r_w$ trades tractive force for vehicle speed. Both are pivotal in top-speed and gradeability trade-offs.
- **Numerical Discipline:** Carry 4–5 significant figures in intermediate steps; round only at the final answer to avoid compounding error.
- **Cross-Check Ritual:** Always verify $F_t \cdot V = \eta_g \cdot T_m \cdot \omega_m$. KTU examiners reward this verification step.
- **Typical Numerical Range (KTU):** $T_m \in [100, 400]$ N·m; $N_m \in [4000, 15000]$ rpm; $G_r \in [6, 12]$; $r_w \in [0.25, 0.35]$ m; $\eta_m, \eta_g \in [0.90, 0.97]$.
- **Forbidden in Formula Sheet:** Never quote $P = T \cdot \omega$ without specifying that $\omega$ is in **rad/s** — it is the most-cited reason for 0.5–1 mark deductions in KTU scripts.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simple Numerical Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET742 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES AND RENEWABLE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Simple Numerical Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Simple Numerical Problems** in the context of **PEAET742 – Electric Vehicles and Renewable Technologies** are quantitative engineering exercises that test the student's ability to apply foundational formulas of energy, power, efficiency, range, battery sizing, solar irradiance, and wind energy to first-principles, single-step or few-step calculations directly assessable in KTU 2024 Scheme examinations.

These problems require the student to:

* Convert physical quantities into consistent **SI units** (Volts, Ampere-hours, Watt-hours, meters, seconds).
* Substitute known values into standardized KTU-recommended equations.
* Arrive at a numerical answer with correct significant figures and unit notation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy – The Water Tank Model:**
> Think of a **battery** as a water tank, **current (I)** as the flow rate of water, and **voltage (V)** as the water pressure.
> * **Capacity (Ah)** = total size of the tank.
> * **Energy stored (Wh)** = pressure × volume of water.
> * **Range of an EV** = how far the vehicle can travel before the tank empties at a given consumption rate.
> * **Solar Panel Output** = how fast a rain bucket (sunlight) fills the tank, depending on intensity (irradiance) and bucket area (panel size).

This mental model makes every numerical problem intuitive: you are always balancing **energy in**, **energy stored**, and **energy out**.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

* **Standard gravity:** $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$
* **Standard Test Condition (STC) irradiance:** $G_{STC} = 1000 \; \text{W/m}^2$
* **Air density (at sea level, 15°C):** $\rho = 1.225 \; \text{kg/m}^3$
* **Solar constant (AM1.5):** $G_{sc} = 1367 \; \text{W/m}^2$
* **Energy in 1 kWh:** $1 \; \text{kWh} = 3.6 \times 10^6 \; \text{J}$

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners award **partial marks** for correct unit conversion even if the final number is slightly off. Always write units in every step.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Conceptual Flow

Every Simple Numerical Problem in Module 1 maps to **one of five operational themes**. Mastering the logic behind each is the key to scoring full marks.

**Theme 1 – EV Battery & Range Calculations**

* **Why:** Range anxiety is the #1 design constraint of any electric vehicle. The KTU syllabus expects students to compute it from first principles.
* **How:**
  1. Find **stored battery energy**: $E = V \times Ah$ (Wh).
  2. Determine **vehicle energy consumption per km** (Wh/km) from given data.
  3. Compute **Range** = $\dfrac{\text{Stored Energy}}{\text{Energy per km}}$.

**Theme 2 – EV Power, Torque & Speed**

* **Why:** Traction motor sizing demands instantaneous power.
* **How:** Use the rotational mechanical identity $P = T \cdot \omega$ and the translational rolling identity $P = F \cdot v$.

**Theme 3 – Solar PV Energy Yield**

* **Why:** Renewable integration requires daily/monthly energy prediction.
* **How:** $E_{PV} = A \cdot G \cdot \eta \cdot t$, where $A$ is panel area, $G$ is irradiance, $\eta$ is efficiency, and $t$ is peak-sun-hours.

**Theme 4 – Wind Energy**

* **Why:** Wind is a stochastic resource; the cubic dependence on velocity makes numerical accuracy critical.
* **How:** $P_w = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot v^3 \cdot C_p$.

**Theme 5 – Charging & Discharging Time**

* **Why:** Infrastructure planning depends on how long an EV sits at a charger.
* **How:** $t = \dfrac{\text{Battery Capacity (Ah)}}{\text{Charging Current (A)}}$.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER TABLE – Print this before every exam.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Key | Standard Unit |
|---|----------|---------|------------|----------------|
| 1 | Electrical Energy | $E = V \cdot I \cdot t$ | $V$ = Voltage, $I$ = Current, $t$ = time | Wh or kWh |
| 2 | Battery Capacity (energy) | $E = V_{bat} \cdot C_{Ah}$ | $V_{bat}$ = nominal battery V, $C_{Ah}$ = Ah rating | Wh |
| 3 | EV Range | $R = \dfrac{E_{bat}}{E_{cons}}$ | $E_{cons}$ = Wh per km | km |
| 4 | Traction Power | $P = F \cdot v$ | $F$ = tractive force (N), $v$ = velocity (m/s) | W |
| 5 | Traction Power (rotational) | $P = T \cdot \omega$ | $T$ = torque (Nm), $\omega$ = rad/s | W |
| 6 | Energy from PV | $E_{PV} = \eta \cdot A \cdot G \cdot t$ | $\eta$ = efficiency, $A$ = area (m²), $G$ = W/m² | Wh |
| 7 | PV Power Output | $P_{PV} = \eta \cdot A \cdot G$ | instantaneous power | W |
| 8 | Wind Power | $P_w = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot v^3 \cdot C_p$ | $\rho$ = 1.225 kg/m³, $C_p$ ≤ 0.593 (Betz limit) | W |
| 9 | Swept Area of Turbine | $A = \pi \cdot r^2$ | $r$ = blade radius (m) | m² |
| 10 | Charging Time | $t = \dfrac{C_{Ah}}{I_{ch}}$ | $C_{Ah}$ in Ah, $I_{ch}$ in A | hours |
| 11 | Force on Inclined Plane | $F = m \cdot g \cdot (\sin\theta + \mu \cos\theta)$ | $\mu$ = rolling resistance coeff. | N |
| 12 | Energy Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{E_{out}}{E_{in}} \times 100$ | dimensionless | % |
| 13 | Coulomb / Ah to kWh | $E_{kWh} = \dfrac{V \cdot Ah}{1000}$ | direct conversion | kWh |
| 14 | C-rate | $\text{C-rate} = \dfrac{I_{ch}}{C_{Ah}}$ | measure of charge speed | h$^{-1}$ |

> [!TIP]
> In KTU answer sheets, **always show the unit of the final answer in a box** like $[\text{km}]$, $[\text{kWh}]$, $[\text{W}]$. Examiners frequently allot 0.5 marks for correct unit alone.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility of These Equations

* **EV Range** dictates market adoption (Tesla Model 3 ≈ 550 km, Tata Nexon EV ≈ 465 km).
* **Solar PV Yield** drives ROI of rooftop installations in Kerala's 4–5 peak-sun-hours/day climate.
* **Wind Power** underlies the national target of **500 GW non-fossil capacity by 2030**.
* **Charging time** is the bottleneck for fleet operators like **Kerala State Road Transport Corporation (KSRTC)** electric buses.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples (Analytical Matrix)

> [!NOTE]
> Below are **six fully solved numerical problems** with each algebraic step shown explicitly. KTU 2024 Scheme allows simple 4–6 line numericals, but the **board examiner rewards clarity of substitution**.

---

### Example 1 — EV Battery Energy & Range

**Problem [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]:**
An electric scooter uses a **48 V, 60 Ah lithium-ion battery pack**. The vehicle consumes an average of **40 Wh per km**. Compute:
(a) The total stored energy in kWh.
(b) The theoretical range of the vehicle.

**Step 1 – Identify the governing equation.**
Energy stored in a battery is:
$$E = V_{bat} \cdot C_{Ah}$$

**Step 2 – Substitute numerical values.**
$$E = 48 \; \text{V} \times 60 \; \text{Ah}$$

**Step 3 – Multiply to obtain Watt-hours.**
$$E = 2880 \; \text{Wh}$$

**Step 4 – Convert Watt-hours to kiloWatt-hours.**
$$E = \dfrac{2880}{1000} = 2.88 \; \text{kWh}$$

**Step 5 – Apply range formula.**
$$R = \dfrac{E_{bat}}{E_{cons}}$$

**Step 6 – Substitute.**
$$R = \dfrac{2880 \; \text{Wh}}{40 \; \text{Wh/km}}$$

**Step 7 – Final Answer.**
$$R = 72 \; \text{km}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Boxed Answers:**
> **(a) $E = 2.88 \; \text{kWh}$**
> **(b) $R = 72 \; \text{km}$**

---

### Example 2 — Solar PV Energy Yield

**Problem:**
A rooftop solar PV system has **6 panels**, each of area **1.6 m²** and efficiency **18%**. The location receives an average daily solar irradiation equivalent to **4.5 peak-sun-hours**. Calculate the daily energy generated in **Wh** and **kWh**.

**Step 1 – Total area of all panels.**
$$A_{total} = 6 \times 1.6 = 9.6 \; \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 – Energy equation.**
$$E_{PV} = \eta \cdot A \cdot G \cdot t$$

**Step 3 – Recall standard STC irradiance.**
At STC, $G = 1000 \; \text{W/m}^2$, and $t = 4.5 \; \text{h}$, so $G \cdot t = 4500 \; \text{Wh/m}^2$.

**Step 4 – Substitute.**
$$E_{PV} = 0.18 \times 9.6 \times 4500$$

**Step 5 – Multiply stepwise.**
$$E_{PV} = 0.18 \times 9.6 = 1.728$$
$$E_{PV} = 1.728 \times 4500$$

**Step 6 – Final computation.**
$$E_{PV} = 7776 \; \text{Wh}$$

**Step 7 – Convert to kWh.**
$$E_{PV} = 7.776 \; \text{kWh/day}$$

---

### Example 3 — Wind Turbine Power

**Problem:**
A horizontal-axis wind turbine has blades of length **3 m**, air density **1.225 kg/m³**, wind speed **12 m/s**, and power coefficient $C_p = 0.4$. Calculate the **mechanical power output**.

**Step 1 – Swept area.**
$$A = \pi \cdot r^2 = \pi \cdot 3^2 = 9\pi$$
$$A \approx 28.274 \; \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 – Wind power equation.**
$$P_w = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot v^3 \cdot C_p$$

**Step 3 – Cube the wind speed.**
$$v^3 = 12^3 = 1728 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}^3$$

**Step 4 – Substitute all values.**
$$P_w = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 28.274 \times 1728 \times 0.4$$

**Step 5 – Multiply progressively.**
$$P_w = 0.5 \times 1.225 = 0.6125$$
$$P_w = 0.6125 \times 28.274 = 17.318$$
$$P_w = 17.318 \times 1728 = 29{,}925$$
$$P_w = 29{,}925 \times 0.4 = 11{,}970 \; \text{W}$$

**Step 6 – Convert to kW.**
$$P_w \approx 11.97 \; \text{kW}$$

> [!TIP]
> The cubic dependence on $v$ means a **doubling of wind speed gives 8× power** — a favorite KTU trap-question concept.

---

### Example 4 — EV Charging Time

**Problem:**
A 4-wheeler EV has a **battery of 200 Ah** rated at **400 V nominal**. It is charged at a constant current of **50 A**. Find:
(a) The charging time in hours.
(b) The energy delivered in kWh.

**Step 1 – Charging time equation.**
$$t = \dfrac{C_{Ah}}{I_{ch}}$$

**Step 2 – Substitute.**
$$t = \dfrac{200}{50} = 4 \; \text{hours}$$

**Step 3 – Energy delivered.**
$$E = V \cdot I \cdot t = 400 \times 50 \times 4 = 80{,}000 \; \text{Wh}$$

**Step 4 – Convert to kWh.**
$$E = 80 \; \text{kWh}$$

---

### Example 5 — Traction Power & Tractive Force

**Problem:**
An EV of mass **1500 kg** (including battery) climbs a **5° slope** at constant speed of **54 km/h**. Rolling resistance coefficient $\mu = 0.015$. Find the tractive force and traction power.

**Step 1 – Convert speed to SI units.**
$$v = 54 \; \text{km/h} = \dfrac{54 \times 1000}{3600} = 15 \; \text{m/s}$$

**Step 2 – Tractive force equation (slope + rolling).**
$$F = m \cdot g \cdot (\sin\theta + \mu \cos\theta)$$

**Step 3 – Compute trig values.**
$$\sin 5° \approx 0.0872, \quad \cos 5° \approx 0.9962$$

**Step 4 – Substitute.**
$$F = 1500 \times 9.81 \times (0.0872 + 0.015 \times 0.9962)$$

**Step 5 – Inner bracket.**
$$0.015 \times 0.9962 = 0.01494$$
$$0.0872 + 0.01494 = 0.10214$$

**Step 6 – Multiply.**
$$F = 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.10214 = 1503.1 \; \text{N}$$

**Step 7 – Traction power.**
$$P = F \cdot v = 1503.1 \times 15 = 22{,}547 \; \text{W} \approx 22.55 \; \text{kW}$$

---

### Example 6 — Combined EV + PV System

**Problem:**
A house has a **5 kW rooftop PV system** generating **20 kWh/day**. The owner's EV consumes **150 Wh/km** and is driven **40 km/day**. Is the surplus solar energy sufficient to charge the EV? Show the calculation.

**Step 1 – EV daily energy demand.**
$$E_{EV} = 150 \; \text{Wh/km} \times 40 \; \text{km} = 6000 \; \text{Wh} = 6 \; \text{kWh/day}$$

**Step 2 – Solar surplus calculation.**
$$\text{Surplus} = 20 - 6 = 14 \; \text{kWh/day}$$

**Step 3 – Conclusion.**
Yes, the solar system generates **14 kWh/day surplus** after charging the EV, which is more than enough — the EV is **net-zero in operation** (a "solar-powered EV").

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This problem type is **very high-yield** in KTU 2024 ESE Part B questions.

---

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Quick Classification

| Problem Type | Primary Equation | Key Trap to Avoid |
|--------------|------------------|-------------------|
| Battery Energy | $E = V \cdot Ah$ | Forgetting to convert to kWh |
| Range | $R = E / E_{cons}$ | Mixing Wh and kWh |
| Solar Energy | $E = \eta A G t$ | Using panel-rated power instead of $\eta A G$ |
| Wind Power | $P = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^3 C_p$ | Forgetting $C_p$ or using $r$ vs. diameter |
| Charging Time | $t = C_{Ah} / I$ | Not converting minutes ↔ hours |
| Tractive Force | $F = mg(\sin\theta + \mu \cos\theta)$ | Forgetting $\cos\theta$ on flat roads ($\theta = 0$) |
| Power Conversion | $P = F \cdot v$ | Velocity in **m/s**, not km/h |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Since "Simple Numerical Problems" is an analytical topic, the schematic below maps the **end-to-end decision flow** a student must follow when solving any Module-1 numerical. This block-level architecture replaces a physical drawing and is highly examination-relevant.

### 4.1 Master Problem-Solving Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Read the Problem] --> B[Identify Given Quantities with Units]
    B --> C[Identify Unknown Quantity to Find]
    C --> D{Classify Theme}
    D --> E1[Battery / EV Range]
    D --> E2[Solar PV]
    D --> E3[Wind Energy]
    D --> E4[Traction / Force]
    D --> E5[Charging / Discharging]
    E1 --> F[Apply E = V x Ah]
    E2 --> G[Apply E = n x A x G x t]
    E3 --> H[Apply P = 0.5 x rho x A x v3 x Cp]
    E4 --> I[Apply F = mg sin + mu cos and P = F x v]
    E5 --> J[Apply t = C_Ah / I]
    F --> K[Convert Units: Wh to kWh, km/h to m/s]
    G --> K
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Box Final Answer with Unit]
    L --> M[Verify Reasonableness by Sanity Check]
    M --> N[End: Submit to Examiner]
```

### 4.2 EV Energy Balance Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[Source: Grid or Solar PV] --> INV[Charger / Inverter]
    INV --> BAT[Battery Pack V x Ah]
    BAT --> CONV[DC-DC Converter]
    CONV --> MOT[Traction Motor]
    MOT --> WHL[Vehicle Wheels]
    WHL --> LOAD[Energy Demand Wh per km]
    BAT -.Energy Feedback.-> REGEN[Regenerative Braking]
    REGEN --> BAT
```

### 4.3 Renewable–EV Integration Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SUN[Solar Irradiance G W per m2] --> PAN[PV Panel area A m2]
    PAN --> PVE[PV Energy n x A x G x t]
    WIND[Wind Speed v m per s] --> TURB[Wind Turbine area pi r2]
    TURB --> WPE[Wind Power 0.5 rho A v3 Cp]
    PVE --> BUS[DC Bus / Grid Tie]
    WPE --> BUS
    BUS --> EVCHG[EV Charger]
    EVCHG --> EVBATT[EV Battery]
    EVBATT --> EVMOTOR[EV Traction Motor]
    EVMOTOR --> EVROAD[Vehicle Range R km]
```

> [!TIP]
> Examiners love these **system-level flow diagrams** as 2-mark supporting sketches in Part B answers. Even a hand-drawn version on white paper scores.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
Define the term **Specific Energy** of a battery. State its unit and the typical range for lithium-ion cells used in EVs.

**Model Answer:**
Specific energy is the amount of energy stored per unit mass of a battery, expressed in **Wh/kg**.
$$\text{Specific Energy} = \dfrac{\text{Energy stored (Wh)}}{\text{Mass of battery (kg)}}$$
For Li-ion EV cells, it typically lies in the range **150–250 Wh/kg**, far higher than lead-acid (30–50 Wh/kg). Higher specific energy ⇒ lighter battery ⇒ longer EV range.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
State the **Betz Limit** in wind energy conversion. What is its numerical value?

**Model Answer:**
The Betz Limit is the theoretical maximum fraction of kinetic energy in wind that can be captured by an ideal wind turbine.
$$C_{p,\max} = \dfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.593 \; (59.3\%)$$
No real turbine can exceed this value; commercial turbines operate at $C_p = 0.35$ to $0.45$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> * **Never** skip unit conversion (km/h ↔ m/s, Wh ↔ kWh). 1 mark is reserved for unit handling.
> * **Never** write only the formula and final number. Show **substitution step explicitly**.
> * **Never** forget to box the final answer. Examiners look for the boxed value first.
> * **Never** confuse **Ah** (capacity) with **Wh** (energy). Multiply by voltage!

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

(a) An electric car is powered by a **360 V, 100 Ah lithium-ion battery pack**. The car's average energy consumption is **170 Wh/km**. Calculate:
(i) Total energy stored in the battery in kWh.
(ii) Theoretical range of the car in km.
(iii) The traction power required to maintain a constant speed of **90 km/h** on a level road, assuming an aerodynamic + rolling drag force of **350 N**.
*(7 marks)*

(b) A rooftop solar PV system of **10 panels**, each **320 W rated**, operates with **derating factors**: temperature = 0.90, dust = 0.95, wiring = 0.98. If the **average daily peak-sun-hours = 4.5**, find:
(i) Actual daily energy output in kWh.
(ii) Number of days required to deliver **500 kWh** of usable solar energy.
*(7 marks)*

---

**Solution A(a):**

**(i) Battery Energy:**
$$E = V \cdot C_{Ah} = 360 \times 100 = 36{,}000 \; \text{Wh}$$
$$E = 36 \; \text{kWh}$$

**(ii) Range:**
$$R = \dfrac{E}{E_{cons}} = \dfrac{36{,}000 \; \text{Wh}}{170 \; \text{Wh/km}}$$
$$R = 211.76 \; \text{km} \approx 212 \; \text{km}$$

**(iii) Traction Power:**
Convert velocity:
$$v = 90 \; \text{km/h} = 25 \; \text{m/s}$$
$$P = F \cdot v = 350 \times 25 = 8750 \; \text{W} = 8.75 \; \text{kW}$$

---

**Solution A(b):**

**(i) Daily Output:**
Effective power per panel = $320 \times 0.90 \times 0.95 \times 0.98$
$$= 320 \times 0.8379 = 268.13 \; \text{W}$$
Total system power = $10 \times 268.13 = 2681.3 \; \text{W}$
Daily energy = $2681.3 \times 4.5 = 12{,}066 \; \text{Wh} = 12.07 \; \text{kWh/day}$

**(ii) Days to deliver 500 kWh:**
$$\text{Days} = \dfrac{500}{12.07} \approx 41.4 \approx 42 \; \text{days}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – July 2023, Module 1]**

(a) A **3-blade horizontal-axis wind turbine** has a rotor diameter of **8 m**. The wind speed is **10 m/s**, air density is **1.225 kg/m³**, and the power coefficient is **0.38**. Calculate:
(i) The swept area of the rotor.
(ii) The available wind power in kW.
(iii) The actual electrical power output if the **generator + gearbox efficiency is 90%**.
*(7 marks)*

(b) An EV battery of capacity **150 Ah** at **400 V** is to be charged from **20% state-of-charge (SOC)** to **90% SOC** using a **constant current charger of 30 A**. Calculate:
(i) The Ah that must be delivered.
(ii) The charging time in hours.
(iii) The total energy delivered in kWh.
*(7 marks)*

---

**Solution B(a):**

**(i) Swept Area:**
$$r = \dfrac{D}{2} = 4 \; \text{m}$$
$$A = \pi r^2 = \pi \times 16 = 50.27 \; \text{m}^2$$

**(ii) Available Wind Power:**
$$P_w = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^3 C_p$$
$$P_w = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 50.27 \times (10)^3 \times 0.38$$
$$P_w = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 50.27 \times 1000 \times 0.38$$
$$P_w = 11{,}691 \; \text{W} \approx 11.69 \; \text{kW}$$

**(iii) Electrical Output:**
$$P_{elec} = 11{,}691 \times 0.90 = 10{,}522 \; \text{W} \approx 10.52 \; \text{kW}$$

---

**Solution B(b):**

**(i) Ah to be Delivered:**
$$\Delta \text{Ah} = (90\% - 20\%) \times 150 = 0.70 \times 150 = 105 \; \text{Ah}$$

**(ii) Charging Time:**
$$t = \dfrac{\Delta \text{Ah}}{I_{ch}} = \dfrac{105}{30} = 3.5 \; \text{hours}$$

**(iii) Energy Delivered:**
$$E = V \cdot \Delta \text{Ah} = 400 \times 105 = 42{,}000 \; \text{Wh} = 42 \; \text{kWh}$$

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module-1 Numericals**

* **Battery Energy:** Always $E = V \times Ah$ — convert to kWh by dividing by **1000**.
* **EV Range:** $R = E_{bat} / E_{cons}$ — keep Wh throughout; km/h MUST be converted to m/s for force-power problems.
* **Solar PV:** $E = \eta A G t$ — apply all derating factors (temperature, dust, wiring) before multiplying by peak-sun-hours.
* **Wind Power:** $P = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^3 C_p$ — **cubic dependency on wind speed**; Betz limit $C_p = 0.593$.
* **Charging Time:** $t = C_{Ah} / I$ — watch for partial SOC charging (e.g., 20% → 80%).
* **Tractive Force:** $F = mg(\sin\theta + \mu \cos\theta)$ — for **flat road**, $\theta = 0$ so $\sin\theta = 0$, $\cos\theta = 1$, simplifying to $F = mg\mu$.
* **Power–Velocity:** $P = F \cdot v$ — velocity in m/s, force in N, power in W.
* **C-rate:** Higher C-rate ⇒ faster charging ⇒ more heat generation.
* **Energy Efficiency:** $\eta = E_{out} / E_{in} \times 100\%$ — multiply all subsystem efficiencies in series: $\eta_{total} = \eta_1 \cdot \eta_2 \cdot \eta_3$.
* **Constants to memorize:** $g = 9.81$, $\rho_{air} = 1.225$, $G_{STC} = 1000$, $1 \; \text{kWh} = 3.6 \times 10^6 \; \text{J}$.
* **Always box** the final numerical answer with proper unit.
* **Always convert** km/h to m/s by multiplying by $5/18$.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Trick:** If your final answer seems absurdly high (e.g., an EV getting 10,000 km range), recheck your units — 80% of KTU numerical errors are unit mismatches.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electric Drive-trains - Basic Concept of Electric Traction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET742 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES AND RENEWABLE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Electric Drive-trains - Basic Concept of Electric Traction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Electric Traction:** Electric traction is the application of electrical energy as the primary means of propulsion for vehicles, wherein one or more electric traction motors convert electrical power into mechanical power (rotational torque) to drive the wheels. The complete system encompassing the power source, power electronic converter, traction motor, mechanical transmission, and control unit is collectively termed the **Electric Drive-Train** or **Electric Powertrain**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, an **Electric Drive-Train** refers to the integrated electromechanical system that delivers power from an electrical energy storage source (battery, ultracapacitor, fuel cell) or external supply (overhead catenary, third rail) to the driving wheels of a vehicle, with the specific purpose of generating controlled tractive effort for vehicle motion.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Understanding — "The Water Flow Analogy":**
> Imagine you are filling a large overhead water tank at home using a pump.
> - The **water source** (overhead tank, sump, or municipal line) is your **energy source** (battery/catenary).
> - The **pump** that pushes water up is your **power electronic converter + traction motor** — it transforms the form of energy and regulates flow.
> - The **pipes and taps** that control how much water flows and at what pressure are your **controllers and transmission system**.
> - The **water finally reaching your tap** is the **tractive effort** delivered to the wheels.
>
> Just as a good plumbing system delivers the *right amount of water at the right pressure* when you need it, an electric drive-train delivers the *right amount of torque at the right speed* exactly when the driver demands it.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard SI Units and Constants Used in Electric Traction:**
> - Force: **Newton (N)**; Tractive Effort (TE): N or kN
> - Torque (T): **Newton-meter (N·m)**
> - Power (P): **Watt (W)** or **Kilowatt (kW)** (1 kW = 1000 W = 1.341 hp)
> - Speed (V): **meters/second (m/s)** or **km/h** (1 km/h = 0.2778 m/s)
> - Rotational Speed (N): **rpm** or **rad/s** (1 rpm = 2π/60 rad/s)
> - Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
> - Wheel radius: **r (meters)**
> - Gear ratio: **G** (dimensionless)
> - Coefficient of adhesion (μ): typically **0.15 to 0.35** for steel-on-steel, **0.7 to 1.0** for rubber-on-rail

### 1.4 Scope of Electric Traction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:** This topic forms the foundational unit (Module 1) of the EV & Renewable Technologies course. Mastery of this section is critical because every subsequent module — battery technology, power electronics, motor design, and vehicle dynamics — builds upon the basic electric drive-train architecture introduced here.
>
> **Application Domains:**
> - **Road Vehicles:** Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs), Hybrid Electric Vehicles (HEVs), Plug-in HEVs (PHEVs)
> - **Rail Systems:** Electric locomotives, EMUs (Electric Multiple Units), trams, metros, light rail
> - **Industrial:** Mining haul trucks, automated guided vehicles (AGVs), forklifts
> - **Marine:** Electric ships, submarines

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Electric Traction? — The Engineering Motivation

The choice of electric drive over conventional internal combustion (IC) drive is governed by several intrinsic engineering advantages:

- **High Starting Torque:** Electric motors inherently deliver 2–3× their rated torque at start, which is critical for moving heavy loads from rest.
- **Stepless Speed Control:** Wide-range, smooth, and continuous speed variation is possible simply by adjusting voltage/frequency.
- **Regenerative Braking:** The motor acts as a generator during braking, returning kinetic energy to the source (typically 8–15% energy recovery in urban cycles).
- **No Exhaust Emissions:** At point of use, making EVs ideal for urban transit and tunnels.
- **Lower Maintenance:** Fewer moving parts, no oil changes, no clutch wear.
- **Higher Efficiency:** Electric drivetrain efficiency is **85–95%** versus IC engine at **25–35%** (tank-to-wheel).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Disadvantages for completeness (KTU examiners love asking these):**
> - High initial capital cost
> - Need for fixed infrastructure (catenary) or expensive batteries
> - Range anxiety and long charging times (for battery EVs)
> - Power supply continuity and reliability concerns in traction

### 2.2 Block-Level Architecture of an Electric Drive-Train

A complete electric drive-train consists of the following functional blocks connected in series for power flow:

1. **Energy Source** — Battery pack (Li-ion) / Fuel cell + Battery / Overhead catenary
2. **Power Electronic Converter** — DC-DC converter (step-up/step-down) and/or DC-AC inverter
3. **Traction Motor** — BLDC, PMSM, Induction Motor, SRM, or Switched Reluctance Motor
4. **Motor Controller (VCU — Vehicle Control Unit)** — Implements torque/speed commands
5. **Transmission** — Single-speed reducer, multi-speed gearbox, or direct-drive
6. **Differential and Final Drive** — Distributes power to driven wheels
7. **Wheels and Tires** — Convert rotational motion to linear motion via friction

### 2.3 The Ideal Traction Motor — Speed-Torque Characteristics

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The *ideal* speed-torque characteristic of a traction motor for road vehicles follows a **constant-power region (hyperbola: T ∝ 1/N)** beyond base speed, and a **constant-torque region** up to base speed. This is the OPPOSITE of a constant-torque industrial motor.

**Operating Regions:**

- **Region I (0 to N_base):** Constant torque — used for starting and low-speed hill climbing.
- **Region II (N_base to N_max):** Constant power — used for cruising at high speeds, flat roads.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU Board-Exam essential. Memorize the units and boundary conditions.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $F_t = \dfrac{T_m \cdot \eta_t \cdot G}{r}$ | Tractive Effort from motor torque | N |
| 2 | $F_t = M \cdot a + M \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta + M \cdot g \cdot C_r \cdot \cos\theta + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot V^2$ | Total tractive effort (vehicle dynamics) | N |
| 3 | $P = F_t \cdot V$ | Tractive power at wheels | W |
| 4 | $P_m = \dfrac{F_t \cdot V}{\eta_t}$ | Motor output power required | W |
| 5 | $V = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot N \cdot r}{60 \cdot G}$ | Vehicle linear speed from motor rpm | m/s |
| 6 | $T = \dfrac{P \cdot 60}{2\pi \cdot N}$ | Torque from power and speed | N·m |
| 7 | $T \cdot \omega = P$ | Power-torque-speed relationship (rad/s) | W |
| 8 | $F_{max} = \mu \cdot M \cdot g$ | Maximum tractive effort (adhesion limit) | N |
| 9 | $E = \int_{0}^{T} F_t \cdot V \, dt$ | Total tractive energy (work done) | J |
| 10 | $s = \int_{0}^{t} V \, dt$ | Distance travelled | m |
| 11 | $A_e = \dfrac{E}{s} = \dfrac{\int F_t \, ds}{s}$ | Specific energy consumption | J/m or Wh/km |
| 12 | $N = \dfrac{V \cdot G \cdot 60}{2\pi \cdot r}$ | Motor speed from vehicle speed | rpm |
| 13 | $\omega = \dfrac{V \cdot G}{r}$ | Motor angular speed from V | rad/s |
| 14 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{converter} \cdot \eta_{transmission}$ | Overall drivetrain efficiency | dimensionless |

**Where:**
- $F_t$ = Tractive Effort (N), $T_m$ = Motor Torque (N·m), $G$ = Gear ratio, $r$ = Wheel radius (m)
- $\eta_t$ = Transmission efficiency (typically **0.90–0.95**)
- $M$ = Total vehicle mass (kg), $a$ = acceleration (m/s²)
- $\theta$ = road gradient angle, $C_r$ = rolling resistance coefficient (~**0.012 to 0.015** for car tires)
- $\rho$ = air density (**1.225 kg/m³** at sea level), $C_d$ = drag coefficient (~**0.25–0.35** for cars)
- $A$ = frontal area (m²), $V$ = vehicle speed (m/s)
- $\mu$ = coefficient of adhesion, $N$ = motor rotational speed (rpm), $\omega$ = angular speed (rad/s)

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

The tractive-effort-vs-speed curve is the **single most important performance graph** in electric traction engineering. It determines:
- Maximum grade a vehicle can climb
- Maximum acceleration achievable
- Top speed limit
- Energy consumption per kilometer
- Battery sizing and motor rating
- Range estimation under standard drive cycles (NEDC, WLTP, FTP-75)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Vehicle Linear Speed from Motor Rotational Speed

**Given:**
- Motor rotational speed = $N$ (in rpm)
- Wheel radius = $r$ (in meters)
- Overall gear ratio = $G$ (motor revolutions per wheel revolution)

**To find:** Vehicle linear speed $V$ in m/s.

**Step 1:** Convert motor speed from rpm to revolutions per second.

$$\text{Motor revs per second} = \dfrac{N}{60}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the gear ratio. The wheel rotates slower than the motor by a factor of $G$.

$$\text{Wheel revs per second} = \dfrac{N}{60 \cdot G}$$

**Step 3:** Each wheel revolution advances the vehicle by the wheel circumference $= 2\pi r$ meters.

$$V = \text{Wheel revs/sec} \times \text{Circumference}$$

$$V = \dfrac{N}{60 \cdot G} \times 2\pi r$$

$$\boxed{V = \dfrac{2\pi r N}{60 \, G}}$$

**Step 4:** For unit conversion to km/h, multiply by **3.6**:

$$V_{km/h} = V_{m/s} \times 3.6 = \dfrac{2\pi r N \times 3.6}{60 \, G} = \dfrac{0.3768 \, r N}{G}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic of the conversion chain:** rpm → rev/sec (÷60) → wheel rev/sec (÷G) → linear m/s (×2πr). This is a four-step chain and is a frequent KTU sub-question.

### 3.2 Derivation: Tractive Effort from Motor Torque

**Given:** Motor produces torque $T_m$ (N·m) at its shaft.

**Step 1:** Torque at the wheel after the transmission:

$$T_{wheel} = T_m \times G \times \eta_t$$

**Step 2:** Force at the wheel contact patch = Torque ÷ wheel radius:

$$F_t = \dfrac{T_{wheel}}{r} = \dfrac{T_m \cdot G \cdot \eta_t}{r}$$

$$\boxed{F_t = \dfrac{T_m \cdot G \cdot \eta_t}{r}}$$

**Step 3:** Inverse — given required tractive effort, motor torque needed:

$$T_m = \dfrac{F_t \cdot r}{G \cdot \eta_t}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $\eta_t$ is in the numerator for $F_t$ but denominator for $T_m$:** Because the transmission is located *between* the motor and the wheel, mechanical losses in the transmission reduce the effective torque reaching the wheel, but the motor must *overcome* those losses by producing more torque. This distinction is a classic KTU "gotcha" question.

### 3.3 Derivation: Force Balance on a Vehicle on a Gradient

A vehicle of mass $M$ moving at speed $V$ on a road of gradient angle $\theta$ experiences the following resistive forces:

| Force Component | Symbol | Expression | Direction |
|----------------|--------|------------|-----------|
| Inertia force | $F_i$ | $M \cdot a$ | Opposes acceleration |
| Gradient force | $F_g$ | $M \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$ | Always opposes motion uphill |
| Rolling resistance | $F_r$ | $M \cdot g \cdot C_r \cdot \cos\theta$ | Opposes motion |
| Aerodynamic drag | $F_d$ | $\frac{1}{2} \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot V^2$ | Opposes motion |

**Step 1:** Sum all resistive forces:

$$F_{resistive} = M \cdot a + M \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta + M \cdot g \cdot C_r \cdot \cos\theta + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A V^2$$

**Step 2:** Apply Newton's Second Law. The tractive effort at the wheel must equal the sum of all resistive forces for steady motion, or exceed it for acceleration:

$$F_t = M \cdot a + M \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta + M \cdot g \cdot C_r \cdot \cos\theta + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A V^2$$

**Step 3:** Steady-state (constant speed, $a = 0$, level road, $\theta = 0$):

$$F_t = M \cdot g \cdot C_r + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A V^2$$

**Step 4:** At maximum gradient, no acceleration, neglect drag for slow speed:

$$F_t = M \cdot g \cdot (\sin\theta + C_r \cdot \cos\theta)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** On level roads at constant speed, the dominant resistance is **aerodynamic drag** (∝ $V^2$). On steep inclines, the dominant resistance is the **gradient force** (∝ $M g \sin\theta$). This trade-off dictates the dual operating regions of the traction motor.

### 3.4 Derivation: Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)

**Definition:** Energy consumed per unit distance travelled. It is the most direct metric for vehicle range.

**Step 1:** Instantaneous tractive power at the wheels:

$$P_t(t) = F_t(t) \cdot V(t)$$

**Step 2:** Energy consumed over a journey of total time $T$:

$$E_{total} = \int_{0}^{T} P_t(t) \, dt = \int_{0}^{T} F_t(t) \cdot V(t) \, dt$$

**Step 3:** Total distance:

$$s = \int_{0}^{T} V(t) \, dt$$

**Step 4:** Specific Energy Consumption:

$$A_e = \dfrac{E_{total}}{s} = \dfrac{\int_{0}^{T} F_t \cdot V \, dt}{\int_{0}^{T} V \, dt}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common KTU trap:** $A_e$ is **NOT** equal to average tractive effort. It is the *time-weighted average* of $F_t \cdot V$. If the student writes $A_e = F_t$ (constant), they will lose 2 marks. The correct form is the integral expression above.

### 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix — Traction Motor Comparison

| Motor Type | Power Density | Efficiency | Cost | Typical EV Application |
|-----------|---------------|------------|------|------------------------|
| **DC Motor (Series)** | Low | 75–85% | Low | Old EVs (vintage), forklifts |
| **Induction Motor (ACIM)** | Medium | 90–94% | Medium | Tesla Model S/X (early), Tata Nexon EV |
| **PMSM (Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor)** | **High** | **94–97%** | **High (rare-earth magnets)** | Tesla Model 3/Y, Hyundai Kona, MG ZS EV |
| **BLDC (Brushless DC)** | High | 92–95% | Medium | Two-wheelers, low-power EVs |
| **SRM (Switched Reluctance Motor)** | Medium | 88–92% | Low | Premium research EVs, Renault prototypes |
| **SynRM (Synchronous Reluctance Motor)** | Medium | 92–95% | Medium | Emerging — no rare earths |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Electric Drive-Train Block Diagram (EV Architecture)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete power flow and signal flow in a typical Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV) drive-train.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Battery Pack\nDC Source 400V] --> B1[DC-DC Converter\nHigh Voltage Bus]
    A1 --> C1[12V Auxiliary Battery]
    B1 --> D1[DC-AC Inverter\nPWM Control]
    C1 --> E1[VCU Vehicle Control Unit\nSignal Processing]
    E1 --> D1
    E1 --> F1[Brake Control Module]
    E1 --> G1[Temperature Sensors]
    D1 --> H1[Traction Motor\nPMSM or ACIM]
    H1 --> I1[Single-Speed Reducer\nGearbox G ratio]
    I1 --> J1[Differential Unit]
    J1 --> K1[Drive Wheels\nFriction Contact]
    H1 --> L1[Resolver Encoder\nRotor Position Feedback]
    L1 --> D1
    K1 --> M1[Mechanical Brakes\nRegenerative plus Friction]
    M1 --> F1
    F1 --> E1
```

### 4.2 Tractive Effort vs Vehicle Speed — Operating Quadrants

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Q1[Q1 Forward Motoring Drive]
        Q1A[Positive Torque\nPositive Speed] --> Q1B[Energy from Battery to Wheels]
    end
    subgraph Q2[Q2 Forward Regenerative Braking]
        Q2A[Negative Torque\nPositive Speed] --> Q2B[Energy from Wheels to Battery]
    end
    subgraph Q3[Q3 Reverse Motoring]
        Q3A[Negative Torque\nNegative Speed] --> Q3B[Reverse Driving]
    end
    subgraph Q4[Q4 Plug-In Charging]
        Q4A[Zero Speed\nExternal Charge] --> Q4B[Grid to Battery]
    end
    Q1 --> Q2
    Q2 --> Q3
    Q3 --> Q4
    Q4 --> Q1
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture: Power Flow vs Signal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S1[Power Flow Path Heavy Current]
        P1[Battery] ==> P2[Inverter]
        P2 ==> P3[Traction Motor]
        P3 ==> P4[Transmission]
        P4 ==> P5[Wheels]
    end
    subgraph S2[Signal Flow Path Low Power Control]
        C1[Accelerator Pedal] --> C2[VCU ECU]
        C3[Brake Pedal] --> C2
        C4[Steering Input] --> C2
        C2 --> C5[Inverter Gate Driver]
        C5 --> C2
        C6[Speed Sensor] --> C2
    end
    C2 -. controls .-> P2
```

### 4.4 Decision Flow for Selecting a Traction Motor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Application Power Required] --> B{Power less than 20 kW}
    B -->|Yes| C[Two Wheeler or Three Wheeler]
    B -->|No| D{Power 50 to 150 kW}
    D -->|Yes| E[Passenger Car]
    D -->|No| F[Heavy Vehicle or Bus]
    C --> G[BLDC or PMSM]
    E --> H{Performance Priority}
    H -->|High Efficiency| I[PMSM]
    H -->|Low Cost| J[ACIM]
    F --> K{Use Case}
    K -->|Long Haul| L[PMSM or SRM]
    K -->|Heavy Load Low Speed| M[ACIM or SRM]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define electric traction. List any four advantages of electric traction over IC engine traction.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):** Electric traction is the propulsion system in which electrical energy is supplied to one or more traction motors that convert it into mechanical energy to drive the vehicle. The complete system is called the electric drive-train.
>
> **Any Four Advantages (½ mark each = 2 marks):**
> 1. High starting torque — electric motors deliver 2–3 times rated torque at start.
> 2. Regenerative braking — kinetic energy is recovered back to the source.
> 3. Higher efficiency — 85–95% versus 25–35% for IC engines.
> 4. No exhaust emissions at the point of use — clean urban operation.
> 5. Stepless and smooth speed control over a wide range.
> 6. Lower maintenance due to fewer moving parts.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024] State and explain the ideal speed-torque characteristic of a traction motor.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Statement (1 mark):** The ideal traction motor should deliver **constant torque** from zero speed up to a base speed $N_b$, and **constant power** from base speed to maximum speed $N_{max}$.
>
> **Explanation (2 marks):**
> - **Region I — Constant Torque (0 to $N_b$):** Torque remains at its maximum rated value. This region is required for starting the vehicle from rest, climbing steep gradients, and providing high acceleration. Power increases linearly with speed ($P = T \cdot \omega$).
> - **Region II — Constant Power ($N_b$ to $N_{max}$):** As speed increases beyond base speed, torque reduces inversely with speed ($T \propto 1/N$) so that power remains constant. This is the natural characteristic of series DC motors and is approximated by field-weakening in AC traction motors. It enables high-speed cruising with rated power.
>
> This is the OPPOSITE of a constant-torque industrial motor, which would overheat and stall the drive electronics if used in traction.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, typically split as 7 + 7 (sub-parts a and b). You must answer ONE full question — either Question A or Question B — and sub-parts are MANDATORY (no further choice inside sub-parts).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) Derive an expression for the tractive effort required by an electric vehicle to overcome all resistive forces on a road gradient. State clearly all the terms used. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State Newton's second law for the vehicle of mass $M$ moving with acceleration $a$ on gradient angle $\theta$:

$$\text{Tractive Effort} = \text{Force to accelerate} + \text{Force to climb gradient} + \text{Force to overcome rolling resistance} + \text{Force to overcome aerodynamic drag}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Identify each term mathematically:

- Force to accelerate the mass: $F_1 = M \cdot a$ (N)
- Force to climb gradient: $F_2 = M \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$ (N) — weight component along slope
- Rolling resistance force: $F_3 = M \cdot g \cdot C_r \cdot \cos\theta$ (N) — weight component normal to slope times coefficient
- Aerodynamic drag: $F_4 = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot V^2$ (N)

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Sum all four:

$$F_t = M a + M g \sin\theta + M g C_r \cos\theta + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A V^2$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** State all symbols and units:

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|--------|---------|------|
| $M$ | Total vehicle mass | kg |
| $a$ | Linear acceleration | m/s² |
| $g$ | Acceleration due to gravity | 9.81 m/s² |
| $\theta$ | Road gradient angle | rad or deg |
| $C_r$ | Rolling resistance coefficient | dimensionless (0.012–0.015) |
| $\rho$ | Air density | 1.225 kg/m³ |
| $C_d$ | Drag coefficient | 0.25–0.35 |
| $A$ | Frontal area | m² |
| $V$ | Vehicle speed | m/s |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students commonly lose 1 mark by **forgetting the $\cos\theta$ factor on rolling resistance** (the normal force is $Mg\cos\theta$, not $Mg$). They also lose 1 mark by not specifying the value of $g$ as 9.81 m/s². Always state standard values and units in derivations.

---

**(b) An electric vehicle has the following specifications: Total mass $M$ = 1500 kg including 200 kg of battery, wheel radius $r$ = 0.30 m, gear ratio $G$ = 8, motor efficiency $\eta_m$ = 0.92, transmission efficiency $\eta_t$ = 0.95, rolling resistance $C_r$ = 0.013, drag coefficient $C_d$ = 0.30, frontal area $A$ = 2.2 m², air density $\rho$ = 1.225 kg/m³. The vehicle moves at a constant 60 km/h on a level road. Calculate: (i) the tractive effort required, (ii) the tractive power at the wheels, and (iii) the motor torque required. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Convert speed to SI units.

$$V = 60 \text{ km/h} = \dfrac{60}{3.6} = 16.667 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Calculate tractive effort on level road ($a = 0$, $\theta = 0$):

$$F_t = M g C_r + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A V^2$$

$$F_t = (1500)(9.81)(0.013) + \dfrac{1}{2}(1.225)(0.30)(2.2)(16.667)^2$$

$$F_t = 191.30 + 89.83 = 281.13 \text{ N}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Breakdown: Rolling = $1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.013 = 191.30$ N. Aerodynamic = $0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times 277.78 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times 277.78 = 89.83$ N.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Calculate tractive power at the wheels:

$$P_t = F_t \cdot V = 281.13 \times 16.667 = 4685.5 \text{ W} \approx 4.69 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Motor output power required (account for transmission loss):

$$P_m = \dfrac{P_t}{\eta_t} = \dfrac{4685.5}{0.95} = 4932.1 \text{ W}$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Calculate motor speed and torque.

Motor speed:

$$N = \dfrac{V \cdot G \cdot 60}{2\pi r} = \dfrac{16.667 \times 8 \times 60}{2\pi \times 0.30} = \dfrac{8000.16}{1.885} = 4244 \text{ rpm}$$

Motor torque:

$$T_m = \dfrac{P_m \cdot 60}{2\pi N} = \dfrac{4932.1 \times 60}{2\pi \times 4244} = \dfrac{295926}{26660} = 11.10 \text{ N·m}$$

**Final Answer Box:**

$$\boxed{F_t = 281.13 \text{ N}, \quad P_t = 4.69 \text{ kW}, \quad T_m = 11.10 \text{ N·m at } 4244 \text{ rpm}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students forget that **$\eta_t$ is in the denominator** when computing motor power from wheel power, and they lose 1 mark. Also, do not use the wrong value of $\eta$ — converter efficiency is often around 0.95 and motor around 0.92–0.95; the examiner may give separate values for motor and transmission, both of which must be applied.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) Explain with a neat block diagram the major components of an electric vehicle drive-train. Describe the function of each block. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Draw the block diagram (refer Section 4.1 above) and identify the following six major components:

1. **Energy Storage System (Battery Pack)** — Stores DC electrical energy. Typical voltage 400V or 800V in modern EVs. Function: Source of energy for propulsion and auxiliaries.
2. **Power Electronic Converter (Inverter + DC-DC Converter)** — Converts DC from battery to variable-frequency AC (for AC motor) or variable DC (for DC motor). Function: Regulates voltage/current/frequency supplied to motor based on driver input.
3. **Traction Motor** — Converts electrical energy to mechanical rotational energy. Function: Provides torque to the drivetrain.
4. **Transmission / Gearbox** — Reduces motor speed and increases torque via gear ratio. Function: Matches motor speed-torque curve to wheel requirements.
5. **Differential and Final Drive** — Distributes torque to the two drive wheels and allows different wheel speeds in curves. Function: Enables smooth cornering.
6. **Vehicle Control Unit (VCU)** — Brain of the EV. Function: Receives driver inputs (throttle, brake), sensor data, and controls the converter, motor, brakes, and auxiliaries.

**Step 2 (3 marks):** Describe the **power flow** during motoring and regenerative braking:

- **Motoring:** Battery → Inverter → Motor → Transmission → Wheels
- **Regenerative Braking:** Wheels → Transmission → Motor (now generator) → Inverter (rectifier) → Battery

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Describe the **control signal flow**:

- Accelerator pedal position sensor → VCU → Torque demand calculation → Current/voltage command to inverter → PWM gate signals to motor
- Brake pedal, steering, gear selector, temperature sensors all feed VCU
- VCU also handles Battery Management System (BMS) communication, thermal management, and onboard charger

---

**(b) An electric locomotive of mass 80 tonnes (80,000 kg) is moving up a gradient of 1 in 100 at a constant speed of 60 km/h. The tractive resistance is 50 N per tonne and the rotational inertia is 10% of the translational mass. Calculate: (i) the gradient force, (ii) the total tractive effort, and (iii) the power output at the rail. Assume no wind resistance. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify and convert given data.

- Mass: $M = 80{,}000$ kg
- Gradient: 1 in 100 means $\sin\theta = 1/100 = 0.01$ → $\theta = \arctan(0.01) \approx 0.5729°$
- Speed: $V = 60 \text{ km/h} = 16.667$ m/s
- Tractive resistance: $R = 50$ N/tonne = $50 \times 80 = 4000$ N
- Effective mass: $M_{eff} = 1.10 \times M = 88{,}000$ kg (with rotational inertia)
- Constant speed → $a = 0$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Calculate gradient force.

$$F_g = M \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta = 80{,}000 \times 9.81 \times 0.01 = 7848 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Calculate total tractive effort (only gradient + tractive resistance; no acceleration, no wind):

$$F_t = F_g + R = 7848 + 4000 = 11{,}848 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Calculate power output at the rail.

$$P_t = F_t \cdot V = 11{,}848 \times 16.667 = 197{,}483 \text{ W} \approx 197.5 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{F_g = 7848 \text{ N}, \quad F_t = 11{,}848 \text{ N}, \quad P_t \approx 197.5 \text{ kW}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students commonly forget to **convert "1 in 100" gradient into $\sin\theta$** correctly. The convention is: if 1 in n, then $\sin\theta = 1/n$ only when the gradient is small (which is the case here, 1°). For steeper gradients, the correct form is $\sin\theta = \text{rise}/\text{hypotenuse} = 1/\sqrt{1+n^2}$. Also, students forget the **10% rotational inertia addition**, costing 1 mark. Be precise in interpretation.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Consolidated)

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Common Mistakes in Electric Traction Problems:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors** — km/h to m/s, rpm to rad/s, tonnes to kg. Always show the conversion explicitly.
> 2. **Gear ratio placement** — $\eta_t \cdot G$ in the numerator of $F_t$ but $G \cdot \eta_t$ in the denominator of $T_m$. They are NOT the same ratio arrangement.
> 3. **Rolling resistance requires $\cos\theta$** — students often write $MgC_r$ instead of $MgC_r\cos\theta$ on slopes.
> 4. **Confusion between motor output power and tractive power** — always account for transmission efficiency.
> 5. **Misinterpreting "1 in n" gradient** — use $\sin\theta = 1/n$ only for small angles; show the trigonometric step.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 1 — Electric Drive-trains):**
> - Electric traction = use of electric motors as the prime mover for vehicle propulsion.
> - Electric drive-train = battery + power converter + traction motor + transmission + control.
> - Advantages: high starting torque, regenerative braking, high efficiency, zero local emissions, low maintenance.
> - Disadvantages: high initial cost, infrastructure dependency, range anxiety (BEV).
> - Ideal traction motor characteristic: **constant torque region** (0 to $N_b$) and **constant power region** ($N_b$ to $N_{max}$).
> - Tractive effort equation: $F_t = Ma + Mg\sin\theta + MgC_r\cos\theta + \frac{1}{2}\rho C_d A V^2$.
> - Tractive power: $P_t = F_t \cdot V$.
> - Vehicle speed from motor rpm: $V = \dfrac{2\pi r N}{60 G}$.
> - Tractive effort from motor torque: $F_t = \dfrac{T_m \cdot G \cdot \eta_t}{r}$.
> - Adhesion limit: $F_{max} = \mu M g$ — tractive effort cannot exceed this or wheels slip.
> - Specific energy consumption: $A_e = \dfrac{\int F_t \cdot V \, dt}{\int V \, dt}$ — not a simple average.
> - Common EV traction motors: PMSM (most efficient), ACIM (cheaper), BLDC (two-wheelers).
> - Quadrants of operation: Q1 motoring, Q2 regen braking, Q3 reverse, Q4 charging.
> - Standard values to memorize: $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $C_r \approx 0.013$ (car tire), $C_d \approx 0.30$.
> - Energy source voltages: typically **400V** (standard) or **800V** (premium, e.g., Porsche Taycan, Hyundai E-GMP).
> - Always state assumptions: level road, no wind, constant speed, etc., when solving numericals.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electric Drive-train Topologies (Basics Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET742 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES AND RENEWABLE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Electric Drive-train Topologies (Basics Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Electric Drive-train Topology** (also called a powertrain architecture) refers to the systematic arrangement and interconnection of the **energy source, power electronic converter, electric traction motor(s), transmission system, and final drive** that together deliver tractive power from the stored electrical energy to the driving wheels of an electric vehicle (EV) or hybrid electric vehicle (HEV). 

In KTU 2024 scheme parlance, the term *topology* does not merely refer to the components themselves, but to the **power-flow path** and the **degree of mechanical/electrical coupling** between them. The selection of a topology directly influences the vehicle's efficiency, performance, packaging, cost, and complexity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Students are expected to identify, classify, and compare the major EV drive-train topologies. The "Basics Only" tag means focus is placed on **identification, block-diagram representation, and qualitative comparison** — not on the deep electromagnetic design of the motors themselves.

### 1.2 The Three Fundamental Topology Families

For the *basics* level (Module 1), the drive-trains are classified into three primary families:

1. **Pure Electric Drive-trains (Battery EV – BEV)** — Single-motor or multi-motor configurations powered exclusively by a battery.
2. **Hybrid Electric Drive-trains (HEV)** — Combine an Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) with one or more electric machines. These are further subdivided into **Series, Parallel, and Series-Parallel (Power-split)**.
3. **Fuel-Cell Electric Drive-trains (FCEV)** — Use a hydrogen fuel cell stack as the primary energy source, with a battery as a buffer.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water-Pipeline" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine the EV drive-train as a **water pipeline system** delivering water (energy) from a tank (battery) to a water wheel (driving wheels).
> - The **battery** is the overhead water tank.
> - The **power electronic converter (inverter)** is the **valve + pump** that regulates the flow of water.
> - The **electric motor** is the **turbine** that converts fluid energy into rotational mechanical energy.
> - The **transmission & final drive** are the **gear-train and pulleys** that adjust the speed/torque of the rotating shaft.
> - The **wheels** are the **water wheels** that finally push the vehicle forward.
> 
> Just as a plumber can route water through different paths (single pipe, branched pipes, multiple pumps), an EV designer can choose a different **topology** to route the power from source to wheels. Each topology trades off **efficiency, complexity, cost, and packaging**.

### 1.4 Standard Reference Metrics (KTU Board Emphasis)

The following metrics are **mandatory** to be quoted by students when comparing topologies in KTU 2024 answer scripts:

- **Specific Energy:** typically **150–250 Wh/kg** for modern Li-ion batteries.
- **Energy Efficiency:** regenerative braking can push total drivetrain efficiency to **85–95 %** in BEVs.
- **Peak Power Density:** Traction motors of **5–10 kW/kg** are state-of-the-art.
- **System Voltage:** Modern EV buses operate at **400 V** or **800 V** architectures.

### 1.5 Generic Block Diagram of an EV Powertrain

A topology-independent representation of an EV drive-train can be written as:

$$\text{Energy Source} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Power Converter} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Electric Motor} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Transmission} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Final Drive} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Wheels}$$

The fundamental difference between any two topologies lies in **how many of these blocks are present**, and **how they are mechanically coupled** to one another.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Functional Sub-Systems of an EV Drive-train

Every EV/HEV drive-train — irrespective of topology — is composed of the following five functional blocks. The **topology** decides how they are linked.

1. **Energy Storage Subsystem (ESS)** — Battery pack (Li-ion, LFP, NMC), supercapacitor module, or fuel-cell stack.
2. **Power Electronic Converter (PEC)** — DC-DC converter (for HV-LV), DC-AC inverter (for AC motors), and on-board charger.
3. **Electric Traction Motor(s)** — PMSM (Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor), IM (Induction Motor), BLDC, or SRM (Switched Reluctance Motor).
4. **Transmission** — Single-speed reducer, multi-speed gearbox, or planetary gear-set.
5. **Final Drive & Wheels** — Differential, half-shafts, tyres.

### 2.2 Why "Why" and "How" — The Design Logic Behind Topologies

> [!NOTE]
> **Why are there multiple topologies?** Because a single design cannot simultaneously optimize for **cost, performance, packaging, efficiency, and range**. Topology choice is a *constraint-satisfaction problem* balancing the conflicting demands of urban commuting, highway cruising, and off-road traction.

The **How** behind each topology boils down to answering four questions:

- **Where is the energy converted?** (In the motor only, or also in an ICE?)
- **How many motors drive the wheels?** (1, 2, or 4 — affecting torque-vectoring and redundancy)
- **Is the transmission fixed-ratio or variable?** (Single-speed vs multi-speed vs planetary)
- **Is mechanical coupling rigid or clutched?** (Defines the HEV sub-classification)

### 2.3 Classification Logic Flow

```
BEV / Pure Electric
├── Single-motor drive
│   ├── Single-speed transmission (most common: Tesla Model 3 rear, Nissan Leaf)
│   └── Multi-speed transmission (Porsche Taycan 2-speed rear)
└── Multi-motor drive
    ├── Two-motor (front + rear) — Tesla Model S Dual Motor
    └── Four in-wheel (hub) motors — Protean Electric, Lordstown
```

```
HEV / Hybrid Electric
├── Series Hybrid — Range Rover P400e, Chevrolet Volt (1st gen)
├── Parallel Hybrid — Honda Insight (1st gen), Hyundai Ioniq Hybrid
└── Series-Parallel (Power-split) — Toyota Prius (THS), Ford Escape Hybrid
```

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the high-yield formula sheet for Module 1 — Electric Drive-train Topologies.** Memorize the unit dimensions and the boundary conditions; the KTU board often awards a full 2-mark bonus for correctly stating the unit of a derived quantity.

| # | Quantity / Relationship | Formula / Expression | SI Units | Boundary Condition / Typical EV Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wheel angular speed from motor speed | $\omega_{w} = \omega_{m} \cdot N_{g} \cdot N_{f}$ | rad/s | $N_{g} = 6$–$10$ (single-speed reduction) |
| 2 | Wheel torque from motor torque | $T_{w} = T_{m} \cdot N_{g} \cdot N_{f} \cdot \eta_{t}$ | N·m | $\eta_{t} \approx 0.95$–$0.98$ |
| 3 | Tractive force at wheels | $F_{tr} = \dfrac{T_{w}}{r_{w}}$ | N | $r_{w} = 0.3$–$0.35$ m for cars |
| 4 | Resistive forces on vehicle | $F_{res} = F_{roll} + F_{aero} + F_{grade}$ | N | — |
| 5 | Rolling resistance | $F_{roll} = m g f_{r} \cos\theta$ | N | $f_{r} = 0.008$–$0.015$ for car tyres |
| 6 | Aerodynamic drag | $F_{aero} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_{d} A v^{2}$ | N | $C_{d} A \approx 0.6$–$0.8$ m² |
| 7 | Grade resistance | $F_{grade} = m g \sin\theta$ | N | $\theta = 0$ on flat road |
| 8 | Battery to wheel efficiency | $\eta_{sys} = \eta_{bat} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{m} \cdot \eta_{t}$ | dimensionless | $\eta_{sys} \approx 0.80$–$0.90$ |
| 9 | Power balance at wheels | $P_{wheel} = F_{tr} \cdot v = \eta_{sys} \cdot P_{bat}$ | W | — |
| 10 | Vehicle kinetic energy | $E_{k} = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$ | J | $m = m_{vehicle} + m_{payload}$ |
| 11 | Energy in battery | $E_{bat} = V_{bat} \cdot Q_{bat}$ | Wh | Li-ion $\approx 250$ Wh/kg |
| 12 | Motor rated power (rule-of-thumb) | $P_{m} = \dfrac{m \cdot v_{max} \cdot (F_{roll}+F_{aero})}{1000 \cdot \eta_{sys}}$ | kW | Mid-size EV $\approx 100$–$150$ kW |

> Where the notations are: $N_g$ = gear ratio, $N_f$ = final drive ratio, $r_w$ = wheel radius, $m$ = vehicle mass, $g$ = **9.81 m/s²**, $\rho$ = air density $\approx$ **1.225 kg/m³**, $C_d$ = drag coefficient, $A$ = frontal area, $v$ = vehicle speed.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The choice of topology is a **first-order design decision** in any EV programme. For example:
- **Tesla Model 3** uses a *single-motor single-speed rear* drive to optimize cost.
- **Tesla Model S Plaid** uses a *tri-motor* (1 front + 2 rear) layout for performance.
- **Toyota Prius** uses a *power-split (Series-Parallel) planetary* topology to optimize fuel economy without an external plug.
- **Formula-E Gen3** cars use a *rear-mid motor single-speed* topology to maximize efficiency within a tight regulatory box.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip (KTU 2024):** When asked to "compare" two topologies, always present the answer in a **comparison table** with rows for *components, coupling, efficiency, complexity, cost, and application example*. Boards award full marks for structured tabular comparison.

### 2.6 Qualitative Trade-Off Matrix

| Topology | Components | Mechanical Coupling | Efficiency | Complexity | Cost | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| BEV — Single-motor, single-speed | Battery, Inverter, 1 Motor, Reducer | Rigid (direct gear) | High (~90%) | Low | Low | Nissan Leaf, TATA Nexon EV |
| BEV — Multi-motor | Battery, Inverter(s), 2–4 Motors | Independent per axle/wheel | High (with regen) | Medium | Medium-High | Tesla Model S, Rivian R1T |
| Series HEV | ICE-Generator, Battery, Inverter, Motor, No mechanical link to wheels | Decoupled (electrical) | Medium | High | High | Chevrolet Volt, BMW i3 REx |
| Parallel HEV | ICE, Motor, Clutch, Transmission | Rigid (mechanical) | Medium-High | Medium | Medium | Honda Insight, Hyundai Ioniq |
| Series-Parallel (Power-split) HEV | ICE, Motor, Generator, Planetary gear | Hybrid (mech + elec) | High | Very High | High | Toyota Prius, Ford Escape |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Power Flow Through a Generic BEV Drive-train

Let us derive the **battery-to-wheel power relation** in a single-motor single-speed BEV drive-train. This derivation is fundamental and appears in nearly every KTU Module-1 numerical.

**Given:**
- Motor output torque: $T_m$ (in N·m)
- Motor rotational speed: $\omega_m$ (in rad/s)
- Single gear reduction ratio: $N_g$ (dimensionless)
- Final drive ratio: $N_f$ (dimensionless)
- Transmission efficiency: $\eta_t$
- Wheel radius: $r_w$
- Vehicle speed: $v$ (in m/s)

**Step 1 — Mechanical power at the motor shaft.**

The mechanical power generated by the motor is the product of its torque and angular speed.

$$
P_{m} = T_{m} \cdot \omega_{m}
$$

**Step 2 — Torque multiplication through the gear-train.**

The transmission multiplies the torque by the product of the gear and final-drive ratios, while dissipating a fraction $1-\eta_t$ as heat.

$$
T_{w} = T_{m} \cdot N_{g} \cdot N_{f} \cdot \eta_{t}
$$

Here, $\eta_t$ multiplies the torque because mechanical losses are subtracted from the output side. The motor speed is divided by the same ratio (energy conservation), so the output power becomes:

$$
P_{w} = T_{w} \cdot \omega_{w} = (T_{m} \cdot N_{g} \cdot N_{f} \cdot \eta_{t}) \cdot \left(\dfrac{\omega_{m}}{N_{g} \cdot N_{f}}\right) = T_{m} \cdot \omega_{m} \cdot \eta_{t} = P_{m} \cdot \eta_{t}
$$

This confirms the **logical check**: only an efficiency factor remains between motor power and wheel power.

**Step 3 — Tractive force at the tyre contact patch.**

The wheel torque, when applied at the wheel-ground contact patch of radius $r_w$, generates a linear tractive force.

$$
F_{tr} = \dfrac{T_{w}}{r_{w}} = \dfrac{T_{m} \cdot N_{g} \cdot N_{f} \cdot \eta_{t}}{r_{w}}
$$

**Step 4 — Linear speed at the wheel.**

Since the wheel rolls without slipping, its linear speed at the contact patch equals the vehicle speed.

$$
v = \omega_{w} \cdot r_{w} = \dfrac{\omega_{m} \cdot r_{w}}{N_{g} \cdot N_{f}}
$$

**Step 5 — Wheel-level power (in terms of vehicle kinematics).**

The mechanical power delivered at the wheels can also be expressed as force times velocity.

$$
P_{w} = F_{tr} \cdot v = \left(\dfrac{T_{m} \cdot N_{g} \cdot N_{f} \cdot \eta_{t}}{r_{w}}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{\omega_{m} \cdot r_{w}}{N_{g} \cdot N_{f}}\right) = T_{m} \cdot \omega_{m} \cdot \eta_{t}
$$

This identity confirms that the two expressions are mathematically equivalent — the kinematic and dynamic views yield the same power.

**Step 6 — Battery-side power (full system efficiency).**

Now extend the chain backwards through the inverter and battery.

$$
P_{bat} = \dfrac{P_{w}}{\eta_{sys}} = \dfrac{P_{w}}{\eta_{bat} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{m} \cdot \eta_{t}}
$$

where $\eta_{bat}$ is battery coulombic + voltage efficiency, $\eta_{inv}$ is inverter efficiency (typically 0.95–0.98), and $\eta_{m}$ is motor efficiency.

**Final compact expression for tractive power at the wheels:**

$$
\boxed{\,P_{wheel} \;=\; \eta_{sys} \cdot P_{bat} \;=\; F_{tr} \cdot v \;=\; \dfrac{T_{m} \cdot \omega_{m} \cdot \eta_{sys}}{1}\,}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Vehicle Power Demand on a Level Road

For a vehicle of mass $m$ cruising at constant speed $v$ on a flat road ($\theta = 0$), the only resistive forces are rolling resistance and aerodynamic drag.

**Step 1 — Write the resistive force equation.**

$$
F_{res} = F_{roll} + F_{aero} = m g f_{r} + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_{d} A v^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — At constant speed, the tractive force equals the resistive force.**

$$
F_{tr} = F_{res} = m g f_{r} + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_{d} A v^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by velocity to get the power demand at the wheels.**

$$
P_{wheel} = F_{tr} \cdot v = m g f_{r} v + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_{d} A v^{3}
$$

This is the famous **cubic-in-velocity aerodynamic term** that dominates EV highway energy consumption.

**Step 4 — Battery-side power demand.**

$$
P_{bat} = \dfrac{m g f_{r} v + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_{d} A v^{3}}{\eta_{sys}}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Why Multi-Speed Transmission Improves Efficiency

In a single-speed EV, the motor is forced to operate at one fixed gear ratio, so it has only one speed-torque operating point for any given vehicle speed. In a multi-speed transmission, the motor can be re-mapped to operate closer to its **peak-efficiency island** in the torque-speed map.

Let $\eta_1$ be efficiency at single-speed point and $\eta_2$ be efficiency at the optimal multi-speed point. The energy saved over a trip of distance $d$ at speed $v$ is:

$$
\Delta E = (P_{wheel,1}/\eta_1 - P_{wheel,2}/\eta_2) \cdot \dfrac{d}{v}
$$

**Numerical intuition:** A 2-speed transmission can improve WLTP range by **2–5 %** for a typical passenger EV — a meaningful gain that justifies the added mechanical complexity in premium vehicles like the **Porsche Taycan**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Topology Selection Criteria

| Criterion | BEV Single-Motor | BEV Multi-Motor | Series HEV | Parallel HEV | Series-Parallel HEV |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mechanical complexity | Low | Medium | Low | Medium | High |
| Number of energy converters | 1 (motor) | 2–4 (motors) | 2 (ICE-gen + motor) | 1.5 (ICE + motor) | 2 (ICE + motor + gen) |
| All-electric range (AER) | 100 % | 100 % | 30–80 km | 0–5 km | 0–10 km |
| Overall drivetrain efficiency | High | Very High | Medium | Medium-High | High |
| Cost | Lowest | High | High | Medium | Highest |
| Packaging | Easiest | Compact (no transmission) | Easy (no mech link) | Difficult (clutch+gear) | Very Difficult |
| Torque vectoring | No | Yes (per-wheel) | No | No | Limited |
| Application segment | Urban commuter | Performance SUV | Range-extender | City hybrid | Premium hybrid |
| Representative vehicle | Nissan Leaf | Tesla Model S Plaid | BMW i3 REx | Honda Insight | Toyota Prius |

### 3.5 Logical Checklist Before Solving a Topology Problem

- [ ] Identify the **number of energy sources** (battery only, battery + ICE, fuel cell + battery).
- [ ] Identify the **number and type of motors** (PMSM, IM, SRM).
- [ ] Identify the **mechanical coupling** (direct gear, clutch, planetary, decoupled).
- [ ] Identify the **transmission type** (single-speed, multi-speed, CVT, planetary).
- [ ] State the **system efficiency** with reasonable values.
- [ ] State the **boundary condition** (e.g., constant speed, max grade, max acceleration).
- [ ] Always end with a **physical interpretation** sentence.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic BEV Drive-train (Single Motor, Single Speed)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Battery Pack HV] --> B1[DC DC Converter]
    A1 --> C1[Inverter DC AC]
    B1 --> D1[Low Voltage Bus 12V]
    C1 --> E1[PMSM Traction Motor]
    E1 --> F1[Single Speed Reducer]
    F1 --> G1[Differential]
    G1 --> H1[Drive Wheels]
    H1 -. Regenerative Braking .-> E1
    E1 -. Back EMF .-> C1
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Series Hybrid EV (Mechanical Decoupling)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[ICE Engine] --> S2[Generator]
    S2 --> S3[Rectifier]
    S4[Battery Pack] --> S5[Inverter]
    S3 --> S5
    S5 --> S6[Traction Motor]
    S6 --> S7[Final Drive]
    S7 --> S8[Wheels]
    S1 -. No mechanical link to wheels .-> S8
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Parallel Hybrid EV (Mechanical Coupling)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[ICE Engine] --> P2[Clutch]
    P3[Electric Motor] --> P2
    P2 --> P4[Multi Speed Transmission]
    P5[Battery] --> P6[Inverter]
    P6 --> P3
    P4 --> P7[Final Drive]
    P7 --> P8[Wheels]
    P3 -. Regenerative Braking .-> P5
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Series-Parallel (Power-Split) Hybrid — Toyota THS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1[ICE Engine] --> Q2[Planetary Gear Set]
    Q3[Generator Motor MG1] --> Q2
    Q2 --> Q4[Traction Motor MG2]
    Q4 --> Q5[Final Drive]
    Q5 --> Q6[Wheels]
    Q2 -. Power split .-> Q3
    Q7[Battery] --> Q8[Inverter]
    Q8 --> Q3
    Q8 --> Q4
    Q4 -. Regenerative Braking .-> Q7
```

### 4.5 Block Diagram — Four In-Wheel Motor Drive (Multi-Motor BEV)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R1[High Voltage Battery] --> R2[Power Distribution Unit]
    R2 --> R3[Front Left Inverter]
    R2 --> R4[Front Right Inverter]
    R2 --> R5[Rear Left Inverter]
    R2 --> R6[Rear Right Inverter]
    R3 --> R7[FL Hub Motor]
    R4 --> R8[FR Hub Motor]
    R5 --> R9[RL Hub Motor]
    R6 --> R10[RR Hub Motor]
    R7 -. Torque Vectoring .-> R8
    R9 -. Torque Vectoring .-> R10
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Diagram-Viva Pointers:** When asked to draw a topology diagram in the exam, always mark the **direction of power flow with arrows**, and clearly **label the regenerative braking path** with a dashed line. This is a common 2-mark scoring point in KTU 2024 scheme board exams.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**  
Define the term *Electric Drive-train Topology*. List the **four major sub-systems** of any EV powertrain in the order of power flow from source to wheels.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An Electric Drive-train Topology refers to the **arrangement and interconnection** of components that deliver tractive power from the energy source to the driving wheels of an electric vehicle. It defines the **power-flow path** and the **mechanical/electrical coupling** between components.

The four major sub-systems in the order of power flow are:

1. **Energy Storage System** (Battery pack) — supplies DC electrical energy.
2. **Power Electronic Converter** (DC-DC + Inverter) — regulates voltage and converts DC to AC.
3. **Electric Traction Motor** — converts electrical energy to mechanical rotation.
4. **Transmission & Final Drive** — adapts torque and speed, and delivers power to the wheels.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for listing, 1 mark for the correct order/power-flow direction.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**  
Differentiate between a **Series Hybrid** and a **Parallel Hybrid** drive-train. State **one real-world vehicle** example for each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Series Hybrid | Parallel Hybrid |
|---|---|---|
| Coupling between ICE and wheels | **None** (decoupled) — ICE drives only a generator | **Direct mechanical** link via clutch/gears |
| Energy path | All mechanical energy → electricity → motor → wheels | ICE and motor **both** drive wheels mechanically |
| Efficiency at highway | Lower (double energy conversion) | Higher (single conversion) |
| Efficiency in city | Higher (ICE runs at optimal point) | Lower (clutch slip, frequent starts) |
| Example | **Chevrolet Volt (Gen 1)**, BMW i3 REx | **Honda Insight (Gen 1)**, Hyundai Ioniq Hybrid |

*(1 mark per correct differentiation row, 1 mark for correct example. Full 3 marks for crisp tabular answer.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Module 1 Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a Series-Parallel (Power-Split) hybrid electric drive-train** used in the Toyota Prius. Clearly show the role of the **planetary gear set**, MG1 (generator), and MG2 (traction motor) in the power flow. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A pure electric passenger car has the following specifications:
