Vehicle mass $m = 1500$ kg, rolling resistance coefficient $f_r = 0.012$, drag coefficient $C_d = 0.30$, frontal area $A = 2.2$ m², wheel radius $r_w = 0.32$ m, gear ratio $N_g = 8.5$, final drive ratio $N_f = 1.0$, motor efficiency $\eta_m = 0.93$, inverter efficiency $\eta_{inv} = 0.96$, transmission efficiency $\eta_t = 0.97$. Air density $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³. The car cruises at a constant speed of $90$ km/h on a level road.  
**Compute:** (i) the tractive force required, (ii) the tractive power at the wheels, and (iii) the battery power required. (7 Marks)

### Model Solution for Question A

#### (a) Series-Parallel (Power-Split) Drive-train — Block Diagram & Explanation (7 Marks)

The **power-split** topology uses a **planetary gear set (PGS)** as the power-dividing element. The three ports of the PGS are:

- **Sun gear** — connected to **MG1 (generator motor)**.
- **Planet carrier** — connected to the **ICE engine**.
- **Ring gear** — connected to the **MG2 (traction motor)** and the **final drive** that turns the wheels.

**Power-flow modes (1.5 marks for naming them):**

1. **Pure EV mode (low speed, light load):** ICE off. MG2 alone drives the wheels. MG1 is stationary. Power flow: **Battery → Inverter → MG2 → Wheels**.
2. **Pure ICE mode (highway cruise):** ICE drives the planet carrier. MG1 spins the sun gear to keep the ring gear at the correct wheel speed. MG2 may assist or stay neutral. Power flow: **ICE → Carrier → Ring → Wheels**, with **ICE → MG1 → Battery** for charging.
3. **Power-split mode (acceleration / hill climb):** Both ICE and battery deliver power. ICE splits its power — part goes mechanically through the PGS to the wheels, part goes through MG1 as electricity to MG2 to add to wheel torque. Power flow: **ICE → (mech) Ring + (elec via MG1) → MG2 → Wheels**, and **Battery → MG2** simultaneously.
4. **Regenerative braking mode:** MG2 acts as a generator, sending power back to the battery through the inverter.

**Key advantage:** The PGS allows **continuous variable transmission (CVT-like) behaviour** without a stepped gearbox, with the ICE always running near its **Best Operating Line (BOL)**. This is why the Toyota Prius achieves ~50 % thermal efficiency.

*(Block diagram: 2 marks; Mode explanation: 3 marks; Real-world example: 1 mark; Power flow arrows: 1 mark.)*

#### (b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given data:**
- $m = 1500$ kg
- $f_r = 0.012$, $C_d = 0.30$, $A = 2.2$ m², $r_w = 0.32$ m
- $N_g = 8.5$, $N_f = 1.0$
- $\eta_m = 0.93$, $\eta_{inv} = 0.96$, $\eta_t = 0.97$
- $v = 90$ km/h $= 25$ m/s
- $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\theta = 0$

**Step 1 — Rolling resistance force (1 mark).**

$$
F_{roll} = m g f_r = 1500 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.012 = 176.58 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2 — Aerodynamic drag force (1 mark).**

$$
F_{aero} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^{2} = 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.30 \cdot 2.2 \cdot (25)^{2}
$$

Intermediate calculation:

$$
(25)^{2} = 625
$$

$$
F_{aero} = 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.30 \cdot 2.2 \cdot 625
$$

$$
F_{aero} = 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.66 \cdot 625
$$

$$
F_{aero} = 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 412.5
$$

$$
F_{aero} = 252.66 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 — Tractive force required (i) (1 mark).**

Since the road is level and speed is constant, $F_{grade} = 0$ and $F_{accel} = 0$.

$$
F_{tr} = F_{roll} + F_{aero} = 176.58 + 252.66 = 429.24 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4 — Tractive power at the wheels (ii) (1 mark).**

$$
P_{wheel} = F_{tr} \cdot v = 429.24 \cdot 25 = 10731 \text{ W} \approx 10.73 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 5 — System efficiency (1 mark).**

$$
\eta_{sys} = \eta_{m} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{t} = 0.93 \cdot 0.96 \cdot 0.97
$$

Intermediate: $0.93 \cdot 0.96 = 0.8928$; $0.8928 \cdot 0.97 = 0.866$

$$
\eta_{sys} = 0.866 \;(\text{or } 86.6\%)
$$

**Step 6 — Battery power required (iii) (1 mark).**

$$
P_{bat} = \dfrac{P_{wheel}}{\eta_{sys}} = \dfrac{10731}{0.866} \approx 12391 \text{ W} \approx 12.39 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 7 — Final interpretation (1 mark).**

To cruise at 90 km/h on a level road, the EV draws only **~12.4 kW** from the battery, which is well within the 100–150 kW capability of a typical EV traction motor. This explains why EVs are highly efficient at steady-state highway cruising, and where the bulk of the battery energy is spent (overcoming aerodynamic drag, which scales as $v^3$).

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice — [KTU University Exam — Module 1 Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Compare the **Single-motor Single-speed** and **Two-motor (front + rear)** BEV drive-train topologies with respect to: number of components, packaging, efficiency, torque-vectoring capability, cost, and suitable application. Use a **comparison table** in your answer. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Explain the **functional role of the planetary gear set** in a Series-Parallel (Power-split) hybrid drive-train. Why is it called a *power-split* device? How does it allow the ICE to operate near its best efficiency line? (7 Marks)

### Model Solution for Question B

#### (a) Comparison of Single-motor vs Two-motor BEV Topologies (7 Marks)

| Parameter | Single-Motor Single-Speed BEV | Two-Motor (Front + Rear) BEV |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of traction motors** | 1 | 2 (one per axle) |
| **Transmission** | Single-speed reducer (1 per motor) | Single-speed reducer per motor (2 total) |
| **Inverters** | 1 | 2 (one per motor) |
| **Differential** | Mechanical differential required | Not required (electronic differential via torque vectoring) |
| **Packaging** | Simple, but differential takes space | Compact (no prop-shaft or differential) |
| **Drivetrain efficiency** | ~85–90 % | ~86–91 % (slightly higher due to regen on both axles) |
| **Torque vectoring** | Not possible (only brake-based) | Full electronic torque vectoring |
| **All-wheel drive** | No (FWD or RWD only) | Yes (AWD inherent) |
| **Redundancy / fail-safe** | Motor failure = vehicle stranded | One motor failure = limp-home mode possible |
| **Cost** | Low | 30–60 % higher |
| **Weight** | Lighter | Heavier (extra motor + inverter) |
| **Application** | Urban commuter (Nissan Leaf, TATA Nexon EV) | Performance / AWD SUV (Tesla Model S, Audi e-tron, Rivian) |

*(1 mark per correct, well-explained parameter; 1 mark for a real-world example.)*

#### (b) Functional Role of the Planetary Gear Set in a Power-Split HEV (7 Marks)

**Mechanical description (2 marks):** A planetary gear set (PGS) consists of three rotating members — a **sun gear**, a set of **planet gears** mounted on a **planet carrier**, and an outer **ring gear** with internal teeth. Any one member can be the input, another the output, and the third can be held stationary or driven at a variable speed. This gives **two degrees of rotational freedom** in steady state, which is the key to its versatility in a hybrid drivetrain.

**Why it is called a "power-split" device (2 marks):** The ICE is connected to the planet carrier. The ICE's mechanical power is **split into two paths** at the PGS:
- **Mechanical path:** Carrier → Ring gear → MG2 → Wheels.
- **Electrical path:** Carrier → Sun gear → MG1 (generator) → Inverter → MG2 (motor) → Wheels.

The relative power split between the two paths is **continuously variable** by controlling the speed of MG1 (the sun gear). This is why the system is called a *power-split* hybrid, as opposed to a *series* or *parallel* hybrid where the split is discrete.

**Why ICE operates at its best efficiency line (3 marks):** The ICE's best thermal efficiency is achieved at a narrow range of **torque-speed combinations** (the Best Operating Line, BOL). In a conventional parallel hybrid, the ICE speed is tied to the wheel speed through the gearbox. In a power-split hybrid, the ICE is decoupled from the wheel speed by the PGS — the ICE can be held at any (torque, speed) point on its BOL by **modulating MG1's speed** through the inverter. The wheel speed is independently set by MG2 (driven by both the ring-gear mechanical path and the MG1-MG2 electrical path). This **decoupling** is the core reason Toyota's Hybrid Synergy Drive (THS) achieves thermal efficiencies of ~40 %, which is exceptional for a gasoline engine.

*(2 marks for PGS description, 2 marks for "power-split" naming logic, 3 marks for ICE-on-BOL explanation.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up Series vs Parallel:** Many students write that "in a Series hybrid, the ICE and motor are mechanically connected." This is **wrong** — in a Series hybrid, the ICE is **mechanically decoupled** from the wheels. Loss of 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing MG1 and MG2 roles:** In Toyota THS, MG1 is the **generator**, MG2 is the **traction motor**. Do not interchange. Loss of 1 mark.
> 3. **Forgetting regenerative braking in diagrams:** The KTU 2024 board explicitly checks for the dashed-line regen path from wheel → motor → inverter → battery. Forgetting it costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Using SI units inconsistently:** Always express speed in **m/s** in formulas, then convert final answer to km/h. Mixing units mid-derivation loses 0.5–1 mark.
> 5. **Skipping the "interpretation" sentence:** Numerical answers without a closing physical interpretation lose 0.5–1 mark. Always end with: *"This is consistent with [expected range] because [physical reason]."*

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Definition:** A drive-train *topology* is the **power-flow path** and **coupling** between ESS → PEC → Motor → Transmission → Wheels.
- [ ] **Five sub-systems** of any EV/HEV drive-train: **Energy Source, Power Electronic Converter, Electric Motor, Transmission, Final Drive**.
- [ ] **Three major families:** BEV, HEV (Series/Parallel/Series-Parallel), FCEV.
- [ ] **BEV sub-classes:** Single-motor single-speed, single-motor multi-speed, two-motor (front+rear), four in-wheel hub motors.
- [ ] **HEV sub-classes:** Series (mech-decoupled, all electrical), Parallel (mech-coupled, ICE + motor both to wheels), Series-Parallel (planetary, power-split).
- [ ] **Toyota THS** uses a **planetary gear set** with **MG1 (sun, generator)** and **MG2 (ring, traction motor)** — the ICE is on the **planet carrier**.
- [ ] **System efficiency** of a modern BEV ≈ **0.80–0.90** (battery × inverter × motor × transmission).
- [ ] **Aerodynamic drag** scales as **$v^3$** — this is why highway cruising dominates EV energy use.
- [ ] **Multi-speed transmissions** can improve WLTP range by **2–5 %** by keeping the motor on its peak-efficiency island.
- [ ] **Multi-motor drive-trains** enable **electronic torque vectoring** for superior handling and AWD.
- [ ] **Regenerative braking** is a *topology-independent* feature — it can be implemented in any drive-train that has a reversible motor + inverter.
- [ ] **Standard constants to memorize:** $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\rho_{air} = 1.225$ kg/m³, $f_r = 0.008$–$0.015$ for car tyres.
- [ ] **Energy flow arrow** in every diagram: draw a **solid arrow** for motoring, and a **dashed arrow** for regenerative braking — this is a 2-mark KTU scoring point.
- [ ] **Famous vehicles to remember:** Tesla Model 3 (BEV, single-motor), Tesla Model S Plaid (BEV, tri-motor), Toyota Prius (Power-split HEV), Honda Insight (Parallel HEV), Chevrolet Volt (Series HEV), BMW i3 REx (Series HEV), Porsche Taycan (2-speed BEV).
- [ ] **Numerical must-know formula:** $P_{wheel} = mg f_r v + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^{3}$ for level-road constant-speed cruise.
- [ ] **Boundary condition for "level road constant speed":** $F_{grade} = 0$, $F_{accel} = 0$, so $F_{tr} = F_{roll} + F_{aero}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Flow Control in Electric Drive-train Topologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET742 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES AND RENEWABLE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Power Flow Control in Electric Drive-train Topologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Power Flow Control in Electric Drive-train Topologies* refers to the systematic management and regulation of energy transfer between multiple power sources (Internal Combustion Engine, Fuel Cell, Battery, Supercapacitor, Solar PV) and the propulsion load (traction motor) across various drive-train architectures — namely, **Series Hybrid, Parallel Hybrid, Series-Parallel (Power-Split) Hybrid, Pure Electric, and Fuel-Cell Hybrid** configurations — to achieve optimal vehicle efficiency, minimum fuel consumption, reduced emissions, and extended driving range.

The **electric drive-train topology** is the topological arrangement of prime movers, energy storage systems (ESS), power electronic converters, and traction motors that together deliver mechanical power to the wheels. The **power flow controller** is the supervisory Energy Management System (EMS) that decides *instantaneously* — based on driver demand, SoC, vehicle speed, and torque request — how much power must be drawn from each source and routed to each sink.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Pipeline Network"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of an EV drive-train as a **water pipeline network** in a smart city.
> - The **water tanks** (sources) are your Battery, Fuel Cell, and Engine.
> - The **faucets** (control valves) are your DC-DC converters and DC-AC inverters.
> - The **tap (load)** is your traction motor + wheels.
> - The **plumber** who decides which tank feeds the tap, and in what proportion, at every second — that is your **Power Flow Controller / Energy Management Strategy (EMS)**.
>
> If the city needs **high pressure quickly** (acceleration), the plumber opens the battery tap fully and supplements with the engine. If demand is low (cruising), only the engine (most efficient at steady state) supplies the tap. The plumber's *logic* is the *power flow control algorithm*.

## 1.3 Why Power Flow Control is the *Heart* of EV Design

| Reason | Engineering Impact |
|---|---|
| **Efficiency Optimization** | Each source has a unique peak-efficiency operating point; the controller must keep each source *near* its sweet spot. |
| **Range Extension** | Smart source blending reduces fuel/energy waste. |
| **Battery Life Preservation** | Prevents deep discharge, over-charge, and thermal runaway. |
| **Emission Compliance** | Keeps ICE in its lowest BSFC region. |
| **Driveability** | Ensures instantaneous torque response during transients. |
| **Regenerative Braking Integration** | Routes recovered kinetic energy back to ESS. |

## 1.4 Key Physical & Standard Metrics

- **State of Charge (SoC):** Expressed as a percentage (typically **0% – 100%**), operating window commonly **30% – 80%** for Li-ion.
- **Power (P):** In **kW** or **W**; traction power typically **50–300 kW** for passenger EVs.
- **Torque (T):** In **Nm**; instantaneous for acceleration.
- **Speed (N or ω):** In **rpm** or **rad/s**; ω = 2πN/60.
- **Efficiency (η):** Dimensionless ratio; modern EV motors achieve **> 95%** peak efficiency.
- **Specific Energy:** Battery ~**150–250 Wh/kg**; Fuel Cell system ~**350–600 Wh/kg**.
- **BSFC (Brake Specific Fuel Consumption):** In **g/kWh**; ICE minimum typically **220–250 g/kWh**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Electric Drive-train Topologies

The KTU 2024 syllabus groups the drive-train topologies into **five canonical architectures**. Each has a distinct power flow path.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The student must be able to *draw, label, and explain* the power flow arrows for **each** of the five topologies — this is a guaranteed 7-mark KTU question.

### 2.1.1 Pure Electric Drive-train (Battery EV — BEV)

**Architecture:**
`Battery → DC-DC Converter → Inverter → Traction Motor (AC) → Transmission → Wheels`

**Power Flow Path (single source):**
$$P_{batt} = P_{motor} + P_{aux\ losses}$$

**Key Characteristics:**
- **Zero tailpipe emission**, simplest topology.
- Battery must be oversized to handle both peak propulsion and regenerative charging.
- **Regenerative braking** is mandatory to recover kinetic energy.
- Auxiliary power steering, HVAC, lighting drawn via separate **12V/48V DC-DC**.

### 2.1.2 Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle (SHEV)

**Architecture:**
`ICE → Generator → Rectifier ⇉ DC-Link ⇇ Battery ⇇ Inverter → Motor → Wheels`

The **ICE is mechanically decoupled** from the wheels. The ICE drives a generator that produces electricity, which either charges the battery or directly powers the traction motor through the electrical bus.

**Power Equations:**
$$P_{ICE} = P_{gen\ elec} = P_{motor\ elec} + P_{batt\ charge} + P_{losses}$$

$$P_{wheel} = P_{motor\ mech} \times \eta_{transmission}$$

**Controller Logic:**
- At low load: ICE charges battery.
- At high load: ICE + battery jointly feed motor.
- At idle: ICE can shut off (start-stop).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Series?** The ICE can always run at its *single best-efficiency point*, regardless of vehicle speed — giving excellent urban fuel economy.

### 2.1.3 Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicle (PHEV)

**Architecture:**
`ICE ⫼ Mechanical Coupler (clutch/gear) ⫼ Transmission → Wheels`
`Battery → Inverter → Motor ⫼ (coupled mechanically to same shaft)`

Both ICE and electric motor are **mechanically coupled** to the drive shaft, so they can *simultaneously* propel the vehicle.

**Power Flow Equation (mechanical summation):**
$$P_{wheel} = P_{ICE\ shaft} + P_{motor\ shaft} - P_{losses}$$

$$T_{wheel} = T_{ICE} \times i_g \times \eta_g + T_{motor} \times i_g \times \eta_g$$

Where $i_g$ = gear ratio, $\eta_g$ = transmission efficiency.

**Operating Modes:**
1. **ICE only** (highway cruising, high SoC).
2. **Motor only** (city, low SoC recharge needed).
3. **Combined** (max acceleration).
4. **Regenerative** (braking).
5. **ICE charging battery** (battery depleted mid-journey).

### 2.1.4 Series-Parallel Hybrid (Power-Split Hybrid — PSHEV)

This is the **Toyota Prius THS (Toyota Hybrid System)** architecture — the most commercially successful hybrid.

**Architecture:**
`ICE → Planetary Gear Set (Power Split Device) ⫼`
`├─ Path 1: Ring gear → Wheels`
`├─ Path 2: Sun gear → Generator (MG1) → Rectifier → DC-Link`
`└─ Battery ⇄ Inverter ⇄ Motor (MG2) → Wheels`

The **planetary gear set** acts as an *analog* power splitter. MG1 acts as both a generator and a *reactive* motor to control ICE speed.

**Kinematic Constraint (Willis Equation):**
$$(N_s + N_r) \cdot N_c = N_s \cdot N_r$$

For a single-planet gear with sun (S), ring (R), carrier (C):
$$(1 + k) \cdot \omega_c = \omega_s + k \cdot \omega_r$$

Where $k = N_r / N_s$ is the gear ratio. The ICE is on the carrier; MG1 on the sun; MG2 + wheels on the ring.

**Why this matters:** By varying MG1 speed, the controller can **independently set ICE torque and ICE speed**, decoupling them from wheel torque and wheel speed — perfect for optimizing BSFC.

### 2.1.5 Fuel Cell Hybrid Electric Vehicle (FCHV)

**Architecture:**
`H₂ Tank → Fuel Cell Stack → DC/DC Boost → DC-Link ⇇ Battery (or Ultra-capacitor) ⇇ Inverter → Motor → Wheels`

The **fuel cell** has slow dynamic response; the **battery/supercapacitor** handles transients. The power flow controller must split load between slow fuel cell and fast battery.

**Power Sharing Logic:**
$$P_{FC} = P_{load,avg} \quad ; \quad P_{batt} = P_{load,peak} - P_{FC}$$

This keeps the fuel cell at near-constant optimal power, improving stack life.

## 2.2 Energy Management Strategies (EMS) — The Brain of Power Flow Control

| Strategy | Principle | Real-time Capable? | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Rule-Based (Deterministic)** | IF-THEN rules using SoC and torque request | ✅ Yes | ★★★ Most asked |
| **Thermostatic (On-Off)** | ICE ON if SoC < SoC_min, OFF if SoC > SoC_max | ✅ Yes | ★★ |
| **Power Follower** | ICE follows average load; battery handles transients | ✅ Yes | ★★★ |
| **Equivalent Consumption Minimization Strategy (ECMS)** | Converts electrical energy use to equivalent fuel | ✅ Yes (adaptive) | ★★★★ |
| **Dynamic Programming (DP)** | Global optimization, backward-looking | ❌ Offline only | ★★★ |
| **Pontryagin's Minimum Principle (PMP)** | Continuous co-state optimization | Near-real-time | ★★★★ |
| **Model Predictive Control (MPC)** | Rolling-horizon optimization | ✅ Yes | ★★★★★ Trendy |
| **Fuzzy Logic / Neural / RL** | AI-based, learns from data | ✅ Yes | ★★★★ |
| **Game-Theoretic** | Treats driver and EMS as players | Emerging | ★★ |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table verbatim** — it covers 80% of the numerical questions asked on Module 1.

| # | Formula | Description | Unit / Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = T \cdot \omega$ | Mechanical power from torque & speed | W (T in Nm, ω in rad/s) |
| 2 | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | Convert rpm to rad/s | rad/s |
| 3 | $P_{wheel} = \eta_t \cdot P_{motor}$ | After transmission losses | η_t ≈ 0.90–0.95 |
| 4 | $E = \int P \, dt$ | Energy = Power × time (in Wh if P in W, t in hr) | Wh |
| 5 | $\text{SoC}(t) = \text{SoC}_0 - \dfrac{1}{Q_{bat}} \int I \, dt$ | Coulomb counting | SoC dimensionless |
| 6 | $V_{oc} = V_{nom} + R_{int}(I_{load} - I_{rest})$ | Battery model (R-int) | V |
| 7 | $\text{BSFC} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{fuel}}{P_{brake}}$ | ICE fuel efficiency | g/kWh |
| 8 | $F_{trac} = m g f_r \cos\theta + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 + m g \sin\theta + m \delta \dfrac{dv}{dt}$ | Tractive force equation | N |
| 9 | $P_{trac} = F_{trac} \cdot v$ | Tractive power demand | W |
| 10 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{trans} \cdot \eta_{bat}$ | Total drivetrain efficiency | 0.6–0.85 typical |
| 11 | $(1+k)\omega_c = \omega_s + k\omega_r$ | Planetary gear (Willis) | rad/s, k = N_r/N_s |
| 12 | $\eta_{regen} = \dfrac{E_{recovered}}{E_{kinetic\ lost}}$ | Regenerative braking efficiency | 0.3–0.7 |
| 13 | $P_{demand} = \dfrac{v}{\eta_t}\left(mgf_r + \dfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2 + m a\right)$ | Simplified tractive power | W |
| 14 | $\dot{m}_{fuel,eq} = \dot{m}_{fuel,ICE} + s \cdot \dfrac{P_{bat}}{Q_{LHV}}$ | ECMS equivalent fuel | kg/s |
| 15 | $\Delta\text{SoC} = \dfrac{\int (P_{bat}/V_{bat}) \, dt}{Q_{bat}}$ | SoC change over drive cycle | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> In Table row 1, the `P` is *not* a regex; it is the mechanical power symbol. Similarly in row 4, the integrand uses standard `P` for power.

---

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Tractive Force and Tractive Power (Foundational EV Equation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation appears in nearly every KTU numerical problem. Master it.

### Step 1 — Free-Body Forces on a Vehicle Climbing a Slope

Consider a vehicle of mass $m$ moving at velocity $v$ up a slope of angle $\theta$. The four forces resisting motion are:

1. **Rolling resistance:** $F_r = m g f_r \cos\theta$
   - $f_r$ = rolling resistance coefficient (≈ **0.01** for car on asphalt, **0.015** for truck).
2. **Aerodynamic drag:** $F_a = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2$
   - $\rho$ = air density ≈ **1.225 kg/m³**, $C_d$ = drag coefficient, $A$ = frontal area.
3. **Grading force:** $F_g = m g \sin\theta$
4. **Inertial force (acceleration):** $F_i = m \cdot \delta \cdot a = m \cdot \delta \cdot \dfrac{dv}{dt}$
   - $\delta$ = mass factor (rotational inertia) ≈ **1.05–1.4**.

### Step 2 — Tractive Force Equilibrium

The motor must overcome all four:
$$F_{trac} = m g f_r \cos\theta + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 + m g \sin\theta + m \delta \dfrac{dv}{dt}$$

### Step 3 — Tractive Power (Mechanical Power at Wheels)

Power = Force × Velocity:
$$P_{trac} = F_{trac} \cdot v$$

Substituting:
$$P_{trac} = v \left[ m g f_r \cos\theta + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 + m g \sin\theta + m \delta \dfrac{dv}{dt} \right]$$

### Step 4 — Special Case: Level Road, Constant Speed ($a = 0$, $\theta = 0$)

$$P_{trac} = v \left[ m g f_r + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 \right]$$

This is the **cruising power** the battery/ICE must continuously supply.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — Series Hybrid Power Sharing

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem (KTU Pattern):** A series HEV has an ICE-generator set rated at 30 kW, a battery pack of 200 V nominal, and a traction motor drawing 60 kW peak. The vehicle cruises at constant 50 km/h on level road. Rolling resistance coefficient = 0.012, $C_d A$ = 0.7 m², $m$ = 1500 kg, $g$ = 9.81, $\rho$ = 1.225. **Find: (a) Tractive power demand, (b) Whether the ICE alone can supply cruising power, (c) Battery current if battery supplements.**

### Solution

**Step (a) — Tractive power demand at 50 km/h on level road**

Convert velocity: $v = 50 / 3.6 = 13.89$ m/s.

$$P_{trac} = v \left[ m g f_r + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 \right]$$

Compute rolling term:
$$F_r = 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.012 = 176.58 \text{ N}$$

Compute aero term:
$$F_a = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.7 \times (13.89)^2 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.7 \times 192.9$$
$$F_a = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.7 \times 192.9 = 82.72 \text{ N}$$

Total tractive force:
$$F_{trac} = 176.58 + 82.72 = 259.30 \text{ N}$$

Tractive power:
$$P_{trac} = 259.30 \times 13.89 = 3601 \text{ W} \approx 3.6 \text{ kW}$$

**Step (b) — Can ICE alone supply?**

$$P_{ICE} = 30 \text{ kW} \gg 3.6 \text{ kW}$$

✅ **Yes, ICE alone is sufficient.** The EMS will command the ICE to operate at its minimum BSFC point (say 8 kW) and the **excess** (≈ 4.4 kW) will be routed to **charge the battery**.

**Step (c) — Battery current when supplementing**

If during acceleration the traction motor demands **40 kW** and ICE is held at **8 kW**:
$$P_{bat} = 40 - 8 = 32 \text{ kW}$$

Battery current at 200 V:
$$I_{bat} = \dfrac{P_{bat}}{V_{bat}} = \dfrac{32000}{200} = 160 \text{ A}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Planetary Gear Speed Relationship (Power Split Device)

The **planetary gear set** is the mechanical heart of the Toyota THS and many PSHEVs.

### Step 1 — Geometry

A planetary gear has three members:
- **Sun gear** (S) — $N_s$ teeth
- **Ring gear** (R) — $N_r$ teeth
- **Planet carrier** (C) — holds the planet pinions

### Step 2 — Ratio Definition

Define:
$$k = \dfrac{N_r}{N_s}$$

For a typical Prius: $k \approx 2.6$.

### Step 3 — Relative Velocity (Fundamental Kinematic Equation)

A point on the ring is at the same tangential velocity as the contact point with the planet. The planet rolls without slipping between sun and ring:
$$\dfrac{\omega_r - \omega_c}{\omega_s - \omega_c} = -\dfrac{N_s}{N_r} = -\dfrac{1}{k}$$

### Step 4 — Willis Equation (Rearranging)

$$\omega_r - \omega_c = -\dfrac{1}{k}(\omega_s - \omega_c)$$

$$k(\omega_r - \omega_c) = -(\omega_s - \omega_c)$$

$$k \omega_r - k \omega_c = -\omega_s + \omega_c$$

$$k \omega_r + \omega_s = k \omega_c + \omega_c = (k+1)\omega_c$$

$$\boxed{(1 + k)\omega_c = \omega_s + k\omega_r}$$

### Step 5 — Applying to Toyota THS

- **Carrier (C)** = ICE shaft → $\omega_c = \omega_{ICE}$
- **Sun (S)** = MG1 (generator) → $\omega_s = \omega_{MG1}$
- **Ring (R)** = MG2 + wheels → $\omega_r = \omega_{wheels} \times i_g$

Therefore:
$$(1 + k)\omega_{ICE} = \omega_{MG1} + k \cdot \omega_{wheels} \cdot i_g$$

The EMS controls $\omega_{MG1}$ (via MG1 torque) to set ICE speed independently of wheel speed — this is **the key to BSFC optimization**.

---

## 3.4 ECMS Derivation Sketch (Equivalent Consumption Minimization Strategy)

### Step 1 — Define Cost Function

At each instant, minimize equivalent fuel consumption rate:
$$\dot{m}_{eq}(t) = \dot{m}_{fuel,ICE}(t) + s(t) \cdot \dfrac{P_{bat}(t)}{Q_{LHV}}$$

Where $s(t)$ is the **equivalence factor** (penalty for using stored electric energy), and $Q_{LHV}$ is the lower heating value of fuel (≈ 43 MJ/kg for gasoline).

### Step 2 — Constraint

$$P_{ICE}(t) + P_{bat}(t) = P_{demand}(t)$$

$$P_{bat}^{min} \leq P_{bat} \leq P_{bat}^{max}$$

$$\text{SoC}_{min} \leq \text{SoC}(t) \leq \text{SoC}_{max}$$

### Step 3 — Lagrange Formulation

$$\mathcal{L} = \dot{m}_{eq} + \lambda_1 (P_{ICE} + P_{bat} - P_{demand}) + \lambda_2 (g(\text{SoC}))$$

Solving via KKT conditions yields optimal $P_{bat}^*$ as a function of $s$ and $\partial \dot{m}_{fuel}/\partial P_{ICE}$.

### Step 4 — Adaptive $s(t)$

In **Adaptive ECMS**, $s$ is updated online to keep SoC near target:
$$s(t+1) = s(t) + K_p \cdot (\text{SoC}_{target} - \text{SoC}(t))$$

This is real-time implementable and is therefore **the KTU-preferred modern EMS**.

---

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block-Diagram: Comparative Drive-train Topologies

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph TOPO1["TOPOLOGY A: Pure Electric BEV"]
        B1["Battery Pack"] -->|DC| DC1["DC-DC Converter"]
        DC1 -->|DC Link| INV1["Inverter"]
        INV1 -->|3-phase AC| M1["Traction Motor"]
        M1 -->|Mechanical| T1["Transmission"]
        T1 --> W1["Wheels"]
        B1 -.->|Regen| INV1
    end

    subgraph TOPO2["TOPOLOGY B: Series Hybrid SHEV"]
        I2["ICE Engine"] --> G2["Generator"]
        G2 -->|AC-DC| RECT2["Rectifier"]
        RECT2 -->|DC Link| INV2["Inverter"]
        B2["Battery"] <-->|Bidirectional| RECT2
        INV2 -->|AC| M2["Traction Motor"]
        M2 --> T2["Transmission"]
        T2 --> W2["Wheels"]
    end

    subgraph TOPO3["TOPOLOGY C: Parallel Hybrid PHEV"]
        I3["ICE Engine"] --> C3["Clutch Coupler"]
        B3["Battery"] --> INV3["Inverter"]
        INV3 --> EM3["E-Motor"]
        EM3 --> C3
        C3 --> T3["Transmission"]
        T3 --> W3["Wheels"]
    end
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow: Series-Parallel Power-Split (THS) Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    ICE["ICE Engine"] -->|Carrier| PG["Planetary Gear Set"]
    PG -->|Sun| MG1["MG1 Generator"]
    MG1 -->|AC-DC| DCBUS["DC Bus"]
    BAT["Battery Pack"] <-->|Bidirectional| DCBUS
    DCBUS <-->|Inverter| MG2["MG2 Traction Motor"]
    MG2 --> WHL["Wheels"]
    PG -->|Ring| WHL
    EMS["EMS Controller"] -.->|Torque Cmd| MG1
    EMS -.->|Torque Cmd| MG2
    EMS -.->|Throttle Cmd| ICE
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Flow: Fuel Cell Hybrid Drive-train

```mermaid
graph TB
    H2["Hydrogen Tank"] --> FC["Fuel Cell Stack"]
    FC -->|Low V DC| BOOST["DC-DC Boost Converter"]
    BOOST -->|High V DC| DCBUS["DC Bus"]
    BESS["Battery / Supercapacitor"] <-->|Bidirectional| DCBUS
    DCBUS --> INV["Traction Inverter"]
    INV --> MOT["Traction Motor"]
    MOT --> WHL["Wheels"]
    CTRL["Power Flow Controller"] -.->|Current Ref| BOOST
    CTRL -.->|Current Ref| BESS
    CTRL -.->|Torque Ref| MOT
```

## 4.4 Comparative Architecture Summary Table

| Topology | Mechanical Coupling | Sources to Wheels | Complexity | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **BEV** | None (pure electric) | Battery → Motor | ★ Low | Nissan Leaf, Tesla |
| **Series HEV** | None (ICE off wheels) | ICE→Gen→Battery→Motor | ★★ Medium | City buses, garbage trucks |
| **Parallel HEV** | Direct mechanical | ICE ∥ Motor → Wheels | ★★ Medium | Honda Insight (early) |
| **Series-Parallel (PSHEV)** | Planetary | ICE + MG1 + MG2 split | ★★★★ High | Toyota Prius, Ford Escape |
| **Fuel Cell HEV** | None (FC off wheels) | FC + Battery → Motor | ★★★ High | Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo |
| **Solar-Assisted BEV** | None | PV + Battery → Motor | ★★ Medium | Lightweight urban EVs |

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam — Jun 2023]**
> *Differentiate between Series and Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicle drive-train topologies with neat sketches. List two advantages of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Series HEV | Parallel HEV |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical link ICE–wheels** | ❌ None | ✅ Direct (via clutch/gear) |
| **Components** | ICE + Generator + Battery + Motor | ICE + Motor + Battery + Clutch |
| **ICE operating point** | Fixed at best BSFC (decoupled from wheel) | Coupled to wheel speed |
| **Efficiency** | Good for stop-go urban duty | Good for highway |
| **Size of motor** | Large (only traction) | Smaller (shares load) |

**Series advantages:** (i) Simple mechanical layout, no transmission needed; (ii) ICE always runs at optimal point.
**Parallel advantages:** (i) Smaller traction motor and battery; (ii) ICE can directly propel at highway, avoiding conversion losses.

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
> *Explain the role of the Energy Management System (EMS) in a hybrid electric vehicle with reference to power flow control.**

**Model Answer:**
The **EMS** is the supervisory controller that decides, at every instant, the **power split** between the ICE, battery, and motor. It receives inputs (SoC, vehicle speed, torque demand, driver aggression) and outputs torque commands to each actuator.
- In **Series HEV**: controls ICE on/off and generator torque.
- In **Parallel HEV**: decides ICE-only / motor-only / combined mode.
- In **PSHEV**: controls MG1 and MG2 torques to optimize planetary gear power flow.

The EMS aims to: (i) minimize fuel consumption, (ii) maintain SoC within window, (iii) keep emissions low, (iv) ensure driveability. Common strategies: rule-based, ECMS, MPC, fuzzy logic.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **power flow in a Series-Parallel (Power-Split) Hybrid Electric Vehicle** using a planetary gear set. Derive the Willis equation relating ICE, MG1, and wheel speeds. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A 1500 kg parallel HEV is cruising at 60 km/h on a level road. The ICE supplies 25 kW at 28% thermal efficiency, and the e-motor supplies 5 kW at 92% efficiency. Rolling resistance = 0.012, $C_d A$ = 0.65 m², $g$ = 9.81, $\rho$ = 1.225. Compute: (i) tractive power demand, (ii) total fuel input rate, (iii) overall drivetrain efficiency. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Block diagram** (refer to Section 4.2 Mermaid figure). ICE on carrier, MG1 on sun, MG2 + wheels on ring.

**Willis derivation** (see Section 3.3 full derivation):
- Define $k = N_r / N_s$.
- Rolling constraint: $(\omega_r - \omega_c)/(\omega_s - \omega_c) = -1/k$.
- Rearranging: $(1+k)\omega_c = \omega_s + k\omega_r$.
- **Physical meaning:** the controller adjusts $\omega_{MG1}$ to set ICE speed independently of wheel speed, keeping ICE in its BSFC sweet spot.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step (i): Tractive power at 60 km/h on level road**

$v = 60/3.6 = 16.67$ m/s.

Rolling force: $F_r = 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.012 = 176.58$ N.

Aero force:
$$F_a = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.65 \times (16.67)^2$$
$$F_a = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.65 \times 277.8$$
$$F_a = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.65 \times 277.8 = 110.6 \text{ N}$$

Total $F_{trac} = 176.58 + 110.6 = 287.18$ N.

$$P_{trac} = 287.18 \times 16.67 = 4786 \text{ W} \approx 4.79 \text{ kW}$$

**Step (ii): Total fuel input rate**

Total mechanical demand at wheels: 4.79 kW. But ICE supplies 25 kW (excess charges battery) and motor supplies 5 kW. Wait — interpreting the question literally: ICE provides 25 kW *mechanical* and motor 5 kW *mechanical*, totaling 30 kW of input to drivetrain. The tractive power demand is 4.79 kW, so 25.21 kW is lost or stored.

Per question intent, the **fuel rate** comes from ICE alone:
$$\eta_{ICE} = 28\% = 0.28$$
$$P_{fuel} = \dfrac{P_{ICE}}{\eta_{ICE}} = \dfrac{25}{0.28} = 89.29 \text{ kW (chemical)}$$

If gasoline ($Q_{LHV} = 43$ MJ/kg):
$$\dot{m}_{fuel} = \dfrac{89.29 \times 10^3}{43 \times 10^6} = 2.08 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s} = 2.08 \text{ g/s}$$

**Step (iii): Overall drivetrain efficiency**

Useful mechanical output at wheels = 4.79 kW. Total fuel energy input (chemical) from ICE = 89.29 kW. Plus motor electrical energy 5 kW (which we can include in input only if we know its source — assume from battery charged earlier, so not in current input).

For ICE-only pathway:
$$\eta_{overall,ICE} = \dfrac{P_{trac}}{P_{fuel}} = \dfrac{4.79}{89.29} = 5.4\%$$

This low value reflects the fact that 25 kW ICE is grossly oversized for 4.79 kW demand — illustrating **why EMS keeps ICE near optimal load**, not oversized.

### Question B — Option 2 (Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **(a)** Explain the **architecture of a Fuel Cell Hybrid Electric Vehicle (FCHV)**. Discuss why a battery or supercapacitor is essential in this topology. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Derive the **tractive force equation** for an EV moving on a road gradient. A 1200 kg EV climbs a 5° slope at constant 30 km/h. $f_r$ = 0.013, $C_d A$ = 0.55 m², $\rho$ = 1.225, $g$ = 9.81. Find the tractive force and the tractive power. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Architecture** (refer to Section 4.3 Mermaid diagram):
`H₂ Tank → Fuel Cell Stack → DC-DC Boost → DC Bus ⇄ Battery/Supercapacitor ⇄ Inverter → Motor → Wheels`

**Why battery/ultracapacitor is essential:**

1. **Slow dynamic response of FC:** Fuel cells have time constants of **1–10 seconds** to ramp power; vehicle accelerations need sub-second response. The battery (≈ 100 ms response) bridges this.
2. **Cold start:** FC needs warm-up; battery provides initial propulsion.
3. **Braking energy recovery:** FC cannot absorb regenerative energy; battery/UC stores it.
4. **Peak shaving:** Battery handles peaks, FC handles average → FC sizing reduces by 30–50%.
5. **Voltage stabilization:** DC bus voltage maintained within inverter tolerance window.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Tractive force equation** (full derivation in Section 3.1):
$$F_{trac} = m g f_r \cos\theta + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 + m g \sin\theta + m \delta \dfrac{dv}{dt}$$

For constant speed on 5° slope, $a = 0$, $\delta$ term vanishes.

**Numerical computation:**

$v = 30/3.6 = 8.33$ m/s.
$\theta = 5°$, $\sin 5° = 0.0872$, $\cos 5° = 0.9962$.

Rolling force:
$$F_r = 1200 \times 9.81 \times 0.013 \times 0.9962 = 152.4 \text{ N}$$

Aero force:
$$F_a = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.55 \times (8.33)^2 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.55 \times 69.4 = 23.4 \text{ N}$$

Grade force:
$$F_g = 1200 \times 9.81 \times 0.0872 = 1026.4 \text{ N}$$

Total tractive force:
$$F_{trac} = 152.4 + 23.4 + 1026.4 = 1202.2 \text{ N} \approx 1.20 \text{ kN}$$

Tractive power:
$$P_{trac} = 1202.2 \times 8.33 = 10014 \text{ W} \approx 10.0 \text{ kW}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Forgetting the mass factor $\delta$** in acceleration problems — most students drop it, costing 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing rad/s and rpm** when applying $P = T\omega$ — always state unit conversion explicitly.
> 3. **In Series HEV numericals, students wrongly assume ICE = wheel power.** Remember: ICE → Generator → Rectifier → Battery/Motor → Wheels. There are **multiple** conversion stages, each with efficiency loss.
> 4. **In PSHEV, students forget that MG1 is reactive** (it does not deliver net power to wheels — it only adjusts ICE speed).
> 5. **Unit of tractive force must be N**, not kgf. Convert if given.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (print this on exam eve):**

- ✅ **5 topologies:** BEV, Series, Parallel, Series-Parallel (Power-Split), FCHV — know block diagram + power flow for each.
- ✅ **Tractive force equation:** $F_{trac} = mgf_r\cos\theta + \frac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2 + mg\sin\theta + m\delta a$ — **memorize the four terms**.
- ✅ **Willis equation:** $(1+k)\omega_c = \omega_s + k\omega_r$ — anchor ICE→carrier, MG1→sun, wheels→ring.
- ✅ **EMS strategies:** Rule-based (most common in KTU), ECMS (modern), DP/PMP (theoretical benchmark), MPC (trending).
- ✅ **Series HEV** decouples ICE from wheels; ICE always at best BSFC.
- ✅ **Parallel HEV** mechanical summation: $T_{wheel} = T_{ICE} + T_{motor}$ (after gear ratio).
- ✅ **PSHEV** uses planetary gear as analog power splitter — Toyota THS.
- ✅ **FCHV** needs battery for transient response (FC time constant 1–10 s).
- ✅ **Regenerative braking:** recovers up to 30–70% of kinetic energy.
- ✅ **Unit conversions:** $v$ (km/h → m/s): divide by 3.6; $N$ (rpm → rad/s): multiply by $2\pi/60$.
- ✅ **SoC operating window:** typically 30%–80% for Li-ion to maximize cycle life.
- ✅ **Efficiencies to remember:** Motor >95%, Inverter >95%, Battery round-trip 85–90%, Fuel Cell 50–60% (HHV).
- ✅ **Real-world EV examples:** Tesla Model 3 (BEV), Toyota Prius (PSHEV), Honda Civic Hybrid (Parallel), Hyundai Nexo (FCEV).

---

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET742 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES AND RENEWABLE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fuel Efficiency Analysis (Derivations not required)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Fuel Efficiency Analysis in the EV Context?

In the domain of **Electric Vehicles and Renewable Technologies**, *Fuel Efficiency Analysis* refers to the systematic quantification and comparative evaluation of energy utilization in electric vehicles (EVs) against conventional Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) vehicles. Because EVs do not consume "fuel" in the traditional hydrocarbon sense, the analysis is reframed around **electrical energy consumption per unit distance** and the **end-to-end (Well-to-Wheel) efficiency** of the entire energy chain — from primary energy source (coal, solar, wind, hydro) to the kinetic energy delivered at the wheels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** Fuel Efficiency Analysis is the engineering study that quantifies the energy required to propel a vehicle over a specified drive cycle, expressed in standardized metrics such as **kWh/km**, **kWh/100 km**, or **MPGe (Miles Per Gallon Equivalent)**, and evaluates the cumulative thermodynamic, electrical, and mechanical losses from the primary energy source to the road.

The two principal analytical frameworks are:
1. **Tank-to-Wheel (TTW) Efficiency** — the efficiency measured from the on-board energy reservoir (battery or fuel tank) to the wheels.
2. **Well-to-Wheel (WTW) Efficiency** — the cumulative efficiency spanning energy extraction, processing, transportation, storage, and final conversion at the wheels.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The Water Bucket Analogy:** Imagine you are carrying water from a well (primary energy source) to a thirsty plant at the end of your garden (the wheels of the car).
> - **Tank-to-Wheel** measures how much water reaches the plant from the bucket you carry.
> - **Well-to-Wheel** measures how much water reaches the plant from the *original* water in the well — accounting for spills during pumping, leaks in the pipe, and evaporation along the way.
> - A **conventional ICE car** is like using a leaking bucket, pouring water through a noisy engine that loses a lot to heat.
> - An **electric car** is like using a sealed insulated bottle, with a small electric pump — much less is lost in transit.

### 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics in EV Fuel Efficiency Analysis

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Application Context |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Energy Consumption | $E_c$ | kWh/100 km | Direct EV electricity usage |
| Equivalent Fuel Economy | $MPG_e$ | miles/gallon | Cross-comparison with ICE vehicles |
| Tank-to-Wheel Efficiency | $\eta_{TTW}$ | % | On-board energy conversion performance |
| Well-to-Wheel Efficiency | $\eta_{WTW}$ | % | Holistic cradle-to-road analysis |
| Regenerative Braking Recovery | $\eta_{regen}$ | % | Energy recaptured during deceleration |
| Drive Cycle Energy Demand | $E_{cycle}$ | kWh | Standardized test cycle consumption |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Energy Equivalence Constant:** **1 litre of gasoline ≈ 8.9 kWh of energy** and **1 US gallon of gasoline ≈ 33.7 kWh**. These constants form the basis of $MPG_e$ calculation and are universally adopted by the US EPA and KTU reference materials.

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Deconstructing the Fuel Efficiency Chain

The complete fuel efficiency analysis of an EV can be decomposed into the following structured logical steps:

**Step 1 — Quantify Energy Demand at the Wheels**
- The wheels must overcome four fundamental resistive forces:
  1. **Aerodynamic Drag** ($F_d$) — resistance from air displacement.
  2. **Rolling Resistance** ($F_{rr}$) — friction between tyres and road.
  3. **Gradient Force** ($F_g$) — gravitational resistance on inclines.
  4. **Acceleration Force** ($F_a$) — inertial resistance during speed changes.

**Step 2 — Convert Wheel Demand to Battery Discharge**
- Multiply the wheel demand by the **inverse of the drivetrain efficiency** to account for losses in the motor, inverter, transmission, and battery internal resistance.

**Step 3 — Apply Regenerative Braking Credit**
- During deceleration, the motor acts as a generator, returning a portion of kinetic energy to the battery. This is **subtracted** from the net energy demand.

**Step 4 — Account for Auxiliary Loads**
- HVAC (heating, ventilation, air conditioning), lighting, infotainment, and battery thermal management consume energy independently of traction.

**Step 5 — Scale to Standard Drive Cycle**
- The energy demand is normalized over standardized drive cycles (FTP-75, NEDC, WLTP, or Indian Drive Cycle IDC) to obtain a **comparable, repeatable** efficiency figure.

**Step 6 — Convert to Standard Reporting Metric**
- Express the final result in kWh/100 km, MPGe, or km/kWh depending on the audience and regulatory framework.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are presented for direct application. Derivations are intentionally omitted as per the topic's KTU 2024 directive.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $E_c = \dfrac{E_{used}}{d}$ | Energy consumption per unit distance | kWh/km |
| 2 | $E_c^{100} = \dfrac{E_{used}}{d} \times 100$ | Standardized energy consumption | kWh/100 km |
| 3 | $MPG_e = \dfrac{33.7}{E_c^{100mi}}$ | Miles per gallon equivalent (US EPA) | miles/gallon |
| 4 | $\eta_{TTW} = \dfrac{E_{wheel}}{E_{battery}} \times 100\%$ | Tank-to-wheel efficiency | dimensionless (%) |
| 5 | $\eta_{WTW} = \eta_{WTT} \times \eta_{TTW}$ | Well-to-wheel efficiency | dimensionless (%) |
| 6 | $\eta_{regen} = \dfrac{E_{recovered}}{E_{kinetic}} \times 100\%$ | Regenerative braking efficiency | dimensionless (%) |
| 7 | $P_{aux} = P_{HVAC} + P_{lights} + P_{infotainment} + P_{BMS}$ | Total auxiliary power load | kW |
| 8 | $E_{net} = E_{battery,discharge} - E_{regen} - E_{aux}$ | Net traction energy | kWh |
| 9 | $CO_2^{saved} = E_c \times EF_{grid} \times d$ | CO₂ reduction estimation | kg CO₂ |
| 10 | $C_{op} = E_c \times P_{elec} \times d$ | Operating cost per trip | ₹ or \$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions Applicable to All Formulas:**
> - All efficiencies $\eta$ lie in the closed interval [0, 1] (or [0 %, 100 %]).
> - $E_{regen}$ cannot exceed $E_{kinetic}$ (regeneration efficiency is bounded by motor-generator limits and battery SoC headroom).
> - Drive cycle energy demand is **vehicle-specific** — typical EV values: **15–25 kWh/100 km** for compact cars, **20–30 kWh/100 km** for SUVs.

### 2.3 Engineering Utility of Fuel Efficiency Analysis

- **Regulatory Compliance:** Enables manufacturers to certify vehicles under Indian CMVR (Central Motor Vehicle Rules) and international WLTP norms.
- **Consumer Decision-Making:** Allows direct cost-of-ownership comparison between EV and ICE vehicles.
- **Renewable Integration Planning:** Helps grid planners estimate the additional electricity demand from large-scale EV adoption and align it with renewable generation capacity.
- **Battery Sizing:** The energy demand per km directly determines the required kWh capacity of the traction battery.
- **Thermal Management Design:** Auxiliary load estimation informs HVAC and cooling system sizing.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Directive Acknowledged:** As explicitly stated in the syllabus, **derivations are not required** for this topic. The analytical matrix below therefore presents **application-based numerical analysis** with full step-by-step evaluation logic, treating each formula as a "given" tool and demonstrating its correct usage.

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Converting kWh/100 km to MPGe

**Problem Statement:**
An electric vehicle consumes **18 kWh per 100 km**. Compute its Miles Per Gallon Equivalent ($MPG_e$).

**Analytical Strategy:**
- Step A — Convert distance from km to miles.
- Step B — Apply the EPA equivalence constant of **33.7 kWh per US gallon**.
- Step C — Substitute into the standard $MPG_e$ formula.

**Solution Walkthrough:**

Given:
- Energy consumption = 18 kWh/100 km

First, compute consumption per 100 miles:
- 100 km = 62.137 miles (using **1 km = 0.62137 miles**)

$$
E_c^{100mi} = 18 \;\text{kWh} \times \frac{100 \;\text{mi}}{100 \;\text{km}} \times \frac{1 \;\text{km}}{0.62137 \;\text{mi}}
$$

$$
E_c^{100mi} = \frac{18}{0.62137} \approx 28.97 \;\text{kWh/100 mi}
$$

Now compute $MPG_e$:

$$
MPG_e = \frac{33.7 \;\text{kWh}}{28.97 \;\text{kWh}} \approx 1.163 \times 10^{1} \approx 116.3 \;\text{MPGe}
$$

**Result:** The vehicle achieves approximately **116.3 MPGe**, a benchmark typical of modern compact EVs like the Tata Nexon EV.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Well-to-Wheel Efficiency Comparison

**Problem Statement:**
Compare the Well-to-Wheel efficiency of a petrol ICE car and a battery EV charged from the Indian grid mix.

**Analytical Strategy:**
- Step A — Identify Tank-to-Wheel efficiency of each vehicle.
- Step B — Identify Well-to-Tank efficiency of the corresponding fuel pathway.
- Step C — Multiply to obtain $\eta_{WTW}$.

**Data Table:**

| Parameter | ICE (Petrol) | EV (Grid Mix India) | EV (100% Solar) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Tank-to-Wheel $\eta_{TTW}$ | 0.20 (20 %) | 0.77 (77 %) | 0.77 (77 %) |
| Well-to-Tank $\eta_{WTT}$ | 0.80 (80 %) | 0.35 (35 %) | 0.95 (95 %) |
| **Well-to-Wheel $\eta_{WTW}$** | **0.16 (16 %)** | **0.27 (27 %)** | **0.73 (73 %)** |

**Evaluation Logic for Petrol ICE:**

$$
\eta_{WTW}^{ICE} = \eta_{WTT}^{ICE} \times \eta_{TTW}^{ICE}
$$

$$
\eta_{WTW}^{ICE} = 0.80 \times 0.20 = 0.16 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 16\,\%
$$

**Evaluation Logic for EV on Indian Grid Mix:**

$$
\eta_{WTW}^{EV,grid} = \eta_{WTT}^{grid} \times \eta_{TTW}^{EV}
$$

$$
\eta_{WTW}^{EV,grid} = 0.35 \times 0.77 = 0.2695 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 27\,\%
$$

**Evaluation Logic for EV on 100 % Solar:**

$$
\eta_{WTW}^{EV,solar} = \eta_{WTT}^{solar} \times \eta_{TTW}^{EV}
$$

$$
\eta_{WTW}^{EV,solar} = 0.95 \times 0.77 = 0.7315 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 73\,\%
$$

**Interpretation Matrix:**

$$
\Delta\eta_{EV\;vs\;ICE}^{grid} = 0.27 - 0.16 = 0.11 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 11\;\text{percentage points higher}
$$

$$
\Delta\eta_{EV\;vs\;ICE}^{solar} = 0.73 - 0.16 = 0.57 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 57\;\text{percentage points higher}
$$

**Key Engineering Insight:** When powered by **100 % renewable electricity**, an EV is **4.5 times more efficient** end-to-end than a petrol car. On the current Indian grid mix, it is still **~1.7 times more efficient** — a substantial gain.

### 3.3 Worked Example 3: Net Energy Demand with Regenerative Braking

**Problem Statement:**
An EV travels 150 km on a standard drive cycle. The battery delivers 30 kWh. Regenerative braking recovers 4.5 kWh. Auxiliary loads consume 1.8 kWh. Compute:
- (a) Net traction energy
- (b) Equivalent kWh/100 km
- (c) MPGe

**Analytical Strategy:**
- Step A — Apply the net energy formula: $E_{net} = E_{battery,discharge} - E_{regen} - E_{aux}$.
- Step B — Normalize over 100 km.
- Step C — Convert to MPGe.

**Solution:**

$$
E_{net} = 30 - 4.5 - 1.8 = 23.7 \;\text{kWh}
$$

$$
E_c^{100} = \frac{23.7}{150} \times 100 = 15.8 \;\text{kWh/100 km}
$$

For MPGe, convert to per 100 miles:
- 150 km = 93.21 miles

$$
E_c^{100mi} = \frac{23.7}{93.21} \times 100 = 25.43 \;\text{kWh/100 mi}
$$

$$
MPG_e = \frac{33.7}{25.43} \approx 132.5 \;\text{MPGe}
$$

**Result:** The EV delivers **15.8 kWh/100 km** and **132.5 MPGe** — a high-efficiency profile, demonstrating the compounding benefit of regenerative braking on urban drive cycles.

### 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix: EV vs ICE Fuel Efficiency

| Parameter | Conventional Petrol ICE | Hybrid (HEV) | Battery EV (BEV) | Fuel Cell EV (FCEV) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Energy Source | Petrol | Petrol + Battery | Grid Electricity | Hydrogen |
| Typical kWh/100 km equivalent | 70–80 | 45–55 | 15–22 | 25–35 |
| MPGe Range | 30–40 | 50–60 | 110–140 | 70–90 |
| $\eta_{TTW}$ (%) | 20–25 | 30–40 | 75–85 | 50–60 |
| $\eta_{WTW}$ (%) Grid Average | 14–18 | 18–25 | 22–30 | 22–28 |
| $\eta_{WTW}$ (%) Renewable | 14–18 | 18–25 | 70–80 | 35–45 |
| CO₂ Emissions (g/km) | 150–200 | 90–120 | 50–100 (grid) / 0 (solar) | 0 (tailpipe) |
| Noise Level | High | Moderate | Very Low | Very Low |

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Well-to-Wheel Energy Flow Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["WELL: Crude Oil"] --> B1["Refinery Processing"]
    A2["WELL: Solar or Wind"] --> B2["Power Generation"]
    A3["WELL: Natural Gas or Electrolyser"] --> B3["Hydrogen Production"]

    B1 --> C1["Petrol Distribution"]
    B2 --> C2["Grid Transmission"]
    B3 --> C3["H2 Storage and Dispensing"]

    C1 --> D1["ICE Vehicle Tank"]
    C2 --> D2["EV Battery Pack"]
    C3 --> D3["Fuel Cell EV Tank"]

    D1 --> E1["ICE Engine Conversion"]
    D2 --> E2["Electric Motor and Drivetrain"]
    D3 --> E3["Fuel Cell Stack and Motor"]

    E1 --> F1["WHEEL: Kinetic Energy"]
    E2 --> F1
    E3 --> F1

    E1 -.->|Heat Loss 70 percent| G1["Waste Energy"]
    E2 -.->|Heat Loss 20 percent| G2["Waste Energy"]
    E3 -.->|Heat Loss 40 percent| G3["Waste Energy"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Each row represents a stage in the energy pathway. The thickness of arrows (visually, the focus) emphasizes that **ICE vehicles lose the majority of energy as heat at the engine stage**, whereas **EVs lose significantly less** in the motor/drivetrain stage. This is the structural reason EVs achieve higher Well-to-Wheel efficiency.

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: EV On-Board Fuel Efficiency Analysis Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph inputLayer["INPUT ENERGY DOMAIN"]
        in1["Grid Power Input kWh"]
        in2["Solar PV Direct Input kWh"]
    end

    subgraph conversionLayer["ON-BOARD CONVERSION SUBSYSTEM"]
        conv1["Battery Charging Controller"]

    end

    subgraph regenLayer["REGENERATIVE SUBSYSTEM"]
        reg1["Brake Pedal Sensor"]
        reg2["Motor acting as Generator"]
        reg3["Battery Charging from Regen"]
    end

    subgraph auxLayer["AUXILIARY LOAD DOMAIN"]
        aux1["HVAC Compressor and Blower"]
        aux2["Infotainment and Lighting"]
        aux3["Battery Thermal Management"]
    end

    subgraph outputLayer["VEHICLE OUTPUT DOMAIN"]
        out1["Wheel Torque and Speed"]
        out2["Distance Travelled km"]
        out3["Net Energy Consumption kWh per 100 km"]
        out4["Equivalent MPGe"]
    end

    in1 --> conv1
    in2 --> conv1
    conv1 --> conv2
    conv2 --> conv3
    conv3 --> conv4
    conv4 --> conv5
    conv5 --> out1
    out1 --> out2
    conv2 --> out3
    out2 --> out3
    out3 --> out4

    reg1 --> reg2 --> reg3 --> conv2
    aux1 --> conv2
    aux2 --> conv2
    aux3 --> conv2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Functional Block Explanation:** This architecture maps the **complete energy flow** inside an EV, separating traction energy, regenerative recovery, and auxiliary loads. The key takeaway is that **net energy consumption** is determined by subtracting regen and auxiliaries from the total battery discharge, then normalizing over distance.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model Question]**

**Q1. Define Tank-to-Wheel (TTW) efficiency and Well-to-Wheel (WTW) efficiency of an electric vehicle. Why is WTW considered a more holistic metric for fuel efficiency analysis?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **TTW efficiency** is the ratio of mechanical energy delivered to the wheels of the vehicle to the chemical or electrical energy stored on-board, typically 20–25 % for petrol ICE vehicles and 75–85 % for BEVs. **(1 mark)**
- **WTW efficiency** includes the energy losses in extraction, processing, and transportation of the primary fuel before it reaches the on-board storage, multiplied by TTW efficiency. **(1 mark)**
- WTW is more holistic because it captures the **complete cradle-to-road** energy chain, revealing the true impact of fuel source — for instance, an EV charged from solar achieves 70–80 % WTW, while the same EV on a coal-heavy grid achieves only 22–30 %. **(1 mark)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]**

**Q2. What is MPGe? Explain its significance in fuel efficiency comparison between EVs and ICE vehicles with the standard energy equivalence constant.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **MPGe (Miles Per Gallon Equivalent)** is a metric introduced by the US EPA that translates the electrical energy consumption of an EV into an equivalent gasoline-fuel economy figure, allowing direct comparison with ICE vehicles. **(1 mark)**
- The standard equivalence constant is **1 US gallon of gasoline ≈ 33.7 kWh** of energy. **(1 mark)**
- Significance: It allows consumers and regulators to intuitively compare an EV (e.g., 120 MPGe) with a petrol car (e.g., 35 MPG) using a familiar unit, despite fundamentally different energy carriers. It is the universally adopted regulatory metric. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Module 1, Slot X2]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the methodology of fuel efficiency analysis of an electric vehicle using the Well-to-Wheel framework. Compare the WTW efficiency of a petrol ICE car, a BEV charged from the Indian grid mix, and a BEV charged from 100 % solar energy, using suitable numerical values. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An EV consumes 22 kWh/100 km. Compute its (i) MPGe, (ii) operating cost per 100 km at an electricity tariff of ₹8/kWh, and (iii) CO₂ emissions per km assuming a grid emission factor of 0.71 kg CO₂/kWh. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — WTW Methodology and Comparison:**

The Well-to-Wheel analysis methodology involves the following structured steps:
1. **Define the energy pathway** — from primary source to wheel.
2. **Compute Well-to-Tank (WTT) efficiency** — extraction, refining/generation, transport.
3. **Compute Tank-to-Wheel (TTW) efficiency** — on-board conversion to kinetic energy.
4. **Multiply** $\eta_{WTT} \times \eta_{TTW}$ to obtain $\eta_{WTW}$.

**Numerical Comparison (assumption: Indian context):**

| Vehicle Type | $\eta_{TTW}$ | $\eta_{WTT}$ | $\eta_{WTW}$ |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Petrol ICE | 0.20 | 0.80 | **0.16 (16 %)** |
| BEV on Indian Grid | 0.77 | 0.35 | **0.27 (27 %)** |
| BEV on 100 % Solar | 0.77 | 0.95 | **0.73 (73 %)** |

**Evaluation:**

$$
\eta_{WTW}^{ICE} = 0.20 \times 0.80 = 0.16
$$

$$
\eta_{WTW}^{BEV,grid} = 0.77 \times 0.35 = 0.2695 \approx 0.27
$$

$$
\eta_{WTW}^{BEV,solar} = 0.77 \times 0.95 = 0.7315 \approx 0.73
$$

**Conclusion:** The BEV on solar is **~4.5× more efficient** than the petrol ICE; on the grid mix it is **~1.7× more efficient**. **(Full 7 marks — methodology 3 marks, computation 3 marks, interpretation 1 mark.)**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation:**

**Given:** $E_c = 22$ kWh/100 km, Tariff $P_{elec} = ₹8$/kWh, Grid factor $EF = 0.71$ kg CO₂/kWh, $1$ US gallon $\equiv 33.7$ kWh, $1$ km $= 0.62137$ miles.

**(i) MPGe Calculation:**

- 100 km = 62.137 miles
- 22 kWh per 100 km = 22 kWh per 62.137 miles

$$
E_c^{100mi} = \frac{22}{62.137} \times 100 = 35.41 \;\text{kWh/100 mi}
$$

$$
MPG_e = \frac{33.7}{35.41} \approx 95.2 \;\text{MPGe}
$$

**(ii) Operating Cost per 100 km:**

$$
C_{op} = 22 \;\text{kWh} \times ₹8/\text{kWh} = ₹176 \;\text{per 100 km}
$$

**(iii) CO₂ Emissions per km:**

$$
CO_2^{per\;km} = \frac{22 \times 0.71}{100} = \frac{15.62}{100} = 0.1562 \;\text{kg CO}_2/\text{km}
$$

**Final Answers:** **(i)** 95.2 MPGe, **(ii)** ₹176/100 km, **(iii)** 0.156 kg CO₂/km. **(Full 7 marks — 2 + 2 + 3 marks for each subpart.)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the energy flow in an electric vehicle from the grid to the wheels, identifying all major loss components. Discuss how regenerative braking improves the overall fuel efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An EV completes a 200 km trip. Battery discharge is 42 kWh. Regenerative braking recovers 6 kWh. Auxiliary loads (HVAC, lights, BMS) consume 3.2 kWh. Compute (i) net traction energy, (ii) kWh/100 km, (iii) MPGe, and (iv) operating cost at ₹7.5/kWh. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — Energy Flow and Regen Braking:**

**Block Diagram (textual representation, to be redrawn in exam):**

$$
\text{Grid} \rightarrow \text{Charger} \rightarrow \text{Battery} \rightarrow \text{Inverter} \rightarrow \text{Motor} \rightarrow \text{Transmission} \rightarrow \text{Wheels}
$$

**Loss components at each stage:**
- Charger: ~5–10 % (AC-DC conversion)
- Battery: ~5–10 % (internal resistance, SoC dependent)
- Inverter: ~3–5 % (switching losses)
- Motor: ~5–8 % (copper, iron, friction)
- Transmission: ~2–5 % (gear mesh, bearings)

**Total drivetrain efficiency:** $\eta_{drivetrain} \approx 0.75$ to $0.85$.

**Regenerative Braking Contribution:**
- During deceleration, motor acts as a generator, converting kinetic energy back to electrical energy.
- Typical regen efficiency: **60–80 %** of kinetic energy recovered.
- On urban drive cycles, regen can contribute **15–30 %** improvement in fuel efficiency.

**Mathematical Expression:**

$$
\eta_{regen} = \frac{E_{recovered}}{E_{kinetic,decel}} \times 100\,\%
$$

$$
\Delta E_{c} = \frac{E_{regen}}{d} \;\text{(reduction in net energy consumption per km)}
$$

**(Full 7 marks — diagram 2 marks, loss identification 3 marks, regen analysis 2 marks.)**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation:**

**Given:** $d = 200$ km, $E_{discharge} = 42$ kWh, $E_{regen} = 6$ kWh, $E_{aux} = 3.2$ kWh, Tariff = ₹7.5/kWh.

**(i) Net Traction Energy:**

$$
E_{net} = E_{discharge} - E_{regen} - E_{aux}
$$

$$
E_{net} = 42 - 6 - 3.2 = 32.8 \;\text{kWh}
$$

**(ii) kWh per 100 km:**

$$
E_c^{100} = \frac{32.8}{200} \times 100 = 16.4 \;\text{kWh/100 km}
$$

**(iii) MPGe:**

- 200 km = 124.27 miles

$$
E_c^{100mi} = \frac{32.8}{124.27} \times 100 = 26.39 \;\text{kWh/100 mi}
$$

$$
MPG_e = \frac{33.7}{26.39} \approx 127.7 \;\text{MPGe}
$$

**(iv) Operating Cost:**

$$
C_{op} = 32.8 \;\text{kWh} \times ₹7.5/\text{kWh} = ₹246
$$

**Final Answers:** **(i)** 32.8 kWh, **(ii)** 16.4 kWh/100 km, **(iii)** 127.7 MPGe, **(iv)** ₹246. **(Full 7 marks — 1.5 + 1.5 + 2 + 2 marks.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Unit Inversion Error:** Students frequently write $\frac{100}{E_c}$ instead of $\frac{33.7}{E_c^{100mi}}$ for MPGe — verify the denominator is in **kWh per 100 miles**, not per 100 km.
> 2. **Forgetting to Subtract Regen and Aux:** Net energy calculation must **always** be $E_{discharge} - E_{regen} - E_{aux}$. Direct use of $E_{discharge}$ inflates consumption figures and costs 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Confusing TTW with WTW:** TTW is on-board only; WTW multiplies TTW by WTT. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 4. **Forgetting to Convert km to miles:** For MPGe, **100 km ≠ 100 miles**. Always multiply km by 0.62137 to get miles.
> 5. **Not Labelling Units:** A formula without units is incomplete in KTU valuation — always append kWh, km, %, ₹/kWh explicitly.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Fuel Efficiency Analysis (KTU Module 1):**

- **Core Definition:** Fuel efficiency in EVs is measured as **kWh/100 km** or **MPGe**, not litres/100 km.
- **Key Equivalence Constant:** **1 US gallon of petrol = 33.7 kWh**; **1 litre of petrol ≈ 8.9 kWh**.
- **TTW Efficiency:** Petrol ICE ≈ **20–25 %**; BEV ≈ **75–85 %**.
- **WTW Efficiency:** Petrol ICE ≈ **14–18 %**; BEV on Indian grid ≈ **22–30 %**; BEV on solar ≈ **70–80 %**.
- **MPGe Formula:** $MPG_e = \dfrac{33.7}{E_c^{100mi}}$; remember to convert km to miles first.
- **Net Energy Equation:** $E_{net} = E_{discharge} - E_{regen} - E_{aux}$.
- **Regenerative Braking:** Recovers **60–80 %** of kinetic energy during deceleration, improving urban cycle efficiency by **15–30 %**.
- **Drive Cycles:** FTP-75 (US), NEDC (older EU), WLTP (current global), IDC (Indian) — always state the cycle when reporting efficiency.
- **Auxiliary Loads:** HVAC is the dominant aux load (1–3 kW typical); must be included in net consumption.
- **Renewable Impact:** WTW efficiency of an EV scales with grid renewable penetration — **the cleaner the grid, the more efficient the EV**.
- **CO₂ Emission Factor:** Indian grid ≈ **0.71 kg CO₂/kWh**; solar PV ≈ **0.04–0.08 kg CO₂/kWh** lifecycle.
- **Operating Cost Formula:** $C_{op} = E_{net} \times \text{Tariff} \times \dfrac{d}{100}$ ₹/100 km.
- **Exam Tip:** Always present efficiency comparisons in a **table** with $\eta_{TTW}$, $\eta_{WTT}$, and $\eta_{WTW}$ columns for clarity and full marks.
- **Avoid:** Confusing efficiency (dimensionless ratio) with consumption (kWh/100 km) — they are **inverse** concepts.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DC Drives - Motoring using a PM DC Machine - DC Motor Electric Drive using DC-DC Converter - Generating/Braking using a PM DC Machine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET742 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES AND RENEWABLE TECHNOLOGIES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** DC Drives - Motoring using a PM DC Machine - DC Motor Electric Drive using DC-DC Converter - Generating/Braking using a PM DC Machine # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is a Permanent Magnet (PM) DC Machine? A **Permanent Magnet DC (PMDC) Machine** is a self-excited DC machine in which the field flux **Φ** is produced by high-energy permanent magnets (typically Neodymium-Iron-Boron or Samarium-Cobalt) instead of conventional field winding excitation. Since there is no field winding, the flux **Φ remains constant** throughout the operating range, simplifying the machine's mathematical model considerably. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition:** A PM DC motor is a DC motor that uses permanent magnets to produce the field flux. The absence of field copper losses, brush losses in the field circuit, and the elimination of separate field power supply makes the PMDC motor highly efficient and compact — a primary reason for its use in **Electric Two-Wheelers, E-Bikes, and small EV traction applications**. The **armature circuit equivalent** of a PMDC machine is shown conceptually as a back-EMF source in series with armature resistance: $$V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$$ where $V_a$ is the applied armature voltage, $E_b$ is the back-EMF, $I_a$ is the armature current, and $R_a$ is the armature resistance. ## 1.2 What is an Electric "Drive" (DC Drive)? An **Electric Drive** is an electronic/electromechanical system used to control the speed, torque, and direction of an electric motor. For a PMDC machine, the drive typically consists of: 1. **Power Converter** (DC-DC chopper / inverter) 2. **Controller / PWM Generator** (logic-level signal generation) 3. **Sensors** (current, voltage, speed/position feedback) 4. **Protection Circuitry** (over-current, over-voltage, thermal) > [!NOTE] > In an EV context, the **battery is the DC source**, and the **DC-DC converter acts as the "accelerator pedal of electrons"** — controlling how much energy flows from the battery to the motor. ## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — The "Water Tap" Model Imagine a **water tap connected to a tank and a water wheel**: | Drive Element | Water Analogy | |---|---| | Battery (Source) | Water tank | | DC-DC Chopper | The tap (valve) | | PMDC Motor | Water wheel | | Armature Current $I_a$ | Water flow rate | | Torque $T$ | Force turning the wheel | | Speed $\omega$ | Rotational speed of the wheel | | Vehicle Load | Resistance on the wheel | - **Opening the tap more (higher duty cycle $D$):** More water flows → wheel spins faster → vehicle accelerates (MOTORING). - **Closing the tap:** Water stops → wheel slows (COASTING / FREE RUN). - **Forcing the wheel backward (descending a hill):** Water is pushed *back* into the tank → energy recovery (GENERATING / REGENERATIVE BRAKING). - **Sudden braking with tap closed & resistor in path:** Wheel pushes water against a "brake resistor" dissipating heat (DYNAMIC BRAKING). > [!TIP] > **Intuition in one line:** *Motoring = Battery pushes the motor. Braking/Generating = Motor pushes the battery (or a resistor).* ## 1.4 The Four Operating Modes of a DC Drive A PMDC machine operating through a DC-DC converter can function in **four quadrants** of the speed-torque plane: | Quadrant | Speed $\omega$ | Torque $T$ | Mode | Power Flow | |---|---|---|---|---| | **I** | Forward (+ve) | Forward (+ve) | Forward Motoring | Battery → Motor | | **II** | Forward (+ve) | Reverse (−ve) | Forward Braking / Regeneration | Motor → Battery | | **III** | Reverse (−ve) | Reverse (−ve) | Reverse Motoring | Battery → Motor | | **IV** | Reverse (−ve) | Forward (+ve) | Reverse Braking / Regeneration | Motor → Battery | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Motoring** occurs in **Quadrants I and III**. **Generating/Braking** occurs in **Quadrants II and IV**. This classification is a **favourite KTU question**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Governing Equations of a PMDC Machine Because the field flux $\Phi$ is **constant** in a PMDC machine (no field control), the variables reduce to two: $V_a$ (control input) and $I_a$ (response). ### (a) Back-EMF Equation When the armature rotates in the magnetic field, an EMF is induced (Faraday's Law) opposing the applied voltage: $$E_b = K_e \cdot \Phi \cdot \omega \;\;\;\text{[Volts]}$$ For PMDC, since $\Phi$ is constant, we absorb it into the constant: $$E_b = K_m \cdot \omega \;\;\;\text{where } K_m = K_e \Phi \text{ (V·s/rad)}$$ ### (b) Armature Voltage Equation (KVL around the armature loop) $$V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$$ ### (c) Electromagnetic Torque Equation $$T_e = K_t \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a = K_m \cdot I_a \;\;\;\text{[N·m]}$$ > In SI units, $K_e = K_t$ (numerically equal), both with units **V·s/rad = N·m/A**. ### (d) Speed Equation (Solving for $\omega$) $$\omega = \frac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K_m}$$ ### (e) Speed-Torque Characteristic (Mechanical Equation) The mechanical load satisfies $T_e - T_L = J \dfrac{d\omega}{dt}$. In **steady-state**, $T_e = T_L$: $$\omega = \frac{V_a}{K_m} - \frac{R_a}{(K_m)^2}\,T_e$$ This is a **straight line** with: - **No-load speed:** $\omega_0 = V_a / K_m$ - **Slope:** $-R_a / K_m^2$ (negative — speed drops as load torque increases) - **Stall torque:** $T_{stall} = V_a \cdot K_m / R_a$ ## 2.2 Power Flow Analysis | Power Component | Expression | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---| | **Electrical input power** | $P_{in} = V_a I_a$ | Power drawn from battery | | **Mechanical developed power** | $P_{mech} = T_e \cdot \omega = E_b I_a$ | Useful mechanical power | | **Armature copper loss** | $P_{cu} = I_a^2 R_a$ | Heat loss in $R_a$ | | **Power balance** | $V_a I_a = E_b I_a + I_a^2 R_a$ | KVL × current | ## 2.3 DC-DC Converter (Chopper) Drive — Theory of Operation A **DC-DC chopper** is a static power electronic device that converts a fixed DC input voltage $V_s$ (battery) into a variable DC output voltage $V_a$ (motor armature) by **chopping** the input at a high switching frequency $f_s$. **Key Parameters:** - Switching period: $T_s = 1/f_s$ - On-time: $T_{on}$ - Off-time: $T_{off}$ - **Duty cycle:** $D = T_{on}/T_s$, with $0 \le D \le 1$ ### (a) Buck (Step-Down) Chopper — For Forward Motoring Used when the required motor voltage $V_a < V_s$: $$V_a = D \cdot V_s$$ $$I_{a,\text{avg}} = \dfrac{V_s - E_b}{R_a}\cdot D$$ ### (b) Boost (Step-Up) Chopper — For Regenerative Braking Used to **push current back** into a higher-voltage battery during regeneration: $$V_s = (1-D)\,V_a \quad\Rightarrow\quad V_a = \dfrac{V_s}{1-D}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > During **regenerative braking**, the motor acts as a generator with $E_b > V_s$. The boost chopper steps up the motor's terminal voltage to exceed the battery voltage so current can flow *back* into the source. ### (c) Two-Quadrant Chopper (Class A or Class B) - **Class A (Two-Quadrant I & IV):** Uses a chopper with **two switches and two diodes**. Average output voltage can be **positive or negative**; current is unidirectional. - **Class B (Two-Quadrant I & II):** Voltage is unidirectional; current is bidirectional (used for motoring + regenerative braking of one rotation direction). ### (d) Four-Quadrant (H-Bridge) Chopper Uses **four switches** (e.g., IGBTs/MOSFETs S1, S2, S3, S4) arranged in an **H-bridge** with the motor in the middle. By selective switching: | S1 | S2 | S3 | S4 | Mode | |---|---|---|---|---| | ON | OFF | OFF | ON | Forward Motoring (Q-I) | | OFF | ON | ON | OFF | Reverse Motoring (Q-III) | | ON | OFF | ON | OFF | Forward Braking/Regen (Q-II) | | OFF | ON | OFF | ON | Reverse Braking/Regen (Q-IV) | ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$ | Armature KVL | V | | 2 | $E_b = K_m \omega$ | Back-EMF | V | | 3 | $T_e = K_m I_a$ | Electromagnetic torque | N·m | | 4 | $\omega = \dfrac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K_m}$ | Speed under load | rad/s | | 5 | $\omega_0 = \dfrac{V_a}{K_m}$ | No-load speed | rad/s | | 6 | $T_{stall} = \dfrac{V_a K_m}{R_a}$ | Starting / stall torque | N·m | | 7 | $P_{mech} = T_e \omega$ | Developed mechanical power | W | | 8 | $P_{in} = V_a I_a$ | Electrical input power | W | | 9 | $P_{cu} = I_a^2 R_a$ | Copper loss | W | | 10 | $V_a = D \cdot V_s$ | Buck chopper output | V | | 11 | $V_a = \dfrac{V_s}{1-D}$ | Boost chopper output | V | | 12 | $\eta_{\text{drive}} = \dfrac{P_{mech}}{P_{in}}$ | Drive efficiency | – | | 13 | $E_{\text{regen}} = \int T_e \omega\,dt$ | Energy recovered in braking | J | | 14 | $\Delta\omega = -\dfrac{R_a\,T_L}{(K_m)^2}$ | Speed regulation | rad/s | ## 2.5 Real-World Utility in EV Engineering - **Battery-fed PMDC drives** are widely used in **low-power EVs (e-rickshaws, e-scooters, e-bikes)** due to simple control, low cost, high starting torque, and absence of field copper losses. - **DC-DC chopper** decouples the fixed battery voltage (~48 V / 72 V) from the variable motor terminal voltage, allowing smooth torque/speed profiling. - **Regenerative braking** using a boost chopper extends the EV range by 5–15% in urban stop-go cycles. - **Plugging** is used as a **fail-safe emergency brake** (e.g., in elevators, hoists) — though it is energy-wasteful. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Speed–Torque Characteristic of a PMDC Motor **Starting Point:** Armature KVL. $$V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$$ **Step 1 — Replace $E_b$ using the EMF equation:** $$V_a = K_m \omega + I_a R_a$$ **Step 2 — Solve for $I_a$ in terms of $\omega$:** $$I_a = \frac{V_a - K_m \omega}{R_a}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute $I_a$ into the torque equation $T_e = K_m I_a$:** $$T_e = K_m \cdot \frac{V_a - K_m \omega}{R_a} = \frac{K_m V_a - K_m^2 \omega}{R_a}$$ **Step 4 — Rearrange to express $\omega$ as a function of $T_e$:** $$K_m^2 \omega = K_m V_a - T_e R_a$$ $$\boxed{\;\omega = \frac{V_a}{K_m} - \frac{R_a}{K_m^2}\,T_e\;}$$ **Physical Interpretation:** - The first term $V_a / K_m$ is the **no-load speed** $\omega_0$ (the intercept on the $\omega$-axis). - The second term is the **speed drop** proportional to load torque. - The slope of this line is $-R_a / K_m^2$ — a **steeper drop** occurs for machines with **higher armature resistance** or **lower machine constant**. > [!NOTE] > **Energy check:** The mechanical power developed at any operating point is $P_{mech} = T_e \cdot \omega = K_m I_a \cdot \omega = E_b I_a$, which is consistent with the energy balance $P_{in} = P_{mech} + P_{cu}$. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Average Output Voltage of a Buck Chopper Driving a PMDC Motor **Circuit Assumption:** A buck chopper feeds a PMDC motor (inductive load) such that the armature current $I_a$ is **continuous** (ripple smoothed by armature inductance $L_a$). **Step 1 — When switch S is ON (duration $T_{on} = D T_s$):** The diode is reverse-biased. The source $V_s$ is applied directly to the motor: $$v_a = V_s \quad \text{for } 0 \le t \le D T_s$$ **Step 2 — When switch S is OFF (duration $T_{off} = (1-D) T_s$):** Current freewheels through the diode. Assuming continuous conduction, $v_a \approx 0$ (ideal diode drop ignored): $$v_a = 0 \quad \text{for } D T_s \le t \le T_s$$ **Step 3 — Average output voltage over one period:** $$V_{a,\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{T_s}\left[\int_0^{D T_s} V_s\, dt + \int_{D T_s}^{T_s} 0\, dt\right]$$ $$V_{a,\text{avg}} = \frac{V_s \cdot D T_s}{T_s}$$ $$\boxed{\;V_{a,\text{avg}} = D \cdot V_s\;}$$ **Step 4 — Average armature current (steady-state, continuous conduction):** Applying $V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$ in averaged form: $$D V_s = K_m \omega + I_{a,\text{avg}} R_a$$ $$I_{a,\text{avg}} = \frac{D V_s - K_m \omega}{R_a}$$ **Step 5 — Developed torque:** $$T_e = K_m I_{a,\text{avg}} = K_m \cdot \frac{D V_s - K_m \omega}{R_a}$$ $$\boxed{\;T_e = \frac{K_m D V_s}{R_a} - \frac{K_m^2}{R_a}\omega\;}$$ **Key Insight:** By **varying $D$** (via PWM), we effectively **vary $V_a$** and hence shift the entire speed-torque line **parallel to itself** along the $\omega$-axis — this is the basis of **armature voltage control** of PMDC drives. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Regenerative Braking Energy Recovery **Scenario:** An EV of mass $M$ moving at velocity $v$ begins to brake. The motor operates as a generator with $E_b > V_s$, and a **boost chopper** pumps the current back into the battery. **Step 1 — Kinetic energy of the vehicle:** $$E_k = \frac{1}{2} M v^2 \quad \text{[J]}$$ **Step 2 — Power developed by the motor as generator:** With $T_{brake}$ acting opposite to motion: $$P_{\text{gen}} = T_{brake} \cdot \omega = E_b \cdot I_a \quad \text{[W]}$$ **Step 3 — Power actually recovered into the battery (boost chopper efficiency $\eta_{ch}$):** $$P_{recovered} = \eta_{ch} \cdot (E_b I_a - I_a^2 R_a) = \eta_{ch}\cdot V_s I_{a,\text{batt}}$$ where $I_{a,\text{batt}}$ is the current flowing into the battery. **Step 4 — Total recoverable energy (assuming $v$ drops from $v_1$ to $v_2$):** $$\boxed{\;E_{recovered} = \eta_{ch}\cdot \int_{t_1}^{t_2} \bigl(E_b I_a - I_a^2 R_a\bigr)\, dt\;}$$ **Numerical Example (KTU-style):** For $M = 500$ kg, $v_1 = 60$ km/h = 16.67 m/s, $v_2 = 30$ km/h = 8.33 m/s: $$E_k(60) = \frac{1}{2}\cdot 500\cdot (16.67)^2 = 69{,}444\;\text{J}$$ $$E_k(30) = \frac{1}{2}\cdot 500\cdot (8.33)^2 = 17{,}361\;\text{J}$$ $$\Delta E_k = 52{,}083\;\text{J} \approx 52\;\text{kJ}$$ If $\eta_{ch} = 0.85$, the recoverable energy ≈ **44.3 kJ**, equivalent to extending the EV range significantly. ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Plugging (Reverse-Current) Braking In **plugging**, the armature terminals are **reversed** while the motor is running.
