> The Clarke transform is **independent of rotor position** — it only depends on the spatial geometry of the stator windings (120° apart).

### Pillar 2 — Park Transformation (Stationary $\rightarrow$ Rotating Frame, $\alpha\beta \rightarrow dq$)

The $\alpha\beta$ frame is rotated by the rotor electrical angle $\theta_e$ to align the $d$-axis with the rotor flux.

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_d &= i_\alpha \cos\theta_e + i_\beta \sin\theta_e \\
i_q &= -i_\alpha \sin\theta_e + i_\beta \cos\theta_e
\end{aligned}
$$

Matrix form:
$$
\begin{bmatrix} i_d \\ i_q \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_e & \sin\theta_e \\ -\sin\theta_e & \cos\theta_e \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} i_\alpha \\ i_\beta \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Pillar 3 — Inverse Park & Inverse Clarke (Back to 3-Phase PWM)

After the controller generates reference voltages $V_d^*$ and $V_q^*$:
1. **Inverse Park** → $V_\alpha^*, V_\beta^*$
2. **Inverse Clarke** → $V_a^*, V_b^*, V_c^*$
3. **SVPWM (Space Vector Modulation)** → gate signals for the 3-phase inverter IGBTs

---

## 2.2 Why FOC Decouples Torque and Flux

For a PMSM, the torque equation in the $dq$-frame is:

$$
T_e = \frac{3}{2}\,P\,\left[\lambda_{pm}\,i_q + (L_d - L_q)\,i_d\,i_q\right]
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Decoupling Insight**:
> - $i_d$ directly controls the air-gap flux linkage: $\lambda_d = L_d i_d + \lambda_{pm}$
> - $i_q$ directly controls the torque (assuming $L_d = L_q$ for surface PMSM)
> - Because $i_d$ and $i_q$ are orthogonal, **changing $i_q$ does NOT change the flux, and vice versa**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready)

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $V_d = R_s i_d + L_d \dfrac{di_d}{dt} - \omega_e L_q i_q$ | $d$-axis stator voltage (PMSM) | V |
| 2 | $V_q = R_s i_q + L_q \dfrac{di_q}{dt} + \omega_e (L_d i_d + \lambda_{pm})$ | $q$-axis stator voltage (PMSM) | V |
| 3 | $T_e = \dfrac{3}{2} P \left[ \lambda_{pm} i_q + (L_d - L_q) i_d i_q \right]$ | Electromagnetic torque | N·m |
| 4 | $T_e - T_L = J \dfrac{d\omega_m}{dt} + B\omega_m$ | Mechanical dynamics (shaft) | N·m |
| 5 | $\theta_e = P \int \omega_m \, dt$ | Electrical-to-mechanical angle relation | rad |
| 6 | $\omega_e = P \cdot \omega_m$ | Electrical speed in rad/s | rad/s |
| 7 | $i_{\alpha,\beta} = \dfrac{2}{3}\left(i_a + a i_b + a^2 i_c\right)$ | Space vector definition (where $a = e^{j2\pi/3}$) | A |
| 8 | $\dfrac{2}{3}\left(V_d i_d + V_q i_q\right) = P_{in}$ | Input electrical power | W |
| 9 | $\eta = \dfrac{T_e \omega_m}{P_{in}}$ | Drive efficiency (FOC typically **> 95%**) | % |
| 10 | $BW_{current} \approx \dfrac{R_s}{2 \pi L_d}$ | Current loop bandwidth (design rule) | Hz |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip**: When asked to "derive the FOC equations", examiners almost always expect you to start from the **3-phase stator voltage equations**, apply the **Clarke transform**, then apply the **Park transform**, and arrive at the $d$-$q$ axis equations shown above (Eq. 1, 2, 3).

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

| Application | Why FOC is used |
| :--- | :--- |
| **EV Traction (Tesla Model 3, Nissan Leaf, Tata Nexon EV)** | Instantaneous torque response, high efficiency at low speeds, smooth regenerative braking |
| **Industrial Servo Drives (CNC, Robotics)** | Sub-millisecond position tracking, zero steady-state error |
| **HVAC Compressors** | Variable torque at variable speeds with high part-load efficiency |
| **Wind Turbine Generators (PMSG)** | Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT) by controlling $i_d$ |
| **Elevator Drives** | Jerk-free start/stop, holds load at zero speed without braking |
| **Electric Aircraft (eVTOL Propellers)** | Redundant, fault-tolerant, ultra-high reliability |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For PEAET742 KTU 2024**: Focus on **PMSM-based FOC for EV traction** — this is the most heavily weighted topic in Module 2 of the syllabus.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: 3-Phase Stator Voltage $\rightarrow$ $dq$-Frame Voltage Equations

### Step 1 — Start with the 3-Phase PMSM Stator Voltage Equations

For a balanced 3-phase Y-connected PMSM, the per-phase stator voltage equations in the natural $abc$ frame are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_a &= R_s i_a + \frac{d\lambda_a}{dt} \\
v_b &= R_s i_b + \frac{d\lambda_b}{dt} \\
v_c &= R_s i_c + \frac{d\lambda_c}{dt}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\lambda_a, \lambda_b, \lambda_c$ are the phase flux linkages. The flux linkage of each phase depends on the *self-inductance* ($L_{aa}, L_{bb}, L_{cc}$) of that phase, the *mutual-inductances* between phases, and the *rotor permanent magnet flux* ($\lambda_{pm}$).

**Writing this compactly for all three phases in vector form:**

$$
\vec{v}_{abc} = R_s \vec{i}_{abc} + \frac{d\vec{\lambda}_{abc}}{dt}
$$

### Step 2 — Express the Phase Flux Linkages

The flux linkage of any phase (say, phase $a$) is the sum of the stator-current-induced flux and the permanent-magnet-induced flux:

$$
\lambda_a = L_{aa} i_a + L_{ab} i_b + L_{ac} i_c + \lambda_{pm} \cos(\theta_e)
$$

For a *non-salient* surface-mounted PMSM, $L_{aa} = L_{bb} = L_{cc} = L_s$ and $L_{ab} = L_{ac} = L_{bc} = M$. Using $i_a + i_b + i_c = 0$, the flux linkage simplifies to:

$$
\lambda_a = (L_s - M) i_a + \lambda_{pm} \cos(\theta_e)
$$

Define the **differential inductance** $L = L_s - M$. So:

$$
\lambda_a = L i_a + \lambda_{pm} \cos(\theta_e)
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Clarke Transformation ($abc \rightarrow \alpha\beta$)

Using the amplitude-invariant Clarke matrix:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} v_\alpha \\ v_\beta \end{bmatrix} = \frac{2}{3}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -\tfrac{1}{2} & -\tfrac{1}{2} \\ 0 & \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} & -\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v_a \\ v_b \\ v_c \end{bmatrix}
$$

Apply the same to currents and flux linkages. After the Clarke transform, the $\alpha\beta$ equations become:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_\alpha &= R_s i_\alpha + \frac{d\lambda_\alpha}{dt} \\
v_\beta &= R_s i_\beta + \frac{d\lambda_\beta}{dt}
\end{aligned}
$$

The flux linkages in the $\alpha\beta$ frame are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda_\alpha &= L i_\alpha + \lambda_{pm} \cos(\theta_e) \\
\lambda_\beta &= L i_\beta + \lambda_{pm} \sin(\theta_e)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why does the rotor magnet flux appear with $\cos\theta_e$ and $\sin\theta_e$?**
Because the $\alpha$-axis is fixed in the stator, and the rotor magnet physically rotates at $\theta_e$ — the projection of the magnet flux on the stationary $\alpha\beta$ axes varies sinusoidally with rotor position.

### Step 4 — Apply the Park Transformation ($\alpha\beta \rightarrow dq$)

We rotate the $\alpha\beta$ frame by $\theta_e$ to align the $d$-axis with the rotor flux:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} v_d \\ v_q \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_e & \sin\theta_e \\ -\sin\theta_e & \cos\theta_e \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v_\alpha \\ v_\beta \end{bmatrix}
$$

Applying this rotation, the cross-coupling terms arising from the time derivative of the sinusoidal flux linkages produce **speed-voltage terms** ($\omega_e L i_q$ and $\omega_e \lambda_{pm}$). After algebraic simplification, the **final $dq$-frame voltage equations** are:

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
v_d &= R_s i_d + L_d \frac{di_d}{dt} - \omega_e L_q i_q \\
v_q &= R_s i_q + L_q \frac{di_q}{dt} + \omega_e (L_d i_d + \lambda_{pm})
\end{aligned}
}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Key Point**: The terms $-\omega_e L_q i_q$ and $\omega_e L_d i_d$ are called the **cross-coupling terms** (or **back-EMF terms**). In practical FOC, these are cancelled by **feed-forward decoupling** so that $i_d$ and $i_q$ loops become truly independent.

### Step 5 — Derive the Torque Equation in the $dq$-Frame

The electromagnetic power (air-gap power) in the $dq$-frame is:

$$
P_{em} = \frac{3}{2}\left(v_d i_d + v_q i_q\right)
$$

Substituting the voltage equations (ignoring the resistive copper loss, which is dissipated as heat, not converted to mechanical power):

$$
P_{em,\,mech} = \frac{3}{2}\,\omega_e\,\left[\lambda_{pm}\,i_q + (L_d - L_q)\,i_d\,i_q\right]
$$

Dividing by the mechanical speed $\omega_m = \omega_e / P$ (where $P$ is the number of pole pairs):

$$
\boxed{
T_e = \frac{3}{2}\,P\,\left[\lambda_{pm}\,i_q + (L_d - L_q)\,i_d\,i_q\right]
}
$$

**Special cases**:
- **Surface PMSM** ($L_d = L_q = L_s$): $T_e = \dfrac{3}{2} P\, \lambda_{pm}\, i_q$ — purely linear in $i_q$.
- **Interior PMSM (IPM)** ($L_d \neq L_q$): The $(L_d - L_q)i_d i_q$ term adds **reluctance torque**, exploited by **MTPA (Maximum Torque Per Ampere)** strategies.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: PI Controller Design for FOC Current Loop

The plant transfer function for the $d$-axis (or $q$-axis) current loop, after decoupling, is a first-order RL circuit:

$$
G_p(s) = \frac{1}{L_d s + R_s}
$$

A **PI compensator** in standard form:

$$
G_c(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} = K_p \left(1 + \frac{1}{T_i s}\right), \quad T_i = \frac{K_p}{K_i}
$$

**Pole-zero cancellation** is achieved by setting $T_i = L_d / R_s$, which reduces the open-loop transfer function to a pure integrator with proportional gain $K_p / R_s$:

$$
G_{ol}(s) = \frac{K_p / R_s}{s}
$$

The **closed-loop bandwidth** becomes:

$$
\omega_{bw} = \frac{K_p}{R_s} \quad \Rightarrow \quad K_p = \omega_{bw} \cdot L_d
$$

And:

$$
K_i = \frac{K_p R_s}{L_d} = \omega_{bw} \cdot R_s
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Design Rule (used in commercial EV drives)**: Choose $\omega_{bw} \approx \dfrac{1}{5}\,\omega_{sw}$, where $\omega_{sw}$ is the inverter switching frequency (typically 10–20 kHz for EV traction inverters). This gives a current loop bandwidth of **2–4 kHz**, which is fast enough to track torque commands within **0.25–0.5 ms**.

| Design Step | Formula | Typical EV Value (PMSM) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Switching frequency | $f_{sw}$ | **10 kHz** |
| Current loop bandwidth | $\omega_{bw} = 2\pi f_{sw} / 5$ | **12,566 rad/s (2 kHz)** |
| $K_p$ gain | $K_p = \omega_{bw} L_d$ | **0.05 (if $L_d$ = 4 mH)** |
| $K_i$ gain | $K_i = \omega_{bw} R_s$ | **377 (if $R_s$ = 30 m$\Omega$)** |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 FOC Block Diagram — Functional Architecture (Mermaid Flow)

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram is a **block-level functional architecture** showing the complete FOC signal chain for an EV traction PMSM drive.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    speedCmd["Speed Command omega m star from Accelerator Pedal"]
    speedMeas["Mechanical Speed Sensor Encoder or Resolver"]
    thetaCalc["theta e equals P times integral of omega m"]
    
    piSpeed["PI Speed Controller outer loop"]
    iqRef["iq star torque producing reference"]
    idRef["id star flux reference often zero for MTPA"]
    
    iaMeas["Phase A Current Sensor"]
    ibMeas["Phase B Current Sensor"]
    icMeas["Phase C Current Sensor"]
    
    clarke["Clarke Transform abc to alpha beta"]
    park["Park Transform alpha beta to dq"]
    
    piD["PI d axis current controller"]
    piQ["PI q axis current controller"]
    decouple["Cross coupling decoupling omega e Lq id and omega e Ld iq"]
    
    invPark["Inverse Park Transform dq to alpha beta"]
    invClarke["Inverse Clarke Transform alpha beta to abc"]
    
    svpwm["SVPWM Space Vector Modulator"]
    inverter["Three Phase Voltage Source Inverter IGBT or SiC"]
    pmsm["PMSM Traction Motor"]
    
    speedCmd --> piSpeed
    speedMeas --> thetaCalc
    thetaCalc --> park
    thetaCalc --> invPark
    piSpeed --> iqRef
    iqRef --> piQ
    idRef --> piD
    
    iaMeas --> clarke
    ibMeas --> clarke
    icMeas --> clarke
    clarke --> park
    park --> piD
    park --> piQ
    
    piD --> decouple
    piQ --> decouple
    decouple --> invPark
    invPark --> invClarke
    invClarke --> svpwm
    svpwm --> inverter
    inverter --> pmsm
    pmsm --> speedMeas
    pmsm --> iaMeas
    pmsm --> ibMeas
    pmsm --> icMeas
```

**Reading the diagram**:
- **Outer loop (yellow/orange, top)**: Speed control — the accelerator pedal demand is compared with measured rotor speed. The PI speed controller outputs the torque-producing current reference $i_q^*$.
- **Middle loop (green)**: Current control — two independent PI controllers regulate $i_d$ and $i_q$.
- **Inner loop (blue)**: Cross-coupling decoupling — cancels the back-EMF terms so the $d$/$q$ loops are independent.
- **Output stage (purple)**: Inverse Park → Inverse Clarke → SVPWM → IGBT inverter → PMSM.
- **Feedback path (red arrows)**: Phase currents and rotor position are measured by Hall sensors, shunts, and an encoder/resolver.

---

## 4.2 Space Vector Modulation (SVM) Reference Hexagon

The SVPWM modulator outputs 8 possible voltage vectors from the inverter. The reference voltage vector $\vec{V}_{ref}$ is synthesized as a time-weighted average of two adjacent active vectors ($\vec{V}_1, \vec{V}_2$) and the zero vectors ($\vec{V}_0, \vec{V}_7$).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    v0["V0 Zero Vector 000"]
    v1["V1 Active 100"]
    v2["V2 Active 110"]
    v3["V3 Active 010"]
    v4["V4 Active 011"]
    v5["V5 Active 001"]
    v6["V6 Active 101"]
    v7["V7 Zero Vector 111"]
    
    sector1["Sector 1 0 to 60 deg"]
    sector2["Sector 2 60 to 120 deg"]
    sector3["Sector 3 120 to 180 deg"]
    sector4["Sector 4 180 to 240 deg"]
    sector5["Sector 5 240 to 300 deg"]
    sector6["Sector 6 300 to 360 deg"]
    
    v0 --- sector1
    v1 --- sector1
    v2 --- sector1
    v1 --- sector2
    v2 --- sector2
    v3 --- sector2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SVM vs. SPWM**: SVPWM provides **15.5% higher DC-bus utilization** (max line-to-line voltage reaches $V_{dc}/\sqrt{3}$ instead of $V_{dc}/2$) and lower current harmonics — a major reason SVPWM is the default in EV traction inverters.

---

## 4.3 FOC vs. Scalar (V/f) Control — Conceptual Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SCALAR["Scalar V/f Control"]
        S1["Open loop V/f table"]
        S2["No current feedback"]
        S3["Poor low speed torque"]
        S4["Slip dependent"]
    end
    
    subgraph FOC["Field Oriented Control FOC"]
        F1["Closed loop d-q current control"]
        F2["Current and position feedback"]
        F3["Excellent low speed torque"]
        F4["Independent flux and torque"]
    end
    
    SCALAR -.->|inferior for EV| FOC
```

| Parameter | Scalar V/f | FOC Vector Control |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Speed range | 1:10 | **1:1000+** |
| Torque response | 50–100 ms | **< 1 ms** |
| Low-speed torque | Poor | **Excellent** |
| Position control | Not possible | **Possible** |
| Efficiency at part load | 70–85% | **92–97%** |
| Computational cost | Very low | **High (DSP/FPGA required)** |
| Use in EV | Auxiliary pumps | **Main traction motor** |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define Field Oriented Control (FOC) of a PMSM. Mention two key advantages of FOC over scalar V/f control in EV applications.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition** (1 mark): Field Oriented Control (FOC), also called vector control, is a technique to control three-phase AC motors (PMSM/IM) by transforming stator currents into a synchronously rotating $dq$-reference frame aligned with the rotor flux, so that the direct-axis current $i_d$ controls flux and the quadrature-axis current $i_q$ controls torque independently.

> **Advantages** (2 marks, 1 each):
> 1. **Decoupled torque and flux control** — enables instantaneous, precise torque response, critical for EV acceleration and regenerative braking.
> 2. **Wide constant-torque operating region from zero speed** — essential for low-speed hill-start and traffic creep, which scalar V/f cannot achieve without slip compensation.

---

### **Q2. What is the role of the Clarke and Park transformations in FOC? Why is rotor position feedback essential?** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Clarke Transform** (1 mark): Converts balanced 3-phase stator currents/voltages ($i_a, i_b, i_c$) into a 2-phase stationary orthogonal frame ($i_\alpha, i_\beta$). This reduces three coupled AC signals to two stationary phasors.

> **Park Transform** (1 mark): Rotates the stationary $\alpha\beta$ frame by the rotor electrical angle $\theta_e$ to produce $i_d$ (flux component) and $i_q$ (torque component) in a frame that is **stationary from the controller's perspective** (becomes DC quantities).

> **Need for rotor position** (1 mark): The Park transform requires the exact rotor electrical angle $\theta_e$ in real time. Without precise rotor position feedback (resolver, encoder, or sensorless estimator), the $dq$-frame is misaligned, the $d$/$q$ axes are no longer decoupled, and the drive becomes unstable — making position feedback the *sine qua non* of FOC.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — KTU Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 scheme regulations, Part B questions offer an internal choice. Both alternatives carry equal marks (14) and are graded at the same cognitive level (Bloom's L3–L5).

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — FOC Fundamentals and Torque Equation

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> **(a)** Derive the electromagnetic torque equation of a three-phase PMSM in the $dq$-reference frame. Clearly state all assumptions. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Explain with a block diagram the working of a complete FOC scheme for an EV traction PMSM drive. Label all transformations and controller blocks. (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Stator voltage equations in the $abc$ frame** (1 mark):
For a balanced 3-phase Y-connected PMSM, the per-phase voltage equations are:
$$
v_a = R_s i_a + \frac{d\lambda_a}{dt}, \quad v_b = R_s i_b + \frac{d\lambda_b}{dt}, \quad v_c = R_s i_c + \frac{d\lambda_c}{dt}
$$

**Assumptions** (1 mark):
- Balanced sinusoidal distribution of stator MMF
- Negligible saturation
- Negligible damper windings on rotor
- Surface-mounted magnets (L_d = L_q = L_s) unless stated otherwise

**Step 2 — Flux linkage in the $abc$ frame** (1 mark):
$$
\lambda_a = L_s i_a + M i_b + M i_c + \lambda_{pm} \cos(\theta_e)
$$
Using $i_a + i_b + i_c = 0$ and defining $L = L_s - M$:
$$
\lambda_a = L i_a + \lambda_{pm} \cos(\theta_e)
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Clarke and Park transformations** (1 mark):
After Clarke ($abc \rightarrow \alpha\beta$) and Park ($\alpha\beta \rightarrow dq$) transformations, the voltage equations become:
$$
v_d = R_s i_d + L \frac{di_d}{dt} - \omega_e L i_q
$$
$$
v_q = R_s i_q + L \frac{di_q}{dt} + \omega_e (L i_d + \lambda_{pm})
$$

**Step 4 — Air-gap power** (1 mark):
$$
P_{gap} = \frac{3}{2}(v_d i_d + v_q i_q)
$$

**Step 5 — Substituting and simplifying** (1 mark):
For a surface PMSM ($L_d = L_q = L$), the electromagnetic power is:
$$
P_{em} = \frac{3}{2}\,\omega_e\,\lambda_{pm}\,i_q
$$

**Step 6 — Torque equation** (1 mark):
Since $\omega_e = P \omega_m$:
$$
\boxed{T_e = \frac{3}{2}\,P\,\lambda_{pm}\,i_q}
$$

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)**

The complete FOC block diagram for an EV PMSM drive is structured in **three cascaded loops**:

**1. Speed (Outer) Loop — 2 marks**:
- The accelerator pedal sets a speed reference $\omega_m^*$.
- An encoder/resolver measures actual rotor speed $\omega_m$.
- The error $\omega_m^* - \omega_m$ is fed to a **PI speed controller**, which outputs the torque-producing current reference $i_q^*$.
- The flux reference $i_d^*$ is set to **zero** for MTPA operation in surface PMSM.

**2. Current (Inner) Loop — 2 marks**:
- Two-phase currents ($i_a, i_b$) are measured by current sensors (shunts or Hall-effect).
- **Clarke transform** converts $i_a, i_b, i_c$ → $i_\alpha, i_\beta$.
- **Park transform** uses rotor position $\theta_e$ (from encoder) to produce $i_d, i_q$.
- Two independent **PI controllers** regulate $i_d \rightarrow i_d^*$ and $i_q \rightarrow i_q^*$.
- **Cross-coupling decoupling** adds the feed-forward terms $+\omega_e L i_q$ and $-\omega_e (L i_d + \lambda_{pm})$ to linearize the plant.

**3. Modulation and Inverter (Output) Loop — 2 marks**:
- **Inverse Park** produces $V_\alpha^*, V_\beta^*$.
- **Inverse Clarke** produces 3-phase references $V_a^*, V_b^*, V_c^*$.
- **SVPWM** generates switching signals for the 3-phase IGBT/SiC inverter.
- The inverter applies the modulated voltage to the PMSM stator windings.

**Reference signal source — 1 mark**:
- The rotor electrical angle $\theta_e = P \int \omega_m \, dt$ is required by both the Park and inverse Park transforms.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — FOC Transformations and PI Design

`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

> **(a)** Derive the Clarke and Park transformation matrices used in FOC. State the physical meaning of each axis. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** A PMSM has $R_s = 0.5 \,\Omega$, $L_d = 5$ mH, $L_q = 6$ mH, $\lambda_{pm} = 0.1$ Wb, $P = 4$. Design the $q$-axis PI current controller for a desired closed-loop bandwidth of 2 kHz. Calculate $K_p$ and $K_i$. (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Clarke Transformation (3 marks)**:

The 3-phase stator currents $i_a, i_b, i_c$ are 120° apart spatially. The total stator MMF space vector in the complex plane is:
$$
\vec{i}_s = \frac{2}{3}\left(i_a + i_b e^{j2\pi/3} + i_c e^{j4\pi/3}\right)
$$

Separating into real ($\alpha$) and imaginary ($\beta$) parts:
$$
\begin{bmatrix} i_\alpha \\ i_\beta \end{bmatrix} = \frac{2}{3}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -\tfrac{1}{2} & -\tfrac{1}{2} \\ 0 & \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} & -\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} i_a \\ i_b \\ i_c \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Physical meaning** (1 mark):
- The $\alpha$-axis is aligned with phase-A stator winding.
- The $\beta$-axis is orthogonal to $\alpha$, leading by 90° in the stator frame (which is stationary).
- The transformation preserves amplitude (amplitude-invariant form), so the resulting space vector has the same magnitude as the original 3-phase system.

**Step 2 — Park Transformation (3 marks)**:

To track the rotor's rotating flux, the $\alpha\beta$ frame is rotated by the electrical angle $\theta_e$ (the angle between the $\alpha$-axis and the rotor $d$-axis):
$$
\begin{bmatrix} i_d \\ i_q \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_e & \sin\theta_e \\ -\sin\theta_e & \cos\theta_e \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} i_\alpha \\ i_\beta \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Physical meaning** (1 mark):
- The $d$-axis (direct axis) is **locked to the rotor flux vector** — aligned with the permanent magnet field.
- The $q$-axis (quadrature axis) leads the $d$-axis by 90° in the direction of rotation.
- In the $dq$-frame, balanced sinusoidal 3-phase currents appear as **DC quantities** $i_d$ and $i_q$, making PI control trivial.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)**

**Given**:
- $R_s = 0.5 \,\Omega$
- $L_q = 6$ mH $= 6 \times 10^{-3}$ H
- $L_d = 5$ mH
- $\lambda_{pm} = 0.1$ Wb
- $P = 4$
- Desired bandwidth: $\omega_{bw} = 2\pi f_{bw} = 2\pi \times 2000 = 12{,}566$ rad/s

**Step 1 — Plant transfer function for $q$-axis** (1 mark):
$$
G_p(s) = \frac{1}{L_q s + R_s} = \frac{1}{0.006\,s + 0.5}
$$

**Step 2 — PI controller structure** (1 mark):
$$
G_c(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Pole-zero cancellation condition** (1 mark):
Set the PI zero to cancel the plant pole:
$$
T_i = \frac{K_p}{K_i} = \frac{L_q}{R_s} = \frac{0.006}{0.5} = 0.012 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate $K_p$ from bandwidth** (2 marks):
After pole-zero cancellation, the open-loop transfer function is $K_p / (R_s s)$. The closed-loop bandwidth is the gain crossover:
$$
\omega_{bw} = \frac{K_p}{R_s}
$$
$$
K_p = \omega_{bw} \cdot R_s = 12{,}566 \times 0.5 = \mathbf{6{,}283 \, \Omega}
$$

**Step 5 — Calculate $K_i$** (1 mark):
$$
K_i = \frac{K_p R_s}{L_q} = \frac{6{,}283 \times 0.5}{0.006} = \mathbf{523{,}583 \, V/(A \cdot s)}
$$
or equivalently:
$$
K_i = \omega_{bw} \cdot R_s = 12{,}566 \times 0.5 \text{ — wait, use } K_i = \frac{K_p}{T_i} = \frac{6{,}283}{0.012} = \mathbf{523{,}583}
$$

**Step 6 — Verification (Optional, 1 mark)**:
At 2 kHz bandwidth, the controller is well within the practical limit of $f_{sw} / 5$ assuming $f_{sw} = 10$ kHz switching frequency, satisfying the rule of thumb for stable digital control.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** (Where students lose marks):
> 1. **Forgetting the $2/3$ factor in the Clarke matrix** — using power-invariant form when amplitude-invariant is expected (or vice versa) leads to a torque magnitude error of ~13.4%. KTU examiners are strict on this.
> 2. **Mixing up $L_d$ and $L_q$** in the cross-coupling terms — the $d$-axis voltage contains $-\omega_e L_q i_q$, NOT $-\omega_e L_d i_q$. Marks are deducted per wrong term.
> 3. **Missing the pole-zero cancellation step** in PI design — students often write $K_p = \omega_{bw} L$ without justifying the $T_i = L/R$ choice.
> 4. **Sign error in the $q$-axis equation** — the back-EMF term $\omega_e \lambda_{pm}$ is **positive**, not negative.
> 5. **Not drawing feedback arrows** in block diagrams — KTU expects closed-loop diagrams with explicit current and speed feedback paths.
> 6. **Stating $i_d^* = 0$ without justification** — mention MTPA or flux-weakening context to earn full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-minute KTU Exam Revision — Bullet-Point Summary**

### Core Concepts
- [x] **FOC = Vector Control** = Decoupled control of flux ($i_d$) and torque ($i_q$)
- [x] Two transformations: **Clarke** ($abc \rightarrow \alpha\beta$) and **Park** ($\alpha\beta \rightarrow dq$)
- [x] Inverse Park + Inverse Clarke reconstruct the 3-phase references for SVPWM
- [x] Rotor position $\theta_e$ is **mandatory** for the Park transform

### Key Equations to Memorize
- [x] **Torque (surface PMSM)**: $T_e = \dfrac{3}{2} P \lambda_{pm} i_q$
- [x] **Torque (IPM)**: $T_e = \dfrac{3}{2} P \left[\lambda_{pm} i_q + (L_d - L_q) i_d i_q\right]$
- [x] **$d$-axis voltage**: $v_d = R_s i_d + L_d \frac{di_d}{dt} - \omega_e L_q i_q$
- [x] **$q$-axis voltage**: $v_q = R_s i_q + L_q \frac{di_q}{dt} + \omega_e (L_d i_d + \lambda_{pm})$
- [x] **Electrical speed**: $\omega_e = P \omega_m$

### Design Rules of Thumb
- [x] Current loop bandwidth $\omega_{bw} \approx \omega_{sw} / 5$
- [x] $K_p = \omega_{bw} L_d$, $K_i = \omega_{bw} R_s$ (after pole-zero cancellation)
- [x] MTPA for surface PMSM: $i_d^* = 0$
- [x] Flux-weakening (high speed): $i_d^* < 0$ to oppose $\lambda_{pm}$

### Advantages of FOC for EVs
- [x] Instantaneous torque response (< 1 ms)
- [x] Zero-speed full torque (hill start, traffic creep)
- [x] High efficiency (> 95%) across the entire drive cycle
- [x] Smooth regenerative braking
- [x] Enables field-weakening for high-speed cruising

### Common Exam Traps
- [x] Always include the $\omega_e L i$ cross-coupling decoupling terms
- [x] Clarke matrix is **not** dependent on rotor position; Park matrix **is**
- [x] SVPWM gives **15.5% higher DC-bus utilization** than SPWM
- [x] FOC requires DSP/FPGA; scalar V/f can run on a cheap microcontroller

### One-Line Definition to Memorize
> "FOC transforms AC motor control from the time-varying $abc$ domain to the **rotating, DC-like $dq$ domain**, making AC drives behave like separately excited DC motors — a paradigm shift that enabled the modern EV revolution."

---
