topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET742 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES AND RENEWABLE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sensor-based and Sensorless Control (Block Diagram Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Motor Control in EVs?

In any electric vehicle (EV), the traction motor (typically a **PMSM**, **BLDC**, or **Induction Motor**) cannot be directly connected to the DC battery. It needs an intelligent electronic controller that decides *exactly when* and *how much* voltage/current to apply to the motor windings to achieve the driver's demanded torque and speed. This electronic brain is the **Motor Controller (Inverter + Control Algorithm)**.

To run a motor precisely, the controller must continuously know **two critical pieces of information**:
1. **Rotor Position ($\theta_r$)** — *Where is the rotor magnet right now?*
2. **Rotor Speed ($\omega_r$)** — *How fast is it spinning?*

The method by which the controller obtains this information defines the two main control philosophies studied in this module.

---

## 1.2 Sensor-Based Control (Sensored Control)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *Sensor-based control is a closed-loop motor control strategy in which the rotor position and speed are directly measured using dedicated electromechanical position/speed transducers (encoders, resolvers, or Hall-effect sensors) mounted on the motor shaft, and this measured feedback is fed back to the controller in real time.*

### Real-World Analogy 🚗
Imagine you are driving a car on a highway with your eyes **OPEN**. You can constantly *see* your speedometer (sensor feedback) and adjust the accelerator accordingly. Because you have direct sensory information, your control is smooth, accurate, and fast. This is **Sensor-Based Control** — the controller "sees" the rotor directly through a physical sensor.

### Key Components Used
- **Incremental Optical Encoder** (high resolution, 1000–10000 pulses/rev)
- **Absolute Encoder** (knows position even at startup)
- **Resolver** (rugged, used in harsh EV environments)
- **Hall-Effect Sensors** (low-cost, used in BLDC motors — 3 sensors give 60° electrical resolution)

> [!NOTE]  
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In modern EVs, resolvers are preferred over optical encoders because they can withstand high temperatures, vibrations, and dust inside the motor housing.

---

## 1.3 Sensorless Control (Sensorless Control)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *Sensorless control is a closed-loop motor control technique in which the rotor position and speed are NOT measured by any physical shaft-mounted sensor. Instead, they are mathematically estimated in real time from the measurable stator electrical quantities (voltages and currents) using observer-based algorithms.*

### Real-World Analogy 🚗
Now imagine driving the same car, but this time your **dashboard is broken** — you cannot see the speedometer. To maintain the correct speed, you must *infer* your speed by listening to the engine sound, watching the road markings, and feeling the vibration. Your brain becomes an "observer," estimating speed from indirect clues. This is **Sensorless Control** — the controller *estimates* rotor state from electrical signals alone.

### Common Estimation Algorithms
- **Back-EMF Zero-Crossing Detection** (simple, low-speed issues)
- **Sliding Mode Observer (SMO)**
- **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)**
- **Model Reference Adaptive System (MRAS)**
- **High-Frequency Signal Injection** (used at zero/low speed)

---

## 1.4 Why Do We Care? — Comparison at a Glance

| Parameter | Sensor-Based | Sensorless |
|---|---|---|
| **Hardware Cost** | High (sensor + wiring + connectors) | Lower (no shaft sensor) |
| **Reliability** | Reduced (sensor can fail) | Higher (fewer parts) |
| **Position Accuracy** | Very High (down to 0.01°) | Moderate (algorithm-dependent) |
| **Low/Zero Speed Performance** | Excellent | Difficult (Back-EMF ≈ 0) |
| **EV Application** | Used where precision is critical | Used for cost & ruggedness |

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often wrongly state that sensorless control is "always better" because it removes sensors. The truth is that **at very low speeds and at startup (0 RPM)**, sensorless methods like Back-EMF fail completely, and that is exactly when a parked EV must produce smooth torque to start moving! Hence, many production EVs use a **hybrid approach**.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Fundamental Control Loops

Both sensor-based and sensorless control systems in EVs are built from **two nested feedback loops** — this is the universal architecture you must memorize for KTU exams.

### Loop 1: Outer Speed Loop (Slower Loop)
- **Input:** Reference speed $\omega_{ref}$ (from accelerator pedal)
- **Output:** Reference torque $T_{ref}$ (or reference $q$-axis current $i_q^*$)
- **Controller:** Usually a **PI (Proportional-Integral)** controller
- **Feedback:** Measured (sensor) or Estimated (sensorless) rotor speed $\omega_r$

### Loop 2: Inner Current Loop (Faster Loop)
- **Input:** $i_d^*$ and $i_q^*$ (from speed loop)
- **Output:** Voltage commands $v_d^*$ and $v_q^*$
- **Controller:** PI / Hysteresis / Deadbeat
- **Feedback:** Measured stator currents $i_a$, $i_b$ (using current sensors like LEM/ACS712)

> [!NOTE]  
> The inner current loop must be **at least 5–10 times faster** than the outer speed loop for stable cascaded control. This is a classic KTU viva question.

---

## 2.2 The Control Flow — Step-by-Step Logic

Let us walk through what happens in **one control cycle (every ~100 µs to 1 ms)** of a typical EV motor drive:

**Step 1 — Pedal Input:** The driver presses the accelerator pedal. The Vehicle Control Unit (VCU) interprets pedal position (0–100%) and generates a **reference speed $\omega_{ref}$** or reference torque $T_{ref}^*$.

**Step 2 — Speed Error Computation:**  
The controller compares $\omega_{ref}$ with the actual rotor speed:
$$e_\omega(t) = \omega_{ref}(t) - \omega_{actual}(t)$$

**Step 3 — PI Speed Controller Action:**  
The PI controller processes the error and outputs the **reference torque**:
$$T_{ref}(t) = K_p \cdot e_\omega(t) + K_i \int e_\omega(t)\, dt$$
For PMSM/BLDC, this torque is converted to $q$-axis current reference using $T = \frac{3}{2} \cdot p \cdot \psi_m \cdot i_q$.

**Step 4 — Current Control:**  
The reference $i_d^*$ (usually = 0 for surface PMSM) and $i_q^*$ are compared with measured $i_d, i_q$, and PI controllers output voltage references $v_d^*$, $v_q^*$.

**Step 5 — PWM/SVPWM Modulation:**  
The voltage references are converted into switching signals (SVPWM) for the **3-phase Voltage Source Inverter (VSI)** using IGBTs/MOSFETs.

**Step 6 — Inverter → Motor:**  
The inverter applies the synthesized voltages to the stator windings, producing the rotating magnetic field that drags the rotor along.

**Step 7 — Feedback:**  
- **Sensor-based:** Encoder/Resolver/Hall sensors measure $\theta_r$ and $\omega_r$ → fed back to Step 2.
- **Sensorless:** Voltage & current sensors feed an **Observer algorithm** that estimates $\hat{\theta}_r$ and $\hat{\omega}_r$ → fed back to Step 2.

---

## 2.3 How the Sensorless Observer Works — Conceptual View

In a sensorless system, there is **no direct mechanical measurement**. Instead, we use the fact that the **motor's mathematical model** relates measurable voltages/currents to the unmeasurable rotor position.

For a PMSM in the $\alpha$-$\beta$ stationary frame, the model is:
$$v_\alpha = R_s i_\alpha + L_s \frac{di_\alpha}{dt} - \omega_r \psi_m \sin(\theta_r)$$
$$v_\beta = R_s i_\beta + L_s \frac{di_\beta}{dt} + \omega_r \psi_m \cos(\theta_r)$$

The term $\omega_r \psi_m \sin(\theta_r)$ and $\omega_r \psi_m \cos(\theta_r)$ is called the **Back-EMF**. Since $v_\alpha, v_\beta, i_\alpha, i_\beta$ are measured, and $R_s, L_s, \psi_m$ are known motor parameters, an observer (SMO, EKF, MRAS) can **solve for $\theta_r$ and $\omega_r$**.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Why it fails at low speed:** At $\omega_r \approx 0$, the back-EMF term $\omega_r \psi_m \to 0$, so the equations lose the $\theta_r$ information. This is the **fundamental low-speed limitation** of sensorless control.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot p \cdot \psi_m \cdot i_q$ | Electromagnetic torque (PMSM, $i_d=0$) | Nm |
| 2 | $e_\omega = \omega_{ref} - \omega_{actual}$ | Speed error | rad/s |
| 3 | $T_{ref} = K_p e_\omega + K_i \int e_\omega\, dt$ | PI speed controller output | Nm |
| 4 | $v_d = R_s i_d + L_d \frac{di_d}{dt} - \omega_r L_q i_q$ | PMSM d-axis voltage | V |
| 5 | $v_q = R_s i_q + L_q \frac{di_q}{dt} + \omega_r(L_d i_d + \psi_m)$ | PMSM q-axis voltage | V |
| 6 | $E_b = \omega_r \cdot \psi_m$ | Back-EMF constant relation | V |
| 7 | $\theta_r = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{-E_\alpha}{E_\beta}\right)$ | Position from Back-EMF (sensorless) | rad |
| 8 | $f_{PWM} = \frac{1}{T_s} = 5\text{–}20 \text{ kHz}$ | Typical inverter switching frequency | Hz |
| 9 | $\omega_e = p \cdot \omega_r$ | Electrical speed = pole-pairs × mechanical speed | rad/s |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Units Reminder:** In KTU numericals, always convert RPM to rad/s using $\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$. Marks are often lost for missing unit conversions.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Three-Phase Model to Sensorless Position Estimation

We will derive the most important KTU-expected relationship — **how rotor position is extracted from Back-EMF in a sensorless PMSM drive**. We use the $\alpha$-$\beta$ (stationary reference frame) model, obtained by applying the **Clarke Transformation** to the three-phase stator quantities.

### Step 1 — Three-Phase Stator Voltage Equations

For a balanced three-phase PMSM, the per-phase voltage equation is:
$$v_{an} = R_s i_a + \frac{d\lambda_a}{dt}$$
$$v_{bn} = R_s i_b + \frac{d\lambda_b}{dt}$$
$$v_{cn} = R_s i_c + \frac{d\lambda_c}{dt}$$
where $\lambda_a, \lambda_b, \lambda_c$ are the stator flux linkages, which consist of two parts — the self-inductance flux and the permanent magnet flux linkage $\psi_m$ projected onto each phase.

### Step 2 — Clarke Transformation (abc → αβ)

The Clarke transform converts the three-phase system into a two-phase orthogonal stationary system:
$$i_\alpha = \frac{2}{3}\left(i_a - \frac{1}{2}i_b - \frac{1}{2}i_c\right)$$
$$i_\beta = \frac{2}{3}\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}i_b - \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}i_c\right)$$

Applying this to voltages and fluxes similarly, we obtain the simplified $\alpha$-$\beta$ model of the PMSM:
$$v_\alpha = R_s i_\alpha + L_s \frac{di_\alpha}{dt} - \omega_r \psi_m \sin(\theta_r)$$
$$v_\beta = R_s i_\beta + L_s \frac{di_\beta}{dt} + \omega_r \psi_m \cos(\theta_r)$$

where:
- $L_s = L_d = L_q$ (for **surface-mounted PMSM**, which is most common in EVs)
- $\omega_r \psi_m \sin(\theta_r)$ and $\omega_r \psi_m \cos(\theta_r)$ are the **$\alpha$ and $\beta$ components of the Back-EMF**

### Step 3 — Define the Back-EMF Components

Let us denote:
$$E_\alpha \triangleq -\omega_r \psi_m \sin(\theta_r)$$
$$E_\beta \triangleq \omega_r \psi_m \cos(\theta_r)$$

So the model becomes:
$$v_\alpha = R_s i_\alpha + L_s \frac{di_\alpha}{dt} + E_\alpha$$
$$v_\beta = R_s i_\beta + L_s \frac{di_\beta}{dt} + E_\beta$$

This is the standard form that observers (SMO, EKF) operate on.

### Step 4 — Rearranging to Isolate Back-EMF

Since $v_\alpha, v_\beta$ (measured), $i_\alpha, i_\beta$ (measured), $R_s, L_s$ (known parameters) are all available, we can compute:
$$E_\alpha = v_\alpha - R_s i_\alpha - L_s \frac{di_\alpha}{dt}$$
$$E_\beta = v_\beta - R_s i_b - L_s \frac{di_\beta}{dt}$$

### Step 5 — Position Extraction (Atan2 Function)

Now, taking the ratio:
$$\frac{E_\alpha}{E_\beta} = \frac{-\omega_r \psi_m \sin(\theta_r)}{\omega_r \psi_m \cos(\theta_r)} = -\tan(\theta_r)$$

Therefore, the **rotor position is estimated** as:
$$\boxed{\hat{\theta}_r = \text{atan2}\left(-E_\alpha, E_\beta\right)}$$

### Step 6 — Speed Estimation

Once $\hat{\theta}_r$ is obtained at two consecutive time instants, speed is found by differentiation:
$$\hat{\omega}_r(t) = \frac{\hat{\theta}_r(t) - \hat{\theta}_r(t - T_s)}{T_s}$$

where $T_s$ is the sampling period. In practice, a **low-pass filter** is applied to remove noise from the derivative.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Marks are awarded for clearly writing the atan2 function, not the simpler atan. Always write **atan2(−Eα, Eβ)** in your answer script — never forget the minus sign!

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Why $i_d^* = 0$ Control is Used in EV PMSM Drives

In a PMSM, the electromagnetic torque is:
$$T_e = \frac{3}{2} p \left[\psi_m i_q + (L_d - L_q) i_d i_q\right]$$

For a **surface-mounted PMSM** (SPMSM), $L_d = L_q = L_s$, so the second term vanishes:
$$T_e = \frac{3}{2} p \psi_m i_q$$

This means:
- All the torque is produced by the $q$-axis current $i_q$ alone
- The $d$-axis current $i_d$ does not contribute to torque
- If $i_d \neq 0$, it only causes **extra stator copper losses** ($i_d^2 R_s$) with no benefit
- Therefore, the optimal control is to set $i_d^* = 0$ and use $i_q$ for torque control

> [!NOTE]  
> This is why **Field-Oriented Control (FOC)** of SPMSM in EVs is also called the **"Zero d-axis Current Control"** strategy.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]  
> Per the KTU 2024 syllabus, this topic is **"Block Diagram Only"** — meaning the actual analytical derivations and controller design are out of scope, and the student is expected to **draw, label, and explain the block diagram**. Below are the two mandatory block diagrams with full functional explanations.

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Sensor-Based (Sensored) Control of EV Motor

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Driver Accelerator Pedal"] --> B["Reference Speed omega_ref"]
    B --> C["Comparator Speed Error"]
    D["Encoder Resolver Hall Sensor"] --> E["Measured Rotor Speed omega_r"]
    E --> C
    C --> F["PI Speed Controller"]
    F --> G["Reference Torque T_ref"]
    G --> H["Reference Currents id_star iq_star"]
    H --> I["FOC Clarke Park Transform"]
    J["Current Sensors LEM ACS712"] --> K["Measured 3 Phase Currents ia ib ic"]
    K --> I
    I --> L["PI Current Controllers id iq"]
    L --> M["Inverse Park Transform"]
    M --> N["SVPWM Modulator"]
    N --> O["3 Phase Voltage Source Inverter IGBT MOSFET"]
    O --> P["3 Phase PMSM BLDC Motor"]
    P --> D
    P --> Q["Wheels of EV"]
```

### Explanation of Each Block
1. **Accelerator Pedal → Reference Speed $\omega_{ref}$:** The VCU translates pedal depression (0–100%) into a desired motor speed.
2. **Encoder/Resolver/Hall Sensor:** Mounted on the motor shaft, generates digital/analog pulses proportional to rotor position. A differentiator in software gives speed.
3. **Comparator (Σ):** Computes $e_\omega = \omega_{ref} - \omega_{measured}$.
4. **PI Speed Controller:** Outputs reference torque $T_{ref}$ (or $i_q^*$).
5. **FOC Transform Block:** Clarke + Park transforms convert measured $i_a, i_b, i_c$ into the rotating $d$-$q$ frame, giving $i_d, i_q$.
6. **PI Current Controllers:** Two PI loops, one for $d$-axis, one for $q$-axis, produce voltage references $v_d^*, v_q^*$.
7. **Inverse Park Transform:** Converts $v_d^*, v_q^*$ back to stationary $\alpha$-$\beta$ frame.
8. **SVPWM Modulator:** Synthesizes the gating signals for the inverter.
9. **3-Phase VSI:** A 6-switch IGBT/MOSFET bridge converts DC battery voltage to 3-phase AC.
10. **PMSM/BLDC Motor:** Produces mechanical torque to drive the wheels.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Sensorless Control of EV Motor (Back-EMF Method)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Driver Accelerator Pedal"] --> B["Reference Speed omega_ref"]
    B --> C["Comparator Speed Error"]
    C --> D["PI Speed Controller"]
    D --> E["Reference Currents id_star iq_star"]
    F["Current Sensors ia ib ic"] --> G["Clarke Transform abc to alphabeta"]
    G --> H["Measured ialpha ibeta"]
    I["DC Link Voltage Sensor Vdc"] --> J["Inverter Model Estimated valpha vbeta"]
    J --> H
    H --> K["Back EMF Observer SMO EKF MRAS"]
    K --> L["Estimated Back EMF Ealpha Ebeta"]
    L --> M["Position Estimator atan2"]
    M --> N["Estimated Rotor Position theta_hat"]
    N --> O["Speed Estimator Differentiator LPF"]
    O --> P["Estimated Speed omega_hat"]
    P --> C
    E --> Q["PI Current Controllers"]
    G --> Q
    Q --> R["Inverse Park Transform"]
    R --> S["SVPWM Modulator"]
    S --> T["3 Phase Voltage Source Inverter"]
    T --> U["3 Phase PMSM Motor"]
    U --> F
    U --> V["EV Wheels"]
```

### Explanation of Sensorless-Specific Blocks
1. **No Encoder/Resolver:** Notice the absence of any shaft-mounted position sensor.
2. **Current Sensors (LEM/ACS712):** Measure the three-phase stator currents $i_a, i_b, i_c$.
3. **Clarke Transform:** Converts the three-phase currents into 2-phase stationary frame $i_\alpha, i_\beta$.
4. **Inverter Model (DC Link Sensor):** From the measured DC bus voltage $V_{dc}$ and the inverter switching states $S_a, S_b, S_c$, the **actual applied stator voltages** $v_\alpha, v_\beta$ are reconstructed. This is critical because the commanded voltage ≠ actual voltage during transients.
5. **Back-EMF Observer:** This is the heart of sensorless control. Common implementations:
   - **Sliding Mode Observer (SMO)** — robust, popular in EVs
   - **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** — best accuracy, high computation
   - **MRAS** — simple, model-reference based
6. **Position Estimator (atan2 block):** $\hat{\theta}_r = \text{atan2}(-E_\alpha, E_\beta)$ — the formula we derived in Section 3.
7. **Speed Estimator (Differentiator + LPF):** Differentiates position and filters noise to give $\hat{\omega}_r$.
8. **Feedback to Comparator:** The estimated speed closes the outer speed loop.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Hybrid Sensor-Sensorless Control (Industry Standard in EVs)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> Modern EVs (e.g., Tesla Model 3, Nissan Leaf, Tata Nexon EV) use a **hybrid strategy** — sensorless at medium/high speed, sensor-based (or HF injection) at zero/low speed.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Accelerator Pedal"] --> B["Reference Speed omega_ref"]
    B --> C["Speed Error Comparator"]
    D["Speed Threshold Detector Low Med High"] --> E["Mode Selector Switch"]
    F["Hall Sensor or HF Injection"] --> E
    G["Back EMF Observer"] --> E
    E --> C
    C --> H["PI Speed Controller"]
    H --> I["Current Reference id_star iq_star"]
    I --> J["PI Current Controllers"]
    J --> K["SVPWM Modulator"]
    K --> L["3 Phase Inverter"]
    L --> M["PMSM Motor"]
    M --> N["EV Drivetrain"]
```

### Working Principle
- **At 0 to ~5% of rated speed:** Use **Hall sensors** or **High-Frequency Signal Injection** to detect rotor saliency/position.
- **At > 5% rated speed:** Switch to **Back-EMF sensorless observer** for higher efficiency and lower hardware dependence.
- The **Mode Selector Switch** performs a smooth (bumpless) transfer between the two modes.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

### **Q1. Differentiate between sensor-based and sensorless control of EV motors. List any two advantages of sensorless control.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Sensor-Based | Sensorless |
|---|---|---|
| Position sensing | Physical shaft sensor (encoder/resolver) | Mathematical estimation from $V$ & $I$ |
| Cost | Higher | Lower |
| Reliability | Lower (sensor failure possible) | Higher (no moving-part sensor) |
| Low-speed performance | Excellent | Poor (Back-EMF ≈ 0) |

**Two advantages of sensorless control:**
1. **Reduced cost and complexity** — eliminates the encoder/resolver and its wiring harness.
2. **Higher reliability in harsh environments** — no sensor to fail due to heat, vibration, or dust.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

### **Q2. Why is the back-EMF based sensorless method unsuitable at zero and very low rotor speeds? How is this limitation overcome in practice?**

**Model Answer:**

The Back-EMF is given by $E_b = \omega_r \psi_m$. At zero rotor speed ($\omega_r = 0$), the Back-EMF is also zero, meaning the measured voltages and currents contain **no information about rotor position**. Hence, the atan2-based position estimator fails.

**Practical Overcoming Methods:**
1. **Use Hall sensors or encoder at low speed**, then switch to sensorless at higher speed (hybrid control).
2. **High-Frequency Signal Injection (HFSI):** Inject a high-frequency voltage signal and detect rotor saliency (works even at 0 RPM for IPMSM).
3. **I-f starting method:** Use open-loop V/f control to ramp up the speed to a level where Back-EMF is detectable.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Module 2 — Internal Choice Question Pattern**  
> **Answer ANY ONE full question from below. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Pattern, Module 2]**

**(a) Draw and explain the block diagram of a sensor-based Field-Oriented Control (FOC) scheme for a PMSM drive used in an electric vehicle. Clearly show the speed loop, current loop, and the position sensor feedback. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes)

**Explanation (Step-by-Step):**

1. **Reference Generation:** The accelerator pedal position is converted into a reference rotor speed $\omega_{ref}$ by the VCU.

2. **Outer Speed Loop:**
   - The **encoder** mounted on the motor shaft measures the rotor position $\theta_r$, which is differentiated to obtain rotor speed $\omega_r$.
   - A **comparator** computes the speed error $e_\omega = \omega_{ref} - \omega_r$.
   - A **PI speed controller** processes this error and outputs the reference torque $T_{ref}$, which is translated into the $q$-axis current reference $i_q^*$ using $T = \frac{3}{2} p \psi_m i_q$.
   - The $d$-axis reference $i_d^*$ is set to **zero** for surface PMSM (MTPA strategy).

3. **Inner Current Loop:**
   - The **current sensors** (LEM/ACS712) measure the three-phase stator currents $i_a, i_b, i_c$.
   - A **Clarke transform** converts them to the stationary $\alpha$-$\beta$ frame: $i_\alpha, i_\beta$.
   - A **Park transform** using the measured rotor position $\theta_r$ converts to rotating $d$-$q$ frame: $i_d, i_q$.
   - Two **PI current controllers** process the errors $(i_d^* - i_d)$ and $(i_q^* - i_q)$ and output voltage references $v_d^*, v_q^*$.
   - An **inverse Park transform** converts these back to the $\alpha$-$\beta$ frame.
   - A **SVPWM modulator** generates the 6 switching signals for the inverter.
   - The **3-phase VSI** drives the PMSM with synthesized sinusoidal currents.

4. **Sensor Feedback:** The encoder continuously updates the position $\theta_r$ for Park transforms and the speed $\omega_r$ for the outer speed loop. This closed-loop structure ensures precise torque and speed control, essential for smooth EV acceleration.

---

**(b) Draw and explain the block diagram of a sensorless control scheme for a PMSM drive using the Back-EMF estimation method. How is rotor position estimated from the measured voltages and currents? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes)

**Explanation:**

1. **Reference Speed and Speed Loop:** Same as sensor-based — $\omega_{ref}$ is compared with estimated speed $\hat{\omega}_r$, and the PI controller outputs $i_d^*, i_q^*$.

2. **Voltage Reconstruction:** The actual stator voltages $v_\alpha, v_\beta$ are reconstructed in real time from the **DC link voltage sensor** $V_{dc}$ and the inverter switching states $(S_a, S_b, S_c)$:
$$v_\alpha = \frac{V_{dc}}{3}(2 S_a - S_b - S_c)$$
$$v_\beta = \frac{V_{dc}}{\sqrt{3}}(S_b - S_c)$$

3. **Current Measurement & Clarke Transform:** $i_a, i_b, i_c$ are measured and converted to $i_\alpha, i_\beta$.

4. **Back-EMF Extraction:** Using the motor model, the back-EMF components are computed:
$$E_\alpha = v_\alpha - R_s i_\alpha - L_s \frac{di_\alpha}{dt}$$
$$E_\beta = v_\beta - R_s i_\beta - L_s \frac{di_\beta}{dt}$$

5. **Position Estimation (atan2 block):**  
$$\hat{\theta}_r = \text{atan2}(-E_\alpha, E_\beta)$$

6. **Speed Estimation:** Differentiating $\hat{\theta}_r$ and applying a low-pass filter:
$$\hat{\omega}_r = \frac{d\hat{\theta}_r}{dt}$$

7. **Closing the Loop:** The estimated $\hat{\theta}_r$ and $\hat{\omega}_r$ are fed back to the Park/inverse-Park transforms and the speed comparator, completing the sensorless closed-loop control.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Pattern, Module 2]**

**(a) Explain with a block diagram how rotor position and speed estimation is achieved in a sensorless PMSM drive using a Sliding Mode Observer (SMO). List the advantages of SMO over the Back-EMF method. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram — Sensorless PMSM with SMO:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["3 Phase PMSM Motor"] --> B["Current Sensors ia ib ic"]
    A --> C["DC Link Voltage Vdc"]
    B --> D["Clarke Transform"]
    D --> E["Measured ialpha ibeta"]
    C --> F["Inverter Model valpha vbeta"]
    E --> G["Sliding Mode Observer SMO"]
    F --> G
    G --> H["Estimated Back EMF Ealpha_hat Ebeta_hat"]
    H --> I["atan2 Position Estimator"]
    I --> J["Estimated Position theta_hat"]
    J --> K["LPF Differentiator"]
    K --> L["Estimated Speed omega_hat"]
    L --> M["Outer Speed Loop PI Controller"]
    M --> N["Current References id_star iq_star"]
    E --> O["PI Current Controllers"]
    N --> O
    O --> P["SVPWM and Inverter"]
    P --> A
```

**Working Principle of SMO:**

1. The SMO is a **nonlinear state observer** that estimates the back-EMF (and hence rotor position) using the sliding mode concept.
2. It uses a **sliding surface** defined by the current estimation error $\tilde{i} = \hat{i} - i$.
3. A **signum function** $\text{sgn}(\tilde{i})$ drives the estimate toward the sliding surface, where the back-EMF is mathematically extracted.
4. The SMO is mathematically described as:
$$\frac{d\hat{i}_\alpha}{dt} = \frac{1}{L_s}\left[v_\alpha - R_s \hat{i}_\alpha - k \cdot \text{sgn}(\hat{i}_\alpha - i_\alpha)\right]$$
$$\frac{d\hat{i}_\beta}{dt} = \frac{1}{L_s}\left[v_\beta - R_s \hat{i}_\beta - k \cdot \text{sgn}(\hat{i}_\beta - i_\beta)\right]$$
where $k$ is the sliding mode gain. The low-pass filtered $\text{sgn}$ output gives $\hat{E}_\alpha$ and $\hat{E}_\beta$.

**Advantages of SMO over Plain Back-EMF Method:**
- **Robustness to parameter variations** ($R_s, L_s$ changes with temperature)
- **Better noise immunity** due to the high-gain sliding action
- **Faster dynamic response** during speed transients
- **Does not require precise motor model** — works even with imperfect parameter knowledge

---

**(b) With a neat block diagram, explain the hybrid sensor-sensorless control strategy used in modern electric vehicles. Why is this approach preferred over pure sensorless control? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.3 — Hybrid Control)

**Explanation:**

1. **Low-Speed Region (0 to ~5% rated speed):** The **Mode Selector** chooses the **Hall sensor / encoder** branch (or High-Frequency Signal Injection). Back-EMF is too small to be useful here.
2. **Medium/High-Speed Region (> 5% rated speed):** The **Mode Selector** switches to the **Back-EMF observer** branch for higher efficiency and lower cost.
3. **Mode Selector Logic:** Implemented in software with hysteresis to avoid chattering between modes.
4. **Smooth (Bumpless) Transfer:** During mode switch, the controller ensures that the speed and current references are continuous (no torque jerk felt by the driver).

**Why Hybrid is Preferred:**
- Pure sensorless fails at 0 RPM (vehicle startup on a slope).
- Pure sensor-based increases cost and has sensor reliability issues.
- Hybrid gives the **best of both worlds** — sensor reliability at low speed, sensorless efficiency at high speed.
- Used in production EVs: **Tata Nexon EV, Hyundai Kona, Tesla Model 3.**

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**  
> 1. **Forgetting the atan2 minus sign:** Writing $\hat{\theta}_r = \text{atan2}(E_\alpha, E_\beta)$ instead of $\text{atan2}(-E_\alpha, E_\beta)$ — *lose 1 mark*.  
> 2. **Not labeling the units** of speed, voltage, current in the block diagram — *lose 1 mark*.  
> 3. **Confusing Clarke and Park transforms** — Clarke is abc→αβ, Park is αβ→dq. Writing them in reverse order costs marks.  
> 4. **Drawing the block diagram without arrows** showing signal flow direction — *lose up to 2 marks* in 14-mark questions.  
> 5. **Writing "sensorless is better" without justification** — must mention the low-speed limitation to get full credit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀

> 📌 **Rapid Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before the exam):**

- ✅ **Sensor-Based Control** = uses physical shaft sensor (encoder/resolver/Hall). High accuracy, high cost, lower reliability.
- ✅ **Sensorless Control** = estimates rotor state from $V$ & $I$ using observers. Low cost, high reliability, but **fails at zero/low speed** because Back-EMF $E_b = \omega_r \psi_m \to 0$.
- ✅ **Two-loop structure** = Outer speed loop (slow, PI) + Inner current loop (fast, PI). Current loop must be 5–10× faster than speed loop.
- ✅ **Back-EMF Position Formula (MUST MEMORIZE):**  
  $\hat{\theta}_r = \text{atan2}(-E_\alpha, E_\beta)$ — note the **minus sign**.
- ✅ **Zero d-axis control** for SPMSM: $i_d^* = 0$, all torque from $i_q$. Torque equation: $T_e = \frac{3}{2} p \psi_m i_q$.
- ✅ **Voltage Reconstruction** from DC link voltage: $v_\alpha = \frac{V_{dc}}{3}(2S_a - S_b - S_c)$, $v_\beta = \frac{V_{dc}}{\sqrt{3}}(S_b - S_c)$.
- ✅ **Observer Types:** SMO (robust, popular), EKF (accurate, complex), MRAS (simple, adaptive), HFSI (zero-speed capable).
- ✅ **Hybrid Control** is the industry standard — sensor-based at low speed, sensorless at high speed.
- ✅ **Block Diagram Components** (must appear): Pedal → Reference → Comparator → PI Speed Controller → FOC → Current Sensors → PI Current Controllers → SVPWM → Inverter → Motor → Sensor/Observer → back to Comparator.
- ✅ **Real EV examples:** Tata Nexon EV (sensorless), Tesla Model 3 (hybrid), Nissan Leaf (resolver-based).
- ✅ **Exam Tip:** Always convert RPM → rad/s using $\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$ in numerical problems.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sizing the Drive System - Matching the Electric Machine and the Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET742 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES AND RENEWABLE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sizing the Drive System - Matching the Electric Machine and the Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Drive System Sizing** in a hybrid electric vehicle (HEV) is the systematic engineering procedure of determining the optimal **peak power**, **continuous power**, **peak torque**, **base speed**, and **energy storage capacity** of the on-board energy sources — the **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)** and the **Electric Machine (EM)** — such that the combined powertrain satisfies the vehicle's dynamic performance requirements (maximum gradability, acceleration time from 0–100 km/h, and top cruising speed) while simultaneously minimizing the **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)**, **fuel consumption**, and **tailpipe emissions** over a standardized drive cycle (e.g., NEDC, WLTP, or MIDC).

**Matching the Electric Machine and the ICE** is the specific sub-process of harmonizing the **complementary characteristics** of these two prime movers so that their individual operating envelopes jointly form a **continuous, seamless, and efficient** propulsion envelope for the vehicle.

| Source | Specific Energy (Wh/kg) | Specific Power (W/kg) | Best Operating Regime |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| ICE (Gasoline) | **≈ 1200** | **≈ 800** | Narrow sweet spot (mid-speed, mid-load) |
| EM (PMSM) | **≈ 50** (machine alone) | **≈ 5000** | Very wide constant-power region |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The student must clearly differentiate the *specific energy* (capacity for sustained work) versus *specific power* (capacity for instantaneous work) when justifying the matching strategy. The ICE is an **energy-dense, power-limited** source, whereas the EM is a **power-dense, energy-limited** source. This asymmetry is the *fundamental engineering motivation* for hybridization.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two athletes on a tandem bicycle (a bicycle built for two):
* The **ICE** is the *marathon runner* seated at the rear — strong, steady, and able to pedal for hours at a moderate cadence. He cannot, however, sprint instantly when a steep hill appears.
* The **EM** is the *sprint cyclist* seated at the front — explosive, capable of instantaneous bursts of enormous torque, but his small "energy bar" (battery) runs out within minutes.

**Sizing the drive system** is analogous to selecting the *physique* (peak power rating) and *training regimen* (continuous rating) of these two athletes. **Matching** is the act of synchronising their pedalling so that during a hill climb the sprinter assists, during cruising only the marathon runner pedals, and during braking the sprinter acts as a brake to recover energy — the *regenerative braking* mode.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key takeaway for KTU exam:** If the EM is *undersized*, the vehicle will be sluggish in transient response and the ICE will be forced to operate in its inefficient low-load region. If the EM is *oversized*, the battery pack becomes unnecessarily large, heavy, and expensive (a phenomenon known as the **"over-hybridization penalty"**).

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Metrics

* Air density: $\rho = \mathbf{1.2 \; kg/m^3}$ (at standard sea-level conditions)
* Gravitational acceleration: $g = \mathbf{9.81 \; m/s^2}$
* Coefficient of rolling resistance (passenger car tyres): $\mu_{rr} \approx \mathbf{0.015}$
* Wheel-to-road coefficient of friction (dry asphalt): $\mu \approx \mathbf{0.8 - 1.0}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Vehicle Longitudinal Dynamics Foundation

The traction force at the wheel must overcome four fundamental resistance components. Deriving from Newton's second law applied to a vehicle of mass $m$ moving at velocity $v$ up a gradient of angle $\theta$:

$$F_{traction} = F_{rolling} + F_{aero} + F_{climb} + F_{acceleration}$$

| Term | Physical Meaning | Formula | Engineering Note |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $F_{rolling}$ | Tyre deformation loss | $\mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta$ | Speed-independent approx. |
| $F_{aero}$ | Air-drag | $\tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2$ | Dominant at $v > 60$ km/h |
| $F_{climb}$ | Gravitational | $m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$ | Zero on flat road |
| $F_{accel}$ | Inertial | $m \cdot a$ | Dominant during 0–60 km/h |

### 2.2 The Three Cornerstones of Drive System Sizing

1. **Maximum Power Sizing (Transient / Peak Rating):**
   Dictated by the most demanding *combined* demand — usually **0–100 km/h acceleration time** on a flat road **OR** maintaining **a target speed on a steep gradient**. The engine + machine must jointly deliver this peak.

2. **Continuous Power Sizing (Sustained / Cruise Rating):**
   Dictated by the *steady-state* power consumption at a high cruise speed (e.g., 120 km/h) on a flat road. This determines the **continuous** thermal rating of components.

3. **Energy Sizing (Fuel + Battery Capacity):**
   Dictated by the *total energy* demanded by a drive cycle. The fuel tank must satisfy the range; the battery must satisfy the regenerative energy captured and the electric-only mode range.

### 2.3 Matching Criteria — The Engine/Machine Compatibility Conditions

For a **parallel hybrid configuration**, matching demands two simultaneous conditions:

**Condition 1 — Power Additivity at Peak Demand:**
$$P_{total\_peak} \geq P_{ICE\_peak} + P_{EM\_peak}$$
(parallel mechanical coupling) or $P_{total\_peak} \geq \max(P_{ICE\_peak},\; P_{EM\_peak})$ (torque-coupled through a planetary gear, e.g., Toyota THS).

**Condition 2 — Torque-Speed Envelope Continuum:**
The **joint operating envelope** of the ICE and EM must cover the *entire* torque-demand curve $T_{req}(v)$ from 0 to $v_{max}$ without any dead zones.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Matching ≠ Summing Ratings:** A common KTU exam mistake is to assume that simply adding ICE and EM peak power is sufficient. The matching is *also* about *speed range overlap* and *control-band complementarity*. For example, an EM with a *constant-power region* from 1500–8000 rpm perfectly fills the gap left by the ICE's narrow peak-efficiency band of 2000–4000 rpm.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $P_{wheel} = F_{traction} \cdot v$ | Power demanded at the wheel | kW |
| 2 | $P_{req} = \dfrac{P_{wheel}}{\eta_{drivetrain}}$ | Power required at the prime-mover shaft | kW |
| 3 | $T = \dfrac{P}{\omega} = \dfrac{60 \cdot P}{2\pi \cdot N}$ | Torque from power and rotational speed | N·m |
| 4 | $P_{accel} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot \dfrac{v_f^2 - v_i^2}{\Delta t}$ | Peak power to accelerate $v_i \to v_f$ in $\Delta t$ | W |
| 5 | $DOH = \dfrac{P_{EM}}{P_{EM} + P_{ICE}}$ | Degree of Hybridization (0 = pure ICE, 1 = pure EV) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $E_{req} = \int_{cycle} P_{req}(t)\; dt$ | Energy demanded by a drive cycle | kWh |
| 7 | $\omega_{EM} \cdot r_{EM} = \omega_{ICE} \cdot r_{ICE}$ | Speed matching in a fixed gear-ratio coupling | rad/s |
| 8 | $T_{combined} = T_{ICE} \cdot \eta_g + T_{EM}$ | Torque summation in a parallel hybrid | N·m |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The matching logic is *the* foundational calculation behind every mass-production hybrid on the road:

* **Toyota Prius (THS-II):** $P_{ICE} = 72$ kW, $P_{EM1} = 60$ kW, $P_{EM2} = 30$ kW. The two motors are *torque-coupled* via a planetary gear set — the ICE never stops; it is *electrically* clutched in and out. Sizing was optimized for the **JC08** and **WLTP** cycles.
* **Honda i-MMD:** $P_{ICE} = 107$ kW, $P_{EM} = 135$ kW. The ICE is used almost exclusively as a *range extender* (series mode above 70 km/h) — a totally different matching philosophy.
* **BMW i3 REx:** $P_{ICE} = 28$ kW, $P_{EM} = 125$ kW. Here $DOH \approx 0.82$ — a *highly electrified* match.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** A question on "matching" is rarely a plug-and-chug question. The examiner expects you to (a) derive the vehicle power demand, (b) split the demand into ICE and EM contributions, and (c) justify the split using efficiency maps and drive-cycle analysis.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Total Power Required at the Drive Shaft

We start with Newton's second law on a vehicle of mass $m$ climbing an incline $\theta$ with acceleration $a$ at instantaneous speed $v$:

$$F_{traction} = m \cdot a + F_{rolling} + F_{aero} + F_{climb}$$

**Step 1:** Substitute the four resistance terms explicitly:

$$F_{traction} = m \cdot a + \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta + \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2 + m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$$

**Step 2:** The wheel must deliver this force at speed $v$ (in m/s). Therefore, the **wheel power** is:

$$P_{wheel} = F_{traction} \cdot v$$

**Step 3:** Accounting for drivetrain losses (transmission, differential, bearings) with an aggregate efficiency $\eta_{dt}$:

$$P_{shaft} = \dfrac{P_{wheel}}{\eta_{dt}} = \dfrac{v}{\eta_{dt}} \left[ m \cdot a + \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta + \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2 + m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta \right]$$

**Step 4:** This $P_{shaft}$ is the *combined* power the ICE + EM must jointly deliver. The **sizing** of the individual machines is then the *partitioning* problem:

$$P_{shaft} = P_{ICE} + P_{EM}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Peak Power from Acceleration Time (a Classic KTU 14-Mark Style Problem)

For a *level road* ($\theta = 0$), ignoring rolling and aerodynamic resistance for an *approximate* peak-power sizing (valid in the 0–60 km/h low-speed regime):

$$P_{accel\_peak} \approx m \cdot a \cdot v$$

**Step 1:** For a constant acceleration $a$ from $v_i$ to $v_f$ in time $\Delta t$:

$$a = \dfrac{v_f - v_i}{\Delta t}$$

**Step 2:** Since power is not constant (it grows with $v$), we use the **kinetic energy approach** (work-energy theorem):

$$W_{kinetic} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot (v_f^2 - v_i^2)$$

**Step 3:** The *average* mechanical power delivered over the interval is:

$$P_{avg} = \dfrac{W_{kinetic}}{\Delta t} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot \dfrac{v_f^2 - v_i^2}{\Delta t}$$

**Step 4:** The *peak* power is typically 2× the average (engineering rule of thumb for triangular power profile), so the **required peak** is:

$$P_{peak} \approx m \cdot \dfrac{v_f^2 - v_i^2}{\Delta t}$$

This formula is the workhorse for the **0–100 km/h** acceleration constraint in KTU problems.

### 3.3 Derivation: Torque-Speed Matching in a Parallel Hybrid

In a **parallel configuration** through a fixed gear ratio $G$ (motor/engine crankshaft → wheels), the wheel angular speed is:

$$\omega_{wheel} = G \cdot \omega_{machine}$$

The wheel torque is the *sum* of the two prime-mover torques reflected through the gear:

$$T_{wheel} = G \cdot (T_{ICE} \cdot \eta_g + T_{EM})$$

The required wheel torque as a function of vehicle speed is:

$$T_{wheel\_req}(v) = \dfrac{P_{shaft}(v)}{\omega_{wheel}(v)} = \dfrac{F_{traction}(v) \cdot r_{wheel}}{G}$$

**The matching condition** is: *for every operating speed $v$ in the vehicle envelope, there exists a feasible $(\omega_{ICE}, T_{ICE}, \omega_{EM}, T_{EM})$ such that the above equations are satisfied simultaneously.*

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Aligned to a Typical KTU 14-Mark Question)

**Given:**
* Vehicle mass $m = 1500$ kg
* Top speed $v_{max} = 180$ km/h $= 50$ m/s
* Acceleration target: 0 to 100 km/h (27.78 m/s) in $\Delta t = 10$ s
* Maximum gradient: 30% (i.e., $\sin\theta = 0.30$, $\theta = 17.46°$)
* $C_d = 0.30$, $A = 2.2$ m², $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³, $\mu_{rr} = 0.015$, $\eta_{dt} = 0.90$, $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1: Peak power from acceleration**

$$P_{accel} = m \cdot \dfrac{v_f^2 - v_i^2}{\Delta t} = 1500 \cdot \dfrac{27.78^2 - 0}{10} = 115.74 \; \text{kW}$$

**Step 2: Power needed to maintain 180 km/h on flat road (continuous rating)**

$$P_{aero} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^3 = 0.5 \cdot 1.2 \cdot 0.30 \cdot 2.2 \cdot 50^3 = 49.50 \; \text{kW}$$

$$P_{roll} = \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot v = 0.015 \cdot 1500 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 50 = 11.04 \; \text{kW}$$

$$P_{flat\_180} = (49.50 + 11.04) / 0.90 = 67.16 \; \text{kW}$$

**Step 3: Power needed to maintain 80 km/h (22.22 m/s) on 30% gradient**

$$P_{climb} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta \cdot v = 1500 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.30 \cdot 22.22 = 98.07 \; \text{kW}$$

$$P_{aero\_80} = 0.5 \cdot 1.2 \cdot 0.30 \cdot 2.2 \cdot 22.22^3 = 4.34 \; \text{kW}$$

$$P_{roll\_80} = 0.015 \cdot 1500 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 22.22 = 4.91 \; \text{kW}$$

$$P_{30pct\_80} = (98.07 + 4.34 + 4.91) / 0.90 = 119.25 \; \text{kW}$$

**Step 4: Governing design power** is the **maximum** of all scenarios:

$$P_{design} = \max(115.74,\; 67.16,\; 119.25) = 119.25 \; \text{kW}$$

**Step 5: Matching split (Degree of Hybridization = 0.4)**

$$P_{EM} = 0.4 \cdot 119.25 = 47.70 \; \text{kW}, \quad P_{ICE} = 0.6 \cdot 119.25 = 71.55 \; \text{kW}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering insight:** The grader expects you to identify that *gradient climbing* is the governing constraint here (119.25 kW), *not* the 0–100 km/h sprint (115.74 kW). For a heavier SUV, the gradient case always dominates. For a lighter sports car, the acceleration case dominates.

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Source Should Handle Which Demand?

| Vehicle Operating State | Primary Source | Secondary Source | KTU Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Idle / Stop (traffic signal) | ICE OFF | EM ready | Fuel = 0 during stop |
| Launch from rest | **EM** | ICE off | EM has 100% torque at 0 rpm; ICE stalls |
| Low-speed cruise (< 40 km/h) | EM | ICE off | ICE is highly inefficient at low load |
| Acceleration (mid-speed) | **BOTH** | — | Sum of ratings |
| High-speed cruise (> 80 km/h) | **ICE** | EM assist | ICE at its sweet spot |
| Hard acceleration (kickdown) | **BOTH** | — | Peak power demand |
| Deceleration / Braking | ICE OFF | EM as generator | Regenerative braking |
| Battery low (SOC < threshold) | **ICE** | EM as generator | Compulsory charging mode |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Matching Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Vehicle Performance Targets] --> A1[Top Speed e.g. 180 km/h]
    A --> A2[0-100 km/h Time e.g. 10 s]
    A --> A3[Max Gradient e.g. 30 percent]
    A --> A4[Drive Cycle e.g. WLTP]
    A4 --> A5[Energy Demand kWh per 100 km]

    A1 --> B[Total Traction Power Calculation]
    A2 --> B
    A3 --> B

    B --> B1[Compute P flat road]
    B --> B2[Compute P acceleration peak]
    B --> B3[Compute P gradient climb]

    B1 --> C[Select P design as MAX]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C

    C --> D[Partition via DOH Choice]
    D --> D1[DOH 0.3 Micro Hybrid]
    D --> D2[DOH 0.5 Mild Hybrid]
    D --> D3[DOH 0.7 Full Hybrid]
    D --> D4[DOH 1.0 Pure EV]

    D --> E[Assign Ratings to ICE]
    D --> F[Assign Ratings to EM]
    D --> G[Sizing of Battery Pack]
    D --> H[Sizing of Fuel Tank]

    A5 --> G
    A5 --> H

    E --> I[Validate against Torque-Speed Envelope]
    F --> I
    I --> J{Continuous Coverage of T req v ?}
    J -->|NO| K[Re-iterate sizing loop]
    K --> D
    J -->|YES| L[Final Matched Drive System]
```

### 4.2 Torque-Speed Envelope Visualization (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SP1[ICE Operating Envelope]
        nodeICE[ICE Constant Power Region]
    end
    subgraph SP2[EM Operating Envelope]
        nodeEM1[EM Peak Torque Zone]
        nodeEM2[EM Constant Power Zone]
        nodeEM3[EM High Speed Field Weakening]
    end
    subgraph SP3[Combined Envelope]
        nodeCOMB[Total Hybrid Envelope]
    end
    subgraph SP4[Vehicle Demand]
        nodeDEM[Required T vs v Curve]
    end
    nodeICE --> nodeCOMB
    nodeEM1 --> nodeCOMB
    nodeEM2 --> nodeCOMB
    nodeEM3 --> nodeCOMB
    nodeCOMB --> nodeCHK{Envelope contains Demand?}
    nodeDEM --> nodeCHK
    nodeCHK -->|YES| nodeOK[Sized Successfully]
    nodeCHK -->|NO| nodeFAIL[Re-size: Increase P or extend range]
```

### 4.3 Parallel Hybrid Drivetrain Coupling Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeBAT[Battery Pack] --> nodeINV[Power Inverter DC to AC]
    nodeINV --> nodeEM[Electric Machine]
    nodeFUEL[Fuel Tank] --> nodeICE[Internal Combustion Engine]
    nodeEM --> nodeCOUP[Mechanical Coupler or Planetary Gear]
    nodeICE --> nodeCOUP
    nodeCOUP --> nodeGBX[Transmission]
    nodeGBX --> nodeDIFF[Differential]
    nodeDIFF --> nodeWHF[Front Wheels]
    nodeDIFF --> nodeWHR[Rear Wheels]
    nodeBRK[Brake Pedal] --> nodeCTRL[Hybrid Control Unit]
    nodeACC[Accelerator Pedal] --> nodeCTRL
    nodeCTRL --> nodeEM
    nodeCTRL --> nodeICE
    nodeCTRL --> nodeINV
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams for KTU exams:** The first diagram depicts the *sizing algorithm* (top-down). The second depicts the *verification* (does the combined torque envelope contain the demand?). The third depicts the *physical architecture* (how ICE and EM are mechanically linked). All three are commonly asked as "draw and explain" in KTU 14-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEAET742]**
*Define the "Degree of Hybridization (DOH)" in a hybrid electric vehicle. What does DOH = 0 and DOH = 1 represent in terms of drive system matching?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Degree of Hybridization (DOH)** is the ratio of the **peak electrical power** ($P_{EM}$) of the electric machine to the **total peak power** of the combined prime movers (electric + ICE), defined as:

$$\text{DOH} = \dfrac{P_{EM}}{P_{EM} + P_{ICE}}$$

* **DOH = 0** implies $P_{EM} = 0$ — a **pure conventional ICE vehicle** (no electric assist). The matching problem reduces to sizing a single ICE.
* **DOH = 1** implies $P_{ICE} = 0$ — a **pure battery electric vehicle (BEV)**. The matching problem reduces to sizing a single electric machine and battery.
* **0 < DOH < 1** represents all true hybrids, with **mild hybrids** typically at DOH ≈ 0.2–0.3 and **full hybrids** at DOH ≈ 0.4–0.6.

This parameter is the *single most important knob* in matching, as it governs the relative ratings of the two prime movers.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEAET742]**
*State any three complementary characteristics between an Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) and an Electric Machine (EM) that justify hybrid drive matching.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | ICE Characteristic | EM Characteristic | Hybrid Benefit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Low specific power, **≈ 800 W/kg** | High specific power, **≈ 5000 W/kg** | EM handles transient peak demands |
| 2 | High specific energy, **≈ 1200 Wh/kg** | Low specific energy, **≈ 50 Wh/kg** | ICE handles sustained cruise energy |
| 3 | Poor part-load efficiency, **20–30%** | High efficiency across a wide range, **> 90%** | EM handles low-load urban driving |
| 4 | Cannot start under load; needs gearbox | **100% torque at 0 rpm** | EM handles launch from rest |
| 5 | Cannot regenerate energy | Fully **regenerative** at braking | EM recovers kinetic energy |

These five complementary traits form the *thermodynamic-economic-engineering basis* for hybridizing the two sources.

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

## ✦ QUESTION A — 14 Marks (Internal Choice Option 1)

**Q.A(a) [7 marks] [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, PEAET742]**
*Derive the total tractive power required by a hybrid electric vehicle of mass $m$ moving at velocity $v$ on a road of gradient $\theta$ with acceleration $a$. Clearly identify the contribution of (i) rolling resistance, (ii) aerodynamic drag, (iii) gradient climbing, and (iv) inertia. State the units of each term and explain how this equation is used to size the combined ICE + EM drive system.*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

From Newton's second law, the net tractive force at the wheel must equal the sum of the four resistance and inertial forces:

$$F_{tr} = F_{roll} + F_{aero} + F_{climb} + F_{accel}$$

**(i) Rolling resistance force** (tyre deformation loss):

$$F_{roll} = \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta \quad [\text{N}]$$

**(ii) Aerodynamic drag force** (viscous + form drag of the body):

$$F_{aero} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2 \quad [\text{N}]$$

**(iii) Gradient climbing force** (gravitational component along the slope):

$$F_{climb} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta \quad [\text{N}]$$

**(iv) Inertial force** (linear acceleration of vehicle mass):

$$F_{accel} = m \cdot a \quad [\text{N}]$$

The **power required at the wheel** is $P_{wheel} = F_{tr} \cdot v$, and the power required at the prime-mover shaft (accounting for drivetrain efficiency $\eta_{dt} \approx 0.85$–$0.95$) is:

$$P_{shaft} = \dfrac{v \cdot \left[ m \cdot a + \mu_{rr} m g \cos\theta + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 + m g \sin\theta \right]}{\eta_{dt}}$$

**Sizing Utility (3 sub-points for full marks):**

1. **Peak rating** = max of $P_{shaft}$ evaluated at the worst-case corner (e.g., maximum $a$, steepest $\theta$, highest $v$).
2. **Continuous rating** = $P_{shaft}$ at the high-speed cruise condition.
3. The individual ICE and EM ratings are then obtained by *splitting* $P_{shaft}$ according to the chosen DOH and the operating strategy (which source handles launch, cruise, peak, etc.).

---

**Q.A(b) [7 marks]**
*A parallel hybrid electric vehicle has the following data:*
* *Kerb mass $m = 1800$ kg*
* *ICE peak power: 75 kW @ 4500 rpm*
* *EM peak power: 50 kW @ 3000 rpm*
* *Top speed target: 160 km/h on flat road*
* *Maximum gradient: 25% at 50 km/h*
* *Coefficient of drag $C_d = 0.32$, Frontal area $A = 2.4$ m², $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³, $\mu_{rr} = 0.013$, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\eta_{dt} = 0.90$*

*Calculate: (i) the power required to maintain 160 km/h on a flat road, (ii) the power required to maintain 50 km/h on a 25% gradient, and (iii) verify whether the combined ICE + EM rating (125 kW) is sufficient. Also compute the Degree of Hybridization.*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**(i) Flat road at 160 km/h (44.44 m/s):**

$$P_{aero} = 0.5 \cdot 1.2 \cdot 0.32 \cdot 2.4 \cdot (44.44)^3 = 0.4608 \cdot 87719 = 40.43 \; \text{kW}$$

$$P_{roll} = 0.013 \cdot 1800 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 44.44 = 10198 \; \text{W} = 10.20 \; \text{kW}$$

$$P_{flat\_160} = (40.43 + 10.20) / 0.90 = \mathbf{56.26 \; kW}$$

**(ii) 25% gradient at 50 km/h (13.89 m/s):**

For 25% gradient, $\sin\theta = 0.25 / \sqrt{1 + 0.25^2} = 0.2425$, so $\theta \approx 14.04°$ and $\cos\theta = 0.9701$.

$$P_{climb} = 1800 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.2425 \cdot 13.89 = 59.42 \; \text{kW}$$

$$P_{aero\_50} = 0.5 \cdot 1.2 \cdot 0.32 \cdot 2.4 \cdot (13.89)^3 = 0.4608 \cdot 2680 = 1.235 \; \text{kW}$$

$$P_{roll\_50} = 0.013 \cdot 1800 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.9701 \cdot 13.89 = 3187 \; \text{W} = 3.19 \; \text{kW}$$

$$P_{25pct\_50} = (59.42 + 1.24 + 3.19) / 0.90 = \mathbf{70.94 \; kW}$$

**(iii) Verification:**

Governing design power: $P_{design} = \max(56.26,\; 70.94) = 70.94$ kW.

Combined available power = $P_{ICE} + P_{EM} = 75 + 50 = 125$ kW.

Since $125 \text{ kW} \gg 70.94 \text{ kW}$, the **combined drive system is sufficient** (with a healthy margin of ≈ 76% above the peak demand). This margin is intentional — it ensures the ICE can operate in its *high-efficiency sweet spot* while the EM handles transient loads.

**Degree of Hybridization:**

$$\text{DOH} = \dfrac{P_{EM}}{P_{EM} + P_{ICE}} = \dfrac{50}{50 + 75} = \mathbf{0.40}$$

This corresponds to a **mild-to-full hybrid** configuration.

---

## ✦ QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Internal Choice Option 2)

**Q.B(a) [7 marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2024, PEAET742]**
*With the aid of a labelled torque-speed (T-ω) diagram, explain how the operating envelope of an Internal Combustion Engine is matched with that of an Electric Machine in a hybrid electric vehicle. Why is a "constant-power region" important in the EM characteristic, and what is its analog in the ICE?*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

The **torque-speed diagram** is the canonical KTU figure for this question.

**ICE T-ω Characteristic:**
The ICE produces torque that rises slightly with speed up to a peak around 2000–3000 rpm, then falls *hyperbolically* as the *constant-power region* extends from ≈ 4000 to ≈ 6000 rpm. Beyond the redline, the torque collapses to zero.

**EM T-ω Characteristic:**
The EM delivers **constant peak torque** from 0 rpm to its *base speed* (e.g., 1500–2500 rpm). Above base speed, *field weakening* (in a PMSM) or *constant V/f* (in an induction machine) reduces the torque hyperbolically in a *constant-power* region extending up to 3–5× the base speed.

**Matching Logic (3 key points for full marks):**

1. **At low speed (launch, 0–20 km/h):** EM delivers 100% torque at 0 rpm; ICE is OFF. The EM covers this region exclusively.
2. **At medium speed (20–80 km/h):** The ICE enters its high-efficiency region (≈ 2500–4000 rpm, mid-load). Both sources may operate in parallel; the EM provides the *torque boost* during acceleration events.
3. **At high speed (> 80 km/h):** The ICE operates at constant power (highway cruise); the EM provides *transient assist* for overtaking and absorbs *regenerative energy* during braking.

**Why the constant-power region matters:**
The constant-power region of the EM provides *power-dense flexibility* — the same peak power can be delivered at low rpm (high torque) or high rpm (low torque), allowing a **single-gear transmission** in some HEVs. The ICE's analog is its own limited constant-power region (e.g., 3000–5500 rpm), but it is **narrower and less efficient** than that of the EM. The EM's wider constant-power region is one of the *primary engineering motivations* for hybridization.

**Conclusion:** The matching is *complementary* — the EM fills the ICE's low-speed dead zone, the ICE handles sustained cruise where the EM's energy would deplete rapidly, and both share the peak demand.

---

**Q.B(b) [7 marks]**
*Explain the concept of "Degree of Hybridization" (DOH) with reference to drive-system sizing. For each of the following scenarios, suggest an appropriate DOH value and justify your choice: (i) A city delivery auto-rickshaw (≤ 40 km/h, frequent stops), (ii) A highway sedan for intercity travel (cruise 100 km/h, 600 km range), (iii) A plug-in family SUV (200 km electric-only range, 800 km total range).*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

Recall: $\text{DOH} = \dfrac{P_{EM}}{P_{EM} + P_{ICE}}$.

**(i) City Delivery Auto-Rickshaw — Suggested DOH ≈ 0.7–0.9 (Strong Hybrid / Near-Pure EV):**

* **Why:** Speed is low (≤ 40 km/h), so aerodynamic drag is negligible. Frequent stops make **regenerative braking** a major energy source. The ICE has no efficient operating window at such low average power.
* **Matching logic:** A *small* ICE (10–15 kW) as range extender, with a *larger* EM (30–50 kW) and a modest battery. Most real auto-rickshaw hybrids (e.g., Mahindra Treo, Piaggio Ape Electrik) are effectively **BEVs with a small ICE backup** — DOH approaching 1.

**(ii) Highway Sedan — Suggested DOH ≈ 0.2–0.3 (Mild Hybrid):**

* **Why:** The dominant operating mode is **steady high-speed cruise**, where the ICE is at its thermal-efficiency sweet spot. A large battery is *unnecessary* (and adds weight that hurts highway fuel economy). A small 48 V EM suffices for *transient assist* during overtaking and to enable start-stop.
* **Matching logic:** Large ICE (≈ 100–150 kW) with a small belt-driven or 48 V integrated starter-generator (ISG, ≈ 10–20 kW). Examples: **Mercedes-Benz EQ Boost**, **Audi 48 V MHEV**, **Honda Insight**. DOH ≈ 0.20.

**(iii) Plug-in Family SUV — Suggested DOH ≈ 0.6–0.8 (Plug-in Hybrid, PHEV):**

* **Why:** The 200 km electric-only range is *larger than typical daily commute* (40–60 km/day), so most trips are **electric**. The 800 km total range requires *occasional* ICE for long trips. Both sources must be *fully* capable.
* **Matching logic:** Substantial EM (≈ 100–150 kW) and substantial ICE (≈ 80–100 kW). Examples: **Toyota RAV4 Prime**, **BYD Tang DM-i**, **Volvo XC60 T8**. DOH ≈ 0.6. The battery is sized to supply 200 km of electric driving (typically 15–25 kWh).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Common 2–3 mark loss):**
> Students often confuse the **peak-power** DOH with the **energy-share** DOH. The *power* DOH (as defined here) governs **sizing**; the *energy* DOH (ratio of electric energy consumed to total energy consumed) is a *result* of the drive cycle. The two are *not* the same! For a PHEV driven mostly on electric mode, the *energy* DOH may be 0.7 even though the *power* DOH is only 0.5. State clearly which DOH you are computing.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Drive system sizing** is the *partitioning* of a single power demand into two complementary prime-mover ratings (ICE + EM).
- [x] The **fundamental power equation** at the wheel is $P = v \cdot [m \cdot a + \mu_{rr} m g \cos\theta + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 + m g \sin\theta] / \eta_{dt}$.
- [x] The **governing design power** is the *maximum* of: (i) 0–100 km/h acceleration, (ii) top-speed cruise, (iii) gradient climbing. Identify which dominates in your problem.
- [x] **DOH** is defined from *peak powers*: $\text{DOH} = P_{EM} / (P_{EM} + P_{ICE})$. Range 0 (pure ICE) to 1 (pure EV).
- [x] The **ICE** is energy-dense but power-limited; the **EM** is power-dense but energy-limited. *Matching exploits this complementarity.*
- [x] At **launch (0 rpm)**: only the EM can deliver torque. The ICE requires a clutch and minimum rpm.
- [x] At **high-speed cruise**: the ICE is in its efficient region; the EM is best used for *transient* assist.
- [x] The **constant-power region** of the EM (via field weakening) is *wider* than that of the ICE — a key matching advantage.
- [x] **Energy** for the battery is sized from drive-cycle analysis: $E = \int P \, dt$, not from peak power.
- [x] Always state **units** explicitly in derivations (N, N·m, kW, m/s, kg). KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing units.
- [x] **Standard test conditions**: $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s². Use these unless the problem specifies otherwise.
- [x] Common **DOH ranges**: 0.1–0.3 = Mild (MHEV); 0.3–0.6 = Full (HEV); 0.6–0.9 = Plug-in (PHEV); 1.0 = BEV.
- [x] **Matching ≠ Summation**: An *envelope-containment* check (does the combined T-ω curve contain the demand curve?) is the gold-standard verification, not just $P_{ICE} + P_{EM} \geq P_{design}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sizing the Propulsion Motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET742 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES AND RENEWABLE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sizing the Propulsion Motor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 : Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sizing the Propulsion Motor** is the systematic engineering procedure of determining the **rated power, peak power, rated torque, and rated speed** of an electric machine (PMSM, IM, BLDC, or SRM) used to propel an Electric Vehicle (EV) such that the vehicle satisfies the prescribed **performance specifications** — maximum cruise velocity, maximum gradeability, 0–100 km/h acceleration time, and targeted driving range — while operating within the thermal, mechanical, and electrical limits of the machine and the energy storage system (battery/fuel cell).

In formal terms, motor sizing resolves the *inverse problem* of vehicle dynamics: given the **desired vehicle performance envelope**, we mathematically back-calculate the **machine envelope** (torque–speed plane) that will deliver that performance after accounting for transmission losses, road loads, and acceleration dynamics.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are choosing a **water pump** to lift water to the roof of a building. Before buying it, you must know:
- *How much water per second?* → analogous to **Power (kW)**
- *How high must it push?* → analogous to **Torque (Nm)** overcoming grade
- *How fast must the water flow out?* → analogous to **Motor speed (rpm)**

Similarly, sizing the propulsion motor means asking:
1. "How heavy is the vehicle, and how steep a hill must it climb?" → **Torque demand**
2. "How fast must it go, and how quickly must it accelerate?" → **Power demand**
3. "How often will it run hard vs. cruise gently?" → **Continuous vs. Peak rating**

A pump too small = no water reaches the top. A motor too small = no hill climb / sluggish acceleration. Oversized = wasted cost, mass, and battery energy.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Values used in EV Motor Sizing (SI Units):**
> - Gravitational acceleration: **$g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$**
> - Air density at sea level: **$\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$**
> - Coefficient of rolling resistance (car tire on asphalt): **$\mu_{rr} \approx 0.008$ to $0.015$**
> - Aerodynamic drag coefficient (sedan): **$C_d \approx 0.25$ to $0.35$**
> - Typical gear efficiency (single-speed): **$\eta_g \approx 0.95$ to $0.97$**
> - Drivetrain transmission efficiency (overall): **$\eta_t \approx 0.90$ to $0.94$**
> - Equivalent vehicle mass factor (including rotational inertia): **$\delta \approx 1.05$ to $1.10$**

These constants are **the foundation of every tractive-force calculation** and must be memorized for KTU numerical problems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 : Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Resistive Forces Acting on an EV

To size the motor, we must first quantify the **total tractive force $F_{te}$** required at the wheel contact patch. The vehicle, when propelled, must overcome exactly **four fundamental resistances**:

#### A. Rolling Resistance Force ($F_{rr}$)
- Caused by tire hysteresis deformation as the wheel rolls.
- Depends on vehicle mass, road surface, tire pressure, and grade.
- **Logic:** Heavier car → bigger tire deformation → bigger loss.

#### B. Aerodynamic Drag Force ($F_{ad}$)
- Caused by air molecules being pushed aside at high speed.
- Quadratic in velocity → dominates above ~60 km/h.
- **Logic:** Doubling speed quadruples the drag force.

#### C. Hill-Climbing (Grade) Force ($F_{hc}$)
- Gravitational component along the slope.
- Independent of speed; depends only on mass and gradient.
- **Logic:** A 30 % grade (≈ 16.7°) demands a large fraction of vehicle weight.

#### D. Linear Acceleration Force ($F_{la}$)
- Required to overcome linear inertia $m \cdot a$.
- Plus a smaller rotational inertia term from wheels, gears, and motor rotor.

### 2.2 Total Tractive Effort Equation

$$F_{te} = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{la}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The motor must be capable of producing, **at the wheels**, a force $\geq F_{te}$ for every instant of the design drive cycle. KTU numerical problems typically ask the student to evaluate $F_{te}$ for **three worst-case operating points**: (i) max-speed cruise, (ii) max-grade at low speed, (iii) 0–100 km/h acceleration.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> Master the table below — every KTU numerical on this topic is a rearrangement of one or more of these equations.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Rolling resistance | $F_{rr} = \mu_{rr} \, m \, g \cos\alpha$ | N | $\alpha$ = grade angle |
| 2 | Aerodynamic drag | $F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2} \, \rho \, C_d \, A_f \, v^2$ | N | $v$ in m/s |
| 3 | Grade force | $F_{hc} = m \, g \sin\alpha$ | N | Or $= m \, g \cdot \tfrac{\%G}{100}$ for small angles |
| 4 | Acceleration force | $F_{la} = \delta \, m \, a$ | N | $\delta$ includes rotational inertia |
| 5 | Total tractive effort | $F_{te} = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{la}$ | N | Vector sum at wheel |
| 6 | Power at wheels | $P_w = F_{te} \cdot v$ | W | $v$ in m/s |
| 7 | Power at motor shaft | $P_m = \dfrac{P_w}{\eta_t}$ | W | $\eta_t$ = transmission efficiency |
| 8 | Wheel torque | $T_w = F_{te} \cdot r_w$ | N·m | $r_w$ = effective wheel radius |
| 9 | Motor torque | $T_m = \dfrac{T_w}{G \cdot \eta_g}$ | N·m | $G$ = overall gear ratio |
| 10 | Motor speed | $N_m = \dfrac{v \cdot G}{2 \pi r_w} \cdot 60$ | rpm | $v$ in m/s |
| 11 | Grade (%) to angle | $\alpha = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{\%G}{100}\right)$ | rad | Or use small-angle approximation |
| 12 | Energy required (cycle) | $E = \displaystyle\int P_m \, dt$ | J or Wh | Used for battery sizing linkage |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive OEM Design Studios** (Tata Motors, Mahindra Electric, Ather, Tesla, BYD) use these exact equations in **MATLAB/Simulink** and **AVL CRUISE** to size machines.
- **FSAE Student Competitions** (Formula Student): sizing the motor is the *first* design step before choosing a battery pack.
- **E-bike and E-rickshaw industries** in India use simplified versions to rate BLDC hub motors in W and rpm.
- The output of this sizing exercise — the **torque–speed envelope** — directly determines the **inverter current rating**, **battery C-rate**, and **thermal cooling system** sizing, which are all interlinked KTU learning outcomes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 : Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — From Vehicle Performance Spec to Motor Torque-Speed Envelope

We will derive, in a single continuous logical flow, how a set of *vehicle-level targets* translates to a *machine-level rating*. Each line below corresponds to a physically meaningful step.

**Step 1 — Define the four road loads.**
The vehicle, moving at velocity $v$ on a grade of angle $\alpha$ and accelerating at rate $a$, must overcome:

$$F_{te} = \underbrace{\mu_{rr} \, m \, g \cos\alpha}_{F_{rr}} + \underbrace{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f v^2}_{F_{ad}} + \underbrace{m \, g \sin\alpha}_{F_{hc}} + \underbrace{\delta \, m \, a}_{F_{la}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the tractive power at the wheel-road interface.**
Power is force times velocity (dot product, but collinear here):

$$P_w = F_{te} \cdot v$$

**Step 3 — Push the power upstream through the drivetrain.**
The transmission (gearbox + differential) absorbs a fraction $(1-\eta_t)$ of mechanical power as heat, friction, and churning loss. Therefore the motor shaft must deliver *more* power than reaches the road:

$$P_m = \frac{P_w}{\eta_t} = \frac{F_{te} \cdot v}{\eta_t}$$

**Step 4 — Convert wheel force to wheel torque.**
The wheel is a lever arm of length $r_w$ (effective rolling radius). Torque at the wheel is force times radius:

$$T_w = F_{te} \cdot r_w$$

**Step 5 — Push the torque upstream through the gear ratio.**
The gearbox with overall ratio $G$ (input/output speed ratio, with $G > 1$ for a speed-reducer) multiplies torque (conservation of power minus losses):

$$T_m = \frac{T_w}{G \cdot \eta_g} = \frac{F_{te} \cdot r_w}{G \cdot \eta_g}$$

**Step 6 — Convert wheel linear speed to motor rotational speed.**
Linear wheel speed $v$ (m/s) corresponds to wheel angular speed $\omega_w = v / r_w$ (rad/s). The gearbox multiplies this by $G$:

$$\omega_m = G \cdot \omega_w = \frac{G \cdot v}{r_w}$$

**Step 7 — Convert angular speed to rpm.**
Engineering practice expresses motor speed in revolutions per minute:

$$N_m = \frac{\omega_m \cdot 60}{2\pi} = \frac{60 \cdot G \cdot v}{2\pi \cdot r_w}$$

**Step 8 — Peak vs. Continuous Rating.**
The motor is now characterized by two operating points:

- **Peak (Short-term, e.g., 30 s – 2 min):** Defined by the maximum of $F_{te}$ across all transient maneuvers (acceleration at low speed, hill launch).
- **Continuous (Long-term, e.g., > 30 min):** Defined by the maximum $P_m$ required during sustained cruise on the design drive cycle (e.g., UDDS, NEDC, WLTP).

**Step 9 — Envelope construction.**
The motor's permissible operating region is a closed shape on the $T$–$N$ plane:

1. A **constant-torque region** from $N=0$ to base speed $N_b$ (current-limited).
2. A **constant-power region** from $N_b$ to maximum speed $N_{max}$ (voltage/flux-weakening-limited).
3. A **thermal boundary** that confines continuous operation below a hyperbola $T \cdot N = \text{const}$.

The vehicle load curve $(T_m(v), N_m(v))$ for the entire drive cycle must lie *inside* this envelope, with appropriate safety margins (typically 15–25 % for KTU/industry design problems).

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Which Equation to Use for Which Performance Target

| Performance Target | Dominant $F_{te}$ Terms | Which $v$ to Use | Which Motor Spec it Sizing |
|---|---|---|---|
| Maximum cruise speed $v_{max}$ | $F_{rr} + F_{ad}$ (flat road) | $v = v_{max}$ | **Peak Power** |
| Maximum gradeability at low speed | $F_{rr} + F_{hc}$ | $v \approx$ crawl (1 m/s typical) | **Peak Torque at low speed** |
| 0 → $v_{acc}$ acceleration time | $F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{la}$ | Average velocity | **Peak Power & Energy** |
| Sustained hill cruise | $F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{hc}$ | Constant | **Continuous Power** |
| Range on a drive cycle | All four integrated over cycle | Time-varying | **Battery Energy + Avg Power** |

### 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Sub-Derivation — Acceleration Time to a Given Speed

This derivation frequently appears in KTU Part-B 14-mark questions.

Given: vehicle mass $m$, $\mu_{rr}$, $C_d$, $A_f$, $G$, $r_w$, $\eta_t$, $\eta_g$, target acceleration time $t_f$ to reach speed $v_f$, starting from rest, on level road ($\alpha = 0$).

**Step 3.3.1 — Write $F_{te}$ for the maneuver (ignoring drag at very low speed, a common KTU assumption):**

$$F_{te} = \mu_{rr} m g + \delta m a$$

**Step 3.3.2 — Solve for the constant acceleration $a$ required to reach $v_f$ in $t_f$:**

$$a = \frac{v_f}{t_f}$$

**Step 3.3.3 — Substitute $a$ back into $F_{te}$:**

$$F_{te} = \mu_{rr} m g + \delta m \cdot \frac{v_f}{t_f}$$

**Step 3.3.4 — Compute the average tractive force.** Since $F_{te}$ is dominated by $\delta m a$ at low $v$ and by $\mu_{rr} m g$ at high $v$, a practical engineering approximation is:

$$F_{te,\text{avg}} \approx \mu_{rr} m g + \frac{1}{2} \delta m \cdot \frac{v_f}{t_f}$$

**Step 3.3.5 — Compute the average tractive power (assuming linear velocity rise):**

$$P_{w,\text{avg}} = F_{te,\text{avg}} \cdot \frac{v_f}{2}$$

**Step 3.3.6 — Required motor peak power (with safety factor $k_s \approx 1.15$):**

$$P_{m,\text{peak}} = \frac{k_s \cdot P_{w,\text{avg}}}{\eta_t}$$

**Step 3.3.7 — Required motor peak torque (at launch, $v \to 0$, only $a$ matters):**

$$T_{m,\text{peak}} = \frac{r_w}{G \cdot \eta_g}\!\left(\mu_{rr} m g + \delta m \cdot \frac{v_f}{t_f}\right)$$

This completes the closed-form derivation. KTU students should be able to plug in numerical values and present a clean labeled result.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 : Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Vehicle Resistance Force Decomposition

flowchart LR

    A2[Velocity v]

    F1[Motor Torque T_m and Power P_m]
    G1[Motor Speed N_m in rpm]
    H1[Torque-Speed Envelope]

    A1 --> B1
    A1 --> B3
    A1 --> B4
    A2 --> B2
    A3 --> B1
    A3 --> B3
    A4 --> B4

    B3 --> C1
