<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 : KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**
*List the four resistive forces that oppose the motion of an electric vehicle and write the expression for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The four resistive forces are:
1. **Rolling resistance:** $F_{rr} = \mu_{rr} m g \cos\alpha$ — caused by tire deformation.
2. **Aerodynamic drag:** $F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f v^2$ — caused by air resistance.
3. **Hill-climbing force:** $F_{hc} = m g \sin\alpha$ — gravity component along slope.
4. **Acceleration (inertia) force:** $F_{la} = \delta m a$ — Newton's second law, including rotational inertia via factor $\delta$.

Total tractive effort: $F_{te} = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{la}$. **(3 marks for four expressions with one-line justifications.)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Sample, Module 2]**
*What is the difference between the peak power and the continuous (rated) power of an EV propulsion motor? On which operating point is each based?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Peak power** is the maximum mechanical power the motor can deliver for a short duration (typically 30 s to 2 min), limited by the inverter's current limit and the motor's magnetic saturation. It is determined by the **worst-case acceleration** or **grade-launch** requirement. **(1.5 marks)**
- **Continuous (rated) power** is the power the motor can deliver indefinitely without exceeding its thermal steady-state temperature. It is determined by the **sustained cruise** or **constant-grade** demand over a long drive cycle. **(1.5 marks)**
- Typical ratio: $P_{peak}/P_{cont} \approx 2$ to $3$ for automotive traction machines.

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module 2 Internal Choice)

---

**Q-A. [KTU University Exam – Past Pattern, Module 2] — 14 Marks**

An electric car has the following data:
- Curb mass $m = 1500 \text{ kg}$ (including 75 kg driver)
- Frontal area $A_f = 2.2 \text{ m}^2$
- Drag coefficient $C_d = 0.30$
- Rolling resistance coefficient $\mu_{rr} = 0.012$
- Wheel radius $r_w = 0.32 \text{ m}$
- Overall gear ratio $G = 8.5$
- Gearbox efficiency $\eta_g = 0.96$
- Transmission (overall drivetrain) efficiency $\eta_t = 0.92$
- Air density $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Equivalent mass factor $\delta = 1.08$

**Performance targets:**
- Maximum speed on level road: $v_{max} = 140 \text{ km/h}$
- Maximum grade at $v = 30 \text{ km/h}$: 25 %
- 0 → 100 km/h acceleration time: $t_a = 10 \text{ s}$

**(a)** Compute the **peak power** required at the motor shaft to achieve $v_{max}$ on a level road. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **peak torque** required at the motor shaft to launch the car on a 25 % grade at $v = 30 \text{ km/h}$, neglecting aerodynamic drag at this low speed. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution Q-A:**

**Part (a) — Peak Power for $v_{max}$ (7 marks):**

Step 1: Convert $v_{max}$ to m/s.

$$v_{max} = 140 \times \frac{1000}{3600} = 38.89 \text{ m/s}$$

Step 2: For level road, $\alpha = 0$, so $\sin\alpha = 0$, $\cos\alpha = 1$, and at constant speed $a = 0$. Hence $F_{te}$ has only rolling and drag components:

$$F_{rr} = \mu_{rr} \, m \, g = 0.012 \times 1500 \times 9.81 = 176.58 \text{ N}$$

$$F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f v^2 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times (38.89)^2$$

$$F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times 1512.4 = 611.8 \text{ N}$$

Step 3: Total tractive effort at $v_{max}$:

$$F_{te} = 176.58 + 611.8 = 788.4 \text{ N}$$

Step 4: Wheel power:

$$P_w = F_{te} \cdot v_{max} = 788.4 \times 38.89 = 30{,}663 \text{ W} \approx 30.66 \text{ kW}$$

Step 5: Motor peak power (through drivetrain):

$$P_{m,peak} = \frac{P_w}{\eta_t} = \frac{30{,}663}{0.92} = 33{,}329 \text{ W} \approx \mathbf{33.3 \text{ kW}}$$

**Part (b) — Peak Torque for 25 % Grade Launch (7 marks):**

Step 1: Convert grade to angle (small-angle option valid for 25 %):

$$\alpha = \arctan(0.25) = 14.04^\circ$$

Step 2: At $v = 30$ km/h on a 25 % grade at crawl, we evaluate $F_{te}$ with $a = 0$ (steady launch speed) and neglect $F_{ad}$ as instructed:

$$v = 30 \times \frac{1000}{3600} = 8.333 \text{ m/s}$$

$$F_{rr} = \mu_{rr} \, m \, g \cos\alpha = 0.012 \times 1500 \times 9.81 \times \cos(14.04^\circ)$$

$$F_{rr} = 0.012 \times 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.9701 = 171.3 \text{ N}$$

$$F_{hc} = m \, g \sin\alpha = 1500 \times 9.81 \times \sin(14.04^\circ)$$

$$F_{hc} = 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.2425 = 3568.5 \text{ N}$$

Step 3: Total tractive effort at the wheels:

$$F_{te} = 171.3 + 3568.5 = 3739.8 \text{ N}$$

Step 4: Wheel torque:

$$T_w = F_{te} \cdot r_w = 3739.8 \times 0.32 = 1196.7 \text{ N·m}$$

Step 5: Motor torque through gearbox:

$$T_{m,peak} = \frac{T_w}{G \cdot \eta_g} = \frac{1196.7}{8.5 \times 0.96} = \frac{1196.7}{8.16} \approx \mathbf{146.7 \text{ N·m}}$$

**Final Result:** Motor must be capable of delivering at least **33.3 kW peak power** and **~147 N·m peak torque**, with appropriate speed rating and continuous-power rating derived from a typical drive cycle (which would be done in a follow-up Part-B sub-question).

---

**Q-B. [KTU University Exam – Past Pattern, Module 2] — 14 Marks**

A light electric 3-wheeler (e-rickshaw) has the following parameters:
- Total mass $m = 600 \text{ kg}$ (vehicle + 4 passengers)
- $C_d = 0.45$, $A_f = 1.8 \text{ m}^2$, $\mu_{rr} = 0.018$, $r_w = 0.28 \text{ m}$
- $\eta_g = 0.94$, $\eta_t = 0.90$, $G = 6.5$, $\delta = 1.05$
- Target maximum speed on level road: $v_{max} = 45 \text{ km/h}$
- Target 0 → 30 km/h time: $t_a = 8 \text{ s}$

**(a)** Compute the **motor power** required for $v_{max}$ on a level road and the corresponding **motor speed** in rpm. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **average tractive power** required during 0 → 30 km/h acceleration, and the **motor peak torque** at launch. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution Q-B:**

**Part (a) — Motor Power at $v_{max}$ and Motor Speed (7 marks):**

Step 1: Convert $v_{max}$:

$$v_{max} = 45 \times \frac{5}{18} = 12.5 \text{ m/s}$$

Step 2: Resistive forces on level road at $v_{max}$:

$$F_{rr} = 0.018 \times 600 \times 9.81 = 105.95 \text{ N}$$

$$F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.45 \times 1.8 \times (12.5)^2 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.45 \times 1.8 \times 156.25 = 77.43 \text{ N}$$

$$F_{te} = 105.95 + 77.43 = 183.38 \text{ N}$$

Step 3: Wheel power:

$$P_w = 183.38 \times 12.5 = 2292.3 \text{ W}$$

Step 4: Motor power:

$$P_m = \frac{2292.3}{0.90} = \mathbf{2547 \text{ W} \approx 2.55 \text{ kW}}$$

Step 5: Motor speed in rpm:

$$N_m = \frac{60 \times G \times v_{max}}{2\pi \, r_w} = \frac{60 \times 6.5 \times 12.5}{2\pi \times 0.28} = \frac{4875}{1.7593} = \mathbf{2771 \text{ rpm}}$$

**Part (b) — Average Tractive Power and Launch Torque (7 marks):**

Step 1: Convert target speed:

$$v_f = 30 \times \frac{5}{18} = 8.333 \text{ m/s}$$

Step 2: Required acceleration:

$$a = \frac{v_f}{t_a} = \frac{8.333}{8} = 1.042 \text{ m/s}^2$$

Step 3: Average tractive force (engineering approximation, $\cos\alpha = 1$ for level road, drag neglected at low speed per standard problem statement):

$$F_{te,\text{avg}} \approx \mu_{rr} m g + \delta m \cdot \frac{a}{2}$$

$$F_{te,\text{avg}} = 105.95 + 1.05 \times 600 \times 0.521 = 105.95 + 328.2 = 434.1 \text{ N}$$

Step 4: Average tractive power (assuming linear velocity rise, average velocity = $v_f/2$):

$$P_{w,\text{avg}} = 434.1 \times \frac{8.333}{2} = 434.1 \times 4.167 = 1808.8 \text{ W}$$

Step 5: Peak launch tractive force (at $v = 0$, $a = 1.042$ m/s², drag $\approx 0$):

$$F_{te,\text{launch}} = \mu_{rr} m g + \delta m a = 105.95 + 1.05 \times 600 \times 1.042 = 105.95 + 656.5 = 762.4 \text{ N}$$

Step 6: Wheel torque at launch:

$$T_w = F_{te,\text{launch}} \cdot r_w = 762.4 \times 0.28 = 213.5 \text{ N·m}$$

Step 7: Motor torque at launch:

$$T_{m,\text{peak}} = \frac{T_w}{G \cdot \eta_g} = \frac{213.5}{6.5 \times 0.94} = \frac{213.5}{6.11} = \mathbf{34.9 \text{ N·m}}$$

**Final Result:** Motor must be rated at **≥ 2.55 kW** at **~2771 rpm** with a **peak torque of at least ~35 N·m** at launch.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Sizing Problems:**
> 1. **Unit confusion on velocity** — KTU often gives speed in **km/h** in the problem statement. Forgetting to convert to **m/s** (multiply by 5/18) yields a result off by a factor of 3.6. Always state the conversion explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting $\eta_t$ and $\eta_g$** — Inexperienced students compute wheel-side values and call them "motor" values. KTU examiners explicitly look for the step $P_m = P_w / \eta_t$ and $T_m = T_w / (G \eta_g)$.
> 3. **Ignoring rolling resistance at high speed** — Some students drop $F_{rr}$ when computing max-speed power. This loses 1–2 marks. $F_{rr}$ is small but **not zero** and should be retained unless explicitly told to ignore.
> 4. **Mixing grade % and grade angle** — 25 % grade means $\tan\alpha = 0.25$, *not* $\alpha = 25^\circ$. KTU gives 0.5 marks just for correctly identifying this.
> 5. **Sign of $F_{la}$** — For deceleration (braking/regen), $a$ is negative. In sizing problems with regen, the formula changes sign and power flows back to the battery. KTU has a CO-level question on this in the 2024 scheme.
> 6. **Using the wrong mass** — Some students use **kerb weight** and forget the **passenger + cargo mass**. The problem statement's $m$ is always the **total ready-to-drive mass**.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Sizing the Propulsion Motor" (Module 2):**
> - [ ] Always start with the **four-force equation**: $F_{te} = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{la}$.
> - [ ] **Rolling resistance:** $F_{rr} = \mu_{rr} m g \cos\alpha$ — small but persistent.
> - [ ] **Aerodynamic drag:** $F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f v^2$ — dominant at $v > 60$ km/h.
> - [ ] **Grade force:** $F_{hc} = m g \sin\alpha$ — dominant for hill-climb sizing.
> - [ ] **Acceleration force:** $F_{la} = \delta m a$ — dominant for 0–100 km/h sizing; $\delta \approx 1.05$–$1.10$.
> - [ ] Convert all **velocities to m/s** before plugging in.
> - [ ] Convert all **grades from % to angle** using $\alpha = \arctan(\%G/100)$.
> - [ ] **Wheel-side** values: $P_w = F_{te} \cdot v$, $T_w = F_{te} \cdot r_w$.
> - [ ] **Motor-side** values: $P_m = P_w / \eta_t$, $T_m = T_w / (G \eta_g)$, $N_m = (60 G v) / (2\pi r_w)$.
> - [ ] **Peak power** ← $v_{max}$ or acceleration; **Peak torque** ← launch / grade; **Continuous power** ← cruise.
> - [ ] Motor's **torque–speed envelope** has a **constant-torque** region (below base speed) and a **constant-power** region (above base speed, in field weakening).
> - [ ] Always check that the **vehicle load curve** lies **inside** the motor envelope, with a **15–25 % safety margin**.
> - [ ] The chosen motor determines downstream specs: **battery power/energy**, **inverter current**, **cooling system**, and **VCU torque map**.
> - [ ] Standard constants to memorize: $g = 9.81$, $\rho = 1.225$, $\mu_{rr} \approx 0.01$, $C_d \approx 0.3$ for sedans.
> - [ ] Remember the **rotational inertia factor $\delta$** — losing this in an exam is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.

**End of Notes — Sizing the Propulsion Motor, Module 2, PEAET742.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sizing the Power Electronics - Switch Technology Selection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET742 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES AND RENEWABLE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sizing the Power Electronics - Switch Technology Selection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Power Electronics Sizing** is the systematic engineering process of determining the optimal voltage, current, switching frequency, and thermal ratings of semiconductor switching devices used in the power conversion stage of an Electric Vehicle (EV) powertrain. **Switch Technology Selection** is the subsequent decision-making step where the appropriate semiconductor family (e.g., IGBT, MOSFET, SiC-MOSFET, GaN-HEMT) is chosen based on the application voltage, power level, switching frequency, efficiency target, and thermal constraints.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, this topic addresses the *intersection* of power semiconductor physics, thermal engineering, and EV system-level requirements, ensuring the selected switch operates within its **Safe Operating Area (SOA)** throughout the mission profile of the vehicle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The selection is **NOT** a "biggest is best" exercise. The switch must be *optimally* sized — over-rating increases conduction losses, cost, and packaging parasitics, while under-rating leads to catastrophic thermal runaway and SOA violation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Understanding)

Think of selecting a power switch like **choosing the right size of water pipeline for a city**:
- A **pipeline too narrow** (under-rated switch) → water pressure builds up, pipe bursts (device fails due to over-voltage/over-current).
- A **pipeline too wide** (over-rated switch) → water flows sluggishly, costs more, occupies unnecessary space (higher cost, larger footprint, lower efficiency).
- The **ideal pipeline diameter** depends on: peak water demand, pressure, distance, and cost budget — exactly analogous to the EV switch sizing problem.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical EV Range |
|---|---|---|
| DC Bus Voltage | $V_{DC}$ | **400 V** (standard) / **800 V** (modern) |
| Blocking Voltage | $V_{BR}$ | **600 V – 1200 V** |
| Continuous Current | $I_C$ / $I_D$ | **100 A – 1000 A** |
| Switching Frequency | $f_{sw}$ | **5 kHz – 20 kHz** (Si IGBT), **50 kHz – 200 kHz** (SiC/GaN) |
| Junction Temperature | $T_j$ | **≤ 150 °C** (Si), **≤ 200 °C** (SiC) |
| Thermal Resistance (Junction-to-Case) | $R_{th(j-c)}$ | **0.1 – 1.0 °C/W** |
| Derating Factor | $k_d$ | **1.2 – 1.5** (typical for voltage), **1.3 – 2.0** (current) |

> [!NOTE]
> A **derating factor** is a safety multiplier applied to the theoretical rating. For instance, a 600 V device on a 400 V bus uses a 1.5× derating, meaning the actual blocking capability is comfortably above transient spikes.

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in EVs

The traction inverter of an EV processes **60–80% of the total battery energy**. The power switch is the *heart* of this inverter — every electron flowing from the battery to the motor passes through a semiconductor switch. An incorrect selection can:
- Reduce driving range by **5–15%** (excess losses).
- Cause premature failure under hard acceleration.
- Limit the **maximum torque and power** deliverable to the wheels.
- Increase cooling system cost and weight.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Sizing & Selection Framework — Step-by-Step Logic

The KTU 2024 module expects students to follow a **structured, 6-step framework** for switch selection:

### Step 1 — Define the Electrical Operating Envelope
Determine the *worst-case* DC link voltage, peak phase current, and switching frequency. These are dictated by the **battery pack voltage, motor power rating, and PWM strategy**.

### Step 2 — Apply Voltage Derating
Compute the **minimum blocking voltage** $V_{BR(min)}$ by multiplying the peak DC bus voltage by a derating factor $k_{dv}$ (typically **1.2 to 1.5**) to accommodate:
- Regenerative braking transients (DC link rises to ~1.15× nominal).
- Load dump and parasitic inductance spikes ($V_{spike} = L \cdot \frac{di}{dt}$).
- Safety margin against manufacturing tolerance.

### Step 3 — Apply Current Derating
The **continuous current rating** must exceed the RMS phase current by a current derating factor $k_{di}$ (typically **1.3 to 2.0**). Peak currents (during hard acceleration) must respect the **pulse current rating** of the device.

### Step 4 — Evaluate Switching Frequency vs. Loss Trade-off
Higher $f_{sw}$ allows smaller magnetics (inductors, capacitors) but increases **switching losses**, which are *proportional* to $f_{sw}$. A Si IGBT at 10 kHz is acceptable; at 50 kHz, SiC is mandatory.

### Step 5 — Thermal Budget Verification
Calculate total power dissipation $P_{loss} = P_{cond} + P_{sw}$ and ensure the junction temperature $T_j$ stays below the maximum rating using:

$$T_j = T_a + P_{loss} \cdot (R_{th(j-c)} + R_{th(c-s)} + R_{th(s-a)})$$

### Step 6 — Cost, Availability & Packaging
Finally, weigh against the BOM cost, automotive qualification (AEC-Q101), and mechanical footprint.

## 2.2 Switch Technology Comparison (KTU Exam Favourite)

| Switch Type | Voltage Range | Frequency Range | Conduction Loss | Switching Loss | Best EV Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Power BJT** | < 1200 V | < 5 kHz | High (saturation $V_{CE(sat)}$) | High | Legacy low-cost DC-DC |
| **Power MOSFET** | < 250 V | > 100 kHz | $R_{DS(on)}$ dominated | Very Low | **12 V / 48 V auxiliary, OBC low-voltage stage** |
| **IGBT** | 600 V – 1700 V | 5 – 20 kHz | Moderate ($V_{CE(sat)}$ flat) | Moderate | **Traction inverter (400 V mainstream)** |
| **GTO / IGCT** | > 2500 V | < 1 kHz | High | High (tail current) | High-power trains, not EVs |
| **SiC MOSFET** | 600 V – 1700 V | 50 – 200 kHz | Very Low (low $R_{DS(on)}$) | Very Low | **800 V traction inverters, ultra-fast OBC** |
| **GaN HEMT** | < 650 V | 100 – 500 kHz | Very Low | Lowest | **Compact OBC, LDC, low-voltage traction** |

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" of SiC Dominance in Modern EVs:** Silicon Carbide has a **3× wider bandgap (3.26 eV vs 1.12 eV)** and **10× higher critical electric field** than silicon. This allows thinner die with higher breakdown voltage and dramatically lower on-state resistance — making it the **dominant technology for 800 V EV architectures** (Taycan, Ioniq 5/6, Lucid Air).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{BR(min)} = k_{dv} \cdot V_{DC,peak}$ | Minimum blocking voltage after derating | V |
| 2 | $I_{C(min)} = k_{di} \cdot I_{RMS,phase}$ | Minimum continuous current rating | A |
| 3 | $P_{cond} = V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_{avg} + R_{DS(on)} \cdot I_{rms}^{2}$ | Conduction loss (IGBT / MOSFET) | W |
| 4 | $E_{sw} = E_{on} + E_{off}$ | Switching energy per cycle | J |
| 5 | $P_{sw} = E_{sw} \cdot f_{sw}$ | Switching loss | W |
| 6 | $P_{total} = P_{cond} + P_{sw}$ | Total loss per switch | W |
| 7 | $T_j = T_c + P_{loss} \cdot R_{th(j-c)}$ | Junction temperature (with heatsink $T_c$ known) | °C |
| 8 | $R_{th(ja)} = R_{th(jc)} + R_{th(cs)} + R_{th(sa)}$ | Total thermal resistance (junction-to-ambient) | °C/W |
| 9 | $SOA = V_{DS} \cdot I_{D} \le P_{max,inst}$ | Safe Operating Area constraint | W (instantaneous) |
| 10 | $\eta_{inv} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + 6 P_{switch}}$ | Inverter efficiency (6 switches) | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** In Formula 6, students often forget the factor of **6** (three legs × two switches per leg) in Formula 10. The total inverter loss is **6 × P_switch** for a three-phase two-level VSI.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The framework above is not academic — it is used **daily** by Tier-1 automotive suppliers (Bosch, ZF, BorgWarner, Vitesco, BYD) to qualify inverters for production. The AEC-Q101 standard *requires* that all power semiconductors pass **1000 hours of HTOL (High Temperature Operating Life) testing** at 80% of rated $V_{BR}$ — which is why conservative derating is non-negotiable in production designs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: Conduction Loss of an IGBT in a Traction Inverter

**Problem Setup:** A three-phase VSI feeds a traction motor with sinusoidal PWM. The IGBT switches are in saturation during the ON-state, characterized by a constant $V_{CE(sat)}$ and a dynamic resistance $r_{CE}$. The phase current is sinusoidal: $i(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t)$.

**Step 1 — Instantaneous Conduction Loss**
The instantaneous power dissipated in the IGBT is:

$$p_{cond}(t) = V_{CE(sat)} \cdot i(t) + r_{CE} \cdot i^{2}(t)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Sinusoidal Current**
Insert $i(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_{cond}(t) &= V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_m \sin(\omega t) + r_{CE} \cdot I_m^{2} \sin^{2}(\omega t)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Average Over One Fundamental Cycle**
Average the first term over a full cycle: the average of $\sin(\omega t)$ over $0$ to $2\pi$ is **0**, so the $V_{CE(sat)}$ term does **not** contribute to average loss.

The average of $\sin^{2}(\omega t)$ is $\tfrac{1}{2}$. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{cond,avg} &= V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_m \cdot \underbrace{\frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{0}^{2\pi} \sin\theta \, d\theta}_{= \, 0} + r_{CE} \cdot I_m^{2} \cdot \underbrace{\frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{0}^{2\pi} \sin^{2}\theta \, d\theta}_{= \, 1/2} \\
&= 0 + r_{CE} \cdot I_m^{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Express in Terms of RMS Current**
Since $I_{rms} = I_m / \sqrt{2}$, we have $I_m^{2}/2 = I_{rms}^{2}$:

$$\boxed{P_{cond} = V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_{avg} + r_{CE} \cdot I_{rms}^{2}}$$

For a sinusoidal PWM with modulation index $m_a$, the average IGBT current over the fundamental cycle is $I_{avg} = I_m / \pi$. The final form used in industry is:

$$P_{cond} = \frac{V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_m}{\pi} + \frac{r_{CE} \cdot I_m^{2}}{4}$$

## 3.2 Derivation II: Total Switching Loss vs. Frequency Trade-off

**Setup:** A switch transitions between ON and OFF in finite times $t_{on}$ and $t_{off}$, with the voltage falling/rising linearly during the transition. The energy dissipated per switch event is the integral of $v \cdot i$ overlap.

**Step 1 — Turn-ON Energy**
During turn-on, voltage falls linearly from $V_{DC}$ to 0 over $t_{on}$ while current rises linearly from 0 to $I$:

$$E_{on} = \int_{0}^{t_{on}} V_{DC} \left(1 - \frac{t}{t_{on}}\right) \cdot I \cdot \frac{t}{t_{on}} \, dt$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the Integral**
The integrand is a product of a linearly decreasing voltage and a linearly increasing current. Evaluating:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{on} &= V_{DC} \cdot I \int_{0}^{t_{on}} \left(\frac{t}{t_{on}} - \frac{t^{2}}{t_{on}^{2}}\right) dt \\
&= V_{DC} \cdot I \left[\frac{t^{2}}{2 t_{on}} - \frac{t^{3}}{3 t_{on}^{2}}\right]_{0}^{t_{on}} \\
&= V_{DC} \cdot I \left[\frac{t_{on}}{2} - \frac{t_{on}}{3}\right] \\
&= V_{DC} \cdot I \cdot \frac{t_{on}}{6}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Turn-OFF Energy (Analogous)**
By symmetry, $E_{off} = \tfrac{1}{6} \cdot V_{DC} \cdot I \cdot t_{off}$ — although in practice IGBTs have a **tail current** that makes $E_{off}$ larger.

**Step 4 — Total Switching Loss**
For switching frequency $f_{sw}$:

$$\boxed{P_{sw} = (E_{on} + E_{off}) \cdot f_{sw} = \frac{V_{DC} \cdot I}{6} \cdot (t_{on} + t_{off}) \cdot f_{sw}}$$

**Step 5 — The Critical Insight**
$P_{sw} \propto f_{sw}$. If you double the switching frequency (to shrink magnetics), the switching losses *also double*. This is the fundamental trade-off that drives the migration from **Si IGBT → SiC MOSFET** in modern 800 V EV inverters.

## 3.3 Derivation III: Junction Temperature Calculation (Complete Thermal Chain)

**Given:** $P_{loss} = 85$ W per switch, $R_{th(j-c)} = 0.3$ °C/W (junction-to-case), $R_{th(c-s)} = 0.15$ °C/W (case-to-heatsink with thermal interface material), $R_{th(s-a)} = 0.25$ °C/W (heatsink-to-ambient with forced-air cooling), ambient temperature $T_a = 45$ °C.

**Step 1 — Total Thermal Resistance (Junction-to-Ambient)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{th(j-a)} &= R_{th(j-c)} + R_{th(c-s)} + R_{th(s-a)} \\
&= 0.3 + 0.15 + 0.25 \\
&= 0.70 \; \text{°C/W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Junction Temperature Rise Above Ambient**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T &= P_{loss} \cdot R_{th(j-a)} \\
&= 85 \cdot 0.70 \\
&= 59.5 \; \text{°C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Absolute Junction Temperature**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_j &= T_a + \Delta T \\
&= 45 + 59.5 \\
&= 104.5 \; \text{°C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify Against SOA**
Maximum $T_j$ for Si IGBT is typically **150 °C**. The safety margin is $150 - 104.5 = 45.5$ °C, which is healthy for an EV traction application. If the calculated $T_j$ were $>150$ °C, the designer would need to either (a) select a switch with lower $R_{th(j-c)}$, (b) increase heatsink capacity, or (c) reduce $f_{sw}$ to cut $P_{sw}$.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing Between IGBT and SiC

| Decision Criterion | IGBT Wins When... | SiC MOSFET Wins When... |
|---|---|---|
| DC Bus Voltage | ≤ 600 V | ≥ 800 V |
| Switching Frequency | ≤ 10 kHz | ≥ 20 kHz |
| Peak Power | ≤ 150 kW | ≥ 200 kW (better current sharing) |
| Cost Sensitivity | High (mass-market EV) | Premium / performance EV |
| Cooling System | Liquid cooling available | Limited / wants air cooling |
| Efficiency Target | ≥ 95% | ≥ 97% required |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Switch Selection Decision Flowchart (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define EV Powertrain Specs] --> B[Determine V_DC and I_peak from Motor Rating]
    B --> C{DC Bus Voltage?}
    C -->|less than 400 V| D[Consider MOSFET or GaN HEMT]
    C -->|400 V to 800 V| E{Peak Power?}
    C -->|greater than 800 V| F[SiC MOSFET Mandatory]
    E -->|less than 150 kW| G[IGBT Viable]
    E -->|greater than 150 kW| H[SiC MOSFET Preferred]
    D --> I[Check Switching Frequency Target]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    F --> I
    I --> J{f_sw target?}
    J -->|less than 10 kHz| K[IGBT or SiC both work]
    J -->|10 to 50 kHz| L[IGBT borderline, SiC preferred]
    J -->|greater than 50 kHz| M[SiC or GaN mandatory]
    K --> N[Apply Voltage and Current Derating]
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Compute Total Loss P_cond + P_sw]
    O --> P[Verify Junction Temperature T_j within SOA]
    P --> Q{T_j less than 150 C?}
    Q -->|Yes| R[Select Switch and Heatsink]
    Q -->|No| S[Upgrade to SiC or Improve Cooling]
    R --> T[Final Selection Validated]
    S --> O
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an EV Traction Inverter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Battery Pack 400 V / 800 V DC] --> B[DC Link Capacitor Bank]
    B --> C[Three Phase VSI Power Stage]
    C --> C1[Switch S1 Upper IGBT SiC MOSFET]
    C --> C2[Switch S2 Lower IGBT SiC MOSFET]
    C --> C3[Diode D1 D2 Freewheeling]
    C1 --> D[Gate Driver Board with Isolated DC DC]
    C2 --> D
    D --> E[Vehicle Control Unit VCU]
    E --> F[Resolver Encoder on Motor]
    F --> E
    C --> G[Three Phase AC Output to Traction Motor]
    G --> H[Traction Motor PMSM or Induction]
    H --> I[Reduction Gearbox]
    I --> J[Wheels]
    C --> K[Cold Plate Liquid Cooling]
    K --> C
```

## 4.3 Loss Breakdown in a Power Switch (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Power Loss P_total] --> B[Conduction Loss P_cond]
    A --> C[Switching Loss P_sw]
    A --> D[Blocking Loss P_block]
    B --> B1[V_CE sat times I_avg for IGBT]
    B --> B2[R_DS on times I_rms squared for MOSFET]
    C --> C1[Turn ON Energy E_on times f_sw]
    C --> C2[Turn OFF Energy E_off times f_sw]
    C --> C3[Reverse Recovery E_rr times f_sw]
    D --> D1[Leakage current times V_DC]
    D1 --> E[Usually negligible less than 0.1 percent]
```

## 4.4 Thermal Resistance Network (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Junction T_j] -->|R_th_jc| B[Case T_c]
    B -->|R_th_cs with TIM| C[Heatsink T_s]
    C -->|R_th_sa with convection| D[Ambient T_a]
    A --> E[Power Dissipation P_loss injected]
    E --> A
    style A fill:#ffd700,stroke:#333
    style D fill:#87ceeb,stroke:#333
```

> [!NOTE]
> The thermal network is **analogous to an electrical circuit** — temperature is "voltage," power dissipation is "current," and thermal resistance is "resistance." The thermal-Electrical analogy is: $T \leftrightarrow V$, $P \leftrightarrow I$, $R_{th} \leftrightarrow R$. This is heavily tested in KTU university exams.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
> **Define Safe Operating Area (SOA) of a power semiconductor switch. List any four parameters that limit the SOA.**

**Model Answer:**
The Safe Operating Area (SOA) is the locus of voltage and current pairs ($V_{CE}$, $I_C$) over which a power switch can be operated *simultaneously* without entering thermal runaway or secondary breakdown. It is typically represented on a log-log plot of $V$ vs. $I$ bounded by four limits:
1. **Maximum continuous current limit** — bounded by the bond-wire current carrying capacity.
2. **Maximum peak current limit** — bounded by the latch-up limit of the internal parasitic thyristor.
3. **RBSOA (Reverse Bias SOA)** — the energy the device can absorb during inductive turn-off.
4. **Thermal limit line** — set by the maximum junction temperature $T_{j,max}$ and the transient thermal impedance $Z_{th(j-c)}(t)$.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Exam]**
> **Why is SiC MOSFET preferred over Si IGBT in modern 800 V EV traction inverters? Give four reasons.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Higher breakdown voltage per unit die thickness** — 10× critical electric field of Si, enabling smaller die for same $V_{BR}$.
2. **Lower switching losses** — no tail current (unlike IGBT), enabling 5–10× higher $f_{sw}$ (50–200 kHz), which shrinks magnetic components.
3. **Higher junction temperature capability** — $T_{j,max} = 200$ °C vs. 150 °C for Si, reducing cooling system burden.
4. **Lower conduction losses** at high voltage — $R_{DS(on)}$ for SiC scales much more favorably with blocking voltage than IGBT's $V_{CE(sat)}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Q3(a) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Exam]**
> **Question A (Choice 1):**
> **(a)** Derive the expression for the average conduction loss of an IGBT operating in a sinusoidal PWM three-phase voltage source inverter fed by a DC link voltage $V_{DC}$. Assume sinusoidal phase current $i(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t)$, IGBT saturation voltage $V_{CE(sat)}$ and dynamic resistance $r_{CE}$. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 3-phase VSI uses IGBTs with $V_{CE(sat)} = 1.8$ V and $r_{CE} = 15$ mΩ. The DC link is 400 V, peak phase current is 300 A, and the switching frequency is 10 kHz. Given that $E_{on} + E_{off} = 4$ mJ per switching event, calculate (i) the total conduction loss per IGBT, (ii) the total switching loss per IGBT, and (iii) the inverter efficiency if the output power is 80 kW. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**
This is the standard derivation shown in Section 3.1. Key steps the examiner expects:
1. Express instantaneous conduction loss: $p_{cond}(t) = V_{CE(sat)} \cdot i(t) + r_{CE} \cdot i^{2}(t)$ — **1 mark**
2. Substitute $i(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t)$ — **1 mark**
3. Recognize that the average of $\sin(\omega t)$ over one cycle is zero — **1 mark**
4. Recognize that the average of $\sin^{2}(\omega t)$ is $\tfrac{1}{2}$ — **1 mark**
5. Express final result using $I_{avg} = I_m/\pi$ and $I_{rms} = I_m/\sqrt{2}$ — **2 marks**
6. Final boxed answer: $P_{cond} = \frac{V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_m}{\pi} + \frac{r_{CE} \cdot I_m^{2}}{4}$ — **1 mark**

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Conduction Loss:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_m &= 300 \; \text{A} \\
P_{cond} &= \frac{1.8 \cdot 300}{\pi} + \frac{0.015 \cdot 300^{2}}{4} \\
&= \frac{540}{3.1416} + \frac{0.015 \cdot 90000}{4} \\
&= 171.89 + 337.5 \\
&= 509.4 \; \text{W per IGBT}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Switching Loss:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{sw} &= E_{sw} \cdot f_{sw} = 4 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 10 \times 10^{3} = 40 \; \text{W per IGBT}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Inverter Efficiency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{total \, loss} &= 6 \cdot (P_{cond} + P_{sw}) = 6 \cdot (509.4 + 40) = 3296.4 \; \text{W} \\
\eta &= \frac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_{loss}} = \frac{80000}{80000 + 3296.4} \\
&= \frac{80000}{83296.4} \\
&= 0.9604 = 96.04\%
\end{aligned}
$$

---

> **Q3(b) [Internal Choice — Alternative Question]**
> **Question B (Choice 2):**
> **(a)** Explain the step-by-step methodology for selecting a power semiconductor switch for an EV traction inverter application. List the six essential criteria that govern the selection. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A power MOSFET is used in a 48 V to 12 V DC-DC converter (Low-Voltage DC-DC, LDC) of an EV. The MOSFET has $R_{DS(on)} = 8$ mΩ at $T_j = 100$ °C. The RMS current through the switch is 60 A. If the switching frequency is 50 kHz and the total switching energy per cycle is 8 μJ, calculate (i) the conduction loss, (ii) the switching loss, and (iii) the total loss. The ambient temperature is 40 °C, $R_{th(j-c)} = 0.5$ °C/W, $R_{th(c-s)} = 0.2$ °C/W, and $R_{th(s-a)} = 1.0$ °C/W. Verify whether the junction temperature stays within the 150 °C SOA limit. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Six Criteria (7 marks):**
The 6-step framework from Section 2.1: (1) electrical operating envelope, (2) voltage derating, (3) current derating, (4) switching frequency trade-off, (5) thermal budget, (6) cost & packaging — award 1 mark each, with **1 bonus mark** for explaining the *trade-off philosophy* (over-rating vs. under-rating).

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Conduction Loss:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{cond} &= R_{DS(on)} \cdot I_{rms}^{2} = 0.008 \cdot 60^{2} = 0.008 \cdot 3600 = 28.8 \; \text{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Switching Loss:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{sw} &= E_{sw} \cdot f_{sw} = 8 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 50 \times 10^{3} = 0.4 \; \text{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Total Loss and Junction Temperature:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{total} &= 28.8 + 0.4 = 29.2 \; \text{W} \\
R_{th(j-a)} &= 0.5 + 0.2 + 1.0 = 1.7 \; \text{°C/W} \\
\Delta T &= 29.2 \cdot 1.7 = 49.64 \; \text{°C} \\
T_j &= 40 + 49.64 = 89.64 \; \text{°C}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $T_j = 89.64$ °C $< T_{j,max} = 150$ °C, the design is **SAFE** with a margin of $60.36$ °C. **(2 marks for verification)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Most common mistake (–2 marks):** Students forget to multiply the *per-switch* loss by **6** when computing inverter efficiency. Always remember: a 3-phase VSI has **6 switches**.
> 2. **Second common mistake (–1 mark):** Using the DC bus voltage as the blocking voltage requirement without adding the derating factor $k_{dv} = 1.2$–$1.5$.
> 3. **Third common mistake (–1 mark):** Confusing $R_{DS(on)}$ (which gives $I^{2}R$ loss, increases with current) with $V_{CE(sat)}$ (which gives $V \cdot I$ loss, more linear). They have *different* loss mechanisms.
> 4. **Fourth common mistake (–1 mark):** Omitting the thermal interface material (TIM) resistance $R_{th(c-s)}$ — it is *not* free; in practice, it contributes 20–40% of the total thermal resistance.
> 5. **Exam Hall Tip:** Always draw a clearly labeled thermal resistance network diagram before plugging into equations. Examiners allocate **partial marks** for the correct diagram even if the arithmetic is wrong.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Power switch sizing** is a 6-step framework: envelope → voltage derate → current derate → $f_{sw}$ trade-off → thermal verify → cost/availability.
- [x] **Voltage derating factor $k_{dv} = 1.2$–$1.5$** accounts for regenerative transients, parasitic inductance spikes ($L \cdot di/dt$), and safety margin.
- [x] **Current derating factor $k_{di} = 1.3$–$2.0$** accounts for thermal de-rating and peak torque transients.
- [x] **Conduction loss formula:** $P_{cond} = V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_{avg} + r_{CE} \cdot I_{rms}^{2}$ (IGBT) OR $P_{cond} = R_{DS(on)} \cdot I_{rms}^{2}$ (MOSFET).
- [x] **Switching loss formula:** $P_{sw} = (E_{on} + E_{off}) \cdot f_{sw}$ — it is **linearly proportional** to $f_{sw}$.
- [x] **Thermal chain:** $R_{th(j-a)} = R_{th(j-c)} + R_{th(c-s)} + R_{th(s-a)}$ — never forget the TIM (case-to-sink) layer.
- [x] **Inverter efficiency** uses 6× per-switch loss: $\eta = P_{out} / (P_{out} + 6 P_{switch})$.
- [x] **SiC MOSFET** dominates 800 V EV traction (Taycan, Ioniq 5, Lucid Air) due to no tail current, higher $T_j$, and 5–10× higher $f_{sw}$.
- [x] **GaN HEMT** dominates low-voltage (≤ 650 V) high-frequency applications (compact OBC, LDC, 48 V mild-hybrid).
- [x] **Safe Operating Area (SOA)** must always be verified *before* finalizing the design — failure to do so is the #1 cause of field returns in EV power electronics.
- [x] **Thermal-Electrical Analogy** is exam-favorite: $T \leftrightarrow V$, $P \leftrightarrow I$, $R_{th} \leftrightarrow R$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ripple Capacitor Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET742 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES AND RENEWABLE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ripple Capacitor Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Ripple Capacitor** (also called a *smoothing capacitor*, *filter capacitor*, or *reservoir capacitor*) is a bulk energy-storage capacitor placed at the output of a rectifier or switched-mode power supply (SMPS) to minimize the AC ripple component superimposed on the DC output. The fundamental design objective is to select a capacitance value $C$, voltage rating $V_{rated}$, and Equivalent Series Resistance (ESR) such that the peak-to-peak ripple voltage $V_{r(pp)}$ remains within the tolerance band demanded by the downstream load (motor inverter, BMS, auxiliary DC bus).

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance (Module 2 – Power Electronics for EV Drivetrains and Charging), ripple capacitor design is the process of computing the minimum filter capacitance required to keep the output DC voltage of:
- On-board EV battery chargers (single-phase or three-phase full-wave rectifiers)
- Auxiliary DC-DC converters (HV-LV converters)
- Solar MPPT front-end rectifiers

within the ripple specification mandated by the traction inverter's DC-link tolerance (typically **5–10 %** of nominal DC voltage).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The design of filter components at the output of uncontrolled/controlled rectifiers feeding EV traction drives and renewable energy interfaces is a high-weightage topic. Students must master both the analytical $C_{min}$ calculation and the practical component selection (ESR, ripple current, temperature derating).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Water Tower"

Imagine a city water supply fed by a pump that pushes water in pulses (systolic bursts, like a heart). Without a **water tower (reservoir)**, every pulse would cause pressure spikes and dips at the consumer's tap — a pulsating, unusable flow.

A **ripple capacitor is the electrical equivalent of the water tower**:

| Water Tower Analogy | Electrical Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Pulsating pump | Rectified AC output (pulsating DC) |
| Tower's storage volume | Capacitance $C$ |
| Height of water | Output DC voltage $V_{DC}$ |
| Pressure fluctuations at tap | AC ripple voltage $V_{r}$ |
| Consumer demand rate | Load current $I_{L}$ |
| Valve leakage from tower | Capacitor leakage current & ESR losses |

The bigger the tower (larger $C$), the steadier the pressure at the tap (lower ripple). But the tower must be built tall enough to handle the maximum pump pressure (**voltage rating**) and the walls must be strong enough to handle the refill cycles (**ripple current rating**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (Board-Examiner Ready):** "A ripple capacitor is a filter capacitor connected across the load of a rectifier to shunt the AC ripple component to ground, providing a smooth, near-constant DC output. Its value is chosen so that the time constant $R_L C$ is much greater than the ripple period $T_r$."

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Permittivity of free space:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m
- **Permittivity of electrolytic aluminum oxide:** $\varepsilon_r \approx 8$ to $10$ (for aluminum electrolytics commonly used)
- **Mains frequency (India):** $f_{line} = 50$ Hz $\Rightarrow$ Full-wave rectifier ripple frequency $f_r = 2 f_{line} = 100$ Hz
- **Standard acceptable ripple factor:** $\gamma \leq 5\%$ to $10\%$
- **Typical aluminum electrolytic ESR:** $0.01 \,\Omega$ to $1 \,\Omega$ depending on capacitance and voltage rating
- **Maximum allowable DC-link ripple for traction inverters (typical):** $V_{r(pp)} \leq 5\%$ of $V_{DC,nom}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Origin of Ripple — Why Does It Exist?

An ideal rectifier would produce a pure DC voltage, but practical rectifiers (diodes, thyristors) only *chop* the AC waveform into unidirectional segments. The resulting output is:

$$v_o(t) = V_{DC} + v_{r}(t)$$

where $v_{r}(t)$ is the undesired AC component called **ripple**. The capacitor's job is to *store charge* during the peaks of the rectified waveform and *release charge* into the load during the valleys — effectively averaging the waveform.

### 2.2 Operational Principle — Charge-Discharge Cycle

For a full-wave rectifier with capacitor filter, each rectified half-cycle has period $T_r = 1/f_r = 1/(2f_{line})$. Within one such period:

1. **Charging Phase (Interval $t_1 \to t_2$):** When the rectified source voltage exceeds the capacitor voltage, the diode conducts and rapidly charges $C$ up to near the peak value $V_m$.
2. **Discharging Phase (Interval $t_2 \to t_1 + T_r$):** Once the source voltage falls below the capacitor voltage, the diode becomes reverse-biased and stops conducting. The capacitor then supplies the load current $I_L$ alone, discharging exponentially.

The capacitor's discharge follows:

$$v_C(t) = V_m \cdot e^{-t/(R_L C)} \approx V_m \left(1 - \frac{t}{R_L C}\right) \quad \text{for } t \ll R_L C$$

The linear approximation is valid in the KTU regime of interest (ripple < 10 %), giving the classical textbook result.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Units | Boundary / Validity |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ripple frequency (full-wave) | $f_r$ | $f_r = 2 f_{line}$ | Hz | For bridge & center-tapped FW rectifiers |
| 2 | Ripple period | $T_r$ | $T_r = 1/f_r$ | s | Time between consecutive peaks |
| 3 | DC (average) output voltage | $V_{DC}$ | $V_{DC} = V_m - \dfrac{I_L}{2 f_r C}$ | V | Capacitor-filtered FW rectifier |
| 4 | Peak-to-peak ripple voltage | $V_{r(pp)}$ | $V_{r(pp)} = \dfrac{I_L}{f_r C} = \dfrac{V_m}{2 f_r R_L C}$ | V | Linear discharge assumption |
| 5 | RMS ripple voltage | $V_{r(rms)}$ | $V_{r(rms)} = \dfrac{V_{r(pp)}}{2\sqrt{3}}$ | V | For sawtooth/triangular ripple shape |
| 6 | Ripple factor | $\gamma$ | $\gamma = \dfrac{V_{r(rms)}}{V_{DC}} = \dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{3} f_r R_L C}$ | dimensionless | Should be $\ll 1$ |
| 7 | Minimum capacitance | $C_{min}$ | $C_{min} = \dfrac{I_L}{f_r \cdot V_{r(pp),max}}$ | F | Solve ripple constraint for $C$ |
| 8 | Time constant | $\tau$ | $\tau = R_L C$ | s | Should be $\gg T_r$ |
| 9 | Diode conduction angle | $2\theta$ | $2\theta \approx \sqrt{2 V_{r(pp)} / V_m}$ | rad | Small-angle approximation |
| 10 | RMS ripple current through $C$ | $I_{C,rms}$ | $I_{C,rms} \approx \dfrac{I_L}{\sqrt{3}} \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{4\sqrt{3}}{9}\cdot\dfrac{1}{R_L C f_r}}$ | A | Used for capacitor heating check |
| 11 | Stored energy | $E$ | $E = \dfrac{1}{2} C V^2$ | J | Hold-up time calculation |
| 12 | Hold-up time | $t_{hold}$ | $t_{hold} = R_L C \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{V_1}{V_2}\right)$ | s | From $V_1$ to $V_2$ discharge |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Safety Note:** All absolute values and conditions in the table above use $\vert \cdot \vert$ or descriptive text — never the unescaped pipe character — to preserve markdown table integrity.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility in EV & Renewable Systems

- **On-board EV Chargers:** Ripple capacitor on the rectified mains bus ensures the DC link feeding the PFC stage and isolated DC-DC converter has low enough ripple to comply with **IEC 61851-23** charging standards.
- **Solar PV Micro-inverters / String Inverters:** Output filter capacitor smooths the rectified PV array output before MPPT boost stage.
- **48 V Mild-Hybrid DC-DC Converters:** Output LC filter with carefully designed $C$ keeps voltage ripple below 1 % to prevent ECU malfunction.
- **Wind Turbine Rectifiers:** Filter capacitors on the diode-rectified output of a PMSG absorb the high-frequency switching ripple and the 6-pulse harmonic ripple from uncontrolled rectifiers.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Peak-to-Peak Ripple Voltage for Full-Wave Rectifier with Capacitor Filter

**Starting Physical Setup:**
- Full-wave bridge rectifier with peak voltage $V_m$, load $R_L$ drawing current $I_L = V_{DC}/R_L$
- Filter capacitor $C$ across the load
- Ripple frequency $f_r = 2 f_{line}$ → ripple period $T_r = 1/f_r$

**Step 1:** During the discharge interval (most of the period), the capacitor alone supplies the load current. The charge removed from the capacitor in one period is:

$$\Delta Q = I_L \cdot T_r = \frac{I_L}{f_r}$$

**Step 2:** This charge removal causes the capacitor voltage to fall by:

$$\Delta V = \frac{\Delta Q}{C} = \frac{I_L}{f_r \, C}$$

This $\Delta V$ is, by definition, the peak-to-peak ripple voltage.

**Step 3:** Therefore, the fundamental ripple equation is:

$$\boxed{V_{r(pp)} = \frac{I_L}{f_r \, C}}$$

> **Logic of the Conversion:** The capacitor is a charge reservoir. The amount of charge drained by the load in one ripple period equals the load current times that period. Since $Q = C V$, draining this charge lowers the voltage by $\Delta Q / C$.

**Step 4:** Substituting $I_L = V_{DC}/R_L$ and $V_{DC} \approx V_m$ (for small ripple):

$$V_{r(pp)} = \frac{V_m}{2 f_r R_L C}$$

**Step 5:** Solving for the minimum capacitance to meet a specified ripple specification $V_{r(pp)}^{spec}$:

$$\boxed{C_{min} = \frac{I_L}{f_r \cdot V_{r(pp)}^{spec}}}$$

This is the single most important design equation in ripple capacitor design for KTU problems.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — RMS Ripple Voltage and Ripple Factor

**Step 1:** Assume the discharge waveform is approximately linear (sawtooth), which holds for ripple $\ll V_{DC}$. The waveform is:

$$v_r(t) = V_{r(pp)} \left(1 - \frac{t}{T_r}\right) \quad \text{for } 0 \le t \le T_r$$

**Step 2:** Compute the RMS value over one period:

$$V_{r(rms)}^2 = \frac{1}{T_r} \int_0^{T_r} v_r^2(t) \, dt$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the integral:

$$V_{r(rms)}^2 = \frac{V_{r(pp)}^2}{T_r} \int_0^{T_r} \left(1 - \frac{t}{T_r}\right)^2 dt$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $u = 1 - t/T_r$, $du = -dt/T_r$:

$$\int_0^{T_r} \left(1 - \frac{t}{T_r}\right)^2 dt = T_r \int_0^1 u^2 du = \frac{T_r}{3}$$

**Step 5:** Therefore:

$$V_{r(rms)}^2 = \frac{V_{r(pp)}^2}{3} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad V_{r(rms)} = \frac{V_{r(pp)}}{\sqrt{3}}$$

Wait — for a symmetric triangular waveform (centered around zero), the factor is $2\sqrt{3}$ in the denominator. For a *unipolar sawtooth* (capacitor discharge from peak to peak), it is $\sqrt{3}$. Board-correct form is:

$$\boxed{V_{r(rms)} = \frac{V_{r(pp)}}{2\sqrt{3}} \quad \text{(symmetric triangular AC component)}}$$

The KTU convention uses the symmetric form because the ripple is defined as the AC component about the DC mean.

**Step 6:** Ripple factor definition:

$$\gamma = \frac{V_{r(rms)}}{V_{DC}}$$

$$\boxed{\gamma = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{3} \, f_r \, R_L \, C}}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Design Procedure with Worked Numerical Example

**Problem (KTU-Style):**
A single-phase full-wave bridge rectifier operating from 230 V, 50 Hz Indian mains supplies a DC load of 50 V, 2 A in an EV auxiliary power module. Design a smoothing capacitor such that the peak-to-peak ripple voltage does not exceed 5 % of $V_{DC}$. Also compute the ripple factor and ripple frequency.

**Given Data:**
- $V_{rms,line} = 230$ V, $f_{line} = 50$ Hz
- $V_{DC} = 50$ V, $I_L = 2$ A
- Allowed ripple: $V_{r(pp)} \le 0.05 \times 50 = 2.5$ V

**Step 1:** Compute peak source voltage (assuming 1:1 transformer tap for simplicity, or use direct mains and step-down — here we use a step-down so that peak rectified voltage approximates the required DC plus diode drops):

$$V_m = V_{DC} + 2 V_D + \frac{V_{r(pp)}}{2} \approx 50 + 2(0.7) + 1.25 = 52.65 \text{ V}$$

Take $V_m \approx 53$ V for margin.

**Step 2:** Ripple frequency for full-wave:

$$f_r = 2 \times 50 = 100 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 3:** Compute minimum capacitance using the fundamental ripple equation:

$$C_{min} = \frac{I_L}{f_r \cdot V_{r(pp)}^{spec}} = \frac{2}{100 \times 2.5}$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate:

$$C_{min} = \frac{2}{250} = 8 \times 10^{-3} \text{ F} = 8000 \,\mu F$$

**Step 5:** Apply engineering safety factor (typically 1.5× to 2× to account for ESR, aging, temperature):

$$C_{design} = 2 \times C_{min} = 16000 \,\mu F$$

Select the next standard value: **$C = 22000 \,\mu F$** (standard E12/E24 electrolytic series).

**Step 6:** Voltage rating — choose at least 1.25× to 1.5× the working voltage for derating:

$$V_{rated} \ge 1.5 \times V_m \approx 1.5 \times 53 = 79.5 \text{ V} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{V_{rated} = 100 \text{ V (standard)}}$$

**Step 7:** Ripple factor check:

$$\gamma = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{3} \cdot 100 \cdot \left(\frac{50}{2}\right) \cdot 22000 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\gamma = \frac{1}{2 \times 1.732 \times 100 \times 25 \times 0.022} = \frac{1}{190.5} \approx 0.00525 = 0.525\%$$

This is well below 5 % — design is conservative and acceptable.

**Step 8:** Ripple current rating of the capacitor. The RMS ripple current through the electrolytic must be checked:

$$I_{C,rms} \approx \frac{I_L}{2\sqrt{3}} = \frac{2}{3.464} \approx 0.577 \text{ A}$$

The selected 22000 µF / 100 V aluminum electrolytic must have $I_{ripple}$ rating $\ge 0.6$ A at 100 Hz and 105 °C.

**Final Design Summary:**

$$\boxed{C = 22000 \,\mu F, \quad V_{rated} = 100 \text{ V}, \quad I_{ripple} \ge 0.6 \text{ A at 105 °C, ESR} \le 0.05 \,\Omega}$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Effect of ESR on Output Ripple

A real capacitor is *not* ideal; it has Equivalent Series Resistance $R_{ESR}$. The actual peak-to-peak ripple includes both capacitive and resistive components:

$$V_{r(pp),total} = V_{r(pp),C} + I_{r(peak)} \cdot R_{ESR}$$

where $I_{r(peak)}$ is the peak pulse current through the capacitor at each recharge instant. For an aluminum electrolytic with $C = 22000 \,\mu F$, $R_{ESR} = 0.05 \,\Omega$, and $I_{r(peak)} \approx 2 I_L = 4$ A (approximation):

$$V_{r(pp),ESR} = 4 \times 0.05 = 0.2 \text{ V}$$

This is non-negligible and must be added to the capacitive ripple when the design is tight.

> [!NOTE]
> **Advanced Insight (Often Tested in KTU Module 2 End-Sem):** As electrolytic capacitors age, both $C$ decreases and $R_{ESR}$ increases. A common rule of thumb is that $R_{ESR}$ doubles and $C$ drops to 80 % of nominal at end-of-life. Designers must apply a 1.5× to 2× derating on capacitance to maintain ripple spec across the design life (typically 10 years for EV applications).

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define ripple factor. Derive the expression for the ripple factor of a full-wave rectifier with a capacitor filter in terms of load resistance $R_L$ and capacitance $C$.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Ripple factor $\gamma$ is the ratio of RMS value of the AC ripple component to the absolute value of the DC component in the rectifier output.

$$\gamma = \frac{V_{r(rms)}}{V_{DC}}$$

**Derivation (2 marks):** For a full-wave rectifier, $f_r = 2 f_{line}$. The peak-to-peak ripple is:

$$V_{r(pp)} = \frac{I_L}{f_r C} = \frac{V_{DC}}{f_r R_L C}$$

For a symmetric triangular ripple component:

$$V_{r(rms)} = \frac{V_{r(pp)}}{2\sqrt{3}}$$

Therefore:

$$\gamma = \frac{V_{r(rms)}}{V_{DC}} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{3} \, f_r \, R_L \, C}$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*List any three factors that must be considered in the practical selection of a filter capacitor for an EV on-board charger, beyond just the capacitance value.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Voltage Rating with Derating:** The rated voltage must be at least 1.25× to 1.5× the working peak voltage to handle transients and ensure long-term reliability.
2. **RMS Ripple Current Rating ($I_{ripple}$):** The capacitor's datasheet specifies a maximum allowable RMS ripple current at a given frequency and temperature. Exceeding this causes self-heating and premature failure.
3. **Equivalent Series Resistance (ESR):** Low-ESR capacitors reduce $I^2 R$ losses and lower the resistive component of ripple. Critical at high switching frequencies in EV chargers.

*(Other valid factors: temperature rating, lifetime expectancy, physical size, dielectric type — aluminum electrolytic vs. film vs. ceramic.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**

**(a)** A single-phase full-wave bridge rectifier operates from a 50 Hz, 230 V RMS AC supply through a step-down transformer with turns ratio 5:1. The rectifier feeds a resistive load $R_L = 50 \,\Omega$ through a smoothing capacitor $C = 1000 \,\mu F$. Assuming diode drops of 0.7 V each and continuous conduction:
   1. Calculate the peak secondary voltage $V_m$.
   2. Determine the DC (average) output voltage.
   3. Calculate the peak-to-peak ripple voltage and ripple factor.
   4. Compute the RMS ripple current through the capacitor.

**(b)** With reference to EV on-board chargers, explain with a block diagram the role of the ripple filter capacitor, and discuss how ESR affects the choice of capacitor type and rating.

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Numerical Design (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $V_{primary,rms} = 230$ V, $f_{line} = 50$ Hz
- Turns ratio $N_1:N_2 = 5:1$
- $R_L = 50 \,\Omega$, $C = 1000 \,\mu F$
- $V_D = 0.7$ V per diode

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Secondary RMS voltage:

$$V_{2,rms} = \frac{V_{1,rms}}{a} = \frac{230}{5} = 46 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Peak secondary voltage (before diode drops):

$$V_{m,sec} = \sqrt{2} \times 46 = 65.05 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Peak voltage at filter capacitor output (after 2 diode drops in bridge):

$$V_m = V_{m,sec} - 2 V_D = 65.05 - 1.4 = 63.65 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** DC output voltage (capacitor-filtered FW rectifier):

$$V_{DC} = V_m - \frac{I_L}{2 f_r C}$$

Load current: $I_L = V_{DC}/R_L$. Iterating with $V_{DC} \approx V_m$ as first guess:

$$I_L^{(0)} = \frac{63.65}{50} = 1.273 \text{ A}$$

$$V_{r(pp)} = \frac{1.273}{2 \times 50 \times 1000 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1.273}{0.1} = 12.73 \text{ V}$$

$$V_{DC} = 63.65 - \frac{12.73}{2} = 63.65 - 6.365 = 57.29 \text{ V}$$

Refined current: $I_L = 57.29/50 = 1.146$ A. Recompute ripple:

$$V_{r(pp)} = \frac{1.146}{0.1} = 11.46 \text{ V}$$

$$V_{DC} = 63.65 - 5.73 = 57.92 \text{ V} \approx 58 \text{ V (1 mark)}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Ripple factor:

$$\gamma = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{3} \, f_r \, R_L \, C} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{3} \times 100 \times 50 \times 1000 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\gamma = \frac{1}{17.32} = 0.0577 \approx 5.77\%$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** RMS ripple current through capacitor (approximation for sawtooth):

$$I_{C,rms} = \frac{I_L}{2\sqrt{3}} = \frac{1.146}{3.464} \approx 0.331 \text{ A}$$

#### Part (b) — Conceptual (7 Marks)

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** Draw the standard EV on-board charger signal chain:

`AC Mains → EMI Filter → Bridge Rectifier → Ripple Capacitor → DC Link → PFC → Isolated DC-DC → HV Battery`

Explain that the ripple capacitor is placed *immediately after* the bridge rectifier to provide a low-impedance path for AC ripple currents to ground, while the DC component flows to the load.

**Role of Capacitor (2 marks):**
- Stores energy during the peaks of the rectified waveform
- Releases it during the valleys to the load
- Acts as a local voltage source with very low output impedance
- Bypasses high-frequency AC components to ground

**ESR Discussion (2 marks):**
- ESR is the small parasitic resistance appearing in series with the ideal capacitance
- Real ripple voltage = capacitive ripple + ESR × peak ripple current
- High-ESR capacitors cause additional $I^2 R$ heating, reducing capacitor life
- For EV applications, **low-ESR aluminum electrolytics** or **film capacitors** (e.g., polypropylene) are preferred at the DC-link
- Modern EV chargers increasingly use a *hybrid* configuration: large aluminum electrolytic for bulk energy storage + small film/ceramic capacitor in parallel for high-frequency ripple bypass

---

**Question B — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2] — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Design a smoothing (ripple) capacitor for a 48 V, 10 A DC auxiliary bus in an EV, fed from a single-phase 50 Hz full-wave rectifier. The maximum allowable peak-to-peak ripple is 4 % of the nominal DC voltage. Specify the capacitance, voltage rating, ESR limit, and ripple current rating.

**(b)** Compare aluminum electrolytic, film, and ceramic capacitors for use as DC-link filter capacitors in EV power electronics. State the merits and demerits of each in a tabular form, and justify the hybrid capacitor configuration commonly used in modern EV traction inverters.

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Design Problem (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $V_{DC} = 48$ V, $I_L = 10$ A, $f_{line} = 50$ Hz
- Allowed ripple: $V_{r(pp)} \le 0.04 \times 48 = 1.92$ V

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Ripple frequency for full-wave:

$$f_r = 2 \times 50 = 100 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute minimum capacitance:

$$C_{min} = \frac{I_L}{f_r \cdot V_{r(pp)}^{spec}} = \frac{10}{100 \times 1.92} = 52.08 \times 10^{-3} \text{ F} \approx 52000 \,\mu F$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Apply 1.5× safety factor for aging and temperature:

$$C_{design} = 1.5 \times 52000 = 78000 \,\mu F$$

Select standard value: **$C = 82000 \,\mu F$** (or two 47000 µF in parallel).

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Voltage rating (assume peak ≈ 48 + ripple/2 + transients):

$$V_m \approx 48 + 1.0 + 10\%\text{ transient margin} = 54.8 \text{ V}$$

$$V_{rated} \ge 1.5 \times 54.8 = 82.2 \text{ V} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{V_{rated} = 100 \text{ V (standard)}}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** ESR limit — keep resistive ripple < 20 % of total:

$$V_{r(pp),ESR} \le 0.2 \times 1.92 = 0.384 \text{ V}$$

With peak ripple current $I_{r,peak} \approx 2 I_L = 20$ A:

$$R_{ESR,max} = \frac{0.384}{20} = 0.0192 \,\Omega \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{R_{ESR} \le 0.02 \,\Omega}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** RMS ripple current rating:

$$I_{C,rms} \approx \frac{I_L}{2\sqrt{3}} = \frac{10}{3.464} = 2.89 \text{ A}$$

Select capacitor with $I_{ripple} \ge 3.0$ A at 100 Hz and 105 °C.

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Final design specification summary:

$$\boxed{C = 82000 \,\mu F / 100 \text{ V}, \quad R_{ESR} \le 0.02 \,\Omega, \quad I_{ripple} \ge 3 \text{ A}}$$

#### Part (b) — Comparative Analysis (7 Marks)

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Aluminum Electrolytic | Film (Polypropylene) | Ceramic (MLCC) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Capacitance range | 1 µF to 1 F+ | 1 nF to 100 µF | 1 pF to 100 µF |
| Voltage range | 6.3 V to 500 V+ | 50 V to 2000 V+ | 6.3 V to 1000 V+ |
| ESR | Moderate to high | Very low | Extremely low |
| Ripple current handling | High (special grades) | High | Low (small packages) |
| Energy density | Very high | Low | Low to moderate |
| Polarity | Polarized | Non-polarized | Non-polarized |
| Cost per µF | Lowest | Moderate | High (large values) |
| Lifetime | Limited (electrolyte dry-out) | Excellent | Excellent |
| Failure mode | Open (with venting) | Open (self-healing) | Short (can be violent) |
| Frequency response | Poor at HF | Good | Excellent |
| Best use in EV | Bulk DC-link, low-freq ripple | DC-link + snubber | HF bypass, EMI filter |

**Hybrid Configuration Justification (3 marks):**

Modern EV traction inverters and OBCs use a *parallel hybrid* of all three types:
- **Bulk aluminum electrolytic** (or metallized polypropylene film) provides the high energy storage ($\frac{1}{2}CV^2$) needed for hold-up time and low-frequency (100 Hz / 300 Hz) ripple suppression.
- **Film capacitors** handle the high RMS ripple current from the inverter switching (typical 5–20 kHz) and provide a low-inductance path.
- **Ceramic MLCCs** bypass the very-high-frequency switching noise (>1 MHz) and provide decoupling right at the IGBT/SiC/MOSFET terminals.

This combination optimizes energy density, ripple current capability, ESR, and frequency response simultaneously.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1 (–2 marks):** Students often write $f_r = f_{line}$ for full-wave rectifiers. **Correct:** $f_r = 2 f_{line} = 100$ Hz for single-phase full-wave.
> - **Common Mistake 2 (–1 mark):** Using $V_{r(rms)} = V_{r(pp)}/\sqrt{3}$ instead of $V_{r(rms)} = V_{r(pp)}/(2\sqrt{3})$ for symmetric AC ripple. Both appear in textbooks depending on definition; KTU expects the **$2\sqrt{3}$** denominator.
> - **Common Mistake 3 (–1 mark):** Forgetting to apply a **safety factor (1.5× to 2×)** on $C_{min}$ and **derating factor (1.25× to 1.5×)** on voltage rating. Always show this in design answers.
> - **Common Mistake 4 (–1 mark):** Not checking the **ripple current rating** of the selected capacitor. A common reason for electrolytic capacitor failure in EV chargers is exceeding $I_{ripple}$, not voltage.
> - **Common Mistake 5 (–1 mark):** Neglecting **ESR contribution** to total ripple in tight designs.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Fundamental Ripple Equation:** $V_{r(pp)} = I_L / (f_r C)$ — *the single most important equation*.
- **Ripple Frequency Rule:** Single-phase full-wave $\Rightarrow f_r = 100$ Hz (India); three-phase full-wave $\Rightarrow f_r = 300$ Hz.
- **Minimum Capacitance:** $C_{min} = I_L / (f_r \cdot V_{r(pp)}^{spec})$.
- **Design Margins:** Always use **1.5× to 2×** safety factor on $C_{min}$ and **1.25× to 1.5×** derating on voltage rating.
- **Ripple Factor Formula:** $\gamma = 1/(2\sqrt{3} \, f_r R_L C)$ — should be well below 5 % for most EV applications.
- **ESR is Not Free:** Real ripple = capacitive ripple + $I_{r,peak} \cdot R_{ESR}$.
- **RMS Ripple Current Check:** $I_{C,rms} \approx I_L / (2\sqrt{3})$; datasheet $I_{ripple}$ rating must exceed this at operating frequency and temperature.
- **Diode Conduction:** Diodes conduct only near the peaks; the rest of the period is supported by the capacitor alone.
- **Time Constant Rule:** $R_L C \gg T_r$ for the design to be in the small-ripple (linear discharge) regime.
- **Hybrid Capacitor Architecture:** Bulk electrolytic + film + ceramic in parallel is the modern EV industry standard.
- **Standard Tolerance:** Aluminum electrolytics have ±20 % tolerance; design with the *minimum* value in mind.
- **Lifetime Derating:** Capacitor lifetime halves for every 10 °C rise above rated temperature — critical in EV under-hood environments.
- **Self-Healing Film Capacitors:** Preferred for high-reliability EV applications (e.g., DC-link of traction inverter) due to open-circuit failure mode.
- **Hold-Up Energy:** $E = \frac{1}{2}CV^2$ — used to calculate ride-through time during input power interruptions.
- **KTU Board Pattern:** A 14-mark question typically combines one numerical design (7 marks) with one conceptual/comparative discussion (7 marks). Practice both halves.
- **Common Units to Memorize:** µF, mF, V, Hz, A, °C, mΩ, Ω, J (energy).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Switching Frequency and PWM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET742 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES AND RENEWABLE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Switching Frequency and PWM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Switching Frequency ($f_{sw}$)** is the rate at which the power semiconductor devices (IGBTs, MOSFETs, SiC, or GaN transistors) in the inverter or DC-DC converter of an Electric Vehicle (EV) are turned ON and OFF per second. It is measured in **Hertz (Hz)** or **kilohertz (kHz)**, and in modern EV traction inverters it typically lies in the range of **5 kHz to 20 kHz** for silicon IGBTs, and up to **50 kHz – 100 kHz** for wide-bandgap (SiC/GaN) devices.

**Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)** is a modulation technique used to encode a reference sinusoidal waveform (the desired output) as a series of digital switching pulses, where the **width (duty cycle)** of each pulse is varied in proportion to the instantaneous amplitude of the reference signal. In EV traction inverters, the three-phase PWM waveform drives the stator windings of the PMSM (Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor) or Induction Motor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The combination of *Switching Frequency* and *PWM* forms the **control backbone** of the entire EV powertrain. From the DC-DC boost converter to the traction inverter, every power stage uses PWM with a defined $f_{sw}$ to control voltage, current, and torque.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are controlling the **speed of a ceiling fan** using a light switch that only has ON and OFF positions (no dimmer). To make the fan spin at half speed, you would flicker the switch — ON for 1 second, OFF for 1 second, ON for 1 second, OFF for 1 second... The fan, being heavy, will average this out and spin at roughly half speed.

- **PWM is exactly this "flickering switch"** — but done thousands of times per second.
- **Switching frequency** is how fast you flick the switch.
- **Duty cycle (D)** is what fraction of the time the switch is ON.

If you flicker too slowly (low $f_{sw}$), the fan jerks and hums. If you flicker too fast (high $f_{sw}$), the switch wastes energy as heat. The **sweet spot** in EV design balances smoothness, efficiency, noise, and component cost.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world EV Example:** A Tesla Model 3 traction inverter uses SiC MOSFETs switching at approximately **20–25 kHz**, just above the audible range of humans (which is 20 Hz – 20 kHz). This avoids the annoying high-pitched whine that older EVs with 8 kHz IGBTs used to produce.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics at a Glance

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value (EV) |
|---|---|---|
| Switching Frequency | $f_{sw}$ | **5 kHz – 50 kHz** |
| Modulation Index | $m_a$ | **0 – 1.0** (linear region) |
| Fundamental Output Frequency | $f_1$ | **0 – 600 Hz** (motor speed) |
| Switching-to-Fundamental Ratio | $f_{sw}/f_1$ | **50 – 200** |
| Dead Time | $t_d$ | **0.5 – 3 $\mu$s** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why PWM is Used in EVs (The "Why")

The DC bus voltage of an EV battery pack is **fixed** (e.g., 400 V nominal). The motor, however, requires a **variable-frequency, variable-amplitude AC voltage** to control speed and torque. A PWM inverter achieves this without bulky variable-frequency generators.

## 2.2 Sinusoidal PWM (SPWM) — Operational Breakdown

The **sinusoidal PWM** technique uses three reference signals (one per phase) and one high-frequency **triangular carrier wave**:

1. **Reference Signal** $v_{ref}(t) = V_{ref,m} \sin(\omega_1 t)$ — the desired sinusoidal output.
2. **Carrier Signal** $v_{cr}(t)$ — a triangular wave at switching frequency $f_{sw}$.
3. **Comparison Rule**:
   - If $v_{ref} > v_{cr}$ → Upper switch of the leg is **ON** (output = $+V_{dc}/2$).
   - If $v_{ref} < v_{cr}$ → Lower switch of the leg is **ON** (output = $-V_{dc}/2$).

### The Modulation Index
The amplitude ratio is called the **modulation index**:

$$m_a = \frac{V_{ref,m}}{V_{cr,m}}$$

Where:
- $m_a \leq 1.0$ → **Linear modulation region** (output is faithful reproduction of reference).
- $m_a > 1.0$ → **Overmodulation** (output becomes distorted, square-wave-like).
- $m_a \approx 4/\pi$ → **Square wave** (maximum output possible).

### The Frequency Modulation Ratio
The frequency ratio controls how many pulses appear per fundamental cycle:

$$m_f = \frac{f_{sw}}{f_1}$$

- $m_f \geq 21$ → Synchronous PWM (used in EV inverters for low $f_1$).
- $m_f$ must be an **integer multiple of 3** for three-phase inverters to eliminate certain harmonics.

## 2.3 Space Vector PWM (SVPWM)

**SVPWM** is the industry-standard technique used in modern EV traction inverters. It treats the three-phase inverter as a single unit and synthesizes a rotating voltage vector:

$$\vec{V}_{ref} = \frac{2}{3}\left(V_a + V_b e^{j2\pi/3} + V_c e^{j4\pi/3}\right)$$

SVPWM achieves a **DC bus utilization 15.47% higher** than SPWM:

$$\frac{V_{1,rms}^{SVPWM}}{V_{1,rms}^{SPWM}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \frac{1}{\cos(30°)} \cdot 1 = \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \frac{1}{2} = 1.1547$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This **15.47% extra DC bus utilization** means SVPWM gives you 15.47% more torque from the same battery voltage — a critical advantage in EVs.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $m_a = V_{ref,m}/V_{cr,m}$ | Modulation Index | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $m_f = f_{sw}/f_1$ | Frequency Modulation Ratio | Integer |
| 3 | $V_{1,rms} = m_a \cdot \frac{V_{dc}}{2\sqrt{2}}$ | Fundamental RMS output (linear) | Volts |
| 4 | $V_{1,peak} = m_a \cdot \frac{V_{dc}}{2}$ | Fundamental peak output (linear) | Volts |
| 5 | $V_{LL,rms}^{SVPWM} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2\sqrt{2}} m_a V_{dc}$ | Line-line SVPWM RMS (max $m_a=1$) | Volts |
| 6 | $P_{sw,loss} = f_{sw} \cdot (E_{on} + E_{off}) \cdot \frac{V_{dc}}{V_{rated}} \cdot \frac{I_{avg}}{I_{rated}}$ | Switching power loss | Watts |
| 7 | $\Delta I_L = \frac{V_{dc} \cdot D \cdot (1-D)}{f_{sw} \cdot L}$ | Inductor current ripple (buck) | Amps |
| 8 | $f_{res} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | LC filter resonant frequency | Hertz |
| 9 | $THD = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} V_h^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%$ | Total Harmonic Distortion | Percent |
| 10 | $t_{dead} \geq \frac{t_{d(off)} - t_{d(on)}}{2}$ | Minimum dead time | Seconds |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on notation:** In all formula tables, vertical absolute-value bars have been rendered as `\\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In a commercial EV inverter (e.g., Bosch, ZF, Tesla):
- **Low $f_{sw}$ (5 kHz)**: Smaller switching losses, but **loud motor whine** and high torque ripple → uncomfortable ride.
- **High $f_{sw}$ (50 kHz SiC)**: Silent operation, smaller magnetics, but **higher EMC complexity** and EMI filtering cost.
- The optimal $f_{sw}$ is chosen using a **Pareto optimization** across loss, acoustics, EMC, and thermal envelope.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Fundamental RMS Output Voltage of SPWM

### Step 1 — Start with the PWM Output Waveform

The two-level inverter leg output is:

$$v_{ao}(t) = \begin{cases} +V_{dc}/2 & \text{if } v_{ref} > v_{cr} \\ -V_{dc}/2 & \text{if } v_{ref} < v_{cr} \end{cases}$$

### Step 2 — Express as a Fourier Series

A periodic PWM waveform with $m_a \leq 1$ has the Fourier expansion:

$$v_{ao}(t) = m_a \cdot \frac{V_{dc}}{2} \sin(\omega_1 t) + \text{harmonic terms at } k f_{sw} \pm n f_1$$

The amplitude of the **fundamental component** is:

$$V_{1,peak} = m_a \cdot \frac{V_{dc}}{2}$$

### Step 3 — Convert Peak to RMS

$$V_{1,rms} = \frac{V_{1,peak}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{m_a \cdot V_{dc}}{2\sqrt{2}}$$

### Step 4 — Line-to-Line Voltage (Three-Phase)

In a balanced three-phase system, the line-to-line voltage is $\sqrt{3}$ times the phase voltage:

$$V_{LL,rms} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{1,rms} = \frac{\sqrt{3} \cdot m_a \cdot V_{dc}}{2\sqrt{2}}$$

### Step 5 — Numerical Sanity Check

For $V_{dc} = 400$ V and $m_a = 0.85$:

$$V_{LL,rms} = \frac{\sqrt{3} \cdot 0.85 \cdot 400}{2\sqrt{2}} = \frac{589.0}{2.828} \approx 208.3 \text{ V}$$

This matches typical 400 V EV powertrain operating line-to-line voltages. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Switching Power Loss

### Step 1 — Energy Loss Per Switching Event

Every turn-on and turn-off dissipates energy in the semiconductor:

$$E_{total} = E_{on} + E_{off}$$

### Step 2 — Scaling to Operating Point

Datasheets specify $E_{on}$ and $E_{off}$ at a *rated* voltage $V_{rated}$ and current $I_{rated}$. For different operating points, scale linearly:

$$E_{sw}(V, I) = (E_{on} + E_{off}) \cdot \frac{V}{V_{rated}} \cdot \frac{I}{I_{rated}}$$

### Step 3 — Multiply by Switching Frequency

$$P_{sw,loss} = f_{sw} \cdot E_{sw}(V, I) = f_{sw} \cdot (E_{on} + E_{off}) \cdot \frac{V_{dc}}{V_{rated}} \cdot \frac{I_{avg}}{I_{rated}}$$

### Step 4 — Six Switches in a Three-Phase Inverter

$$P_{inv,sw,total} = 6 \cdot P_{sw,loss}$$

(Assuming balanced three-phase operation with IGBTs; MOSFETs include body-diode reverse recovery.)

### Step 5 — Numerical Example

A 50 A IGBT with $E_{on} + E_{off} = 5$ mJ at 600 V/50 A, switching at 10 kHz, in a 400 V system at 30 A average:

$$P_{sw,loss} = 10000 \cdot 0.005 \cdot \frac{400}{600} \cdot \frac{30}{50} = 10000 \cdot 0.005 \cdot 0.667 \cdot 0.6 = 20 \text{ W per switch}$$

For six switches: $6 \times 20 = 120$ W total switching loss — a major contributor to the inverter's thermal load.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Inductor Current Ripple in a Buck Converter

### Step 1 — Voltage Across Inductor During ON-Time

When the switch is ON (duration $DT_s$), the inductor sees:

$$v_L = V_{in} - V_{out} = V_{dc} - V_{out}$$

### Step 2 — Inductor Equation

$$v_L = L \frac{di_L}{dt}$$

### Step 3 — Solve for Slope

$$\frac{di_L}{dt} = \frac{V_{dc} - V_{out}}{L}$$

### Step 4 — Integrate Over ON-Time

$$\Delta I_L = \frac{(V_{dc} - V_{out}) \cdot D \cdot T_s}{L}$$

### Step 5 — Apply Volt-Second Balance

In steady state, $V_{out} = D \cdot V_{dc}$, so $V_{dc} - V_{out} = V_{dc}(1 - D)$:

$$\Delta I_L = \frac{V_{dc} \cdot D \cdot (1 - D)}{f_{sw} \cdot L}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight:** The current ripple is **inversely proportional** to $f_{sw}$. Doubling $f_{sw}$ halves the ripple — but doubles the switching loss. This is the **central engineering trade-off** in EV power electronics.

### Step 6 — Numerical Example

For a 48 V → 12 V auxiliary DC-DC converter with $L = 50$ $\mu$H, $D = 0.25$, $f_{sw} = 100$ kHz:

$$\Delta I_L = \frac{48 \cdot 0.25 \cdot 0.75}{100000 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{9.0}{5.0} = 1.8 \text{ A}$$

This 1.8 A ripple is acceptable for a 12 V/20 A auxiliary battery system.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: $f_{sw}$ Trade-offs at a Glance

| Design Parameter | Low $f_{sw}$ (5 kHz) | High $f_{sw}$ (50 kHz) | KTU Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| Switching Loss | **Low** ✓ | **High** ✗ | Limits Si IGBT use to ~10 kHz |
| Conduction Loss | Same | Same | $f_{sw}$-independent |
| Inductor Size | Large ✗ | Small ✓ | Determines EV weight/volume |
| Acoustic Noise | Loud ✗ | Silent ✓ | NVH (Noise-Vibration-Harshness) |
| EMI Filter Size | Large ✗ | Small ✓ | Affects BOM cost |
| Torque Ripple | High ✗ | Low ✓ | Affects drivability |
| Efficiency | 96% | 94% (silicon) / 98% (SiC) | Range impact |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: PWM Signal Generation in an EV Traction Inverter

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[Battery Pack Vdc 400V] --> nodeB[DC Link Capacitor]
    nodeB --> nodeC[3-Phase VSI Inverter]
    nodeC --> nodeD[PMSM Motor]
    
    nodeE[Torque Command from Accelerator] --> nodeF[VCU Vehicle Control Unit]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Field Oriented Control FOC]
    nodeG --> nodeH[PI Current Controllers Id Iq]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Inverse Park Transform]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Reference Va Vb Vc Sinusoidal]
    
    nodeK[Triangular Carrier Generator fsw 20kHz] --> nodeL[SPWM Comparator]
    nodeJ --> nodeL
    nodeL --> nodeM[Gate Driver Signals 6 PWM Pulses]
    nodeM --> nodeC
    
    nodeC --> nodeN[Phase Current Sensors]
    nodeN --> nodeH
    
    style nodeA fill:#e1f5ff
    style nodeD fill:#ffe1e1
    style nodeG fill:#fff4e1
    style nodeL fill:#e1ffe1
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Closed-Loop PWM Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Reference Speed RPM] --> nodeB[Speed PI Controller]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Reference Torque Te]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Current Reference Idref Iqref]
    nodeE[Measured Speed] --> nodeB
    nodeD --> nodeF[Current PI Controllers]
    nodeG[Measured Id] --> nodeF
    nodeH[Measured Iq] --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeI[SVPWM Modulator]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Inverter Switching at fsw]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[PMSM Motor]
    nodeK --> nodeE
    nodeK --> nodeG
    nodeK --> nodeH
    style nodeA fill:#e1f5ff
    style nodeF fill:#fff4e1
    style nodeI fill:#e1ffe1
    style nodeK fill:#ffe1e1
```

## 4.3 PWM Modulation Concept — Reference vs Carrier

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[Sinusoidal Reference Va] --> nodeC[Comparator]
    nodeB[Triangular Carrier fsw] --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD{If Vref greater than Vcr}
    nodeD -->|Yes| nodeE[Upper Switch ON: Output Plus Vdc/2]
    nodeD -->|No| nodeF[Lower Switch ON: Output Minus Vdc/2]
    nodeE --> nodeG[PWM Pulse Train]
    nodeF --> nodeG
    style nodeA fill:#e1f5ff
    style nodeB fill:#fff4e1
    style nodeG fill:#e1ffe1
```

## 4.4 Frequency Spectrum (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Fundamental f1 0 to 600Hz] --> nodeB[Switching Harmonics at fsw 20kHz]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Sidebands fsw plus or minus 2f1 3f1 ...]
    nodeC --> nodeD[High Frequency Cluster 40 to 60kHz]
    nodeD --> nodeE[EMI Filter Required to Attenuate]
    style nodeA fill:#e1ffe1
    style nodeE fill:#ffe1e1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in EVs:** The high-frequency cluster around $f_{sw}$ and its multiples is what the EV's **EMC/EMI filter** must attenuate to meet automotive EMC standards (CISPR 25). Higher $f_{sw}$ pushes these clusters further away from AM radio (530–1700 kHz), making filtering easier.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Q1.b]**
**Define switching frequency and modulation index in a PWM inverter used for EV traction.**

**Model Answer:**
**Switching Frequency ($f_{sw}$):** It is the rate at which the semiconductor switches (IGBT/SiC/MOSFET) of the inverter are turned ON and OFF. It determines the rate of pulse generation and directly impacts the size of passive components and switching losses. In EV traction inverters, $f_{sw}$ typically ranges from **5 kHz to 20 kHz** for silicon IGBTs.

**Modulation Index ($m_a$):** It is the ratio of the peak amplitude of the reference sinusoidal signal to the peak amplitude of the triangular carrier signal, expressed as:

$$m_a = \frac{V_{ref,m}}{V_{cr,m}}$$

It controls the **amplitude** of the fundamental output voltage. The value $m_a = 1$ marks the boundary between linear modulation and overmodulation.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Sample, Q2.a]**
**List any three advantages of Space Vector PWM (SVPWM) over Sinusoidal PWM (SPWM) in EV applications.**

**Model Answer:**
