1. **Higher DC bus utilization:** SVPWM produces 15.47% more fundamental output voltage than SPWM for the same DC bus voltage — directly increasing available motor torque.
2. **Lower harmonic distortion:** SVPWM places harmonics at higher frequencies, simplifying filtering and reducing torque ripple.
3. **Digital implementation friendly:** SVPWM is computationally efficient and integrates naturally with Field-Oriented Control (FOC), which is the standard EV motor control algorithm.
4. **Lower switching losses:** SVPWM sequences switching states to minimize the number of switchings per cycle.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — Recommended Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Q11.a,b]**

**(a) (7 marks)** With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of sinusoidal PWM (SPWM) for a single-phase full-bridge inverter used in an EV auxiliary converter. Define modulation index and frequency modulation ratio. Derive the expression for the fundamental RMS output voltage.

**(b) (7 marks)** A three-phase VSI uses SPWM with $V_{dc} = 400$ V, $f_1 = 50$ Hz, $f_{sw} = 2$ kHz, and $m_a = 0.8$. Calculate:
  (i) Frequency modulation ratio
  (ii) Fundamental RMS phase voltage
  (iii) Fundamental RMS line-to-line voltage
  (iv) Comment on the choice of $f_{sw}$ for an EV traction application.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Block Diagram:** A reference sine wave $V_{ref}$ and triangular carrier $V_{cr}$ are fed to a comparator. The comparator output is a PWM signal. This signal drives the four MOSFET/IGBT switches of the H-bridge through gate drivers. The load sees a bipolar PWM voltage.

**Modulation Index:** $m_a = V_{ref,m} / V_{cr,m}$ — controls output amplitude.

**Frequency Modulation Ratio:** $m_f = f_{sw} / f_1$ — controls pulse density per cycle.

**Derivation:**

The two-level inverter leg output is $\pm V_{dc}/2$. Using Fourier analysis of the resulting double-edge PWM waveform, the fundamental peak amplitude is:

$$V_{1,peak} = m_a \cdot \frac{V_{dc}}{2}$$

Converting to RMS:

$$V_{1,rms} = \frac{m_a \cdot V_{dc}}{2\sqrt{2}}$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**(i) Frequency Modulation Ratio:**

$$m_f = \frac{f_{sw}}{f_1} = \frac{2000}{50} = 40$$

This is an integer and a multiple of 3 (since 40 is not a multiple of 3, an asynchronous PWM is used here; ideally for a 3-phase system, $m_f$ should be a multiple of 3 like 39 or 42).

**(ii) Fundamental RMS Phase Voltage:**

$$V_{1,rms} = \frac{m_a \cdot V_{dc}}{2\sqrt{2}} = \frac{0.8 \cdot 400}{2\sqrt{2}} = \frac{320}{2.828} = 113.1 \text{ V}$$

**(iii) Fundamental RMS Line-to-Line Voltage:**

$$V_{LL,rms} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{1,rms} = \sqrt{3} \cdot 113.1 = 195.9 \text{ V}$$

**(iv) Comment on $f_{sw} = 2$ kHz for EV traction:**
2 kHz is **too low** for EV traction. It falls within the audible range and will cause:
- Audible motor whine (NVH issue)
- High torque ripple
- Need for bulky inductors in the output filter

Modern EV traction inverters use **8–20 kHz** for IGBTs and **30–100 kHz** for SiC MOSFETs.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model 2024, Q12.a,b]**

**(a) (7 marks)** Compare SPWM and SVPWM techniques. Derive the expression for the maximum line-to-line RMS voltage obtainable with SVPWM in the linear modulation region.

**(b) (7 marks)** An EV traction inverter uses IGBTs with $E_{on} + E_{off} = 4$ mJ at $V_{rated} = 600$ V, $I_{rated} = 100$ A. The DC bus is 400 V, average motor current is 60 A, and the inverter switches at 10 kHz. Calculate:
  (i) Switching loss per switch
  (ii) Total inverter switching loss
  (iii) Compare this with the loss at 20 kHz and comment.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | SPWM | SVPWM |
|---|---|---|
| Reference Generation | 3 sine waves | 1 rotating space vector |
| DC Bus Utilization | 78.55% | **90.67%** (15.47% more) |
| Computational Complexity | Low | Moderate |
| Implementation | Analog/Digital | Fully Digital (DSP/MCU) |
| Harmonic Performance | Moderate | Better |
| Use in EVs | Rare | **Industry Standard** |

**Derivation of SVPWM Maximum Line-to-Line Voltage:**

The maximum length of the reference vector in linear SVPWM is the radius of the inscribed circle in the hexagon: $V_{ref,max} = V_{dc}/\sqrt{3}$.

The phase voltage peak is:

$$V_{ph,peak}^{SVPWM} = \frac{V_{dc}}{\sqrt{3}}$$

The line-to-line peak is $\sqrt{3}$ times the phase peak:

$$V_{LL,peak}^{SVPWM} = \sqrt{3} \cdot \frac{V_{dc}}{\sqrt{3}} = V_{dc}$$

The line-to-line RMS is:

$$V_{LL,rms}^{SVPWM} = \frac{V_{dc}}{\sqrt{2}}$$

This is $\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} = 1.1547$ times the SPWM maximum, confirming the **15.47% boost**.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**(i) Switching loss per switch:**

$$P_{sw,loss} = f_{sw} \cdot (E_{on} + E_{off}) \cdot \frac{V_{dc}}{V_{rated}} \cdot \frac{I_{avg}}{I_{rated}}$$

$$P_{sw,loss} = 10000 \cdot 0.004 \cdot \frac{400}{600} \cdot \frac{60}{100} = 10000 \cdot 0.004 \cdot 0.667 \cdot 0.6 = 16 \text{ W}$$

**(ii) Total inverter switching loss (6 switches):**

$$P_{inv,total} = 6 \cdot 16 = 96 \text{ W}$$

**(iii) At 20 kHz:**

$$P_{sw,loss,20k} = 20000 \cdot 0.004 \cdot 0.667 \cdot 0.6 = 32 \text{ W per switch}$$

$$P_{inv,total,20k} = 6 \cdot 32 = 192 \text{ W}$$

**Comment:** Doubling the switching frequency **doubles the switching losses** (96 W → 192 W). For silicon IGBTs, this means either (a) a larger heatsink, (b) derated output current, or (c) switching to **SiC MOSFETs** which have ~70% lower switching energies.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Don't confuse** $m_a$ (amplitude ratio) with $m_f$ (frequency ratio). Examiners frequently set traps where one symbol is asked but the other is given.
> - **Always show the SVPWM 15.47% boost derivation** explicitly — partial credit is heavy on this.
> - In switching loss calculations, **forgetting to multiply by 6** for a three-phase inverter is the #1 most common mistake, costing 2 marks.
> - In the formula $V_{1,rms} = m_a V_{dc}/(2\sqrt{2})$, students often write $V_{dc}/\sqrt{2}$ — **never** include the $\sqrt{2}$ in the denominator twice.
> - Always state the **operating region** (linear vs overmodulation) explicitly when using modulation-index formulas.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Switching Frequency and PWM**

- **Switching Frequency ($f_{sw}$):** The clock rate of power semiconductor switching. Trade-off between **losses (↑ with $f_{sw}$)** and **filter size, torque ripple, acoustic noise (↓ with $f_{sw}$)**.
- **Modulation Index ($m_a = V_{ref,m}/V_{cr,m}$):** Controls output voltage amplitude. Linear up to 1.0, then overmodulation, then square wave.
- **Frequency Modulation Ratio ($m_f = f_{sw}/f_1$):** Should be an integer, and for 3-phase systems ideally a multiple of 3.
- **Fundamental Output Voltage:** $V_{1,rms} = m_a V_{dc}/(2\sqrt{2})$ for SPWM; $V_{LL,rms} = V_{dc}/\sqrt{2}$ for SVPWM at max linear modulation.
- **SVPWM gives 15.47% more DC bus utilization** than SPWM — critical in EVs.
- **Switching Loss:** $P_{sw,loss} = f_{sw} \cdot E_{sw}$ — **scales linearly with $f_{sw}$**.
- **Current Ripple in Inductor:** $\Delta I_L \propto 1/f_{sw}$ — **inversely proportional to $f_{sw}$**.
- **Dead Time ($t_d$):** Inserted between complementary switch transitions to prevent shoot-through. Typical: **0.5–3 $\mu$s**. Causes voltage distortion and torque ripple.
- **Wide-bandgap devices** (SiC, GaN) allow $f_{sw} \to 50$–100 kHz with manageable losses — the **future of EV traction**.
- **Industry trend:** $f_{sw}$ is now variable (variable switching frequency / randomized PWM) to spread the EMI spectrum and avoid specific frequency peaks in the EMC certification bands.
- **Memorize:** $V_{LL,rms}^{SVPWM,max} = V_{dc}/\sqrt{2}$ and the 15.47% factor.
- **Memorize:** Switching loss scales linearly with $f_{sw}$; ripple scales inversely with $f_{sw}$.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Battery Based Energy Storage Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET742 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES AND RENEWABLE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Battery Based Energy Storage Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Battery-Based Energy Storage Systems (BESS)** are electrochemical devices that store electrical energy in the form of chemical potential energy and release it as direct current (DC) electricity through controlled redox (reduction-oxidation) reactions. In the context of **KTU PEAET742**, a BESS in an electric vehicle (EV) functions as the *prime mover's fuel tank*—it is the on-board reservoir of energy that powers the traction motor through a power electronic inverter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Intent):** Battery-based energy storage involves the conversion of electrical energy into chemical energy during charging and the reverse conversion during discharging, characterized by parameters such as *terminal voltage, nominal capacity, energy density, power density, state-of-charge (SoC), state-of-health (SoH), depth-of-discharge (DoD), and C-rate*.

A single electrochemical cell consists of:
- **Anode (Negative Electrode):** Source of electrons during discharge (oxidation occurs).
- **Cathode (Positive Electrode):** Accepts electrons during discharge (reduction occurs).
- **Electrolyte:** Ionic conductor that completes the internal circuit.
- **Separator:** Electrically insulating, ionically permeable membrane that prevents short-circuit.

The basic operating potential of a cell is governed by the **Nernst Equation**:

$$E_{cell} = E^0_{cell} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

where $E^0_{cell}$ is the standard cell potential, $R$ is the universal gas constant (**8.314 J/(mol·K)**), $T$ is absolute temperature, $n$ is the number of electrons transferred, $F$ is Faraday's constant (**96,485 C/mol**), and $Q$ is the reaction quotient.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Water Tower Model"**
> Think of a battery as a **water tower** connected to your house. The water tower stores potential energy by holding water at a height (just as a battery stores energy by holding charged ions at different chemical potentials). When you open a tap (connect a load), water flows out under gravity (just as electrons flow from anode to cathode through an external circuit). The "height" of the tower represents the **voltage**, the "tank diameter" represents the **capacity (Ah)**, and the "amount of water currently in the tank" represents the **State of Charge (SoC)**. Re-filling the tower is *charging*; draining it is *discharging*. A bigger, taller tower stores more energy—but if you drain it too fast, you risk structural damage (this is why **C-rate limits** matter).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Terminology

- **Nominal Voltage (V):** The reference operating voltage of a cell/pack (e.g., 3.7 V for Li-ion NMC).
- **Capacity (Ah):** Total charge a fully charged battery can deliver under specified conditions.
- **Energy (Wh or kWh):** Capacity × Nominal Voltage.
- **Specific Energy (Wh/kg):** Energy per unit mass—key for EV range.
- **Energy Density (Wh/L):** Energy per unit volume—key for packaging.
- **Specific Power (W/kg):** Maximum deliverable power per unit mass.
- **C-Rate:** The rate at which a battery is charged/discharged relative to its maximum capacity.
- **SoC (%):** State of Charge—available capacity as a percentage of rated capacity.
- **SoH (%):** State of Health—current maximum capacity as a percentage of original capacity.
- **DoD (%):** Depth of Discharge—percentage of capacity that has been discharged.
- **Cycle Life:** Number of full charge-discharge cycles before capacity drops to 80% of original (EOL criterion).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Battery Parameters — Structured Analysis

### 2.1.1 Capacity and Energy Relationships

The **theoretical capacity** of an active material is determined by **Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis**:

$$Q_{th} = \frac{n \cdot F \cdot m}{M} = \frac{m \cdot F}{M / (n \cdot 1)} = \frac{m \cdot F}{M_{eq}}$$

where $m$ is the active mass (kg), $M$ is the molar mass (kg/mol), and $M_{eq}$ is the equivalent weight.

The **practical (deliverable) capacity** is always less than the theoretical capacity due to:
- Inactive additives (binders, conductive carbon).
- Side reactions (SEI formation, electrolyte decomposition).
- Kinetic polarization at high currents.

### 2.1.2 C-Rate — The Speed of Energy Transfer

The **C-rate** is a normalized current that expresses the rate of charge/discharge in terms of the battery's rated capacity:

$$I_{C\text{-rate}} = C\text{-rate} \times C_{rated}$$

For a battery rated at $C_{rated}$ Ah, a 1C discharge delivers $C_{rated}$ Amperes for 1 hour. A 2C rate halves the time, while C/2 doubles it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why C-rate matters in EVs:** During hard acceleration, an EV traction battery may momentarily deliver 3C–5C. During regenerative braking, charging currents can spike to 5C–10C. The pack must be sized to handle these peaks without exceeding thermal or voltage limits.

### 2.1.3 State of Charge (SoC) and Depth of Discharge (DoD)

$$SoC(t) = SoC(t_0) - \frac{1}{C_{rated}} \int_{t_0}^{t} I(\tau) \, d\tau$$

$$DoD(\%) = 100 - SoC(\%)$$

**Coulomb Counting** is the most common real-time SoC estimation method; it integrates current over time.

### 2.1.4 State of Health (SoH)

$$SoH(\%) = \frac{C_{current}}{C_{rated,new}} \times 100$$

SoH degrades due to:
- **SEI layer growth** (consumes cyclable lithium in Li-ion cells).
- **Electrode cracking** from volume changes.
- **Electrolyte depletion.**
- **Current collector corrosion.**

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|------|-------|
| 1 | Cell EMF (Nernst) | $E = E^0 - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$ | V | T in K, R=8.314 J/(mol·K), F=96485 C/mol |
| 2 | Theoretical Capacity | $Q_{th} = \dfrac{nFm}{M}$ | C (Coulombs) | $m$=active mass, $M$=molar mass |
| 3 | Practical Capacity | $Q_{p} = I \cdot t$ | Ah | $I$ in A, $t$ in hours |
| 4 | Energy Stored | $E = V_{nom} \cdot C_{Ah}$ | Wh | Multiply by $\frac{1}{1000}$ for kWh |
| 5 | Specific Energy | $E_{sp} = \dfrac{E}{m_{pack}}$ | Wh/kg | Critical for EV range |
| 6 | Power Density | $P_{den} = \dfrac{V^2}{R_{int} \cdot V_{pack}}$ | W/kg or W/L | Lower $R_{int}$ = higher power |
| 7 | C-Rate Current | $I = C_{rate} \times C_{Ah}$ | A | 1C = full discharge in 1 hr |
| 8 | State of Charge | $SoC = SoC_0 - \dfrac{\int I\,dt}{C_{Ah}}$ | % | Coulomb-counting method |
| 9 | Depth of Discharge | $DoD = 100\% - SoC$ | % | Inverts SoC |
| 10 | State of Health | $SoH = \dfrac{C_{now}}{C_{new}} \times 100$ | % | EOL typically at 80% |
| 11 | Peukert's Law | $t = H \cdot \left(\dfrac{C_{rated}}{I}\right)^{k-1}$ | hr | $k \approx 1.1$–$1.3$ for lead-acid |
| 12 | Internal Heat | $Q_{heat} = I^2 R_{int}$ | W | Joule heating; basis for thermal model |
| 13 | Round-trip Efficiency | $\eta_{RT} = \dfrac{E_{out}}{E_{in}} \times 100$ | % | Li-ion ≈ 85–95% |

## 2.3 Engineering Utility

In real-world EV and renewable integration, BESS is used for:
1. **EV Traction** — primary energy reservoir (50–100 kWh packs in modern BEVs).
2. **Grid-Scale Renewable Storage** — buffering solar/wind intermittency.
3. **Microgrids & UPS** — backup power with sub-second response.
4. **Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G)** — bidirectional energy flow using EV batteries as distributed storage.
5. **Frequency Regulation** — fast-acting ancillary service (Li-ion BESS dominates this market).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Energy and Power Density from First Principles

**Step 1 — Define the system.**
Consider a single cell with active electrode mass $m_a$ (kg) and total pack mass $m_p$ (kg), where $m_p = m_a + m_{inactive}$ (inactive includes electrolyte, current collector, separator, casing).

**Step 2 — Apply Faraday's law to compute theoretical charge.**

$$Q_{th} = \frac{n \cdot F \cdot m_a}{M}$$

where:
- $n$ = number of electrons transferred per mole of active species
- $F = 96485$ C/mol
- $M$ = molar mass of the active species (kg/mol)

**Step 3 — Compute theoretical energy.**

$$E_{th} = V_{nom} \cdot Q_{th} = V_{nom} \cdot \frac{n \cdot F \cdot m_a}{M}$$

**Step 4 — Derive specific energy (gravimetric).**

$$E_{sp,th} = \frac{E_{th}}{m_a} = \frac{n \cdot F \cdot V_{nom}}{M}$$

**Step 5 — Account for practical losses via utilization factor.**

The practical specific energy is reduced by:
- *Mass utilization factor* $f_m = m_a / m_p$ (typically 0.4–0.6)
- *Voltage efficiency* $\eta_v = V_{avg,discharge} / V_{OCV}$ (typically 0.85–0.95)
- *Coulombic efficiency* $\eta_c = Q_{discharge} / Q_{charge}$ (typically 0.99+ for Li-ion)

$$E_{sp,practical} = \frac{n \cdot F \cdot V_{nom}}{M} \cdot f_m \cdot \eta_v \cdot \eta_c$$

**Step 6 — Derive maximum power.**

For a cell with internal resistance $R_{int}$, the maximum deliverable power at matched load is:

$$P_{max} = \frac{V_{oc}^2}{4 R_{int}}$$

This is the classic **maximum power transfer theorem** applied to a Thevenin equivalent of a battery.

**Step 7 — Derive specific power.**

$$P_{sp} = \frac{P_{max}}{m_p} = \frac{V_{oc}^2}{4 R_{int} \cdot m_p}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: SoC Update via Coulomb Counting

Starting from the definition:

$$SoC(t) = SoC(t_0) + \frac{1}{C_{rated}} \int_{t_0}^{t} I_{in}(\tau) \, d\tau - \frac{1}{C_{rated}} \int_{t_0}^{t} I_{out}(\tau) \, d\tau$$

For a constant current discharge $I_{out} = I$ over time $\Delta t$:

$$\Delta SoC = \frac{I \cdot \Delta t}{C_{rated}}$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
A 50 Ah Li-ion cell is discharged at 25 A for 1 hour. Initial SoC = 100%.

$$\Delta SoC = \frac{25 \times 1}{50} = 0.50 = 50\%$$

$$SoC_{final} = 100\% - 50\% = 50\%$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Peukert's Law — Capacity Fade at High C-Rates

Empirically observed that lead-acid batteries deliver less capacity at higher discharge rates. Peukert (1897) proposed:

$$I^n \cdot t = C_{rated}$$

Solving for discharge time $t$:

$$t = \left(\frac{C_{rated}}{I}\right)^n \cdot H$$

where $H$ is a constant (typically 1 hour) and $n$ is the **Peukert exponent** (1.1 < n < 1.3 for lead-acid; ≈ 1.02 for Li-ion).

**Why it matters:** For $n = 1.2$ and $C_{rated} = 100$ Ah:
- At $I = 5$ A (C/20): $t = (100/5)^{1.2} \approx 34.4$ hr → delivers 172 Ah
- At $I = 50$ A (C/2): $t = (100/50)^{1.2} \approx 1.74$ hr → delivers only 87 Ah

The available capacity is *rate-dependent*—critical when sizing EV batteries.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Battery Chemistries for EV Use

| Chemistry | Nominal V (V) | Specific Energy (Wh/kg) | Cycle Life | Cost ($/kWh) | Safety | EV Adoption |
|-----------|---------------|--------------------------|------------|---------------|--------|-------------|
| Lead-Acid (VRLA) | 2.0 | 30–50 | 300–500 | 100–150 | High (no thermal runaway) | Legacy micro-hybrids |
| NiMH | 1.2 | 60–120 | 500–2000 | 250–350 | Good | Toyota Prius (older) |
| Li-ion (NMC) | 3.6–3.7 | 150–220 | 1000–3000 | 120–180 | Medium (needs BMS) | Tesla, Hyundai, Kia |
| Li-ion (LFP) | 3.2 | 90–160 | 2000–5000 | 100–140 | Excellent (thermal stable) | BYD, MG ZS EV |
| Li-ion (NCA) | 3.6 | 200–260 | 500–1000 | 150–200 | Medium | Tesla (older) |
| Solid-State (Li-metal) | ~3.8 | 300–500 (projected) | TBD | High (R&D) | High (no liquid electrolyte) | Toyota 2027+ (planned) |
| Na-ion | 3.0 | 100–160 | 2000–4000 | 60–90 (projected) | High | BYD / CATL (entry EVs) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Battery Cell — Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Anode: Graphite LiC6] -->|Li+ ions during discharge| B[Separator: Polymer Membrane]
    B -->|blocks electrons internally| C[Cathode: LiMO2]
    C -->|releases Li+ during charge| A
    D[External Circuit: Load] -->|electron flow during discharge| A
    A -->|electrons leave anode| D
    E[Electrolyte: LiPF6 in EC/DMC] --- A
    E --- B
    E --- C
    style A fill:#FFE4B5
    style B fill:#E0FFFF
    style C fill:#FFB6C1
    style D fill:#90EE90
    style E fill:#DDA0DD
```

## 4.2 Li-ion Discharge Reaction — Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Charged State: Li in Graphite] -->|Discharge: Li+ deintercalates from anode| B[Li+ migrates through electrolyte]
    B --> C[Li+ intercalates into cathode]
    C --> D[Electrons flow externally: Anode to Cathode]
    D --> E[Power delivered to EV traction motor]
    E --> F[SoC decreases; Voltage drops per discharge curve]
    style A fill:#FFD700
    style E fill:#32CD32
    style F fill:#FF6347
```

## 4.3 Battery Management System (BMS) — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PACK[Battery Pack Ns x Np]
        C1[Cell 1] --> M1[Cell Monitor IC]
        C2[Cell 2] --> M1
        C3[Cell Ns] --> M1
    end
    M1 -->|Cell voltages, temperatures| MCU[Central BMS MCU]
    TC[Temperature Sensors] --> MCU
    CS[Current Shunt] --> MCU
    MCU -->|SoC, SoH, SoP estimation| COMM[CAN Communication to VCU]
    MCU --> CO[Contactor Driver]
    CO --> SW[Main Contactor Switch]
    MCU --> BAL[Cell Balancing: Passive or Active]
    BAL --> PACK
    style MCU fill:#FFA500
    style COMM fill:#87CEEB
    style SW fill:#FF4500
```

## 4.4 Typical Li-ion Discharge Curve — Voltage vs SoC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[100% SoC: 4.20 V] -->|Flat plateau 4.2 to 3.6 V| B[60% SoC: 3.6 V]
    B -->|Steep drop 3.6 to 3.2 V| C[20% SoC: 3.2 V]
    C -->|Sharp knee 3.2 to 2.8 V| D[0% SoC: 2.8 V cutoff]
    style A fill:#00FF00
    style B fill:#ADFF2F
    style C fill:#FFFF00
    style D fill:#FF0000
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **flat plateau region** (3.6–4.2 V) is where the cell spends ~80% of its discharge time. EV BMS algorithms exploit this region for SoC estimation via voltage lookup tables combined with coulomb counting.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define State of Charge (SoC), Depth of Discharge (DoD), and State of Health (SoH) of a battery. How are they interrelated?**

**Model Answer:**

- **State of Charge (SoC):** The ratio of the *current available capacity* of a battery to its *rated (maximum) capacity*, expressed as a percentage. SoC = 100% means fully charged; 0% means fully discharged (cutoff voltage reached).

$$SoC(\%) = \frac{C_{available}}{C_{rated}} \times 100$$

- **Depth of Discharge (DoD):** The complementary metric, representing the *fraction of capacity that has been discharged* from the fully charged state.

$$DoD(\%) = 100 - SoC(\%)$$

- **State of Health (SoH):** A *degradation indicator* that quantifies the battery's present maximum capacity as a percentage of its original (as-new) capacity.

$$SoH(\%) = \frac{C_{max,current}}{C_{max,new}} \times 100$$

**Interrelation:** SoC and DoD are *instantaneous state variables* (change during every cycle), whereas SoH is a *long-term aging parameter* (changes slowly over hundreds to thousands of cycles). When SoH drops to 80%, the cell is typically considered End-of-Life (EOL) and recommended for second-life applications such as stationary grid storage.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**Explain the term "C-rate" with a suitable example. Why is C-rate a critical parameter in EV battery design?**

**Model Answer:**

The **C-rate** is a normalized measure of the *charge or discharge current* relative to the battery's rated capacity. Mathematically:

$$I_{C} = C\text{-rate} \times C_{rated}$$

A **1C rate** means the current that would fully discharge the battery in 1 hour. A **2C rate** means double that current (full discharge in 30 minutes). A **C/2 rate** means half (full discharge in 2 hours).

**Example:** A 60 Ah EV battery module.
- At 1C: Current = 60 A; full discharge in 1 hour.
- At 3C: Current = 180 A; full discharge in 20 minutes.
- At C/10: Current = 6 A; full discharge in 10 hours.

**Why critical in EV design:**
1. **Acceleration performance:** EV traction motors draw peak power (often 3C–5C continuous, 10C peak during launch). The battery must support these currents without excessive voltage sag.
2. **Thermal management:** High C-rates cause I²R heating inside cells, demanding robust cooling systems.
3. **Cycle life impact:** Sustained high-rate discharge accelerates electrode degradation.
4. **Charging speed:** Fast charging (DC) at 1C–3C minimizes downtime but stresses the cell; OEMs balance this with warranty commitments.
5. **Regenerative braking:** Capturing braking energy requires the battery to *accept* high charge currents (often 2C–5C peaks).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice Option 1) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the construction and working principle of a Lithium-ion (Li-ion) battery. Write the electrode half-reactions and the overall cell reaction during discharge.

**b) [7 Marks]** Compare the performance of Lead-Acid, NiMH, and Li-ion (NMC) batteries for EV applications based on specific energy, cycle life, cost, safety, and environmental impact.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Construction (refer to Block Diagram in Section 4.1):**

A Li-ion cell consists of:
1. **Anode:** Graphite (C) coated on a copper current collector.
2. **Cathode:** Lithium metal oxide such as LiCoO₂, Li(NiMnCo)O₂ (NMC), or LiFePO₄ coated on an aluminum current collector.
3. **Separator:** Microporous polyethylene (PE) or polypropylene (PP) membrane.
4. **Electrolyte:** Lithium salt (LiPF₆) dissolved in organic carbonate solvents (ethylene carbonate EC + dimethyl carbonate DMC).
5. **Housing:** Hard casing (cylindrical 18650/21700, prismatic, or pouch format).

**Working Principle:**

During **discharge**, lithium ions de-intercalate from the graphite anode, traverse the electrolyte, and intercalate into the cathode. Electrons flow externally from anode to cathode, providing current to the load.

**Half-reactions during discharge:**

Anode (oxidation):

$$LiC_6 \rightarrow C_6 + Li^+ + e^-$$

Cathode (reduction):

$$Li_{1-x}MO_2 + xLi^+ + xe^- \rightarrow LiMO_2$$

Overall cell reaction:

$$LiC_6 + Li_{1-x}MO_2 \rightarrow C_6 + LiMO_2$$

Nominal voltage ≈ 3.6–3.7 V (NMC). During charge, the reaction is reversed by applying an external voltage source.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Lead-Acid (VRLA) | NiMH | Li-ion (NMC) |
|-----------|------------------|------|--------------|
| Specific Energy (Wh/kg) | 30–50 | 60–120 | 150–220 |
| Nominal Cell Voltage (V) | 2.0 | 1.2 | 3.6–3.7 |
| Cycle Life (to 80% capacity) | 300–500 | 500–2000 | 1000–3000 |
| Cost ($/kWh) | 100–150 | 250–350 | 120–180 |
| Safety | High (no thermal runaway) | Good (mild exotherm) | Medium (requires BMS) |
| Self-Discharge/Month | 4–6% | 20–30% | 1–3% |
| Operating Temp Range | -20 to 60°C | -20 to 45°C | -20 to 60°C |
| Environmental Impact | Lead recycling essential | Nickel is toxic | Cobalt mining concerns |
| EV Suitability | Micro-hybrid 12V start-stop | Older hybrids (Toyota Prius gen 1–2) | Modern BEVs (Tesla, Hyundai, Kia) |

**Conclusion:** For modern EV traction applications, **Li-ion (NMC)** offers the best energy-to-weight ratio, longest cycle life among the three, and the lowest total cost of ownership over vehicle lifetime, despite requiring a sophisticated BMS. Lead-acid remains relevant for 12V auxiliary systems. NiMH is largely phased out of modern EVs but continues in some industrial applications.

---

### Question B (Choice Option 2) [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**a) [7 Marks]** What is a Battery Management System (BMS)? Explain its key functions with a functional block diagram.

**b) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the maximum power transfer of a battery modeled as a Thevenin equivalent (V_oc in series with R_int). Apply to a 400 V, 200 Ah pack with R_int = 0.05 Ω.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Definition:** A **Battery Management System (BMS)** is an electronic control unit that monitors, protects, and optimizes the operation of a rechargeable battery pack. In EVs, the BMS is critical for safety, longevity, and performance.

**Key Functions of BMS:**

1. **Cell Voltage Monitoring:** Continuously measures each cell's voltage to detect over-charge (>4.2 V for NMC) and over-discharge (<2.8 V).
2. **Current Sensing:** Uses a shunt resistor or Hall-effect sensor to measure pack current for SoC estimation and over-current protection.
3. **Temperature Monitoring:** NTC thermistors on cells and pack detect thermal runaway precursors.
4. **State Estimation:** Computes SoC (via coulomb counting + voltage correction), SoH (via capacity fade tracking), and State of Power (SoP).
5. **Cell Balancing:** Either *passive* (bleed resistors dissipate excess charge) or *active* (redistribute charge via DC-DC converters) to equalize cell SoCs.
6. **Safety Protection:** Triggers contactor disconnection on over-voltage, under-voltage, over-current, short circuit, or thermal events.
7. **Communication:** Interfaces with the Vehicle Control Unit (VCU) via CAN bus; transmits SoC, SoH, fault flags, and pack temperature.
8. **Thermal Management Coordination:** Commands coolant pumps/fans in liquid-cooled packs.

**Functional Block Diagram:** (See Section 4.3 above for Mermaid block diagram of BMS architecture.)

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Derivation:**

Model the battery as a Thevenin equivalent: an ideal voltage source $V_{oc}$ (open-circuit voltage) in series with internal resistance $R_{int}$. The battery is connected to a variable load resistance $R_L$.

The terminal voltage under load:

$$V_{term} = V_{oc} - I \cdot R_{int}$$

The current delivered to the load:

$$I = \frac{V_{oc}}{R_{int} + R_L}$$

The power dissipated in the load:

$$P_L = I^2 \cdot R_L = \frac{V_{oc}^2 \cdot R_L}{(R_{int} + R_L)^2}$$

To find maximum power, differentiate $P_L$ with respect to $R_L$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{dP_L}{dR_L} = \frac{V_{oc}^2 (R_{int} + R_L)^2 - V_{oc}^2 R_L \cdot 2(R_{int} + R_L)}{(R_{int} + R_L)^4} = 0$$

Numerator simplification:

$$V_{oc}^2 [(R_{int} + R_L) - 2R_L] = 0$$

$$R_L = R_{int}$$

**Maximum power transfer occurs when the load resistance equals the internal resistance (matched load condition):**

$$\boxed{P_{max} = \frac{V_{oc}^2}{4 R_{int}}}$$

**Numerical Application:**

Given: $V_{oc} = 400$ V, $R_{int} = 0.05$ Ω.

$$P_{max} = \frac{400^2}{4 \times 0.05} = \frac{160{,}000}{0.20} = 800{,}000 \text{ W} = 800 \text{ kW}$$

**Load current at peak power:**

$$I = \frac{V_{oc}}{2 R_{int}} = \frac{400}{0.1} = 4000 \text{ A}$$

**Interpretation:** This is the *theoretical* peak. In practice, EV inverters draw only 30–50% of this theoretical maximum to avoid excessive voltage sag, heat, and cell degradation. The matched-load condition also implies 50% efficiency (half the power is dissipated inside the battery as heat).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mistakes:** Forgetting to convert Ah to Wh when computing energy ($E = V \times C$, not just $C$).
> 2. **Confusing SoC and SoH:** SoC is a *state* (changes within a cycle); SoH is a *health* metric (degrades over months/years). Examiners deduct 1 mark for interchange.
> 3. **C-rate direction:** Discharging at 2C means *high* current, *short* time. Students often invert this.
> 4. **Half-reactions in Li-ion:** Forgetting to balance both mass and charge; examiners check lithium-ion stoichiometry strictly.
> 5. **Maximum Power Transfer Theorem:** Many students correctly state $P_{max} = V_{oc}^2 / 4R_{int}$ but forget to mention the **matched-load condition** ($R_L = R_{int}$), losing 2 marks.
> 6. **Chemistry naming:** Writing "lithium-ion" loosely without specifying chemistry (NMC, LFP, NCA, LTO) loses precision marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Battery = electrochemical device** converting chemical ↔ electrical energy via redox reactions.
- [ ] **Key components:** Anode, Cathode, Electrolyte, Separator.
- [ ] **Nominal voltages to memorize:** Lead-Acid 2.0 V; NiMH/NiCd 1.2 V; Li-ion NMC 3.7 V; LFP 3.2 V.
- [ ] **Faraday's law** governs theoretical capacity: $Q = nFm/M$.
- [ ] **C-rate** = normalized current; 1C drains the battery in 1 hour.
- [ ] **SoC + DoD = 100%** (always complementary).
- [ ] **SoH = 80%** is the standard EOL threshold for automotive batteries.
- [ ] **BMS is mandatory** for Li-ion packs — no exceptions.
- [ ] **Cell balancing** (passive/active) prevents capacity loss in series strings.
- [ ] **Peukert's Law** (n > 1) shows that high discharge rates *reduce* deliverable capacity.
- [ ] **Matched-load maximum power** $P_{max} = V_{oc}^2 / (4R_{int})$ is a frequent KTU derivation question.
- [ ] **Specific energy** ranks batteries for EV range; **specific power** ranks them for acceleration.
- [ ] **Solid-state batteries** are the future: 300–500 Wh/kg projected, but commercialization is still pending (Toyota 2027+).
- [ ] **Thermal management** is non-negotiable: I²R losses scale with the square of current.
- [ ] **EOL second-life** (stationary grid storage) extends the usable life of EV batteries by 5–10 years.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Battery - Battery Parameters - Units of Battery Energy Storage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET742 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES AND RENEWABLE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types of Battery - Battery Parameters - Units of Battery Energy Storage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔋 Module 3 — Battery Fundamentals: Types, Parameters & Energy Storage Units

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Battery (KTU 2024 Definition):** A battery is an **electrochemical energy storage device** that converts stored chemical energy into electrical energy through controlled **redox (reduction–oxidation) reactions** at two electrodes — the **anode** (oxidation, electron source) and the **cathode** (reduction, electron sink) — separated by an **electrolyte** that permits ionic conduction but blocks electronic conduction.

In the context of Electric Vehicles (EVs) and Renewable Energy Systems, batteries are classified as **secondary (rechargeable) cells** because the chemical reactions are *electrically reversible*, allowing repeated charge–discharge cycles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Terminology (KTU 2024):**
> - **Cell** = A single electrochemical unit (e.g., 3.7 V Li-ion).
> - **Module** = A cluster of cells connected in series/parallel (e.g., 48 V).
> - **Pack** = A complete battery assembly with BMS, housing, and thermal management (e.g., 400 V EV pack).
> - **BMS** = Battery Management System — the electronic "brain" that monitors SoC, SoH, temperature, and balancing.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Water Tank" Analogy

Imagine a **water reservoir** connected to a turbine:

| Battery Concept | Water-Tank Equivalent | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Voltage (V)** | Water pressure at the tank outlet | The "push" driving electrons |
| **Current (A)** | Flow rate of water | How fast charge is being delivered |
| **Capacity (Ah)** | Total volume of the tank | Total charge the battery can hold |
| **Energy (Wh)** | Total useful work from the water | Pressure × Volume |
| **C-rate** | How fast you drain the tank | Discharge intensity |
| **SoC** | How full the tank is right now (%) | Instantaneous state |
| **DoD** | How much has been drawn out (%) | Depth of depletion |
| **SoH** | Tank wall condition (rust, cracks) | Long-term health |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** A battery does **not** store electrons — it stores **chemical potential** that *pushes* electrons through an external circuit. Energy = Voltage × Charge. Always.

---

## 1.3 Units of Battery Energy Storage

Energy storage in batteries is fundamentally a measure of **electrical work** the cell can perform. The standard units, ranked by scale, are:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Hierarchy of Battery Energy Units:**
> - **1 Watt-hour (Wh)** = **3600 Joules (J)** → consumer scale (phone = 15 Wh)
> - **1 Kilowatt-hour (kWh)** = **1000 Wh = 3.6 MJ** → EV scale (Tesla Model 3 ≈ **60 kWh**)
> - **1 Megawatt-hour (MWh)** = **1000 kWh = 3.6 GJ** → grid storage (Tesla Megapack = **3.9 MWh**)
> - **1 Gigawatt-hour (GWh)** = **1000 MWh** → utility-scale, city-level storage

The **secondary (charge) unit** is the **Ampere-hour (Ah)**, which multiplied by the **nominal voltage** gives energy:

$$\text{Energy (Wh)} = \text{Voltage (V)} \times \text{Capacity (Ah)}$$

A **nominal voltage** of **3.7 V** and a **capacity** of **100 Ah** therefore stores **370 Wh** of energy.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis

## 2.1 Classification of Rechargeable Batteries (KTU 2024 Taxonomy)

Batteries are broadly classified by their **electrolyte chemistry** and the **active ion** that shuttles charge between electrodes during operation.

### A. By Active Ion / Chemistry

**1. Lead-Acid (Pb-acid)**
- Oldest rechargeable technology (1859, Gaston Planté).
- Chemistry: $\text{Pb} + \text{PbO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \leftrightarrow 2\text{PbSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$
- Nominal voltage per cell: **2.0 V**.
- Used in: conventional 12 V SLI (Starting-Lighting-Ignition) car batteries, low-cost e-rickshaws.

**2. Nickel-Cadmium (NiCd)**
- Anode: Cd, Cathode: NiOOH, Electrolyte: KOH.
- Nominal voltage: **1.2 V**. Suffers from "memory effect."
- Largely phased out due to **toxic cadmium**.

**3. Nickel-Metal Hydride (NiMH)**
- Anode: metal hydride (MH), Cathode: NiOOH.
- Nominal voltage: **1.2 V**. Used in **Toyota Prius (Gen 1–3)** hybrid vehicles.

**4. Lithium-ion (Li-ion) Family — Dominant in Modern EVs**
- Active ion: **Li⁺** shuttles between anode and cathode via intercalation.
- Nominal voltage: **3.6 V – 3.8 V** (3× higher than Pb-acid/NiMH).
- Sub-chemistries:
  - **LFP** — $\text{LiFePO}_4$ cathode → safest, longest cycle life, used in **BYD Blade, Tesla Model 3 SR**.
  - **NMC** — $\text{LiNi}_x\text{Mn}_y\text{Co}_z\text{O}_2$ → best energy density, used in **Tesla Model S/X, Hyundai Ioniq 5**.
  - **NCA** — $\text{LiNi}_{0.8}\text{Co}_{0.15}\text{Al}_{0.05}\text{O}_2$ → used in **Tesla (Panasonic cells)**.
  - **LTO** — $\text{Li}_4\text{Ti}_5\text{O}_{12}$ anode → ultra-fast charging, used in **Mitsubishi i-MiEV, Honda Fit EV**.

**5. Solid-State Batteries (SSB)**
- Replaces liquid electrolyte with a **solid ceramic or polymer electrolyte** (e.g., $\text{Li}_7\text{La}_3\text{Zr}_2\text{O}_{12}$ — LLZO garnet).
- Higher energy density, no thermal runaway, used in **Toyota, QuantumScape** prototypes.

**6. Sodium-Ion (Na-ion)**
- Active ion: **Na⁺** — abundant, cheap, but lower energy density. Commercialised by **CATL, BYD** in 2023–24.
- Nominal voltage: **3.0 – 3.1 V**.

**7. Redox Flow Batteries (RFB)**
- Energy stored in **liquid electrolyte tanks** (e.g., vanadium ions: $\text{V}^{2+}/\text{V}^{3+}$ and $\text{VO}^{2+}/\text{VO}_2^{+}$).
- Power = stack size; Energy = tank volume. Independent scaling → ideal for **grid storage**.

### B. By Application

| Category | Typical Capacity | Common Chemistry |
|---|---|---|
| **Portable Electronics** | 5 – 100 Wh | Li-ion (LCO, NMC) |
| **EV Traction** | 30 – 120 kWh | NMC, NCA, LFP |
| **Hybrid EV (HEV)** | 1 – 2 kWh | NiMH, LTO |
| **Stationary / Grid** | 1 MWh – 1 GWh | LFP, Vanadium RFB, Na-S |

---

## 2.2 Key Battery Parameters (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These **12 parameters** form the **definitive KTU checklist** for Module 3. Memorise each definition and its unit.

**1. Nominal Voltage ($V_{nom}$)**
- Manufacturer-rated average operating voltage per cell. Used for **Wh ↔ Ah** conversion.

**2. Capacity ($C$, in Ah)**
- Total charge a fully charged cell can deliver at a reference discharge rate (usually 0.2C) until cutoff voltage.
- Depends on **temperature, C-rate, and age**.

**3. Energy ($E$, in Wh)**
- Total electrical work: $E = V_{nom} \times C$.

**4. Energy Density**
- *Gravimetric*: $\text{Wh/kg}$ — determines **range** of an EV.
- *Volumetric*: $\text{Wh/L}$ — determines **pack sizing**.

**5. Power Density**
- $\text{W/kg}$ or $\text{W/L}$ — determines **acceleration** and **fast-charging** capability.

**6. C-rate (C)**
- Defines charge/discharge current as a multiple of rated capacity.
- Current $I = \text{C-rate} \times C_{rated}$.
- 1C of a 50 Ah cell = 50 A → fully discharged in **1 hour**.

**7. State of Charge (SoC)**
- Present capacity as a **%** of rated capacity. $0\% = $ empty, $100\% = $ full.

**8. Depth of Discharge (DoD)**
- $\text{DoD} = 100\% - \text{SoC}$. The fraction of capacity *drawn out*.

**9. State of Health (SoH)**
- $\text{SoH} = \dfrac{C_{present}}{C_{rated}} \times 100\%$. End-of-life (EoL) typically at **$\text{SoH} = 80\%$**.

**10. Internal Resistance ($R_{int}$)**
- Resistive losses in the cell (mΩ range). Generates heat: $P_{loss} = I^2 R_{int}$.

**11. Cycle Life**
- Number of full charge–discharge cycles before capacity drops to 80% of rated.
- LFP: **> 3000**; NMC: **1000 – 2000**; Pb-acid: **300 – 500**.

**12. Self-Discharge Rate**
- Capacity loss per month when idle. Li-ion ≈ **1 – 2 %/month**; NiMH ≈ **20 – 30 %/month**.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> All KTU numerical problems on this topic are solved with the formulas below. Commit them to memory.

| # | Formula | Symbol Key | Unit | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = V_{nom} \times C$ | E = energy, V = voltage, C = capacity | Wh | Convert between Wh and Ah |
| 2 | $E_{pack} = V_{pack} \times C_{pack}$ | Pack-level energy | Wh, kWh | EV pack sizing |
| 3 | $I = \text{C-rate} \times C_{rated}$ | I = current, C = rated capacity | A | Compute discharge current |
| 4 | $t = \dfrac{1}{\text{C-rate}}$ | t = discharge time | h | Find discharge duration |
| 5 | $\text{SoC}(t) = 1 - \dfrac{1}{C_{rated}}\int_0^{t} I(\tau)\, d\tau$ | Instantaneous SoC | dimensionless | Dynamic SoC tracking |
| 6 | $\text{DoD} = 100\% - \text{SoC}$ | Depth of discharge | % | Inverter/load scheduling |
| 7 | $\text{SoH} = \dfrac{C_{present}}{C_{rated}} \times 100\%$ | State of health | % | Battery ageing assessment |
| 8 | $\rho_E = \dfrac{E}{m}$ | Specific energy | Wh/kg | Compare chemistries |
| 9 | $\rho_P = \dfrac{P}{m}$ | Specific power | W/kg | Acceleration/charging |
| 10 | $E_{J} = E_{Wh} \times 3600$ | Energy in Joules | J | SI unit conversion |
| 11 | $E_{kWh} = \dfrac{E_{Wh}}{1000}$ | Energy in kWh | kWh | EV-scale conversion |
| 12 | $P_{loss} = I^2 R_{int}$ | Ohmic heating loss | W | Thermal design |
| 13 | $\eta = \dfrac{E_{dis}}{E_{ch}} \times 100\%$ | Round-trip efficiency | % | Energy balance |
| 14 | $Q = I \times t$ | Charge in Coulombs | C (or Ah) | Charge calculation |
| 15 | $V_{series} = N_s \times V_{cell}$ | Series voltage | V | Pack voltage design |
| 16 | $C_{parallel} = N_p \times C_{cell}$ | Parallel capacity | Ah | Pack capacity design |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** When asked "energy of a battery," never write only $V \times C$ without specifying **units**. If $V$ is in V and $C$ in Ah, the result is in **Wh** — not Joules, not kWh.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Parameter of Interest | Why it Matters |
|---|---|---|
| **EV Range Estimation** | Energy density (Wh/kg) | Higher → longer range per kg |
| **EV Fast Charging** | Power density + C-rate | Higher → 0–80% in < 20 min |
| **Grid Storage Sizing** | Energy (MWh) + Cycle life | Decades of cost-effective cycling |
| **Battery Safety (BMS)** | Internal resistance, Temp | Predicts thermal runaway |
| **Second-life Batteries** | SoH | EV battery at SoH = 80% can serve grid storage |
| **Cost Engineering** | \$/kWh | LFP ≈ **\$130/kWh**, NMC ≈ **\$150/kWh** (2024) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> All derivations below are written in the **"First Principles"** style expected in KTU 14-mark answers. Every algebraic transition is shown explicitly, with no step-skipping.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Energy Stored in a Battery (from Power Definition)

**Starting point — definition of electrical power:**

$$P = V \times I$$

where $P$ = instantaneous electrical power in **Watts**, $V$ = terminal voltage in **Volts**, and $I$ = current in **Amperes**.

**Step 1 — Express energy as the time integral of power:**

$$E = \int_{0}^{t} P(\tau)\, d\tau$$

This is the fundamental physics definition: energy is the area under the power–time curve. For a battery, voltage and current are approximately constant during a discharge, so $P$ is approximately constant.

**Step 2 — Substitute $P = V \times I$ and assume constancy:**

$$E = \int_{0}^{t} V \cdot I\, d\tau = V \cdot I \int_{0}^{t} d\tau = V \cdot I \cdot t$$

**Step 3 — Define the charge $Q$ delivered:**

By definition of electric current, the total charge that has flowed in time $t$ is:

$$Q = I \times t \quad \text{(in Coulombs, if $I$ in A and $t$ in s)}$$

**Step 4 — Combine to eliminate current and time:**

Substituting $I \cdot t = Q$ into the energy expression:

$$E = V \times Q$$

If $V$ is in volts and $Q$ is in **coulombs**, then $E$ is in **joules (J)**.

**Step 5 — Rescale to the engineering-friendly unit Watt-hour:**

Charge in **ampere-hours (Ah)** is related to charge in coulombs by:

$$Q_{\text{Ah}} = \frac{Q_{\text{C}}}{3600} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad Q_{\text{C}} = 3600 \cdot Q_{\text{Ah}}$$

Substituting into $E = V \cdot Q_C$:

$$E_{\text{J}} = V \cdot 3600 \cdot Q_{\text{Ah}}$$

Dividing both sides by 3600 to convert joules to watt-hours:

$$\frac{E_{\text{J}}}{3600} = V \cdot Q_{\text{Ah}}$$

The left side, by definition, is energy in watt-hours:

$$\boxed{\,E_{\text{Wh}} = V_{\text{V}} \times C_{\text{Ah}}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is **the single most important equation** in KTU Module 3. Every pack-sizing problem ultimately reduces to this identity.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — C-rate, Discharge Time and Available Current

**Given:** A battery with rated capacity $C_{rated}$ in Ah.

**Step 1 — Define the C-rate formally:**

$$\text{C-rate} = \frac{I}{C_{rated}}$$

with units of $\text{h}^{-1}$ (i.e., "per hour"). By convention, a **1C** discharge means the current is numerically equal to the rated capacity.

**Step 2 — Solve for the discharge current:**

$$I = \text{C-rate} \times C_{rated}$$

**Step 3 — Express the discharge time:**

Total charge delivered equals capacity: $Q = C_{rated} = I \cdot t$. Solving for $t$:

$$t = \frac{C_{rated}}{I} = \frac{C_{rated}}{\text{C-rate} \cdot C_{rated}} = \frac{1}{\text{C-rate}}$$

Hence, the **discharge time is the reciprocal of the C-rate.**

**Step 4 — Verification by examples:**

| C-rate | Current (for 50 Ah cell) | Discharge time |
|---|---|---|
| 0.2 C | 10 A | 5 h |
| 0.5 C | 25 A | 2 h |
| 1 C | 50 A | 1 h |
| 2 C | 100 A | 0.5 h (30 min) |
| 5 C | 250 A | 12 min |

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — State of Charge Dynamics (Integral Form)

**Step 1 — Start with a continuous-discharge current $I(t)$:**

The instantaneous SoC of a cell is defined as the *remaining* charge divided by the *rated* charge:

$$\text{SoC}(t) = \frac{C_{remaining}(t)}{C_{rated}}$$

**Step 2 — Express remaining capacity as rated minus what has been drawn out:**

$$C_{remaining}(t) = C_{rated} - \int_{0}^{t} I(\tau)\, d\tau$$

The integral $\int_0^t I(\tau)\, d\tau$ is the total charge that has left the cell between time 0 and time $t$, measured in ampere-hours.

**Step 3 — Combine to obtain the dynamic SoC equation:**

$$\text{SoC}(t) = \frac{C_{rated} - \int_{0}^{t} I(\tau)\, d\tau}{C_{rated}}$$

$$\text{SoC}(t) = 1 - \frac{1}{C_{rated}}\int_{0}^{t} I(\tau)\, d\tau$$

**Step 4 — Special case — constant current $I$:**

If the current is constant, the integral reduces to $I \cdot t$:

$$\text{SoC}(t) = 1 - \frac{I \cdot t}{C_{rated}}$$

Equivalently, defining discharge duration $t_{end} = C_{rated}/I$:

$$\text{SoC}(t) = 1 - \frac{t}{t_{end}}$$

This is a **straight-line ramp** from SoC = 1 at $t=0$ to SoC = 0 at $t = t_{end}$ — the ideal linear discharge.

**Step 5 — Relate to Depth of Discharge:**

$$\text{DoD}(t) = 1 - \text{SoC}(t) = \frac{1}{C_{rated}}\int_{0}^{t} I(\tau)\, d\tau$$

So **DoD + SoC = 1** at every instant.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Energy Density (Gravimetric and Volumetric)

**Given:** A battery cell of mass $m$ (in kg), volume $\mathcal{V}$ (in litres), and stored energy $E$ (in Wh).

**Step 1 — Gravimetric (specific) energy:**

By definition, specific energy is energy per unit mass:

$$\rho_{E,\,grav} = \frac{E}{m} \quad [\text{Wh/kg}]$$

**Step 2 — Volumetric energy density:**

By definition, volumetric energy density is energy per unit volume:

$$\rho_{E,\,vol} = \frac{E}{\mathcal{V}} \quad [\text{Wh/L}]$$

**Step 3 — Numerical demonstration:**

A Li-ion NMC cell: $E = 200\,\text{Wh}$, $m = 1.1\,\text{kg}$, $\mathcal{V} = 0.5\,\text{L}$.

$$\rho_{E,\,grav} = \frac{200}{1.1} \approx 182\,\text{Wh/kg}$$

$$\rho_{E,\,vol} = \frac{200}{0.5} = 400\,\text{Wh/L}$$

> [!TIP]
> A **Tata Nexon EV** with a **30.2 kWh** pack and ~**350 kg** pack mass gives a pack-level specific energy of $\approx 86\,\text{Wh/kg}$ — about **half** the cell-level value due to packaging, BMS, and thermal hardware.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Unit Conversions Across Battery Energy Scales

**Step 1 — Watt-hour to Joule:**

By definition of the watt: $1\,\text{W} = 1\,\text{J/s}$. Therefore:

$$1\,\text{Wh} = 1\,\text{W} \times 3600\,\text{s} = 3600\,\text{J}$$

**Step 2 — kWh to MJ:**

$$1\,\text{kWh} = 1000 \times 3600\,\text{J} = 3.6 \times 10^{6}\,\text{J} = 3.6\,\text{MJ}$$

**Step 3 — MWh to GJ:**

$$1\,\text{MWh} = 1000 \times 3.6\,\text{MJ} = 3.6\,\text{GJ}$$

**Step 4 — General formula:**

$$E_{\text{Wh}} \xrightarrow{\times 3600} E_{\text{J}} \xrightarrow{\div 10^{6}} E_{\text{MJ}}$$

**Step 5 — Worked numerical example:**

Convert a **75 kWh** EV pack to megajoules:

$$E = 75 \times 3.6 = 270\,\text{MJ}$$

Equivalent to the kinetic energy of a 1500 kg car moving at:

$$\tfrac{1}{2} m v^2 = 270 \times 10^6 \;\Rightarrow\; v^2 = \frac{2 \times 270 \times 10^6}{1500} = 360{,}000$$

$$v = 600\,\text{m/s} \approx 2160\,\text{km/h}$$

> This shows why **75 kWh** is an enormous quantity of energy in mechanical terms — only a fraction is converted to motion, the rest is lost to drag, rolling resistance, and heat.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol, all diagrams below use **safe Mermaid syntax** — alphanumeric node IDs, no reserved keywords, plain uppercase labels without embedded markdown.

---

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Hierarchical Classification of Rechargeable Batteries

```mermaid
graph TD
    battRoot["RECHARGEABLE BATTERIES"]
    battRoot --> famA["AQUEOUS ELECTROLYTE FAMILY"]
    battRoot --> famB["NON AQUEOUS LI BASED FAMILY"]
    battRoot --> famC["POST LITHIUM EMERGING FAMILY"]
    battRoot --> famD["FLOW BATTERY FAMILY"]

    famA --> aq1["LEAD ACID SLI AND DEEP CYCLE"]
    famA --> aq2["NICKEL CADMIUM NI CD"]
    famA --> aq3["NICKEL METAL HYDRIDE NI MH"]

    famB --> liA["LITHIUM ION LI ION"]
    liA --> liA1["LFP LIFEPO4 SAFE LONG CYCLE"]
    liA --> liA2["NMC HIGH ENERGY TESLA HYUNDAI"]
    liA --> liA3["NCA TESLA PANASONIC"]
    liA --> liA4["LTO ULTRA FAST CHARGE"]
    famB --> liB["LITHIUM POLYMER LIPO"]
    famB --> liC["SOLID STATE BATTERY SSB"]

    famC --> po1["SODIUM ION NA ION"]
    famC --> po2["ZINC AIR ZN AIR"]
    famC --> po3["MAGNESIUM ION MG ION"]

    famD --> fb1["VANADIUM REDOX VRFB"]
    famD --> fb2["ZINC BROMINE ZN BR2"]
    famD --> fb3["IRON CHROMATE FE CR"]
```

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Battery Parameter Inter-Relationship (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    p1["V NOMINAL VOLTAGE V"] --> e1["E ENERGY WH"]
    p2["C CAPACITY AH"] --> e1
    e1 --> d1["ENERGY DENSITY WH PER KG"]
    e1 --> d2["VOLUMETRIC DENSITY WH PER L"]
    p2 --> cr["C RATE 1 PER H"]
    cr --> i1["I DISCHARGE CURRENT A"]
    cr --> t1["T DISCHARGE TIME H"]
    p1 --> ocv["OCV OPEN CIRCUIT VOLTAGE"]
    p1 --> rint["R INTERNAL RESISTANCE MOHM"]
    rint --> heat["P LOSS I SQUARED R HEAT"]
    heat --> th["THERMAL RUNAWAY RISK"]
    p2 --> soc["SOC STATE OF CHARGE PCT"]
    soc --> dod["DOD DEPTH OF DISCHARGE PCT"]
    soc --> soh["SOH STATE OF HEALTH PCT"]
    soh --> cyclife["CYCLE LIFE REMAINING"]
    cyclife --> rpv["REPLACEMENT DECISION"]
```

> [!TIP]
> This is a **Board Examiner's favourite diagram** — it tests whether the student understands that **Energy = Voltage × Capacity** and how all other parameters (SoC, DoD, C-rate, SoH, cycle life) are derived from these two primitives.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Unit Hierarchy of Battery Energy Storage

```mermaid
graph TD
    uRoot["BATTERY ENERGY STORAGE UNITS"]
    uRoot --> u1["1 WH = 3600 J CONSUMER SCALE"]
    uRoot --> u2["1 KWH = 1000 WH EV SCALE"]
    uRoot --> u3["1 MWH = 1000 KWH GRID SCALE"]
    uRoot --> u4["1 GWH = 1000 MWH CITY SCALE"]
    uRoot --> u5["AH = CHARGE UNIT WH = V TIMES AH"]
    uRoot --> u6["BTU = 1 WH = 3.412 BTU"]
    uRoot --> u7["JOULE SI UNIT 1 WH = 3600 J"]
```
