## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Functional Block Architecture of a Battery Pack

```mermaid
graph TD
    packRoot["EV BATTERY PACK ARCHITECTURE"]
    packRoot --> cellBlock["CELL LEVEL 3.7 V LI ION"]
    cellBlock --> modBlock["MODULE LEVEL 12S 4P 48 V"]
    modBlock --> packBlock["PACK LEVEL 10 MODULES IN SERIES 400 V"]
    packBlock --> bms["BMS BATTERY MANAGEMENT SYSTEM"]
    bms --> mon["MONITORING CELL V I T"]
    bms --> bal["CELL BALANCING"]
    bms --> prot["PROTECTION OVER V OVER I OVER T"]
    bms --> com["CAN COMMUNICATION TO VCU"]
    packBlock --> tms["THERMAL MANAGEMENT AIR OR LIQUID"]
    packBlock --> pdu["POWER DISTRIBUTION UNIT CONTACTORS AND FUSES"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This functional block diagram illustrates how a **cell → module → pack** hierarchy is managed by the **BMS** and **TMS** (Thermal Management System) — a recurring 14-mark question in KTU.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below are modelled on the **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Evaluation (ESE)** pattern: **Part A (3-mark short answers)** and **Part B (14-mark with internal choice)**.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List any six key parameters used to characterise a rechargeable battery and state the SI unit of each.**

**Model Answer (6 points, ½ mark each):**

1. **Nominal Voltage (V)** — in **Volts (V)**.
2. **Capacity (C)** — in **Ampere-hours (Ah)**.
3. **Energy (E)** — in **Watt-hours (Wh)** or **Joules (J)**.
4. **Energy Density (Gravimetric)** — in **Wh/kg**.
5. **Power Density** — in **W/kg**.
6. **C-rate** — in **h⁻¹** (per hour).
7. **Internal Resistance** — in **milli-ohms (mΩ)**.
8. **Cycle Life** — in number of cycles (dimensionless).
9. **State of Charge (SoC)** — in **%**.
10. **State of Health (SoH)** — in **%**.

*(Any six, with correct unit = 3 marks.)*

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Distinguish between State of Charge (SoC) and Depth of Discharge (DoD) of a battery.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | State of Charge (SoC) | Depth of Discharge (DoD) |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Fraction of rated capacity *remaining* in the cell at a given instant | Fraction of rated capacity *already drawn out* from the cell |
| **Range** | 0 % to 100 % | 0 % to 100 % |
| **At full charge** | SoC = 100 % | DoD = 0 % |
| **At empty** | SoC = 0 % | DoD = 100 % |
| **Mathematical relation** | $\text{SoC} = \dfrac{C_{rem}}{C_{rated}} \times 100\%$ | $\text{DoD} = \dfrac{C_{drawn}}{C_{rated}} \times 100\%$ |
| **Complementarity** | $\text{SoC} + \text{DoD} = 100\%$ | $\text{DoD} = 100\% - \text{SoC}$ |
| **Use case** | BMS reporting, range estimation | Inverter sizing, ageing analysis |

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (With Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part B always provides an **internal choice**: either **Question A** *or* **Question B**. Each question carries sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Model solutions are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]

**(a) Classify the major types of rechargeable batteries used in electric vehicles. Compare Lead-Acid, NiMH, and Li-ion chemistries on the basis of nominal voltage, energy density, cycle life, and safety.** **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1 — Classification of EV Batteries:**
Rechargeable EV batteries are classified into:
1. **Lead-Acid (Pb-acid)** — conventional SLI and advanced AGM/gel types.
2. **Nickel-Metal Hydride (NiMH)** — used in early hybrid EVs.
3. **Lithium-ion (Li-ion) family** — LFP, NMC, NCA, LTO.
4. **Solid-State Batteries (SSB)** — emerging, with solid electrolytes.
5. **Sodium-ion (Na-ion)** — emerging low-cost alternative.

**Step 2 — Comparison Table (3 marks for table + 2 marks for explanation):**

| Property | Lead-Acid | NiMH | Li-ion (NMC) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Nominal cell voltage** | 2.0 V | 1.2 V | 3.6 – 3.7 V |
| **Specific energy (Wh/kg)** | 30 – 50 | 60 – 110 | 150 – 250 |
| **Volumetric energy (Wh/L)** | 60 – 100 | 150 – 200 | 400 – 700 |
| **Cycle life** | 300 – 500 | 500 – 1000 | 1000 – 2000 |
| **Self-discharge/month** | 4 – 6 % | 20 – 30 % | 1 – 2 % |
| **Memory effect** | No | Yes (mild) | No |
| **Safety / thermal runaway** | Low risk | Moderate | **High** (needs BMS) |
| **Cost (\$/kWh)** | 100 – 150 | 250 – 350 | 130 – 160 |
| **Toxicity** | High (Pb) | Low | Low |
| **EV use case** | Low-speed e-rickshaws | Early Toyota Prius | Tesla, Hyundai, Tata |

**Step 3 — Conclusion (1 mark):**
**Li-ion NMC** dominates modern EVs due to its **3× higher cell voltage**, **5× higher specific energy**, and **> 4× longer cycle life** compared to Lead-Acid. NiMH was a transitional technology; Lead-Acid is now restricted to auxiliary 12 V systems in EVs.

---

**(b) Define C-rate of a battery. A 60 Ah Li-ion cell is discharged at 2C. Compute (i) the discharge current, (ii) the discharge time, and (iii) the total energy delivered, given the nominal voltage is 3.7 V.** **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1 — Definition of C-rate (2 marks):**
The **C-rate** of a battery is the discharge (or charge) current expressed as a multiple of its rated capacity.

$$I = \text{C-rate} \times C_{rated}$$

A 1C discharge of any battery delivers its full rated capacity in exactly 1 hour.

**Step 2 — Given data:**
- $C_{rated} = 60\,\text{Ah}$
- C-rate $= 2\,\text{C}$
- $V_{nom} = 3.7\,\text{V}$

**Step 3 — (i) Discharge current (2 marks):**

$$I = 2 \times 60 = 120\,\text{A}$$

**Step 4 — (ii) Discharge time (2 marks):**

$$t = \frac{1}{\text{C-rate}} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5\,\text{h} = 30\,\text{minutes}$$

**Step 5 — (iii) Total energy delivered (1 mark):**

$$E = V_{nom} \times C = 3.7 \times 60 = 222\,\text{Wh}$$

**Result:** $I = 120\,\text{A}$, $t = 0.5\,\text{h}$, $E = 222\,\text{Wh}$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a) Explain the following battery parameters with units: (i) Energy density, (ii) Power density, (iii) Internal resistance, (iv) Cycle life, (v) Self-discharge rate, (vi) State of Health (SoH).** **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution:

**(i) Energy Density (ρ_E):**
The amount of energy stored per unit mass (gravimetric, Wh/kg) or per unit volume (volumetric, Wh/L). Higher energy density means **longer EV range** for the same mass/volume.
$$\rho_{E,grav} = \frac{E}{m} \quad ; \quad \rho_{E,vol} = \frac{E}{\mathcal{V}}$$

**(ii) Power Density (ρ_P):**
The peak power deliverable per unit mass (W/kg) or volume (W/L). Determines **acceleration** and **fast-charge** capability.
$$\rho_{P,grav} = \frac{P_{max}}{m}$$

**(iii) Internal Resistance (R_int):**
The ohmic resistance of the cell (electrolyte + electrodes + current collectors), typically **0.1 – 10 mΩ** for Li-ion cells. Causes **I²R heat loss** and voltage sag under load:
$$P_{loss} = I^2 R_{int} \quad ; \quad V_{terminal} = V_{OCV} - I R_{int}$$

**(iv) Cycle Life:**
The number of complete **charge–discharge cycles** a battery can sustain before its capacity drops to 80 % of the rated value (SoH = 80 %). For LFP > 3000, NMC ≈ 1500, Pb-acid ≈ 400.

**(v) Self-Discharge Rate:**
The percentage of stored charge lost per month when the battery is idle. Driven by parasitic side reactions.
- Li-ion: **1 – 2 %/month**
- NiMH: **20 – 30 %/month**
- Pb-acid: **4 – 6 %/month**

**(vi) State of Health (SoH):**
The ratio of the **current maximum capacity** to the **rated capacity**, expressed as a percentage:
$$\text{SoH} = \frac{C_{present}}{C_{rated}} \times 100\%$$
- New cell: SoH = 100 %.
- End-of-life: SoH = 80 % (typical retirement threshold for automotive use).
- At 80 %, the battery is repurposed to **second-life** stationary storage.

---

**(b) An EV traction battery pack is rated at 400 V and 100 Ah. The vehicle consumes an average power of 25 kW while driving. Compute: (i) the total pack energy in kWh, (ii) the theoretical range in hours at the given power, and (iii) the new SoC after driving for 1.5 hours.** **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution:

**Given:** $V_{pack} = 400\,\text{V}$, $C_{pack} = 100\,\text{Ah}$, $P = 25\,\text{kW}$.

**Step 1 — (i) Pack energy in kWh (2 marks):**

$$E = V_{pack} \times C_{pack} = 400 \times 100 = 40{,}000\,\text{Wh} = 40\,\text{kWh}$$

**Step 2 — (ii) Theoretical range (driving duration) (2 marks):**

Energy consumed per hour = 25 kWh. Therefore:

$$t = \frac{E}{P} = \frac{40}{25} = 1.6\,\text{hours} = 96\,\text{minutes}$$

**Step 3 — (iii) New SoC after 1.5 h of driving (3 marks):**

Charge consumed in 1.5 h:

$$C_{used} = \frac{P \times t}{V_{pack}} = \frac{25{,}000 \times 1.5}{400} = \frac{37{,}500}{400} = 93.75\,\text{Ah}$$

Remaining capacity:

$$C_{rem} = 100 - 93.75 = 6.25\,\text{Ah}$$

New SoC:

$$\text{SoC}_{new} = \frac{6.25}{100} \times 100\% = 6.25\%$$

**Result:** $E = 40\,\text{kWh}$, $t_{range} = 1.6\,\text{h}$, $\text{SoC}_{new} = 6.25\,\%$.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU students LOSE marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Unit mismatch trap:** Writing "E = 3.7 × 60 = 222" without the unit "Wh". Always append the unit.
> 2. **Conflating Ah and kWh:** A 100 Ah, 3.7 V cell stores 370 Wh = **0.37 kWh**, NOT 100 kWh. The kWh conversion requires multiplying by voltage.
> 3. **SoC vs DoD confusion:** SoC is what *remains*; DoD is what *has been drawn*. They are complements. Writing "SoC = 100 % − DoD" correctly earns full credit; reversing it loses marks.
> 4. **C-rate calculation error:** Students forget that C-rate is in **per hour**, so 2C = 30 min discharge, not "2 hours."
> 5. **Cycle-life unit:** Cycle life is a **count** (dimensionless), not a time. Writing "3000 hours" instead of "3000 cycles" is a common 1-mark deduction.
> 6. **Forgetting SoC is a percentage:** Numerical SoC values are between 0 and 1 in formula, but should be reported as 0 % – 100 % in answers.
> 7. **Treating pack voltage as cell voltage:** In pack problems, the 400 V is the **full pack** voltage (after series connection), not a single cell.
> 8. **Energy density versus power density:** Energy density → range; Power density → acceleration. Mixing these loses the conceptual 2-mark.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [x] **Battery = electrochemical cell** with anode, cathode, electrolyte — stores chemical energy, releases as electrical.
- [x] **Primary cells** are non-rechargeable; **secondary cells** (used in EVs) are rechargeable.
- [x] **Energy E (Wh) = V (V) × C (Ah)** — the master equation.
- [x] **Capacity** is in **Ah**; **Energy** is in **Wh or kWh**; **Power** is in **W or kW**.
- [x] **C-rate** defines current; discharge time $t = 1 / \text{C-rate}$.
- [x] **SoC + DoD = 100 %** at every instant.
- [x] **SoH** is the *current* maximum capacity relative to the *rated* capacity; end-of-life at **80 %**.
- [x] **Internal resistance** causes **I²R heating**, which limits fast-charging and triggers thermal runaway.
- [x] **Cycle life** depends on DoD — shallower cycles (e.g., 80 % DoD) extend life significantly.
- [x] **Li-ion NMC** = best energy density; **LFP** = best safety + cycle life; **Pb-acid** = cheapest, heaviest.
- [x] **Unit ladder:** 1 Wh = 3600 J → 1 kWh = 3.6 MJ → 1 MWh = 3.6 GJ → 1 GWh = 3.6 TJ.
- [x] **Cell → Module → Pack** hierarchy, managed by **BMS** and **TMS**.
- [x] **Solid-state batteries** replace liquid electrolyte → higher energy, safer, but expensive and emerging.
- [x] **Flow batteries** decouple **power** (stack) from **energy** (tank) — ideal for grid-scale.
- [x] **A 75 kWh EV pack stores 270 MJ** — equivalent to a 1500 kg car theoretically reaching 600 m/s (energy is dissipated as heat, drag, and rolling resistance, not all converted to motion).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capacity Rate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET742 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES AND RENEWABLE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Capacity Rate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of electric vehicles (EVs) and renewable energy storage systems, **Capacity Rate (commonly abbreviated as C-rate)** is a standardized, dimensionless metric that quantifies the rate at which a battery (or any electrochemical energy storage device) is charged or discharged **relative to its nominal maximum capacity**.

$$C\text{-rate} = \frac{\text{Applied Current (A)}}{\text{Nominal Capacity (Ah)}}$$

Equivalently, in power-domain form, the capacity rate is also expressed as the ratio of applied power to the nominal energy capacity:

$$C\text{-rate} = \frac{\text{Applied Power (W)}}{\text{Nominal Energy Capacity (Wh)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Examiner Standard):**
> The *Capacity Rate* is defined as the constant current (or power) at which a fully charged battery is completely discharged in a specified number of hours. A battery discharged at **1C** must deliver its full rated capacity in exactly **1 hour**, whereas a discharge at **0.5C** delivers the same energy in **2 hours**.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — "The Water Tank Model"

Imagine a water tank with a capacity of **100 litres**.

- A **1C** discharge is like opening a tap that drains the **entire 100 litres in exactly 1 hour** (i.e., 100 L/h flow rate).
- A **2C** discharge is a tap draining the **full 100 litres in 30 minutes** (i.e., 200 L/h flow rate) — a much more aggressive flow.
- A **0.2C** discharge is a slow trickle that takes **5 hours** to empty (i.e., 20 L/h flow rate).

In a battery, the "water" is electric charge (Ampere-hours), and the "tap flow" is the current (Amperes). The C-rate simply normalizes this flow to the size of the tank so we can compare batteries of different sizes on a common scale.

> [!NOTE] **Why It Matters in EVs:**
> A **Tata Nexon EV** with a **30.2 kWh** battery discharged at **1C** delivers **30.2 kW** of power continuously for 1 hour. At **3C**, it delivers **90.6 kW** — a typical peak power needed for highway acceleration. The C-rate is therefore the **bridge between energy (kWh) and power (kW)** in any EV powertrain design.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics and constants are used throughout this topic:

- **1C** = full capacity discharged in **1 hour** (e.g., 1C on a 50 Ah battery = **50 A**).
- **C/2 (0.5C)** = full capacity discharged in **2 hours**.
- **2C** = full capacity discharged in **30 minutes** (fast-discharge / high-power application).
- **10C or higher** = typical for **supercapacitors** and power-dense cells used in regenerative braking buffers.
- **C/20 (0.05C)** = typical low-rate benchmark used by manufacturers to specify nominal **Ah** ratings.
- **Time-to-full (or empty)**: $t = \dfrac{1}{C\text{-rate}}$ (in hours).

> [!TIP] **Examiner's Tip:** Whenever a question specifies "C/3" or "0.2C", convert to a fraction: **0.2C = 0.2 × Capacity**. Never assume C = 1; always **multiply the C-rate by the capacity rating**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conceptual Breakdown — The "Why" and "How"

The capacity rate is not just a scaling factor — it physically determines the internal electrochemistry, thermal response, and voltage profile of a battery. Here is the operational logic:

- **At low C-rates (≤ 0.5C):** The ions have ample time to diffuse into the electrode pores. The battery delivers close to its **rated (nameplate) capacity** with minimal voltage sag and low heat generation.
- **At high C-rates (≥ 1C):** The ionic transport becomes rate-limited by **solid-state diffusion** and **electrolyte conductivity**. Only the surface-near active material reacts, so the *usable* capacity drops (this is called **rate-capacity effect**).
- **Thermal coupling:** Heat generated $\propto I^{2}R_{int}$, so doubling the C-rate quadruples the ohmic heat. This is why **fast-charging at 2C–4C** requires sophisticated liquid-cooling systems in modern EVs.
- **Voltage polarization:** The terminal voltage drops further under high C-rates due to **ohmic + activation + concentration overpotentials** ($\eta = IR + \eta_{act} + \eta_{conc}$).

## 2.2 Operational Steps in a C-Rate Calculation

1. **Identify the rated capacity** of the battery (in **Ah** for current-domain, or **Wh** for power-domain).
2. **Identify the C-rate** as specified in the problem (e.g., 0.5C, 2C).
3. **Compute the current** $I = (\text{C-rate}) \times C_{rated}$.
4. **Compute the discharge (or charge) time** $t = \dfrac{1}{\text{C-rate}}$ hours.
5. **Check for capacity derating** at high rates using Peukert's law (lead-acid) or empirical look-up tables (Li-ion).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C\text{-rate} = \dfrac{I}{C_{Ah}}$ | Current-domain C-rate definition | $I$ in A, $C_{Ah}$ in Ah; **dimensionless** |
| 2 | $C\text{-rate} = \dfrac{P}{E_{Wh}}$ | Power-domain C-rate definition | $P$ in W, $E_{Wh}$ in Wh; **dimensionless** |
| 3 | $I = C_{Ah} \times (\text{C-rate})$ | Discharge / charge current | Amperes (A) |
| 4 | $t = \dfrac{1}{\text{C-rate}}$ | Time to fully charge or discharge | Hours (h) |
| 5 | $P = E_{Wh} \times (\text{C-rate})$ | Instantaneous power at given C-rate | Watts (W) |
| 6 | $Q_{delivered} = I \times t$ | Charge delivered (check) | Ampere-hours (Ah) |
| 7 | $E_{delivered} = V_{avg} \times I \times t$ | Energy delivered | Watt-hours (Wh) |
| 8 | $Q_{actual} = C_{Ah} \cdot t^{1-n}$ | Peukert's capacity (lead-acid reference) | $n \approx 1.1$–$1.3$ |
| 9 | $P_{heat} = I^{2} \cdot R_{int}$ | Internal ohmic heat dissipation | Watts (W) |
| 10 | $E_{usable}(\text{C}) = \dfrac{C_{Ah}}{1 + k(\text{C-rate} - 1)}$ | Empirical Li-ion usable capacity | $k \approx 0.05$–$0.1$ (chemistry-specific) |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Boundary Conditions for Board Exams:**
> - If the problem says "rated at C/20" then the rated capacity is the energy delivered in **20 hours**.
> - If a battery is rated **"100 Ah at the 1-hour rate"**, it means 1C = 100 A; at 0.5C (2-hour rate) it may deliver slightly more (e.g., 105 Ah) due to rate-capacity recovery.
> - For Li-ion EV packs, the **continuous C-rate is typically 1C–2C**, and the **peak C-rate (for ~10 s bursts) is 5C–10C**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application | Typical C-Rate |
|---|---|---|
| EV Fast Charging (DC) | 0% → 80% in 30 min | **1.6C – 3C** |
| EV Highway Cruising | Continuous load | **0.3C – 0.5C** |
| EV Hard Acceleration | Peak power (10 s) | **3C – 8C** |
| Regenerative Braking | Charge acceptance | **1C – 5C** (short bursts) |
| Grid Storage (Solar) | 4-hour discharge cycle | **0.25C** |
| Hybrid Supercapacitor Buffer | Peak assist | **10C – 50C** |
| Mobile / Laptop Battery | Slow overnight charge | **0.2C – 0.5C** |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Fundamental Relationship Between C-Rate and Discharge Time

**Statement:** For a battery of nominal capacity $C_{Ah}$ Ampere-hours, discharged at a constant current $I$ Amperes, the relationship between C-rate and discharge time $t$ is given by:

$$C\text{-rate} = \frac{1}{t}$$

**Proof (from first principles):**

**Step 1:** Start with the definition of C-rate in current-domain form:

$$
C\text{-rate} = \frac{I}{C_{Ah}}
$$

**Step 2:** The total charge delivered by a constant current $I$ flowing for time $t$ hours is, by the elementary definition of current:

$$
Q = I \cdot t
$$

**Step 3:** For a **full discharge**, the charge delivered $Q$ must equal the nominal capacity of the battery:

$$
I \cdot t = C_{Ah}
$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange to isolate the current:

$$
I = \frac{C_{Ah}}{t}
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute Step 4 into the C-rate definition from Step 1:

$$
C\text{-rate} = \frac{\left(\dfrac{C_{Ah}}{t}\right)}{C_{Ah}}
$$

**Step 6:** Cancel the common term $C_{Ah}$ in the numerator and denominator:

$$
C\text{-rate} = \frac{1}{t}
$$

**Step 7:** Hence, the **discharge time in hours** is the **reciprocal of the C-rate**:

$$
t = \frac{1}{C\text{-rate}} \quad \text{(in hours)}
$$

> **Conversion note:** To obtain time in **minutes**, multiply by 60:
> $t_{min} = \dfrac{60}{C\text{-rate}}$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Power-Domain Equivalence of C-Rate

**Statement:** A battery of nominal energy $E_{Wh}$ Watt-hours, when discharged at C-rate $C_{r}$, delivers an instantaneous power $P = E_{Wh} \cdot C_{r}$ Watts.

**Proof:**

**Step 1:** Recall the power-domain C-rate definition:

$$
C_{r} = \frac{P}{E_{Wh}}
$$

**Step 2:** Multiply both sides by $E_{Wh}$:

$$
P = C_{r} \cdot E_{Wh}
$$

**Step 3:** Recall the energy delivered by a constant-power discharge for time $t$ is:

$$
E = P \cdot t
$$

**Step 4:** Substituting Step 2 into Step 3:

$$
E = (C_{r} \cdot E_{Wh}) \cdot t
$$

**Step 5:** But for a full discharge, the energy delivered $E$ must equal the rated energy $E_{Wh}$:

$$
E_{Wh} = C_{r} \cdot E_{Wh} \cdot t
$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $t$:

$$
t = \frac{1}{C_{r}} \quad \text{(in hours)}
$$

This is **identical to the current-domain result**, confirming the self-consistency of the C-rate concept in both power and current domains.

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (Board-Style)

**Problem:** An EV battery pack has a rated capacity of **60 kWh** and a nominal voltage of **400 V**. The vehicle's motor controller demands a peak power of **180 kW** during acceleration. Compute:

(a) The C-rate during peak acceleration.
(b) The corresponding discharge current drawn from the pack.
(c) The maximum duration for which this peak power can be sustained if the SOC must remain ≥ 20%.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — C-rate at peak power:**

$$
C_{r} = \frac{P}{E_{Wh}} = \frac{180{,}000 \text{ W}}{60{,}000 \text{ Wh}} = 3.0
$$

So the C-rate is **3C**.

**Step 2 — Discharge current at 400 V:**

$$
I = \frac{P}{V} = \frac{180{,}000}{400} = 450 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 3 — Usable energy above 20% SOC:**

$$
E_{usable} = 0.80 \times 60{,}000 = 48{,}000 \text{ Wh} = 48 \text{ kWh}
$$

**Step 4 — Maximum peak-power duration:**

$$
t = \frac{E_{usable}}{P} = \frac{48{,}000}{180{,}000} = 0.2667 \text{ h} = 16.0 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Step 5 — Cross-check using C-rate:**

$$
t = \frac{1}{C_{r}} \times \text{(SOC window)} = \frac{1}{3} \times 0.80 = 0.2667 \text{ h} \quad \checkmark
$$

> [!NOTE] **Examination Insight:** Always carry **units through every step**. Board examiners award partial marks for unit consistency even if the final number is mis-computed. Also note that peak C-rates (3C–5C) are typically only **sustainable for 10–30 seconds**; sustained 3C discharge would overheat the pack.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: Selecting the Right C-Rate for EV Design

| Design Criterion | Recommended Continuous C-Rate | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Urban commuter EV (range-priority) | **0.3C – 0.5C** | Maximizes energy throughput per cycle; minimizes degradation. |
| Performance EV (Tesla Model S Plaid class) | **1C – 2C** continuous, **4C peak** | High power for 0–100 km/h sub-2 s launches. |
| Heavy-duty EV bus / truck | **0.5C – 1C** | Steady torque, large packs; charging at 1C–2C. |
| DC Fast Charging (350 kW class) | **2C – 4C** (charging side) | Requires liquid cooling, advanced BMS, and graphite-Si anodes. |
| Plug-in Hybrid (PHEV) buffer battery | **5C – 10C** | Small pack (~10 kWh), high assist power. |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — C-Rate in an EV Powertrain

The following diagram maps how a single C-rate specification propagates through the entire electric vehicle power flow, from the battery cell to the road wheels.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Battery Pack rated E_Wh kWh] --> B[BMS Computes C-rate from P demand]
    B --> C{C-rate classification}
    C -->|C le 0 point 5| D[Low rate discharge]
    C -->|C eq 1| E[Nominal continuous discharge]
    C -->|C eq 2 to 4| F[High rate discharge peak power]
    C -->|C gt 4| G[Pulsed peak 10 s maximum]
    D --> D1[Full usable capacity minimal heat]
    E --> E1[5 to 10 percent capacity derating moderate heat]
    F --> F1[Liquid cooling required 15 to 25 percent heat]
    G --> G1[Reserved for supercap buffer or 10 s boost]
    D1 --> H[Power to Inverter]
    E1 --> H
    F1 --> H
    G1 --> H
    H --> I[DC to AC Inverter]
    I --> J[Three Phase Traction Motor]
    J --> K[Reduction Gearbox]
    K --> L[Road Wheels]
    H --> M[Onboard Charger AC to DC]
    M --> N[Regenerative Braking Energy]
    N --> A
```

**How to read this diagram:**
- The BMS dynamically classifies the **instantaneous C-rate** demanded by the driver.
- Each branch indicates the **thermal and capacity consequence** of operating at that rate.
- A **closed loop** is shown between the wheels and the battery (regenerative braking) where the C-rate becomes *negative* (i.e., charging).

## 4.2 State-Transition Schematic — C-Rate vs. Time During a Realistic EV Drive Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Ignition On SOC 90 percent] --> B[Idle 0C]
    B --> C[Low speed urban 0.2C]
    C --> D[Highway cruise 0.5C]
    D --> E[Overtake acceleration 3C peak 15 s]
    E --> F[Highway cruise 0.5C]
    F --> G[Brake lift off 0C]
    G --> H[Regenerative brake 1.5C charge 8 s]
    H --> I[Traffic stop 0C]
    I --> J[Destination SOC 65 percent]
    J --> K[DC fast charge 2C]
    K --> L[SOC 80 percent in 7.5 min]
```

**Pedagogical Note:** This sequence illustrates that the **C-rate is a time-varying quantity** in real driving — it is not a single number stamped on the battery. The BMS samples the C-rate hundreds of times per second and enforces thermal and voltage constraints accordingly.

## 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram — Effect of C-Rate on Battery Output

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Same Battery 50 Ah Cell] --> B1[Discharge at 0.2C eq 10 A]
    A --> B2[Discharge at 1C eq 50 A]
    A --> B3[Discharge at 3C eq 150 A]
    B1 --> C1[Delivered capacity 51 Ah terminal voltage flat]
    B2 --> C2[Delivered capacity 48 Ah moderate voltage sag]
    B3 --> C3[Delivered capacity 42 Ah steep voltage sag]
    B1 --> D1[Cell temperature 28 C]
    B2 --> D2[Cell temperature 38 C]
    B3 --> D3[Cell temperature 58 C thermal limit]
    C1 --> E1[Efficiency 99 percent]
    C2 --> E2[Efficiency 94 percent]
    C3 --> E3[Efficiency 84 percent]
```

> [!TIP] **Memory Hook for Exams:** As C-rate **increases by 3×**, the delivered capacity drops by **~15%** and the cell temperature rises by **~20°C** for a typical Li-ion NMC cell. This triple penalty (capacity, voltage, temperature) is the fundamental reason EV designers prefer **larger packs operated at lower C-rates**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Capacity Rate (C-rate) of a battery. Why is it an important parameter in the design of an Electric Vehicle battery pack?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Capacity Rate (C-rate)** is defined as the ratio of the discharge (or charge) current of a battery to its nominal rated capacity, expressed as:

$$
C\text{-rate} = \frac{I}{C_{rated}} \quad \text{(dimensionless)}
$$

Alternatively, in power-domain: $C\text{-rate} = P / E_{rated}$.

A **1C discharge** completely empties the battery in 1 hour; **0.5C** in 2 hours; **2C** in 30 minutes.

**Importance in EV design:**
- It **translates energy storage (kWh) into deliverable power (kW)**, determining the motor's peak power capability.
- It governs **thermal management** requirements (heat $\propto I^{2}R$).
- It sets the **fast-charging acceptance** of the pack.
- It influences **cycle life** — sustained high C-rates accelerate degradation.
- It affects the **usable capacity** (rate-capacity effect).

**(3 marks — definition 1M, importance 2M)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**A 60 Ah battery is rated at the 20-hour rate. Calculate: (i) the rated discharge current, (ii) the current at 1C and 2C, and (iii) the time taken for a 0.5C discharge.**

**Model Answer:**

The 20-hour rate corresponds to **C/20 = 0.05C**.

**(i) Rated discharge current:**
$$
I_{rated} = \frac{C_{Ah}}{20} = \frac{60}{20} = 3 \text{ A}
$$

**(ii) Currents at 1C and 2C:**
$$
I_{1C} = 1 \times 60 = 60 \text{ A}
$$
$$
I_{2C} = 2 \times 60 = 120 \text{ A}
$$

**(iii) Time for 0.5C discharge:**
$$
t = \frac{1}{0.5} = 2 \text{ hours} = 120 \text{ minutes}
$$

**(3 marks — three parts 1M each)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Capacity Rate Problems:**
> - **Common error #1:** Confusing the **20-hour rate (C/20)** with **1C** when the problem only gives "rated capacity in Ah". The 20-hour rate is **0.05C**, not 1C.
> - **Common error #2:** Forgetting to apply the **usable SOC window** (typically 20%–80% or 10%–90%) when computing real EV range.
> - **Common error #3:** Mixing up **C (capacity)** and **C-rate**. They are different symbols.
> - **Common error #4:** Reporting discharge time in hours but writing "minutes" (or vice versa). Always convert: $t_{min} = 60 / C_{r}$.
> - **Common error #5:** Ignoring the **Peukert derating** for lead-acid batteries — at high C-rates the actual delivered capacity is **less** than the nameplate.

---

### Part B — Choice A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]`

**(a)** A 200 Ah lead-acid battery is rated at the 20-hour rate.
- (i) Determine the C-rate corresponding to its rated discharge.
- (ii) If the same battery is discharged at 0.5C, 1C, and 2C respectively, compute the discharge current and the theoretical discharge time in each case.
- (iii) Using Peukert's law with exponent $n = 1.25$, compute the actual delivered capacity when discharged at 2C. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain how the **C-rate affects the terminal voltage profile and the deliverable capacity** of a Li-ion cell during constant-current discharge. Discuss why EV traction batteries are rarely operated continuously above **2C** even though their peak rating may be **5C–8C**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Choice A(a)

**Step 1 — Identify the rated C-rate:**
The 20-hour rate implies $C/20 = 0.05C$. So the **rated C-rate is 0.05C**.

**Step 2 — Currents at 0.5C, 1C, 2C:**

$$
I_{0.5C} = 0.5 \times 200 = 100 \text{ A}
$$
$$
I_{1C} = 1 \times 200 = 200 \text{ A}
$$
$$
I_{2C} = 2 \times 200 = 400 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical discharge times:**

$$
t_{0.5C} = \frac{1}{0.5} = 2 \text{ h} = 120 \text{ min}
$$
$$
t_{1C} = \frac{1}{1} = 1 \text{ h} = 60 \text{ min}
$$
$$
t_{2C} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5 \text{ h} = 30 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Peukert's law:**

Peukert's equation: $Q_{actual} = I^{1-n} \cdot C_{rated}^{n}$ (with the C-rate already embedded). Using the standard form:

$$
Q_{actual} = C_{Ah} \cdot t^{1-n} \quad \text{with} \quad t = 1/C_{r}
$$

$$
Q_{actual} = 200 \cdot (0.5)^{1 - 1.25} = 200 \cdot (0.5)^{-0.25}
$$

$$
Q_{actual} = 200 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{0.5}\right)^{0.25} = 200 \cdot (2)^{0.25}
$$

$$
Q_{actual} = 200 \cdot 1.1892 = 237.84 \text{ Ah at the 20-hour rate equivalent}
$$

> **Comment:** This is the *effective* Peukert-corrected capacity. The **key point** is that at high C-rates, although the *current* is doubled, the **actual usable Ampere-hours** drops because the discharge must be terminated earlier (terminal voltage falls below cutoff faster). The available energy is therefore less than the nameplate rating.

---

#### Model Solution — Choice A(b)

**Sketch (describe verbally for the answer sheet):**

> Plot **terminal voltage $V_t$ (y-axis)** versus **depth of discharge DoD (x-axis)** for three constant-current discharge rates: **0.5C, 1C, 2C**. All three curves start at the same open-circuit voltage (e.g., 4.2 V for Li-ion NMC), but the **2C curve sags earliest and steepest**, the **1C curve is intermediate**, and the **0.5C curve is the flattest**, delivering energy closest to the rated capacity before reaching the cutoff voltage (typically 3.0 V for NMC).

**Observations:**
- The **0.5C curve** delivers nearly the full rated capacity (~100%) before cutoff.
- The **1C curve** delivers ~92–95% of rated capacity.
- The **2C curve** delivers only ~80–85% of rated capacity.

**Why EVs avoid continuous operation > 2C:**

1. **Thermal runaway risk:** Heat $\propto I^{2}R$, so 3C operation generates 9× the heat of 1C.
2. **Accelerated degradation:** High C-rates cause lithium plating at the anode and SEI growth, reducing cycle life.
3. **Voltage sag:** Motor controller sees lower bus voltage, limiting torque.
4. **Capacity loss:** Usable energy per discharge cycle drops by 15–25%.
5. **Peak (5C–8C) is reserved for short bursts** (acceleration, regen) where thermal mass and time constant absorb the heat.

---

### Part B — Choice B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]`

**(a)** A battery pack for a three-wheeler EV has a nominal energy of **8 kWh** and a nominal voltage of **96 V**.
- (i) Compute the ampere-hour capacity of the pack.
- (ii) If the pack is fast-charged at **1.2C**, calculate the charging current, charging power, and the time required to charge from 20% to 100% SOC.
- (iii) If the motor draws a peak current of **200 A**, compute the peak C-rate of discharge. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of C-rate in three distinct EV subsystems — **(i) traction drive / acceleration, (ii) regenerative braking energy recovery, and (iii) DC fast charging** — with quantitative examples for each. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Choice B(a)

**Step 1 — Compute Ah capacity:**

$$
C_{Ah} = \frac{E_{Wh}}{V_{nom}} = \frac{8000}{96} = 83.33 \text{ Ah}
$$

**Step 2 — Charging at 1.2C:**

$$
I_{charge} = 1.2 \times 83.33 = 100 \text{ A}
$$

$$
P_{charge} = V_{nom} \times I_{charge} = 96 \times 100 = 9600 \text{ W} = 9.6 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Time to charge from 20% → 100%:**

SOC window $\Delta SOC = 0.80$. Energy to add:

$$
\Delta E = 0.80 \times 8 = 6.4 \text{ kWh}
$$

$$
t = \frac{\Delta E}{P_{charge}} = \frac{6.4 \text{ kWh}}{9.6 \text{ kW}} = 0.6667 \text{ h} = 40 \text{ min}
$$

**Cross-check using C-rate:** $t = \Delta SOC / C_{r} = 0.80 / 1.2 = 0.6667$ h ✓

**Step 4 — Peak discharge C-rate at 200 A:**

$$
C_{r,peak} = \frac{I_{peak}}{C_{Ah}} = \frac{200}{83.33} = 2.4
$$

So the peak C-rate is **2.4C** — within typical Li-ion capability for short bursts.

---

#### Model Solution — Choice B(b)

**(i) Traction / Acceleration:**
During hard acceleration, the inverter demands peak power. For example, a **40 kWh** pack at **3C** delivers **120 kW** peak — sufficient for a 0–100 km/h sprint under 6 seconds. Peak C-rate is typically **3C–5C** for short durations (10–20 s).

**(ii) Regenerative Braking:**
When the driver lifts off, the motor acts as a generator. The acceptance C-rate of a Li-ion pack is typically **1C–5C** for brief windows. For instance, a 200 Ah pack accepting 2C (400 A) regen for 5 seconds recovers:

$$
\Delta Q = 400 \times \dfrac{5}{3600} = 0.556 \text{ Ah}
$$

This is small per event, but cumulative over urban driving it can extend range by **10–20%**.

**(iii) DC Fast Charging:**
Modern 150 kW chargers force **2C–4C** into the pack. A **50 kWh** pack charged at **3C** accepts **150 kW** — allowing 10% → 80% in roughly **17 minutes**. The BMS must throttle the C-rate above 80% SOC to prevent lithium plating; this is the **tapered charge profile** visible on every public DC fast charger.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** $C\text{-rate} = I / C_{Ah} = P / E_{Wh}$ (dimensionless).
- **Time reciprocal:** $t = 1 / C_{r}$ (hours); $t_{min} = 60 / C_{r}$.
- **Power shortcut:** $P = E_{Wh} \cdot C_{r}$ — multiply rated energy by the C-rate to get instantaneous power.
- **Current shortcut:** $I = C_{Ah} \cdot C_{r}$.
- **High C-rate penalties (the 3-fold penalty):** Reduced capacity, lower terminal voltage, higher cell temperature.
- **Heat law:** $P_{heat} \propto I^{2}$, so doubling C-rate quadruples heat.
- **Rated 20-hour rate = 0.05C** — used for nameplate Ah.
- **Typical EV traction limits:** Continuous ≤ 2C; peak ≤ 5C (10 s).
- **Fast-charging thresholds:** 1C–2C (slow DC), 2C–4C (fast DC), 4C+ (extreme; 350 kW class).
- **Peukert's law** applies to lead-acid (and weakly to Li-ion) — capacity falls at high C-rates.
- **BMS role:** Dynamically enforces C-rate limits based on SOC, temperature, and SOH.
- **Regenerative braking C-rate:** 1C–5C, sustained for seconds only.
- **Examiner trap:** Never confuse **C (capacity in Ah)** with **C-rate** — they are different physical quantities sharing the same letter.
- **Conversion discipline:** Always state units; 1 hour = 60 min = 3600 s.
- **SOC window:** For real EVs, only the **20%–80%** SOC band is used for daily operation; full 0%–100% cycling accelerates degradation.

> **Final Note:** The capacity rate is the **single most important dimensionless number** in any EV or renewable energy storage design. Master its definition, its reciprocal relationship with time, and the three penalties (capacity, voltage, heat) — and you can answer every C-rate question KTU will ever ask.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cell Voltage - Specific Energy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET742 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES AND RENEWABLE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cell Voltage - Specific Energy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Cell Voltage — Formal Definition

In the context of rechargeable electrochemical storage systems used in Electric Vehicles (EVs), **Cell Voltage** is defined as the electric potential difference established between the positive (cathode) and negative (anode) terminals of a single galvanic (voltaic) cell. This voltage is a direct thermodynamic manifestation of the difference in electrochemical potential of the two electrode materials and the electrolyte, governed fundamentally by the **Gibbs Free Energy** of the cell reaction.

For KTU 2024 Scheme examination purposes, Cell Voltage is **NOT** treated as a single scalar value; instead, it must be classified into four operationally distinct voltages:

| Voltage Type | Definition | Typical Context |
|---|---|---|
| **Open Circuit Voltage (OCV / $E_{oc}$)** | Terminal voltage when the cell is at rest (no current flow, $I = 0$) | Thermodynamic equilibrium value |
| **Nominal Voltage ($V_{nom}$)** | The standardized reference voltage assigned by the manufacturer | Used for system design, datasheets |
| **Working / Operating Voltage ($V_{work}$)** | Actual terminal voltage during charge or discharge ($I \neq 0$) | Real-time EV operation |
| **Cut-off Voltage ($V_{cut-off}$)** | Predefined minimum (discharge) or maximum (charge) allowable voltage | End-of-charge/discharge protection threshold |

### 1.2 Specific Energy — Formal Definition

**Specific Energy** is defined as the amount of electrical energy stored per unit mass (gravimetric) or per unit volume (volumetric) of a battery cell or pack. It is the single most important metric that determines the **driving range** of an Electric Vehicle.

- **Gravimetric Specific Energy** = Energy Stored / Mass → Units: **Wh/kg**
- **Volumetric Specific Energy** = Energy Stored / Volume → Units: **Wh/L**

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Water Tank" Analogy**
>
> Imagine a water tank on the roof of your house:
>
> - **Cell Voltage (V)** is like the **height of the water tank above the tap**. The higher the tank, the more pressure (force) with which water gushes out.
> - **Capacity (Ah)** is like the **width/diameter of the tank**. The wider it is, the more water it can hold.
> - **Specific Energy (Wh/kg)** is like the **total amount of useful water you can deliver per kg of tank material**. A taller AND wider tank gives more water per kg — just as a higher voltage AND larger capacity battery gives more energy per kg.
>
> Hence, **Energy = Voltage × Capacity**, and **Specific Energy = Energy ÷ Mass of the battery**.

### 1.4 Why These Two Parameters Are Coupled

The KTU 2024 syllabus groups **Cell Voltage** and **Specific Energy** together because they are mathematically and physically interlocked. A higher cell voltage directly increases the specific energy because energy is the product of voltage and charge:

$$E_{cell} = V_{cell} \times Q_{cell}$$

Where $Q_{cell}$ is the cell capacity in Coulomb-equivalent (Ampere-hours). Therefore, doubling the cell voltage while keeping the mass constant **doubles** the specific energy, making chemistry selection (which determines voltage) the primary lever for improving EV range.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The module expects students to derive the link between cell voltage, equivalent weight, Faraday's constant, and specific energy. Memorize the standard **Faraday Constant: F = 96,485 C/mol ≈ 26,801 mAh/mol**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Origin of Cell Voltage — The Thermodynamic Basis

Cell voltage is fundamentally determined by the **Nernst Equation**, which relates the cell's open-circuit voltage to the standard electrode potentials and the activities (concentrations) of reactants and products.

$$E_{cell} = E^{\circ}_{cell} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q_{r}$$

Where:
- $E^{\circ}_{cell}$ = Standard Cell Potential (V)
- $R$ = Universal Gas Constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = Absolute Temperature (K)
- $n$ = Number of electrons transferred per mole of reaction
- $F$ = Faraday's Constant = **96,485 C/mol**
- $Q_{r}$ = Reaction Quotient

At standard conditions (298 K, 1 M, 1 atm), the second term vanishes and $E_{cell} = E^{\circ}_{cell}$.

### 2.2 Working Voltage — The Effect of Internal Resistance

During real operation, current flows through the electrolyte and electrodes, producing an **IR drop** across the cell's internal resistance $r_{int}$. Therefore:

**During Discharge:**
$$V_{work} = E_{oc} - I \cdot r_{int}$$

**During Charge:**
$$V_{work} = E_{oc} + I \cdot r_{int}$$

This is why a battery's working voltage is always **lower than OCV during discharge** and **higher than OCV during charge**.

### 2.3 Specific Energy — The Two Definitions

#### 2.3.1 Gravimetric Specific Energy (Engineering Definition)
$$E_{sp,\,grav} = \frac{V_{avg} \times Q_{Ah}}{\text{Mass of cell (kg)}} \quad \left[\frac{\text{Wh}}{\text{kg}}\right]$$

#### 2.3.2 Theoretical (Electrochemical) Specific Energy
This is derived purely from the **thermodynamic free energy** and the **equivalent weight** of the active materials:

$$E_{sp,\,theo} = \frac{n \cdot F \cdot E^{\circ}_{cell}}{\sum M_i \cdot 3600} \quad \left[\frac{\text{Wh}}{\text{kg}}\right]$$

Where:
- $n$ = electrons transferred per mole
- $F$ = Faraday's constant (C/mol)
- $E^{\circ}_{cell}$ = Standard cell potential (V)
- $\sum M_i$ = sum of molecular weights of reactants per mole of reaction (kg/mol)
- $3600$ = conversion factor from Joules to Watt-hours

### 2.4 Energy Density (Volumetric Specific Energy)

$$E_{den} = \frac{V_{avg} \times Q_{Ah}}{\text{Volume of cell (L)}} \quad \left[\frac{\text{Wh}}{\text{L}}\right]$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{cell} = E^{\circ}_{cell} - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln Q_r$ | Nernst Equation | V |
| 2 | $E^{\circ}_{cell} = E^{\circ}_{cathode} - E^{\circ}_{anode}$ | Standard Cell Potential from half-cells | V |
| 3 | $V_{work} = E_{oc} - I r_{int}$ | Discharge working voltage | V |
| 4 | $V_{work} = E_{oc} + I r_{int}$ | Charge working voltage | V |
| 5 | $E = V \times Q$ | Total electrical energy stored | Wh |
| 6 | $E_{sp} = \dfrac{V_{avg} \times Q_{Ah}}{m_{cell}}$ | Gravimetric Specific Energy | Wh/kg |
| 7 | $E_{sp,theo} = \dfrac{n F E^{\circ}_{cell}}{3600 \sum M_i}$ | Theoretical Specific Energy | Wh/kg |
| 8 | $E_{den} = \dfrac{V_{avg} \times Q_{Ah}}{V_{cell}}$ | Volumetric Energy Density | Wh/L |
| 9 | $Q = \dfrac{n F}{3600}$ | Theoretical capacity per mole | Ah/mol |
| 10 | $I \cdot t = \dfrac{m \cdot n \cdot F}{M \cdot 3600}$ | Charge passed in electrolysis | Ah |

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in EVs

- **Range Estimation:** A Tesla Model 3 LR uses NCA (Li-ion) cells with ~250 Wh/kg. Range ≈ 560 km.
- **Battery Sizing:** If a vehicle needs 75 kWh pack and cells offer 250 Wh/kg → pack mass ≈ 300 kg.
- **Chemistry Selection:** Engineers compare chemistries on a "voltage vs. specific energy" plot to decide between LFP (low V, moderate Wh/kg) and NMC (higher V, high Wh/kg).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: From Faraday's Laws to Theoretical Specific Energy

**Goal:** Derive a direct expression for theoretical specific energy using only the cell's standard potential and the molar masses of reactants.

**Step 1 — Start from the Gibbs Free Energy**

The maximum electrical work obtainable from a cell reaction is equal to the decrease in Gibbs Free Energy:

$$W_{max} = -\Delta G$$

**Step 2 — Relate $\Delta G$ to Cell Potential**

The fundamental electrochemical relation gives:

$$\Delta G = -n \cdot F \cdot E^{\circ}_{cell}$$

**Step 3 — Energy Per Mole of Reaction (in Joules)**

Substituting into Step 1:

$$W_{max} = n \cdot F \cdot E^{\circ}_{cell} \quad [\text{Joules per mole}]$$

**Step 4 — Convert Joules to Watt-hours**

Since $1 \text{ Wh} = 3600 \text{ J}$:

$$W_{max} = \frac{n \cdot F \cdot E^{\circ}_{cell}}{3600} \quad [\text{Wh per mole}]$$

**Step 5 — Divide by Mass Per Mole of Reaction ($\sum M_i$ in kg)**

To obtain **energy per unit mass**:

$$E_{sp,theo} = \frac{n \cdot F \cdot E^{\circ}_{cell}}{3600 \cdot \sum M_i} \quad \left[\frac{\text{Wh}}{\text{kg}}\right]$$

This is the **master equation** for theoretical specific energy.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Worked Numerical Example — Lithium-Manganese Dioxide Coin Cell

Consider the half-reactions:

$$\text{Anode (Oxidation):} \quad \text{Li} \rightarrow \text{Li}^+ + e^- \quad E^\circ = -3.04 \text{ V}$$

$$\text{Cathode (Reduction):} \quad \text{MnO}_2 + \text{Li}^+ + e^- \rightarrow \text{LiMnO}_2 \quad E^\circ = +0.99 \text{ V}$$

**Step 1 — Compute Standard Cell Potential**

$$E^{\circ}_{cell} = E^{\circ}_{cathode} - E^{\circ}_{anode} = 0.99 - (-3.04) = 4.03 \text{ V}$$

But practically, $E^\circ_{cell} \approx 3.0$ V (theoretical = 4.03 V, nominal = 3.0 V).

**Step 2 — Molar Masses of Reactants**

- Li: $M = 6.941 \text{ g/mol}$
- MnO$_2$: $M = 86.937 \text{ g/mol}$

$$\sum M_i = 6.941 + 86.937 = 93.878 \text{ g/mol} = 0.093878 \text{ kg/mol}$$

**Step 3 — Number of Electrons Transferred**

$$n = 1$$

**Step 4 — Plug into Master Equation**

$$E_{sp,theo} = \frac{n \cdot F \cdot E^{\circ}_{cell}}{3600 \cdot \sum M_i}$$

$$E_{sp,theo} = \frac{1 \times 96{,}485 \times 4.03}{3600 \times 0.093878}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate Numerator**

$$\text{Numerator} = 96{,}485 \times 4.03 = 388{,}834.55 \text{ C·V} = 388{,}834.55 \text{ J}$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate Denominator**

$$\text{Denominator} = 3600 \times 0.093878 = 337.96 \text{ kJ/kg} \cdot \text{?}$$

Re-checking units: $(3600 \text{ s}) \times (0.093878 \text{ kg/mol}) = 337.96 \text{ s·kg/mol}$

So:

$$E_{sp,theo} = \frac{388{,}834.55}{337.96} \approx 1150.6 \text{ Wh/kg}$$

**Step 7 — Result**

$$\boxed{E_{sp,theo,\,Li/MnO_2} \approx 1150 \text{ Wh/kg}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **practical** specific energy of a Li/MnO$_2$ cell is only ~250 Wh/kg — about 22% of theoretical. The rest is lost to inactive components: current collectors, electrolyte, separator, casing, and packaging.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Relating Nominal Voltage to Specific Energy of a Pack

Suppose an EV pack has:
- **Total Mass:** $M = 300$ kg
- **Nominal Voltage:** $V_{pack} = 400$ V
- **Capacity:** $Q_{pack} = 75$ Ah
- **Cell Voltage (Nominal):** $V_{cell} = 3.7$ V
- **Number of Cells in Series:** $N_s = V_{pack} / V_{cell} = 400 / 3.7 \approx 108$
- **Capacity per cell:** $Q_{cell} = Q_{pack} = 75$ Ah (series connection preserves capacity)

**Step 1 — Pack Energy**

$$E_{pack} = V_{pack} \times Q_{pack} = 400 \times 75 = 30{,}000 \text{ Wh} = 30 \text{ kWh}$$

(Note: This is **pack-level** energy, much lower than cell-level rated energy.)

**Step 2 — Pack Specific Energy**

$$E_{sp,pack} = \frac{E_{pack}}{M_{pack}} = \frac{30{,}000}{300} = 100 \text{ Wh/kg}$$

This shows that pack-level specific energy is **always lower** than cell-level due to the housing, BMS, and wiring overhead.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Common EV Chemistries

| Chemistry | Nominal Cell Voltage (V) | Theoretical Sp. Energy (Wh/kg) | Practical Sp. Energy (Wh/kg) | Energy Density (Wh/L) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Lead-Acid** | 2.0 | 171 | 30–50 | 60–110 |
| **NiMH** | 1.2 | 240 | 60–120 | 140–300 |
| **LFP (LiFePO$_4$)** | 3.2 | 410 | 90–160 | 200–350 |
| **NMC (Li-Ni-Mn-Co)** | 3.7 | 780 | 150–220 | 400–700 |
| **NCA (Li-Ni-Co-Al)** | 3.6 | 887 | 200–260 | 500–800 |
| **Li-S** | 2.1 | 2600 | 250–400 (lab) | 350–500 |
| **Solid-State Li (theoretical)** | 3.7+ | ~1000+ | 350–500 (projected) | 800–1000 |

### 3.5 Derivation 4: Energy Delivered During a Full Discharge Cycle

For a constant-current discharge from $V_{max}$ to $V_{cut-off}$:

$$E_{delivered} = I \int_{0}^{t_{end}} V(t) \, dt$$

Assuming an **approximately linear discharge curve** (a common KTU simplification):

$$V(t) \approx V_{max} - \left(\frac{V_{max} - V_{cut-off}}{Q_{Ah}}\right) \cdot I \cdot t$$

Integrating:

$$E_{delivered} = I \cdot \left[V_{max} \cdot t_{end} - \frac{(V_{max} - V_{cut-off}) \cdot I \cdot t_{end}^2}{2 \cdot Q_{Ah}}\right]$$

Using $I \cdot t_{end} = Q_{Ah}$:

$$E_{delivered} = V_{max} \cdot Q_{Ah} - \frac{(V_{max} - V_{cut-off}) \cdot Q_{Ah}}{2}$$

$$\boxed{E_{delivered} = Q_{Ah} \cdot \frac{V_{max} + V_{cut-off}}{2}}$$

This shows that for a linear discharge, the average working voltage is the **arithmetic mean** of $V_{max}$ and $V_{cut-off}$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Cell Voltage Classification — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    nodeA[Cell Voltage Vcell] --> nodeE[Cutoff Voltage Vcut]

    nodeB[Open Circuit Voltage Eoc] --> nodeF[I equals 0A, thermodynamic equilibrium]

    nodeD[Working Voltage Vwork] --> nodeK[Charge: Eoc plus I x rint]

```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between Open Circuit Voltage (OCV) and Nominal Voltage of a battery cell. Why are these two values different in practice?**

**Model Answer:**

**Open Circuit Voltage (OCV):** The terminal voltage of a cell measured when **no current flows** ($I = 0$ A). It represents the thermodynamic equilibrium potential determined by the Nernst equation and depends on temperature, state of charge, and electrolyte concentration.

**Nominal Voltage:** A **standardized reference value** assigned by the manufacturer, typically representing the average working voltage during a typical discharge cycle. It is an engineering simplification, not a thermodynamic quantity.

**Why the values differ:**
- OCV is the *maximum* voltage (e.g., 4.2 V for Li-ion at 100% SOC).
- Nominal voltage is the *average* working voltage during the discharge plateau (e.g., 3.6–3.7 V for Li-ion).
- Manufacturers round the nominal voltage to standard values (3.6 V, 3.7 V) for design convenience.

#### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Gravimetric Specific Energy and Volumetric Energy Density. State their SI units and explain their significance in EV design.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Gravimetric Specific Energy:** The amount of electrical energy stored per unit mass of the battery. Units: **Wh/kg**. Higher values mean a **lighter battery** for the same range, directly improving vehicle efficiency.

- **Volumetric Energy Density:** The amount of electrical energy stored per unit volume of the battery. Units: **Wh/L**. Higher values mean a **smaller pack volume**, allowing better packaging inside the vehicle chassis.

**Significance in EV Design:**
- Specific Energy dictates the **driving range per kg** — critical for long-range EVs.
- Energy Density dictates **pack footprint** — critical for city cars with limited under-floor space.
- A well-designed EV battery must optimize **both** parameters.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

#### Question B-A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**(a)** Derive an expression for the **theoretical specific energy** of an electrochemical cell in terms of standard cell potential, Faraday's constant, number of electrons transferred, and equivalent weight of the active materials. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A Li-ion cell uses the reaction: LiCoO$_2$ + C$_6$ $\rightleftharpoons$ LiC$_6$ + CoO$_2$. Given:
- Standard cell potential $E^\circ_{cell} = 3.7$ V
- Number of electrons transferred $n = 1$
- Equivalent weight of active materials = 0.1034 kg/mol
- Faraday's constant F = 96,485 C/mol

Calculate the **theoretical specific energy** and compare it with the **practical specific energy** of 180 Wh/kg. Comment on the percentage utilization of active material. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Gibbs Free Energy and Cell Potential:**

The maximum electrical work from a cell reaction equals the negative of the change in Gibbs free energy:

$$W_{max} = -\Delta G$$

The thermodynamic relationship between $\Delta G$ and cell potential is:

$$\Delta G = -n \cdot F \cdot E^{\circ}_{cell}$$

Substituting:

$$W_{max} = n \cdot F \cdot E^{\circ}_{cell} \quad [\text{J/mol}]$$

**Step 2 — Convert to Watt-hours:**

$$W_{max} = \frac{n \cdot F \cdot E^{\circ}_{cell}}{3600} \quad [\text{Wh/mol}]$$

**Step 3 — Divide by Mass per mole (equivalent weight in kg):**

$$\boxed{E_{sp,theo} = \frac{n \cdot F \cdot E^{\circ}_{cell}}{3600 \cdot M_{eq}} \quad [\text{Wh/kg}]}$$

**(b) Numerical Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Substitute values into master equation:**

$$E_{sp,theo} = \frac{1 \times 96{,}485 \times 3.7}{3600 \times 0.1034}$$

**Step 2 — Numerator:**

$$96{,}485 \times 3.7 = 356{,}994.5 \text{ J/mol}$$

**Step 3 — Denominator:**

$$3600 \times 0.1034 = 372.24$$

**Step 4 — Result:**

$$E_{sp,theo} = \frac{356{,}994.5}{372.24} \approx 958.9 \text{ Wh/kg}$$

**Step 5 — Comparison and Utilization:**

$$\text{Utilization \%} = \frac{E_{sp,practical}}{E_{sp,theoretical}} \times 100 = \frac{180}{958.9} \times 100 \approx 18.77\%$$

**Comment:** Only about **19%** of the theoretical energy is practically utilized. The remaining ~81% is lost due to:
- Inactive materials (binder, separator, current collectors)
- Packaging overhead
- Electrolyte and casing mass
- Voltage drop during discharge

---

#### Question B-B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**(a)** Explain the concept of **Cell Voltage** in an EV battery. With the help of a discharge characteristic curve, illustrate the difference between OCV, nominal voltage, working voltage, and cut-off voltage. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An EV uses a battery pack consisting of **100 cells in series**, each with:
- Nominal voltage = 3.7 V
- Capacity = 50 Ah
- Cell mass = 0.9 kg

Calculate:
1. The **pack nominal voltage** and **pack capacity**,
2. The **total pack energy** in kWh,
3. The **gravimetric specific energy** of the pack, and
4. The **range** of the vehicle if it consumes 150 Wh/km. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Conceptual Explanation (7 Marks):**

Cell Voltage is the potential difference between the cathode and anode of an electrochemical cell. It is classified into four key values based on the operating state:

- **OCV ($E_{oc}$):** Voltage at zero current — measured after long rest.
- **Nominal Voltage ($V_{nom}$):** Manufacturer-specified average voltage for design.
- **Working Voltage ($V_{work}$):** Voltage during actual operation; lower than OCV during discharge due to $IR$ drop.
- **Cut-off Voltage ($V_{cut-off}$):** Minimum voltage below which discharge must stop to prevent damage.

**Discharge Curve (Verbal Sketch):**

The discharge curve begins at OCV (~4.2 V for Li-ion at 100% SOC), drops slightly to the **nominal plateau** (~3.7 V), maintains this through most of the discharge, then falls through the **knee region** to reach the **cut-off voltage** (~2.7–3.0 V) at 100% DoD. The average voltage lies between $V_{max}$ and $V_{cut-off}$.

**(b) Numerical Calculation (7 Marks):**

**1. Pack Voltage and Capacity:**

Since cells are in **series**, voltages add; capacity remains the same:

$$V_{pack} = N_s \times V_{cell} = 100 \times 3.7 = 370 \text{ V}$$

$$Q_{pack} = Q_{cell} = 50 \text{ Ah}$$

**2. Total Pack Energy:**

$$E_{pack} = V_{pack} \times Q_{pack} = 370 \times 50 = 18{,}500 \text{ Wh} = 18.5 \text{ kWh}$$

**3. Gravimetric Specific Energy of Pack:**

Total pack mass:

$$M_{pack} = N_s \times m_{cell} = 100 \times 0.9 = 90 \text{ kg}$$

Specific energy:

$$E_{sp} = \frac{E_{pack}}{M_{pack}} = \frac{18{,}500}{90} \approx 205.6 \text{ Wh/kg}$$

**4. Driving Range:**

$$\text{Range} = \frac{E_{pack}}{\text{Energy Consumption}} = \frac{18{,}500 \text{ Wh}}{150 \text{ Wh/km}} \approx 123.3 \text{ km}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit Confusion in Specific Energy Formula:** Students often forget to convert Faraday's constant units (C/mol) into Wh/kg by including the **3600** divisor. Writing $E_{sp} = nFE^\circ/M$ without dividing by 3600 yields an answer in J/kg, which is **dimensionally incorrect** for Wh/kg.
>
> 2. **Mixing Series and Parallel Calculations:** In a pack, **series** adds voltage but keeps capacity constant; **parallel** adds capacity but keeps voltage constant. Mixing these up leads to wrong pack energy.
>
> 3. **Confusing OCV with Nominal Voltage:** OCV is the maximum (4.2 V for Li-ion); Nominal is the average (3.7 V). Examiners **deduct 1 mark** for using them interchangeably.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to Convert Equivalent Weight to kg:** Using g/mol instead of kg/mol inflates the specific energy by 1000× — a very common numerical error.
>
> 5. **Ignoring the IR Drop:** When asked about working voltage, students often write $V_{work} = E_{oc}$. The correct expression is $V_{work} = E_{oc} \pm I \cdot r_{int}$ depending on charge/discharge mode.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Cell Voltage is thermodynamic in origin**, governed by Gibbs free energy via $E^\circ_{cell} = -\Delta G / nF$.
- **Four voltage types** must be remembered: OCV, Nominal, Working, Cut-off.
- **Working Voltage** is reduced during discharge by the $IR$ drop: $V_{work} = E_{oc} - Ir_{int}$.
- **Nernst Equation** defines OCV under non-standard conditions.
- **Specific Energy (Wh/kg)** is calculated as $E_{sp} = V_{avg} \times Q_{Ah} / m_{cell}$.
- **Theoretical Specific Energy** uses the master equation: $E_{sp,theo} = nFE^\circ_{cell} / (3600 \cdot M_{eq})$.
- **Energy Density (Wh/L)** is the volumetric counterpart — critical for pack packaging.
- **Practical Specific Energy is ~20–30%** of theoretical for most Li-ion chemistries.
- **Pack voltage is additive in series**; **pack capacity is additive in parallel**.
- **Cell voltage directly determines specific energy** — higher cell potential = higher Wh/kg.
- **Faraday's Constant = 96,485 C/mol ≈ 26,801 mAh/mol** — memorize both forms.
- **Average Voltage = $(V_{max} + V_{cut-off})/2$** for linear discharge approximation.
- **LFP = 3.2 V nominal, ~160 Wh/kg practical** — safe, long-cycle, low-energy.
- **NMC = 3.7 V nominal, ~200 Wh/kg practical** — high energy, mainstream EV choice.
- **NCA = 3.6 V nominal, ~250 Wh/kg practical** — used by Tesla, highest gravimetric energy in production.
- **EV range equation:** $\text{Range (km)} = \frac{E_{pack} (\text{Wh})}{\text{Energy consumption (Wh/km)}}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cycle Life
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET742 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES AND RENEWABLE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cycle Life

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Cycle Life** is defined as the total number of complete **charge–discharge cycles** that a rechargeable energy storage device (predominantly a battery cell or pack) can sustain before its usable **capacity degrades to a predefined end-of-life threshold**, conventionally fixed at **80% of the original rated capacity** (sometimes 70% depending on the chemistry and OEM specification).

Mathematically, it is the integer count $N_{cycle}$ such that:

$$C(N_{cycle}) = 0.8 \cdot C_{rated}$$

where:
- $C(N_{cycle})$ = capacity of the battery after $N_{cycle}$ full equivalent cycles
- $C_{rated}$ = initial nameplate capacity of the battery

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition:** Cycle Life is NOT the calendar age of the battery. It is a *cyclic degradation metric* — a battery sitting idle has calendar aging, not cycle aging. KTU examiners will deduct marks if these are conflated.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Bottle" Intuition

Imagine a **squeezable water bottle** representing a battery cell:
- **Charging** = pouring water in (energy storage).
- **Discharging** = squeezing water out (energy delivery).
- **Cycle** = one full fill-and-squeeze operation.

Every squeeze permanently weakens the bottle's elasticity. After ~**500 to ~3000 squeezes** (depending on bottle material — which represents the battery chemistry), the bottle can no longer hold 80% of its original water. That final squeeze count is its **Cycle Life**.

| Battery "Material" | Analogy | Real-World Cycle Life |
|---|---|---|
| Lead-Acid (old rubber) | Stiff, cracks fast | ~300–500 cycles |
| NMC Lithium-Ion (silicone) | Flexible, durable | ~1000–2000 cycles |
| LFP (reinforced polymer) | Very tough, long-lasting | ~2000–5000 cycles |
| Solid-State (advanced alloy) | Near-elastic | ~5000+ cycles (projected) |

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics Introduced

- **Depth of Discharge (DoD)** — the percentage of battery capacity discharged relative to total capacity. *Partial cycles still consume cycle life*, but at a reduced rate.
- **State of Charge (SoC) Window** — operating between 20%–80% SoC dramatically extends cycle life.
- **C-Rate** — speed of charge/discharge; higher C-rates accelerate degradation.
- **End-of-Life (EoL) Criterion** — universally accepted as **80% capacity retention** for EV packs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric (KTU Highlight):** The industry-standard EoL threshold is **80%** of initial capacity (per ISO 12405-4 and IEC 62660-1). Some manufacturers (notably Tesla for LFP packs) use 70% as EoL.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Degradation Mechanism — "Why" Cycle Life is Finite

Cycle life degradation in lithium-ion cells occurs through **four primary parasitic mechanisms**, each consuming one full cycle's worth of damage:

### 2.1.1 Solid Electrolyte Interphase (SEI) Layer Growth
- During every charge cycle, a thin passivation layer (~**10–100 nm**) forms on the **anode (graphite) surface**.
- This layer consumes cyclable **lithium ions**, reducing capacity.
- **Why it matters:** Over thousands of cycles, this layer thickens, consuming active lithium and increasing internal resistance.

### 2.1.2 Lithium Plating
- At high charge rates (high **C-rate**) or low temperatures, metallic lithium deposits on the anode instead of intercalating.
- This lithium is **permanently lost** — it cannot be re-intercalated.

### 2.1.3 Cathode Cracking & Transition Metal Dissolution
- Repeated lattice expansion/contraction fractures cathode particles.
- Manganese or cobalt ions dissolve into the electrolyte, poisoning the anode.

### 2.1.4 Electrolyte Decomposition
- Side reactions at high voltages (>4.2 V) decompose organic carbonates in the electrolyte.
- Gas evolution (CO₂, H₂) causes **swelling** and impedance rise.

## 2.2 Factors Influencing Cycle Life (The "Big Five")

| # | Factor | Effect on Cycle Life | Engineering Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Depth of Discharge (DoD)** | Higher DoD → lower cycle life | Full 100% DoD = ~1× life; 50% DoD ≈ 3–4× life |
| 2 | **C-Rate** | Higher C-rate → accelerated degradation | Fast charging degrades 2–3× faster |
| 3 | **Temperature** | Elevated T → SEI growth; Cold T → plating | Optimal: 15–35 °C |
| 4 | **SoC Window** | High SoC (>80%) stress cathode | Buffer cycling extends life |
| 5 | **Charge Voltage Cut-off** | 4.2 V standard; 4.1 V ≈ 2× life | Energy density vs. longevity |

## 2.3 The Cycle Life vs. DoD Relationship (Peukert-Inspired Model)

The most common empirical model used in KTU problems:

$$N_{cycle}(DoD) = N_{100} \cdot \left(\frac{100}{DoD\,\%}\right)^{k}$$

where:
- $N_{100}$ = cycle life at 100% DoD
- $k$ = empirical exponent (typically **0.8 to 1.2** for Li-ion)
- $DoD\,\%$ = depth of discharge expressed as a percentage

> **Example:** If $N_{100} = 1000$ cycles and $k = 1.0$, then at 50% DoD the cell delivers $\approx 2000$ cycles.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Examination-Ready)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C(N) = C_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda N}$ | Capacity fade vs. cycles (exponential model) | Ah, cycles |
| 2 | $\lambda = \frac{\ln(C_0 / C_{EoL})}{N_{EoL}}$ | Decay constant | cycles⁻¹ |
| 3 | $N_{cycle}(DoD) = N_{100} \cdot (100/DoD)^{k}$ | Cycle life vs. DoD | cycles |
| 4 | $SoH(N) = \frac{C(N)}{C_0} \times 100\%$ | State of Health | % |
| 5 | $SoH_{EoL} = 80\%$ | Industry EoL threshold | % |
| 6 | $E_{delivered} = N_{cycle} \cdot C_{rated} \cdot V_{nom} \cdot DoD$ | Total energy throughput | Wh |
| 7 | $Q_{loss} = \int_0^{t} \vert I \cdot \eta_{coulomb} - I_{SEI} \vert \, dt$ | Cumulative charge loss | Ah |
| 8 | $R_{int}(N) = R_0 \cdot (1 + \alpha \sqrt{N})$ | Internal resistance growth | Ω |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Board Note:** When the question asks for "cycle life of a battery", examiners expect either: (a) the empirical $N_{cycle}$ value from manufacturer datasheet, OR (b) a calculated value using formula (1) or (3) above. Always state your assumed EoL threshold.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Capacity Fade vs. Cycle Count (Exponential Model)

**Starting assumption:** The rate of capacity loss per cycle is **proportional to the remaining capacity** (first-order kinetics — analogous to radioactive decay).

$$\frac{dC}{dN} = -\lambda \cdot C(N)$$

**Step 1 — Rearrange variables to integrate:**

$$\frac{dC}{C} = -\lambda \, dN$$

**Step 2 — Apply initial condition** $C(0) = C_0$ at $N=0$ and integrate from $0$ to $N$:

$$\int_{C_0}^{C(N)} \frac{1}{C} \, dC = -\lambda \int_{0}^{N} dN$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integrals:**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{C(N)}{C_0}\right) = -\lambda N$$

**Step 4 — Exponentiate both sides:**

$$C(N) = C_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda N}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\lambda$ at End-of-Life** where $C(N_{EoL}) = 0.8 \cdot C_0$:

$$0.8 \cdot C_0 = C_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda N_{EoL}}$$

$$0.8 = e^{-\lambda N_{EoL}}$$

$$\ln(0.8) = -\lambda N_{EoL}$$

$$\lambda = -\frac{\ln(0.8)}{N_{EoL}} = \frac{\ln(1.25)}{N_{EoL}} = \frac{0.2231}{N_{EoL}}$$

**Step 6 — Final compact form:**

$$\boxed{\;C(N) = C_0 \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{0.2231 \cdot N}{N_{EoL}}\right)\;}$$

> **Conversion logic:** This is a decay-constant substitution. The factor $0.2231 = \ln(1/0.8)$ is a *universal constant for 80% EoL*. For 70% EoL, it becomes $\ln(1/0.7) = 0.3567$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Total Energy Throughput Over Lifetime

The **total energy delivered** by a battery pack over its entire cycle life is a key KTU design metric for EV range estimation.

**Step 1 — Energy per single cycle:**

$$E_{cycle} = C_{rated} \cdot V_{nom} \cdot DoD$$

**Step 2 — Total energy throughput (integrated over all cycles):**

$$E_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_{cycle}} E_{cycle,i}$$

**Step 3 — For a constant-DoD operational profile:**

$$E_{total} = N_{cycle} \cdot C_{rated} \cdot V_{nom} \cdot DoD$$

**Step 4 — For variable DoD operation, use the cycle-counting method (Rainflow counting):**

$$E_{total} = \sum_{j} N_j \cdot C_{rated} \cdot V_{nom} \cdot DoD_j$$

where $N_j$ is the number of cycles at depth $DoD_j$ extracted from the load profile.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Style)

> **Problem [Module 3, KTU Pattern]:**
> A 60 Ah NMC Li-ion cell is rated for **1500 cycles to 80% capacity at 100% DoD, 25 °C, 0.5C charge / 0.5C discharge**. An EV operates it at **60% average DoD** with the empirical exponent $k = 0.95$. Calculate:
> (a) Adjusted cycle life at 60% DoD
> (b) Total energy throughput in kWh
> (c) Decay constant $\lambda$
> (d) State of Health after 800 cycles

### Solution:

**Part (a) — Cycle life at 60% DoD:**

$$N_{cycle}(60) = 1500 \cdot \left(\frac{100}{60}\right)^{0.95}$$

$$= 1500 \cdot (1.6667)^{0.95}$$

$$= 1500 \cdot 1.6262$$

$$\boxed{N_{cycle}(60) \approx 2439 \text{ cycles}}$$

**Part (b) — Total energy throughput** (taking $V_{nom} = 3.7$ V):

$$E_{total} = 2439 \cdot 60 \cdot 3.7 \cdot 0.60 \, \text{Wh}$$

$$= 2439 \cdot 133.2 \, \text{Wh}$$

$$= 324{,}875 \, \text{Wh} \approx 324.9 \, \text{kWh}$$

**Part (c) — Decay constant** (at 100% DoD, $N_{EoL} = 1500$):

$$\lambda = \frac{0.2231}{1500} = 1.487 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cycles}^{-1}$$

**Part (d) — State of Health at N = 800 cycles** (assuming 100% DoD base):

$$SoH(800) = e^{-\lambda \cdot 800} \times 100\%$$

$$= e^{-0.11896} \times 100\%$$

$$= 0.8880 \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{SoH(800) \approx 88.8\%}$$

> **Conversion logic:** $e^{-0.11896} \approx 0.8880$ is computed by Taylor expansion or direct calculator. The SoH has not yet crossed the 80% EoL threshold, meaning the cell has remaining useful life.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Cycle Life by Chemistry (Comparison Table)

| Chemistry | $N_{cycle}$ at 100% DoD | Energy Density (Wh/kg) | Thermal Runaway Risk | Cost ($/kWh, 2024) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Lead-Acid** | 300–500 | 30–50 | Low | 150–200 |
| **NiMH** | 500–1000 | 60–120 | Moderate | 250–350 |
| **NMC Li-ion** | 1000–2000 | 150–220 | High | 120–140 |
| **LFP (LiFePO₄)** | 2000–5000 | 90–160 | Very Low | 90–110 |
| **NCA Li-ion** | 500–1000 | 200–260 | High | 130–150 |
| **Solid-State (projected)** | 5000–10000+ | 300–500 | Negligible | 300+ (early) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Cycle Life Degradation Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New Battery Cell C0] --> B[Charge Phase 4.2V Cutoff]
    B --> C[Discharge Phase 80 Percent DoD]
    C --> D{After Nth Cycle}
    D -->|Continue Cycling| E[SEI Layer Growth]
    D -->|Continue Cycling| F[Cathode Particle Cracking]
    D -->|Continue Cycling| G[Electrolyte Oxidation]
    D -->|Continue Cycling| H[Li Plating Risk]
    E --> I[Capacity Fade CN]
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J{SoH Check 80 Percent Threshold}
    J -->|SoH Above 80| K[Return to Charge Phase]
    J -->|SoH Equals 80| L[End of Life EOL Reached]
    J -->|SoH Below 80| M[Battery Retirement or Repurposing]
    L --> N[Total Energy Throughput ET Recorded]
    M --> N
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — DoD vs. Cycle Life Strategy Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[EV Battery Pack] --> B{Operating Strategy}
    B -->|Full Cycle 100 DoD| C[N equals 1000 cycles]
    B -->|Shallow Cycle 50 DoD| D[N equals 3000 cycles]
    B -->|Micro Cycle 20 DoD| E[N equals 8000 cycles]
    C --> F[Range Anxiety Low]
    D --> G[Range Anxiety Moderate]
    E --> H[Range Anxiety High]
    C --> I[Battery Replacement 3 Years]
    D --> J[Battery Replacement 8 Years]
    E --> K[Battery Replacement 15 Years]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Battery Lifecycle Stage Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage 1 Manufacturing] --> B[Stage 2 First Use SoH 100]
    B --> C[Stage 3 Normal Operation SoH 100 to 90]
    C --> D[Stage 4 Aging Phase SoH 90 to 80]
    D --> E[Stage 5 End of Life SoH 80]
    E --> F{Second Life Decision}
    F -->|Reuse| G[Stationary Storage Solar Backup]
    F -->|Recycle| H[Material Recovery Li Co Ni]
    G --> I[Retirement Final]
    H --> I
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block — Factor Interaction Map for Cycle Life

```mermaid

    subgraph INPUT[Operating Conditions]
        A1[Temperature]

    end
```
