> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram interpretation guide:** In a typical KTU board answer, the examiner will award full marks only if the student draws a labeled flow connecting: *Cause → Mechanism → Symptom → Consequence*. The above Mermaid diagrams formalize exactly this structure.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Define cycle life of a battery. State the industry-accepted end-of-life criterion.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Cycle life is the number of complete charge–discharge cycles a rechargeable battery can perform before its usable capacity drops below a specified threshold.
- **EoL criterion (1 mark):** Industry-standard is **80% of initial rated capacity** retention (per IEC 62660-1).

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**List any three factors affecting the cycle life of a Li-ion battery.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Depth of Discharge (DoD)** — higher DoD reduces cycle life.
2. **C-rate** — high charge/discharge rates accelerate degradation.
3. **Operating temperature** — both extremes (sub-zero and elevated) damage the cell.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Module 3 Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the exponential capacity fade model $C(N) = C_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda N}$ for a Li-ion cell. Define the decay constant and express it in terms of the EoL cycle count for an 80% capacity threshold. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 50 Ah NMC cell has $N_{100} = 1200$ cycles at 100% DoD. The empirical DoD exponent is $k = 1.05$. Determine: (i) cycle life at 40% DoD, (ii) total energy throughput in kWh at 40% DoD operation, and (iii) the SoH after 500 equivalent full cycles. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question A:

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

Assume first-order capacity decay:

$$\frac{dC}{dN} = -\lambda C(N)$$

Separating variables:

$$\int_{C_0}^{C(N)} \frac{dC}{C} = -\lambda \int_0^N dN$$

Integrating:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{C(N)}{C_0}\right) = -\lambda N$$

Exponentiating:

$$C(N) = C_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda N} \quad \text{(3 marks for derivation)}$$

At EoL, $C(N_{EoL}) = 0.8 C_0$:

$$\lambda = \frac{-\ln(0.8)}{N_{EoL}} = \frac{0.2231}{N_{EoL}} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

Definition: $\lambda$ is the fractional capacity loss per cycle, with units cycles⁻¹. **(2 marks)**

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

(i) Cycle life at 40% DoD:

$$N_{40} = 1200 \cdot \left(\frac{100}{40}\right)^{1.05} = 1200 \cdot (2.5)^{1.05}$$

$(2.5)^{1.05} = 2.5 \cdot 2.5^{0.05} = 2.5 \cdot 1.0559 = 2.6398$

$$N_{40} \approx 1200 \times 2.6398 \approx 3168 \text{ cycles} \quad \text{(3 marks)}$$

(ii) Energy throughput at $V_{nom} = 3.7$ V:

$$E_{total} = 3168 \times 50 \times 3.7 \times 0.40 = 3168 \times 74 = 234{,}432 \text{ Wh} \approx 234.4 \text{ kWh} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

(iii) SoH after 500 cycles:

$$\lambda = \frac{0.2231}{1200} = 1.859 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cycles}^{-1}$$

$$SoH(500) = e^{-1.859 \times 10^{-4} \times 500} \times 100\% = e^{-0.09296} \times 100\%$$

$$= 0.9112 \times 100\% = 91.12\% \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the four primary degradation mechanisms that limit cycle life in Li-ion batteries. How does the SEI layer contribute to capacity loss? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the cycle life of LFP, NMC, and Lead-Acid batteries for EV applications. Justify why LFP is preferred for city electric buses despite its lower energy density. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution Outline — Question B:

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**
- SEI growth (1.5 marks) — consumes cyclable Li, grows with cycle count
- Li plating (1.5 marks) — high C-rate / low T causes metallic Li deposition
- Cathode cracking (2 marks) — particle fracture, transition metal dissolution
- Electrolyte decomposition (2 marks) — gas evolution, impedance rise

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

| Property | LFP | NMC | Lead-Acid |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cycle Life | 2000–5000 | 1000–2000 | 300–500 |
| Energy Density | 90–160 Wh/kg | 150–220 Wh/kg | 30–50 Wh/kg |
| Safety | Very High | Moderate | High |
| Cost | Lowest | Moderate | Lowest upfront |

**Justification (3 marks):** City buses operate on **fixed routes with frequent charge cycles per day** (3–5 cycles), making high cycle life more valuable than energy density. LFP's **3000+ cycles × daily 200 km = 600,000+ km** matches bus service life. Its **thermal stability** also suits depot-charging infrastructure. Lower energy density is acceptable because buses have ample volume for larger packs.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (Cycle Life Questions):**
>
> 1. **Confusing cycle life with calendar life** — They are distinct aging modes. *Cycle life = charge/discharge count*; *Calendar life = time-based aging even at rest*. KTU examiners explicitly test this distinction.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to state the EoL threshold** — A bare number without "to 80% capacity" is incomplete. Always write: *"Cycle life = 1500 cycles to 80% capacity."*
>
> 3. **Using wrong formula version** — When DoD ≠ 100%, you MUST use the adjusted formula $N_{cycle}(DoD) = N_{100} \cdot (100/DoD)^k$. Simply using $N_{100}$ directly loses 2–3 marks.
>
> 4. **Sign error in $\lambda$ derivation** — The decay is *negative exponential*, not positive. Write $e^{-\lambda N}$, never $e^{+\lambda N}$.
>
> 5. **Mixing units** — Energy throughput must be in **Wh or kWh**, not Joules (unless explicitly asked). $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \times 10^6 \text{ J}$.
>
> 6. **Not specifying the chemistry** — "Cycle life of a battery" is meaningless without chemistry. Always state (LFP, NMC, etc.).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Definition:** Cycle life = total full charge–discharge cycles to EoL (80% capacity by default).
- ✅ **Four degradation causes:** SEI growth, Li plating, cathode cracking, electrolyte loss.
- ✅ **Big Five factors:** DoD, C-rate, Temperature, SoC window, Cutoff voltage.
- ✅ **Master formula (must memorize):**
  - Capacity fade: $C(N) = C_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda N}$
  - Decay constant: $\lambda = 0.2231 / N_{EoL}$
  - DoD-adjusted life: $N(DoD) = N_{100} \cdot (100/DoD)^k$
  - SoH: $C(N)/C_0 \times 100\%$
- ✅ **EoL threshold = 80%** (industry default).
- ✅ **Chemistry hierarchy (cycle life, low→high):** Lead-Acid < NiMH < NCA < NMC < LFP < Solid-State.
- ✅ **Engineering rule of thumb:** Operating at 50% DoD and 0.5C rate can **triple** the cycle life.
- ✅ **Energy throughput formula:** $E_{total} = N_{cycle} \cdot C_{rated} \cdot V_{nom} \cdot DoD$.
- ✅ **For KTU board answers:** Always state the chemistry, EoL threshold, and DoD in the first line of any numerical problem.
- ✅ **For 14-mark derivations:** Show the differential equation → integration → boundary condition → final form. Skipping the integration step loses 2–3 marks.
- ✅ **Second-life reuse:** A battery at 80% SoH is *end-of-life for EV traction* but *suitable for stationary storage* — this is a frequently asked 3-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Self-discharge
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET742 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES AND RENEWABLE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Self-discharge

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Self-Discharge (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> *Self-discharge is the gradual, spontaneous loss of electrochemical energy stored in a battery when it is left in an open-circuit condition (i.e., disconnected from any external load), caused by internal parasitic chemical and electrochemical side reactions occurring between the electrodes, the electrolyte, and any internal impurities.*

In the context of **Electric Vehicles (EVs)**, self-discharge is a *parasitic loss mechanism* that directly reduces the available State of Charge (SoC) during parking, overnight storage, or long-term idle periods. For an EV parked at the airport for 30 days, the battery must still be able to crank the motor and run the BMS — this is only possible if the self-discharge rate is tightly characterized and predicted.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Used in KTU Valuation:**
> Self-discharge rate is universally expressed as a **percentage of rated capacity lost per unit time** (e.g., **% per month** or **% per year**). The empirical benchmark for a healthy Li-ion EV cell is **2 – 3 % per month** at **25 °C**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Leaky Water Tank

Imagine a fully filled **overhead water tank** that has no tap open, yet slowly empties on its own because of:
- A hairline crack at the bottom (internal short / micro-leak),
- Slow evaporation through the walls (side reactions),
- A loose washer inside the pipe (impurity reactions).

The water (charge) inside the tank (battery) drops even when no consumer is drawing it. **Self-discharge is the battery equivalent of this leakage.** The "crack width" widens with temperature and battery age, meaning an EV parked in **Kerala's tropical heat (35 – 40 °C)** will lose charge nearly **2 – 3× faster** than one parked in a cool climate-controlled garage at **15 °C**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Fact:** The Arrhenius rule of thumb states that for every **10 °C rise** in temperature, the self-discharge rate approximately **doubles**.

---

## 1.3 Why Self-Discharge Matters in EVs

| EV Subsystem | Impact of Self-Discharge |
|---|---|
| **Range Prediction** | SoC estimator gives wrong reading after long parking |
| **Battery Warranty** | Manufacturers void warranty if idle loss > threshold |
| **Thermal Management** | Parasitic heat from self-discharge adds to cooling load |
| **BMS Algorithm** | Coulomb-counting SoC drifts — needs periodic re-calibration |
| **Second-Life Cells** | Aged cells have high self-discharge → unsafe for reuse |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Layers of Self-Discharge Mechanism

Self-discharge in an electrochemical cell is **not a single phenomenon** but the combined result of multiple parasitic processes. Understanding each layer is mandatory for KTU 14-mark questions.

### Layer 1 — Electrochemical Side Reactions
- At the **negative electrode (anode)**: the slow oxidation of the active material (e.g., graphite in Li-ion cells reacts with residual electrolyte solvent).
- At the **positive electrode (cathode)**: slow reduction of the cathode transition metal (Mn, Co, Ni dissolution) by the electrolyte.
- These are **faradaic** losses — they consume usable active lithium.

### Layer 2 — Chemical Decomposition
- **Electrolyte decomposition** (e.g., LiPF₆ salt hydrolyzes to HF, which corrodes the SEI).
- **Binder decomposition** (PVDF breaks down at high SoC).
- These are **non-faradaic** and are usually **irreversible**.

### Layer 3 — Internal Micro-Short Circuits
- Dendrite growth during cycling creates microscopic metallic bridges between electrodes.
- Separator degradation creates pinholes.
- These cause **continuous internal current flow** even in open-circuit conditions.

---

## 2.2 Classification of Self-Discharge

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reversible Self-Discharge:** Capacity lost can be **recovered by simply recharging** the cell without any performance degradation.
> *Example:* Temporary voltage depression due to polarization built up during storage.

> [!WARNING]
> **Irreversible Self-Discharge:** Capacity lost is **permanently gone** because active material has been consumed.
> *Example:* Lithium plating, electrolyte decomposition, electrode corrosion. This directly reduces cycle life.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning of Each Symbol | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q(t) = Q_0 \, e^{-k \, t}$ | $Q(t)$ = capacity remaining at time $t$; $Q_0$ = initial capacity; $k$ = self-discharge rate constant (per day); $t$ = storage time (days) | First-order exponential decay model for SoC prediction during parking |
| 2 | $\alpha = \dfrac{\Delta Q}{Q_0 \cdot \Delta t} \times 100\%$ | $\alpha$ = self-discharge rate (%/month); $\Delta Q$ = capacity lost; $\Delta t$ = time interval | Linear approximation — used for short-term SoC tracking in BMS |
| 3 | $k = A \, e^{-E_a / (R \, T)}$ | $A$ = pre-exponential constant; $E_a$ = activation energy (J/mol); $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K); $T$ = absolute temperature (K) | Arrhenius temperature dependence — used to predict self-discharge at field temperatures |
| 4 | $I_{sd} = \dfrac{V_{oc}}{R_{sd}}$ | $I_{sd}$ = internal self-discharge current (A); $V_{oc}$ = open-circuit voltage; $R_{sd}$ = equivalent self-discharge resistance (Ω) | Equivalent circuit modelling for EV BMS |
| 5 | $Q_{loss} = \int_{0}^{t} I_{sd}(\tau) \, d\tau$ | $Q_{loss}$ = total charge lost (Ah); $\tau$ = integration variable | Used for warranty and storage-loss calculations |
| 6 | $\text{SoH} = \left(1 - \dfrac{\sum Q_{loss,irrev}}{Q_{rated}}\right) \times 100\%$ | SoH = State of Health; $Q_{loss,irrev}$ = cumulative irreversible loss | Used to retire cells when SoH < 80 % |

---

## 2.4 Factors Controlling the Self-Discharge Rate

- **Temperature** — Single most dominant factor; governed by Arrhenius law.
- **State of Charge (SoC)** — Higher SoC = higher cathode potential = faster oxidation of electrolyte. A 100 % SoC cell self-discharges ~3× faster than a 40 % SoC cell.
- **Battery Chemistry** (see table below).
- **Cell Age & Cycle Count** — Aged cells have thicker/damaged SEI → higher irreversible loss.
- **Storage Duration** — Long storage allows side reactions to compound.
- **Manufacturing Purity** — Trace water in LiPF₆ electrolyte → HF → cathode corrosion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparative Self-Discharge Rates at 25 °C (KTU High-Yield Data):**
> - Lithium-ion (LFP / NMC) → **2 – 3 % per month**
> - Lead-acid (flooded) → **4 – 6 % per month**
> - NiMH → **15 – 30 % per month**
> - NiCd → **10 – 20 % per month**
> - LFP (the safest EV chemistry) has the **lowest** self-discharge — this is why Tesla and Tata EVs increasingly use LFP packs.

---

## 2.5 Equivalent Circuit Model for Self-Discharge

In a BMS simulation environment, self-discharge is modelled as a **large parallel resistor $R_{sd}$** connected across the ideal cell:

$$I_{sd} = \frac{V_{oc}}{R_{sd}}$$

$$Q_{loss} = I_{sd} \times t = \frac{V_{oc} \cdot t}{R_{sd}}$$

The smaller the $R_{sd}$, the higher the parasitic leakage. For a healthy 50 Ah NMC cell, $R_{sd}$ is typically in the range of **10 – 50 kΩ**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Exponential Self-Discharge Decay Model

**Given:** A battery loses charge at a rate proportional to its instantaneous charge. The internal parasitic reactions behave as first-order kinetics.

**Step 1 — Write the differential rate equation:**

The rate of change of stored charge is proportional to the charge present:

$$\frac{dQ}{dt} = -k \, Q$$

where $k$ is the self-discharge rate constant (units: per day, per month, or per year, depending on $t$).

**Step 2 — Separate variables:**

$$\frac{dQ}{Q} = -k \, dt$$

**Step 3 — Integrate both sides from initial state $(t = 0, Q = Q_0)$ to $(t, Q)$:**

$$\int_{Q_0}^{Q(t)} \frac{dQ}{Q} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integrals:**

$$\ln Q(t) - \ln Q_0 = -k \, t$$

$$\ln \left( \frac{Q(t)}{Q_0} \right) = -k \, t$$

**Step 5 — Exponentiate to obtain the closed-form solution:**

$$\frac{Q(t)}{Q_0} = e^{-k \, t}$$

$$\boxed{\,Q(t) = Q_0 \, e^{-k \, t}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A larger $k$ means a faster decay. For an LFP cell with $k \approx 0.0007$ per day, after 30 days the remaining capacity is $Q(30) = Q_0 \, e^{-0.0007 \times 30} \approx 0.979 \, Q_0$ — i.e., only ~2.1 % loss in a month, exactly matching the KTU textbook benchmark.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Linear Approximation for Short-Duration Storage

**Given:** For short parking durations (a few days), $k \, t \ll 1$, so the exponential can be expanded using a Taylor series.

**Step 1 — Taylor series expansion of $e^{-k \, t}$:**

$$e^{-k \, t} = 1 - k \, t + \frac{(k \, t)^2}{2!} - \frac{(k \, t)^3}{3!} + \dots$$

**Step 2 — Neglect higher-order terms because $k \, t \ll 1$:**

$$e^{-k \, t} \approx 1 - k \, t$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back into the exponential model:**

$$Q(t) \approx Q_0 (1 - k \, t)$$

**Step 4 — Express the lost capacity as a percentage:**

$$\text{Loss} \, \% = \frac{Q_0 - Q(t)}{Q_0} \times 100\% = k \, t \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{\,\alpha = k \times 100\% \, \text{per unit time}\,}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always state explicitly that the linear model is valid **only** for $k \, t \leq 0.1$ (i.e., up to 10 % loss). Beyond this, you **must** use the exponential model or you will lose marks in the derivation step.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Arrhenius Temperature Correction

**Given:** Self-discharge is a thermally activated process. The rate constant depends on temperature through the Arrhenius equation.

**Step 1 — Write the Arrhenius relation:**

$$k(T) = A \, e^{-E_a / (R \, T)}$$

**Step 2 — Take the ratio of rate constants at two temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$:**

$$\frac{k(T_2)}{k(T_1)} = \frac{A \, e^{-E_a / (R \, T_2)}}{A \, e^{-E_a / (R \, T_1)}}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the ratio:**

$$\frac{k(T_2)}{k(T_1)} = e^{\frac{E_a}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} \right)}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $T_1 = 298 \, \text{K}$ (25 °C) and $T_2 = 313 \, \text{K}$ (40 °C) with $E_a = 60 \, \text{kJ/mol}$ (typical Li-ion value):**

$$\frac{k(313)}{k(298)} = e^{\frac{60000}{8.314} \left( \frac{1}{298} - \frac{1}{313} \right)}$$

$$\frac{k(313)}{k(298)} = e^{7216 \times (0.003356 - 0.003195)} = e^{7216 \times 0.000161}$$

$$\frac{k(313)}{k(298)} = e^{1.162} \approx 3.20$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result:**

$$\boxed{\,k(40 \, \text{°C}) \approx 3.2 \times k(25 \, \text{°C})\,}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Implication for Kerala Climate:** An EV stored in summer (40 °C) self-discharges **~3.2 times faster** than the same EV stored at 25 °C. This is why KTU examiners love asking "Why should an EV be parked in shade in Kerala summer?" — the answer is rooted in this Arrhenius derivation.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A 60 Ah NMC EV battery pack is parked for 90 days. The manufacturer datasheet gives $k = 0.0025$ per day at 25 °C. The car is parked in Trivandrum, where the average temperature is 35 °C. Take $E_a = 55$ kJ/mol.

**(i) Capacity remaining if stored at 25 °C:**

$$Q(90) = 60 \times e^{-0.0025 \times 90} = 60 \times e^{-0.225} = 60 \times 0.7985 = 47.91 \, \text{Ah}$$

**(ii) Temperature-corrected $k$ at 35 °C (308 K):**

$$\frac{k(308)}{k(298)} = e^{\frac{55000}{8.314} \left( \frac{1}{298} - \frac{1}{308} \right)} = e^{6615 \times 0.000109} = e^{0.721} = 2.056$$

$$k(308) = 0.0025 \times 2.056 = 0.00514 \, \text{per day}$$

**(iii) Capacity remaining at 35 °C:**

$$Q(90) = 60 \times e^{-0.00514 \times 90} = 60 \times e^{-0.4626} = 60 \times 0.6296 = 37.78 \, \text{Ah}$$

**(iv) Extra loss due to 10 °C rise:**

$$\Delta Q = 47.91 - 37.78 = 10.13 \, \text{Ah} \, (\approx 16.9\%)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** A 10 °C temperature increase caused an **extra 10 Ah loss** in 3 months — a clear illustration of why thermal management of idle EVs is essential in tropical climates.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Self-Discharge Across Chemistries

| Parameter | Lead-Acid | NiCd | NiMH | LFP (Li-ion) | NMC (Li-ion) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Rate (%/month at 25 °C) | 4 – 6 | 10 – 20 | 15 – 30 | 1 – 3 | 2 – 5 |
| Temperature Sensitivity | High | Medium | Medium | Low | Medium |
| Reversibility | Mostly | Mostly | Mostly | Mostly | Partial |
| Dominant Mechanism | Grid corrosion | Oxygen cycle | Separator leakage | SEI growth | Cathode dissolution |
| EV Suitability | Mild-hybrid only | Legacy EVs | Toyota hybrids | Tesla Model 3 SR, Tata Nexon EV | Most modern EVs |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Self-Discharge Mechanism Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Battery in Open Circuit] --> B[Parasitic Internal Reactions]
    B --> C1[Electrochemical Side Reactions]
    B --> C2[Chemical Decomposition]
    B --> C3[Internal Micro-Short]
    C1 --> D1[Anode Oxidation]
    C1 --> D2[Cathode Reduction]
    C2 --> E1[Electrolyte Hydrolysis]
    C2 --> E2[Binder Breakdown]
    C3 --> F1[Dendrite Growth]
    C3 --> F2[Separator Pinhole]
    D1 --> G[Capacity Loss]
    D2 --> G
    E1 --> G
    E2 --> G
    F1 --> G
    F2 --> G
    G --> H[Reversible Fraction]
    G --> I[Irreversible Fraction]
    H --> J[Recoverable by Recharge]
    I --> K[Permanent SoH Degradation]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Equivalent Circuit with Self-Discharge Resistor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Vcell[Ideal Cell Voltage Vcell] --> Rp[Rsd Parallel Self-Discharge Resistance]
    Rp --> Loss[Parasitic Current Isd]
    Loss --> Heat[Heat Generation]
    Vcell --> Rint[Internal Resistance Rint]
    Rint --> Vterm[Terminal Voltage Vterm]
    Vterm --> Load[External EV Load]
    Loss --> BMS[BMS SoC Drift]
    BMS --> SoC[State of Charge Correction]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Process Flow — Arrhenius Temperature Correction Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Datasheet k at 25 C] --> Calc[Compute Ea or use given Ea]
    Calc --> Ratio[Form Ratio k_T2 over k_T1]
    Ratio --> Apply[Exponentiate using Arrhenius form]
    Apply --> NewK[New k at Field Temperature]
    NewK --> Model[Apply Exponential Decay Q of t]
    Model --> Result[Capacity Remaining Ah]
    Result --> Loss[Compute SoC Loss]
    Loss --> Display[Display Result to Driver Dashboard]
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Comparison Matrix — Reversible vs Irreversible Self-Discharge

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Self[Self-Discharge Event] --> Q1{Is Active Material Consumed?}
    Q1 -- No --> Rev[Reversible Loss]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Irr[Irreversible Loss]
    Rev --> R1[Voltage Depression]
    Rev --> R2[Recharge Restores Capacity]
    Irr --> I1[SEI Growth]
    Irr --> I2[Electrode Dissolution]
    Irr --> I3[Permanent Capacity Fade]
    I3 --> Retire[SoH below 80 percent triggers Cell Retirement]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]
**Define self-discharge in a battery. List the four main internal mechanisms that contribute to it.**

**Model Answer:**
Self-discharge is the spontaneous loss of stored electrochemical energy in a battery when it is left in open-circuit condition (no external load connected). The four main internal mechanisms are:

1. **Electrochemical side reactions** at the anode (oxidation) and cathode (reduction).
2. **Chemical decomposition** of the electrolyte (e.g., hydrolysis of LiPF₆ salt to HF).
3. **Internal micro-short circuits** due to dendrite growth or separator pinholes.
4. **Impurity reactions** with trace water or metallic contaminants inside the cell.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for the four mechanisms.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]
**State the Arrhenius equation governing the temperature dependence of self-discharge. What happens to the self-discharge rate if temperature rises by 10 °C?**

**Model Answer:**
The Arrhenius equation is:

$$k(T) = A \, e^{-E_a / (R \, T)}$$

A rise of 10 °C in temperature approximately **doubles** the self-discharge rate (rule of thumb derived from the Arrhenius expression). For typical Li-ion cells with $E_a \approx 55$ – $60$ kJ/mol, the rate actually increases by a factor of **2.0 – 3.2** for a 10 °C rise.

*(3 marks: 1 for equation + 2 for quantitative effect.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks split as (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Two full alternative questions are provided.

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]

**(a) Derive the exponential self-discharge model $Q(t) = Q_0 \, e^{-k \, t}$ starting from the first-order rate equation. State two conditions where this model is preferred over the linear approximation.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State the governing differential equation.**
The rate of charge loss is proportional to the charge present:

$$\frac{dQ}{dt} = -k \, Q$$

**Step 2 — Separate variables and integrate:**

$$\int_{Q_0}^{Q(t)} \frac{dQ}{Q} = -k \int_0^t dt$$

$$\ln \left( \frac{Q(t)}{Q_0} \right) = -k \, t$$

**Step 3 — Exponentiate to obtain the closed form:**

$$Q(t) = Q_0 \, e^{-k \, t}$$

**Step 4 — Identify the two conditions for using the exponential model:**
1. **Long storage duration** (months to years) where $k \, t$ is no longer small and the linear approximation introduces > 5 % error.
2. **High temperatures or aged cells** where the rate constant is large, making $k \, t > 0.1$ even for short durations.

*(Award: 1 mark for stating the differential equation, 3 marks for derivation steps, 1 mark for final box equation, 2 marks for the two conditions.)*

---

**(b) A 50 Ah Li-ion EV battery is stored for 6 months at 30 °C. The self-discharge rate constant is $k = 0.0045$ per day. Calculate (i) the remaining capacity, (ii) the percentage loss, and (iii) whether the linear approximation would give an acceptable answer here.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $Q_0 = 50$ Ah, $t = 6 \times 30 = 180$ days, $k = 0.0045$ per day.

**(i) Remaining capacity (exponential model):**

$$Q(180) = 50 \times e^{-0.0045 \times 180} = 50 \times e^{-0.81}$$

$$Q(180) = 50 \times 0.4449 = 22.25 \, \text{Ah}$$

**(ii) Percentage loss:**

$$\text{Loss} \, \% = \frac{50 - 22.25}{50} \times 100\% = 55.5\%$$

**(iii) Validity of linear approximation:**

Check the parameter $k \, t = 0.0045 \times 180 = 0.81$.

Since $0.81 \gg 0.1$, the linear model is **NOT acceptable**. The linear model would give:

$$Q_{lin}(180) = 50 \times (1 - 0.81) = 9.5 \, \text{Ah}$$

This is **less than half** the true answer, proving the linear model grossly underestimates the remaining capacity.

*(Award: 2 marks each for parts (i) and (ii), 3 marks for part (iii) including the validity check and the linear-model error calculation.)*

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style — Alternative Choice]

**(a) Explain the three layers of self-discharge mechanisms (electrochemical, chemical, micro-short). For each layer, give one practical mitigation strategy used in EV battery packs.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Layer 1 — Electrochemical side reactions:**
These occur at the electrode-electrolyte interface, where the active material (e.g., lithiated graphite) is slowly oxidized by the electrolyte, or the cathode transition metal dissolves.
*Mitigation:* A stable **Solid Electrolyte Interphase (SEI) layer** is engineered on the anode during the first formation cycles, blocking further side reactions. Electrolyte additives (e.g., vinylene carbonate) further stabilize the SEI.

**Layer 2 — Chemical decomposition:**
The electrolyte salt (e.g., LiPF₆) hydrolyzes in the presence of trace water to form HF, which corrodes the cathode.
*Mitigation:* Ultra-dry manufacturing environments (dew point below −40 °C), use of more stable salts like **LiFSI** or **LiTFSI**, and hermetically sealed cell housings.

**Layer 3 — Internal micro-shorts:**
Dendrite growth (especially during fast charging at low temperatures) and separator degradation create microscopic metallic bridges.
*Mitigation:* **Ceramic-coated separators**, controlled charging protocols that limit C-rate at low temperatures, and pressure-sensitive current-interrupt devices (CIDs) in the cell cap.

*(Award: 1 mark for naming each layer, 1 mark for the mechanism explanation, 1 mark for the mitigation. Total 3 layers × ~2.3 marks each = 7 marks.)*

---

**(b) With the help of the Arrhenius equation, prove that the self-discharge rate of an LFP cell approximately doubles for every 10 °C rise in temperature. Assume $E_a = 50$ kJ/mol. Comment on the practical significance for EVs operating in tropical India.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Use the Arrhenius form:**

$$k(T) = A \, e^{-E_a / (R \, T)}$$

**Step 2 — Form the ratio at $T_1 = 298$ K and $T_2 = 308$ K:**

$$\frac{k(308)}{k(298)} = e^{\frac{E_a}{R} \left( \frac{1}{298} - \frac{1}{308} \right)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the temperature term:**

$$\frac{1}{298} - \frac{1}{308} = \frac{308 - 298}{298 \times 308} = \frac{10}{91784} = 1.0895 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the exponent:**

$$\frac{E_a}{R} \times 1.0895 \times 10^{-4} = \frac{50000}{8.314} \times 1.0895 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$= 6014 \times 1.0895 \times 10^{-4} = 0.6553$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the ratio:**

$$\frac{k(308)}{k(298)} = e^{0.6553} \approx 1.926$$

$$\boxed{\,k(308) \approx 1.93 \times k(298) \approx \text{doubled}\,}$$

**Step 6 — Practical comment for tropical India (Kerala / TN / AP):**
EVs in Kerala experience ambient temperatures of **30 – 40 °C** for 6 – 8 months a year. A car parked in summer sun at 40 °C will lose charge **~4× faster** than the same car parked in an AC garage at 20 °C. This is why EV manufacturers specify **storage temperature limits of 15 – 25 °C** and recommend parking in shade. Battery management systems also apply **temperature-compensated SoC correction** to avoid showing a falsely low range after long parking in hot conditions.

*(Award: 4 marks for the derivation, 1 mark for the boxed numerical result, 2 marks for the practical comment specific to Indian/tropical EV usage.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Losing Pitfalls in Self-Discharge Questions:**
> 1. **Mixing reversible and irreversible** — students often list both but fail to give a concrete example for each. The valuation key explicitly asks for an *example* (e.g., SEI growth = irreversible; voltage depression = reversible). Missing this = lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting the units of $k$** — examiners check whether you mentioned that $k$ is in *per day*, *per month*, or *per year*. Silent assumption = lose 0.5 mark.
> 3. **Linear-vs-exponential validity** — blindly using the linear model for long storage is the **#1 cause of full-mark loss** in 7-mark numerical problems. Always state the condition $k \, t \leq 0.1$ and verify it.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert temperature to Kelvin** before substituting into the Arrhenius equation. Using 30 directly instead of 303 K is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 5. **Ignoring tropical-India context** — KTU 2024 scheme explicitly awards marks for *regional application context*. Always add a one-line comment on Kerala/tropical climate wherever self-discharge or Arrhenius is discussed.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Self-Discharge — High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- **Definition:** Spontaneous charge loss in open-circuit conditions due to internal parasitic reactions.
- **Standard Metric:** % per month at 25 °C; benchmark for Li-ion is **2 – 3 %**.
- **Three Mechanisms:** Electrochemical side reactions, chemical decomposition, internal micro-shorts.
- **Two Types:** Reversible (recovered by recharge) vs Irreversible (permanent SoH loss).
- **Master Equation:** $Q(t) = Q_0 \, e^{-k \, t}$ (exponential, first-order kinetics).
- **Linear Approximation:** $Q(t) \approx Q_0 (1 - k \, t)$; **valid only when $k \, t \leq 0.1$**.
- **Arrhenius Law:** $k(T) = A \, e^{-E_a / (R \, T)}$; **rate doubles per 10 °C rise**.
- **Equivalent Circuit Model:** Self-discharge modelled as a **large parallel resistance $R_{sd}$** across the cell.
- **Chemistry Ranking (low → high self-discharge):** LFP < NMC < Lead-acid < NiCd < NiMH.
- **Storage Best Practice:** Store at **40 – 60 % SoC** and **15 – 25 °C** to minimize both reversible and irreversible loss.
- **EV-Specific Concerns:** Range drift, BMS SoC error, warranty disputes, second-life cell screening.
- **Tropical India Implication:** Kerala summer (35 – 40 °C) accelerates self-discharge by **2 – 3×** vs lab reference (25 °C).
- **Mitigation Tools:** Stable SEI engineering, ceramic-coated separators, dry-room manufacturing, BMS temperature-compensated SoC, periodic top-up charging for long storage.
- **KTU 2024 Favourite Question Type:** "Derive the exponential model and explain its temperature dependence using Arrhenius with a numerical example." Memorize the derivation steps verbatim.
- **Numerical Heuristic:** $k \, t > 0.1$ → use exponential; $k \, t \leq 0.1$ → linear is acceptable (saves computation time in the exam).
- **Reversible vs Irreversible Decision Rule:** If active material mass changes → **irreversible**; if only electrode polarization or voltage depression → **reversible**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Battery Management System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET742 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES AND RENEWABLE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Battery Management System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Battery Management System (BMS) — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Battery Management System (BMS)** is an embedded electronic control unit (ECU) comprising hardware (sensors, microcontrollers, power electronics, communication transceivers) and firmware (state-estimation algorithms, balancing logic, protection routines) that monitors, controls, and protects a rechargeable battery pack — most commonly a Lithium-ion (Li-ion) pack in electric vehicles (EVs). It performs real-time measurement of cell voltages, pack current, and temperatures; computes critical **State functions** (SOC, SOH, SOP, SOE); enforces safety limits; manages **cell balancing**; regulates **thermal management**; and communicates with the Vehicle Control Unit (VCU) and the onboard charger via standardized protocols such as **CAN (Controller Area Network)** as per **ISO 11898**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The course outcome (CO) for this module emphasizes the student's ability to *describe the architecture, functions, and protection strategies of BMS used in modern EVs*, including cell balancing, SOC/SOH estimation, and thermal management.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

Imagine a **battery pack** as a *chain of water tanks* stacked vertically to supply a high-pressure water system (your EV motor). Each tank (cell) has a slightly different capacity, leakage rate, and temperature. Without supervision:
- Some tanks overfill → **over-voltage → thermal runaway → fire**
- Some tanks underfill → **deep discharge → permanent damage**
- One tank leaks heat faster → **localized overheating → pack failure**

The **BMS is the "supervisor"** — it constantly checks each tank's level (voltage), tracks total water used (current integration = **Coulomb Counting → SOC**), measures how warm each tank gets (temperature), redistributes water from fuller tanks to emptier ones (**cell balancing**), shuts off the supply if any tank approaches danger (**protection cutoffs**), and reports the system's overall "health age" (**SOH** — like the wear on a tire) to the dashboard.

In short: **The BMS is the "brain + nervous system + reflexes" of the battery pack.** Without it, modern Li-ion EVs would be unreliable, unsafe, and uneconomical.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in BMS

| Metric | Standard Unit | Typical EV Range |
|---|---|---|
| Nominal Cell Voltage (Li-ion) | Volts (V) | **3.6 V – 3.7 V** |
| Pack Voltage (400 V class) | Volts (V) | **350 V – 450 V** |
| Cell Capacity | Ampere-hour (Ah) | **50 Ah – 100 Ah** |
| State of Charge (SOC) | Percentage (%) | **0 % – 100 %** |
| State of Health (SOH) | Percentage (%) | **80 % – 100 %** (end-of-life) |
| C-rate (charge/discharge) | 1/h | **0.5 C – 3 C** |
| Operating Temperature | °C | **-20 °C to +60 °C** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constant Used in BMS Math:**
> **Faraday's Constant** $F = 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}$ — used in advanced electrochemistry-based SOC estimation models.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Primary Functions of a BMS (The "Big Six")

A KTU examiner expects students to enumerate these in order. The BMS performs six core functions:

1. **Monitoring (Data Acquisition):**
   - Measures individual cell voltages via analog front-end (AFE) ICs (e.g., LTC6811, BQ76952).
   - Measures pack current using a **shunt resistor** (low-side) or **Hall-effect sensor** (isolated).
   - Measures temperature at NTC (Negative Temperature Coefficient) thermistor locations — typically 4 to 16 sensors per pack.

2. **State Estimation:**
   - **SOC** (State of Charge) — remaining energy as a % of rated capacity.
   - **SOH** (State of Health) — current maximum capacity as a % of original rated capacity.
   - **SOP** (State of Power) — peak charge/discharge power the pack can deliver *right now* for the next 2–10 seconds.
   - **SOE** (State of Energy) — energy in Wh (not just charge in Ah); accounts for voltage depression at low SOC.

3. **Cell Balancing:**
   - **Passive balancing:** Dissipates excess energy of stronger cells as heat through resistors.
   - **Active balancing:** Redistributes energy from stronger cells to weaker cells via DC-DC converters, inductors, capacitors, or transformers.

4. **Thermal Management:**
   - Activates liquid cooling pumps, fans, or PTC heaters to keep cells in the **20 °C – 40 °C** optimal window.
   - Prevents thermal runaway propagation (cell-to-cell fire spread).

5. **Protection (Safety Cutoffs):**
   - **OVP** (Over-Voltage Protection): typically at **4.2 V/cell** for Li-ion.
   - **UVP** (Under-Voltage Protection): typically at **2.5 V – 2.8 V/cell**.
   - **OCP** (Over-Current Protection): software + fuse + contactor.
   - **OTP/UTP** (Over/Under-Temperature Protection).
   - **SCP** (Short-Circuit Protection): < 1 ms response via hardware comparators.

6. **Communication & Diagnostics:**
   - CAN 2.0B / CAN FD messages to VCU, charger, instrument cluster.
   - Stores **DTCs** (Diagnostic Trouble Codes) per **UDS** (ISO 14229) standard.
   - Isolated communication for high-voltage safety.

## 2.2 BMS Architecture Topologies

KTU may ask: *"Compare centralized, distributed, and modular BMS architectures."*

| Architecture | Description | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Centralized** | One master BMS module connected to all cells via a large wiring harness | Low cost, simple | Long cables → noise, harness weight, single point of failure |
| **Distributed** | Each cell (or small group) has its own "slave" BMS IC; one master coordinates | Modular, scalable, short cables, high accuracy | Higher per-cell cost, more complex firmware |
| **Modular (Master-Slave)** | Compromise: groups of 8–16 cells per slave board; multiple slaves linked to a master | Balanced cost/scalability, easier maintenance | Slightly more complex than centralized |

## 2.3 SOC Estimation Methods — Theory and Trade-offs

This is a **favourite KTU Part-B question (7–14 marks).** Memorize the three main methods.

### 2.3.1 Coulomb Counting (Current Integration)

$$SOC(t) = SOC(t_0) + \frac{1}{Q_{rated}} \int_{t_0}^{t} \eta \cdot I(\tau) \, d\tau$$

Where:
- $Q_{rated}$ = rated capacity in Ah
- $I(\tau)$ = instantaneous current (positive = discharge, negative = charge by convention)
- $\eta$ = Coulombic efficiency (≈ 0.99 for Li-ion)

**Limitation:** Open-loop → drift over time due to current-sensor offset; requires periodic recalibration from a known full-charge event.

### 2.3.2 Open-Circuit Voltage (OCV) Method

$$SOC = f^{-1}(V_{oc})$$

Where the function $f$ is established experimentally as the **OCV–SOC curve** (a non-linear monotonic curve due to the flat Li-ion discharge plateau).

**Limitation:** Requires the battery to be at **rest for 1–4 hours** to remove polarization — impractical during driving.

### 2.3.3 Model-Based / Observer Methods (Advanced)

- **Equivalent Circuit Models (ECM):** R-int, Thevenin, dual-polarization RC.
- **Kalman Filter (KF)**, **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)**, and **Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF)** combine OCV + Coulomb counting, self-correcting drift.
- **Machine-learning approaches** (Neural Networks, Gaussian Process Regression) — emerging in 2024+ research.

## 2.4 Cell Balancing — Mathematical Foundation

### 2.4.1 Passive Balancing

Energy dissipated in a balancing resistor $R_{bal}$ over time $t$:

$$E_{dissipated} = \frac{V_{cell}^2}{R_{bal}} \cdot t = I_{bal}^2 \cdot R_{bal} \cdot t$$

Typical passive balancing current: **50 mA – 200 mA** per cell → balancing takes several hours.

### 2.4.2 Active Balancing — Capacitor-Shuttle Method

Charge transferred per switching cycle (one cycle = two phases):

$$\Delta Q = C_{shuttle} \cdot (V_{high} - V_{low})$$

### 2.4.3 Active Balancing — Inductor/Converter Method

Energy transferred per cycle (for a buck-boost inductor balancer):

$$\Delta E = \frac{1}{2} L \left( I_{peak}^2 - I_{valley}^2 \right) \approx V_{cell} \cdot I_{avg} \cdot \Delta t$$

**Efficiency:** Active balancing achieves **85 % – 95 %** energy efficiency vs. **0 %** (full loss) for passive.

## 2.5 Thermal Model — Cauer / Foster RC Ladder

Heat generation rate in a cell:

$$Q_{gen} = I^2 \cdot R_{internal} + I \cdot T \cdot \frac{\partial V_{oc}}{\partial T}$$

The lumped thermal model is:

$$m \cdot c_p \cdot \frac{dT}{dt} = Q_{gen} - \frac{T - T_{ambient}}{R_{th}}$$

Where $R_{th}$ is thermal resistance (K/W) and $c_p$ is specific heat (J/kg·K).

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbols & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $SOC(t) = SOC_0 + \frac{1}{Q_{rated}} \int \eta I \, dt$ | $Q_{rated}$ (Ah), $I$ (A) | Coulomb counting |
| 2 | $SOH = \frac{Q_{max,current}}{Q_{rated}} \times 100\%$ | $Q_{max}$ (Ah) | Capacity fade |
| 3 | $SOH_R = \frac{R_{internal,current} - R_{internal,new}}{R_{internal,new}}$ | $R$ (Ω) | Resistance growth |
| 4 | $E_{dissipated} = \frac{V^2}{R_{bal}} \cdot t$ | $R_{bal}$ (Ω) | Passive balancing |
| 5 | $\Delta Q_{active} = C_{shuttle}(V_H - V_L)$ | $C$ (F) | Capacitor shuttle |
| 6 | $P_{peak} = V_{min,OCV} \cdot I_{max} - I_{max}^2 \cdot R_{int}$ | $P$ (W) | State of Power |
| 7 | $Q_{gen} = I^2 R_{int} + IT \frac{\partial V_{oc}}{\partial T}$ | $Q$ (W) | Heat generation |
| 8 | $\tau = R_{th} \cdot C_{th}$ | seconds | Thermal time constant |
| 9 | Energy in pack: $E_{pack} = V_{nom} \cdot Q_{pack} / 1000$ | kWh | Pack capacity |
| 10 | C-rate: $C = \frac{I}{Q_{rated}}$ | 1/h | Charge/discharge rate |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas appear in KTU Part-B questions, lab viva, and even in placement interviews at Tata Motors, Mahindra Electric, Ather, and Ola Electric. A working engineer at Bosch or Continental uses #1, #2, and #6 daily in BMS firmware.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: SOC from Coulomb Counting (Continuous-Time → Discrete-Time)

The continuous-time SOC equation is:

$$SOC(t) = SOC(t_0) + \frac{1}{Q_{rated}} \int_{t_0}^{t} \eta \cdot I(\tau) \, d\tau$$

**Step 1 — Recognize the integral as accumulated charge:**

The integral of current over time is, by definition, **electric charge** (in Ampere-seconds = Coulombs).

$$Q_{transferred}(t) = \int_{t_0}^{t} I(\tau) \, d\tau \quad \text{[Coulombs]}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to Ampere-hours** (industry convention; 1 Ah = 3600 C):

$$Q_{transferred,Ah} = \frac{1}{3600} \int_{t_0}^{t} I(\tau) \, d\tau$$

**Step 3 — Normalize against rated capacity to get a fraction (0 to 1):**

$$SOC(t) = SOC(t_0) + \frac{\eta \cdot Q_{transferred,Ah}}{Q_{rated,Ah}}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to discrete-time form (microcontroller implementation, sampled every $\Delta t$):**

$$SOC[k+1] = SOC[k] + \frac{\eta \cdot I[k] \cdot \Delta t}{3600 \cdot Q_{rated}}$$

**Step 5 — Apply numerical sign convention** (discharging = positive current removes SOC):

$$SOC[k+1] = SOC[k] - \frac{\eta \cdot I[k] \cdot \Delta t}{3600 \cdot Q_{rated}}$$

This is the exact equation embedded in production BMS firmware (e.g., TI BQ40z80, NXP MC33771).

## 3.2 Derivation: State of Health (SOH) from Capacity Fade

**Step 1 — Define initial and current maximum capacities:**

At the Beginning of Life (BOL), the cell delivers its rated capacity $Q_{rated}$. After $N$ cycles and $T$ years, it delivers $Q_{max}(t)$.

**Step 2 — Capacity-fade SOH (most common definition):**

$$SOH_C(t) = \frac{Q_{max}(t)}{Q_{rated}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 3 — Apply a typical Li-ion fade model (Arrhenius-style):**

$$Q_{max}(t) = Q_{rated} \cdot \left(1 - \alpha \cdot e^{-E_a / (R \cdot T)} \cdot \sqrt{Ah_{throughput}}\right)$$

Where:
- $\alpha$ = a fitted constant (typically $1 \times 10^{-4}$ to $1 \times 10^{-3}$)
- $E_a$ = activation energy (J/mol), ~ 30,000 J/mol for graphite/NMC
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)
- $Ah_{throughput}$ = cumulative ampere-hours cycled

**Step 4 — When SOH drops to 80 %, the cell is considered End-of-Life (EOL)** for automotive second-life applications.

## 3.3 Derivation: State of Power (SOP) — Peak Discharge for 10 s

**Step 1 — Battery terminal voltage during discharge:**

$$V_{term} = V_{oc}(SOC) - I \cdot R_{int}$$

**Step 2 — Enforce minimum allowed terminal voltage $V_{min}$:**

$$V_{min} = V_{oc}(SOC) - I_{peak} \cdot R_{int}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for peak current $I_{peak}$:**

$$I_{peak} = \frac{V_{oc}(SOC) - V_{min}}{R_{int}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute peak power (for 10 s pulse):**

$$P_{peak} = V_{min} \cdot I_{peak} = V_{min} \cdot \frac{V_{oc}(SOC) - V_{min}}{R_{int}}$$

**Step 5 — Alternative expression in terms of $V_{oc}$:**

$$\boxed{P_{peak} = \frac{\left(V_{oc}(SOC) - V_{min}\right) \cdot V_{min}}{R_{int}}}$$

This $P_{peak}$ is what the BMS reports to the VCU for torque-limiting during hard accelerations. The VCU will never demand more than this $P_{peak}$ — protecting the pack from over-current and voltage collapse.

## 3.4 Derivation: Active Capacitor-Shuttle Balancing Energy Transfer

**Step 1 — Initially, the shuttle capacitor $C_s$ is connected to the higher-voltage cell $V_H$:**

$$Q_1 = C_s \cdot V_H$$

**Step 2 — Capacitor is switched to the lower-voltage cell $V_L$:**

After switching, the capacitor voltage becomes $V_L$, releasing charge:

$$\Delta Q = C_s \cdot (V_H - V_L)$$

**Step 3 — Energy transferred per switching cycle:**

$$\Delta E = \frac{1}{2} C_s \left(V_H^2 - V_L^2\right) = \frac{1}{2} C_s (V_H - V_L)(V_H + V_L)$$

**Step 4 — For $N$ switching cycles, the total energy moved:**

$$E_{total} = N \cdot \frac{1}{2} C_s (V_H^2 - V_L^2)$$

**Step 5 — Practical limitation:** Capacitor shuttle is **charge-based** (only $\Delta Q$ moves per cycle), and switching losses mean efficiency ≈ 80–90 %. Inductor-based and transformer-based active balancers achieve higher currents and efficiency.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — KTU-style Problem

**Problem (typical 7-mark KTU Part-B sub-question):**

> A 400 V, 100 Ah EV battery pack has 100 cells in series (each 3.7 V nominal). The BMS uses Coulomb counting. If the initial SOC is 80 % and the vehicle draws a constant **150 A** for **30 minutes** (driving), calculate the final SOC. Assume Coulombic efficiency $\eta = 0.99$. Take a sensor offset of **+0.3 A** (constant error).

**Given:**
- $SOC_0 = 80\%$
- $I_{actual} = 150$ A
- $I_{sensor} = 150.3$ A (measured)
- $\Delta t = 30 \text{ min} = 1800 \text{ s}$
- $Q_{rated} = 100$ Ah
- $\eta = 0.99$

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert $\Delta t$ to hours:**

$$\Delta t = 1800 \text{ s} \div 3600 = 0.5 \text{ h}$$

**Step 2 — Compute charge transferred (in Ah):**

$$Q_{transferred} = I_{sensor} \cdot \Delta t = 150.3 \text{ A} \times 0.5 \text{ h} = 75.15 \text{ Ah}$$

**Step 3 — Apply efficiency and SOC update formula:**

$$SOC_{final} = SOC_0 - \frac{\eta \cdot Q_{transferred}}{Q_{rated}}$$

$$SOC_{final} = 80\% - \frac{0.99 \times 75.15 \text{ Ah}}{100 \text{ Ah}} = 80\% - 74.3985\%$$

$$\boxed{SOC_{final} \approx 5.60\%}$$

**Step 4 — Sensor-offset error analysis:**

True charge transferred = $150 \times 0.5 = 75.0$ Ah. The error in SOC = $(0.99 \times 0.15)/100 = 0.1485\%$. Over 1000 cycles, this could accumulate to >10 % drift — hence the need for **periodic OCV-based recalibration** at rest.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion for SOC management:** Combine Coulomb counting (good during driving) with periodic OCV calibration (at rest) → best of both worlds. This is the **hybrid SOC estimation** approach used in Tesla, BYD, and Hyundai BMS.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level BMS Functional Architecture (Block Diagram)

The diagram below maps every subsystem of a production EV BMS, showing signal flow between the battery pack, the BMS hardware, the vehicle's other ECUs, and external infrastructure (charger, dashboard, diagnostic tool).

```mermaid
flowchart TB

```

## 4.2 Cell-Balancing Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start["Start Balancing Cycle every 60 s"]
    readSOC["Read all cell SOCs"]
    compute["Compute SOC_max minus SOC_min = Delta_SOC"]
    checkDelta{"Delta_SOC greater than threshold 2 percent"}
    off["Balancing OFF no action"]
    checkSoc{"SOC_max greater than 95 percent"}
    passive["Enable PASSIVE balancing on cells with SOC_max"]
    active["Enable ACTIVE balancing DC-DC converter from high SOC to low SOC"]
    log["Log balancing event to DTC buffer"]
    next["Wait 60 s loop"]

    start --> readSOC
    readSOC --> compute
    compute --> checkDelta
    checkDelta -- No --> off
    checkDelta -- Yes --> checkSoc
    checkSoc -- Yes, near full --> passive
    checkSoc -- No, mid range --> active
    passive --> log
    active --> log
    off --> next
    log --> next
```

## 4.3 SOC Estimation Hybrid Algorithm (Coulomb Counting + OCV Recalibration)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    init["Initialize SOC from OCV at vehicle KEY ON"]
    measure["Measure I, V, T every 100 ms"]
    driveMode{"Vehicle in DRIVE mode"}
    coulomb["Update SOC via Coulomb Counting SOC plus eta times I times dt over Q_rated"]
    restMode{"Vehicle at REST more than 2 hours"}
    ocvCal["Read V_terminal equals OCV recalibrate SOC from OCV curve"]
    display["Send SOC to dashboard CAN message ID 0x6B0"]
    loop["Return to measure"]

    init --> measure
    measure --> driveMode
    driveMode -- Yes --> coulomb
    driveMode -- No --> restMode
    restMode -- Yes --> ocvCal
    restMode -- No --> measure
    coulomb --> display
    ocvCal --> display
    display --> loop
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams for KTU exams:** In the exam hall, you can redraw these schematics freehand — the examiner awards marks for **clear signal-flow arrows, correct subsystem names, and showing the feedback loop between the MCU and the contactors/contactors** (the safety cutoffs). Do not worry about aesthetic perfection.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]
**Q: Define State of Charge (SOC) and State of Health (SOH) of a battery. Why is accurate SOC estimation critical in an EV?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **SOC** is the ratio of the remaining capacity to the rated capacity of a battery, expressed as a percentage: $SOC = \frac{Q_{remaining}}{Q_{rated}} \times 100\%$. It indicates the equivalent "fuel level" of the battery pack.
- **SOH** is the ratio of the current maximum deliverable capacity to the original rated capacity, indicating the age and degradation of the battery: $SOH = \frac{Q_{max,current}}{Q_{rated}} \times 100\%$.
- Accurate SOC estimation is critical because (i) it directly determines the **driving range prediction** shown to the driver, (ii) it prevents over-charge and over-discharge that damage Li-ion cells, and (iii) it enables the VCU to **limit available power** appropriately to protect the pack.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: What is cell balancing? Differentiate between passive and active cell balancing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Cell balancing** is the process of equalizing the SOC (or voltage) of individual cells in a series-connected battery pack to prevent divergence and maximize usable capacity.
- **Passive balancing** dissipates excess energy from higher-SOC cells as heat through parallel resistors. It is simple, cheap, but wastes energy as heat and is slow.
- **Active balancing** redistributes energy from higher-SOC cells to lower-SOC cells using DC-DC converters, inductors, capacitors, or transformers. It is more efficient (85–95 %) and faster, but more complex and expensive.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Sample Paper Style]

**Q: With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture and functions of a Battery Management System (BMS) used in electric vehicles. Discuss in detail the various SOC estimation methods and their merits/demerits.**

#### Part (a) — Architecture and Functions [7 marks]

**Model Answer:**

A BMS is an electronic system that supervises a rechargeable battery pack. It consists of the following subsystems (refer to the block diagram in Section 4.1):

1. **Sensors and Analog Front-End (AFE):**
   - Cell voltage monitor ICs (e.g., LTC6811, BQ76952) measure each cell voltage with ±1 mV accuracy.
   - A shunt resistor (typically 100 µΩ to 500 µΩ) or a Hall-effect sensor measures pack current with ±0.5 % accuracy.
   - NTC thermistors at 4–16 locations measure temperature.

2. **BMS Microcontroller (MCU):**
   - Runs state-estimation algorithms (SOC/SOH/SOP) at 10–100 Hz.
   - Executes protection logic.
   - Manages communication via CAN.

3. **Power Electronics:**
   - High-side and low-side contactors (typically 400 A continuous, 600 A peak) for pack isolation.
   - Pre-charge circuit (resistor + relay) to limit inrush current when contactors close.
   - Balancing circuits (resistors for passive; inductors/transformers for active).

4. **Thermal Management Subsystem:**
   - Coolant pump, radiator, fan, PTC heater, controlled by the BMS to keep cells in 20–40 °C.

**Primary Functions:**
- Monitoring, state estimation, cell balancing, thermal management, protection, communication.

#### Part (b) — SOC Estimation Methods [7 marks]

| Method | Principle | Merits | Demerits |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Coulomb Counting** | Integrates current over time | Real-time, simple, low compute | Accumulates sensor drift; needs periodic reset |
| **Open-Circuit Voltage (OCV)** | Maps resting voltage to SOC via OCV–SOC curve | Accurate, drift-free | Requires long rest (1–4 h); flat OCV curve in mid-SOC reduces resolution |
| **Kalman Filter (EKF/UKF)** | Model-based observer; combines model + measurements | Self-correcting, robust to noise | High compute; needs accurate cell model |
| **Machine Learning (Neural Nets)** | Learns from training data | Handles non-linearities | Requires huge training dataset; black-box |

**Best practice:** Use **hybrid approach** — Coulomb counting during driving + OCV recalibration during long rest periods.

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q: Explain cell balancing in detail. Derive the energy balance equations for a passive resistor balancer and an active capacitor-shuttle balancer. Compare their efficiencies with numerical examples.**

#### Part (a) — Cell Balancing Concepts and Passive Balancer [7 marks]

Cells in a series pack inevitably diverge in SOC due to manufacturing tolerances (capacity mismatch of ±2–3 %) and temperature gradients. This causes the pack to reach its limits based on the weakest cell, reducing usable capacity by 5–15 %.

**Passive balancing** connects a resistor $R_{bal}$ in parallel with each cell via a switch. When the cell voltage exceeds a threshold, the switch turns on and bleeds current:

$$I_{bal} = \frac{V_{cell}}{R_{bal}}, \quad P_{dissipated} = \frac{V_{cell}^2}{R_{bal}}$$

**Numerical example:** For $V_{cell} = 4.0$ V and $R_{bal} = 33$ Ω, $I_{bal} = 121$ mA and $P_{dissipated} = 0.48$ W. To bleed 1 Ah from a cell takes $\approx 8.3$ hours. The wasted energy is $E = V \cdot Q = 4.0 \times 1 = 4$ Wh per Ah balanced.

**Efficiency = 0 %** (all energy is lost as heat).

#### Part (b) — Active Capacitor-Shuttle Balancer [7 marks]

An active balancer uses a switch network to repeatedly connect a flying capacitor $C_s$ alternately to the highest- and lowest-SOC cells.

**Energy transferred per cycle:**

$$\Delta E = \frac{1}{2} C_s \left(V_H^2 - V_L^2\right)$$

**Numerical example:** $C_s = 100$ µF, $V_H = 4.0$ V, $V_L = 3.9$ V:

$$\Delta E = 0.5 \times 100 \times 10^{-6} \times (16 - 15.21) = 39.5 \text{ µJ per cycle}$$

At 10 kHz switching frequency:

$$P_{transfer} = 39.5 \text{ µJ} \times 10{,}000 = 0.395 \text{ W}$$

To transfer 1 Ah (3600 C) at 4 V average requires 14,400 J. Time required ≈ 14,400 / 0.395 ≈ **10.1 hours** for a single 100 µF cap. Larger capacitors and multi-capacitor arrays reduce this dramatically.

**Efficiency ≈ 85–90 %** (losses due to switch conduction, capacitor ESR, and gate charge).

**Conclusion:** Active balancing is energy-efficient and fast, but passive balancing remains dominant in low-cost EVs (e.g., entry-level two-wheelers) due to its simplicity and cost.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> Students commonly lose marks on Part-B by:
> 1. **Confusing passive vs. active balancing efficiency** — Passive is 0 % *useful* efficiency (all energy is wasted), not "0 % efficient" in the absolute sense.
> 2. **Forgetting to include the pre-charge circuit** in the BMS block diagram (mandatory in KTU 2024 evaluation).
> 3. **Mixing up units** in SOC calculations — Always express $Q$ in Ah, $I$ in A, $t$ in hours for the discrete formula; convert seconds to hours (÷ 3600).
> 4. **Omitting the safety contactor** explanation — Examiners award 1–2 marks specifically for the contactor + pre-charge discussion.
> 5. **Not labeling the Y-axis correctly** in any graph — the SOC vs. Time graph must show % on Y-axis and hours on X-axis.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **BMS = Brain of the battery pack.** Monitors, estimates, balances, protects, communicates.
- **Six core functions** (memorize order): Monitoring → Estimation → Balancing → Thermal → Protection → Communication.
- **Three SOC methods:** Coulomb counting (driving), OCV (rest), Kalman Filter (advanced). Production uses **hybrid**.
- **SOH end-of-life threshold = 80 %** for automotive Li-ion.
- **Cell balancing is necessary** for series packs; passive = cheap/slow, active = efficient/fast.
- **Architecture choices:** Centralized (cheap), distributed (scalable), modular (balanced).
- **Communication:** CAN bus per ISO 11898, UDS diagnostics per ISO 14229.
- **Safety limits (Li-ion, 4.2 V max, 2.5 V min):** OVP at 4.2 V/cell, UVP at 2.5 V/cell, OTP at 60 °C, UTP at -20 °C.
- **Thermal runaway prevention** = BMS's #1 safety job; cooling in 20–40 °C window.
- **State of Power (SOP)** formula: $P_{peak} = V_{min}(V_{oc} - V_{min})/R_{int}$ — used to limit motor torque.
- **Energy transfer** in capacitor shuttle: $\Delta E = \frac{1}{2} C_s (V_H^2 - V_L^2)$.
- **Active balancers achieve 85–95 %** efficiency vs. **0 %** for passive (energy is wasted as heat).
- **Coulomb-counting discrete formula:** $SOC[k+1] = SOC[k] - \eta I[k] \Delta t / (3600 \cdot Q_{rated})$.
- **Pre-charge resistor** prevents inrush current when contactors close — always include in the diagram.
- **Faraday's constant** $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol appears in advanced electrochemistry-based models.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** KTU often pairs a Part-B question on BMS with one on **thermal management of Li-ion cells**. Cross-link the two: explain how the thermal controller (Section 4.1) uses the heat-generation formula $Q_{gen} = I^2 R_{int} + I T (\partial V_{oc}/\partial T)$ from Section 2.5 to predict cell temperature rise and trigger the coolant pump. This integrated answer scores full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Other Storage Topologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET742 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES AND RENEWABLE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Other Storage Topologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Other Storage Topologies** refer to advanced and modular battery/energy storage system (BESS) architectures employed in Electric Vehicles (EVs) and renewable energy applications that go beyond conventional series-parallel (S/P) battery pack configurations. These topologies integrate power electronic interfaces, distributed intelligence, and reconfigurable cell-level interconnections to enhance safety, scalability, efficiency, state-of-charge (SOC) balancing, fault tolerance, and lifetime of the energy storage subsystem.

The principal **Other Storage Topologies** covered under the KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3 syllabus include:

| S.No | Topology | Core Functional Principle |
|------|----------|---------------------------|
| 1 | **Modular Battery Topology with Integrated DC-DC Converters** | Each module has a dedicated non-isolated/isolated converter for voltage regulation and SOC balancing. |
| 2 | **Cell-to-Pack (CTP) / Cell-to-Chassis (CTC)** | Eliminates intermediate module housings; cells directly form the structural pack. |
| 3 | **Cascaded H-Bridge (CHB) Battery Topology** | Multiple H-bridge cells are cascaded to synthesize a multilevel DC output. |
| 4 | **Reconfigurable / Switch-Matrix Battery Topology** | A switch matrix dynamically reconnects cells to bypass faulty ones and balance SOC. |
| 5 | **Battery Swapping Topology (BST)** | Modular packs are physically exchanged at swap stations for rapid turnaround. |
| 6 | **Hybrid Energy Storage System (HESS)** | Combines batteries + supercapacitors (or fuel cells) through a common DC bus. |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 syllabus parlance, "Other Storage Topologies" emphasizes architectures where **power electronics and energy storage are co-designed** rather than treated as independent subsystems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a classroom of **60 students (battery cells)** preparing for a marathon. In a **conventional series-parallel pack**, all students must walk together in a single rigid line. If one student falls sick, the entire line stops. The slow students and the fast students cannot be separated.

In an **"Other Storage Topology"**, the classroom is split into **smaller groups (modules)**, each with its **own coach (DC-DC converter)**. Coaches can adjust the walking pace of their group independently. If one student falls sick, **only that group pauses** — the rest continue. The coaches report to a **head coach (BMS master controller)** who ensures everyone finishes together at the finish line (common DC bus voltage). This is exactly what advanced EV battery topologies achieve!

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Lithium-ion cell nominal voltage:** **3.6 V – 3.7 V**
- **Supercapacitor operating voltage:** **2.5 V – 2.7 V** per cell
- **Faraday's constant (F):** **96,485 C/mol**
- **Pack voltage (typical EV):** **400 V (standard) / 800 V (high-voltage architectures)**
- **Gravimetric energy density target (2025+):** **> 300 Wh/kg**
- **Standard Coulombic efficiency target:** **> 99.5 %**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Analytical Breakdown of Each Topology

### 2.1.1 Modular Battery Topology with Integrated DC-DC Converters

**Operational Logic:**
- A high-voltage battery pack is split into **N low-voltage sub-modules**.
- Each sub-module contains a series string of cells with a dedicated **bidirectional DC-DC converter**.
- The outputs of all converters are connected in **series** (for output-series) or **parallel** (for output-parallel) at the DC link.
- The DC-DC converter regulates its module's terminal voltage to enforce **SOC balancing** and **fault isolation**.

**Why this topology?**
- Eliminates the need for a single massive pack-level converter.
- Allows the use of **lower-voltage, lower-cost cells**.
- Provides **graceful degradation** — faulty modules are bypassed without total pack failure.

### 2.1.2 Cascaded H-Bridge (CHB) Battery Topology

**Operational Logic:**
- Each battery module is interfaced through a **full H-bridge** (4 IGBTs/MOSFETs).
- By phase-shifting PWM signals across cascaded H-bridges, a **multilevel output waveform** is synthesized.
- For DC-link integration, the H-bridge outputs are simply series-connected.
- SOC balancing is performed by modulating the **duty cycle / zero-state insertion** of each H-bridge.

**Why this topology?**
- Produces **sinusoidal-like waveforms** with low harmonic distortion (THD < 5 %).
- Reduces voltage stress on individual switching devices to **V_dc / N**.
- Native bidirectional power flow — ideal for **regenerative braking**.

### 2.1.3 Reconfigurable / Switch-Matrix Battery Topology

**Operational Logic:**
- A **matrix of semiconductor switches (MOSFETs/relays)** sits between individual cells and the load.
- A central BMS controller dynamically reconfigures the cell interconnections (series, parallel, bypass).
- Faulty cells are **electronically bypassed**; healthy cells are **regrouped** to maintain rated voltage.

**Why this topology?**
- Maximizes **pack utilization** despite cell-level failures.
- Enables **online SOC balancing** without energy-wasting resistor dissipation.
- Used in research-grade EVs and aerospace applications (NASA's ROBoTT).

### 2.1.4 Cell-to-Pack (CTP) and Cell-to-Chassis (CTC)

**Operational Logic:**
- **CTP**: Cells are bonded directly into the pack housing using structural adhesives. No intermediate module enclosures.
- **CTC**: The pack serves as a **structural chassis member**, integrating with the vehicle body.

**Why this topology?**
- Increases **volumetric energy density by 15–25 %**.
- Reduces manufacturing cost and part count.
- Pioneered by **BYD (Blade Battery CTP)** and **Tesla (4680 CTC)**.

### 2.1.5 Hybrid Energy Storage System (HESS) Topology

**Operational Logic:**
- Combines **high-energy-density devices (batteries)** with **high-power-density devices (supercapacitors / ultra-capacitors)**.
- Two principal architectures:
  - **Passive HESS** (direct parallel connection).
  - **Active / Semi-active HESS** (with DC-DC converter interfacing one or both sources).
- Energy Management Strategy (EMS) dispatches power based on **dynamic load demand profile**.

**Why this topology?**
- Batteries handle **steady-state cruising loads**; supercapacitors absorb **transient peak loads** (acceleration, regen braking spikes).
- Extends battery cycle life by **5× – 10×**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **directly examinable** in KTU University examinations (Part A / Part B).

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbols / Meaning | Units / Condition |
|---|---------------------|-------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | $V_{pack} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} V_i$ | Series-connected cell voltages | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $I_{pack} = \sum_{j=1}^{M} I_j$ | Parallel branch currents | Amperes (A) |
| 3 | $SOC(t) = SOC_0 - \frac{1}{C_N}\int_{0}^{t} i(\tau)\, d\tau$ | Coulomb counting SOC equation | Dimensionless (%) |
| 4 | $E_{pack} = V_{nom} \times C_{Ah}$ | Pack energy capacity | Wh |
| 5 | $\eta_{col} = \frac{Q_{discharge}}{Q_{charge}} \times 100\%$ | Coulombic efficiency | % |
| 6 | $V_{CHB,total} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} V_{dc,k}$ | CHB output DC link voltage | V |
| 7 | $\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} V_h^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%$ | Total Harmonic Distortion | % |
| 8 | $P_{HESS} = P_{batt} + P_{sc}$ | HESS instantaneous power split | W |
| 9 | $\tau = R_{cell} \times C_{cell}$ | Battery/supercapacitor RC time constant | seconds (s) |
| 10 | $E_{gravity} = \frac{E_{pack}}{m_{pack}}$ | Gravimetric energy density | Wh/kg |
| 11 | $\Delta V_{balance} = \sum_{i=1}^{N}(V_i - \bar{V})$ | Module SOC balancing error | V |
| 12 | $\eta_{conv} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_{loss}}$ | DC-DC converter efficiency | % |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Tesla Model 3 (2024 refresh):** Uses a structural **CTC battery pack** with 4680 cells.
- **BYD Seal / Han:** Employs **CTP Blade Battery** topology with LFP chemistry.
- **BMW iX (Gen5 eDrive):** Uses a **modular pack with integrated DC-DC converters** for 800 V operation.
- **Formula E (Gen3):** Uses a **reconfigurable battery topology** to dynamically redistribute load across cells.
- **Solar PV microgrids:** Use **CHB battery storage** for grid-tie bidirectional power flow.
- **Hybrid Electric Buses (Volvo, BYD):** Use **active HESS** with Li-ion + supercapacitors for stop-go duty cycles.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: SOC Balancing in Modular Battery Topology

Consider a battery pack comprising **N series-connected modules**, each with a dedicated bidirectional DC-DC converter (e.g., a **bidirectional buck-boost**). The aim is to derive the **control law** for SOC balancing.

### Step 1: Define the State of Charge of each module

The state of charge of the $i$-th module is given by the standard Coulomb-counting equation:

$$SOC_i(t) = SOC_i(t_0) - \frac{1}{C_{N,i}}\int_{t_0}^{t} i_i(\tau)\, d\tau$$

where:
- $SOC_i(t_0)$ is the initial SOC of module $i$.
- $C_{N,i}$ is the nominal capacity of module $i$ (in Ah).
- $i_i(\tau)$ is the instantaneous current of module $i$.

### Step 2: Express the SOC deviation

Let the **average pack SOC** be:

$$\bar{SOC}(t) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} SOC_i(t)$$

Then the SOC deviation of module $i$ is:

$$\Delta SOC_i(t) = SOC_i(t) - \bar{SOC}(t)$$

### Step 3: Differentiate to obtain dynamic deviation

Differentiating both sides with respect to time:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\Delta SOC_i(t) = \frac{d}{dt} SOC_i(t) - \frac{1}{N}\sum_{j=1}^{N} \frac{d}{dt} SOC_j(t)$$

Substituting the Coulomb-counting derivative:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\Delta SOC_i(t) = -\frac{i_i(t)}{C_{N,i}} + \frac{1}{N}\sum_{j=1}^{N} \frac{i_j(t)}{C_{N,j}}$$

### Step 4: Apply the assumption of identical nominal capacities

Assume all modules share the same nominal capacity $C_N$ (a reasonable engineering assumption for matched cells):

$$\frac{d}{dt}\Delta SOC_i(t) = -\frac{i_i(t)}{C_N} + \frac{1}{N \cdot C_N}\sum_{j=1}^{N} i_j(t)$$

### Step 5: Express the module current using the converter control variable

For a bidirectional buck-boost converter with duty cycle $d_i$, the average module current relates to the pack (DC-link) current $I_{pack}$ as:

$$i_i(t) = d_i(t) \cdot I_{pack}(t)$$

Substituting into the deviation equation:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\Delta SOC_i(t) = -\frac{d_i(t) \cdot I_{pack}(t)}{C_N} + \frac{I_{pack}(t)}{N \cdot C_N}\sum_{j=1}^{N} d_j(t)$$

### Step 6: Define the proportional balancing controller

A proportional controller with gain $K_p > 0$ assigns duty cycle deviation as:

$$\Delta d_i(t) = K_p \cdot \Delta SOC_i(t)$$

The full duty cycle becomes:

$$d_i(t) = \frac{1}{N} + K_p \cdot \Delta SOC_i(t)$$

### Step 7: Stability check — closed-loop dynamics

Substituting the controller back:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\Delta SOC_i(t) = -\frac{K_p \cdot \Delta SOC_i(t) \cdot I_{pack}(t)}{C_N} + \frac{I_{pack}(t)}{N \cdot C_N}\sum_{j=1}^{N} K_p \cdot \Delta SOC_j(t)$$

By definition, $\sum_{j=1}^{N} \Delta SOC_j(t) = 0$, so the last term vanishes. Therefore:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\Delta SOC_i(t) = -\frac{K_p \cdot I_{pack}(t)}{C_N}\Delta SOC_i(t)$$

This is a **first-order linear homogeneous ODE** with solution:

$$\Delta SOC_i(t) = \Delta SOC_i(0) \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{K_p \cdot I_{pack}(t)}{C_N}\, t\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The exponent is negative for $K_p > 0$ and $I_{pack} > 0$ (discharge mode), proving **exponential convergence** of SOC deviation to zero. The convergence time constant is:

$$\tau_{balance} = \frac{C_N}{K_p \cdot I_{pack}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Higher proportional gain $K_p$ and higher pack current $I_{pack}$ both **accelerate SOC convergence**. Designers must, however, ensure $K_p$ does not saturate the converter duty cycle ($0 \le d_i \le 1$).

## 3.2 Derivation: HESS Power Split using Frequency Decoupling

For an active HESS, the battery handles **low-frequency power** and the supercapacitor handles **high-frequency power**. Using a **low-pass filter** with cutoff frequency $\omega_c$:

$$P_{batt}(s) = \frac{\omega_c}{s + \omega_c}\, P_{load}(s)$$

$$P_{sc}(s) = P_{load}(s) - P_{batt}(s) = \frac{s}{s + \omega_c}\, P_{load}(s)$$

For a step load change $P_{load}(t) = P_0 \cdot u(t)$ (where $u(t)$ is the unit step):

$$P_{batt}(t) = P_0 \left(1 - e^{-\omega_c t}\right)$$

$$P_{sc}(t) = P_0 \cdot e^{-\omega_c t}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> At $t = 0$, the supercapacitor absorbs **100 % of the transient**, protecting the battery from peak current stress. As $t \to \infty$, the battery provides **100 % of the steady-state load**.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Topology | Energy Density | Power Density | Safety | Cost | Complexity | Cycle Life |
|----------|:--------------:|:-------------:|:------:|:----:|:----------:|:----------:|
| Conventional S/P Pack | Medium | Medium | Low | Low | Low | Medium |
| Modular DC-DC | Medium | High | High | High | High | High |
| CHB Multilevel | Low | High | High | High | Very High | High |
| Reconfigurable Switch-Matrix | High | Medium | Very High | Very High | Very High | Very High |
| CTP / CTC | Very High | Medium | High | Low | Low | Medium |
| Active HESS | High | Very High | High | High | High | Very High |
