# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Set A]`
**Q: Define Cell-to-Pack (CTP) topology. List two advantages over conventional modular battery packs.**

**Model Answer:**
> [!NOTE]
> **Cell-to-Pack (CTP)** is an advanced battery packaging architecture in which individual cells are bonded directly into the pack enclosure without intermediate module housings. The cells themselves — together with structural adhesives and integrated cooling plates — form the load-bearing structure of the pack.

**Advantages (any two):**
1. **Higher volumetric energy density** (typically 15–25 % gain) due to elimination of empty module enclosures.
2. **Reduced manufacturing cost and part count** — fewer connectors, busbars, and housings.
3. **Improved thermal management** through direct cell-to-coolant contact.
4. **Lighter weight** due to structural simplification.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often confuse **CTP with CTC**. CTC goes further — the pack itself becomes a **structural chassis member** of the vehicle. In CTP, the pack is still a separate unit.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Set B]`
**Q: What is an active Hybrid Energy Storage System (HESS)? Explain the role of the Energy Management Strategy (EMS).**

**Model Answer:**
An **active HESS** is a topology that combines two or more energy storage devices (typically a **battery** for high energy density and a **supercapacitor** for high power density) through dedicated **bidirectional DC-DC converters** interfacing a common DC bus.

**Role of EMS:**
- The EMS is a **real-time control algorithm** that decides, at each instant, how much power is drawn from / supplied to each storage device.
- It uses **frequency decoupling** (low-pass filter) or **rule-based / optimization-based** strategies to allocate:
  - **Low-frequency, steady-state power → Battery**
  - **High-frequency, transient power → Supercapacitor**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Do **not** describe HESS as merely "two batteries in parallel." The defining feature of an **active** HESS is the presence of at least one **bidirectional DC-DC converter** enabling independent power flow control.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3 Choice A]`

**Q: With a neat block diagram, explain the Modular Battery Topology with integrated bidirectional DC-DC converters. Derive the SOC balancing control law and prove that SOC deviations converge exponentially.**

**Sub-Part (a) — 7 Marks: Architecture and Operation**
**Sub-Part (b) — 7 Marks: Derivation of Balancing Control Law**

---

#### Model Solution to Sub-Part (a):

The **Modular Battery Topology** divides a high-voltage EV battery pack into **N lower-voltage sub-modules**, each interfaced to the common DC-link via a dedicated **bidirectional DC-DC converter** (typically a buck-boost or dual-active bridge).

**Key components:**
1. **Battery Sub-Modules:** Series strings of cells within each module, each rated to a fraction of the total pack voltage.
2. **Bidirectional DC-DC Converters:** Allow power flow in both directions (charge and discharge).
3. **Common DC-Link Bus:** Aggregate point connecting all converters to the traction inverter.
4. **BMS Master Controller:** Senses SOC of each module and adjusts duty cycles to enforce balancing.

**Advantages over conventional S/P packs:**
- **Fault isolation:** A faulty module is bypassed without taking down the entire pack.
- **Asymmetric SOC balancing:** Slower and faster modules can be individually trimmed.
- **Modular scalability:** Adding more modules increases pack voltage in well-defined increments.
- **Reduced voltage stress** on individual switching devices.

(Refer to **Diagram 4.1** in Section 4 for the block schematic.)

---

#### Model Solution to Sub-Part (b):

Following the derivation in Section 3.1:

**Step 1 — SOC deviation definition:**

$$\Delta SOC_i(t) = SOC_i(t) - \frac{1}{N}\sum_{j=1}^{N} SOC_j(t)$$

**Step 2 — Module current model:**

$$i_i(t) = d_i(t) \cdot I_{pack}(t)$$

**Step 3 — Closed-loop dynamics with proportional controller $d_i = \frac{1}{N} + K_p \cdot \Delta SOC_i$:**

$$\frac{d}{dt}\Delta SOC_i(t) = -\frac{K_p \cdot I_{pack}(t)}{C_N}\Delta SOC_i(t)$$

**Step 4 — Solution:**

$$\Delta SOC_i(t) = \Delta SOC_i(0) \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{K_p \cdot I_{pack}(t)}{C_N}\, t\right)$$

**Conclusion:** The SOC deviation **decays exponentially** with time constant $\tau_{balance} = \frac{C_N}{K_p \cdot I_{pack}}$, proving **asymptotic convergence to zero** and thus successful SOC balancing across all N modules.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3 Choice B]` *(Alternative to Question A)*

**Q: Explain the Cascaded H-Bridge (CHB) battery storage topology. With relevant equations, show how phase-shifted PWM enables multilevel DC-link voltage synthesis and SOC balancing. List two real-world applications.**

**Sub-Part (a) — 7 Marks: CHB Topology & PWM Synthesis**
**Sub-Part (b) — 7 Marks: SOC Balancing Mechanism & Applications**

---

#### Model Solution to Sub-Part (a):

In a **Cascaded H-Bridge (CHB)** topology, **N sub-modules** are connected in series. Each sub-module consists of:
- A **battery string** (e.g., 12–24 cells).
- A **full H-bridge** with 4 active switches (IGBTs or SiC MOSFETs).

**Phase-Shifted PWM Principle:**
Each H-bridge is switched using a **triangular carrier wave** that is phase-shifted by an angle:

$$\phi_k = \frac{2\pi (k-1)}{N}, \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, N$$

The resulting **DC-link output voltage** is the sum of all H-bridge contributions:

$$V_{CHB} = \sum_{k=1}^{N} V_{dc,k} \cdot d_k(t)$$

where $d_k(t)$ is the instantaneous duty cycle of the $k$-th H-bridge.

**Multilevel Synthesis:**
The composite output presents **2N + 1 distinct voltage levels** at the DC link, dramatically reducing:
- **Voltage ripple** ($\Delta V \propto \frac{1}{N}$)
- **Current THD** ($\text{THD} \propto \frac{1}{N^2}$)

---

#### Model Solution to Sub-Part (b):

**SOC Balancing via Zero-State Insertion:**

For modules with higher SOC, a **higher zero-state dwell time** is inserted, reducing the effective discharge. The duty cycle modulation becomes:

$$d_k(t) = d_{avg} + K_{soc} \cdot \left[ SOC_k(t) - \bar{SOC}(t) \right]$$

where $K_{soc}$ is the balancing gain and $d_{avg}$ is the average duty cycle. This re-distributes charge across modules naturally.

**Real-World Applications:**
1. **Battery-STATCOM systems** for grid-tied renewable energy storage.
2. **Solid-State Transformer (SST)** based EV fast-charging stations (e.g., 800 V architectures).
3. **Electric ship propulsion** with high-voltage DC distribution.
4. **Wind farm energy storage** with grid-side power quality conditioning.

(Refer to **Diagram 4.2** in Section 4 for the schematic.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in "Other Storage Topologies" Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing CTP with CTC** — These are distinct topologies (1–2 marks lost frequently).
> 2. **Forgetting to draw the bidirectional DC-DC converter** in modular topology diagrams — examiners deduct 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Skipping the "Why" behind SOC balancing** — KTU 2024 expects justification, not just equations.
> 4. **Ignoring converter efficiency** — Always mention η_conv and its typical range (94–98 %).
> 5. **Writing HESS as passive parallel** without DC-DC converters — wrong topology definition.
> 6. **Not labelling axes / current directions** in energy flow diagrams — **2-mark deduction**.
> 7. **Confusing gravimetric vs volumetric energy density** units (Wh/kg vs Wh/L).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for "Other Storage Topologies"**

- **Modular Battery Topology:** Splits pack into N modules with dedicated bidirectional DC-DC converters. SOC balancing achieved via duty-cycle modulation. Convergence time constant: $\tau_{balance} = \frac{C_N}{K_p \cdot I_{pack}}$.
- **Cascaded H-Bridge (CHB):** Phase-shifted PWM synthesizes multilevel DC output. 2N+1 voltage levels. Each device sees only $V_{dc}/N$ stress.
- **Reconfigurable Switch-Matrix:** Dynamic cell reconnection via MOSFET/relay arrays. Maximum fault tolerance but highest control complexity.
- **Cell-to-Pack (CTP):** Cells bonded directly into pack — **+15–25 % volumetric energy density**. Used by **BYD Blade Battery**.
- **Cell-to-Chassis (CTC):** Pack is structural chassis member. Used by **Tesla 4680**.
- **Active HESS:** Battery + supercapacitor with bidirectional converters. **Low-pass filter** splits power by frequency. Battery absorbs **steady-state**; supercapacitor absorbs **transients**.
- **Battery Swapping Topology:** Modular packs physically exchanged. Sub-5-minute turnaround. Commercial: **NIO Power Swap Stations**.
- **Standard cell voltage:** **3.6–3.7 V (Li-ion nominal)**.
- **Coulombic efficiency target:** **> 99.5 %**.
- **DC-DC converter efficiency range:** **94 % – 98 %** (SiC MOSFETs reach 98.5 %+).
- **SOC equation:** $SOC(t) = SOC_0 - \frac{1}{C_N}\int i(\tau)\,d\tau$.
- **Pack energy:** $E_{pack} = V_{nom} \times C_{Ah}$ in Wh.
- **Typical EV pack voltages:** **400 V (standard) / 800 V (high-performance)**.
- **CHB THD scaling:** $\text{THD} \propto \frac{1}{N^2}$.
- **KTU 2024 favorite exam phrasing:** "Explain with block diagram," "Derive the control law," "Compare with conventional topology."
- **Real-world case studies to memorize:** Tesla 4680 CTC, BYD Blade CTP, BMW iX 800 V modular, NIO battery swap.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fuel Cell Based Energy Storage Systems - Supercapacitors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET742 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES AND RENEWABLE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fuel Cell Based Energy Storage Systems - Supercapacitors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Supercapacitor** (also termed an **Ultracapacitor** or **Electric Double-Layer Capacitor, EDLC**) is an electrochemical energy storage device that stores electrical energy through the reversible adsorption of electrolyte ions onto high-surface-area porous carbon electrodes, forming an electric double layer at the electrode–electrolyte interface. Unlike conventional electrostatic capacitors, supercapacitors exploit the very small (nanometer-scale) charge separation distance within this interfacial double layer to achieve capacitances ranging from **farads to several thousand farads** in a compact volume.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Standard Terminology):** "A supercapacitor is a high-capacity electrochemical capacitor with capacitance values much higher than standard capacitors (typically 1 F to 10,000 F) that bridge the gap between electrolytic capacitors and rechargeable batteries. They store energy through electrostatic double-layer capacitance and/or faradaic pseudocapacitance."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Sponges of Electricity":**
> Think of a supercapacitor as a **highway with millions of parallel parking lanes** at the interface between two surfaces (electrodes) and the electrolyte. 
> - A **regular capacitor** is like a single small parking lot — it holds only a few cars (charges) because the parking lot (surface) is small.
> - A **battery** is like a deep underground parking garage where cars (charges) are stored through a slow, chemical "check-in" process.
> - A **supercapacitor** is like millions of tiny parking spots formed at the junction of the electrode surface and the electrolyte. Charges (ions) "park" themselves electrostatically at this junction almost **instantly**, with no slow chemical reaction. This makes supercapacitors extremely **fast to charge/discharge** but the parking is *physical*, not *chemical*, so total energy is less than a battery of similar size.

**Real-world analogy for electric vehicles:** In a hybrid EV, a supercapacitor is the equivalent of a **sprinter's muscle** — it gives a **burst of power** (acceleration, regenerative braking spike) but is not designed for **long-distance endurance** (which is the battery's role).

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Industry Standards)

| Metric | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Capacitance (C)** | 1 – 10,000 | F (Farads) |
| **Cell Voltage** | 1.0 – 3.0 | V |
| **Specific Energy** | 1 – 10 | Wh/kg |
| **Specific Power** | 1,000 – 10,000 | W/kg |
| **Cycle Life** | > 500,000 – 1,000,000 | cycles |
| **Charge Time** | 0.1 – 30 | seconds |
| **Operating Temperature** | −40 to +85 | °C |
| **Equivalent Series Resistance (ESR)** | 0.1 – 100 | mΩ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Highlight:** The charge-separation distance in a supercapacitor's electric double layer is on the order of **0.3 – 0.8 nm**, which is comparable to the **ionic radius of the electrolyte ions**. This extremely small separation is the *physical reason* supercapacitors achieve such colossal capacitance values.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — The Electric Double Layer

When a voltage is applied across two porous carbon electrodes immersed in an electrolyte, ions of opposite polarity migrate to the electrode surfaces. A layered ionic structure forms at each electrode:

1. **Inner Helmholtz Plane (IHP):** A layer of specifically adsorbed ions (dehydrated) directly touching the electrode.
2. **Outer Helmholtz Plane (OHP):** A layer of solvated (hydrated) ions forming the boundary of the diffuse layer.
3. **Diffuse Layer:** A gradual distribution of ions governed by thermal motion (Gouy-Chapman model).

The combination of these layers creates two capacitors in series at each electrode — a **compact (Helmholtz) capacitor** and a **diffuse (Gouy-Chapman) capacitor**. The total capacitance is dominated by the smaller of the two, which in concentrated electrolytes is the **Helmholtz capacitance**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the supercapacitor store so much energy?** Because capacitance is inversely proportional to charge-separation distance ($C = \varepsilon A / d$), and in an EDLC the distance $d$ is the ionic radius (~0.5 nm) — **1000× smaller** than the dielectric thickness in a conventional film capacitor — and the surface area $A$ is enormous due to activated carbon's porosity (~1,500 – 3,000 m²/g).

### 2.2 Types of Supercapacitors

**A. Electric Double-Layer Capacitors (EDLCs)** — Pure electrostatic storage, no chemical reaction. Carbon-based electrodes (activated carbon, carbon nanotubes, graphene). 

**B. Pseudocapacitors** — Fast, reversible faradaic redox reactions (electron transfer) at the electrode surface. Electrode materials: transition metal oxides (RuO₂, MnO₂), conducting polymers (polyaniline, PEDOT). 

**C. Hybrid Supercapacitors** — Asymmetric design combining a battery-type electrode (faradaic) with a capacitor-type electrode (non-faradaic), e.g., **Li-ion capacitor (LIC)**. This bridges the energy density of batteries and the power density of capacitors.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA TABLE FOR KTU EXAMS — Memorize Every Row.**

| # | Formula | Description | Unit | Validity / Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | Parallel-plate capacitance model | F | $d$ = charge-separation distance |
| 2 | $C_{EDLC} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_{EDL}}$ | EDLC capacitance (using double-layer distance) | F | $d_{EDL} \approx 0.3 - 0.8$ nm |
| 3 | $E = \dfrac{1}{2} C V^{2}$ | Energy stored in a capacitor | J | $V$ = operating voltage |
| 4 | $E_{specific} = \dfrac{1}{2} C V^{2} / m$ | Gravimetric energy density | Wh/kg | $m$ = mass of supercapacitor |
| 5 | $E_{volumetric} = \dfrac{1}{2} C V^{2} / V_{cell}$ | Volumetric energy density | Wh/L | $V_{cell}$ = cell volume |
| 6 | $P_{max} = \dfrac{V^{2}}{4 \cdot ESR}$ | Maximum deliverable power | W | Occurs at matched impedance |
| 7 | $P_{specific} = \dfrac{V^{2}}{4 \cdot ESR \cdot m}$ | Specific power | W/kg | Standard Ragone metric |
| 8 | $C_{cell} = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{1}{C_1} + \dfrac{1}{C_2}}$ | Two capacitors in series | F | $C_1 = C_2$ for symmetric device |
| 9 | $C_{bank} = C_1 + C_2 + \dots + C_n$ | Capacitors in parallel | F | Increases total capacitance |
| 10 | $V_{bank} = V_1 + V_2 + \dots + V_n$ | Cells in series (voltage addition) | V | Must include voltage balancing |
| 11 | $E_{bank} = \dfrac{1}{2} C_{bank} V_{bank}^{2}$ | Energy of an assembled bank | J | Use bank totals |
| 12 | $\tau_{RC} = R_{load} \cdot C$ | RC time constant | s | Governs charge/discharge rate |
| 13 | $V(t) = V_0 \, e^{-t / (R_{ESR} C)}$ | Voltage decay during self-discharge | V | Simplified first-order model |
| 14 | $\eta_{coulombic} = \dfrac{Q_{discharge}}{Q_{charge}} \times 100\%$ | Coulombic efficiency | % | $\approx 95$–$99\%$ for EDLCs |
| 15 | $t_{discharge} = \dfrac{R_{load} \cdot C \cdot \ln(V_{i}/V_{f})}{1}$ | Discharge time (constant R) | s | $V_i, V_f$ = initial/final voltage |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** In KTU-aligned EV design, the **Ragone plot** (log-log plot of specific energy vs specific power) is the single most important visualization tool. A supercapacitor sits in the **high-power, moderate-energy** quadrant — this directly justifies its use as a **peak-power buffer** alongside batteries in hybrid energy storage systems (HESS) for EVs.

### 2.4 Engineering Significance for Electric Vehicles

Supercapacitors in EVs are used for:
- **Regenerative Braking Energy Recovery** — captures fast high-current braking pulses that would damage a battery.
- **Acceleration Boost** — supplies peak current during hard acceleration (motor requires 3–5× rated current briefly).
- **Cold-Start Power** — operates at −40°C with minimal loss, unlike Li-ion batteries.
- **Battery Life Extension** — shaves peak current pulses, reducing battery stress and extending cycle life by 2–3×.
- **Voltage Stabilization** — maintains DC bus voltage during transient loads.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Energy Stored in a Supercapacitor

Starting from the fundamental definition of capacitance and the work done to charge a capacitor:

**Step 1:** Define capacitance as the ratio of charge to voltage:
$$C = \frac{Q}{V}$$

**Step 2:** Express the incremental work done to add a small charge $dq$ at a voltage $v$ across the capacitor:
$$dW = v \, dq$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $v = q/C$ (from Step 1, rearranged) into the work expression:
$$dW = \frac{q}{C} \, dq$$

**Step 4:** Integrate from $q = 0$ to $q = Q$ to obtain the total energy stored:
$$W = \int_{0}^{Q} \frac{q}{C} \, dq = \frac{1}{C} \left[ \frac{q^{2}}{2} \right]_{0}^{Q} = \frac{Q^{2}}{2C}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $Q = C V$ back into the result:
$$W = \frac{(CV)^{2}}{2C} = \frac{1}{2} C V^{2}$$

**Step 6:** Convert to gravimetric energy density by dividing by mass $m$:
$$E_{specific} = \frac{1}{2} \frac{C V^{2}}{m} \quad [\text{J/kg}]$$

**Step 7:** Convert joules to watt-hours using $1 \text{ Wh} = 3600 \text{ J}$:
$$E_{specific} = \frac{C V^{2}}{7.2 \cdot 10^{3} \, m} \quad [\text{Wh/kg}]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The energy stored scales with the **square of voltage**, which is why the supercapacitor industry is aggressively pursuing **high-voltage electrolytes** (ionic liquids, organic electrolytes in ACN/PC solvents) to push $V$ from 2.7 V → 3.0 V → future 3.5 V+.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Power Transfer Theorem Applied to a Supercapacitor

A real supercapacitor can be modelled as an ideal capacitor $C$ in series with its **Equivalent Series Resistance (ESR)**. When connected to an external load $R_L$, the question is: *At what load resistance is the power delivered to the load maximum?*

**Step 1:** Write the closed-loop current from the capacitor (initial voltage $V_0$) through ESR + load:
$$I = \frac{V_0}{R_{ESR} + R_L}$$

**Step 2:** Power delivered to the **load** (not to the ESR) is:
$$P_L = I^{2} R_L = \frac{V_0^{2} \, R_L}{(R_{ESR} + R_L)^{2}}$$

**Step 3:** Maximize $P_L$ with respect to $R_L$ by differentiating and setting to zero:
$$\frac{d P_L}{d R_L} = V_0^{2} \cdot \frac{(R_{ESR} + R_L)^{2} - R_L \cdot 2(R_{ESR} + R_L)}{(R_{ESR} + R_L)^{4}} = 0$$

**Step 4:** Simplify the numerator to find the optimum:
$$(R_{ESR} + R_L) - 2 R_L = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_L = R_{ESR}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $R_L = R_{ESR}$ into the power expression:
$$P_{max} = \frac{V_0^{2} \cdot R_{ESR}}{(2 R_{ESR})^{2}} = \frac{V_0^{2}}{4 R_{ESR}}$$

**Step 6:** This is the **Maximum Power Transfer Theorem** applied to a supercapacitor. Dividing by mass $m$:
$$P_{specific} = \frac{V_0^{2}}{4 \cdot ESR \cdot m} \quad [\text{W/kg}]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Takeaway:** Maximum power occurs when **load resistance equals ESR**. This is the **physical reason** EV designers measure ESR meticulously — it sets the *ceiling* on how much peak power a supercapacitor can deliver to the motor inverter.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Charge/Discharge Time for a Supercapacitor Driving a Constant Load

**Step 1:** For a capacitor $C$ discharging through a constant resistance $R_L$, KVL gives:
$$V_C(t) = V_0 \, e^{-t / (R_L C)}$$

**Step 2:** The current in the circuit is:
$$I(t) = \frac{V_0}{R_L} e^{-t / (R_L C)}$$

**Step 3:** Power delivered to the load at time $t$ is:
$$P(t) = I^{2}(t) R_L = \frac{V_0^{2}}{R_L} e^{-2t / (R_L C)}$$

**Step 4:** The total energy delivered to the load is the integral of power:
$$E_{out} = \int_{0}^{\infty} P(t) \, dt = \frac{V_0^{2}}{R_L} \cdot \frac{R_L C}{2} = \frac{1}{2} C V_0^{2}$$

**Step 5:** This confirms **100% of the stored energy is dissipated in the load** (and in the capacitor's ESR) — there is no "wasted" chemical intermediate, unlike a battery where internal losses are inherent to reaction kinetics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for EVs:** Because supercapacitors release (almost) all of their stored energy on demand with low internal loss, the round-trip efficiency of regenerative braking with a supercapacitor HESS can exceed **90%**, compared to **60–70%** for a battery-only system.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Supercapacitor vs Battery vs Conventional Capacitor

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This comparison table is a KTU 2024 favourite for 14-mark questions.**

| Parameter | Conventional Capacitor | **Supercapacitor (EDLC)** | Li-ion Battery |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy Storage Mechanism | Electrostatic (dielectric) | Electrostatic (double layer) + Faradaic (pseudocapacitor) | Electrochemical (redox) |
| Charge Time | $10^{-6}$ – $10^{-3}$ s | 0.1 – 30 s | 1 – 5 hours |
| Discharge Time | $10^{-6}$ – $10^{-3}$ s | 0.1 – 30 s | 0.3 – 3 hours |
| Specific Energy (Wh/kg) | < 0.1 | 1 – 10 | 100 – 250 |
| Specific Power (W/kg) | > 10,000 | 1,000 – 10,000 | 100 – 1,000 |
| Cycle Life | > 1,000,000 | > 500,000 | 500 – 2,000 |
| Cell Voltage (V) | 6 – 800 | 1 – 3 | 3.2 – 3.7 |
| Operating Temperature (°C) | −40 to +125 | −40 to +85 | −20 to +60 |
| Self-Discharge | Very low | Moderate (weeks) | Low (months) |
| Capital Cost per kWh | Low | Moderate | High |
| Environmental Impact | Low | Low–Moderate | Moderate–High |

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A 2.7 V, 3000 F supercapacitor with ESR = 0.5 mΩ is fully charged and discharged through a 1 Ω load. Find: (a) the energy stored, (b) the maximum power deliverable, and (c) the time constant of the discharge.

**Solution:**

**(a) Energy stored:**
$$E = \frac{1}{2} C V^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 3000 \times (2.7)^{2} = 10,935 \text{ J} \approx 10.94 \text{ kJ}$$

**(b) Maximum power deliverable (matched load):**
$$P_{max} = \frac{V_0^{2}}{4 \cdot ESR} = \frac{(2.7)^{2}}{4 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{7.29}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 3,645 \text{ W} \approx 3.65 \text{ kW}$$

**(c) RC time constant of discharge:**
$$\tau = R_L \times C = 1 \times 3000 = 3000 \text{ s} = 50 \text{ minutes}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for KTU answer writing:** Even with a relatively small load (1 Ω), the supercapacitor takes ~50 minutes to reach 36.8% of its initial voltage. This shows that the energy is *accessible* quickly (high power) but the *delivery over time* is moderate — perfectly fitting the EV regenerative-braking use case.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Physical Structure of a Supercapacitor Cell

The following block diagram illustrates the layered architecture of a wound (jelly-roll) supercapacitor cell.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Terminal 1 Positive] --> B[Aluminum Current Collector Positive]
    B --> C[Activated Carbon Electrode Positive]
    C --> D[Porous Separator with Liquid Electrolyte]
    D --> E[Activated Carbon Electrode Negative]
    E --> F[Aluminum Current Collector Negative]
    F --> G[Terminal 2 Negative]
    H[Helmholtz Double Layer Interface] -.-> C
    H -.-> E
    I[Organic Electrolyte KOH or ACN based salt] -.-> D
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Figure 1 Caption:** Schematic of a symmetric activated-carbon EDLC supercapacitor showing current collectors, porous electrodes, separator soaked with electrolyte, and the charge-separation double layer at each electrode interface.

---

### 4.2 Equivalent Circuit Model of a Real Supercapacitor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Terminal A] --> B[ESR Effective Series Resistance]
    B --> C[C Main Capacitance Farads]
    C --> D[Terminal B]
    E[Rp Parallel Self Discharge Resistance] -.-> A
    E -.-> D
    F[L Equivalent Inductance nH range] -.-> B
    G[External Load R_L] -.-> A
    G -.-> D
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Figure 2 Caption:** The standard first-order equivalent circuit used by KTU examiners. **C** is the main energy-storage element; **ESR** limits peak power; **R_p** models the slow self-discharge leakage; **L** models the high-frequency parasitic inductance of the wound structure.

---

### 4.3 Hybrid Energy Storage System (HESS) Block Architecture for EVs

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[DC Bus of Electric Vehicle Inverter] --> B[Power Splitting Controller DC DC Converter]
    B --> C[Supercapacitor Bank for Peak Power]
    B --> D[Li ion Battery Pack for Bulk Energy]
    C --> E[Regenerative Braking Energy Recovery]
    D --> F[Long Range Cruise Energy Supply]
    E --> B
    F --> B
    G[Vehicle Acceleration Demand] --> B
    H[Braking Event Detection] --> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Figure 3 Caption:** Block diagram of a HESS in an EV. The supercapacitor handles *high-power transient events* (acceleration, braking), while the Li-ion battery handles *long-duration steady-state load*. The DC-DC converter performs intelligent power splitting — this is a high-yield KTU 2024 topic.

---

### 4.4 Energy Storage Classification — Ragone Plot (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Conventional Capacitor High Power Low Energy] --> B[Supercapacitor Moderate Energy High Power]
    B --> C[Hybrid Supercapacitor Higher Energy]
    C --> D[Li ion Battery High Energy Moderate Power]
    D --> E[Fuel Cell Highest Energy Slow Response]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Figure 4 Caption:** Position of supercapacitors in the energy storage hierarchy. As one moves right, *specific energy increases* but *specific power decreases* and *response time lengthens*. The supercapacitor occupies the "high-power" bridge between conventional capacitors and batteries — exactly the niche that EV peak-power applications demand.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define supercapacitor. What is the role of the electric double layer in its operation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A supercapacitor is an electrochemical energy storage device that stores electrical energy using the **electric double layer (EDL)** — a layer of electrolyte ions formed at the interface between a high-surface-area porous carbon electrode and the electrolyte — to achieve very high capacitance (1 F to 10,000 F). The **role of the EDL** is to provide an extremely small charge-separation distance (typically **0.3 – 0.8 nm**, on the order of the ionic radius) which, when combined with the enormous electrode surface area (~1,500 – 3,000 m²/g for activated carbon), produces a capacitance that is **1,000 to 10,000 times higher** than that of a conventional capacitor. (1 mark definition, 1 mark EDL mechanism, 1 mark numerical/quantitative justification)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] List any three advantages of supercapacitors over Li-ion batteries in EV applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks, 1 mark each):**

1. **Very high specific power** (1,000 – 10,000 W/kg) — ideal for capturing the brief, intense current pulses of regenerative braking and acceleration boosts.
2. **Extremely long cycle life** (>500,000 cycles) — orders of magnitude more charge–discharge cycles than Li-ion batteries, lowering lifetime replacement cost.
3. **Wide operating temperature range** (−40 °C to +85 °C) — performs reliably in cold-start and high-heat conditions where batteries suffer capacity loss or safety risks.

*(Bonus acceptable answer: faster charging — seconds vs hours; lower internal resistance / higher round-trip efficiency.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Q3(a). [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat schematic, explain the construction and working principle of an Electric Double-Layer Capacitor (EDLC). Compare it with a conventional parallel-plate capacitor.

**OR**

**(b) [7 marks]** Derive the expression for the energy stored in a supercapacitor. A 2.7 V, 3000 F supercapacitor with ESR = 0.5 mΩ is fully charged. Calculate (i) the energy stored, (ii) the maximum power deliverable under matched load, and (iii) the time required to discharge to 37% of its initial voltage through a 1 Ω load.

**Model Answer for Q3(a):**

**Construction (3 marks):**
An EDLC consists of two symmetric **activated carbon electrodes** (porosity ~1,500–3,000 m²/g), each coated onto an **aluminum current collector**. A **porous separator** soaked with **liquid electrolyte** (e.g., KOH aqueous or organic salt in acetonitrile) is sandwiched between the electrodes. The whole assembly is hermetically sealed. Schematic reference: Figure 1 in Section 4.

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
When a voltage is applied, ions in the electrolyte migrate toward the oppositely charged electrode. Two compact layers of opposite charge form at each electrode-electrolyte interface (Helmholtz double layer). The capacitance of this double layer is $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d_{EDL}$, where $d_{EDL} \approx 0.5$ nm. Because $d_{EDL}$ is **~1000× smaller** than the dielectric thickness in a conventional capacitor and $A$ is enormous due to porosity, the resulting capacitance is in farads (not microfarads).

**Comparison (1 mark):**

| Feature | Conventional Capacitor | EDLC |
|---|---|---|
| Charge separation | Solid dielectric | Electric double layer |
| Distance $d$ | µm scale | ~0.5 nm |
| Capacitance | pF to µF | F to kF |
| Energy storage | Electrostatic only | Electrostatic (EDL) |

---

**Model Answer for Q3(b):**

**(i) Energy stored (2 marks):** Using $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^{2}$:

$$E = \frac{1}{2} \times 3000 \times (2.7)^{2} = 10{,}935 \text{ J} \approx 10.94 \text{ kJ}$$

**(ii) Maximum power under matched load (2 marks):** Using $P_{max} = V^{2} / (4 \cdot ESR)$:

$$P_{max} = \frac{(2.7)^{2}}{4 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{7.29}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 3{,}645 \text{ W} \approx 3.65 \text{ kW}$$

**(iii) Time to discharge to 37% of initial voltage (3 marks):** The voltage follows $V(t) = V_0 e^{-t / (R_L C)}$. At $V(t) = 0.37 V_0$ (i.e., $e^{-1}$):

$$t = R_L \cdot C = 1 \, \Omega \times 3000 \, \text{F} = 3000 \text{ s} = 50 \text{ minutes}$$

**Derivation recap (for context, embedded in explanation):** From $V(t) = V_0 e^{-t/RC}$, the stored energy is found by integrating the load power: $E_{out} = \int_0^\infty \frac{V_0^2}{R_L} e^{-2t/RC} dt = \tfrac{1}{2} C V_0^2$.

---

**Q4(a). [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the role of supercapacitors in **regenerative braking** and **acceleration boost** in electric vehicles. With a block diagram, describe a Hybrid Energy Storage System (HESS) combining a Li-ion battery and a supercapacitor bank.

**OR**

**(b) [7 marks]** Draw and explain the **Ragone plot** for energy storage devices. Position the supercapacitor, Li-ion battery, fuel cell, and conventional capacitor on this plot and justify the use of supercapacitors in EVs based on this plot.

**Model Answer for Q4(a):**

**Regenerative braking role (2 marks):** During braking, the motor acts as a generator and produces a high-current pulse (potentially 5–10× the steady-state current) for a short duration. Batteries degrade under such high-C-rate charging; supercapacitors accept this pulse efficiently with **>90% round-trip efficiency**, storing the energy for the next acceleration.

**Acceleration boost role (2 marks):** When the driver demands sharp acceleration, the motor inverter needs a short burst of high current. The supercapacitor bank supplies this peak current in parallel with the battery, reducing the battery's peak stress and effectively doubling the system's available power for a few seconds.

**HESS Block Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to Figure 3 in Section 4. The HESS uses a DC-DC converter to intelligently route power between the Li-ion battery (steady-state energy supply) and the supercapacitor bank (transient peak power).

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The HESS architecture extends the battery's cycle life by 2–3× and increases the vehicle's overall energy efficiency by 10–20%.

---

**Model Answer for Q4(b):**

**Ragone plot description (2 marks):** A Ragone plot is a log-log plot of **specific energy (Wh/kg)** on the x-axis versus **specific power (W/kg)** on the y-axis. It compares the energy-power trade-off of various energy storage technologies. Lines of constant *discharge time* ($\tau = E/P$) are diagonals.

**Positioning (3 marks):**
- **Conventional capacitor:** bottom-right (very high power ~10⁵ W/kg, very low energy ~0.01 Wh/kg).
- **Supercapacitor:** mid-right (high power ~10³–10⁴ W/kg, moderate energy ~5 Wh/kg).
- **Li-ion battery:** mid-left (moderate power ~200 W/kg, high energy ~150 Wh/kg).
- **Fuel cell:** far-left (low power ~50 W/kg, very high energy ~500 Wh/kg).

**Justification for EV use (2 marks):** The supercapacitor occupies the **high-power moderate-energy** niche, which is precisely the requirement for *transient* EV events (acceleration peaks, regenerative braking pulses). The Li-ion battery covers long-distance cruise; the supercapacitor covers peak events — together they cover the full operating envelope.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "supercapacitor" with "battery":** Many students incorrectly say supercapacitors "store charge chemically." They do **NOT** — pure EDLCs store charge **electrostatically** (pseudocapacitors have a partial faradaic component, but not in the same sense as a battery).
> 2. **Forgetting the square in the energy formula:** Writing $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C V$ instead of $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^{2}$ costs the full 1 mark. Always remember: energy scales with **voltage squared**.
> 3. **Skipping the unit conversion in numericals:** Energy in joules must be converted to Wh using $1 \text{ Wh} = 3600 \text{ J}$. Many KTU toppers lose the 1-mark unit step.
> 4. **Forgetting ESR in maximum power:** Students often write $P = V^2/R_L$ instead of the correct matched-load formula $P_{max} = V^{2} / (4 \cdot ESR)$. The "4" in the denominator comes from the Maximum Power Transfer Theorem.
> 5. **Not drawing labelled diagrams:** In 7-mark sub-parts, a missing or unlabelled diagram forfeits 1–2 marks. Always label every block, every arrow, and every energy/power flow.
> 6. **Confusing the Helmholtz and Gouy-Chapman layers:** IHP and OHP are part of the **compact (Helmholtz)** layer; the **diffuse (Gouy-Chapman)** layer is separate. Writing them as one confuses 2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read This the Night Before the Exam:**

- A **supercapacitor** stores energy in an **electric double layer** at the electrode-electrolyte interface — *not* in a chemical reaction (with the partial exception of pseudocapacitors).
- The defining feature is the **tiny charge-separation distance** (~0.5 nm) and **enormous surface area** (~2,000 m²/g) of activated carbon.
- Three types: **EDLC** (electrostatic), **Pseudocapacitor** (fast faradaic), **Hybrid** (combination of both).
- **Energy stored:** $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^{2}$ — scales with $V^{2}$, so increasing cell voltage is the most leveraged design parameter.
- **Maximum power** (matched load): $P_{max} = V^{2} / (4 \cdot ESR)$ — ESR is the *limiting* parameter for peak power.
- **RC time constant** governs discharge: $V(t) = V_0 e^{-t/RC}$; the cell releases ~63% of energy in one time constant and ~99% in five.
- **Specific energy:** 1–10 Wh/kg (much less than batteries); **Specific power:** 1,000–10,000 W/kg (much more than batteries).
- **Cycle life:** >500,000 cycles (5–10× better than Li-ion) — perfect for regenerative braking's constant micro-cycles.
- **Operating temperature:** −40 °C to +85 °C — enables cold-weather EV operation where Li-ion batteries fail.
- **Cell voltage:** ~2.7 V (organic electrolyte) to ~3.0 V (ionic liquid); banks are series-connected with active voltage balancing.
- **EV applications:** regenerative braking capture, acceleration boost, cold-start assist, battery life extension, DC bus voltage stabilization.
- **HESS topology:** supercapacitor + Li-ion battery + DC-DC converter + intelligent energy management controller.
- **Self-discharge** is higher than Li-ion (a few weeks vs months) — design consideration for long-parked EVs.
- **Ragone plot** is the canonical visual comparison — supercapacitor occupies the high-power bridge between conventional capacitors and batteries.
- **Equivalent circuit:** $C$ (main) in series with **ESR** (power loss), with parallel $R_p$ (self-discharge leakage) and series $L$ (HF parasitic).
- **Round-trip efficiency:** >90% (vs 60–70% for batteries) — because no slow chemical reaction intermediates are involved.
- **Coulombic efficiency:** ~95–99% per cycle — extremely high because charge transfer is purely electrostatic.
- The **Helmholtz double layer** has an IHP (specifically adsorbed ions), an OHP (solvated ions), and a diffuse layer beyond.
- **Derivation must-knows:** (1) $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^{2}$, (2) $P_{max} = V^{2} / (4 \cdot ESR)$, (3) matched-load condition $R_L = R_{ESR}$, (4) $\tau = RC$.

> **End of Notes — PEAET742 Module 3 — Supercapacitors**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hybridization of Different Energy Storage Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET742 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES AND RENEWABLE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hybridization of Different Energy Storage Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Hybridization of Energy Storage Devices (HESS — Hybrid Energy Storage System)** refers to the deliberate combination of two or more heterogeneous energy storage technologies — typically a high-energy device (e.g., Lithium-ion battery, fuel cell) and a high-power device (e.g., supercapacitor/ultracapacitor, flywheel) — within a single power-conversion architecture, governed by a power-electronics interface and an Energy Management Strategy (EMS), to exploit the *complementary* characteristics of each subsystem.

The **core design objective** of HESS is to decouple the **energy-density requirement** from the **power-density requirement** so that the traction system simultaneously delivers:
- Long driving range (governed by **specific energy, Wh/kg**)
- Fast transient acceleration and regenerative braking capture (governed by **specific power, W/kg**)
- Extended cyclic calendar life (governed by **cycle life at Depth-of-Discharge, DOD%**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Hybridization is *not* the parallel connection of two batteries. It is a multi-domain system combining devices with *different* physical charge-storage mechanisms (Faradaic vs. Non-Faradaic, or electrochemical vs. electromechanical) such that their **Ragone curves** occupy orthogonal regions.

## 1.2 Why Hybridization? — The Complementary Pairing Principle

No single storage device can simultaneously optimize the four pillars of vehicular storage: **Energy, Power, Life, Cost**.

| Device | Specific Energy (Wh/kg) | Specific Power (W/kg) | Cycle Life | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Li-ion Battery | 120–250 | 250–1000 | 1000–3000 | Faradaic (Redox) |
| Ultracapacitor (UC) | 5–10 | 5000–15000 | >500000 | Non-Faradaic (EDLC) |
| Fuel Cell (PEMFC) | 500–800 (system) | 200–500 | 2000–5000 h | Electrochemical |
| Flywheel | 20–80 | 1000–5000 | 20 yrs | Mechanical (Kinetic) |

The **complementary pairing** logic dictates pairing a high-energy, slow device (battery/FC) with a high-power, fast device (UC).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Hybrid Water Supply System

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Two-Tank Household Water System**
>
> Imagine a city that needs **continuous, low-flow water for 24 hours** (like the average cruising energy demand of an EV) and **occasional high-burst flow for fire-fighting** (like the 5-second acceleration peak of 100 kW during overtaking).
>
> - A **massive overhead tank** (the Li-ion battery) holds *huge* water volume (energy) but drains slowly through a thin pipe (limited power density).
> - A **small pressurised accumulator** (the ultracapacitor) holds very little water but can release it *instantly* through a fire-hose (extremely high power density).
> - A **smart valve controller** (the Energy Management Strategy) decides which tank feeds the city at any given instant.
>
> A single tank cannot do both jobs efficiently — the small tank empties too fast, the large tank cannot supply bursts fast enough. **Hybridization** is the engineering realization of this two-tank principle on an electric drivetrain.

## 1.4 Classification of HESS Topologies

Based on the **interface topology** between the storage devices and the DC-link of the traction inverter, HESS is classified into three canonical configurations:

1. **Passive Parallel (Direct Coupling):** Both devices connected directly across the DC bus without any power converter. Voltage matching is mandatory.
2. **Semi-Active (Battery / UC side converter):** Only one device is interfaced through a DC-DC converter; the other is directly connected.
3. **Fully-Active (Dual Converter):** Both devices interface through dedicated bidirectional DC-DC converters. Maximum flexibility, highest cost.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently tests the trade-off between **passive vs. semi-active vs. fully-active** configurations. Memorise: *Passive = cheapest but voltage-locked; Fully-Active = most flexible but expensive and complex EMS.*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Ragone Plot — The Engineering Map of Energy Storage

The **Ragone Plot** is a log–log scatter plot of **Specific Energy (Wh/kg)** on the X-axis versus **Specific Power (W/kg)** on the Y-axis. It is the single most important diagnostic tool for selecting and pairing energy storage devices.

$$E_{specific} \cdot P_{specific}^{-1} \approx \text{Discharge time constant (s)}$$

The diagonal lines of constant **discharge time** $t_d$ are given by:

$$t_d \;=\; \frac{E_{specific}}{P_{specific}} \;\;[\text{hours}]$$

> [!NOTE]
> Devices lying on the **same diagonal** deliver the same mission duration but differ in mass. A device lying **upper-right** is ideal (high E *and* high P); devices lower-left are poor.

## 2.2 The Six Reasons for Hybridization — KTU Core Logic

1. **Power Buffering:** The battery cannot accept/absorb regen current spikes (>3C) without lithium plating. UC absorbs the spike.
2. **Peak Shaving:** During acceleration, UC supplies peak power $P_{peak}$, allowing battery to operate within its safe **continuous discharge rating** ($C_{cont}$).
3. **Life Extension:** Reducing the Root-Mean-Square (RMS) current on the battery lowers its thermal stress, multiplying calendar life by **2× to 5×**.
4. **Cold-Cranking Boost:** UC provides the instantaneous cranking torque at low SoC and low temperature.
5. **Range Anxiety Mitigation:** Battery handles base load (cruising); only energy for steady motion is consumed from the energy-dense device.
6. **Braking Energy Recovery:** UC's high cycle life (>10⁶ cycles) makes it ideal for absorbing unpredictable micro-regen events in urban driving.

## 2.3 Operational Logic of Semi-Active HESS

The most widely-adopted topology (Toyota Prius Gen-3, Formula Student EVs) is **Battery semi-active + UC semi-active** (sometimes called "double semi-active"):

- **DC-Link Voltage:** $V_{dc}$ is regulated by the battery-side converter (typically buck-boost).
- **UC Voltage Window:** $V_{UC}$ is allowed to swing between $V_{UC,min}$ and $V_{UC,max}$ (e.g., 1.5 V to 2.7 V per cell), with the UC-side converter (usually boost) stepping it up to $V_{dc}$.
- **Power Split Equation (Instantaneous):**

$$P_{load}(t) \;=\; P_{batt}(t) \;+\; P_{UC}(t)$$

- **EMS Rule (Frequency-Based Decomposition):**
  - **Low-frequency demand** (steady cruising) → Battery (filtered below cutoff $f_c \approx 0.01$ Hz).
  - **High-frequency demand** (acceleration/braking transients) → UC (above $f_c$).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | KTU Insight |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ragone discharge time | $t_d = E_{sp}/P_{sp}$ | h | Diagonals on Ragone plot |
| 2 | Power split (instantaneous) | $P_{load} = P_{batt} + P_{UC}$ | W | Energy conservation |
| 3 | Energy split (over a cycle) | $E_{load} = \int P_{load}\,dt$ | Wh | Used for sizing |
| 4 | Specific energy (gravimetric) | $E_{sp} = E / m$ | Wh/kg | Compare technologies |
| 5 | Volumetric energy density | $E_{v} = E / V$ | Wh/L | Packaging constraint |
| 6 | Coulombic efficiency | $\eta_c = Q_{out}/Q_{in}$ | % | Round-trip loss indicator |
| 7 | Peukert's Law (capacity fade at high C) | $I^n \cdot t = C_p$ | constant | $n \approx 1.05$–$1.2$ for Li-ion |
| 8 | Internal resistance model | $V_t = V_{oc} - I \cdot R_i$ | V | Used in passive hybrid |
| 9 | Ultracapacitor stored energy | $E_{UC} = \tfrac{1}{2} C (V_{max}^2 - V_{min}^2)$ | J | Usable 75% if $V_{min} = 0.5 V_{max}$ |
| 10 | Battery stress reduction factor | $\sigma = I_{rms,HESS} / I_{rms,batt-only}$ | — | $<1$ confirms hybridization benefit |
| 11 | Hybrid specific energy (weighted) | $E_{sp,H} = \dfrac{E_b + E_{UC}}{m_b + m_{UC}}$ | Wh/kg | Optimization objective |
| 12 | DC-link voltage relation | $V_{dc} = D \cdot V_{UC}$ | V | For boost mode, $D = 1 - V_{UC}/V_{dc}$ |
| 13 | Cycle-life acceleration factor (Arrhenius) | $AF = 2^{(T - 25)/10}$ | — | Every 10 °C doubles degradation |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Pitfall:** Use `\mid` or `\vert` (NOT `|`) for absolute value inside any markdown table row, or the column separator will break.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

HESS is deployed in:
- **Battery Electric Buses** (Capabus, Proterra): UC + LTO battery for opportunity charging at stops.
- **Formula Student Electric**: UC + Li-Po for 75 m sprint acceleration bursts.
- **Fuel Cell Vehicles** (Hyundai NEXO, Toyota Mirai): FC (energy) + Li-ion (buffer) + sometimes UC (peak).
- **Hybrid Mining Locomotives**: Battery + Supercapacitor for regenerative braking on downhills.
- **Grid-Tied Solar**: Battery (long-term) + UC (PV ramp-rate smoothing).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Usable Energy from an Ultracapacitor (75% Rule)

A supercapacitor is rated for a **maximum cell voltage** $V_{max}$ and a **minimum safe voltage** $V_{min}$ (below which electrolyte degradation accelerates). The *usable* energy extraction is the difference between fully-charged and fully-discharged states.

**Step 1: Energy stored in a capacitor at any voltage $V$:**

$$E(V) \;=\; \frac{1}{2} \, C \, V^{2}$$

**Step 2: Usable energy is the difference between maximum and minimum stored energy:**

$$\Delta E_{UC} \;=\; E(V_{max}) - E(V_{min}) \;=\; \frac{1}{2} C V_{max}^{2} - \frac{1}{2} C V_{min}^{2}$$

**Step 3: Factor out $\tfrac{1}{2} C V_{max}^2$ to obtain the utilisation ratio $\eta_{UC}$:**

$$\Delta E_{UC} \;=\; \frac{1}{2} C V_{max}^{2} \left[1 - \left(\frac{V_{min}}{V_{max}}\right)^{2}\right]$$

**Step 4: Substitute the standard design choice $V_{min} = \tfrac{1}{2} V_{max}$ (1.35 V out of 2.7 V for Maxwell BCAP3000):**

$$\eta_{UC} \;=\; 1 - (0.5)^{2} \;=\; 1 - 0.25 \;=\; 0.75 \;\; (75\%)$$

**Conclusion:** A UC is **75% depth-of-discharge capable**, compared to ~80% for LiFePO₄ and ~50% for NMC. This is why UC is *operationally* smaller than its rated energy suggests.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hybrid Specific Energy Optimisation (Mass Minimisation)

**Problem Statement:** An EV requires $E_{req} = 20$ kWh of usable energy and $P_{req} = 90$ kW of peak power for 10 s bursts. A candidate pack consists of Li-ion batteries ($E_{sp,B} = 150$ Wh/kg, $P_{sp,B} = 800$ W/kg) and ultracapacitors ($E_{sp,UC} = 6$ Wh/kg, $P_{sp,UC} = 10000$ W/kg). Determine the *minimum* hybrid mass $m_H$ and the optimal mass split.

**Step 1: Define the design variables.** Let $m_B$ = battery mass (kg), $m_{UC}$ = UC mass (kg), total mass $m_H = m_B + m_{UC}$.

**Step 2: Energy constraint.** The hybrid pack must deliver at least $E_{req}$:

$$150 \, m_B + 6 \, m_{UC} \;\geq\; 20000 \;\; \text{(Wh)}$$

**Step 3: Power constraint.** The hybrid pack must deliver at least $P_{req}$ (sum of *parallel* power capability):

$$800 \, m_B + 10000 \, m_{UC} \;\geq\; 90000 \;\; \text{(W)}$$

**Step 4: Set up the linear programme.** Minimize $m_H = m_B + m_{UC}$ subject to both ≥ constraints and $m_B, m_{UC} \geq 0$.

**Step 5: Solve by constraint intersection (Lagrangian or graphical method).**

Equate both constraints to their minimum:

From energy: $m_{UC} = (20000 - 150 m_B)/6$.

Substitute into power:

$$800 m_B + 10000 \left(\frac{20000 - 150 m_B}{6}\right) \;=\; 90000$$

**Step 6: Simplify.**

$$800 m_B + \frac{10000 \cdot 20000}{6} - \frac{10000 \cdot 150 m_B}{6} \;=\; 90000$$

$$800 m_B + 3.333 \times 10^{7} - 2.5 \times 10^{5} m_B \;=\; 90000$$

$$-249200 \, m_B \;=\; 90000 - 33333333 \;=\; -33243333$$

$$m_B \;=\; \frac{33243333}{249200} \;\approx\; 133.4 \;\; \text{kg}$$

**Step 7: Compute $m_{UC}$ from the energy equation:**

$$m_{UC} \;=\; \frac{20000 - 150 \cdot 133.4}{6} \;=\; \frac{20000 - 20010}{6} \;=\; \frac{-10}{6} \;<\; 0$$

**Step 8: Interpret the result.** A *negative* $m_{UC}$ means the battery alone can satisfy both energy *and* power constraints simultaneously. Hence the optimal HESS is **battery-only**:

$$m_B^{\star} \;=\; \max\left(\frac{E_{req}}{E_{sp,B}},\;\frac{P_{req}}{P_{sp,B}}\right) \;=\; \max(133.33,\; 112.5) \;=\; 133.33 \;\; \text{kg}$$

**Step 9: Compute hybrid specific energy.**

$$E_{sp,H} \;=\; \frac{E_{req}}{m_B^{\star}} \;=\; \frac{20000}{133.33} \;\approx\; 150 \;\; \text{Wh/kg}$$

**Step 10: Re-examine.** Now *tighten* the power requirement to $P_{req} = 250$ kW (true high-performance case) and re-solve:

Power equation: $800 m_B + 10000 m_{UC} = 250000$.

Energy equation: $150 m_B + 6 m_{UC} = 20000$.

Solving simultaneously (matrix form):

$$\begin{bmatrix} 150 & 6 \\ 800 & 10000 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} m_B \\ m_{UC} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 20000 \\ 250000 \end{bmatrix}$$

Determinant $= 150 \cdot 10000 - 6 \cdot 800 = 1500000 - 4800 = 1495200$.

$$m_B \;=\; \frac{(20000)(10000) - (6)(250000)}{1495200} \;=\; \frac{200000000 - 1500000}{1495200} \;=\; \frac{198500000}{1495200} \;\approx\; 132.78 \;\; \text{kg}$$

$$m_{UC} \;=\; \frac{(150)(250000) - (800)(20000)}{1495200} \;=\; \frac{37500000 - 16000000}{1495200} \;=\; \frac{21500000}{1495200} \;\approx\; 14.38 \;\; \text{kg}$$

**Step 11: Final verification.**

Check energy: $150 \cdot 132.78 + 6 \cdot 14.38 = 19917 + 86.28 \approx 20003$ Wh ✓
Check power: $800 \cdot 132.78 + 10000 \cdot 14.38 = 106224 + 143800 = 250024$ W ✓

**Conclusion:** When peak power demand is high relative to energy, adding **14.38 kg of UC** reduces battery stress, allowing battery to operate within its safe continuous rating. Total HESS mass = **147.16 kg**, compared to 312.5 kg if UC were forced to supply all energy — a **53% mass saving**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Passive vs Active Hybrid: Voltage Divergence Analysis

In **passive parallel**, two devices share the same terminal voltage. If battery voltage $V_{B}$ and UC voltage $V_{UC}$ differ at any instant, circulating current flows. The instantaneous current through each branch is governed by Ohm's law applied to the parallel combination:

$$V_{term} \;=\; V_{B} - I_{B} R_{B} \;=\; V_{UC} - I_{UC} R_{UC}$$

The **circulating current** at zero load is:

$$I_{circ} \;=\; \frac{V_{B} - V_{UC}}{R_{B} + R_{UC}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is *why* passive hybrid is impractical for a Li-ion (3.7 V) + UC (2.7 V) combination — large $V_B - V_{UC}$ causes continuous parasitic loss.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Passive | Battery Semi-Active | UC Semi-Active | Fully-Active |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| DC-DC converters | 0 | 1 (batt-side) | 1 (UC-side) | 2 |
| Voltage matching | Mandatory | Battery fixes $V_{dc}$ | UC regulates $V_{dc}$ | None required |
| EMS complexity | Low | Medium | Medium-High | High |
| Cost | Lowest | Medium | Medium-High | Highest |
| Efficiency | 90–95% | 92–96% | 93–97% | 88–93% |
| Weight | Lowest | Medium | Medium | Highest |
| Toyota Prius uses | — | — | ✓ (Gen 1–2) | ✓ (Gen 3+) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Fully-Active HESS Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["BATTERY PACK Li-ion / LFP"] --> B1["BUCK BOOST CONVERTER BATT SIDE"]
    B1 --> DC["DC LINK V dc bus"]
    C1["BUCK BOOST CONVERTER UC SIDE"] --> DC
    DC --> INV["TRACTION INVERTER VSI"]
    INV --> M["TRACTION MOTOR PMSM / INDUCTION"]
    A2["ULTRACAPACITOR MODULE EDLC"] --> C1
    DC --> AUX["AUXILIARY 12V DC DC"]
    EMS["EMS CONTROLLER VCU / BMS HUB"] -.controls.-> B1
    EMS -.controls.-> C1
    EMS -.commands.-> INV
    FB["VEHICLE DEMAND PEDAL BRAKE"] --> EMS
    REG["REGEN BRAKING POWER"] --> DC
```

## 4.2 Energy Management Strategy — Frequency-Based Decomposition Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    P0["DEMAND POWER P load t"] --> F1["LOW PASS FILTER cutoff f c"]
    F1 --> PB["BATTERY POWER P batt filtered low frequency"]
    P0 --> F2["HIGH PASS FILTER cutoff f c"]
    F2 --> PUC["ULTRACAPACITOR POWER P UC transient high frequency"]
    PB --> SUM["DC LINK INSTANTANEOUS POWER"]
    PUC --> SUM
    SUM --> INV["TRACTION MOTOR"]
    SOC["UC STATE OF CHARGE MONITOR"] -.feedback.-> F1
    SOC -.feedback.-> F2
    SOC -.clamp.-> BRK["IF UC SoC LESS THAN 20 percent ROUTE PEAK TO BATTERY"]
```

## 4.3 HESS Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    START["DESIGN START VEHICLE MISSION PROFILE"] --> Q1{"IS PEAK POWER GREATER THAN 3 TIMES CONTINUOUS"}
    Q1 -- YES --> Q2{"IS REGEN ENERGY GREATER THAN 20 percent OF TRIP ENERGY"}
    Q1 -- NO --> BATONLY["BATTERY ONLY PACK SELECT Li-ion"]
    Q2 -- YES --> Q3["REQUIRED HESS ADD ULTRACAPACITOR MODULE"]
    Q2 -- NO --> Q4{"IS COLD START OR TRANSIENT ASSIST CRITICAL"}
    Q4 -- YES --> Q3
    Q4 -- NO --> BATONLY
    Q3 --> TOPO{"BUDGET CONSTRAINED"}
    TOPO -- YES --> SEMI["SEMI ACTIVE TOPOLOGY UC SIDE BOOST CONVERTER"]
    TOPO -- NO --> FULL["FULLY ACTIVE DUAL CONVERTER"]
    BATONLY --> END["PACKAGING AND THERMAL DESIGN"]
    SEMI --> END
    FULL --> END
```

## 4.4 Ragone Plot — Hybridization Visual Logic (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph REGION_E["HIGH ENERGY REGION LEFT"]
        B1["LITHIUM ION BATTERY 150 Wh per kg 800 W per kg"]
        FC1["FUEL CELL 800 Wh per kg 300 W per kg"]
    end
    subgraph REGION_P["HIGH POWER REGION RIGHT"]
        UC1["ULTRACAPACITOR 6 Wh per kg 10000 W per kg"]
        FW1["FLYWHEEL 40 Wh per kg 5000 W per kg"]
    end
    B1 -.complementary pairing.-> UC1
    FC1 -.complementary pairing.-> UC1
    HESS["HYBRID PACK COMBINED E sp BOOSTED P sp ADDED"]
    B1 --> HESS
    UC1 --> HESS
    FW1 --> HESS
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]
**Define the term "Hybridization of Energy Storage Devices" in the context of electric vehicles. State any two advantages.**

**Model Answer:**
Hybridization of energy storage devices refers to the combination of two or more energy storage technologies (typically one high-energy device and one high-power device) in a single powertrain, integrated via power-electronic converters and governed by an Energy Management Strategy (EMS), to exploit their complementary characteristics on the Ragone plot.

**Advantages (any two):**
1. The high-power device (ultracapacitor) supplies transient acceleration and absorbs regenerative braking peaks, protecting the high-energy device (battery) from high C-rate stress and extending its cycle life by 2× to 5×.
2. It decouples the energy-density requirement from the power-density requirement, reducing the total pack mass compared to a battery-only solution designed for peak power.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is a Ragone plot? Why is it important for selecting a hybrid energy storage system?**

**Model Answer:**
A Ragone plot is a log–log graph of specific energy (Wh/kg) on the X-axis versus specific power (W/kg) on the Y-axis, used to compare energy storage technologies. Diagonal lines represent constant discharge-time contours.

**Importance for HESS selection:**
The Ragone plot allows engineers to identify devices that occupy *different* regions — a high-energy device lies to the upper-right but *low* on power, while a high-power device lies lower on energy but upper on power. Pairing devices from complementary regions allows the hybrid to *shift* its operating point higher and to the right than either device alone.

---

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU End-Semester Evaluation pattern: Each Part B question carries 14 marks, divided into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Choose **either Option A OR Option B**.

---

### OPTION A — 14 Marks

#### Question A [KTU University Exam — July 2024, Modified]
**(a)** Compare the three hybrid energy storage topologies — passive parallel, semi-active, and fully-active — with suitable diagrams. Highlight the voltage-matching constraint of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An EV traction system requires $E_{req} = 25$ kWh usable energy and $P_{peak} = 200$ kW for an 8-second overtaking burst. Candidate Li-ion cells have $E_{sp,B} = 160$ Wh/kg, $P_{sp,B} = 900$ W/kg. Candidate UCs have $E_{sp,UC} = 7$ Wh/kg, $P_{sp,UC} = 12000$ W/kg. Determine (i) the mass of a battery-only solution, (ii) the optimal HESS mass and split, and (iii) the percentage mass saving. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

| Topology | Configuration | Voltage Match? | Converters | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Passive | Both directly across DC bus | Mandatory (same $V_t$) | 0 | Low |
| Battery semi-active | Batt → Conv → DC; UC direct | UC = $V_{dc}$ (fixed) | 1 | Medium |
| UC semi-active | UC → Conv → DC; Batt direct | Batt = $V_{dc}$ (fixed) | 1 | Medium |
| Fully-active | Both → Conv → DC | None required | 2 | High |

**Pass / Fail Analysis:**
- **Passive:** Voltage of UC swings (1.35 → 2.7 V/cell) and battery voltage sags under load → impractical unless both curves coincide, e.g., battery + battery.
- **Battery semi-active:** UC voltage is forced to equal $V_{dc}$ → only feasible if UC's voltage window *equals* $V_{dc}$ (rare).
- **UC semi-active:** Battery voltage is well-regulated → preferred low-cost topology.
- **Fully-active:** Most flexible; both devices operate in their native voltage window.

(Diagrams: see SECTION 4 — refer to **Block 4.1** for fully-active schematic.)

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Battery-only mass:**
Energy-limited: $m_B = 25000 / 160 = 156.25$ kg.
Power-limited: $m_B = 200000 / 900 = 222.22$ kg.
Take maximum: $m_B^{\star} = 222.22$ kg.

**Step 2 — HESS optimisation:**

Constraints:
- Energy: $160 m_B + 7 m_{UC} \geq 25000$.
- Power: $900 m_B + 12000 m_{UC} \geq 200000$.

Minimize $m_H = m_B + m_{UC}$.

Setting both constraints active and solving the $2 \times 2$ linear system:

$$\begin{bmatrix} 160 & 7 \\ 900 & 12000 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} m_B \\ m_{UC} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 25000 \\ 200000 \end{bmatrix}$$

Determinant $\Delta = 160 \cdot 12000 - 7 \cdot 900 = 1920000 - 6300 = 1913700$.

$$m_B \;=\; \frac{25000 \cdot 12000 - 7 \cdot 200000}{1913700} \;=\; \frac{300000000 - 1400000}{1913700} \;=\; \frac{298600000}{1913700} \;\approx\; 156.08 \;\; \text{kg}$$

$$m_{UC} \;=\; \frac{160 \cdot 200000 - 900 \cdot 25000}{1913700} \;=\; \frac{32000000 - 22500000}{1913700} \;=\; \frac{9500000}{1913700} \;\approx\; 4.96 \;\; \text{kg}$$

**Step 3 — Total HESS mass:**

$$m_H^{\star} \;=\; 156.08 + 4.96 \;=\; 161.04 \;\; \text{kg}$$

**Step 4 — Percentage mass saving:**

$$\% \text{Saving} \;=\; \frac{222.22 - 161.04}{222.22} \times 100 \;=\; \frac{61.18}{222.22} \times 100 \;\approx\; 27.5\%$$

**Final Answer:**
(i) Battery-only mass = **222.22 kg**.
(ii) HESS optimum = **161.04 kg** (156.08 kg battery + 4.96 kg UC).
(iii) Mass saving = **27.5%**.

---

### OPTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

#### Question B [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**(a)** Explain the concept of a Ragone plot. Sketch the Ragone plot and mark the regions occupied by Li-ion battery, ultracapacitor, fuel cell, and flywheel. Justify why a battery + ultracapacitor hybrid is the most common pairing. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Maxwell BCAP3000 ultracapacitor module has $C = 3000$ F, $V_{max} = 2.7$ V, $V_{min} = 1.35$ V. Compute (i) the total energy stored at full charge, (ii) the usable energy between $V_{max}$ and $V_{min}$, (iii) the usable energy if the minimum voltage is raised to 1.8 V. Comment on the trade-off. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Ragone plot explanation:** As defined in SECTION 5, Part A, Q2. The Ragone plot is a log–log graph of specific energy vs. specific power with diagonal lines of constant discharge time.

**Sketch description (to be drawn in the answer sheet):**
- X-axis: Specific Energy (Wh/kg) — logarithmic, range 0.1 to 1000.
- Y-axis: Specific Power (W/kg) — logarithmic, range 1 to 100000.
- Mark these four regions:
  - **Li-ion battery:** 100–250 Wh/kg, 250–1500 W/kg — middle-left.
  - **Ultracapacitor:** 2–10 Wh/kg, 5000–20000 W/kg — upper-right.
  - **Fuel cell (PEMFC system):** 300–800 Wh/kg (system), 200–500 W/kg — far left, low.
  - **Flywheel:** 10–80 Wh/kg, 1000–5000 W/kg — upper-middle.
- Diagonal lines: 1 s, 10 s, 1 min, 1 h, 10 h contours.

**Justification for Battery + UC pairing:**
1. Battery occupies the high-energy region but is power-limited; UC occupies the high-power region but is energy-limited — they occupy *orthogonal* regions on the Ragone plot.
2. The combined Ragone locus of the hybrid shifts toward the upper-right, achieving both high energy *and* high power.
3. Battery handles 95% of driving time (steady load); UC handles 5% (transients), giving the best of both worlds.
4. Cycle life of UC (>10⁶ cycles) absorbs regen braking cycles that would damage the battery.

(Diagrams: refer to **SECTION 4, Block 4.4** for the complementary-pairing schematic.)

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $C = 3000$ F, $V_{max} = 2.7$ V.

**(i) Total energy stored at full charge:**

$$E_{total} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} C V_{max}^{2} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 3000 \cdot (2.7)^{2} \;=\; 1500 \cdot 7.29 \;=\; 10935 \;\; \text{J}$$

Convert to Wh: $E = 10935 / 3600 = 3.04$ Wh.

**(ii) Usable energy between 2.7 V and 1.35 V:**

$$E_{use} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} C (V_{max}^{2} - V_{min}^{2}) \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 3000 \cdot (2.7^2 - 1.35^2)$$

$$= 1500 \cdot (7.29 - 1.8225) \;=\; 1500 \cdot 5.4675 \;=\; 8201.25 \;\; \text{J}$$

Convert: $E = 8201.25 / 3600 \approx 2.28$ Wh.

**Utilisation ratio:**

$$\eta_{UC} \;=\; \frac{E_{use}}{E_{total}} \;=\; \frac{8201.25}{10935} \;=\; 0.75 \;\; (75\%)$$

**(iii) Usable energy between 2.7 V and 1.8 V:**

$$E_{use,2} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} C (2.7^2 - 1.8^2) \;=\; 1500 \cdot (7.29 - 3.24) \;=\; 1500 \cdot 4.05 \;=\; 6075 \;\; \text{J}$$

Convert: $E = 6075 / 3600 \approx 1.69$ Wh.

**Utilisation ratio:**

$$\eta_{UC,2} \;=\; \frac{6075}{10935} \;\approx\; 0.556 \;\; (55.6\%)$$

**Trade-off comment:**
Raising the lower voltage cutoff from 1.35 V to 1.8 V increases the converter's boost ratio but reduces usable energy by ~26% (from 2.28 Wh to 1.69 Wh). The trade-off is between **converter cost/efficiency** (lower boost ratio = cheaper, more efficient) and **energy utilisation** (lower $V_{min}$ = more usable Wh per kg). A 1.35 V cutoff (50% of $V_{max}$) is the industry-standard 75%-rule compromise.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes (Lose 2–3 marks each):**
>
> 1. **Confusing specific energy with energy density:** $E_{sp}$ is *gravimetric* (Wh/kg); $E_v$ is *volumetric* (Wh/L). Examiner deducts 1 mark if used interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting the 75% rule** when calculating UC usable energy: applying $E = \tfrac{1}{2}CV^2$ at $V_{min} = 0$ gives *total* energy, not *usable* energy. Always subtract $\tfrac{1}{2}CV_{min}^2$.
> 3. **Using battery-only mass for the HESS optimisation problem** instead of solving the $2 \times 2$ linear system — this loses the entire 7-mark sub-part.
> 4. **Drawing passive hybrid with a DC-DC converter** — passive means *zero* converter. Examiner marks it as a conceptual error.
> 5. **Writing EMS as "Battery first then UC"** — the correct logic is *frequency-based decomposition* with simultaneous delivery based on demand bandwidth.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Fire Revision — Memorise Before Exam:**
>
> - **HESS Definition:** Combination of ≥2 storage devices with complementary Ragone characteristics, integrated by power-electronics and an EMS.
> - **Three Topologies:** Passive (0 conv.), Semi-active (1 conv.), Fully-active (2 conv.).
> - **Power Split Equation:** $P_{load}(t) = P_{batt}(t) + P_{UC}(t)$ — always instantaneously true.
> - **Energy Conservation:** $E_{load} = \int P_{load}\,dt$ — used for cycle-level sizing.
> - **75% Rule:** UC usable energy = $\tfrac{1}{2}C(V_{max}^2 - 0.5^2 V_{max}^2) = 0.75 \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}CV_{max}^2$.
> - **Six Reasons for Hybridization:** Power buffering, peak shaving, life extension, cold-cranking, range, regen recovery.
> - **Ragone Plot:** Log-Log, $E_{sp}$ vs $P_{sp}$, diagonal lines = constant discharge time $t_d = E_{sp}/P_{sp}$.
> - **Battery-Only Floor Mass:** $m_B^{\star} = \max(E_{req}/E_{sp,B},\; P_{req}/P_{sp,B})$.
> - **HESS Optimum:** Solve linear system from simultaneously-active energy + power constraints.
> - **EMS Rule:** Low-frequency → Battery; High-frequency → UC; cutoff typically 0.01–0.1 Hz.
> - **Mass Saving:** When $P_{req}/P_{sp,B} > E_{req}/E_{sp,B}$, hybridization reduces mass.
> - **Industry Example:** Toyota Prius Gen-3 uses fully-active Li-ion + Ni-MH (Gen 1–2 used UC semi-active).
> - **Lifecycle:** UC > 500,000 cycles; Li-ion 1,000–3,000 cycles — *orders of magnitude* difference enables regen absorption.
> - **Specific Power of UC (10,000 W/kg) is ~12× that of Li-ion (800 W/kg)** — this is the *raison d'être* of HESS.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overview of Electric Vehicle Battery Chargers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET742 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES AND RENEWABLE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Overview of Electric Vehicle Battery Chargers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Electric Vehicle (EV) Battery Charger** is a power electronic converter system that draws electrical energy from the **AC mains grid** (or a DC source) and delivers it to the **traction battery pack** of an electric vehicle in a controlled manner, ensuring that the battery's voltage, current, temperature, and State of Charge (SoC) remain within manufacturer-specified safety limits.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, EV battery chargers are classified based on:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> An EV battery charger is an electrical device that converts AC grid power into regulated DC power suitable for recharging the high-voltage traction battery of an electric vehicle, incorporating safety, communication (e.g., PLC or CAN), and power factor correction features mandated by standards like **SAE J1772**, **IEC 61851**, and **CHAdeMO**.

### Key Classification Parameters

| Parameter | Values |
|---|---|
| **Power Flow Direction** | Unidirectional (G2V) or Bidirectional (V2G, V2H, V2V) |
| **Location** | On-board (inside the vehicle) or Off-board (stationary) |
| **Current Type** | AC charging (slow/fast) or DC charging (fast) |
| **Coupling Method** | Conductive (plug-in) or Inductive (wireless) |
| **Output Power Level** | Level 1, Level 2, Level 3 (DC Fast) |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Fuel Station with a Brain"

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it like this:** Imagine a traditional petrol pump. You arrive, attach a nozzle, and fuel flows in. Now, a battery charger is *like* that fuel pump, but infinitely more intelligent:
>
> 1. The **grid** is the underground oil reserve (source).
> 2. The **charger** is the pump + the entire refinery (converts and cleans the energy).
> 3. The **battery** is the car's fuel tank (storage).
> 4. The **communication protocol** (like a polite conversation between the pump and the car) ensures the right "fuel grade" and pressure is delivered — no over-filling, no overheating.
>
> Just as you wouldn't pump diesel into a petrol car, a battery charger must "negotiate" the right **voltage, current, and termination point** with the Battery Management System (BMS) before energy flows.

### Real-World Context

The global EV charging infrastructure market is dominated by the standard **"Combo Coupler" (CCS2)** in Europe and India, and **NACS (Tesla)** in North America. Kerala's KSRTC and private fleet operators deploy **CCS2 DC fast chargers** at 50–150 kW along NH-66 and NH-544 corridors.

---

## 1.3 Why Battery Chargers Are Critical in E-Mobility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight – "Charger = Power Electronics Backbone of EV"**
> A modern EV charger is essentially an SMPS (Switched Mode Power Supply) on steroids, with galvanic isolation, bidirectional capability, and grid-supportive features like reactive power compensation.

The charger is the **interface between the grid and the battery** — the most expensive component of an EV (battery = ~40% of vehicle cost). A poorly designed charger can:

- Reduce battery cycle life by **40–60%**
- Cause thermal runaway (fire risk)
- Introduce harmonic distortion into the grid
- Fail to comply with **IEC 61851-1** safety standards
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Architecture of an EV Battery Charger

A modern EV charger is a multi-stage power conversion system. The block-level architecture is:

### Stage 1: **EMI Filter & Input Protection**
- Removes electromagnetic interference from the grid
- Includes surge protection, inrush current limiting (NTC thermistor + relay bypass)
- Complies with **IEC 61000-3-12** (harmonic limits)

### Stage 2: **PFC Stage (Power Factor Correction)**
- A boost converter operating in **CCM (Continuous Conduction Mode)**
- Achieves **PF > 0.99** and **THD < 5%** (as per IEEE 519)
- Output: regulated **DC link voltage** (typically 400 V or 800 V)

### Stage 3: **Isolated DC-DC Converter**
- Provides **galvanic isolation** (mandatory per IEC 61851-1)
- Topologies: **Phase-Shifted Full-Bridge (PSFB)**, **LLC Resonant**, **Dual Active Bridge (DAB)** for bidirectional
- Output: battery-matched voltage and current

### Stage 4: **Output Filter & Control**
- LC filter to smooth output
- Closed-loop control using **PID or Model Predictive Control (MPC)**
- Communicates with BMS via **CAN / PLC / IEC 61850**

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Formulas for KTU Board Exam.** Every formula is verified and exam-ready. The pipe symbol `|` is replaced with `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| S.No | Formula / Concept | Equation | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Charging Time** | $$t_c = \frac{E_{bat}}{P_{ch} \cdot \eta_{ch}}$$ | hours | Time to fully charge a battery |
| 2 | **State of Charge (SoC)** | $$SoC(t) = SoC_0 + \frac{1}{C_{bat}} \int_{0}^{t} I_{ch}(\tau) \, d\tau$$ | % | Battery energy level tracking |
| 3 | **Charger Efficiency** | $$\eta_{ch} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$$ | % | Overall power conversion efficiency |
| 4 | **DC Fast Charging Current Limit** | $$I_{max} = \frac{P_{max}}{V_{bat,min}}$$ | A | Current demand at low SoC |
| 5 | **Cable Size (Current Carrying)** | $$A_{cable} = \frac{I_{ch}}{\sigma \cdot E_{max}}$$ | mm² | Conductor cross-section |
| 6 | **PFC Boost Inductor** | $$L_{PFC} = \frac{V_{in} \cdot D \cdot (1-D)}{f_{sw} \cdot \Delta I_L}$$ | H | PFC stage design |
| 7 | **LLC Resonant Frequency** | $$f_r = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_r C_r}}$$ | Hz | Resonant tank design |
| 8 | **THD (Total Harmonic Distortion)** | $$THD = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} I_h^2}}{I_1} \times 100\%$$ | % | Input current quality |
| 9 | **Power Factor** | $$PF = \cos\phi \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + THD_i^2}}$$ | unitless | Grid-side PF |
| 10 | **Charging Energy Loss** | $$E_{loss} = I_{ch}^2 \cdot R_{int} \cdot t_c$$ | Wh | Internal resistance losses |
| 11 | **Cable Voltage Drop** | $$\Delta V = I_{ch} \cdot R_{cable} \cdot L_{cable}$$ | V | Cable sizing constraint |
| 12 | **DAB Power Transfer (Bidirectional)** | $$P = \frac{n V_1 V_2}{2 f_{sw} L_{lk}} \cdot d(1-d)$$ | W | Bidirectional charger design |

> **Where:**
> - $E_{bat}$ = Battery energy capacity (kWh)
> - $P_{ch}$ = Charger output power (kW)
> - $\eta_{ch}$ = Charger efficiency (typically 0.92–0.96)
> - $C_{bat}$ = Battery capacity (Ah)
> - $d$ = Phase-shift duty ratio (DAB)
> - $L_{lk}$ = Leakage inductance of HF transformer

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Charging Time for a Given Battery

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup (Board-Exam Style):** A Tata Nexon EV has a **30.2 kWh** battery. Using a **7.2 kW AC Type 2** home wallbox charger with an overall efficiency of **92%**, calculate the time required to charge the battery from **20% to 80% SoC**.

### Step 1: Identify Given Data
- $E_{bat} = 30.2 \text{ kWh}$
- $P_{ch,rated} = 7.2 \text{ kW}$
- $\eta_{ch} = 0.92$
- Initial SoC: $SoC_0 = 20\%$
- Final SoC: $SoC_f = 80\%$

### Step 2: Compute Energy to be Delivered

$$E_{deliver} = (SoC_f - SoC_0) \times E_{bat}$$

$$E_{deliver} = (0.80 - 0.20) \times 30.2 \text{ kWh}$$

$$E_{deliver} = 0.60 \times 30.2 = 18.12 \text{ kWh}$$

### Step 3: Account for Charger Efficiency

The energy drawn from the grid must be greater than the energy stored in the battery (due to losses).

$$E_{grid} = \frac{E_{deliver}}{\eta_{ch}}$$

$$E_{grid} = \frac{18.12}{0.92} = 19.695 \text{ kWh}$$

### Step 4: Compute Charging Time

$$t_c = \frac{E_{grid}}{P_{ch,rated}}$$

$$t_c = \frac{19.695 \text{ kWh}}{7.2 \text{ kW}} = 2.735 \text{ hours}$$

### Step 5: Convert to Hours and Minutes

$$t_c = 2 \text{ h} + 0.735 \times 60 \text{ min} = 2 \text{ h } 44 \text{ min}$$

### Final Answer

> **The battery charges from 20% to 80% in approximately 2 hours 44 minutes.**

### Engineering Insight
> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 60% SoC window (20→80%) is the **"sweet spot"** for lithium-ion batteries. Charging above 80% is intentionally slowed by the BMS (Constant Voltage phase) to protect cell longevity.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: PFC Boost Inductor Design

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** Design the boost inductor for a **3.3 kW** on-board charger PFC stage. Specifications: $V_{in,min} = 85 \text{ V}_{rms}$, $V_{out} = 400 \text{ V DC}$, $f_{sw} = 65 \text{ kHz}$, allowable current ripple $\Delta I_L = 20\%$ of peak input current.

### Step 1: Compute Peak Input Current

$$I_{in,peak} = \frac{\sqrt{2} \cdot P_{in}}{V_{in,min}} = \frac{\sqrt{2} \cdot 3300}{85} = 54.91 \text{ A}$$

### Step 2: Compute Allowable Ripple

$$\Delta I_L = 0.20 \times I_{in,peak} = 0.20 \times 54.91 = 10.98 \text{ A}$$

### Step 3: Determine Duty Ratio at Minimum Input

$$D = 1 - \frac{V_{in,peak}}{V_{out}} = 1 - \frac{85 \times \sqrt{2}}{400} = 1 - 0.3006 = 0.6994$$

### Step 4: Apply Boost Inductor Formula

$$L_{PFC} = \frac{V_{in,peak} \cdot D \cdot (1-D)}{f_{sw} \cdot \Delta I_L}$$

$$L_{PFC} = \frac{120.21 \times 0.6994 \times 0.3006}{65000 \times 10.98}$$

$$L_{PFC} = \frac{25.27}{713700} = 35.4 \text{ μH}$$

### Final Answer

> **The PFC boost inductor is approximately 35.4 μH** — a typical value for 3.3 kW OBC designs using SiC MOSFETs.

---
