| Use Case | Recommended Charger | Power Level | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Home overnight charging | Level 1 / Level 2 AC | 1.4 – 7.4 kW | Low cost, no infrastructure upgrade |
| Workplace / fleet depot | Level 2 AC (Type 2) | 11 – 22 kW | 3–6 hour dwell time, low cost per kWh |
| Highway corridor (intercity) | DC Fast (CCS2) | 50 – 150 kW | 20–40 min top-up, high turnover |
| Taxi / ride-share hub | DC Fast (CHAdeMO/CCS) | 60 – 120 kW | Quick turnaround, 2–3 charges/day |
| Heavy-duty truck (e-truck) | MCS (Megawatt Charging) | 750 kW – 3.75 MW | Future standard for Class 8 trucks |
| V2G home backup | Bidirectional OBC (V2H) | 7 – 11 kW | Emergency power, grid support |
| Solar-integrated EV | Off-board DC + PV inverter | 11 – 22 kW | Renewable synergy, net-zero charging |

---

## 3.4 Derivation 3: DAB Bidirectional Power Transfer

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Derivation (for understanding, not for full numerical exam):** The Dual Active Bridge (DAB) is the heart of V2G chargers.

In a DAB, power flows from one DC bus to another via an HF transformer with leakage inductance $L_{lk}$ and phase-shift control between the two H-bridges.

$$P = \frac{n V_1 V_2}{2 f_{sw} L_{lk}} \cdot d(1-d)$$

- $n$ = transformer turns ratio
- $V_1, V_2$ = DC bus voltages on primary and secondary
- $d$ = phase-shift duty ratio (0 ≤ d ≤ 0.5)
- $f_{sw}$ = switching frequency (typically 50–100 kHz)

**Key Insight:** Power flow direction reverses when **d changes sign**. This is the mathematical foundation of **V2G operation**.

### Example Calculation
For $n = 1$, $V_1 = V_2 = 400 \text{ V}$, $f_{sw} = 50 \text{ kHz}$, $L_{lk} = 25 \text{ μH}$, $d = 0.25$:

$$P = \frac{1 \times 400 \times 400}{2 \times 50000 \times 25 \times 10^{-6}} \times 0.25 \times 0.75$$

$$P = \frac{160000}{2.5} \times 0.1875 = 64000 \times 0.1875 = 12000 \text{ W} = 12 \text{ kW}$$

> **A 25% phase-shift delivers 12 kW** — typical of a 22 kW bidirectional OBC at half-power operation.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2

    A_STANDBY --> B_CONNECTED: Plug Inserted Pilot Signal 12V
    B_CONNECTED --> C_CHARGING: BMS Ready PWM 5 Percent
    C_CHARGING --> C_CHARGING: Constant Current Phase
    C_CHARGING --> D_CV: SoC Reaches 80 Percent
    D_CV --> C_CHARGING: Constant Voltage Phase
    C_CHARGING --> E_FAULT: Overvoltage or Overtemp Detected
    D_CV --> F_COMPLETE: SoC 100 Percent Termination
    E_FAULT --> A_STANDBY: Reset by User
    F_COMPLETE --> A_STANDBY: User Unplugs
    B_CONNECTED --> E_FAULT: Ground Fault Detected
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between on-board and off-board EV battery chargers with suitable examples. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | On-Board Charger | Off-Board Charger |
|---|---|---|
| **Location** | Installed inside the vehicle | Mounted on ground (stationary) |
| **Power Range** | 3.3 – 22 kW (typically AC) | 50 – 350 kW (typically DC) |
| **AC/DC** | Converts AC → DC inside the car | Converts AC → DC outside the car |
| **Example** | Tata Nexon EV (3.3 kW OBC) | Tata Power 60 kW DCFC station |

The on-board charger is limited by the vehicle's weight, volume, and thermal constraints, while off-board chargers can be arbitrarily large and liquid-cooled. **(3 Marks)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is meant by "Bidirectional Charging" in EVs? Mention any two standards that support it. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Bidirectional charging** allows power to flow from the grid **to** the vehicle (G2V) **and** from the vehicle battery **back** to the grid (V2G), home (V2H), or another vehicle (V2V). It enables the EV to act as a mobile Distributed Energy Resource (DER).

**Two standards supporting it:**
1. **CHAdeMO** — natively bidirectional, widely deployed in Japan and pilot fleets in India.
2. **ISO 15118 (with CCS2)** — supports Plug & Charge and bidirectional power transfer (V2G).

The vehicle's onboard charger must use a **Dual Active Bridge (DAB)** topology instead of a unidirectional PSFB/LLC converter. **(3 Marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]**

**Q.A. (a)** Classify EV battery chargers based on power levels, location, and current type. Explain the architecture of a **Level 3 DC Fast Charger** with a neat block diagram. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Classification of EV Chargers:**

1. **Based on Power Level (SAE J1772):**
   - Level 1: 1.4 – 1.9 kW (AC, slow)
   - Level 2: 3.3 – 19.2 kW (AC, fast)
   - Level 3: 50 – 150+ kW (DC, fast)

2. **Based on Location:**
   - **On-board** (inside vehicle) — limited to ~22 kW
   - **Off-board** (stationary) — up to 350+ kW

3. **Based on Current Type:**
   - **AC charging** (Level 1 & 2) — uses OBC
   - **DC charging** (Level 3) — bypasses OBC

**Architecture of Level 3 DC Fast Charger (Block Diagram):**

```
AC Grid → EMI Filter → 3-Phase Rectifier (PFC Boost) → 
DC Link (800V) → Isolated DC-DC Converter (LLC) → 
Output Filter → Battery (via CCS2 connector)
        ↑                                    ↑
   DSP Controller ←——————→ BMS (via CAN)
```

**Key Stages:**
- **3-Phase Active Front End (AFE):** Six IGBTs/SIC MOSFETs forming a Vienna rectifier or 6-pulse PFC to achieve **PF > 0.99** and **THD < 5%**.
- **DC Link Capacitor:** Large film/bus capacitor bank (typically 2–5 mF) to handle 2× line frequency ripple.
- **Isolated DC-DC:** High-frequency transformer (20–50 kHz) provides **galvanic isolation** as mandated by **IEC 61851-1**.
- **Cooling System:** Liquid cooling for 150+ kW units due to high power density (~3 kW/kg).
- **Communication:** PLC over pilot wire (HomePlug Green PHY) for ISO 15118 handshake.

> **Conclusion:** A 150 kW CCS2 DC fast charger can replenish a 75 kWh battery (e.g., Hyundai Ioniq 5) from 10% to 80% in approximately **27 minutes**. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Q.A. (b)** A lithium-ion EV battery has a usable capacity of **60 kWh**. The owner uses a **50 kW DC fast charger** with an overall efficiency of **93%** to charge from **10% to 80% SoC**. Calculate: (i) Energy delivered to the battery, (ii) Energy drawn from the grid, (iii) Total charging time. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $E_{bat} = 60 \text{ kWh}$
- $P_{ch} = 50 \text{ kW}$
- $\eta_{ch} = 0.93$
- $SoC_0 = 10\%$, $SoC_f = 80\%$

**Step (i): Energy delivered to battery**

$$E_{deliver} = (0.80 - 0.10) \times 60 = 0.70 \times 60 = 42 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step (ii): Energy drawn from grid**

$$E_{grid} = \frac{E_{deliver}}{\eta_{ch}} = \frac{42}{0.93} = 45.16 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step (iii): Charging time (assuming constant 50 kW)**

$$t_c = \frac{E_{grid}}{P_{ch}} = \frac{45.16}{50} = 0.903 \text{ hours} = 54.2 \text{ minutes}$$

> **Answer:** Battery receives 42 kWh, grid supplies 45.16 kWh, and charging takes ~54 minutes. **In practice, charging tapers beyond 50% SoC** (due to CV phase), so the real time will be ~10–15% longer. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]**

**Q.B. (a)** With the aid of a block diagram, explain the **power electronics architecture** of a **bidirectional on-board charger (OBC)** capable of V2G operation. Compare it with a unidirectional OBC. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram of Bidirectional OBC:**

```
AC Grid ←→ EMI Filter ←→ Bidirectional PFC (Totem-Pole / Bridgeless) ←→
DC Link (400V) ←→ Dual Active Bridge (DAB) ←→ Output Filter ←→ Battery
                         ↑                                    ↑
                    DSP Controller ←—— CAN ——→ BMS
```

**Stage-by-Stage Explanation:**

1. **Bidirectional PFC (Totem-Pole):** Uses **GaN HEMTs** for high-frequency operation (>100 kHz) to achieve high efficiency (>98%) in both directions. Replaces the conventional diode bridge + boost PFC, eliminating reverse-recovery losses.

2. **Dual Active Bridge (DAB):** Two H-bridges with an HF transformer in between. Power flow direction is controlled by the **phase-shift angle** between the primary and secondary H-bridge gating signals.

   $$P = \frac{n V_1 V_2}{2 f_{sw} L_{lk}} \cdot d(1-d)$$

3. **Battery-Side Filter & Protection:** LC filter, pre-charge circuit, and contactor for safe isolation.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Unidirectional OBC | Bidirectional OBC (V2G) |
|---|---|---|
| **Power Flow** | Grid → Battery only | Grid ↔ Battery |
| **DC-DC Topology** | PSFB or LLC | **DAB (mandatory)** |
| **Semiconductor** | IGBT / Si MOSFET | **GaN or SiC MOSFET** |
| **Efficiency** | 92–94% | 95–97% |
| **Cost** | ₹40,000 – ₹80,000 | ₹1,00,000 – ₹1,80,000 |
| **Standards** | IEC 61851-1 (basic) | **ISO 15118 + IEC 61851-1** |
| **Use Case** | Home charging | V2G / V2H backup power |

> **Conclusion:** Bidirectional OBCs transform EVs from passive loads into grid assets, enabling demand response, peak shaving, and renewable energy integration. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Q.B. (b)** An EV battery charger has the following specifications: $V_{in,min} = 110 \text{ V}_{rms}$, $V_{out} = 400 \text{ V DC}$, $f_{sw} = 100 \text{ kHz}$, ripple $\Delta I_L = 15\%$ of peak current, and input power = 3.3 kW. Design the **PFC boost inductor**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $V_{in,min} = 110 \text{ V}_{rms}$
- $V_{out} = 400 \text{ V DC}$
- $f_{sw} = 100 \text{ kHz}$
- $\Delta I_L = 15\%$ of peak
- $P_{in} = 3.3 \text{ kW}$

**Step 1: Peak input voltage**

$$V_{in,peak} = \sqrt{2} \times 110 = 155.56 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2: Peak input current**

$$I_{in,peak} = \frac{\sqrt{2} \times P_{in}}{V_{in,min}} = \frac{\sqrt{2} \times 3300}{110} = 42.43 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3: Allowable ripple**

$$\Delta I_L = 0.15 \times 42.43 = 6.36 \text{ A}$$

**Step 4: Duty ratio at minimum input**

$$D = 1 - \frac{V_{in,peak}}{V_{out}} = 1 - \frac{155.56}{400} = 0.6111$$

**Step 5: Boost inductor value**

$$L_{PFC} = \frac{V_{in,peak} \cdot D \cdot (1-D)}{f_{sw} \cdot \Delta I_L}$$

$$L_{PFC} = \frac{155.56 \times 0.6111 \times 0.3889}{100000 \times 6.36}$$

$$L_{PFC} = \frac{36.97}{636000} = 58.1 \text{ μH}$$

> **Answer:** A PFC boost inductor of **~58 μH** is required. Practically, a ferrite-core inductor with Kool-Mu powder core (Magnetics Inc.) is used to handle the DC bias and avoid saturation. **(7 Marks)**

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (As per KTU Valuation Key):**
>
> 1. **Confusing "OBC" with "DC Fast Charger":** OBC is for slow AC charging; DCFC is off-board and bypasses the OBC. Many students write "DC charger inside the car" — **-1 mark deduction**.
>
> 2. **Using wrong units:** Writing charging time in "minutes" when the formula outputs hours (or vice versa) — **-1 mark**.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the η in charging time formula:** The energy drawn from the grid is always **greater** than the energy stored in the battery. Skipping $\eta_{ch}$ loses **2 marks** in 14-mark problems.
>
> 4. **PFC formula errors:** Writing $D = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in,peak}}$ instead of the correct $D = 1 - \frac{V_{in,peak}}{V_{out}}$. Double-check the boost converter equations.
>
> 5. **Missing communication protocols:** A full-marks answer must mention **CAN, PLC, or ISO 15118** — not just "control system".
>
> 6. **No mention of safety/isolation:** IEC 61851-1 mandates galvanic isolation. Forgetting "isolation" in a 7-mark design question = **-2 marks**.
>
> 7. **CC vs CV confusion:** Charging is **Constant Current** up to ~80% SoC, then **Constant Voltage** until termination. Many students state "constant voltage throughout" — incorrect.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU 2024 ESE**

- **Definition:** EV battery charger = AC-to-DC power converter with safety, isolation, and BMS communication.
- **Two Main Classes:** **On-board** (≤22 kW AC) and **Off-board** (≥50 kW DC).
- **Three Power Levels:** Level 1 (slow), Level 2 (fast AC), Level 3 (DC fast).
- **Bidirectional = V2G/V2H:** Requires **DAB topology** and standards like **CHAdeMO** or **ISO 15118**.
- **Standards Pyramid:** SAE J1772, IEC 61851, IEC 62196, ISO 15118, OCPP 1.6/2.0.1.
- **PFC Goal:** PF > 0.99, THD < 5% (IEEE 519 / IEC 61000-3-12).
- **Charging Phases:** Bulk (CC) → Absorption (CV) → Float (trickle).
- **Battery Sweet Spot:** Operate between **20% – 80% SoC** to maximize cycle life.
- **Formula to Remember:**
  - $t_c = \frac{E_{bat} \cdot \Delta SoC}{P_{ch} \cdot \eta_{ch}}$
  - $L_{PFC} = \frac{V_{in,peak} \cdot D \cdot (1-D)}{f_{sw} \cdot \Delta I_L}$
  - $P_{DAB} = \frac{n V_1 V_2 \cdot d(1-d)}{2 f_{sw} L_{lk}}$
- **Key Components:** EMI filter → PFC boost → DC link → Isolated DC-DC → BMS interface.
- **Indian Context:** India uses **CCS2 + Type 2 AC** (BIS standard); **Bharat DC-001** for low-voltage e-rickshaws.
- **Future:** **800V architecture** (Porsche Taycan, Hyundai E-GMP) enables **350 kW ultra-fast charging** with thinner cables.
- **Coupler Pinout (CCS2):** Type 2 AC pins (L1, L2, L3, N, PE) + 2 large DC pins (DC+, DC−) below.

---

> **End of Module 3 — Overview of EV Battery Chargers | KTU 2024 Scheme**

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET742 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES AND RENEWABLE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
