This is the source of all energy in the chain. *(0.5 mark)* **Block 2 — EVSE (Off-board):** Contains an MCB for overcurrent protection, an RCD (30 mA) for ground-fault safety, an EMI filter, a kWh meter, a controller that generates the **Control Pilot (CP) PWM signal** at 1 kHz, ±12 V, and an electromagnetic connector lock. The EVSE performs a **handshake** with the vehicle: it verifies the protective earth connection, reads the cable's current rating via PP, and only closes the contactor after the EV signals it is "ready" (Status B/C). *(1.5 marks)* **Block 3 — Charging Cable & Inlet:** A multi-core cable (L, N, PE + CP + PP) terminated in a Type 2 connector (or Bharat AC-001) that mates with the vehicle's inlet. *(0.5 mark)* **Block 4 — On-Board Charger (OBC):** A two-stage AC/DC converter. **Stage 1** is a **Boost PFC Rectifier** (typically bridgeless topology using SiC MOSFETs) that draws near-sinusoidal current in phase with grid voltage, achieving PF > 0.99 and producing a stable **400 V DC link**. **Stage 2** is an **Isolated DC-DC Converter** (CLLC resonant or Phase-Shifted Full-Bridge) that steps the 400 V bus down (or up) to the battery's variable terminal voltage (250–420 V) and provides galvanic isolation for safety. The OBC is digitally controlled by a DSP. *(2.5 marks)* **Block 5 — Battery Pack + BMS:** The DC output of the OBC is fed through the **main contactors** (positive, negative, pre-charge) into the HV battery pack. The BMS monitors every cell's voltage, temperature, and pack current; computes SoC; and sends a CAN message back to the OBC commanding the charging current (CC phase) or voltage (CV phase). As SoC approaches the target, the BMS tapers the current (CV phase) until termination. The BMS also performs passive or active cell balancing. *(2 marks)* > The **power flow path** (solid arrows): Grid → EVSE → Cable → OBC → BMS contactors → Battery cells. > The **control/signal path** (dotted arrows): BMS ↔ OBC (CAN) ↔ EVSE controller (Pilot) ↔ Grid interface. --- **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Given data:** $$ E_{pack} = 30 \text{ kWh}, \quad SoC_1 = 30\%, \quad SoC_2 = 80\%, \quad P_{OBC} = 3.3 \text{ kW}, \quad \eta_{OBC} = 0.94, \quad V_{grid} = 230 \text{ V}, \quad \cos\phi = 0.95 $$ **Step 1 — Compute the energy to be delivered to the battery.** $$ E_{batt} = (SoC_2 - SoC_1) \cdot E_{pack} = (0.80 - 0.30) \cdot 30 = 15 \text{ kWh} $$ **Step 2 — Compute the energy that must be drawn from the grid.** $$ E_{grid} = \frac{E_{batt}}{\eta_{OBC}} = \frac{15}{0.94} = 15.957 \text{ kWh} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)} $$ **Step 3 — Compute the time of charging.** The power delivered to the battery: $$ P_{batt} = P_{OBC} \cdot \eta_{OBC} = 3.3 \cdot 0.94 = 3.102 \text{ kW} $$ $$ t = \frac{E_{batt}}{P_{batt}} = \frac{15}{3.102} = 4.836 \text{ h} \approx 4 \text{ h } 50 \text{ min} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)} $$ **Step 4 — Compute the grid rms current.** The grid real power: $$ P_{grid} = P_{OBC} / \eta_{OBC} \approx 3.3 / 0.94 = 3.511 \text{ kW} $$ $$ I_{grid} = \frac{P_{grid}}{V_{grid} \cdot \cos\phi} = \frac{3511}{230 \cdot 0.95} = \frac{3511}{218.5} $$ $$ I_{grid} = 16.07 \text{ A} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)} $$ **Step 5 — Conclusion:** The vehicle draws ~16.07 A from a 230 V, single-phase grid. This is within the standard 16 A Indian household socket limit — a perfect application for **Bharat AC-001** standard (230 V, 15 A) home charging. *(1 mark for conclusion + standard reference)* --- **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]* **(a)** With the help of a neat block schematic, describe the power flow during **DC Fast Charging (CCS2 standard)**. Explain how the OBC is bypassed and how the EVSE communicates with the BMS. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A **120 kW** DC fast charger operates from a 3-phase, **415 V, 50 Hz** grid at a power factor of **0.98**. The EV battery is **400 V nominal** and the BMS requests a constant current of **300 A** during the CC phase. Compute: (i) the grid line current, (ii) the overall EVSE efficiency, and (iii) the energy delivered in 20 minutes. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution B:** **(a) DC Fast Charging Power Flow (7 marks):** **Block 1 — AC Grid:** 3-phase, 415 V, 50 Hz. *(0.5 mark)* **Block 2 — AC Switchgear & Filter:** Three-phase isolator, MCB, SPD, and an LCL filter to suppress switching harmonics generated by the AFE. *(1 mark)* **Block 3 — Active Front End (AFE) Rectifier:** A 3-phase, two-level (or three-level NPC) voltage source converter using IGBT/SiC modules. It performs **PFC** (achieves near-unity PF and low THDi) and produces a regulated **~700–800 V DC link**. *(1.5 marks)* **Block 4 — Isolated DC-DC Stage:** A **Dual Active Bridge (DAB)** or **LLC resonant converter** with a high-frequency isolation transformer. It steps the 800 V bus down to the battery terminal voltage (200–500 V) and provides galvanic isolation. The DC-DC stage is wide-output-range to accommodate the battery voltage sweep during SoC. *(1.5 marks)* **Block 5 — CCS2 Dispenser & Liquid-Cooled Cable:** A heavy, water-cooled cable carries the DC current directly to the vehicle's **CCS2 inlet** (the large-diameter DC pins of the Combo 2 connector). The OBC remains **electrically disconnected** — the vehicle's HV bus is exposed to the external charger. *(1 mark)* **Block 6 — Battery Pack + BMS:** The BMS communicates with the EVSE via **ISO 15118 PLC** (Power Line Communication) or dedicated CAN. The handshake negotiates: maximum voltage, maximum current, target SoC, and timing. The BMS sends a **constant current (CC)** request during bulk charging and **constant voltage (CV)** during saturation. The EVSE cannot deliver more than the BMS allows — this is a fundamental safety feature. *(1.5 marks)* --- **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Given:** $$ P_{DC,out} = V_{batt} \cdot I_{DC} = 400 \cdot 300 = 120,000 \text{ W} = 120 \text{ kW} $$ Wait — we must check the actual rated output. The charger is rated 120 kW; the requested DC power is 400 × 300 = 120 kW. So the charger is operating at its **rated output**. **Step 1 — Grid line current.** $$ I_{grid} = \frac{P_{DC,out}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \eta_{EVSE}} $$ We don't yet know $\eta_{EVSE}$, so we must solve iteratively. First, assume $\eta_{EVSE} = 0.95$: $$ I_{grid} = \frac{120,000}{\sqrt{3} \cdot 415 \cdot 0.98 \cdot 0.95} = \frac{120,000}{672.06} = 178.6 \text{ A} \quad \textbf{(3 marks)} $$ **Step 2 — Verify efficiency and compute grid power.** $$ P_{grid} = \sqrt{3} \cdot 415 \cdot 178.6 \cdot 0.98 = 125,909 \text{ W} \approx 125.9 \text{ kW} $$ $$ \eta_{EVSE} = \frac{P_{DC,out}}{P_{grid}} = \frac{120}{125.9} = 0.9532 = 95.32\% $$ This is consistent with the 0.95 assumption. *(2 marks)* **Step 3 — Energy delivered in 20 minutes.** $$ E_{out} = P_{DC,out} \cdot t = 120 \text{ kW} \cdot \frac{20}{60} \text{ h} = 40 \text{ kWh} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)} $$ This 40 kWh would add roughly **240 km** of range to a typical mid-size EV (at 6 km/kWh efficiency). --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU Exams for this Topic:** > > 1. **Confusing OBC with EVSE** — A 7-mark question that asks you to "draw the block diagram" of charging will be marked ZERO if you draw the EVSE instead of the OBC (or vice versa). Read carefully whether the question says "on-board" or "off-board". > > 2. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{3}$ factor** in 3-φ power calculations costs **2 full marks** in a 7-mark numerical. Always state explicitly: *"For a 3-phase balanced system, $P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$."* > > 3. **Mixing up power flow vs. signal flow** in the block diagram. Power flow uses solid arrows; communication (CAN, PLC, PWM pilot) uses **dotted/dashed arrows**. Examiners specifically test this distinction. > > 4. **Ignoring efficiency** in numerical problems. If the question gives you $\eta$ and you use it incorrectly (e.g., divide when you should multiply), you will lose the entire 7-mark sub-question. > > 5. **Writing "battery is charged by the EVSE"** in DC fast charging — this is technically wrong. The battery is charged by the **EVSE's internal converter**, not "by the EVSE" as a passive device. Be precise. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3, Topic: Grid→EVSE→OBC→Battery Power Flow** > > ✅ The full chain is **Grid → EVSE → Cable/Inlet → OBC → BMS/Contactors → Battery Pack** (AC charging path). > > ✅ For **DC fast charging**, the OBC is **electrically bypassed**; the AC/DC conversion happens in the EVSE. > > ✅ The **OBC has two stages**: (i) PFC rectifier (boost, near-unity PF), (ii) Isolated DC-DC (CLLC or PSFB, regulates battery voltage). > > ✅ The **EVSE does NOT do AC/DC conversion** in AC charging — it only does protection, metering, and pilot signaling. > > ✅ The **Control Pilot (CP)** signal is a **1 kHz PWM** whose duty cycle encodes the maximum allowable current (10% = 6 A min; 96% = 80 A max for AC). > > ✅ **Communication protocols:** PWM pilot (basic), CAN (OBC↔BMS), ISO 15118 PLC (EVSE↔EV in DCFC). > > ✅ **Key formula set:** $P_{1\phi} = V I \cos\phi$; $P_{3\phi} = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi$; $E = P t$; $t = E_{batt}/(P \cdot \eta)$; $\eta_{total} = \prod \eta_i$. > > ✅ **Typical efficiencies:** EVSE (AC) ≈ 99% (cabling); EVSE (DCFC) ≈ 95%; OBC ≈ 94–97%; Battery round-trip ≈ 92–95%. > > ✅ **Charging profiles are CC-CV**: Constant Current until ~70–80% SoC, then Constant Voltage tapering to termination current. > > ✅ **India-specific standards to memorize:** **AIS-138** (adopted from IEC 61851), **Bharat AC-001** (3.3 kW), **Bharat DC-001** (15 kW DCFC for 2-/3-wheelers), **CCS2** (BIS mandated for 4-wheelers). > > ✅ **C-rate is a battery-side concept** — 1C means 1 hour to fully charge; 0.5C = 2 hours; 2C = 30 minutes (theoretical, ignoring taper). > > ✅ **800 V architecture** (vs. legacy 400 V) halves the DC current for the same power, reducing cable losses and thermal stress — this is why modern EVs (Ioniq 5, Taycan, e-tron GT) use 800 V.

topics.title: V2G Concept
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET742 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES AND RENEWABLE TECHNOLOGIES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** V2G Concept # ⚡ V2G CONCEPT — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G)** is a smart-grid paradigm in which bidirectional power flow is established between an Electric Vehicle's (EV) onboard energy storage system (typically a Lithium-ion battery pack) and the external electric power grid, through a compliant **bidirectional Electric Vehicle Supply Equipment (EVSE)** and a **smart energy management controller**, governed by the **ISO 15118** communication protocol family. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Intent):** > *V2G is a system in which plug-in electric vehicles, including BEVs and PHEVs, communicate with the power grid to deliver demand response services by either returning electricity to the grid (discharge) or throttling their charging rate (managed charge), effectively turning the EV fleet into a Distributed Energy Resource (DER).* The bidirectional capability is classified into a hierarchy of five operational modes, all of which the KTU 2024 module expects a student to differentiate: | Mode | Full Form | Direction of Power Flow | Primary Use Case | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **G2V** | Grid-to-Vehicle | Grid → EV Battery | Normal EV charging (unidirectional) | | **V2G** | Vehicle-to-Grid | EV Battery → Grid | Grid ancillary services, peak shaving | | **V2H** | Vehicle-to-Home | EV Battery → Home Load | Residential backup power | | **V2L** | Vehicle-to-Load | EV Battery → AC appliance | Outdoor / emergency appliance powering | | **V2V** | Vehicle-to-Vehicle | EV Battery → Another EV | EV-to-EV charging (e.g., during breakdown) | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Power Bank on Wheels" Imagine you own a large **10,000 mAh power bank** (representing your EV battery). Normally, you only ever *charge* it from a wall socket — that is unidirectional G2V charging. Now, picture a world where the **electricity board** can politely ask your power bank to **send some energy back** to the wall socket during peak evening hours (say 6 PM – 10 PM) when everyone is running ACs and lights, and then **re-fill it for free** (or for a credit) late at night when electricity is cheap and abundant. That is **V2G**. Your EV is parked in the garage for **~22 hours a day on average** (a private car is driven less than **5%** of the day). Instead of sitting idle as a dead asset, the dormant battery capacity becomes a **"spinning reserve on wheels"** for the grid operator. > [!NOTE] > **Key Physical Constants & Metrics to Remember:** > - Average private car utilization: **< 5%** of the day > - Typical EV battery capacity range: **30 kWh – 100 kWh** > - Global V2G pilot standard communication protocol: **ISO 15118-20** > - Bidirectional charger efficiency target: **> 92%** (round-trip AC–DC–AC) ## 1.3 Why V2G Matters in the Renewable-Energy Context Renewable sources (solar PV, wind) are **intermittent** — the sun sets just as air-conditioning load peaks. V2G provides a *decentralized, mobile, fast-responding battery buffer* that can absorb midday solar surplus and inject it back at sunset, smoothing the **"Duck Curve"** of net grid load. This is the central reason Module 3 of PEAET742 dedicates a full section to V2G. # 📚 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 The Four Pillars of a Functional V2G System A V2G system is not just a battery and a plug. For a 14-mark KTU answer, you must explicitly cover these four architectural pillars: **Pillar 1 — The Vehicle Side (Onboard)** - **Bidirectional Onboard Charger (OBC)**: A DC-AC inverter + AC-DC rectifier combined, typically **6.6 kW (Level 1 AC) or 11 kW – 22 kW (Level 2 AC)**. It must handle **G2V (rectify) and V2G (invert)** modes. - **Battery Management System (BMS)**: Acts as the "gatekeeper" — communicates State-of-Charge (SoC), State-of-Health (SoH), cell temperature, and enforces safety cutoffs to **prevent deep discharge below 20% SoC** (to protect battery life). - **EV Communication Controller (EVCC)**: The "translator" that speaks ISO 15118 with the grid. **Pillar 2 — The Supply Equipment Side (Offboard / EVSE)** - **SECC (Supply Equipment Communication Controller)**: Pairs with the EVCC over Power Line Communication (PLC) using **ISO 15118**. - **Smart Meter**: Records bidirectional kWh (export and import measured separately). - **Isolation Transformer & Protection Relays**: Galvanic isolation for grid-tie safety (per IEEE 1547). **Pillar 3 — The Grid / Aggregator Side** - **Aggregator**: A licensed entity (utility or third-party, e.g., **Nuvve, OVO Energy, Fermata Energy**) that pools thousands of EVs into a single **Virtual Power Plant (VPP)**. A single EV is too small (< 100 kWh) to trade on wholesale markets; an aggregator can bid **tens of MWh** into the day-ahead market. - **Distribution System Operator (DSO)**: The local grid operator that issues dispatch signals. - **Energy Management System (EMS)**: The cloud-based software that decides in real-time *which* EV should discharge, *how much*, and *for how long*. **Pillar 4 — The Communication Protocol Stack** - **ISO 15118-1** to **ISO 15118-20**: The international standard for V2G communication. Uses **Power Line Communication (PLC)** over the Control Pilot pin (per IEC 61851-1). - **OCPP 2.0.1** (Open Charge Point Protocol): Communication between the EVSE and the central management system. - **IEEE 2030.5** / **OpenADR 2.0b**: Higher-level demand-response signaling from the utility to the aggregator. ## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step V2G Discharge Event A typical V2G discharge transaction, broken down for KTU theory questions: 1. **Handshake**: EV plugged in. EVCC and SECC perform **SLAC (Signal Level Attenuation Characterization)** to establish PLC link. 2. **Capability Negotiation**: EVCC advertises: `MaxDischargePower = 11 kW`, `MinSoC = 30%`, `MaxSoC = 90%`, `AvailableCapacity = 45 kWh`. 3. **Contract Setup**: Aggregator sends a `ChargeParameterDiscoveryReq` with price signals and target SoC. 4. **Grid Dispatch**: DSO issues a frequency regulation signal. EMS computes the optimal discharge profile. 5. **Power Conversion**: Bidirectional OBC inverts DC battery voltage (~ 400 V) to AC grid voltage (230 V / 50 Hz in India). Power flows: Battery DC → OBC Inverter → EVSE → Home/Grid. 6. **Metering & Settlement**: Smart meter records kWh exported. Aggregator settles payment in **15-minute market intervals** (typical Indian and EU spot-market granularity). 7. **Smart Re-Charge**: At low-tariff night hours, the system auto-recharges the EV back to the user's required morning SoC. ## 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Memorize the table below. These five equations cover > 90% of V2G calculation/numerical questions in KTU exams.** | # | Formula | Symbol Legend | Units | Engineering Use | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $E_{available} = (SoC_{current} - SoC_{min}) \times C_{bat}$ | $E$ = usable energy, $C_{bat}$ = battery capacity | kWh | Compute how much energy an EV can safely export | | 2 | $P_{V2G} = V_{grid} \times I_{grid} \times \eta_{inv} \times \cos\phi$ | $V$ = grid voltage, $I$ = current, $\eta_{inv}$ = inverter efficiency, $\cos\phi$ = power factor | kW | Active power delivered back to grid | | 3 | $C_{VPP} = N_{EV} \times E_{available,avg}$ | $N_{EV}$ = number of EVs in aggregator fleet, $E$ = avg available per EV | kWh | Total capacity of a Virtual Power Plant | | 4 | $N_{cycles} = \frac{E_{discharged,total}}{C_{bat}}$ | Cumulative discharged energy normalized to full cycles | dimensionless | Quantify battery wear from V2G participation | | 5 | $E_{revenue} = E_{exported} \times (P_{peak} - P_{offpeak})$ | $P$ = electricity tariffs in respective slots | ₹ / $ | Economic value of a single V2G session | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Boundary Conditions (frequently asked as "State the limits"):** > - Minimum operational SoC: **20%** (safety floor to prevent Li-ion plating and deep-discharge damage) > - Maximum operational SoC: **90%** (avoid parking at 100% to reduce calendar aging) > - Typical Round-Trip Efficiency (RTE) target: **85% – 92%** > - Maximum continuous V2G discharge rate: limited by **OBC rating**, typically **6.6 kW (1-phase)** or **11 kW / 22 kW (3-phase)** ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility V2G is the cornerstone of the **"Prosumer"** economy. The same physical infrastructure that moves your EV charges you for grid services. In Kerala, where **KSEB** is actively piloting rooftop solar + battery storage under the **Soura scheme**, V2G-ready EVs (such as the **Hyundai Ioniq 5**, **MG ZS EV**, and **BYD Atto 3**) can be aggregated to provide district-level frequency support, especially during the **6 PM – 9 PM peak demand window** when solar generation collapses. # 🧮 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX > [!NOTE] > **The following derivations are the most commonly asked numerical sub-parts in KTU 14-mark V2G questions. Work through every line.** ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Usable V2G Energy from a Single EV **Problem Statement (Typical KTU Style):** An EV has a battery capacity of **60 kWh**. The BMS enforces a minimum SoC of **20%** to protect the pack. If the EV is plugged in at **80% SoC**, calculate the energy available for V2G discharge. **Step 1 — Identify Given Quantities** - $C_{bat} = 60 \text{ kWh}$ (battery nameplate capacity) - $SoC_{current} = 80\% = 0.80$ - $SoC_{min} = 20\% = 0.20$ **Step 2 — Write the Governing Equation** The energy that can be safely discharged back to the grid is the *delta* between the current state of charge and the minimum allowable state of charge, scaled by the full battery capacity: $$ E_{available} = (SoC_{current} - SoC_{min}) \times C_{bat} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute the Values** First, compute the SoC difference (this is the fraction of the battery that is "free" to use): $$ SoC_{delta} = 0.80 - 0.20 = 0.60 $$ This means 60% of the battery is the dispatchable window. **Step 4 — Multiply by Battery Capacity** $$ E_{available} = 0.60 \times 60 \text{ kWh} $$ $$ E_{available} = 36 \text{ kWh} $$ **Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation** Out of a 60 kWh nameplate pack, only **36 kWh** can actually be exported to the grid. The remaining **12 kWh** (the 20% floor) is *non-negotiable reserve* — the BMS will physically prevent further discharge to avoid damaging the lithium-ion cells. The 60 kWh → 36 kWh ratio (**60% usable fraction**) is a textbook KTU benchmark value. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Aggregated Virtual Power Plant (VPP) Capacity **Problem Statement:** An aggregator enrolls **500 EVs**, each with an average available V2G energy of **36 kWh** (as computed in 3.1) and an average onboard charger rating of **11 kW**. (a) Calculate the total energy capacity of the VPP. (b) Calculate the maximum simultaneous power the VPP can deliver to the grid. **Part (a) — Total Energy Capacity** The VPP is simply the linear sum of the dispatchable windows of every connected EV. Mathematically: $$ C_{VPP} = N_{EV} \times E_{available,avg} $$ Substitute $N_{EV} = 500$ and $E_{available,avg} = 36 \text{ kWh}$: $$ C_{VPP} = 500 \times 36 \text{ kWh} $$ $$ C_{VPP} = 18{,}000 \text{ kWh} = 18 \text{ MWh} $$ **Part (b) — Maximum Simultaneous Power** The power rating of the VPP is determined by the *bottleneck* — the slowest charging port. Since all EVs are AC-connected at 11 kW: $$ P_{VPP,max} = N_{EV} \times P_{OBC,avg} $$ $$ P_{VPP,max} = 500 \times 11 \text{ kW} $$ $$ P_{VPP,max} = 5{,}500 \text{ kW} = 5.5 \text{ MW} $$ **Engineering Interpretation:** A 5.5 MW / 18 MWh aggregated fleet is large enough to participate in India's **Day-Ahead Market (DAM)** on the **Indian Energy Exchange (IEX)**. The minimum bid size on IEX is **0.1 MW (100 kW) for solar/wind**, but V2G aggregators can typically bid in **1 MW blocks**. This is exactly why V2G is commercially viable *only* through aggregation — a single 11 kW EV is invisible to the grid operator. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Battery Degradation Cost of V2G Participation This is the **most important** analytical sub-question because it tests whether you understand the *trade-off* at the heart of V2G economics. **Problem Statement:** An EV battery rated at **60 kWh** undergoes **200 full equivalent cycles per year** due to daily V2G participation. Each full cycle consumes approximately **0.02%** of total battery capacity (a literature-accepted rule of thumb for NMC chemistry under moderate DoD). (a) Capacity loss per year. (b) Years to reach 80% SoH (industry "end-of-life" threshold). **Part (a) — Annual Capacity Loss** Each cycle erodes 0.02% of the original 60 kWh capacity: $$ \Delta C_{per\,cycle} = 0.0002 \times 60 \text{ kWh} = 0.012 \text{ kWh} $$ Annual loss is the per-cycle loss times the number of cycles: $$ \Delta C_{annual} = 200 \times 0.012 \text{ kWh} = 2.4 \text{ kWh} $$ **Part (b) — Time to Reach End-of-Life (80% SoH)** The battery is "dead" (for automotive use) when it has lost **20% of its original capacity**, i.e., 12 kWh out of 60 kWh. $$ N_{years} = \frac{12 \text{ kWh}}{2.4 \text{ kWh/year}} = 5 \text{ years} $$ **Engineering Interpretation:** Five years is shorter than the typical **8-year / 1,60,000 km** automotive warranty. This is why **warranty clauses** in OEM contracts (Tata, MG, Hyundai) currently **void the battery warranty if commercial V2G is used without OEM-certified hardware and aggregator partnership**. Modern research shows that *smart* V2G (DoD limited to 30–50%, with thermal management) can reduce degradation to under **0.01% per cycle**, extending life to **10+ years**, which is the target the KTU 2024 module emphasizes. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — V2G vs. Stationary Battery Storage A frequently asked KTU comparison question. Use this matrix verbatim in 14-mark answers: | Parameter | V2G (Mobile Battery) | Stationary BESS (e.g., Tesla Powerwall) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Capital Cost | **Zero marginal** (asset already exists) | ₹ 5–10 Lakh per 10 kWh | | Energy Capacity | **30–100 kWh per vehicle** | Typically 5–20 kWh per unit | | Location | Mobile, follows user | Fixed at installation site | | Availability | **Intermittent** (driven away daily) | **24/7** dispatchable | | Round-Trip Efficiency | 85–92% | 90–95% | | Battery Degradation Impact | User-visible (range loss) | Hidden / behind-the-meter | | Aggregator Needed | **Mandatory** (economies of scale) | Not mandatory for single home | | Best Use Case | **Frequency regulation, VPP** | **Self-consumption, backup** | # 🗺️ STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS > [!NOTE] > **KTU theory questions frequently expect hand-drawn or ASCII block diagrams. The Mermaid block diagrams below model the same physical architecture in a KTU-evaluator-friendly format.** ## 4.1 System-Level V2G Architecture (Functional Block Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[EV Battery Pack
60 kWh Li-ion] --> B[BMS
Battery Management System] B --> C[Bidirectional
Onboard Charger] C --> D[EVSE
AC Bidirectional Charger] D --> E[Smart Energy Meter
Bidirectional kWh] E --> F[Home Load Panel
230V AC] E --> G[Distribution Transformer
11kV to 230V] G --> H[Grid Substation
132kV / 66kV] H --> I[State Load Dispatch Center
SLDC Kerala] I --> J[Aggregator Cloud EMS
Virtual Power Plant] J -. Real-time price signal .-> G J -. ISO 15118 PLC .-> D D -. ISO 15118 PLC .-> C C -. CAN Bus SoC Telemetry .-> B ``` **Reading the diagram:** - **Solid arrows** = bidirectional AC/DC power flow. - **Dotted arrows** = communication / control signaling. - The BMS is the *only* entity that "sees" the cell-level voltage; everything else interacts through aggregated telemetry. --- ## 4.2 V2G Communication Protocol Stack ```mermaid flowchart TB Layer1[Application Layer
ISO 15118-20 XML Messages] --> Layer2 Layer2[Transport Layer
TLS 1.3 Encrypted] --> Layer3 Layer3[Network Layer
IPv6 over PLC] --> Layer4 Layer4[Physical Layer
HomePlug Green PHY on Control Pilot Pin] Layer4 -. Coupled via .-> CPin[Control Pilot Pin
IEC 61851-1] CPin --> Cable[EV Charging Cable] Cable --> EVCC ``` **Why this matters for KTU:** The KTU 2024 module specifically tests whether the student understands that **the same two pins** that carry the unidirectional PWM pilot signal in a normal charger (per IEC 61851-1) are reused in V2G to carry high-bandwidth **PLC data** for ISO 15118 handshaking, billing, and certificate exchange. This is a *clever reuse of existing hardware*. --- ## 4.3 V2G Power Flow Decision Tree (When does an EV Discharge?) ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([EV Plugged In]) --> Q1{Grid Frequency
50 Hz?} Q1 -->|Below 49.8 Hz| Dis1[Inject Active Power
V2G Discharge] Q1 -->|Normal 50 Hz| Q2{Time of Day?} Q1 -->|Above 50.2 Hz| Char1[Throttle or Absorb
G2V Charge] Q2 -->|Peak 6PM to 10PM| Dis2[Discharge if Tariff
High Signal Received] Q2 -->|Off-Peak 11PM to 6AM| Char2[Recharge from Grid
Low Tariff] Q2 -->|Daytime 10AM to 4PM| Q3{Solar Surplus
Available?} Q3 -->|Yes| Char3[Absorb Solar Export
to EV Battery] Q3 -->|No| Idle[Remain Idle at
Setpoint SoC] Dis1 --> Settle[Aggregator Settles
15-min Market Block] Dis2 --> Settle Char1 --> Settle Char2 --> Settle Char3 --> Settle Idle --> End([Parked]) Settle --> End ``` **Examiner's Tip:** A diagram of this form, even in rough hand-drawn form, is worth **3–4 easy marks** in a 14-mark question on "Explain the V2G operating modes with a flowchart." # 📝 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP --- ## **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)** ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]** **Define Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G). List any four operational modes of bidirectional power transfer involving an EV.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G) is a smart energy paradigm in which a plug-in electric vehicle, through a bidirectional EVSE and the ISO 15118 communication protocol, is capable of *both* drawing power from the grid for charging (G2V) *and* discharging stored DC energy back to the grid (or to a local load) to provide ancillary services. The four operational modes are: 1. **G2V** — Grid-to-Vehicle (unidirectional charging). 2. **V2G** — Vehicle-to-Grid (bidirectional, exports to the grid). 3. **V2H** — Vehicle-to-Home (powers the residential load). 4. **V2L** — Vehicle-to-Load (powers portable AC appliances). *(V2V — Vehicle-to-Vehicle is also accepted as a fifth mode.)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** **What is the role of an *aggregator* in a V2G ecosystem? Why is aggregation commercially necessary?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** An **aggregator** is a licensed commercial entity that pools the individual battery capacities of thousands of EVs into a single, dispatchable **Virtual Power Plant (VPP)**. It acts as the intermediary between the EV owners and the grid operator / wholesale electricity market. Aggregation is commercially necessary because: 1. A single EV (10–100 kWh) is too small to bid into the day-ahead wholesale electricity market (minimum bid size typically 0.1–1 MW). 2. Individual EV availability is stochastic (owners drive away). Aggregation averages out uncertainty — out of 1,000 EVs, statistically 700 will be plugged in at any moment. 3. The aggregator provides a single **SLA (Service Level Agreement)** interface to the grid, reducing transaction costs. --- ## **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)** > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Module 3 question carries 14 marks with internal choice. Each question has two sub-parts: (a) 7 marks, (b) 7 marks. The choice is between two full questions, not between sub-parts. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a V2G system. Identify and briefly describe the function of any five major components. *(7 marks)* **(b)** An EV with a 75 kWh battery is plugged in at 85% SoC. The BMS enforces a minimum SoC of 20%. If the bidirectional onboard charger is rated at 11 kW with a round-trip efficiency of 90%, calculate: (i) The energy available for V2G discharge. (ii) The maximum duration of the discharge session. (iii) The actual AC energy delivered to the grid accounting for inverter losses. *(7 marks)* --- #### **Model Solution — Q3(a) — 7 marks** **Introduction (1 mark):** V2G architecture integrates the EV, the bidirectional EVSE, communication networks, and the grid/aggregator platform into a unified system. **Block Diagram (3 marks):** The student should draw the standard 4-pillar architecture: **EV Side (Battery + BMS + Bidirectional OBC + EVCC) → EVSE Side (SECC + Smart Meter + Protection) → Communication Layer (ISO 15118 PLC + OCPP) → Grid / Aggregator Side (DSO + EMS + VPP)**. A labelled diagram showing power flow (solid arrows) and control signals (dotted arrows) gets full marks. **Five Major Components (3 marks — 0.6 each):** 1. **Bidirectional Onboard Charger (OBC)**: Performs both AC-DC rectification (G2V) and DC-AC inversion (V2G). Typically 6.6 kW or 11 kW. 2. **Battery Management System (BMS)**: Monitors cell voltages, temperatures, SoC, SoH. Enforces safety cutoffs. 3. **EV Communication Controller (EVCC)**: Speaks ISO 15118 with the grid. Handles authentication, contract negotiation, and power profile scheduling. 4. **Aggregator / EMS**: Cloud-based software that optimizes charging/discharging schedules across thousands of vehicles and bids into the wholesale market. 5. **Smart Meter**: Records bidirectional kWh separately for billing and settlement in 15-minute market blocks. --- #### **Model Solution — Q3(b) — 7 marks** **Given Data:** - $C_{bat} = 75 \text{ kWh}$ - $SoC_{current} = 85\% = 0.85$ - $SoC_{min} = 20\% = 0.20$ - $P_{OBC} = 11 \text{ kW}$ - $\eta_{RTE} = 90\% = 0.90$ (round-trip efficiency; we approximate inverter stage efficiency for AC delivery as $\eta_{inv} \approx \sqrt{0.90} \approx 0.95$) **Part (i) — Usable V2G Energy (2 marks):** $$ E_{available} = (SoC_{current} - SoC_{min}) \times C_{bat} $$ $$ E_{available} = (0.85 - 0.20) \times 75 \text{ kWh} = 0.65 \times 75 $$ $$ E_{available} = 48.75 \text{ kWh} $$ **Part (ii) — Maximum Discharge Duration (2 marks):** $$ t_{max} = \frac{E_{available}}{P_{OBC}} $$ $$ t_{max} = \frac{48.75 \text{ kWh}}{11 \text{ kW}} = 4.4318 \text{ hours} \approx 4 \text{ h } 26 \text{ min} $$ **Part (iii) — AC Energy Delivered to Grid (3 marks):** Applying inverter efficiency to the DC energy leaving the battery: $$ E_{AC,grid} = E_{available} \times \eta_{inv} $$ $$ E_{AC,grid} = 48.75 \text{ kWh} \times 0.95 = 46.3125 \text{ kWh} $$ **Engineering Interpretation (bonus insight, not mandatory for marks):** Out of the 48.75 kWh drawn from the battery, only 46.31 kWh reaches the grid as usable AC energy. The remaining 2.44 kWh is lost as heat in the inverter's IGBT/MOSFET switches. This **5% loss** is the *cost* of doing V2G and is a key parameter in any economic feasibility study. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative** **Q4. [KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model]** **(a)** Compare V2G with stationary Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS) under the following heads: capital cost, mobility, availability, round-trip efficiency, and typical use case. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Explain the role of the **ISO 15118** communication protocol in V2G. How does it differ from the basic IEC 61851-1 pilot signaling used in ordinary Level 2 AC chargers? *(7 marks)* --- #### **Model Solution — Q4(a) — 7 marks** A tabular comparison is the cleanest presentation. Award 1.4 marks per row × 5 rows = 7 marks. | Parameter | V2G (EV Battery) | Stationary BESS | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Capital Cost** | Zero marginal — the battery is already paid for by the car buyer | ₹ 5–10 Lakh per 10 kWh unit installed | | **Mobility** | Mobile — follows the user; can be relocated to any charging point | Fixed at one site for its lifetime | | **Availability** | Intermittent — unavailable when vehicle is driven | 24/7 dispatchable; always at the site | | **Round-Trip Efficiency** | 85–92% (extra losses through OBC + EVSE chain) | 90–95% (fewer conversion stages) | | **Typical Use Case** | Frequency regulation, VPP, peak shaving for utilities | Home self-consumption, UPS, off-grid backup | **Conclusion (built into the table):** V2G and BESS are **complementary, not competing**. A smart microgrid of the future will combine both: stationary BESS for baseline reliability and aggregated V2G for high-power, short-duration grid services. --- #### **Model Solution — Q4(b) — 7 marks** **What is ISO 15118? (2 marks):** ISO 15118 is the international standard for "Vehicle to Grid Communication Interface." It defines a complete protocol stack — from the physical layer up to the application-layer XML messages — that enables **Plug & Charge**, **bidirectional power transfer scheduling**, **secure authentication using digital certificates (PKI)**, and **contract-based billing** between an EV, an EVSE, and a backend management system. **How it differs from IEC 61851-1 (3 marks):** - **IEC 61851-1** is a *basic control pilot* standard. It uses a simple **1 kHz PWM signal** on the Control Pilot pin, where the **duty cycle** (10% to 96%) communicates only the *maximum allowable current* (e.g., 16 A, 32 A). It supports **no authentication, no billing, no bidirectional control**. - **ISO 15118** is a **high-bandwidth digital protocol** that rides on top of the same Control Pilot pin using **HomePlug Green PHY PLC** (Power Line Communication). It supports **TCP/IP**, **TLS 1.3 encryption**, and **XML-based messages** for rich data exchange. Critically, ISO 15118 supports **bidirectional power profile scheduling** (Schedules.json), which IEC 61851-1 cannot. **Why this matters for V2G (2 marks):** V2G requires the EV to *negotiate* a discharge schedule, *authenticate* the contract holder, *meter* exported energy precisely, and *settle* payments per kWh exported. None of this is possible with the simple analog PWM of IEC 61851-1. Hence **ISO 15118 is the indispensable nervous system of V2G**, while IEC 61851-1 is just the basic "handshake." --- > [!WARNING] > **🔥 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing SoC with SoH** — *State of Charge* is the *current* energy level (like a fuel gauge). *State of Health* is the *degradation* level relative to a new battery. Examiners explicitly deduct 1 mark for swapping these. > 2. **Forgetting the SoC floor** — Writing $E_{available} = SoC \times C_{bat}$ instead of $(SoC_{current} - SoC_{min}) \times C_{bat}$ is a **2-mark deduction** on numericals. Always subtract the 20% safety floor. > 3. **Saying "V2G and BESS are the same"** — They are not. BESS is *stationary* and *always available*; V2G is *mobile* and *intermittent*. Marks lost: 2. > 4. **Not mentioning ISO 15118 in V2G answers** — This is the *expected standard* answer. A V2G explanation without ISO 15118 is considered incomplete for full marks. > 5. **Ignoring battery degradation** — Saying V2G is "free money" without addressing the wear cost shows a lack of engineering depth. Always mention **cycle life vs. depth-of-discharge trade-off**. --- ## 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST - **V2G** = Bidirectional EV ↔ Grid power transfer, standardized by **ISO 15118**. - **Five modes**: G2V, V2G, V2H, V2L, V2V — know the full form and direction of *each*. - **Three operational pillars** of V2G: *Vehicle Side* (BMS + Bidirectional OBC), *EVSE Side* (SECC + Smart Meter), *Grid Side* (Aggregator + DSO + EMS). - **Aggregator** pools EVs into a **Virtual Power Plant (VPP)** — the only commercially viable path to wholesale markets. - **SoC operating window** = **20% to 90%** — never charge to 100% or discharge below 20% to protect the Li-ion pack. - **Master formula for usable V2G energy**: $E_{available} = (SoC_{current} - SoC_{min}) \times C_{bat}$ - **VPP capacity formula**: $C_{VPP} = N_{EV} \times E_{available,avg}$ ; **VPP power**: $P_{VPP} = N_{EV} \times P_{OBC,avg}$ - **Round-Trip Efficiency** is **85–92%** — losses occur in the bidirectional inverter's IGBT switches. - **ISO 15118 vs. IEC 61851-1**: Analog PWM (basic) vs. Digital PLC + TLS 1.3 + XML (rich, bidirectional). - **Battery degradation** from V2G is real but manageable — smart DoD-limited cycling can keep wear under **0.01% per cycle**. - **Real-world V2G pilots** to mention: *Hyundai Ioniq 5 in Korea, Nissan Leaf in UK (V2G trial), Fermata Energy, Nuvve (Denmark/UK), KSEB Soura + EV pilots in Kerala.* - **Examiner's mantra**: *Always mention the three things — the standard (ISO 15118), the safety floor (SoC 20%), and the aggregator (VPP).*

topics.title: Introduction to AC and DC Charging Stations (Basics and Block Diagram Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET742 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES AND RENEWABLE TECHNOLOGIES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Introduction to AC and DC Charging Stations (Basics and Block Diagram Only) # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition An **Electric Vehicle Supply Equipment (EVSE)**, commonly referred to as a charging station, is a dedicated piece of electrical infrastructure that draws power from the utility grid (or a renewable source) and delivers controlled electrical energy to recharge the **traction battery pack** of an Electric Vehicle (EV). Based on the form of electrical energy delivered to the vehicle, charging stations are classified into two primary categories: ### 1.1.1 AC Charging Station (Slow / Level 1 & Level 2) An **AC Charging Station** supplies Alternating Current (AC) directly from the grid to the vehicle's on-board charger (OBC). The rectification and DC voltage regulation occur **inside the vehicle** through the built-in OBC before the energy reaches the battery. Power levels are typically bounded by the rating of the OBC (commonly **3.3 kW to 22 kW**). ### 1.1.2 DC Charging Station (Fast / Level 3) A **DC Charging Station** (also known as a DC Fast Charger or DCFC) performs the AC-to-DC conversion **externally within the charging station itself**, and supplies regulated **DC power directly to the battery**, bypassing the vehicle's on-board charger. Power levels typically range from **50 kW to 350 kW** (and up to **400 kW+** under CCS2 HPC standards). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The module explicitly restricts the scope to **"Basics and Block Diagram Only."** Therefore, deep mathematical analysis of power electronics (PWM, PLL, harmonic distortion) is **out of scope**. Focus on power flow, block-level architecture, and standard classifications. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Refilling Station" Model Imagine a city water tanker filling system: - **AC Charging Station = A Bathroom Tap with a Small Bucket (the OBC inside the car).** The tap provides pressurized water (AC grid power), but you must first pour it into a small bucket (the on-board charger), which filters and channels the water (rectifies it) into a large overhead tank (the battery). The flow rate is limited by the bucket's size (OBC rating, usually 3.3–22 kW). - **DC Charging Station = A High-Pressure Fire Hose Direct to the Tank.** A massive pumping truck (the station's internal rectifier) converts the water pressure and feeds it *directly* into the overhead tank (battery). The fill rate is enormous because the limiting valve is in the truck, not the car (50–350 kW). This analogy highlights the **central architectural difference**: *where* the AC→DC conversion happens (inside the car vs. inside the station). > [!NOTE] > **Key Industry Standards (Must Memorize for KTU):** > - **SAE J1772 (Type 1):** AC charging connector, common in North America/Japan (single-phase). > - **IEC 62196 (Type 2 / Mennekes):** AC charging connector, standard in Europe and mandated in India for AC. > - **CCS2 (Combined Charging System 2):** DC fast charging, mandatory in India for new EVs from 2025 (per AIS-156 amendment). > - **CHAdeMO:** Japanese DC fast-charging standard. > - **GB/T:** Chinese DC standard. --- ## 1.3 Classification Summary Table | Charging Type | Mode | Power Range | Location of Rectifier | Typical Charging Time (for 60 kWh Battery) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **AC Level 1** | Slow | **1.4 kW – 2.4 kW** (120 V, 12–20 A) | Inside Vehicle (OBC) | **20 – 30 hours** | | **AC Level 2** | Semi-Fast | **3.3 kW – 22 kW** (240 V, up to 32 A) | Inside Vehicle (OBC) | **3 – 8 hours** | | **DC Fast (Level 3)** | Fast | **50 kW – 150 kW** | Inside Charging Station | **20 – 60 minutes** | | **DC High-Power (HPC)** | Ultra-Fast | **150 kW – 400 kW+** | Inside Charging Station | **8 – 15 minutes** (10–80% SoC) | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic — "How" the Two Systems Differ ### 2.1.1 AC Charging Station — The "Dumb Pipe" Architecture An AC EVSE is often called a **"dumb pipe"** in the industry because it performs minimal active power processing. The detailed logical steps are: 1. **Grid Interface:** Three-phase or single-phase AC supply is drawn from the low-voltage utility distribution network. 2. **Protection & Isolation:** A Residual Current Device (RCD), MCB, and isolation transformer provide safety against ground faults and over-currents. 3. **Control Pilot Communication (IEC 61851 PWM):** The EVSE generates a 1 kHz, ±12 V PWM signal on the pilot wire. The duty cycle encodes the **maximum allowable current** the EV is permitted to draw. The EV modulates this signal to request its desired current. 4. **Contactor Closure:** Once handshaking is complete, the contactor closes, and AC power flows through the charging cable to the vehicle inlet. 5. **Vehicle-Side Processing:** The on-board charger (OBC) — a high-frequency switched-mode power supply — rectifies the AC to DC and regulates it via a PFC boost stage + isolated DC-DC converter to match the battery's required charging profile (typically CC-CV). ### 2.1.2 DC Charging Station — The "Smart Power Plant" Architecture A DC fast charger is an **active power-electronics system** and contains the following logical blocks: 1. **Grid Interface + Active Front-End (AFE) Rectifier:** A 3-phase IGBT-based PWM rectifier converts the grid AC to a stable DC bus (typically 700–800 V DC). It also performs **Power Factor Correction (PFC)** to maintain near-unity power factor. 2. **DC-Link Capacitor Bank:** A large electrolytic/film capacitor bank smooths the rectified DC and acts as an energy buffer. 3. **Isolated DC-DC Converter Stage:** A high-frequency, isolated DC-DC converter (typically LLC resonant or Dual Active Bridge — DAB topology) steps the DC-link voltage down (or up) to the precise voltage required by the EV battery. 4. **CAN / PLC Communication (ISO 15118 / DIN 70121):** Digital high-level communication occurs over the charging cable (Power Line Communication). The EV and charger negotiate **battery SoH, maximum voltage, maximum current, and target SoC** dynamically. 5. **Output Contactor + Cable Assembly:** Regulated DC power is delivered to the battery through liquid-cooled high-power cables (especially for HPC ≥ 150 kW). 6. **Thermal Management:** Liquid cooling of power modules, inductors, and charging cables is mandatory at high power to prevent derating. > [!NOTE] > **Why is the OBC limited in AC charging?** > The OBC must fit within the vehicle's chassis, be lightweight, and dissipate heat safely in a sealed environment. This is why the OBC is typically capped at **~22 kW** (and ~11 kW in most Indian EVs like Tata Nexon EV, MG ZS EV). The DC charger has no such space/weight constraint because it sits on a concrete plinth, allowing liquid cooling and bulkier magnetics. --- ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application / Boundary Condition | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Charging Time | $t = \dfrac{C \times DoD}{\eta_{chg} \times I}$ | hours (h) | $C$ = Battery capacity (Ah); $DoD$ = Depth of Discharge (0–1); $I$ = Charging current (A); $\eta_{chg}$ = Charger efficiency (typically 0.85–0.95) | | 2 | Energy Delivered | $E = V_{bat} \times I \times t$ | kWh | Used to compute cost of charging at ₹/kWh tariff | | 3 | DC Power at Battery | $P_{DC} = V_{bat} \times I_{bat}$ | kW | Directly determines the **C-rate** of the battery | | 4 | C-Rate | $C_{rate} = \dfrac{I}{C_{Ah}}$ | dimensionless (1/h) | E.g., a 60 Ah battery charged at 120 A has 2C rate | | 5 | Charging Current Limit (AC) | $I_{max} = \dfrac{P_{OBC}}{V_{grid} \times \cos\phi}$ | A | $\cos\phi$ ≈ 1 for PFC OBC; limits AC charging to OBC rating | | 6 | State of Charge (SoC) | $SoC(t) = SoC_0 + \dfrac{\int_0^t I \cdot dt}{C_{Ah}}$ | % | Integration-based; BMS uses Coulomb counting | | 7 | Thermal Heat Generation (loss) | $P_{loss} = P_{in} \times (1 - \eta)$ | W | Determines cooling system design in DCFC | | 8 | Energy from Grid (1 month) | $E_{grid} = n \times C_{bat} \times V_{bat}$ | kWh | $n$ = number of charging cycles/month; $V_{bat}$ in kV | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL LaTeX Rule for KTU Exams:** Never use the `|` symbol in your exam answer sheets for absolute value. Always write $\vert x \vert$ instead. The same applies to determinants — use $\vert A \vert$ not $|A|$. This is a common reason students lose marks in scanned answer sheets. --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **AC Stations** dominate the **residential and workplace** charging market (overnight charging at home, 8-hour office charging). - **DC Stations** dominate the **highway corridor and commercial fleet** market (logistics trucks, intercity taxi operators, highway rest-stops). - In **India**, the Ministry of Heavy Industries mandates that public charging stations must follow **Bharat DC-001** (15 kW, GB/T protocol — for older 2W/3W/4W), **CCS2** (for 4W passenger cars), and **Type 2 AC** standards. - The **Tata Power EZ Charge, Statiq, Ather Grid, and BPCL E-Drive** networks are real-world deployments of both architectures. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > **Scope Rationale:** As per the KTU 2024 syllabus, this module demands **"Basics and Block Diagram Only."** Therefore, exhaustive derivations of converter topologies (LLC, DAB, PFC) are **out of scope**. Instead, this section provides an **analytical matrix of comparisons** and **worked numerical examples** on charging time and energy — both highly likely to be asked as 3-mark short problems in KTU Part A. --- ## 3.1 Analytical Comparison Matrix — AC vs. DC Charging | Parameter | AC Charging Station | DC Charging Station | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Power Flow Direction** | Grid → Cable → **OBC (in car)** → Battery | Grid → **Rectifier (in station)** → Cable → Battery | | **Location of AC-DC Conversion** | Inside the EV (On-Board Charger) | Inside the Charging Station | | **Typical Power Rating** | 3.3 kW – 22 kW | 50 kW – 400 kW | | **Communication Protocol** | PWM Pilot (IEC 61851) — analog, simple | CAN / PLC (ISO 15118) — digital, bidirectional | | **Charging Connector** | Type 1 (SAE J1772) or Type 2 (IEC 62196) | CCS1, CCS2, CHAdeMO, GB/T, NACS | | **Cost of Charger (Approx.)** | ₹ 25,000 – ₹ 1,50,000 | ₹ 5,00,000 – ₹ 50,00,000+ (per unit) | | **Time for 80% SoC (60 kWh battery)** | 4 – 10 hours | 15 – 50 minutes | | **Impact on Battery Degradation** | Low (low C-rate, ≤ 0.5C) | Higher (C-rate ≥ 1C; thermal stress) | | **Grid Impact** | Low; manageable at residential feeders | High; often requires dedicated 11 kV / 33 kV feeder | | **Power Factor / Harmonics** | OBC is high-PFC (>0.99) | AFE rectifier with active PFC (>0.98) | | **Use Case** | Homes, offices, malls, hotels | Highway corridors, depots, fleet hubs | --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 1 — AC Charging Time **Problem:** A Tata Nexon EV has a **30.2 kWh** lithium-ion battery. It is charged using a **7.2 kW AC home charger** (single-phase, 230 V, 32 A). The charger efficiency is **90%** and the depth of discharge (DoD) is **80%**. Calculate the time required to fully charge from 20% SoC to 100% SoC. **Step 1:** Identify the energy to be delivered. $$E_{deliver} = C_{bat} \times DoD = 30.2 \text{ kWh} \times 0.80 = 24.16 \text{ kWh}$$ **Step 2:** Account for charger efficiency. The *useful* energy delivered to the battery is $E_{deliver}$, but the charger draws more from the grid due to losses. $$P_{actual} = P_{rated} \times \eta_{chg} = 7.2 \text{ kW} \times 0.90 = 6.48 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 3:** Apply the fundamental energy-time relationship. $$t = \dfrac{E_{deliver}}{P_{actual}} = \dfrac{24.16 \text{ kWh}}{6.48 \text{ kW}}$$ $$t = 3.728 \text{ hours} \approx 3 \text{ hours } 44 \text{ minutes}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Validation Tip:** In Part A problems, KTU examiners expect you to show all three steps. Many students skip Step 2 (efficiency consideration) and lose 1 full mark. Always check whether the question gives you efficiency. --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 2 — DC Fast Charging Energy Cost **Problem:** An MG ZS EV has a **50.3 kWh** battery. It is DC fast charged from **20% to 80% SoC** at a **60 kW** charging station. The station's efficiency is **92%**, and the electricity tariff is **₹ 18 per kWh**. Calculate: (a) the energy delivered to the battery, (b) the energy drawn from the grid, and (c) the charging cost in INR. **Step 1:** Energy delivered to the battery. $$E_{bat} = 50.3 \text{ kWh} \times (0.80 - 0.20) = 50.3 \times 0.60 = 30.18 \text{ kWh}$$ **Step 2:** Energy drawn from the grid (accounting for station losses). $$E_{grid} = \dfrac{E_{bat}}{\eta_{sta}} = \dfrac{30.18}{0.92}$$ $$E_{grid} = 32.80 \text{ kWh}$$ **Step 3:** Charging cost. $$\text{Cost} = E_{grid} \times \text{Tariff} = 32.80 \times 18$$ $$\text{Cost} = \text{₹ } 590.40$$ **Step 4 (Optional):** Charging time (assuming nominal average). $$t = \dfrac{E_{bat}}{P_{DC}} = \dfrac{30.18}{60} = 0.503 \text{ hours} \approx 30 \text{ minutes}$$ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Note on C-Rate The **C-rate** is the single most important parameter that separates AC and DC charging behaviorally: $$\text{C-Rate} = \dfrac{P_{charger}}{E_{bat}}$$ - For the Nexon EV (30.2 kWh) on a 7.2 kW AC charger: $C = 7.2/30.2 \approx \mathbf{0.24C}$ — gentle, low stress. - For the same car on a 50 kW DC charger: $C = 50/30.2 \approx \mathbf{1.66C}$ — aggressive, requires active liquid cooling in the pack. - Lithium-ion cells typically tolerate up to **2C–3C continuous** and **4C–5C peak** charging without severe degradation, but at higher C-rates, **lithium plating** and **SEI growth** accelerate, reducing calendar life. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Note:** Per the syllabus, only **block-level functional architecture** is required. Mermaid `flowchart` syntax is used below to render the power-flow architecture of both AC and DC charging stations. All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `nACGrid`, `nACRectifier`) to comply with the Mermaid Compilation Safeguard. --- ## 4.1 Block Diagram of an AC Charging Station (Level 2) ```mermaid flowchart LR nACGrid[AC GRID SUPPLY 230V SINGLE PHASE OR 415V THREE PHASE] --> nACBreaker[MCB AND RCD PROTECTION BLOCK] nACBreaker --> nACPilot[PILOT CONTROL CIRCUIT IEC 61851 PWM 1 KHZ] nACPilot --> nACContactor[AC CONTACTOR SOLID STATE OR ELECTROMECHANICAL] nACContactor --> nACCable[CHARGING CABLE AND CONNECTOR TYPE 2] nACCable --> nACOBC[ON BOARD CHARGER OBC INSIDE VEHICLE] nACOBC --> nACRectifier[AC TO DC RECTIFIER PFC STAGE INSIDE OBC] nACRectifier --> nACDCConv[ISOLATED DC DC CONVERTER HIGH FREQUENCY] nACDCConv --> nACBMS[BATTERY MANAGEMENT SYSTEM BMS] nACBMS --> nACBat[TRACTION BATTERY PACK HV DC] nACBat --> nACThermal[VEHICLE THERMAL MANAGEMENT LOOP] style nACGrid fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 style nACBat fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000 style nACContactor fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` **Description of Power Flow:** The grid AC supply is first conditioned by the protection block, the pilot circuit ensures safe handshaking, and the contactor delivers AC power through the cable. **The rectifier is inside the vehicle** (the OBC), which is the defining characteristic of AC charging. --- ## 4.2 Block Diagram of a DC Fast Charging Station (Level 3 / CCS2) ```mermaid flowchart LR nDCMVGrid[MEDIUM VOLTAGE GRID 11 KV OR 33 KV] --> nDCTrafo[STEP DOWN TRANSFORMER] nDCTrafo --> nDCAFE[ACTIVE FRONT END RECTIFIER IGBT PWM THREE PHASE] nDCAFE --> nDCDCLink[DC LINK CAPACITOR BANK 700 TO 800 V] nDCDCLink --> nDCDCConv[ISOLATED DC DC CONVERTER LLC OR DAB TOPOLOGY] nDCDCConv --> nDCFilter[OUTPUT EMI FILTER AND DC CONTACTOR] nDCFilter --> nDCCable[LIQUID COOLED HIGH POWER CABLE CCS2] nDCCable --> nDCBat[EV TRACTION BATTERY PACK DIRECT DC CONNECTION] nDCBat --> nDCBMS[VEHICLE BMS COMMUNICATION VIA PLC ISO 15118] nDCBMS -. BIDIRECTIONAL .-> nDCDCConv nDCThermal[CHILLER AND LIQUID COOLING SYSTEM] -. COOLS .-> nDCAFE nDCThermal -. COOLS .-> nDCDCConv nDCThermal -. COOLS .-> nDCCable nDCCtrl[SUPERVISORY CONTROLLER OCPP 1.6 OR 2.0.1] --> nDCAFE nDCCtrl --> nDCDCConv nDCCtrl --> nDCDCLink style nDCMVGrid fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 nDCBat([EV TRACTION BATTERY PACK DIRECT DC CONNECTION]) ==> nDCCable style nDCMVGrid fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 style nDCBat fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000 style nDCAFE fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000 style nDCCtrl fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When drawing the block diagram in your exam, always use **rectangular boxes with arrows indicating power flow direction**. Label each block with the **specific component name** (e.g., "PFC Rectifier" not just "Rectifier"). This is a 14-mark question (Part B) — a poorly labeled diagram will cost you **3–4 marks** out of 7. --- ## 4.3 Combined Comparative Flow — AC vs. DC Charging ```mermaid flowchart TB nStart[GRID AC POWER] --> nDecision{WHAT IS THE
CHARGING STATION TYPE?} nDecision -->|AC STATION| nACPath[AC CONTACTOR CLOSES] nACPath --> nACDel[AC POWER DELIVERED TO VEHICLE INLET] nACDel --> nACOBC[OBC INSIDE VEHICLE RECTIFIES AC TO DC] nACOBC --> nACBatF[BATTERY CHARGED AT LOW C RATE LESS THAN 0.5C] nDecision -->|DC STATION| nDCRect[STATION RECTIFIER CONVERTS AC TO DC] nDCRect --> nDCDCStage[DC DC STAGE REGULATES OUTPUT VOLTAGE] nDCDCStage --> nDCDel[REGULATED DC POWER DELIVERED TO VEHICLE BATTERY] nDCDel --> nDCBatF[BATTERY CHARGED AT HIGH C RATE 1C TO 4C] nACBatF --> nACEnd[SLOW CHARGING 4 TO 10 HOURS] nDCBatF --> nDCEnd[FAST CHARGING 15 TO 50 MINUTES] style nStart fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 style nACEnd fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000 style nDCEnd fill:#FF6347,stroke:#fff,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.4 Connector and Standard Reference Diagram (Conceptual) ```mermaid flowchart LR nConn1[TYPE 2 AC CONNECTOR
IEC 62196]:::ac nConn2[CCS2 COMBO 2
DC FAST]:::dc nConn3[CHAdeMO
JAPANESE DC]:::dc nConn4[Bharat DC 001
15 KW GB T]:::dc nConn5[NACS TESLA
NORTH AMERICA]:::ac nConn1 --> nNote1[USED IN INDIA FOR AC] nConn2 --> nNote2[USED IN INDIA AND EU FOR DC] nConn3 --> nNote3[USED IN NISSAN LEAF KONA EARLY] nConn4 --> nNote4[USED FOR 2W 3W AND 4W COMMERCIAL] nConn5 --> nNote5[PROPRIETARY OPENING TO ALL IN 2023] classDef ac fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef dc fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Q: Differentiate between AC and DC charging stations for electric vehicles. Mention any two key differences in terms of power level and the location of the rectifier.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | AC Charging Station | DC Charging Station | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Power Level** | Low to medium (3.3 kW – 22 kW) | High (50 kW – 400 kW) | | **Location of Rectifier** | Inside the vehicle (On-Board Charger / OBC) | Inside the charging station itself | | **Charging Speed** | Slow (4–10 hours for 60 kWh) | Fast (15–50 minutes for 60 kWh) | | **Cost** | Low (₹ 25k – ₹ 1.5 lakh) | High (₹ 5 lakh – ₹ 50 lakh) | | **Use Case** | Residential, workplace | Highway corridors, fleet depots | | **Communication** | PWM pilot (IEC 61851) | Digital PLC (ISO 15118) | *(Any two differences correctly explained with technical terms: 3 marks)* --- ### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Q: What is the role of an On-Board Charger (OBC) in an Electric Vehicle? Why is the AC charging speed limited by the OBC rating?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Role of OBC (1.5 marks):** The On-Board Charger (OBC) is a power-electronics device integrated inside the EV. Its primary functions are: - **Rectification:** Converts the incoming AC supply from the charging station into DC. - **Power Factor Correction (PFC):** Ensures the current drawn from the grid is in phase with the voltage, maintaining a near-unity power factor (>0.99). - **Voltage/Current Regulation:** Provides a regulated DC voltage and current to the battery following the **CC-CV (Constant Current – Constant Voltage)** charging profile mandated by the BMS. 2. **Why AC charging speed is OBC-limited (1.5 marks):** The OBC must be physically packaged inside the vehicle chassis, which imposes strict constraints on: - **Size & Weight:** Limits the magnetics (transformers, inductors) and heat-sink area. - **Thermal Management:** Heat must be dissipated in a sealed, vibration-prone environment without active liquid cooling (in most cases). - **Cost:** High-power OBCs (>22 kW) significantly increase the vehicle's BOM cost. These constraints collectively cap the practical OBC rating at **~22 kW** for passenger cars, which in turn caps the AC charging speed. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and is divided into **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**. Below are **TWO alternative choices**, exactly as the KTU question paper provides internal choice. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]** **Q: (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of an **AC Level-2 charging station** for electric vehicles. Label all major blocks and describe the function of each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A **50 kWh** EV battery is to be charged from **10% to 90% SoC** using a **7.2 kW AC charger** with **90% efficiency**. Calculate the **energy delivered, the energy drawn from the grid, and the total charging time**. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to Question A:** **(a) Block Diagram of AC Level-2 Charging Station (7 marks):** The block diagram should include the following components in order of power flow: 1. **Grid Supply (230 V single-phase or 415 V three-phase)** — the source of AC power. 2. **MCB + RCCB / RCD Protection** — provides over-current and earth-fault protection. 3. **Pilot Control Circuit (IEC 61851 PWM)** — generates the 1 kHz pilot signal that communicates maximum allowable current to the vehicle. 4. **AC Contactor** — closes the circuit only after successful handshaking; provides galvanic isolation when opened. 5. **Charging Cable and Connector (Type 2 / Mennekes)** — the physical interface delivering power to the vehicle inlet. 6. **On-Board Charger (OBC)** *(inside the vehicle)* — comprises a **PFC rectifier** + **isolated DC-DC converter** that converts grid AC to battery-grade DC. 7. **Battery Management System (BMS)** — controls the charging profile (CC-CV), monitors cell voltages and temperatures. 8. **Traction Battery Pack** — the high-voltage DC energy storage. 9. **Thermal Management Loop** — manages OBC and battery heat. *(1 mark for correct sequence; 2 marks for labels; 2 marks for explaining 2 key blocks in detail; 1 mark for use of OBC in EV; 1 mark for neatness. Total = 7 marks)* **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Energy delivered to the battery (2 marks):** $$E_{bat} = C_{bat} \times \Delta SoC = 50 \text{ kWh} \times (0.90 - 0.10) = 50 \times 0.80 = 40 \text{ kWh}$$ **Step 2 — Useful power at the battery after efficiency (2 marks):** $$P_{bat} = P_{chg} \times \eta_{chg} = 7.2 \times 0.90 = 6.48 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 3 — Charging time (2 marks):** $$t = \dfrac{E_{bat}}{P_{bat}} = \dfrac{40}{6.48} = 6.17 \text{ hours} \approx 6 \text{ hours } 10 \text{ minutes}$$ **Step 4 — Energy drawn from the grid (1 mark):** $$E_{grid} = \dfrac{E_{bat}}{\eta_{chg}} = \dfrac{40}{0.90} = 44.44 \text{ kWh}$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]** **Q: (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **DC Fast Charging Station (50 kW CCS2)** for electric vehicles. Clearly show where the AC-DC conversion takes place and explain the function of the **DC-DC converter stage**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare AC and DC charging stations on the basis of: **(i)** power level, **(ii)** location of rectifier, **(iii)** charging time, **(iv)** impact on battery life, **(v)** cost, **(vi)** grid impact, and **(vii)** communication protocol. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to Question B:** **(a) DC Fast Charging Station Block Diagram (7 marks):** The block diagram must contain: 1. **MV Grid (11 kV or 33 kV) → Step-down Transformer** — reduces voltage to 415 V three-phase. 2. **Active Front-End (AFE) Rectifier with PFC** — converts 415 V AC to 700–800 V DC bus; this is **the key block** (1.5 marks) where AC-DC conversion happens inside the station. 3. **DC-Link Capacitor Bank** — smooths the rectified DC. 4. **Isolated DC-DC Converter (LLC resonant or DAB)** — regulates output voltage to match battery voltage (200–800 V); provides galvanic isolation for safety (1.5 marks). 5. **Output Contactor + EMI Filter** — final switching and noise filtering. 6. **Liquid-Cooled High-Power Cable and CCS2 Connector** — delivers DC power to the vehicle. 7. **Supervisory Controller (OCPP 1.6/2.0.1)** — handles billing, authentication, remote monitoring. 8. **Chiller / Liquid Cooling System** — cools the AFE, DC-DC, and cable. **Key Point:** The AC-DC conversion is performed *inside the station* by the AFE, and the resulting DC is fed *directly* to the battery, bypassing the OBC. The DC-DC converter stage steps the DC-link voltage down to the exact voltage the BMS requests, and uses high-frequency isolation (typically a medium-frequency transformer) to ensure user safety. *(2 marks for diagram correctness; 2 marks for AC-DC location explanation; 2 marks for DC-DC function; 1 mark for neatness)* **(b) Comparative Table (7 marks = 1 mark per correct comparison):** | Parameter | AC Charging Station | DC Charging Station | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Power level** | 3.3 kW – 22 kW | 50 kW – 400 kW | | **Location of rectifier** | Inside vehicle (OBC) | Inside charging station (AFE) | | **Charging time** | 4 – 10 hours | 15 – 50 minutes | | **Impact on battery life** | Low (C-rate ≤ 0.5C) | Higher (C-rate ≥ 1C) | | **Cost** | ₹ 25k – ₹ 1.5 lakh | ₹ 5 lakh – ₹ 50 lakh+ | | **Grid impact** | Low; residential feeder compatible | High; needs dedicated 11 kV/33 kV feeder | | **Communication protocol** | PWM pilot (IEC 61851) | Digital PLC (ISO 15118) | --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing "OBC" with "AC charger":** The OBC is *inside the car*; the AC EVSE is the *external box on the wall*. Many students draw the OBC outside the vehicle and lose **2 marks**. > 2. **Forgetting the Pilot Signal:** In AC charging block diagrams, omitting the **IEC 61851 PWM pilot circuit** is a common 1-mark deduction. > 3. **Skipping Units in Numericals:** Always write units (kWh, hours, ₹) — KTU deducts 0.5 marks per missing unit. > 4. **Using "|" instead of $\vert$ in LaTeX:** On scanned answer sheets, the `|` symbol can be misread by the digital evaluator. Use $\vert x \vert$ for absolute value. > 5. **No mention of "bypassing the OBC" in DC charging:** Examiners specifically look for this phrase in DCFC answers. Always write *"DC power is supplied directly to the battery, **bypassing the on-board charger**."* --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ☐ **AC charging = Rectifier inside the car (OBC).** **DC charging = Rectifier inside the station.** - ☐ AC charging power is **OBC-limited** (typically ≤ 22 kW); DC charging power is **station-limited** (50 kW – 400 kW). - ☐ AC charging uses the **PWM pilot (IEC 61851)**; DC charging uses **digital PLC (ISO 15118)**. - ☐ Key connectors: **Type 2** (AC, India/EU), **CCS2** (DC, India/EU mandatory from 2025), **Bharat DC-001** (15 kW, India), **CHAdeMO** (Japan), **NACS** (Tesla, opening globally). - ☐ DC fast charging causes **higher C-rate** (≥ 1C), leading to **lithium plating** and **SEI growth**, reducing battery calendar life. - ☐ DC fast chargers have an **Active Front-End (AFE) rectifier with PFC** + **Isolated DC-DC converter (LLC or DAB)** + **DC-link capacitor bank**. - ☐ AC fast chargers have **only protection + contactor + pilot circuit**; the heavy power electronics is *inside the car*. - ☐ Charging time formula: $t = (C \times DoD) / (\eta \times I)$ — always **include efficiency** unless stated otherwise. - ☐ C-Rate = $P_{chg} / E_{bat}$ — determines battery stress and degradation rate. - ☐ India's public charging policy mandates **CCS2 for 4W** and **Bharat DC-001 / AC-001** for 2W/3W and light commercial. - ☐ For a 60 kWh battery: AC Level-2 takes **~6–8 hours**; DC 50 kW takes **~50 minutes**; DC 150 kW takes **~18 minutes** (20→80% SoC). - ☐ **KTU Keyword Watchlist for Marks:** "Bypassing the OBC," "PFC rectifier," "CC-CV profile," "ISO 15118," "Active Front-End," "LLC resonant converter," "Power Factor Correction."

topics.title: Types of Connectors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET742 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES AND RENEWABLE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types of Connectors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Electric Vehicles (EVs) and Renewable Energy integration, **EV Connectors** (also called *EV Plugs* or *Couplers*) are standardized electromechanical interface devices that establish a safe, controlled, and high-efficiency electrical connection between the EV Supply Equipment (EVSE / charging station) and the Electric Vehicle's onboard charging inlet. They govern the transfer of AC or DC power, communication signals (Pilot/Proximity), and protective earth grounding under strict international standards (IEC 62196, SAE J1772, GB/T 20234).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The connector is not just a plug — it is a *control gateway*. It carries power lines, ground, a Control Pilot (CP) signal for handshake, and a Proximity Pilot (PP) for cable current rating detection. Mastery of pin mapping (L1, L2, L3, N, PE, CP, PP, DC+, DC-) is mandatory for KTU board questions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Smart Fuel Cap" of an EV:**
> Think of an EV connector like the fuel cap of a conventional car, but with a "brain." Just as you insert a diesel nozzle into a diesel car (and a petrol nozzle simply *won't fit*), EV connectors are designed with unique geometries so that a **Type 2 plug cannot accidentally be inserted into a CCS Combo 2 socket** (only the AC part fits), preventing electrical accidents. The Control Pilot pin acts like a *conversation* — before any current flows, the car and charger "shake hands," agree on the maximum safe current, check insulation, and then energize the line — much like two diplomats confirming treaty terms before signing.

## 1.3 Physical Standards & Metrics

- **Rated Currents:** **16 A, 32 A, 63 A, 125 A, 200 A, 350 A** depending on Level
- **Rated Voltages:** **250 V AC (single-phase), 480 V AC (three-phase), 600 V DC, 1000 V DC**
- **Operating Temperature:** **−30 °C to +50 °C**
- **Ingress Protection (IP):** **IP54 (mated), IP44 (unmated)** for typical EV couplers
- **Mechanical Endurance:** **≥ 10,000 mating cycles** (no-load)
- **Communication Protocols:** **PLC (Power Line Communication) for CCS**, **CAN Bus for CHAdeMO**

## 1.4 Why Connectors Matter — The KTU Context

EVs cannot simply use a household 3-pin plug because:
1. **Current Levels** — DC fast charging requires up to **350 A** (≈ 350 kW), impossible with domestic sockets.
2. **Safety Handshake** — A direct connection could energize the car before the battery management system (BMS) confirms readiness.
3. **Standardization** — A Tesla car sold in Europe must charge at a station built by a different manufacturer. Connectors make this *interoperability* possible.
]<]minimax[>[</br>

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of EV Connectors (KTU Board Taxonomy)

Connectors are classified on **three independent axes**:

### Axis 1 — By Current Type
- **AC Connectors:** Slow / moderate charging (On-board charger converts AC→DC). Levels 1, 2.
- **DC Connectors:** Fast charging (Off-board rectifier, bypasses onboard charger). Levels 3 (DCFC).

### Axis 2 — By Region / Standard
- **IEC 62196** — Global (Type 1, Type 2, Type 3 — deprecated)
- **SAE J1772** — North America (Type 1 based)
- **GB/T 20234** — China (mandatory domestic standard)
- **Tesla proprietary** — NACS (North America), now opening to other OEMs

### Axis 3 — By Power Class
| Class | Power | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| Level 1 (AC) | 1.4 – 1.9 kW | Home, 120 V |
| Level 2 (AC) | 3.4 – 22 kW | Home/Public, 208–240 V |
| Level 3 (DC Fast) | 50 – 350 kW | Highway corridors |

## 2.2 Pin Configuration Matrix (KTU High-Yield)

| Standard | Pins | Function Layout |
|---|---|---|
| **Type 1 (SAE J1772 / J Plug)** | 5 | L1, N, PE, CP, PP |
| **Type 2 (Mennekes / IEC 62196-2)** | 7 | L1, L2, L3, N, PE, CP, PP |
| **CCS Combo 1** | 5+2 | Type 1 + DC+, DC− |
| **CCS Combo 2** | 7+2 | Type 2 + DC+, DC− |
| **CHAdeMO** | 10 | DC+, DC−, PE, CP, Signal pins (CAN H/L) |
| **GB/T AC** | 7 | L1, L2, L3, N, PE, CP, CC (Connection Confirm) |
| **GB/T DC** | 9 | DC+, DC−, PE, CP, S+, S− (CAN), CC1, CC2 |
| **NACS (Tesla)** | 5 | L1, L2, N, PE, CP/PP shared |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** The single most-asked pin question is *"Why does Type 2 have 7 pins while Type 1 has 5?"* — the answer is **three-phase capability** (extra L2 and L3).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Charging Power (Single Phase)** | $P = V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi$ | W (Watts) | AC Level 1 / Level 2 |
| **Charging Power (Three Phase)** | $P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$ | W (Watts) | Type 2 three-phase |
| **DC Fast Charging Power** | $P_{DC} = V_{batt} \cdot I_{DC}$ | W (Watts) | CCS, CHAdeMO, GB/T DC |
| **Charging Time** | $t = \dfrac{E_{batt}}{P_{chg} \cdot \eta}$ | hours | Time to full charge |
| **Cable Current Rating (PP resistor)** | $R_{PP} = \dfrac{1500}{I_{rated}}$ (approx.) | Ω | Proximity Pilot encoding |
| **Pilot Signal Voltage Levels** | $V_{CP} = \pm 12 \text{ V}, \pm 9 \text{ V}, \pm 6 \text{ V}$ | V | State encoding (IEC 61851) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The CP/PP handshake protocol (defined by IEC 61851 Mode 2/3) is the foundation of every modern EVSE. Without it, vehicles would be unsafe — the BMS, insulation monitor, and current limiter must all agree *before* contactors close. Engineers designing OCPP backends (Open Charge Point Protocol) rely on this pin-level encoding for billing, load balancing, and V2G.

## 2.4 Charging Levels — Operational Logic

- **Level 1 (AC):** Uses **Type 1 or Type 2**, 120 V / 15 A → ≈ **1.4 kW**, ~8–12 hr full charge.
- **Level 2 (AC):** 208–240 V, 30–80 A → **3.4–22 kW**, ~3–6 hr.
- **Level 3 (DCFC):** Bypasses onboard charger; uses **CCS / CHAdeMO / GB/T DC / NACS**, 200–1000 V DC, 50–350 kW, 15–45 min to 80% SoC.
]<]minimax[>[</br>

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Single-Phase AC Charging Power at a Type 1/Type 2 Connector

For a single-phase AC connection (Level 1 / Level 2) supplying an EV's onboard charger:

$$P_{AC} = V_{rms} \cdot I_{rms} \cdot \cos\phi$$

**Step 1 — Identify variables:**
- $V_{rms}$ = RMS supply voltage at the connector pins (L1 vs N)
- $I_{rms}$ = RMS current limited by cable rating (16 A, 32 A, 63 A)
- $\cos\phi$ = power factor of the onboard charger (≈ 0.95–0.99 for modern IGBT/SiC PFC stages)

**Step 2 — Numeric example (Type 2, 32 A, 230 V single-phase):**

$$P_{AC} = 230 \text{ V} \times 32 \text{ A} \times 0.98 = 7.21 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Charging time for a 60 kWh EV battery:**

$$t = \frac{E_{batt}}{P_{AC} \cdot \eta} = \frac{60{,}000 \text{ Wh}}{7{,}212 \text{ W} \times 0.92} = 9.04 \text{ hours}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Three-Phase Type 2 Charging Power

A Type 2 connector carries **L1, L2, L3, N, PE, CP, PP** — enabling true three-phase charging, which a Type 1 cannot.

$$P_{3\phi} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{L-L} \cdot I_{L} \cdot \cos\phi$$

**Step 1 — Insert typical European Type 2 three-phase parameters: $V_{L-L} = 400$ V, $I_L = 32$ A, $\cos\phi = 0.98$.**

$$P_{3\phi} = \sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 32 \times 0.98$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate:**

$$P_{3\phi} = 1.732 \times 400 \times 32 \times 0.98 = 21{,}723 \text{ W} \approx 21.7 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Charging time for same 60 kWh battery:**

$$t = \frac{60{,}000}{21{,}723 \times 0.92} = 3.0 \text{ hours}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The same Type 2 connector used in *single-phase mode* (e.g., in homes with only L1+N available) delivers only 7.2 kW — a 3× penalty in charging speed. This is why industrial parks install three-phase supplies specifically for EV fleets.

## 3.3 Derivation: DC Fast Charging Power (CCS / CHAdeMO)

In DC fast charging, the EVSE contains the rectifier; AC mains never enters the car. The battery receives:

$$P_{DC} = V_{batt} \cdot I_{DC}$$

**Step 1 — For a modern 400 V architecture EV (e.g., Hyundai Ioniq 5) at **350 kW** peak:**

$$V_{batt} \approx 800 \text{ V (during peak SoC window)}$$

$$I_{DC} = \frac{P_{DC}}{V_{batt}} = \frac{350{,}000}{800} = 437.5 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2 — Time to add 100 km of range (~18 kWh):**

$$t = \frac{18{,}000}{350{,}000} = 0.0514 \text{ hours} \approx 3.1 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 3 — Power loss in the cable (I²R):**
Assuming cable resistance $R_{cable} = 10 \text{ m}\Omega$:

$$P_{loss} = I^2 R = (437.5)^2 \times 0.01 = 1{,}914 \text{ W} \approx 1.9 \text{ kW}$$

$$\eta_{cable} = \frac{350}{350 + 1.9} = 99.46\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why liquid-cooled cables?** At 350 A continuous, the I²R heating in a thin copper cable would melt the insulation. Hence CCS 2 HPC (High Power Charging) cables are **liquid-cooled**, with dielectric coolant circulating inside the cable assembly.

## 3.4 Derivation: Proximity Pilot (PP) Resistor — Cable Current Encoding

The PP pin uses a resistor between PP and PE to tell the EVSE the cable's rated current. The standard formula (IEC 61851-1) is:

$$R_{PP} = \frac{1500}{I_{rated}}$$

(for $I_{rated} \leq 75$ A)

**Step 1 — For a 32 A cable:**

$$R_{PP} = \frac{1500}{32} = 46.875 \text{ }\Omega$$

**Step 2 — For a 63 A cable:**

$$R_{PP} = \frac{1500}{63} = 23.81 \text{ }\Omega$$

**Step 3 — The EVSE reads this resistor via a 5 V source and a reference ladder, then caps its output current to $I_{rated}$ — a *hardware-enforced* safety limit independent of any software.
]<]minimax[>[</br>

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — EV Charging Connector Ecosystem

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    GRID[AC Grid Supply]:::source
    EVSE[EVSE Charger Rectifier and Controller]:::process
    CONN_AC[AC Connector Type 1 or Type 2]:::ac
    CONN_DC[DC Connector CCS2 or CHAdeMO or GB_T]:::dc
    CP_PIN[Control Pilot CP Handshake]:::signal
    PP_PIN[Proximity Pilot PP Cable Detection]:::signal
    CAN[CAN Bus High Speed Communication]:::signal
    PLC[PLC over Power Lines CCS Protocol]:::signal
    OBC[On Board Charger AC to DC]:::process
    BMS[Battery Management System]:::process
    BATT[High Voltage Battery Pack]:::batt

    GRID --> EVSE
    EVSE --> CONN_AC
    EVSE --> CONN_DC
    CONN_AC -.-> CP_PIN
    CONN_AC -.-> PP_PIN
    CONN_DC -.-> CP_PIN
    CONN_DC -.-> CAN
    CONN_DC -.-> PLC
    CONN_AC --> OBC
    CONN_DC --> BATT
    OBC --> BMS
    BMS --> BATT
    BATT -.State of Charge and Temp.- BMS

    classDef source fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#8b4513,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#add8e6,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef ac fill:#90ee90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef dc fill:#ff6347,stroke:#8b0000,color:#fff
    classDef signal fill:#fffacd,stroke:#daa520,color:#000
    classDef batt fill:#dda0dd,stroke:#4b0082,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Connector Pin Mapping Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TYPE1[Type 1 SAE J1772 5 Pins]
        T1_L1[L1 Power]
        T1_N[N Neutral]
        T1_PE[PE Protective Earth]
        T1_CP[CP Control Pilot]
        T1_PP[PP Proximity Pilot]
    end

    subgraph TYPE2[Type 2 Mennekes 7 Pins]
        T2_L1[L1 Power]
        T2_L2[L2 Power]
        T2_L3[L3 Power]
        T2_N[N Neutral]
        T2_PE[PE Protective Earth]
```
