```mermaid
        T2_CP[CP Control Pilot]
        T2_PP[PP Proximity Pilot]
    end

    subgraph CCS2[CCS Combo 2 7 Plus 2 Pins]
        CCS2_AC[Type 2 AC Pin Block]
        CCS2_DC[DC Plus and DC Minus]
        CCS2_AC --> CCS2_DC
    end

    subgraph CHADEMO[CHAdeMO 10 Pins]
        CH_DC[DC Plus and DC Minus]
        CH_PE[PE]
        CH_CAN[CAN H and CAN L]
        CH_SIG[Signal Pins]
    end

    subgraph GBT_DC[GB_T DC 9 Pins]
        GB_DC[DC Plus and DC Minus]
        GB_PE[PE]
        GB_CAN[S Plus and S Minus CAN]
        GB_CC[CC1 and CC2 Confirm]
    end
```

## 4.3 Connector Decision Tree — Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Select EV Connector]:::start
    Q1{AC or DC Charging}:::q
    Q2{Region}:::q
    Q3{Power Level}:::q

    A1[Type 1 SAE J1772]:::ac
    A2[Type 2 Mennekes]:::ac
    A3[GB_T AC]:::ac
    A4[Tesla NACS]:::ac

    D1[CCS Combo 1]:::dc
    D2[CCS Combo 2]:::dc
    D3[CHAdeMO]:::dc
    D4[GB_T DC]:::dc
    D5[Tesla Supercharger NACS]:::dc

    START --> Q1
    Q1 -->|AC| Q2
    Q1 -->|DC| Q3
    Q2 -->|North America| A1
    Q2 -->|Europe| A2
    Q2 -->|China| A3
    Q2 -->|Tesla legacy| A4
    Q3 -->|USA up to 350 kW| D1
    Q3 -->|Europe up to 350 kW| D2
    Q3 -->|Japan legacy| D3
    Q3 -->|China up to 250 kW| D4
    Q3 -->|Tesla Supercharger| D5

    classDef start fill:#ffeb3b,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
```

]<]minimax[>[</br>

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Type 1 and Type 2 AC connectors for EVs in terms of pin count, supported phases, and regional adoption.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Type 1 (SAE J1772):** 5 pins (L1, N, PE, CP, PP); single-phase only; used in **North America and Japan** (e.g., Nissan Leaf, Mitsubishi Outlander PHEV).
- **Type 2 (Mennekes / IEC 62196-2):** 7 pins (L1, L2, L3, N, PE, CP, PP); supports **single and three-phase**; standard in **Europe** (e.g., BMW i3, Tesla Model S in EU).
- **Key Difference:** Type 2's extra L2 and L3 pins enable three-phase charging up to **43 kW** (400 V × 63 A × √3 × cosφ), which Type 1 cannot.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the function of the Control Pilot (CP) pin in an EV charging connector?**

**Model Answer:**
The CP pin implements the **IEC 61851-1 handshake protocol**. It is a 1 kHz PWM signal whose duty cycle (10% / 25% / 50% / 100%) and amplitude (+12 V / +9 V / +6 V / -12 V) encode four states:
1. **State A (Standby):** 12 V, vehicle not connected.
2. **State B (Connected/Not Ready):** 9 V, plugged in, BMS not ready.
3. **State C (Charging):** 6 V, BMS authorized current flow.
4. **State D (Ventilation Required):** −12 V (legacy, for indoor charging with battery off-gassing).

The EVSE only energizes the contactors when CP transitions from B→C, ensuring **hardware-level safety** before any current flows.

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students commonly lose marks by (1) confusing CCS Combo 1 with CCS Combo 2, (2) forgetting that CHAdeMO uses **CAN bus** while CCS uses **PLC** for communication, (3) omitting the PP resistor encoding logic, and (4) drawing connector pinouts incorrectly. Always cite the **standard number** (e.g., IEC 62196-2) — examiners award marks for standards awareness.

### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a CCS Combo 2 DC fast charging connector. List its pin functions and state the communication protocol used. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (textual representation):**

```
CCS Combo 2 Coupler
┌──────────────────────────────────┐
│  Upper Section (AC part — Type 2)│
│   ┌──┐┌──┐┌──┐┌──┐┌──┐┌──┐┌──┐  │
│   │L1││L2││L3││ N││PE││CP││PP│  │
│   └──┘└──┘└──┘└──┘└──┘└──┘└──┘  │
│                                  │
│  Lower Section (DC extension)    │
│   ┌──────┐    ┌──────┐           │
│   │ DC+  │    │ DC−  │           │
│   └──────┘    └──────┘           │
└──────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Pin Functions:**
1. **L1, L2, L3, N** — AC phase and neutral (for backward AC charging)
2. **PE** — Protective Earth
3. **CP** — Control Pilot (PWM 1 kHz, IEC 61851-1)
4. **PP** — Proximity Pilot (resistor encodes cable current rating)
5. **DC+, DC−** — Two large contacts for DC fast charging (up to 200 A continuous, 350 A peak in HPC version)

**Communication Protocol:** **ISO 15118** running over **PLC (Power Line Communication)** on the CP/PE pair — supports **Plug & Charge**, V2G, and secure TLS authentication.

**(b) A CCS Combo 2 station delivers 150 kW DC at 400 V to an EV. Calculate (i) the charging current, (ii) the time to charge a 75 kWh battery from 20% to 80% SoC assuming 92% charger efficiency, and (iii) the cable I²R loss if cable resistance is 5 mΩ. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Charging current:**

$$I_{DC} = \frac{P_{DC}}{V_{batt}} = \frac{150{,}000}{400} = 375 \text{ A}$$

**(ii) Energy to be delivered:**

$$E_{required} = (0.80 - 0.20) \times 75 \text{ kWh} = 45 \text{ kWh}$$

$$\text{Time} = t = \frac{E_{required}}{P_{DC} \cdot \eta} = \frac{45{,}000}{150{,}000 \times 0.92} = 0.326 \text{ hours} = 19.6 \text{ minutes}$$

**(iii) Cable I²R loss:**

$$P_{loss} = I^2 \cdot R = (375)^2 \times 0.005 = 703.1 \text{ W}$$

$$\text{Energy lost in 19.6 min} = 703.1 \times 0.326 \approx 229 \text{ Wh} \ (\text{negligible } < 0.5\%)$$

**Answer:** $I = 375$ A, $t \approx 19.6$ min, $P_{loss} \approx 703$ W.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a) Compare CHAdeMO and GB/T DC charging connectors in terms of (i) pin count and function, (ii) communication protocol, (iii) maximum power supported, and (iv) regional adoption. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | **CHAdeMO** | **GB/T DC** |
|---|---|---|
| **Standard** | JEVS G105 (Japan) | GB/T 20234.3 (China) |
| **Pin Count** | 10 | 9 |
| **DC Power Pins** | DC+, DC− | DC+, DC− |
| **Signal Pins** | CAN H, CAN L, CP, GND | S+, S− (CAN), CP, CC1, CC2 |
| **Communication** | **CAN bus (500 kbps)** | **CAN bus (250 kbps)** |
| **Max Power** | 400 kW (CHAdeMO 2.0) | 250 kW (GB/T DC) |
| **Max Current** | 400 A | 250 A |
| **Max Voltage** | 1000 V | 750 V (extensible to 1000 V) |
| **Region** | Japan, with growing EU presence | China (mandatory) |
| **Vehicle Example** | Nissan Leaf, Mitsubishi i-MiEV | BYD, NIO, Xpeng (China models) |

**Key Distinction:** CHAdeMO uses **CAN**; GB/T DC also uses **CAN** but with a different message dictionary (GB/T 27930). Both are DC-only connectors; they cannot carry AC (unlike CCS Combo).

**(b) A three-phase Type 2 AC charger operates at 400 V line-line, 32 A per phase, with a power factor of 0.95. Calculate the total charging power and the time required to charge a 50 kWh battery from 10% to 90% SoC, assuming 90% overall efficiency. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total three-phase power:**

$$P_{3\phi} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{L-L} \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$$

$$P_{3\phi} = \sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 32 \times 0.95$$

$$P_{3\phi} = 1.732 \times 400 \times 32 \times 0.95 = 21{,}069 \text{ W} \approx 21.07 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 — Energy to be delivered:**

$$E_{required} = (0.90 - 0.10) \times 50 \text{ kWh} = 40 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 3 — Charging time:**

$$t = \frac{E_{required}}{P_{3\phi} \cdot \eta} = \frac{40{,}000}{21{,}069 \times 0.90}$$

$$t = \frac{40{,}000}{18{,}962} = 2.11 \text{ hours} \approx 2 \text{ hours } 6 \text{ minutes}$$

**Answer:** $P = 21.07$ kW, $t \approx 2.11$ hours.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Pin this in your exam kit):**

- **Type 1 (SAE J1772):** 5 pins — single-phase — **North America/Japan**.
- **Type 2 (Mennekes):** 7 pins — **three-phase capable** — **Europe/Global**.
- **CCS Combo 1:** Type 1 + DC+ / DC− — **USA**.
- **CCS Combo 2:** Type 2 + DC+ / DC− — **Europe/Global**.
- **CHAdeMO:** 10 pins, **CAN bus**, up to **400 kW (v2.0)** — **Japan**.
- **GB/T AC:** 7 pins (with CC instead of PP) — **China**.
- **GB/T DC:** 9 pins (DC+, DC−, PE, CP, S+, S−, CC1, CC2) — **China**.
- **NACS (Tesla):** 5 pins (L1, L2, N, PE, CP/PP combined) — **USA, now opening**.
- **CP pin:** 1 kHz PWM; states **A (12 V), B (9 V), C (6 V), D (−12 V)**.
- **PP pin:** Resistor $R = 1500 / I_{rated}$ encodes cable current.
- **DC Fast Charging** **bypasses the onboard charger** — EVSE contains the rectifier.
- **ISO 15118 + PLC** — used by CCS for Plug & Charge and V2G.
- **IEC 61851** — defines AC charging modes (Mode 1 to Mode 4).
- **SAE J1772** — defines DC Level 1 and Level 2 charging.
- **HPC cables are liquid-cooled** — because I²R losses at 350–500 A would otherwise melt insulation.
- **Three-phase Type 2 charging** at 32 A, 400 V gives **≈ 22 kW** — ~3× faster than single-phase.
- **Remember the standard prefix:** IEC = International, SAE = Society of Automotive Engineers, GB/T = Guobiao (China), JEVS = Japan Electric Vehicle Standard.
]<]minimax[>[</br>

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CHAdeMO, CCS Type 1 and 2, GB/T
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET742 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES AND RENEWABLE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** CHAdeMO, CCS Type 1 and 2, GB/T

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The global Electric Vehicle (EV) DC fast-charging ecosystem is governed by **four dominant international standards**, each defining the mechanical connector geometry, electrical ratings (voltage and current limits), communication protocol (between the vehicle and the charger), and safety interlocks. These standards are:

- **CHAdeMO** – A Japanese-origin DC fast-charging standard developed by the **CHAdeMO Association** (founded 2010), enabling Level 3 DC charging up to **400 kW** in its latest specification.
- **CCS Type 1 (CCS1)** – The North American Combined Charging System, combining a **SAE J1772** AC inlet with two additional large DC pins, standardized under **SAE J1772 Combo** and **IEC 62196-3** configuration AA.
- **CCS Type 2 (CCS2)** – The European Combined Charging System, combining an **IEC 62196-2 Type 2** AC inlet with two additional DC pins, standardized under **IEC 62196-3** configuration EE.
- **GB/T** – The Chinese mandatory national standard **GB/T 20234.3**, governing DC charging interfaces within China, with the 2015 revision supporting up to **250 kW** and the 2023 revision targeting **800 V / 600 A** (≈ 480 kW).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** Module 3 mandates comparative study of these standards — students must know connector pinouts, communication protocols, power levels, and regional adoption. Frequently tested as short notes and comparison tables.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Fuel Nozzle" Comparison**
> Imagine the world had **four different fuel nozzle shapes** for petrol cars. In the EV world, this is exactly what has happened. If you drive a **Nissan Leaf** in Japan, you use a **CHAdeMO** plug (like a thin circular nozzle). If you drive a **Tesla** or **BMW i4** in Europe, you use a **CCS2** plug (like a bulky nozzle with two extra fat pins). If you buy an EV in **China**, you must use a **GB/T** plug (a unique round 9-pin design). In **North America**, Tesla now also uses **CCS1**. Just like carrying a fuel adapter at airports, an EV owner needs the **right physical connector AND the right digital "handshake"** (communication protocol) for the charger to dispense electricity.

### 1.3 Key Physical & Electrical Constants

- **CHAdeMO** maximum current: **400 A** (liquid-cooled variant)
- **CCS1 / CCS2** maximum current: **500 A** (liquid-cooled, per IEC 62196-3)
- **GB/T 20234.3-2015** maximum current: **250 A** (up to 1000 V DC)
- **CHAdeMO communication protocol:** **CAN bus (ISO 11898)** at 500 kbps
- **CCS communication protocol:** **PLC (Power Line Communication)** over the **Control Pilot (CP)** pin using **ISO 15118** for high-level smart charging (V2G, plug-and-charge)
- **GB/T communication protocol:** **CAN bus (GB/T 27930)** at 250 kbps

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction:** CHAdeMO and GB/T use **CAN bus** (a digital automotive protocol), while CCS uses **PLC** (high-frequency data modulated onto the charging cable itself). This is a common KTU 2-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic Breakdown

Each DC fast-charging standard operates on a **three-layer architecture**:

**Layer 1 — Mechanical / Connector Layer**
- Defines the **pin layout**, locking mechanism, and cable cross-section.
- CHAdeMO: 10-pin circular connector (large).
- CCS1: 5-pin AC combo + 2 large DC pins (J1772-derived).
- CCS2: 7-pin Type 2 AC combo + 2 large DC pins.
- GB/T: 9-pin (or 7-pin) round connector with a locking pin.

**Layer 2 — Electrical / Power Layer**
- Regulates **DC voltage range**, **maximum current**, and **insulation class**.
- All four standards operate as **EV-side conduction systems** (the vehicle inlet connects to the charger plug — the user never touches live conductors).

**Layer 3 — Communication / Control Layer**
- CHAdeMO → **CAN (500 kbps)**
- CCS → **PLC over CP/PE, ISO 15118 / DIN 70121**
- GB/T → **CAN (250 kbps, GB/T 27930)**

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Standard's Design Choice

- **Why did CCS add 2 large DC pins to the AC connector?**  
  → To enable a **single inlet** for both AC (slow) and DC (fast) charging, reducing vehicle cost and inlet real estate.

- **Why does CHAdeMO use CAN bus?**  
  → Because Japanese automakers (Nissan, Mitsubishi, Toyota) already used CAN bus extensively in their vehicles — it leveraged an **existing, proven automotive protocol**.

- **Why does GB/T use a round connector and CAN?**  
  → It was designed independently from IEC standards; the round form factor is mechanically robust and the CAN-based GB/T 27930 protocol was tailored for Chinese BMS (Battery Management Systems).

- **Why does CCS use PLC instead of CAN?**  
  → PLC allows **high-bandwidth data** needed for **plug-and-charge authentication, V2G, and ISO 15118** — features CAN bus cannot easily support.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Comparison Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | CHAdeMO | CCS Type 1 | CCS Type 2 | GB/T 20234.3 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Region | Japan (global) | North America | Europe (global) | China |
| Governing Body | CHAdeMO Association | SAE / IEC | IEC | SAC (China) |
| Standard Reference | IEC 62196-3 (AA config.) | SAE J1772 Combo | IEC 62196-3 (EE config.) | GB/T 20234.3-2015 |
| Max Power (current) | **400 kW** | **350 kW** | **350 kW** | **250 kW** (→ 480 kW) |
| Max DC Voltage | 1000 V | 1000 V | 1000 V | 1000 V |
| Max DC Current | 400 A | 500 A | 500 A | 250 A (→ 600 A) |
| Communication | CAN bus (500 kbps) | PLC (ISO 15118) | PLC (ISO 15118) | CAN (GB/T 27930) |
| Connector Shape | Round, 10-pin | J1772 + 2 DC pins | Type 2 + 2 DC pins | Round, 9-pin |
| AC Charging Support | No (separate inlet) | Yes (J1772 AC) | Yes (Type 2 AC) | No (separate inlet) |
| V2G Capable | Yes (CHAdeMO V2X) | Yes (ISO 15118) | Yes (ISO 15118) | Limited |
| Plug-and-Charge | No (auth via CAN) | Yes (ISO 15118 PnC) | Yes (ISO 15118 PnC) | No |
| Liquid-Cooled Cable | Yes (v2.0+) | Yes | Yes | Yes (v2015+) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "C-C-G-C-P":**  
> **C**HANbus (CHAdeMO), **C**ombo (CCS1/2), **C**AN (GB/T), **P**LC (ISO 15118) — wait, recompose:  
> **"JAPAN = CAN, CHINA = CAN, EUROPE/USA = PLC"** → remember this for 2-mark Q's.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

These standards govern **billions of dollars** in charging infrastructure investment worldwide. Engineers must select the **right standard** based on:
- **Regional regulatory mandates** (China mandates GB/T; EU mandates CCS2).
- **Vehicle OEM choice** (Nissan = CHAdeMO; VW = CCS2; BYD = GB/T).
- **Power requirements** (trucks → CCS2 350 kW; city cars → CHAdeMO 50 kW).
- **Smart-grid integration** (ISO 15118 PLC enables V2G → CCS wins for future-proofing).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section converts the **technical specifications** into **computable engineering quantities** — the type of numerical problems KTU frequently sets in Part B (7-mark sub-parts).

### 3.1 Derivation: Charging Power from Standard Ratings

The fundamental DC charging power equation is:

$$P = V_{\text{DC}} \times I_{\text{DC}}$$

where:
- $P$ = delivered charging power (in **kW**)
- $V_{\text{DC}}$ = DC bus voltage at the EV inlet (in **V**)
- $I_{\text{DC}}$ = DC charging current (in **A**)

**Step 1 — Verify the maximum power limit of the standard.**  
For CCS2: $V_{\max} = 1000\ \text{V}$, $I_{\max} = 500\ \text{A}$

$$P_{\max} = 1000\ \text{V} \times 500\ \text{A} = 500{,}000\ \text{W} = 500\ \text{kW}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the standard's published cap.**  
Although the connector *can* technically deliver 500 kW, the **IEC 62196-3 standard specifies 350 kW** as the typical public-charging limit due to cable thermal limits.

$$P_{\text{design}} = \min\!\left(V_{\max} \cdot I_{\max},\ P_{\text{standard cap}}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Compare across standards for the same 800 V vehicle.**  
A modern EV with an 800 V architecture (e.g., Hyundai Ioniq 5, Porsche Taycan) charging at 350 A:

- **CHAdeMO:** $P = 800\ \text{V} \times 350\ \text{A} = 280\ \text{kW}$ (within its 400 kW cap).
- **CCS2:** $P = 800\ \text{V} \times 350\ \text{A} = 280\ \text{kW}$ (well within 350 kW cap).
- **GB/T 2015:** $P = 800\ \text{V} \times 250\ \text{A} = 200\ \text{kW}$ (capped at 250 A).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Charging Time for a Given Battery Pack

The time to charge an EV battery from $SoC_1$ to $SoC_2$ is:

$$t = \frac{E_{\text{required}}}{P_{\text{avg}}} = \frac{(SoC_2 - SoC_1) \cdot E_{\text{bat}}}{P_{\text{avg}}}$$

where $E_{\text{bat}}$ is the battery's nominal energy (in kWh) and $P_{\text{avg}}$ is the average charging power over the interval.

**Numerical Example (KTU-typical 7-mark problem):**

> A 75 kWh EV (Tesla Model 3 LR) uses a CCS2 charger rated 150 kW, with average charging power 130 kW. Find the time to charge from 20% to 80%.

**Step 1 — Compute required energy.**

$$E_{\text{required}} = (0.80 - 0.20) \times 75\ \text{kWh} = 0.60 \times 75 = 45\ \text{kWh}$$

**Step 2 — Compute time using average power.**

$$t = \frac{E_{\text{required}}}{P_{\text{avg}}} = \frac{45\ \text{kWh}}{130\ \text{kW}} = 0.3462\ \text{h}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to minutes.**

$$t = 0.3462 \times 60 \approx 20.77\ \text{min} \approx 20\ \text{min}\ 46\ \text{s}$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Power vs. Current for Each Standard

The following matrix is **directly testable in KTU Part A (3-mark comparison Q's):**

$$P_{\text{standard}}(V) = I_{\max,\text{standard}} \times V_{\text{EV}}$$

| EV Voltage | CHAdeMO (400 A) | CCS1/2 (500 A) | GB/T 2015 (250 A) | GB/T 2023 (600 A) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 400 V | 160 kW | 200 kW | 100 kW | 240 kW |
| 600 V | 240 kW | 300 kW | 150 kW | 360 kW |
| 800 V | 320 kW | 400 kW | 200 kW | 480 kW |
| 1000 V | 400 kW | 500 kW | 250 kW | 600 kW |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pattern recognition for exams:** Power scales **linearly** with both voltage and current. Higher vehicle architectures (800 V) are **strongly favoured** by the new GB/T 2023 and CHAdeMO 2.0 standards for fast charging.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Cable Cross-Section for a Given Charging Current

Using the **current-density heuristic** for EV DC charging cables (typically $J \approx 5\ \text{A/mm}^2$ for uncooled, $J \approx 12\text{–}15\ \text{A/mm}^2$ for liquid-cooled):

$$A_{\text{cable}} = \frac{I_{\max}}{J}$$

**For CHAdeMO 400 A (liquid-cooled):**

$$A_{\text{cable}} = \frac{400\ \text{A}}{12\ \text{A/mm}^2} \approx 33.3\ \text{mm}^2$$

**For CCS2 500 A (liquid-cooled):**

$$A_{\text{cable}} = \frac{500\ \text{A}}{12\ \text{A/mm}^2} \approx 41.7\ \text{mm}^2$$

**Why liquid cooling?** Without it, the cable would need:

$$A_{\text{uncooled}} = \frac{500\ \text{A}}{5\ \text{A/mm}^2} = 100\ \text{mm}^2$$

which would be **too heavy and stiff** for user handling. This justifies the **liquid-cooled cable mandate** in modern CCS and CHAdeMO high-power chargers.

### 4.2 Communication Architecture: CAN vs. PLC

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeP1["EV BATTERY MANAGEMENT SYSTEM"]
    nodeP2["EV CHARGE CONTROLLER"]
    nodeP3["DC FAST CHARGER STATION"]

    nodeP1 --- nodeP2
    nodeP2 --- nodeP3

    nodeC1["CAN HIGH AND CAN LOW TWISTED PAIR CHAdeMO AND GB T"]
    nodeC2["CONTROL PILOT CP AND PROTECTIVE EARTH PE PLC MODULATION CCS"]

    nodeP2 --- nodeC1
    nodeP2 --- nodeC2
```

**Interpretation:**  
- **CHAdeMO & GB/T** use **two physical wires** (CAN-H, CAN-L) for digital communication, plus separate pilot circuits.  
- **CCS** uses **the same two wires** (CP, PE) for **both charging control AND high-speed data** via PLC — a more elegant, cost-effective design.

---

**Engineering Note:** Modern 350 kW CCS chargers use **modular power stacks** (typically 7 × 50 kW or 10 × 35 kW) that can be dynamically paralleled based on vehicle demand, optimizing **efficiency and grid load**.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**  
**Compare the communication protocols used in CHAdeMO, CCS, and GB/T DC fast-charging standards. Which one is most future-ready for V2G applications and why?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Standard | Communication Protocol | Speed | V2G Support |
|---|---|---|---|
| CHAdeMO | CAN bus (ISO 11898) | 500 kbps | Yes (CHAdeMO V2X since 2020) |
| CCS1/CCS2 | PLC (Power Line Comm.) over CP/PE using **ISO 15118** | Up to 1 Mbps | Yes (native in ISO 15118) |
| GB/T | CAN bus (GB/T 27930) | 250 kbps | Limited |

**Conclusion:** **CCS with ISO 15118** is the most future-ready for V2G (Vehicle-to-Grid) applications because:
1. **Higher bandwidth** (1 Mbps) supports secure TLS-encrypted transactions.
2. **Plug-and-Charge** (PnC) eliminates RFID cards/apps.
3. ISO 15118 natively supports **bidirectional power flow** schedules and **cryptographic certificates** required for grid services.

(3 marks — 1 mark for protocol identification, 1 mark for comparison, 1 mark for V2G conclusion)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**  
**Why does the CCS standard use PLC (Power Line Communication) over the Control Pilot pin instead of dedicated CAN wires like CHAdeMO?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Cost reduction** — Eliminates dedicated CAN-H/CAN-L wires, reducing cable weight and connector pin count (CCS uses only 2 communication wires vs. CHAdeMO's 2 CAN + 1 pilot = 3 wires). [1 mark]

2. **Higher bandwidth** — PLC supports up to 1 Mbps, enabling **ISO 15118** features like plug-and-charge, smart charging schedules, and V2G that 500 kbps CAN cannot handle. [1 mark]

3. **Backward compatibility** — PLC modulation can coexist with the basic **IEC 61851-1 PWM pilot signal** used for slow AC charging, allowing **one physical layer for both AC and DC** control. [1 mark]

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice: Answer A or B)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Pattern**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Adapted]**  
**(a)** Explain the **mechanical design, pin configuration, and electrical ratings** of the **GB/T 20234.3-2015** DC fast-charging connector. Compare it with the **CCS Type 2** connector in terms of maximum power, current, and communication protocol. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Chinese EV with an **800 V architecture** and a **100 kWh** battery is charged at a GB/T 20234.3-2015 station. The charger delivers a **constant 1000 V at its rated maximum current**. Calculate:  
(i) the **maximum charging power** delivered to the vehicle, and  
(ii) the **time taken** to charge the battery from 10% to 90% State of Charge (SoC), assuming **95% charger efficiency** and an **average power factor of 0.95** (use $P_{\text{avg}} = P_{\max} \times \text{PF}$ for the effective power). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) GB/T 20234.3-2015 vs. CCS Type 2 — Comparison (7 marks):**

| Feature | GB/T 20234.3-2015 | CCS Type 2 (IEC 62196-3 EE) |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | China (mandatory) | EU (now global) |
| Connector Geometry | Round, 9 pins | Type 2 AC + 2 DC pins (oval-combo) |
| Max Voltage | 1000 V DC | 1000 V DC |
| Max Current | 250 A (continuous) | 500 A (liquid-cooled) |
| Max Power | 250 kW | 350 kW (public) / 500 kW (theoretical) |
| Communication | CAN bus @ 250 kbps (GB/T 27930) | PLC over CP/PE (ISO 15118) |
| Locking Mechanism | Mechanical latch pin | Electromagnetic latch |
| AC Support | No (separate AC inlet) | Yes (combined inlet) |
| Cable Cooling | Liquid-cooled for >150 kW | Liquid-cooled standard for >200 kW |
| V2G Support | Limited | Native (ISO 15118) |

**Key design differences:**
1. GB/T uses a **single round connector**; CCS uses a **combo (AC+DC)** design — this allows CCS vehicles to use the **same inlet** for both 22 kW AC home charging and 350 kW DC fast charging.
2. GB/T's lower current (250 A) is a direct consequence of the **CAN-based protocol bottleneck** and conservative thermal design of the round pins.
3. CCS's higher power density and smart-charging capability position it as the **de facto global standard** outside Japan and China. [Full 7 marks: 2 for GB/T specifics, 2 for CCS specifics, 2 for comparison, 1 for conclusion]

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $V_{\text{EV}} = 800\ \text{V}$ (vehicle architecture)
- $E_{\text{bat}} = 100\ \text{kWh}$
- Charger voltage = 1000 V
- GB/T max current = 250 A
- Charger efficiency $\eta = 0.95$
- Power factor $PF = 0.95$
- SoC range: 10% → 90%

**Step 1 — Determine the actual charging current.**  
Since the vehicle bus is 800 V and the charger can deliver up to 1000 V, the **battery management system limits current to 250 A** (GB/T cap).

**Step 2 — Compute maximum power delivered at the DC output of the charger.**

$$P_{\text{DC,out}} = V_{\text{DC}} \times I_{\max} = 1000\ \text{V} \times 250\ \text{A} = 250{,}000\ \text{W} = 250\ \text{kW}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the power factor and efficiency to get the effective average power at the battery.**

$$P_{\text{avg,bat}} = P_{\text{DC,out}} \times \eta \times PF$$

$$P_{\text{avg,bat}} = 250\ \text{kW} \times 0.95 \times 0.95 = 225.625\ \text{kW}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the required energy to charge from 10% to 90%.**

$$E_{\text{required}} = (SoC_2 - SoC_1) \times E_{\text{bat}} = (0.90 - 0.10) \times 100\ \text{kWh} = 80\ \text{kWh}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the charging time.**

$$t = \frac{E_{\text{required}}}{P_{\text{avg,bat}}} = \frac{80\ \text{kWh}}{225.625\ \text{kW}} = 0.3546\ \text{h}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to minutes.**

$$t = 0.3546 \times 60\ \text{min} = 21.28\ \text{min} \approx 21\ \text{min}\ 17\ \text{s}$$

**Final Answer (boxed):**
- **(i) Maximum charging power = 250 kW** [3 marks]
- **(ii) Charging time from 10% to 90% ≈ 21.3 minutes** [4 marks]

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Adapted]**  
**(a)** With neat pinout descriptions, explain the **architecture and communication flow** of the **CHAdeMO** and **CCS Type 1** fast-charging standards. Highlight **three key differences** between them. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 60 kWh EV (Hyundai Ioniq 5, 800 V architecture) is charged at a **350 kW CCS2 station**. The charging session follows a **tapered profile**: the first 50% of SoC is charged at the **maximum 350 kW**, and the remaining 50% is charged at an **average of 150 kW** (due to the CC-CV taper of the Li-ion pack). Calculate:  
(i) the **total energy delivered** during the session,  
(ii) the **total time** for a full 10% → 100% charge, and  
(iii) the **average power** over the entire session. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) CHAdeMO vs. CCS Type 1 — Architecture & Key Differences (7 marks):**

**CHAdeMO Architecture (developed by TEPCO + Japanese OEMs):**
- **Connector:** Round, 10-pin design.
- **Pinout:**
  1. Ground (2 pins)
  2. DC+ (high-voltage positive)
  3. DC− (high-voltage negative)
  4. CAN-H (communication high)
  5. CAN-L (communication low)
  6. Charge enable / interlock
  7. Proximity detection
  8. **Charger output (status)** — unique pin confirming the charger is ready.
- **Communication:** Dedicated CAN bus at 500 kbps; messages conform to **CHAdeMO 1.x/2.0 protocol** (charging current, voltage, status, SOC).
- **V2G:** Native support since CHAdeMO 2.0 (2018) — first to market with V2X.

**CCS Type 1 Architecture (SAE / North America):**
- **Connector:** SAE J1772 AC 5-pin base + 2 large DC pins (Combo-1).
- **Pinout:**
  - AC pins: L1, L2/N, PE, CP (Control Pilot), CS (proximity)
  - DC pins: DC+ and DC− (large, oversized for high current)
- **Communication:** **PLC modulated onto CP & PE** using **HomePlug Green PHY**; protocol is **ISO 15118** (for DC) and **DIN 70121** (legacy DC).
- **V2G:** Supported via ISO 15118-20 (bidirectional).

**Three Key Differences:**

| S.No. | CHAdeMO | CCS Type 1 |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Uses **dedicated CAN bus** (separate wires) | Uses **PLC** over existing CP/PE wires |
| 2 | **Separate AC & DC inlets** on the vehicle | **Single combo inlet** for both AC and DC |
| 3 | First to support V2G (since 2018) | Supports V2G via ISO 15118-20 (newer spec) |

[Marks: 2 for CHAdeMO description, 2 for CCS1 description, 3 for differences]

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $E_{\text{bat}} = 60\ \text{kWh}$
- SoC range: 10% → 100% (ΔSoC = 0.90)
- Phase 1 (10% → 60%, i.e., ΔSoC = 0.50): $P_1 = 350\ \text{kW}$
- Phase 2 (60% → 100%, i.e., ΔSoC = 0.40): $P_2 = 150\ \text{kW}$

**Step 1 — Energy delivered in each phase.**

$$E_1 = 0.50 \times 60\ \text{kWh} = 30\ \text{kWh}$$

$$E_2 = 0.40 \times 60\ \text{kWh} = 24\ \text{kWh}$$

$$E_{\text{total}} = 30 + 24 = 54\ \text{kWh} \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 2 — Time for each phase.**

$$t_1 = \frac{E_1}{P_1} = \frac{30\ \text{kWh}}{350\ \text{kW}} = 0.0857\ \text{h} = 5.14\ \text{min}$$

$$t_2 = \frac{E_2}{P_2} = \frac{24\ \text{kWh}}{150\ \text{kW}} = 0.1600\ \text{h} = 9.60\ \text{min}$$

$$t_{\text{total}} = 5.14 + 9.60 = 14.74\ \text{min} \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 3 — Average power over the entire session.**

$$P_{\text{avg}} = \frac{E_{\text{total}}}{t_{\text{total}}} = \frac{54\ \text{kWh}}{14.74/60\ \text{h}} = \frac{54\ \text{kWh}}{0.2457\ \text{h}} \approx 219.8\ \text{kW} \quad \text{...(iii)}$$

**Final Answer (boxed):**
- **(i) Total energy delivered = 54 kWh** [2 marks]
- **(ii) Total charging time ≈ 14.74 minutes (≈ 14 min 44 s)** [3 marks]
- **(iii) Average power over the session ≈ 219.8 kW** [2 marks]

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse connector pin counts.** CCS1 = 5 AC + 2 DC = 7 total; CCS2 = 7 Type 2 + 2 DC = 9 total. Mixing these up is a **1-mark direct cut**.
> 2. **Always state the standard's publication year** (e.g., GB/T 20234.3-2015) — vague answers lose 0.5–1 mark.
> 3. **In numerical problems, the most common error is forgetting to apply the standard's current cap.** If the calculation gives 350 A but the standard caps at 250 A (GB/T), you MUST use 250 A. Examiners specifically check this.
> 4. **Don't write "CHAdeMO is Japanese and uses CAN" and stop there.** Add the *implication* — e.g., "This means CHAdeMO cannot natively support ISO 15118 plug-and-charge, making it less attractive for future V2G smart-grid applications." Examiners reward **engineering insight**, not just facts.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] **Four global DC fast-charging standards:** CHAdeMO (Japan), CCS1 (N. America), CCS2 (Europe), GB/T (China).
- [x] **CHAdeMO** = CAN bus, 10-pin round, max 400 kW (v2.0), first to offer V2G.
- [x] **CCS (Type 1 & 2)** = PLC over CP/PE, combo connector (AC+DC), max 350 kW, ISO 15118 smart charging.
- [x] **GB/T 20234.3-2015** = CAN (GB/T 27930), 9-pin round, 250 kW cap, 2023 revision targets 480 kW.
- [x] **Communication dichotomy:** CAN bus (CHAdeMO & GB/T) vs. PLC (CCS) — most-tested single point.
- [x] **Power formula:** $P = V_{\text{DC}} \times I_{\text{DC}}$; always cap by the **standard's published limit**.
- [x] **Charging time formula:** $t = \frac{\Delta SoC \cdot E_{\text{bat}}}{P_{\text{avg}}}$; remember unit conversion (h ↔ min).
- [x] **Cable cooling:** Modern high-power (≥ 350 kW) requires **liquid cooling** to keep cable cross-section under 50 mm².
- [x] **V2G leader:** CHAdeMO (since 2018) and CCS via ISO 15118-20; GB/T limited.
- [x] **Adoption logic:** China mandates GB/T; EU mandates CCS2; Japan/India gravitating to CCS2; USA uses CCS1.
- [x] **Plug-and-Charge (PnC):** Native to ISO 15118 (CCS) — automatic authentication via digital certificate, no card/app needed.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction - Solar Photovoltaic - Solar Cell Fundamentals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET742 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES AND RENEWABLE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction - Solar Photovoltaic - Solar Cell Fundamentals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solar Photovoltaic (PV) Effect**: The Solar Photovoltaic effect is the direct, solid-state, contactless conversion of sunlight (photons) into electricity (DC voltage/current) at the atomic level of a semiconductor material, most commonly a p–n junction diode (typically crystalline silicon), when incident photon energy exceeds the semiconductor's band-gap energy.

A **Solar Cell** is the fundamental building block of a PV system — essentially a large-area p–n junction diode with a thin, heavily doped top layer and metallic front/back contacts. When packaged and protected against environmental degradation, multiple cells wired together form a **PV module (panel)**, and multiple modules form a **PV array**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Green Leaf of Engineering" Analogy** 🌱
> 
> Imagine a green leaf in a plant. Inside the leaf, **chlorophyll molecules** act like tiny solar panels: they absorb sunlight and use its energy to combine CO₂ and water into sugar (food/electricity). The chloroplast never makes noise, never burns fuel, and runs purely on light.
> 
> A **solar cell is the "chloroplast of engineering"**:
> - **Sunlight (photons)** = the "food" entering the system
> - **Semiconductor (silicon)** = the "chlorophyll" that absorbs it
> - **p–n junction** = the "reaction chamber" that separates the charges
> - **External circuit** = the "vein" that carries the generated electricity away
> 
> The instant a photon hits the cell, the silicon releases a free electron, and the cell becomes a tiny battery. Millions of cells together power an entire EV charging station or a house.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Constants (must memorize for KTU 2024 exam):**
> 
> - **Solar constant (AM1.5G)**: $G_{STC} = 1000 \text{ W/m}^2$ (standard test irradiance)
> - **Cell temperature at STC**: $T_{STC} = 25^{\circ}\text{C} = 298.15 \text{ K}$
> - **Planck's constant**: $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J·s}$
> - **Speed of light**: $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$
> - **Elementary charge**: $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$
> - **Boltzmann constant**: $k = 1.381 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J/K}$
> - **Band-gap of crystalline silicon (c-Si)**: $E_g \approx 1.12 \text{ eV}$

### 1.4 Why Solar PV Matters for Electric Vehicles (EV Linkage)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly links Solar PV to EV technology under **PEAET742**. Key applications include:
> - **Vehicle-Integrated Photovoltaics (VIPV)**: Solar roofs/bonnets on EVs (e.g., Toyota Prius, Hyundai Sonata Hybrid, Lightyear 0, Aptera).
> - **Solar EV Charging Stations**: Off-grid DC fast chargers powered by canopy-mounted PV arrays.
> - **Solar-to-Battery Direct Charging**: Reducing grid dependence and the Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Photovoltaic Logic

The complete energy-to-electricity pipeline of a solar cell follows **six sequential physical steps**. Examiners frequently test these.

1. **Photon Absorption**: A photon from the sun strikes the semiconductor. For absorption to occur, the photon's energy must satisfy:
   
   $$E_{photon} = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda} \geq E_g$$

   where $E_g$ is the band-gap of the semiconductor. Photons with energy **less than $E_g$** pass through unabsorbed; photons with energy **much greater than $E_g$** lose excess energy as heat (thermalization loss).

2. **Electron–Hole Pair Generation**: An absorbed photon excites a valence-band electron into the conduction band, leaving behind a "hole." This creates a free **electron–hole pair (EHP)**.

3. **Charge Separation at the Junction**: The built-in electric field at the p–n junction depletion region sweeps the electron to the n-side and the hole to the p-side (a process called the **photogeneration current**).

4. **Charge Collection at Contacts**: Metallic front (grid) and back contacts collect the separated charges. The front contact is shaped like a thin comb/fingers to maximize light entry while minimizing series resistance.

5. **External Current Flow**: When a load is connected, the collected electrons flow through the external circuit, delivering usable DC electrical power.

6. **Recombination Losses (parasitic)**: Not all EHPs are collected. Some recombine at defects, surfaces, and within the bulk, reducing the cell's efficiency.

### 2.2 The "Why & How" Reasoning

| Step | Why? | How? |
|------|------|------|
| 1. Photon Absorption | Energy quantization requires $E_{photon} \geq E_g$ | Direct bandgap semiconductors absorb stronger |
| 2. EHP Generation | Band theory — photon excites electron across forbidden gap | Happens in p-, n-, and depletion regions |
| 3. Charge Separation | Without field, electrons and holes would just recombine | Built-in $V_{bi}$ at the junction acts as a one-way valve |
| 4. Charge Collection | Need low-resistance path to external world | Front grid + back contact (screen-printed Ag/Al) |
| 5. External Current | Light energy must be delivered to a load | Load completes the circuit |
| 6. Recombination Losses | Crystal defects, surface states, Auger effect | Passivation layers (SiNₓ, Al₂O₃) reduce them |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule for Tables**: Since KTU Engine V10 forbids the `|` character inside table cells, all absolute-value and condition bars use `\vert` or `\mid`.

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Photon Energy | $E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | Energy of a single photon | J or eV |
| 2 | Cut-off Wavelength | $\lambda_c = \dfrac{1.24}{E_g \text{ (eV)}}$ | Max wavelength absorbed ($\mu$m) | $\mu$m |
| 3 | Ideal Diode Current | $I = I_0 \left( e^{\dfrac{qV}{kT}} - 1 \right)$ | Shockley diode equation | A |
| 4 | Solar Cell I–V Equation | $I = I_L - I_0 \left( e^{\dfrac{q(V + IR_s)}{nkT}} - 1 \right) - \dfrac{V + IR_s}{R_{sh}}$ | Real one-diode model | A |
| 5 | Open-Circuit Voltage | $V_{oc} = \dfrac{nkT}{q} \ln\!\left( \dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1 \right)$ | Voltage at zero current | V |
| 6 | Short-Circuit Current | $I_{sc} \approx I_L$ | Current at zero voltage | A |
| 7 | Maximum Power Point | $P_{max} = V_{mp} \times I_{mp}$ | Peak electrical output | W |
| 8 | Fill Factor | $FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} \, I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \, I_{sc}}$ | "Squareness" of I–V curve | Dimensionless (0–1) |
| 9 | Cell Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{V_{oc} \, I_{sc} \, FF}{A \times G}$ | Conversion efficiency | % |
| 10 | Irradiance | $G$ | Power density of sunlight | W/m² |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Designing a 1 kW rooftop PV system** in Kerala: With $G \approx 5 \text{ kWh/m}^2/\text{day}$ and $\eta_{module} \approx 18\%$, area needed $\approx \dfrac{1000}{0.18 \times 5} \approx 6.6 \text{ m}^2$.
- **EV Solar Roof Sizing**: A car roof of $\sim 1.2 \text{ m}^2$ yields $\sim 200 \text{ W}$ peak — enough for $\sim 10$–$15$ km/day of auxiliary range.
- **Grid-Tie Inverter Sizing**: Always oversize the inverter DC-AC ratio by 1.1×–1.3× to harvest early-morning/late-evening light.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: The Ideal Solar Cell I–V Equation (One-Diode Model)

**Starting Point**: A solar cell is modeled as a current source in parallel with a diode. By the superposition principle, the terminal current $I$ is the photogenerated current $I_L$ minus the diode's dark current $I_D$:

$$I = I_L - I_D$$

**Step 1**: Apply the Shockley diode equation for the dark current flowing in the opposite direction:

$$I_D = I_0 \left( e^{\dfrac{qV}{nkT}} - 1 \right)$$

where:
- $I_0$ = diode reverse saturation current (A)
- $V$ = terminal voltage across the junction (V)
- $n$ = ideality factor ($1 \le n \le 2$)
- $kT/q$ = thermal voltage $\approx 25.85 \text{ mV}$ at 300 K

**Step 2**: Substitute $I_D$ back into the KCL equation:

$$I = I_L - I_0 \left( e^{\dfrac{qV}{nkT}} - 1 \right)$$

This is the **ideal one-diode model** of a solar cell.

**Step 3**: Add parasitic resistances to get the **practical one-diode model**. The series resistance $R_s$ (from contacts, grid, and bulk resistance) drops voltage as $IR_s$, and the shunt resistance $R_{sh}$ (from leakage paths) draws a small leakage current $\dfrac{V + IR_s}{R_{sh}}$:

$$\boxed{\,I = I_L - I_0 \left( e^{\dfrac{q(V + IR_s)}{nkT}} - 1 \right) - \dfrac{V + IR_s}{R_{sh}}\,}$$

**Step 4 — Boundary Condition Checks**:

*Case A — Short-Circuit* ($V = 0$): The diode term becomes $I_0(e^0 - 1) = 0$, and the shunt leakage is $0/R_{sh} = 0$. Therefore:

$$I_{sc} = I_L \quad \text{(only if } R_{sh} \to \infty\text{)}$$

*Case B — Open-Circuit* ($I = 0$): Then $0 = I_L - I_0\left(e^{\frac{qV_{oc}}{nkT}} - 1\right) - \dfrac{V_{oc}}{R_{sh}}$. Ignoring $R_{sh}$:

$$V_{oc} = \dfrac{nkT}{q} \ln\!\left( \dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1 \right) \;\approx\; \dfrac{nkT}{q} \ln\!\left( \dfrac{I_{sc}}{I_0} \right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight**: $V_{oc}$ is **logarithmic** in light intensity (doubling the sun does NOT double $V_{oc}$ — it adds $\sim 18$ mV for c-Si). $I_{sc}$ is **linear** in light intensity (doubling sun doubles $I_{sc}$). This asymmetry is why MPPT algorithms track $V_{mp}$ carefully.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Fill Factor (FF)

The **Fill Factor** is the ratio of the rectangle of actual maximum power to the rectangle of ideal maximum power $V_{oc} \times I_{sc}$.

**Step 1**: Define the Maximum Power Point (MPP). At MPP, the differential power is zero:

$$\dfrac{dP}{dV} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{d(IV)}{dV} = 0$$

**Step 2**: Use the product rule:

$$I + V \dfrac{dI}{dV} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{dI}{dV} = -\dfrac{I}{V}$$

**Step 3**: This is solved numerically (no closed-form solution) to yield $V_{mp}$ and $I_{mp}$. The **fill factor** is then defined as:

$$FF = \dfrac{P_{max}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}} = \dfrac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$$

**Step 4 — Empirical Approximation** (Green, 1981, very common in KTU numericals):

$$FF_0 = \dfrac{v_{oc} - \ln(v_{oc} + 0.72)}{v_{oc} + 1}$$

where $v_{oc} = \dfrac{qV_{oc}}{nkT}$ (normalized open-circuit voltage).

**Worked Example**: For $V_{oc} = 0.65 \text{ V}$, $n = 1.3$, $T = 300 \text{ K}$:
$v_{oc} = \dfrac{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 0.65}{1.3 \times 1.381 \times 10^{-23} \times 300} = 19.13$.

$$FF_0 = \dfrac{19.13 - \ln(19.13 + 0.72)}{19.13 + 1} = \dfrac{19.13 - 3.00}{20.13} \approx 0.80$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Conversion Efficiency $\eta$

**Step 1**: Define input solar power incident on cell area $A$:

$$P_{in} = G \cdot A$$

**Step 2**: Define output electrical power at MPP:

$$P_{max} = V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF$$

**Step 3**: Form the ratio:

$$\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF}{G \cdot A} \times 100\%$$

**Worked Example** (typical c-Si cell at STC):
- $V_{oc} = 0.65 \text{ V}$, $I_{sc} = 3.5 \text{ A}$, $FF = 0.80$, $A = 100 \text{ cm}^2 = 0.01 \text{ m}^2$, $G = 1000 \text{ W/m}^2$.

$$\eta = \dfrac{0.65 \times 3.5 \times 0.80}{1000 \times 0.01} = \dfrac{1.82}{10} = 0.182 = 18.2\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Numerical Tip**: Always convert $A$ to **m²** before plugging into the formula. A common student mistake is leaving $A$ in cm², which inflates the denominator by $10^4$.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Band-gap to Cut-off Wavelength

**Step 1**: At the band edge, photon energy equals band-gap:

$$E_{photon} = E_g \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{hc}{\lambda_c} = E_g$$

**Step 2**: Solve for $\lambda_c$:

$$\lambda_c = \dfrac{hc}{E_g}$$

**Step 3**: Plug in numerical values: $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s. If $E_g$ is in eV, then $E_g \text{ (J)} = E_g \text{ (eV)} \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$. A useful shortcut:

$$\lambda_c \, (\mu m) = \dfrac{1.24}{E_g \, (\text{eV})}$$

**For c-Si** ($E_g = 1.12 \text{ eV}$): $\lambda_c = \dfrac{1.24}{1.12} = 1.107 \,\mu m$. Silicon therefore absorbs visible and near-IR light up to ~1.1 $\mu$m.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1: Physical Cross-Section of a Crystalline Silicon Solar Cell

A physical cross-section requires precise layered geometry. To respect the Mermaid safeguards (no complex photorealism), we render the layered structure as a stacked functional schematic.

```mermaid
graph TB
    sun["SUNLIGHT PHOTON FLUX"]:::source
    arc["Anti-Reflection Coating SiNx 70 to 80 nm"]:::layer
    top["Front Contact Silver Grid Fingers"]:::layer
    n["N-Type Emitter Layer Heavily Doped 0.3 micron"]:::layer
    pj["P-N Junction Depletion Region"]:::critical
    p["P-Type Base Wafer 180 to 200 micron"]:::layer
    bsf["Back Surface Field Al Doped p+ Layer"]:::layer
    back["Rear Contact Aluminum Metallization"]:::layer
    load["External DC Load Resistor"]:::output

    sun --> arc --> n --> pj --> p --> bsf --> back
    top -.-> n
    back -.-> load
    load -.-> top

    classDef source fill:#FFD700,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef layer fill:#C0C0C0,stroke:#696969,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef critical fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef output fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Layer-by-Layer Function**:
1. **ARC (SiNₓ)**: Reduces reflection from ~35% to ~2%.
2. **Front Grid**: Collects electrons; finger spacing trades off shadow loss vs. $R_s$.
3. **n⁺ Emitter**: Thin, heavily doped — where most blue light is absorbed.
4. **P-N Junction**: Built-in field separates photogenerated EHPs.
5. **p Base**: Thick absorber — where most red/IR light is absorbed.
6. **BSF (p⁺)**: Repels minority electrons → reduces back-recombination.
7. **Rear Contact**: Reflects unabsorbed light back into the cell (in PERC cells).

### 4.2 Diagram 2: Equivalent Circuit (One-Diode Model)

```mermaid
graph LR
    IL["Photocurrent Source I_L"]:::src
    D["Diode Dark Current I_D"]:::dev
    Rs["Series Resistance R_s"]:::res
    Rsh["Shunt Resistance R_sh"]:::res
    Vout["Terminal Voltage V"]:::out
    Iout["Load Current I"]:::out

    IL ---|parallel| D
    D --- Rsh
    IL --- Rs
    Rs --- Vout
    Vout --- Iout
    Rsh --- Vout

    classDef src fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef dev fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef res fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Diagram 3: Energy Band Diagram Under Illumination

```mermaid
graph TB
    conduction["CONDUCTION BAND E_c"]:::band
    fgap["FORBIDDEN BAND-GAP E_g = 1.12 eV"]:::gap
    valence["VALENCE BAND E_v"]:::band
    ef["QUASI FERMI LEVELS E_fc and E_fv split by qV"]:::ef
    e_gen["Photon Excites Electron from E_v to E_c"]:::event
    e_sep["Built-in Field Separates EHP at Junction"]:::event

    conduction --> fgap --> valence
    e_gen -.-> conduction
    e_sep -.-> ef

    classDef band fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef gap fill:#FFA500,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ef fill:#9370DB,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef event fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

### 4.4 Diagram 4: I–V Characteristic Curve (KTU Exam Staple)

```mermaid
graph TB
    origin["Origin 0,0"]:::axis
    isc["I_sc on Current Axis at V=0"]:::pt
    voc["V_oc on Voltage Axis at I=0"]:::pt
    mpp["Maximum Power Point V_mp x I_mp"]:::pt
    rect["P_max Rectangle Area"]:::area
    rect_idl["Ideal Rectangle V_oc x I_sc"]:::area
    knee["Knee Region MPP Locus"]:::event
    curve_label["Solar Cell I-V Curve Concave-down through I_sc and V_oc"]:::event

    isc ---|x-axis| voc
    mpp -.-> rect
    voc -.-> rect_idl
    isc -.-> rect_idl
    mpp --> knee
    knee --> curve_label

    classDef axis fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef pt fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef area fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef event fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Curve**: The ratio $\dfrac{P_{max} \text{ rectangle area}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \text{ rectangle area}} = FF$. A "square" curve → high FF (~0.85+); a "rounded knee" → low FF (~0.50).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[Q1] [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
> *Define the Solar Photovoltaic effect. With a neat energy band diagram, explain how a photon generates an electron–hole pair in a p–n junction solar cell.*

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

The **Solar Photovoltaic effect** is the direct conversion of light energy into electrical energy at the atomic scale of a semiconductor p–n junction (3 marks full).

*Mechanism*:
- A photon with energy $E = h\nu \geq E_g$ is absorbed in the semiconductor.
- The energy excites a valence-band electron into the conduction band, creating an **electron–hole pair (EHP)**.
- The built-in electric field at the depletion region sweeps the electron to the n-side and the hole to the p-side, generating a **photocurrent** and a **photovoltage**.
- Connected to a load, the device delivers DC electrical power.

---

> **[Q2] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
> *Distinguish between the open-circuit voltage ($V_{oc}$) and short-circuit current ($I_{sc}$) of a solar cell. State the governing equations for both.*

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

| Parameter | Definition | Governing Equation | Behaviour with Light Intensity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **$V_{oc}$** | Terminal voltage when load is disconnected (open circuit) | $V_{oc} = \dfrac{nkT}{q} \ln\!\left( \dfrac{I_{sc}}{I_0} + 1 \right)$ | Logarithmic — small increase per irradiance step |
| **$I_{sc}$** | Current flowing when terminals are shorted | $I_{sc} \approx I_L$ (proportional to $G$) | Linear — doubles with doubled irradiance |

(3 marks: 1 for definitions, 1 for equations, 1 for distinguishing behaviour)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[QUESTION A — Internal Choice 1] [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the ideal one-diode model equation of a solar cell, clearly stating the assumptions and the significance of each term. Show the boundary conditions for open-circuit voltage and short-circuit current.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A silicon solar cell has the following parameters at STC ($1000 \text{ W/m}^2$, $25^{\circ}\text{C}$):
- $I_L = 3.20 \text{ A}$, $I_0 = 9.5 \times 10^{-10} \text{ A}$, $n = 1.30$, $R_s = 0.01 \;\Omega$, $R_{sh} = 500 \;\Omega$.
- Cell area $A = 100 \text{ cm}^2$.
- Calculate (i) $V_{oc}$, (ii) $I_{sc}$, (iii) $FF$ assuming $V_{mp} = 0.50 \text{ V}$, $I_{mp} = 2.90 \text{ A}$, and (iv) the conversion efficiency $\eta$.

**Model Solution for (a)**:
1. State assumption: Solar cell = current source $I_L$ in parallel with diode carrying dark current $I_D$. Apply KCL → $I = I_L - I_D$ [1 mark]
2. Substitute Shockley diode equation: $I_D = I_0 \left( e^{qV/nkT} - 1 \right)$ [1 mark]
3. Derive ideal equation: $I = I_L - I_0 \left( e^{qV/nkT} - 1 \right)$ [1 mark]
4. Add $R_s$ and $R_{sh}$ for practical model [1 mark]
5. Apply $V = 0$ → $I_{sc} = I_L$ (1 mark)
6. Apply $I = 0$ → derive $V_{oc} = \dfrac{nkT}{q} \ln\!\left( \dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1 \right)$ [2 marks]

**Model Solution for (b)**:
- **(i) $V_{oc}$**: Thermal voltage $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q} = 0.02585 \text{ V}$ at 300 K. For $n = 1.30$, $nV_T = 0.0336 \text{ V}$.
  
  $$V_{oc} = 0.0336 \times \ln\!\left( \dfrac{3.20}{9.5 \times 10^{-10}} \right) = 0.0336 \times \ln(3.368 \times 10^{9}) = 0.0336 \times 21.94 = 0.737 \text{ V}$$

- **(ii) $I_{sc}$**: $I_{sc} \approx I_L = 3.20 \text{ A}$ (since $R_{sh} = 500 \;\Omega \gg$ diode forward resistance, shunt leakage is negligible at $V=0$) [1 mark]

- **(iii) $FF$**: 
  
  $$FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} \times I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \times I_{sc}} = \dfrac{0.50 \times 2.90}{0.737 \times 3.20} = \dfrac{1.45}{2.358} = 0.615$$

- **(iv) $\eta$**: $A = 100 \text{ cm}^2 = 0.01 \text{ m}^2$. $P_{in} = 1000 \times 0.01 = 10 \text{ W}$.
  
  $$\eta = \dfrac{V_{oc} \times I_{sc} \times FF}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{0.737 \times 3.20 \times 0.615}{10} = \dfrac{1.450}{10} = 0.145 = 14.5\%$$

---

> **[QUESTION B — Internal Choice 2] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the structure of a crystalline silicon solar cell with a neat cross-sectional diagram. Discuss the role of (i) the anti-reflection coating, (ii) the front grid fingers, and (iii) the back surface field.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat I–V characteristic curve, define and explain the significance of Open-Circuit Voltage ($V_{oc}$), Short-Circuit Current ($I_{sc}$), Maximum Power Point (MPP), Fill Factor (FF), and Conversion Efficiency ($\eta$). A 100 W PV module has $V_{oc} = 22 \text{ V}$, $I_{sc} = 6.0 \text{ A}$, and $FF = 0.75$. Calculate the conversion efficiency if the module area is $0.6 \text{ m}^2$.

**Model Solution for (a)**:
1. Cross-section: ARC | n⁺ emitter | p-base | BSF | rear contact (5 layers) — describe each [2 marks]
2. **ARC role**: Reduces reflection from ~35% to ~2% by index matching ($n_{SiN} \approx 2.0$ between air and Si) [1 mark]
3. **Front grid role**: Collects electrons with low $R_s$; trade-off between shading loss (~3–5%) and conduction loss [2 marks]
4. **BSF role**: Creates a high-low p-p⁺ junction that reflects minority carriers → reduces back-surface recombination → boosts $V_{oc}$ and $I_{sc}$ [2 marks]

**Model Solution for (b)**:
1. Draw I–V curve, label $I_{sc}$ on y-axis, $V_{oc}$ on x-axis, MPP rectangle inside [2 marks]
2. Define all five terms with one-line meanings [2 marks]
3. $P_{max} = V_{oc} \times I_{sc} \times FF = 22 \times 6.0 \times 0.75 = 99 \text{ W}$ (close to rated 100 W) [1 mark]
4. $P_{in} = 1000 \times 0.6 = 600 \text{ W}$ [1 mark]
5. $\eta = \dfrac{99}{600} = 0.165 = 16.5\%$ [1 mark]

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students LOSE marks on this topic**:
> 
> 1. **Unit Conversion Disaster**: Failing to convert $A$ from cm² to m² inflates the denominator of $\eta$ by $10^4$ and yields a nonsensical $\eta \approx 1800\%$. *Always double-check units.*
> 2. **Confusing $I_L$ with $I_{ph}$**: Many textbooks use $I_{ph}$ (photocurrent) and $I_L$ (light-generated current) interchangeably. KTU expects $I_L$. State it explicitly.
> 3. **Forgetting the Ideality Factor $n$**: Setting $n = 1$ when the data says $n = 1.3$ gives an inflated $V_{oc}$ that fails the answer-key check.
> 4. **Sign Error in KCL**: The terminal current equation is $I = I_L - I_D$, NOT $I = I_D - I_L$. Photocurrent flows OUT of the positive terminal.
> 5. **Missing Boundary Conditions in Derivations**: KTU examiners award 1–2 marks specifically for substituting $V=0$ and $I=0$ to obtain $I_{sc}$ and $V_{oc}$. Do not skip these steps.
> 6. **Not Drawing the Energy Band Diagram**: In theory questions (7 marks), 1 mark is reserved for a labelled band diagram with $E_c$, $E_v$, and $E_g$ marked.

### 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> ✅ **Rapid-Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 Module 4 — Solar PV Fundamentals)**

- [ ] **PV Effect**: Light → Electricity via semiconductor p–n junction (solid-state, no moving parts).
- [ ] **Required Condition**: $E_{photon} = h\nu \geq E_g$ for absorption.
- [ ] **Cut-off Wavelength**: $\lambda_c = \dfrac{1.24}{E_g \text{ (eV)}} \,\mu m$ (silicon: ~1.1 $\mu m$).
- [ ] **Photocurrent $I_L$** is **linear** in irradiance $G$.
- [ ] **Open-Circuit Voltage $V_{oc}$** is **logarithmic** in $G$ → $V_{oc} = \dfrac{nkT}{q} \ln(I_L/I_0)$.
- [ ] **One-Diode Equation**: $I = I_L - I_0\!\left( e^{\frac{q(V+IR_s)}{nkT}} - 1 \right) - \dfrac{V+IR_s}{R_{sh}}$.
- [ ] **Fill Factor** $FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} I_{mp}}{V_{oc} I_{sc}}$ — typical 0.6–0.85.
- [ ] **Efficiency** $\eta = \dfrac{V_{oc} I_{sc} FF}{G \cdot A} \times 100\%$.
- [ ] **Cell Layers (top to bottom)**: ARC → front grid → n⁺ emitter → p-n junction → p-base → BSF → rear contact.
- [ ] **Loss Mechanisms**: Reflection (~2–3%), transmission (<E_g photons), thermalization (>E_g photons), recombination (bulk, surface, Auger), series resistance, shunt resistance.
- [ ] **STC Reference**: $G = 1000 \text{ W/m}^2$, $T_{cell} = 25^{\circ}\text{C}$, AM1.5G spectrum.
- [ ] **EV Linkage**: Solar roofs on EVs typically produce 100–300 W peak; major boost to range for vehicles with large roof areas (Aptera, Lightyear).
- [ ] **Three Magic Numbers**: c-Si band-gap $1.12$ eV; cut-off $\lambda \approx 1.1 \,\mu m$; thermal voltage $kT/q = 25.85$ mV.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristics
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET742 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES AND RENEWABLE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Characteristics

# ⚡ Characteristics of Solar PV Systems & Wind Energy Systems

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of Electric Vehicles and Renewable Technologies, **"Characteristics"** refers to the mathematical and graphical behavior of key renewable energy conversion devices — primarily the **Solar Photovoltaic (PV) Cell/Module** and the **Wind Turbine Generator** — describing how their electrical output parameters (Voltage, Current, Power) vary as a function of operating conditions such as **irradiance (G)**, **temperature (T)**, **wind speed (v)**, and **load**.

For Solar PV systems, the two most important characteristic curves are:
- **I–V Characteristic Curve**: Current (I) versus Voltage (V)
- **P–V Characteristic Curve**: Power (P) versus Voltage (V)

For Wind Energy systems, the key characteristic is the **Power vs. Wind Speed curve** governed by the **cubic power law**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The single most examinable characteristic is the **Solar PV I-V curve**, which exhibits a **non-linear, exponential (Shockley diode equation)** behavior. Students must master the effect of **irradiance** (shifts current vertically) and **temperature** (shifts voltage horizontally) on this curve.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Solar PV Cell Analogy — "The Thirsty Camel under the Sun"** 🐪☀️
Imagine a camel (the PV cell) drinking water (current) from a well under the sun (irradiance):
- **More sunlight** → The camel can drink **more water per second** (higher current), but the size of the bucket (voltage) stays roughly the same.
- **Hotter day** → The camel gets tired quickly; the bucket shrinks (voltage drops), and the well dries up faster (efficiency drops).
- The water the camel carries (usable power) is maximum only at **one specific walking speed** (Maximum Power Point).

**Wind Turbine Analogy — "The Spinning Pinwheel in a Breeze"** 🎏🌬️
- A small breeze → pinwheel barely turns (cut-in speed)
- A strong breeze → pinwheel spins fastest, generating maximum power
- A hurricane → pinwheel is locked for safety (cut-out speed)
- Power captured depends on the **cube of wind speed** (doubling wind speed = 8× more power!)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Test Conditions (STC)** are the benchmark: **G = 1000 W/m²**, **T = 25 °C (298.15 K)**, **Air Mass (AM) = 1.5**. All manufacturer datasheets specify PV ratings under STC.

---

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Metrics

| Symbol | Parameter | Standard Value / Unit |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| **k** | Boltzmann's Constant | **1.380 × 10⁻²³ J/K** |
| **q** | Electron Charge | **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C** |
| **η** | Cell Efficiency | **15% – 22%** (commercial Si) |
| **FF** | Fill Factor | **0.7 – 0.85** |
| **STC** | Standard Test Conditions | **1000 W/m², 25 °C, AM 1.5** |
| **ρ** | Air Density | **1.225 kg/m³** (at sea level) |
| **A** | Swept Area | **m²** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Solar PV Equivalent Circuit

A practical solar cell is modeled using the **Single-Diode Model** consisting of:
1. A **photocurrent source (I_ph)** — proportional to irradiance
2. A **diode** — representing the p-n junction
3. A **series resistance (R_s)** — ohmic losses in contacts
4. A **shunt resistance (R_sh)** — leakage current losses

Applying **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)** at the output node:

$$I = I_{ph} - I_{D} - I_{sh}$$

Where:
- $I_{D} = I_{0}\left[\exp\left(\dfrac{q(V + IR_{s})}{nkT}\right) - 1\right]$ (Shockley Diode Equation)
- $I_{sh} = \dfrac{V + IR_{s}}{R_{sh}}$

### 2.2 Key Operating Points on the I-V Curve

- **Short-Circuit Current (I_sc)**: Current when V = 0; occurs at the y-intercept.
- **Open-Circuit Voltage (V_oc)**: Voltage when I = 0; occurs at the x-intercept.
- **Maximum Power Point (MPP)**: Point (V_mp, I_mp) where P = V × I is maximum.
- **Fill Factor (FF)**: Ratio of maximum rectangle area to total rectangle area.

### 2.3 Effects of Irradiance and Temperature (KTU Most-Asked!)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Trick — "CIT" Rule:**
> - **C**urrent depends on **I**rradiance (linear relationship)
> - **T**emperature affects **V**oltage (inverse relationship)

| Parameter | ↑ Irradiance (G) | ↑ Temperature (T) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **I_sc** | ↑ Increases (linearly) | ↑ Slight increase (negligible) |
| **V_oc** | ↑ Increases (logarithmically) | ↓ **Decreases significantly** (~ −2 mV/°C per cell) |
| **P_max** | ↑ Increases | ↓ Decreases |

### 2.4 Wind Turbine Power Characteristics

The mechanical power extracted from wind is governed by:

$$P_{wind} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3} \quad [\text{Watts}]$$

But the turbine can only extract a fraction limited by the **Betz Limit (C_p,max = 16/27 ≈ 0.593)**:

$$P_{turbine} = C_{p} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3}$$

Three critical wind speeds define the operating envelope:
- **v_cut-in** (~3–4 m/s): Turbine starts generating
- **v_rated** (~12–14 m/s): Rated (maximum) power output
- **v_cut-out** (~25 m/s): Turbine shuts down for safety

---

### 2.5 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | $I = I_{ph} - I_{0}\left[\exp\left(\dfrac{qV}{nkT}\right) - 1\right]$ | Ideal PV cell I-V equation | A |
| 2 | $V_{oc} = \dfrac{nkT}{q}\ln\left(\dfrac{I_{ph}}{I_{0}} + 1\right)$ | Open-circuit voltage | V |
| 3 | $P_{max} = V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}$ | Maximum power | W |
| 4 | $FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$ | Fill Factor (0 < FF < 1) | — |
| 5 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}{G \cdot A}$ | Conversion efficiency | % |
| 6 | $P_{wind} = \dfrac{1}{2}\rho A v^{3}$ | Wind power available | W |
| 7 | $P_{turbine} = C_{p} \cdot P_{wind} = \dfrac{16}{27} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2}\rho A v^{3}$ | Turbine power (Betz limit) | W |
| 8 | $\dfrac{dP}{dV}\bigg\vert_{MPP} = 0$ | MPPT condition | — |
| 9 | $V_{T} = \dfrac{kT}{q} \approx 25.85\ \text{mV}$ (at 300 K) | Thermal voltage | V |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** These characteristics are fundamental to designing **MPPT controllers** (Perturb & Observe, Incremental Conductance) used in solar EV charging stations and rooftop PV systems, and to sizing wind farm layouts in Kerala's coastal regions.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Fill Factor (FF) and Cell Efficiency

**Step 1 — Define Maximum Power Point Power**

At the MPP, the product of voltage and current is maximum. Let this product be $P_{max}$:

$$P_{max} = V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}$$

*Logic:* Power is the area of a rectangle under the I-V curve. The MPP is the largest such rectangle that fits under the curve.

**Step 2 — Define the Theoretical Maximum Rectangular Area**

The largest possible rectangle under the I-V curve has dimensions $V_{oc}$ and $I_{sc}$:

$$P_{theoretical} = V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}$$

*Logic:* This is the rectangle that spans from origin to the x- and y-intercepts.

**Step 3 — Form the Fill Factor Ratio**

The **Fill Factor** is defined as the ratio of these two areas:

$$FF = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{theoretical}} = \dfrac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$$

**Step 4 — Derive Conversion Efficiency**

The conversion efficiency $\eta$ is the ratio of useful electrical output to incident solar input:

$$\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in}}$$

The input power is the irradiance $G$ falling on cell area $A$:

$$P_{in} = G \cdot A$$

Substituting $P_{max} = FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}$:

$$\eta = \dfrac{FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}{G \cdot A}$$

**Step 5 — Empirical Approximation for FF**

A widely used empirical relation (Green, 1981) for an ideal cell:

$$FF_{0} = \dfrac{v_{oc} - \ln(v_{oc} + 0.72)}{v_{oc} + 1}$$

where $v_{oc} = \dfrac{qV_{oc}}{nkT}$ is the normalized open-circuit voltage.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Open-Circuit Voltage (V_oc)

**Step 1 — Apply the condition I = 0 to the ideal PV equation**

At open circuit, the terminal current is zero, so:

$$0 = I_{ph} - I_{0}\left[\exp\left(\dfrac{qV_{oc}}{nkT}\right) - 1\right]$$

**Step 2 — Isolate the exponential term**

Rearranging:

$$I_{ph} = I_{0}\left[\exp\left(\dfrac{qV_{oc}}{nkT}\right) - 1\right]$$

**Step 3 — Use the approximation $I_{ph} \gg I_{0}$**

Since photocurrent is typically 1000× larger than reverse saturation current:

$$\dfrac{I_{ph}}{I_{0}} \approx \exp\left(\dfrac{qV_{oc}}{nkT}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Apply natural logarithm to both sides**

$$\ln\left(\dfrac{I_{ph}}{I_{0}}\right) = \dfrac{qV_{oc}}{nkT}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for V_oc**

$$\boxed{V_{oc} = \dfrac{nkT}{q}\ln\left(\dfrac{I_{ph}}{I_{0}} + 1\right)}$$

*Logic:* This shows $V_{oc}$ depends **logarithmically** on irradiance (via $I_{ph}$) and **linearly** on temperature.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Betz Limit (Maximum Wind Power Coefficient)

**Step 1 — Consider an ideal wind turbine disc**

Let $v_1$ = upstream wind speed, $v_2$ = downstream wind speed, $v$ = speed at the disc.

**Step 2 — Apply continuity of mass flow**

Mass flow rate is conserved:

$$\dot{m} = \rho A v = \rho A_{1} v_{1} = \rho A_{2} v_{2}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Euler's turbine equation**

Thrust (force) on the disc:

$$F = \dot{m}(v_1 - v_2) = \rho A v (v_1 - v_2)$$

**Step 4 — Express power extracted**

$$P = F \cdot v = \rho A v^{2}(v_1 - v_2)$$

Using actuator disc theory, the disc velocity is the average:

$$v = \dfrac{v_1 + v_2}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and differentiate to find optimum**

Substituting:

$$P = \rho A \left(\dfrac{v_1 + v_2}{2}\right)^{2}(v_1 - v_2)$$

To maximize P, set $\dfrac{dP}{dv_2} = 0$, which gives:

$$v_2 = \dfrac{v_1}{3}$$

**Step 6 — Calculate maximum power coefficient**

Substituting $v_2 = v_1/3$ back:

$$C_{p,max} = \dfrac{P_{max}}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A v_1^{3}} = \dfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926$$

$$\boxed{C_{p,Betz} = \dfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.593}$$

*Logic:* **No turbine can extract more than 59.3% of kinetic wind energy** — this is the theoretical maximum.

---

### 3.4 📊 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A solar cell has $I_{sc} = 3.5\ \text{A}$, $V_{oc} = 0.62\ \text{V}$, $V_{mp} = 0.51\ \text{V}$, $I_{mp} = 3.2\ \text{A}$, under $G = 1000\ \text{W/m}^2$ on an area $A = 100\ \text{cm}^2 = 0.01\ \text{m}^2$. Calculate FF and $\eta$.

**Solution:**

$$FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}} = \dfrac{0.51 \times 3.2}{0.62 \times 3.5} = \dfrac{1.632}{2.17} \approx 0.752$$

$$P_{in} = G \cdot A = 1000 \times 0.01 = 10\ \text{W}$$

$$\eta = \dfrac{FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{0.752 \times 2.17}{10} = \dfrac{1.632}{10} = 0.1632$$

$$\boxed{\eta \approx 16.32\%}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Solar PV Equivalent Circuit (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Sun(["Solar Irradiance G"]):::input
    Sun -->|Photon Energy| PVnode["Photocurrent Source Iph"]
    PVnode --> Rseries["Series Resistance Rs"]
    Rseries --> Diode["PN Junction Diode"]
    Diode --> Shunt["Shunt Resistance Rsh"]
    Shunt --> OutTerm(["Output Terminals V and I"])
    PVnode --> OutTerm
    Diode --> OutTerm
    classDef input fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.2 I-V and P-V Characteristic Curves — Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Voltage V on X-axis"]:::axis
    B["Current I on Y-axis"]:::axis
    C["I-V Curve: Exponential Decay Shape"]:::curve
    D["P-V Curve: Inverted Bell Shape"]:::curve
    E["Key Point: Isc at V=0"]:::point
    F["Key Point: Voc at I=0"]:::point
    G["Peak Point: MPP at Vmp Imp"]:::point
    H["Region 1: Current Source Region"]:::region
    I["Region 2: Voltage Source Region"]:::region
    A --> C
    B --> C
    C --> E
    C --> F
    C --> G
    C --> H
    C --> D
    classDef axis fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:2px
    classDef curve fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px
    classDef point fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#880E4F,stroke-width:2px
    classDef region fill:#DCEDC8,stroke:#33691E,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Effect of Irradiance on I-V Curve (Visual Logic)

```mermaid
graph TD
    HighG["High Irradiance: 1000 W/m2"]:::high
    MedG["Medium Irradiance: 500 W/m2"]:::med
    LowG["Low Irradiance: 250 W/m2"]:::low
    HighG -->|Higher Isc| HighIV["I-V Curve Shifts UP"]
    MedG -->|Medium Isc| MedIV["I-V Curve Mid"]
    LowG -->|Lower Isc| LowIV["I-V Curve Shifts DOWN"]
    HighIV --> VocStable["Voc nearly CONSTANT"]
    MedIV --> VocStable
    LowIV --> VocStable
    classDef high fill:#FF6F00,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef med fill:#FFA726,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef low fill:#FFE082,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.4 Effect of Temperature on I-V Curve (Visual Logic)

```mermaid
graph TD
    LowT["Low Temperature: 0 deg C"]:::cold
    NormT["Normal Temperature: 25 deg C"]:::norm
    HighT["High Temperature: 75 deg C"]:::hot
    LowT -->|Higher Voc| ColdIV["I-V Curve Shifts RIGHT"]
    NormT -->|Baseline Voc| NormIV["I-V Curve Center"]
    HighT -->|Lower Voc| HotIV["I-V Curve Shifts LEFT"]
    ColdIV --> IscStable["Isc nearly CONSTANT"]
    NormIV --> IscStable
    HotIV --> IscStable
    classDef cold fill:#42A5F5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef norm fill:#66BB6A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef hot fill:#EF5350,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

### 4.5 Wind Turbine Power Curve vs Wind Speed

```mermaid
graph LR
    V1["Cut-in Speed: 3-4 m/s"]:::stage
    V2["Operating Region: 4-12 m/s"]:::stage
    V3["Rated Power Region: 12-14 m/s"]:::stage
    V4["Cut-out Speed: 25 m/s"]:::stage
    V1 --> V2
    V2 --> V3
    V3 --> V4
    V2 -->|Cubic Power P=0.5 rho A v3| Cubic["P proportional to v CUBED"]
    V3 -->|Constant Rated Power| Rated["P = Prated CONSTANT"]
    V4 -->|Shut Down for Safety| Safe["System OFF"]
    classDef stage fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#01579B,stroke-width:2px
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** 
**Define Fill Factor (FF) of a solar cell. What is its typical range for commercial silicon cells?**

**Model Answer:**

Fill Factor is the ratio of the **maximum obtainable power** from the solar cell to the product of its **open-circuit voltage (V_oc)** and **short-circuit current (I_sc)**:

$$FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$$

It is a measure of the **"squareness"** of the I-V curve. For commercial crystalline silicon cells, FF typically ranges from **0.70 to 0.85**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** A higher FF means a more ideal diode behavior (lower R_s and higher R_sh).

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** 
**State the Betz Limit. What is its significance in wind energy conversion?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Betz Limit** states that **no wind turbine can extract more than 59.3% (16/27) of the kinetic energy** from wind. It is the theoretical maximum value of the **power coefficient (C_p)**.

**Significance:**
1. It defines the **upper efficiency bound** for any wind turbine design.
2. It guides **blade design** to approach (but never exceed) this optimum.
3. Real turbines achieve **C_p = 0.35 – 0.45** due to mechanical and aerodynamic losses.

$$\boxed{C_{p,max} = \dfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.593}$$

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** With a neat I-V and P-V characteristic curve, explain the operation of a solar PV cell. Define Isc, Voc, Vmp, Imp, and MPP. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain how the I-V characteristics of a solar cell vary with (i) change in irradiance and (ii) change in temperature. **(7 marks)**

##### **Solution (a):**

The **I-V characteristic** of a solar cell describes the relationship between output current (I) and terminal voltage (V) under constant irradiance and temperature. It is a **non-linear curve** with the following key points:

| Point | Definition | Location on Curve |
|:---:|:---|:---:|
| **I_sc** | Short-circuit current (V=0) | Y-axis intercept |
| **V_oc** | Open-circuit voltage (I=0) | X-axis intercept |
| **V_mp** | Voltage at Maximum Power Point | Knee region |
| **I_mp** | Current at Maximum Power Point | Knee region |
| **MPP** | Point of max P = V·I | Where dP/dV = 0 |

The **P-V curve** has an **inverted-bell (parabolic) shape**, with the peak at MPP. To the left of MPP is the **current source region**; to the right is the **voltage source region**.

The governing equation (ideal single-diode model):

$$I = I_{ph} - I_{0}\left[\exp\left(\dfrac{qV}{nkT}\right) - 1\right]$$

##### **Solution (b):**

**(i) Effect of Irradiance (G):**
- **I_sc increases linearly** with G (since $I_{ph} \propto G$).
- **V_oc increases logarithmically** with G: $V_{oc} \propto \ln(G)$.
- The entire I-V curve **shifts upward** proportionally.
- **P_max increases significantly** with G.

**(ii) Effect of Temperature (T):**
- **I_sc increases very slightly** (~+0.05%/°C).
- **V_oc decreases significantly** (~−2 mV/°C per cell).
- The I-V curve **shifts leftward** (voltage axis).
- **P_max decreases** as temperature rises — this is why PV panels in Kerala's hot climate need **proper ventilation**.

| Parameter | ↑ G (Irradiance) | ↑ T (Temperature) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| I_sc | ↑↑ Linear | ↑ Negligible |
| V_oc | ↑ Logarithmic | ↓↓ Significant |
| P_max | ↑↑ Strong | ↓ Moderate |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - ❌ Common Mistake: Writing "current depends on temperature" — **WRONG.** Current depends on **irradiance**.
> - ❌ Common Mistake: Saying temperature affects I_sc significantly — it does **NOT** (only ~0.05%/°C).
> - ❌ Drawing P-V curve as symmetric parabola — it is **asymmetric** (skewed toward V_oc).
> - ✅ Always state: **"V_oc decreases linearly with T, while I_sc increases linearly with G."**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Derive the expression for maximum power extracted by a wind turbine and explain the Betz Limit. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A solar cell has $V_{oc} = 0.65\ \text{V}$, $I_{sc} = 3.2\ \text{A}$, $V_{mp} = 0.53\ \text{V}$, $I_{mp} = 2.95\ \text{A}$, and area $A = 100\ \text{cm}^2$. Operating at $G = 1000\ \text{W/m}^2$, calculate: (i) Fill Factor (ii) Maximum Power (iii) Conversion Efficiency. **(7 marks)**

##### **Solution (a):**

**Given:** Wind with upstream speed $v_1$ passes through a turbine disc of area A, exiting at $v_2$.

**Step 1:** Mass flow rate:
$$\dot{m} = \rho A v$$

**Step 2:** Thrust force on disc (Euler's equation):
$$F = \dot{m}(v_1 - v_2) = \rho A v(v_1 - v_2)$$

**Step 3:** Velocity at disc (average):
$$v = \dfrac{v_1 + v_2}{2}$$

**Step 4:** Power extracted:
$$P = F \cdot v = \rho A \left(\dfrac{v_1 + v_2}{2}\right)^2 (v_1 - v_2)$$

**Step 5:** Maximize P w.r.t. $v_2$: $\dfrac{dP}{dv_2} = 0 \Rightarrow v_2 = \dfrac{v_1}{3}$

**Step 6:** Substitute and divide by $\frac{1}{2}\rho A v_1^3$:

$$C_{p,max} = \dfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$$

**Betz Limit Significance:**
- Maximum theoretical efficiency of any wind turbine is **59.3%**.
- Real turbines achieve 35–45% due to friction, turbulence, and blade design losses.

##### **Solution (b):**

**(i) Fill Factor:**

$$FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}} = \dfrac{0.53 \times 2.95}{0.65 \times 3.2} = \dfrac{1.5635}{2.08} \approx 0.7517$$

$$\boxed{FF \approx 0.752}$$

**(ii) Maximum Power:**

$$P_{max} = V_{mp} \times I_{mp} = 0.53 \times 2.95 = 1.5635\ \text{W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{max} \approx 1.56\ \text{W}}$$

**(iii) Conversion Efficiency:**

Area $A = 100\ \text{cm}^2 = 100 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^2 = 0.01\ \text{m}^2$

Input power:
$$P_{in} = G \cdot A = 1000 \times 0.01 = 10\ \text{W}$$

Efficiency:
$$\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{1.5635}{10} = 0.15635$$

$$\boxed{\eta \approx 15.64\%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls in Numerical Problems:**
> - ❌ Forgetting to convert cm² → m² (factor of 10⁻⁴)
> - ❌ Using V_oc × I_sc as P_max instead of V_mp × I_mp
> - ❌ Mixing up units (mW vs W)
> - ✅ Always show formula → substitution → final answer with unit

---

### 5.3 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Solar PV & Wind Characteristics**

- ⚡ **Solar Cell Equation:** $I = I_{ph} - I_0\left[\exp(qV/nkT) - 1\right]$
- ⚡ **Fill Factor:** $FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} I_{mp}}{V_{oc} I_{sc}}$ (typical: 0.7–0.85)
- ⚡ **Efficiency:** $\eta = \dfrac{FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}{G \cdot A}$
- ⚡ **V_oc formula:** $V_{oc} = \dfrac{nkT}{q}\ln\left(\dfrac{I_{ph}}{I_0} + 1\right)$
- ⚡ **Irradiance ↑** → I_sc ↑ (linear), V_oc ↑ (log), P_max ↑
