- ⚡ **Temperature ↑** → V_oc ↓ (~−2 mV/°C/cell), P_max ↓
- ⚡ **STC:** 1000 W/m², 25 °C, AM 1.5
- ⚡ **Wind Power:** $P = \dfrac{1}{2}\rho A v^3$
- ⚡ **Betz Limit:** $C_{p,max} = \dfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$ (59.3%)
- ⚡ **Cut-in (3–4 m/s) → Rated (12–14 m/s) → Cut-out (25 m/s)**
- ⚡ **MPP Condition:** $\dfrac{dP}{dV} = 0$
- ⚡ **Thermal Voltage:** $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q} \approx 25.85\ \text{mV}$ at 300 K

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Memory Trick — "CIP TTV":**
> - **C**urrent ↑ with **I**rradiance
> - **V**oltage ↑ with **I**rradiance
> - **V**oltage ↓ with **T**emperature
> - **P**ower **C**ubed with wind speed

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cell Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET742 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES AND RENEWABLE TECHNOLOGIES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Cell Efficiency ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Cell Efficiency** is a dimensionless performance metric that quantifies how effectively an electrochemical, photovoltaic, or fuel cell converts the input energy (chemical, electrical, or radiant) into useful output energy (electrical or vice-versa), while accounting for all thermodynamic, kinetic, and resistive irreversibilities inherent to the conversion process. In the context of **KTU PEAET742 (Electric Vehicles and Renewable Technologies)**, Cell Efficiency is broadly classified under three terminal technologies: | Cell Type | Energy Conversion | Primary Application in EVs / Renewable Tech | |---|---|---| | **Battery Cell** | Chemical $\leftrightarrow$ Electrical | EV traction packs, BESS | | **Fuel Cell** | Chemical $\rightarrow$ Electrical + Heat | FCEV (Hydrogen vehicles), stationary power | | **Photovoltaic Cell** | Radiant (Solar) $\rightarrow$ Electrical | Solar charging of EVs, renewable integration | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 4, Cell Efficiency is treated as the bridge concept between raw energy stored/generated and *usable* energy delivered to the EV traction motor or the grid. Mastering the three sub-efficiencies (**Coulombic**, **Voltage**, and **Energy/Round-trip**) is mandatory for Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Water Tank with a Leaky Pipe"** > > Imagine you pour **100 litres of water** into an overhead tank, but only **85 litres** reach your kitchen tap downstairs. The remaining 15 litres are lost to: > - **Pipe friction** $\rightarrow$ analogous to **ohmic (IR) losses** causing *voltage drop*. > - **Evaporation from joints** $\rightarrow$ analogous to **parasitic side reactions** causing *capacity loss (coulombic loss)*. > - **Spillage during pumping** $\rightarrow$ analogous to **heat dissipation** in the cell. > > The **85 %** that reaches you is the **Cell Efficiency**. No energy conversion is perfectly loss-free; efficiency simply measures how *small* those inevitable losses can be engineered to be. ### 1.3 The Three Pillars of Cell Efficiency For any rechargeable (secondary) cell used in an EV battery pack: $$ \eta_{\text{Cell}} = \underbrace{\eta_{\text{Coulombic}}}_{\text{Charge passing}} \times \underbrace{\eta_{\text{Voltage}}}_{\text{Potential level}} \times \underbrace{\eta_{\text{Thermal/coupling}}}_{\text{Heat & side rxns}} $$ > [!TIP] > **Standard Benchmark Numbers (KTU-referenced industry values):** > - **Lithium-ion (NMC) Cell:** $\eta_{\text{RT}} \approx 85\text{–}95\,\%$ > - **Lead-acid Cell:** $\eta_{\text{RT}} \approx 70\text{–}80\,\%$ > - **PEM Fuel Cell (H$_2$):** $\eta_{\text{electrical}} \approx 40\text{–}60\,\%$ > - **Crystalline-Si Solar Cell:** $\eta \approx 17\text{–}22\,\%$ > - **Carnot Theoretical Upper Bound:** $\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{\text{cold}}}{T_{\text{hot}}}$ ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Coulombic (Faradaic) Efficiency — $\eta_C$ **Definition:** Ratio of the total charge extracted during discharge to the total charge supplied during charge for one complete cycle. $$ \eta_C = \frac{Q_{\text{discharge}}}{Q_{\text{charge}}} \times 100\,\% $$ **Why it is less than 100 %:** - **Side reactions:** Electrolyte decomposition, SEI growth, lithium plating. - **Self-discharge:** Parasitic internal redox reactions. - **Incomplete re-intercalation:** Some Li$^+$ ions become "trapped" in anode. **How it is measured:** Coulomb Counting (CC) method or integrating the current sensor output over a full cycle. ### 2.2 Voltage Efficiency — $\eta_V$ **Definition:** Ratio of the average discharge voltage to the average charge voltage. $$ \eta_V = \frac{V_{\text{discharge, avg}}}{V_{\text{charge, avg}}} \times 100\,\% $$ **Why it is less than 100 %:** - **Ohmic drop:** $V = IR$ loss across electrolyte, electrodes, current collectors. - **Charge-transfer overpotential:** Activation barrier at electrode-electrolyte interface. - **Concentration overpotential:** Mass-transport limitations at high C-rates. - **Internal resistance (impedance) heating** — the second-largest loss after Coulombic losses. ### 2.3 Energy (Round-Trip) Efficiency — $\eta_E$ or $\eta_{RT}$ **Definition:** Ratio of total energy delivered during discharge to total energy supplied during charge. $$ \eta_{RT} = \frac{E_{\text{discharge}}}{E_{\text{charge}}} = \eta_C \times \eta_V $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Relationship:** > Energy Efficiency is the **product** of Coulombic and Voltage efficiencies because *Energy = Voltage × Charge*. This is the most widely used metric in EV applications and the one KTU examiners emphasize. ### 2.4 Fuel Cell Efficiency For a hydrogen fuel cell, the **theoretical (thermodynamic) efficiency** is governed by Gibbs free energy vs. enthalpy: $$ \eta_{\text{theor}} = \frac{\Delta G^\circ}{\Delta H^\circ} \approx 0.83 \quad (\text{for H}_2/\text{O}_2 \text{ at } 25\,^\circ\text{C}) $$ The **practical (voltage) efficiency** is the ratio of operating cell voltage to the reversible thermodynamic voltage: $$ \eta_{FC} = \frac{V_{\text{cell}}}{E^\circ_{\text{rev}}} $$ For **PEM Fuel Cell:** $E^\circ_{\text{rev}} = 1.229$ V at 25 °C; typical operating voltage $\approx 0.6\text{–}0.7$ V, giving $\eta_{FC} \approx 49\text{–}57\,\%$. ### 2.5 Solar (Photovoltaic) Cell Efficiency $$ \eta_{PV} = \frac{P_{\text{max}}}{P_{\text{in}}} = \frac{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF}{P_{\text{in}}} $$ Where the **Fill Factor (FF)** is defined as: $$ FF = \frac{V_{\text{mpp}} \cdot I_{\text{mpp}}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}} $$ ### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\eta_C = Q_{\text{dis}}/Q_{\text{chg}}$ | Coulombic (Faradaic) Efficiency | Unitless, $\times 100$ for % | | 2 | $\eta_V = V_{\text{dis,avg}}/V_{\text{chg,avg}}$ | Voltage Efficiency | Unitless | | 3 | $\eta_{RT} = \eta_C \cdot \eta_V$ | Round-trip Energy Efficiency | Unitless | | 4 | $E_{\text{dis}} = \int V_{\text{dis}}\,I_{\text{dis}}\,dt$ | Total Discharge Energy | Wh | | 5 | $\eta_{FC,\text{theor}} = \Delta G^\circ / \Delta H^\circ$ | Thermodynamic Efficiency of Fuel Cell | $\approx 0.83$ for H$_2$/O$_2$ | | 6 | $\eta_{FC,\text{pract}} = V_{\text{cell}} / E^\circ_{\text{rev}}$ | Voltage Efficiency of Fuel Cell | $E^\circ_{\text{rev}} = 1.229$ V (PEM) | | 7 | $\eta_{PV} = P_{\text{max}}/P_{\text{in}}$ | Solar Cell Efficiency | Typically 15–22 % | | 8 | $FF = (V_{\text{mpp}} I_{\text{mpp}})/(V_{oc} I_{sc})$ | Fill Factor | Always $< 1$ | | 9 | $\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - T_c/T_h$ | Carnot (Thermodynamic) Limit | Theoretical upper bound | | 10 | $V_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ - \eta_{\text{act}} - \eta_{\text{conc}} - IR$ | Cell Voltage with all losses | Polarisation equation | ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Battery Management Systems (BMS):** Real-time $\eta_{RT}$ calculation for State-of-Health (SOH) estimation in EV battery packs. - **EV Range Estimation:** A 5 % drop in $\eta_{RT}$ can reduce vehicle range by 8–12 km per full charge. - **Fuel Cell Stack Sizing:** Determines number of cells required to meet EV traction power requirements. - **Solar EV Charging Stations:** PV efficiency directly dictates panel area needed per kW of charging power. - **Grid-scale BESS:** Round-trip efficiency determines economic payback period of storage installations. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Round-Trip Energy Efficiency from Coulombic and Voltage Efficiencies **Starting Point:** Energy delivered and supplied are integrals of power over time. $$ E_{\text{discharge}} = \int_{0}^{t_{\text{dis}}} V_{\text{dis}}(t) \cdot I_{\text{dis}}(t) \, dt $$ $$ E_{\text{charge}} = \int_{0}^{t_{\text{chg}}} V_{\text{chg}}(t) \cdot I_{\text{chg}}(t) \, dt $$ **Step 1:** Express energy as the product of average voltage and total charge, since $Q = \int I\,dt$. $$ E_{\text{discharge}} = V_{\text{dis, avg}} \cdot Q_{\text{dis}} $$ $$ E_{\text{charge}} = V_{\text{chg, avg}} \cdot Q_{\text{chg}} $$ **Step 2:** Form the round-trip efficiency ratio. $$ \eta_{RT} = \frac{E_{\text{discharge}}}{E_{\text{charge}}} = \frac{V_{\text{dis, avg}} \cdot Q_{\text{dis}}}{V_{\text{chg, avg}} \cdot Q_{\text{chg}}} $$ **Step 3:** Rearrange into the product of two independent ratios. $$ \eta_{RT} = \left(\frac{Q_{\text{dis}}}{Q_{\text{chg}}}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{V_{\text{dis, avg}}}{V_{\text{chg, avg}}}\right) $$ **Step 4:** Recognise the two bracketed terms. $$ \frac{Q_{\text{dis}}}{Q_{\text{chg}}} = \eta_C \qquad \text{and} \qquad \frac{V_{\text{dis, avg}}}{V_{\text{chg, avg}}} = \eta_V $$ **Final Result:** $$ \boxed{\;\eta_{RT} = \eta_C \cdot \eta_V\;} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Logic:** Because *Energy = Voltage × Charge*, and these two factors are *multiplicatively* independent (different physical loss mechanisms), the total efficiency is the *product* — never the *sum* — of the two sub-efficiencies. This is a frequently tested 3-mark KTU Part A question. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Solar Cell Efficiency from I–V Characteristics **Step 1:** The incident solar power on a cell of area $A$ under irradiance $G$ (W/m²) is: $$ P_{\text{in}} = G \cdot A $$ **Step 2:** The maximum electrical power extracted from the cell occurs at the **Maximum Power Point (MPP)**: $$ P_{\text{max}} = V_{\text{mpp}} \cdot I_{\text{mpp}} $$ **Step 3:** Define the Fill Factor (FF) as the "squareness" of the I–V curve: $$ FF = \frac{P_{\text{max}}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}} = \frac{V_{\text{mpp}} \cdot I_{\text{mpp}}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}} $$ **Step 4:** Substitute $P_{\text{max}} = FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}$ into the efficiency definition. $$ \eta_{PV} = \frac{P_{\text{max}}}{P_{\text{in}}} = \frac{FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}{G \cdot A} $$ **Step 5:** Rearrange for clarity. $$ \boxed{\;\eta_{PV} = \frac{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF}{G \cdot A}\;} $$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Practical Fuel Cell Voltage Efficiency **Step 1:** The reversible (thermodynamic, no-loss) cell voltage from Nernst equation at 25 °C: $$ E^\circ_{\text{rev}} = -\frac{\Delta G^\circ}{nF} = 1.229 \;\text{V} \quad (\text{PEM, H}_2\text{/O}_2) $$ **Step 2:** The actual operating cell voltage (after all irreversibilities) is: $$ V_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{rev}} - \eta_{\text{act}} - \eta_{\text{conc}} - I \cdot R_{\text{ohmic}} $$ Where: - $\eta_{\text{act}}$ = activation overpotential (kinetic barrier at electrode) - $\eta_{\text{conc}}$ = concentration overpotential (mass-transport loss) - $IR_{\text{ohmic}}$ = ohmic drop (electrolyte + electrode resistance) **Step 3:** Define practical fuel cell efficiency as ratio of actual to reversible voltage. $$ \eta_{FC} = \frac{V_{\text{cell}}}{E^\circ_{\text{rev}}} = 1 - \frac{\eta_{\text{act}} + \eta_{\text{conc}} + I R_{\text{ohmic}}}{E^\circ_{\text{rev}}} $$ **Step 4:** Plug in typical PEM operating values: $V_{\text{cell}} = 0.7$ V, $E^\circ_{\text{rev}} = 1.229$ V. $$ \eta_{FC} = \frac{0.7}{1.229} \approx 0.569 = 56.9\,\% $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters for FCEVs:** Even a *perfect* PEM fuel cell cannot exceed **83 %** ($\Delta G^\circ / \Delta H^\circ$) because some energy inevitably escapes as heat — this is a **thermodynamic law**, not an engineering defect. --- ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Part B Style) > **Problem:** A lithium-ion EV battery is charged at a constant voltage of 4.2 V supplying a charge of 50 Ah. During discharge at constant 3.7 V average, it delivers 48 Ah. Calculate: (a) Coulombic Efficiency, (b) Voltage Efficiency, (c) Round-trip Energy Efficiency. **(a) Coulombic Efficiency:** $$ \eta_C = \frac{Q_{\text{dis}}}{Q_{\text{chg}}} = \frac{48}{50} = 0.96 = 96\,\% $$ **(b) Voltage Efficiency:** $$ \eta_V = \frac{V_{\text{dis}}}{V_{\text{chg}}} = \frac{3.7}{4.2} \approx 0.881 = 88.1\,\% $$ **(c) Round-trip Energy Efficiency:** $$ \eta_{RT} = \eta_C \cdot \eta_V = 0.96 \times 0.881 \approx 0.846 = 84.6\,\% $$ > [!TIP] > **Cross-check via direct energy method:** $E_{\text{dis}} = 3.7 \times 48 = 177.6$ Wh; $E_{\text{chg}} = 4.2 \times 50 = 210$ Wh; $\eta_{RT} = 177.6/210 = 0.8457 \approx 84.6\,\%$. ✓ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Taxonomy of Cell Efficiency ```mermaid flowchart TD A["CELL EFFICIENCY MASTER NODE"]:::root A --> B["ELECTROCHEMICAL CELL
(Battery / Fuel Cell)"]:::branch1 A --> C["PHOTOVOLTAIC CELL
(Solar Cell)"]:::branch2 B --> D["COULOMBIC EFFICIENCY nC
Qdis / Qchg"]:::node1 B --> E["VOLTAGE EFFICIENCY nV
Vdis avg / Vchg avg"]:::node2 B --> F["ROUND TRIP EFFICIENCY nRT
nC x nV"]:::node3 B --> G["FUEL CELL VOLTAGE EFF
Vcell / Erev"]:::node4 C --> H["FILL FACTOR FF
Vmpp x Impp / Voc x Isc"]:::node5 C --> I["PV EFFICIENCY nPV
Pmax / Pin"]:::node6 D --> J["LOSSES: Side Reactions
SEI Growth, Self Discharge"]:::loss E --> K["LOSSES: Ohmic Drop
Activation Overpotential"]:::loss F --> L["LOSSES: Heat Dissipation
Thermal Coupling"]:::loss classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff classDef branch1 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef branch2 fill:#c55a11,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef node1 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef node2 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef node3 fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef node4 fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef node5 fill:#f4b183,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef node6 fill:#f4b183,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef loss fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Cell Efficiency Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR IN1["INPUT ENERGY
Chemical / Radiant / Electrical"]:::input --> CT["CONVERSION CORE
Electrochemical Cell
or Photovoltaic Junction"]:::core CT --> OUT1["USEFUL OUTPUT
Electrical Energy"]:::output CT --> LOSS1["LOSS CHANNEL A
Ohmic Heating I2R"]:::lossblk CT --> LOSS2["LOSS CHANNEL B
Activation + Concentration Polarisation"]:::lossblk CT --> LOSS3["LOSS CHANNEL C
Side Reactions / Recombination"]:::lossblk LOSS1 --> HEAT["HEAT SINK
Entropy Increase"]:::thermal LOSS2 --> HEAT LOSS3 --> HEAT OUT1 --> SENSOR["BMS / MPPT CONTROLLER
Efficiency Calculation"]:::monitor SENSOR --> REPORT["EFFICIENCY REPORT
nC, nV, nRT, nPV"]:::report classDef input fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef core fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef output fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef lossblk fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef thermal fill:#7f7f7f,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef monitor fill:#bf9000,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef report fill:#385723,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` ### 4.3 Interpretation Guide for the Diagrams - The **left (blue)** flow represents the *desired* energy pathway. - The **red loss channels** highlight *why* cell efficiency is never 100 %. - The **monitor block** reflects how EV BMS / solar MPPT controllers compute efficiency in real-time. - The **right (green) report** is the metric delivered to the user — this is what the KTU examiner expects you to identify and compute. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define Coulombic Efficiency. Mention any two factors that reduce it in a Lithium-ion cell.** **Model Answer:** Coulombic Efficiency is the ratio of charge extracted during discharge to the charge supplied during charge. $$ \eta_C = \frac{Q_{\text{discharge}}}{Q_{\text{charge}}} \times 100\,\% $$ **Two factors reducing $\eta_C$:** 1. **Solid Electrolyte Interphase (SEI) growth** — consumes Li-ions irreversibly during each cycle. 2. **Lithium plating** — metallic Li deposits on anode instead of intercalating, permanently losing active lithium. *(Other acceptable answers: electrolyte decomposition, self-discharge, transition-metal dissolution.)* --- #### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **What is Fill Factor (FF) of a solar cell? Why does it directly affect cell efficiency?** **Model Answer:** Fill Factor is defined as the ratio of the maximum power obtainable from the solar cell to the product of open-circuit voltage and short-circuit current: $$ FF = \frac{V_{\text{mpp}} \cdot I_{\text{mpp}}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}} $$ **Why it affects efficiency:** Since $\eta_{PV} = (V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF) / P_{\text{in}}$, a higher FF means a more "square" I–V curve and greater power extraction at the MPP. Typical FF values are 0.70–0.85 for good crystalline-Si cells. --- ### Part B Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024] #### (a) Derive the expression for Round-Trip Energy Efficiency of a rechargeable battery cell in terms of Coulombic and Voltage efficiencies. *(7 marks)* **Step-by-step model solution:** **Step 1:** State the energy integrals. $$ E_{\text{chg}} = \int V_{\text{chg}}(t) \cdot I_{\text{chg}}(t)\,dt $$ **Step 2:** Introduce average voltage and total charge definitions. $$ E_{\text{chg}} = V_{\text{chg, avg}} \cdot Q_{\text{chg}} \qquad \text{and similarly} \qquad E_{\text{dis}} = V_{\text{dis, avg}} \cdot Q_{\text{dis}} $$ **Step 3:** Form the efficiency ratio. $$ \eta_{RT} = \frac{E_{\text{dis}}}{E_{\text{chg}}} = \frac{V_{\text{dis, avg}} \cdot Q_{\text{dis}}}{V_{\text{chg, avg}} \cdot Q_{\text{chg}}} $$ **Step 4:** Split into the two component ratios. $$ \eta_{RT} = \frac{Q_{\text{dis}}}{Q_{\text{chg}}} \cdot \frac{V_{\text{dis, avg}}}{V_{\text{chg, avg}}} = \eta_C \cdot \eta_V $$ **Final boxed result:** $$ \boxed{\;\eta_{RT} = \eta_C \cdot \eta_V\;} $$ --- #### (b) A Li-ion cell is charged at an average voltage of 4.1 V with a charge input of 60 Ah. On discharge, it delivers 57 Ah at an average voltage of 3.65 V. Compute Coulombic, Voltage and Round-trip efficiencies. Comment on the dominant loss mechanism. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **(i) Coulombic Efficiency:** $$ \eta_C = \frac{Q_{\text{dis}}}{Q_{\text{chg}}} = \frac{57}{60} = 0.950 = 95.0\,\% $$ **(ii) Voltage Efficiency:** $$ \eta_V = \frac{V_{\text{dis, avg}}}{V_{\text{chg, avg}}} = \frac{3.65}{4.10} \approx 0.8902 = 89.02\,\% $$ **(iii) Round-trip Efficiency:** $$ \eta_{RT} = \eta_C \cdot \eta_V = 0.950 \times 0.8902 \approx 0.8457 = 84.57\,\% $$ **Cross-check via direct energy method:** $$ E_{\text{chg}} = 4.10 \times 60 = 246\,\text{Wh} $$ $$ E_{\text{dis}} = 3.65 \times 57 = 208.05\,\text{Wh} $$ $$ \eta_{RT,\text{direct}} = \frac{208.05}{246} = 0.8457 \quad \checkmark $$ **Comment on dominant loss:** - Voltage loss fraction $= 1 - 0.8902 = 10.98\,\%$ - Coulombic loss fraction $= 1 - 0.950 = 5.0\,\%$ The **voltage efficiency loss** dominates. This indicates high **ohmic + polarisation losses** in the cell — likely due to elevated internal resistance, poor electrolyte conductivity, or high C-rate operation. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > - Do **not** forget the cross-check step; many students lose 1–2 marks here. > - Mention both *magnitude* and *physical cause* of the dominant loss — vague statements like "more loss occurs" earn zero credit. > - Always quote efficiency to **two decimal places** (e.g., 84.57 %), not "≈ 85 %". --- ### Part B Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023] #### (a) With a neat diagram, explain the different loss mechanisms that limit the practical efficiency of a PEM fuel cell. Derive the expression for practical fuel cell efficiency. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **Loss Mechanisms (Polarisation Losses):** 1. **Activation overpotential ($\eta_{\text{act}}$):** Slow kinetics of the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) at the cathode — typically the largest loss. 2. **Ohmic overpotential ($IR$):** Resistance to proton flow through the polymer membrane and electron flow through electrodes/current collectors. 3. **Concentration overpotential ($\eta_{\text{conc}}$):** Mass-transport limitation of reactant gases to electrode surfaces, especially at high current densities. **Total Cell Voltage Equation:** $$ V_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{rev}} - \eta_{\text{act}} - \eta_{\text{conc}} - I R_{\text{ohmic}} $$ **Practical Fuel Cell Efficiency Derivation:** $$ \eta_{FC} = \frac{V_{\text{cell}}}{E^\circ_{\text{rev}}} $$ Substituting $E^\circ_{\text{rev}} = 1.229$ V for PEM (H$_2$/O$_2$, 25 °C): $$ \boxed{\;\eta_{FC} = \frac{V_{\text{cell}}}{1.229} \times 100\,\%\;} $$ --- #### (b) A PEM fuel cell stack delivers 0.65 V per cell at rated current. The hydrogen enthalpy of combustion is 285.8 kJ/mol. Compute: (i) Theoretical thermodynamic efficiency, (ii) Practical voltage efficiency, (iii) Overall electrical efficiency assuming $\Delta G^\circ = 237.2$ kJ/mol. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **(i) Theoretical Thermodynamic Efficiency:** $$ \eta_{\text{theor}} = \frac{\Delta G^\circ}{\Delta H^\circ} = \frac{237.2}{285.8} \approx 0.8300 = 83.0\,\% $$ **(ii) Practical Voltage Efficiency:** $$ \eta_{FC,\text{pract}} = \frac{V_{\text{cell}}}{E^\circ_{\text{rev}}} = \frac{0.65}{1.229} \approx 0.5289 = 52.89\,\% $$ **(iii) Overall Electrical Efficiency:** The overall efficiency is the *product* of theoretical upper bound and practical realisation: $$ \eta_{\text{overall}} = \eta_{\text{theor}} \times \eta_{\text{pract}} = 0.8300 \times 0.5289 \approx 0.4390 = 43.9\,\% $$ **Physical Interpretation:** Of every 100 kJ of hydrogen chemical energy: - 83 kJ is theoretically convertible to electricity (rest is bound as heat by the 2nd law). - Only **43.9 kJ** is actually delivered as electricity. - The remaining 56.1 kJ exits as heat (stack cooling load — a major FCEV thermal management challenge). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > - For Part B(b), **units must accompany every numerical answer** — students often write "0.83" instead of "83 %" and lose the final mark. > - A common error is using $\Delta H^\circ$ instead of $\Delta G^\circ$ for the practical voltage efficiency denominator. The correct denominator is **$E^\circ_{\text{rev}} = 1.229$ V**, not the HHVoltage. > - Always state whether you are computing **theoretical**, **practical (voltage)**, or **overall** efficiency — the three are distinct. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Cell Efficiency (KTU PEAET742 Module 4):** - **Coulombic Efficiency** $\eta_C$ relates to *charge conservation*; losses come from **side reactions** (SEI growth, lithium plating, self-discharge). - **Voltage Efficiency** $\eta_V$ relates to *potential losses*; losses come from **ohmic drop, activation, and concentration overpotentials**. - **Round-Trip Efficiency** is the **product** $\eta_{RT} = \eta_C \cdot \eta_V$, never the sum. - **Fuel Cell Theoretical Upper Bound** $= \Delta G^\circ / \Delta H^\circ = 83\,\%$ for H$_2$/O$_2$. - **PEM Reversible Voltage** $E^\circ_{\text{rev}} = 1.229$ V at 25 °C; **SOFC** operates at higher $T$ with different $E^\circ$. - **Solar Cell Efficiency** depends on three terms: $V_{oc}$, $I_{sc}$, and **Fill Factor** — improving FF is a key R&D focus. - **Carnot Limit** is the absolute thermodynamic ceiling; all real cell efficiencies are strictly below it. - **Battery heat generation** $= I^2 R_{\text{internal}}$ — directly degrades $\eta_V$ at high C-rates. - **Industry benchmark values** to memorise: - Li-ion $\eta_{RT} \approx 85\text{–}95\,\%$ - Lead-acid $\eta_{RT} \approx 70\text{–}80\,\%$ - PEM FC $\eta_{\text{practical}} \approx 50\text{–}57\,\%$ - c-Si PV $\eta \approx 17\text{–}22\,\%$ - **BMS uses Coulomb Counting** to track $\eta_C$ in real-time for State-of-Health (SOH) estimation. - **FCEV thermal load** $= (1 - \eta_{\text{overall}}) \times$ total H$_2$ energy input — must be rejected via radiators. - **Energy/charge/voltage are linearly independent factors**; always verify efficiency calculations via both the *ratio method* and the *direct energy method*.

topics.title: Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET742 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES AND RENEWABLE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** *Applications* in the context of Electric Vehicles and Renewable Technologies (EV&RT) refers to the practical deployment and integration of electrified transportation systems and renewable energy generation/conversion technologies across diverse sectors — including transportation, industrial, commercial, residential, and grid-scale domains — to achieve sustainable energy utilization, reduced carbon footprint, and enhanced energy security.

The **applications domain** of EV&RT encompasses:
- **Electric Mobility Applications** — BEVs, HEVs, PHEVs, FCEVs in road, rail, marine, and aerial transport.
- **Renewable Power Generation** — Solar PV, wind, micro-hydro, biomass, geothermal, and hybrid systems.
- **Energy Storage Applications** — Battery, supercapacitor, hydrogen, pumped hydro, and thermal storage integration.
- **Grid Integration Applications** — Microgrids, smart grids, vehicle-to-grid (V2G), and net-metering systems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of the EV&RT Applications landscape as a **"Two-Way Banking System"** for energy. Just like a modern bank lets you deposit, withdraw, and transfer money through multiple channels (apps, ATMs, branches), the modern energy ecosystem lets you **generate** energy from renewables, **store** it in batteries, and **consume** it through electric vehicles or feed it back to the grid. Each application is like a different financial product serving a unique customer need.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Standard Gravitational Acceleration:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
> - **Standard Atmospheric Pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$
> - **Air Density at STP:** $\rho_{air} = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - **Solar Constant (AM1.5):** $G_{sc} = 1367 \text{ W/m}^2$
> - **Energy Unit Equivalence:** $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \text{ MJ} = 860 \text{ kcal}$
> - **Standard Battery Nominal Voltage:** $V_{nom} = 3.6 \text{ V} \text{ to } 3.7 \text{ V (Li-ion cell)}$
> - **Round-trip Efficiency Benchmark:** $\eta_{RT} \geq 80\% \text{ (Li-ion)}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Major Application Domains — Structured Breakdown

#### **A. Transportation Applications**

- **Road Vehicles:** Battery Electric Vehicles (BEV), Hybrid Electric Vehicles (HEV), Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicles (PHEV), Fuel Cell Electric Vehicles (FCEV).
- **Public Transport:** Electric buses (trolley, battery, hydrogen fuel cell), trams, metro rail.
- **Two/Three-Wheelers:** E-scooters, e-bikes, e-rickshaws (dominant in Indian urban mobility).
- **Heavy-Duty and Off-Road:** Mining dump trucks, port vehicles, construction equipment, agricultural tractors.
- **Emerging:** Electric vertical take-off and landing (eVTOL) aircraft, electric boats/ships.

#### **B. Stationary Power Applications**

- **Standalone (Off-Grid) Systems:** Solar home lighting, solar water pumps, telecom tower power, remote village electrification.
- **Grid-Connected Systems:** Rooftop solar PV, solar farms, wind farms, biomass cogeneration.
- **Hybrid Systems:** PV-Wind-Battery-Diesel microgrids for islands and remote communities.

#### **C. Industrial & Commercial Applications**

- **Process Heat:** Solar thermal concentrators, geothermal heat pumps, biomass boilers.
- **Hydrogen Economy:** Green hydrogen production via electrolysis, fuel cells for forklifts, backup power.
- **Cold Chain:** Solar-powered refrigeration for pharmaceuticals and food preservation.

#### **D. Grid Support Applications**

- **Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G):** Bidirectional EV charging — EVs act as distributed storage.
- **Demand Response:** Smart charging aligned with renewable generation peaks.
- **Frequency Regulation:** Fast-acting battery storage stabilizes grid frequency (50 Hz India / 60 Hz US).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Equations for EV&RT Applications**

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| $E_{req} = P_{load} \times t_{op}$ | Daily energy required for an application | kWh |
| $\eta_{sys} = \eta_{PV} \times \eta_{inv} \times \eta_{bat}$ | Overall system efficiency (cascaded) | dimensionless (0–1) |
| $C_{bat} = \dfrac{E_{req} \times DOD_{max}}{V_{sys} \times \eta_{bat}}$ | Battery capacity sizing | Ah |
| $P_{wind} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot A \cdot v^3 \cdot C_p$ | Wind turbine power extraction | W |
| $C_{H2} = \dfrac{E_{req}}{\eta_{el} \times \eta_{FC} \times \text{LHV}_{H2}}$ | Hydrogen consumption rate | kg |
| $A_{PV} = \dfrac{E_{req}}{G_{avg} \times \eta_{PV} \times PR}$ | Solar PV array area required | m² |
| $C_{EOL} = C_{nom} \cdot (1 - DoD)^n$ | Battery state of health (Peukert approximation) | Ah |
| $\text{CO}_2_{saved} = E_{EV} \times EF_{grid}$ | CO₂ emissions avoided by EV vs ICE | kg CO₂ |
| $T_{payback} = \dfrac{C_{install} - C_{subsidy}}{C_{annual\_savings}}$ | Simple payback period | years |
| $SOC(t) = SOC_0 + \dfrac{1}{C_{bat}}\int_{0}^{t}\left(I_{ch}\eta_{ch} - \dfrac{I_{dis}}{\eta_{dis}}\right)d\tau$ | Battery state of charge (Coulomb counting) | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions to Remember:**
> - $0 \leq C_p \leq 0.593$ (Betz Limit for wind turbines).
> - $0 \leq DOD \leq 0.8$ for long-life Li-ion operation.
> - $PR$ (Performance Ratio) for PV systems: **0.7 to 0.85** typical.
> - LHV of Hydrogen: $\text{LHV}_{H2} = 33.33 \text{ kWh/kg}$.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Cities:** Solar rooftops + EV charging + battery storage form integrated urban energy hubs.
- **Disaster Resilience:** Islanded microgrids with PV + battery provide critical power during outages.
- **Carbon Compliance:** Industries use renewable integration to meet **BEE (Bureau of Energy Efficiency)** PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) scheme targets.
- **Agricultural Productivity:** Solar pumps replace diesel pumps — over **3.5 lakh** installed in India under PM-KUSUM scheme.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Sizing a Solar PV System for a Standalone Application

**Problem Statement:** Design a standalone solar PV system to power a load of **$P_L = 500 \text{ W}$** operating for **$t = 6 \text{ hours/day}$** at a remote location with average peak sun hours **$G = 5 \text{ kWh/m}^2\text{/day}$**, PV efficiency **$\eta_{PV} = 0.18$**, Performance Ratio **$PR = 0.75$**, battery round-trip efficiency **$\eta_{bat} = 0.85$**, Depth of Discharge **$DOD = 0.7$**, system DC bus voltage **$V_{sys} = 48 \text{ V}$**, and 1 day of autonomy.

#### **Step 1: Calculate Daily Energy Demand**

The total energy required by the load per day is calculated as the product of the load power and the operating time.

$$
E_{req} = P_L \times t
$$

$$
E_{req} = 500 \text{ W} \times 6 \text{ h} = 3000 \text{ Wh} = 3.0 \text{ kWh/day}
$$

#### **Step 2: Calculate Required PV Array Area**

We substitute the available solar irradiation (in $\text{kWh/m}^2\text{/day}$), the PV module conversion efficiency, and the system's performance ratio to find the array area.

$$
A_{PV} = \dfrac{E_{req}}{G \times \eta_{PV} \times PR}
$$

$$
A_{PV} = \dfrac{3.0}{5 \times 0.18 \times 0.75}
$$

$$
A_{PV} = \dfrac{3.0}{0.675} = 4.444 \text{ m}^2
$$

#### **Step 3: Calculate Battery Bank Capacity**

The battery must store one full day of energy autonomy. We account for allowable depth of discharge and the battery's round-trip energy efficiency.

$$
C_{bat} = \dfrac{E_{req} \times \text{Autonomy}}{V_{sys} \times DOD \times \eta_{bat}}
$$

$$
C_{bat} = \dfrac{3000 \times 1}{48 \times 0.7 \times 0.85}
$$

$$
C_{bat} = \dfrac{3000}{28.56} = 105.04 \text{ Ah}
$$

#### **Step 4: Select Standard Component Sizes**

We round up to commercially available sizes to ensure the design meets or exceeds the calculated requirements.

- **PV Array:** $A_{PV,std} = 5.0 \text{ m}^2$ (next standard module size)
- **Battery Bank:** $C_{bat,std} = 110 \text{ Ah}$ (next standard rating at 48 V)
- **Number of 12 V Batteries in Series:** $N_s = 48/12 = 4$ batteries

#### **Step 5: Calculate Annual CO₂ Emissions Saved**

Using the Indian grid emission factor of **$EF = 0.82 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{/kWh}$** (CEA 2023 average), we estimate the carbon displacement achieved by replacing grid electricity.

$$
\text{CO}_2_{saved} = E_{req} \times 365 \times EF
$$

$$
\text{CO}_2_{saved} = 3.0 \times 365 \times 0.82 = 897.9 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{/year}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Wind Turbine Power Output for Grid-Tied Application

**Problem Statement:** A small wind turbine with rotor diameter **$D = 4 \text{ m}$** operates at average wind speed **$v = 8 \text{ m/s}$** with power coefficient **$C_p = 0.40$**. Calculate the electrical power output, daily energy, and verify Betz limit compliance.

#### **Step 1: Calculate Swept Area**

The swept area of the rotor is derived from the geometric formula for the area of a circle.

$$
A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (4)^2}{4} = 12.566 \text{ m}^2
$$

#### **Step 2: Calculate Available Wind Power**

The raw kinetic power in the wind is given by the standard aerodynamic power equation.

$$
P_{wind} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho_{air} A v^3
$$

$$
P_{wind} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 12.566 \times (8)^3
$$

$$
P_{wind} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 12.566 \times 512 = 3937.5 \text{ W} \approx 3.94 \text{ kW}
$$

#### **Step 3: Calculate Actual Electrical Power Output**

We multiply the available wind power by the turbine's power coefficient and assume a generator-electronics efficiency of **$\eta_g = 0.90$**.

$$
P_{elec} = P_{wind} \times C_p \times \eta_g
$$

$$
P_{elec} = 3937.5 \times 0.40 \times 0.90 = 1417.5 \text{ W} \approx 1.42 \text{ kW}
$$

#### **Step 4: Daily Energy Production**

Assuming **24-hour** operation (ideal case) — in practice capacity factor is used.

$$
E_{daily} = P_{elec} \times CF \times 24
$$

where **$CF = 0.25$** typical capacity factor for small wind.

$$
E_{daily} = 1.42 \times 0.25 \times 24 = 8.52 \text{ kWh/day}
$$

#### **Step 5: Betz Limit Verification**

$$
C_p / 0.593 = 0.40 / 0.593 = 0.6746 \text{ or } 67.46\%
$$

This is within the **Betz limit**, confirming physically realizable design.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Application Selection

| **Parameter** | **Solar PV** | **Wind** | **Biomass** | **Hydrogen FC** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Capacity Range** | 1 W – 1 GW | 100 W – 10 MW | 10 kW – 50 MW | 1 kW – 10 MW |
| **Capacity Factor** | 15–25% | 20–40% | 60–85% | 50–90% |
| **Storage Needed** | Yes (4–8 hr) | Yes (2–6 hr) | No (baseload) | Yes (tank) |
| **Land Use (per MW)** | 1.5–2.5 acres | 5–10 acres | 0.5–1 acre | Minimal |
| **Best Application** | Distributed, rooftops | Wind corridors, offshore | Rural baseload | Heavy transport, backup |
| **CO₂ (g/kWh)** | 40–50 | 10–20 | 30–50 (net) | 0 (green H₂) |
| **LCOE (₹/kWh)** | 3–5 | 3–6 | 5–7 | 8–15 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Integrated EV + Renewable Application Architecture

```mermaid

    inputB["WIND TURBINE"]:::ren

    bus2["DC BUS BAR"]:::bus
```


<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### **Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)**

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*List any three major applications of renewable energy systems in the transportation sector and state one key advantage of each.*

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Applications:**

| **Application** | **Key Advantage** |
|:---|:---|
| Battery Electric Vehicles (BEV) | Zero tailpipe emissions; reduced urban air pollution |
| Solar-Powered Boats / Electric Ferries | Quiet operation; zero fuel cost in operation |
| Hydrogen Fuel Cell Buses | Long range; fast refueling; only water as exhaust |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G) technology. Mention two practical applications of V2G in modern smart grids.*

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** V2G is a smart-grid technology that enables bidirectional power flow between an electric vehicle's battery and the electrical grid, allowing EVs to draw power for charging (G2V) and to inject stored power back to the grid (V2G) based on grid demand signals.

**Two Applications:**
1. **Peak Shaving:** EV fleets discharge during peak demand hours (6–9 PM), reducing strain on grid infrastructure and avoiding costly peaker plant operation.
2. **Frequency Regulation:** EVs respond within milliseconds to grid frequency deviations, providing ancillary services and earning revenue for EV owners.

---

### **Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the major applications of solar PV systems in the domestic, industrial, and agricultural sectors. Discuss the role of net-metering in promoting rooftop solar adoption. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A standalone solar PV system is to be designed for a remote village health center. The load includes: 10 LED bulbs (40 W each) for 6 hours, 2 ceiling fans (60 W each) for 8 hours, and 1 refrigerator (150 W) for 24 hours. Assume peak sun hours = 5 $\text{kWh/m}^2\text{/day}$, $\eta_{PV}$ = 0.17, PR = 0.75, battery DOD = 0.7, $\eta_{bat}$ = 0.85, system voltage = 48 V, 1 day autonomy. Calculate the (i) daily energy demand, (ii) PV array area, and (iii) battery capacity. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

The major applications of solar PV systems span three key sectors:

**1. Domestic Applications:**
- **Rooftop Solar PV:** Grid-connected systems (1–10 kW) supply household loads with net-metering for surplus export.
- **Solar Water Heaters (Thermal):** Flat-plate and evacuated tube collectors for domestic hot water.
- **Solar Home Lighting Systems:** Standalone kits with integrated battery for rural electrification.
- **Solar Air Conditioning:** PV-powered or solar-thermal-driven AC units.

**2. Industrial Applications:**
- **Solar-Powered Manufacturing:** Large-scale ground-mounted PV plants (MW-scale) supplying industrial feeders.
- **Solar Process Heat:** Concentrated Solar Power (CSP) and solar thermal collectors for medium-temperature industrial processes (textile, dairy, food processing).
- **Solar Cold Storage:** For pharmaceutical and food preservation cold chains.

**3. Agricultural Applications:**
- **Solar Water Pumps:** DC/AC pumps replacing diesel pumps (PM-KUSUM scheme).
- **Solar Drying:** Agricultural produce (grains, spices, fruits) dried in solar dryers.
- **Solar Greenhouses:** Hybrid greenhouse structures with integrated PV for off-grid power and ventilation.

**Role of Net-Metering:**
Net-metering is a billing mechanism that credits solar PV system owners for the excess electricity they export to the grid. The bidirectional meter records both import and export, and the consumer is billed only for the **net** consumption. Key benefits:
- Reduces payback period to **4–6 years** in India.
- Eliminates need for expensive battery storage in grid-connected systems.
- Promotes distributed generation and reduces T&D losses.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Step 1: Calculate Energy Consumption of Each Load**

We compute the daily energy for each appliance by multiplying its rated power by its daily usage duration.

- **LED Bulbs:** $E_1 = 10 \times 40 \times 6 = 2400 \text{ Wh}$
- **Ceiling Fans:** $E_2 = 2 \times 60 \times 8 = 960 \text{ Wh}$
- **Refrigerator:** $E_3 = 1 \times 150 \times 24 = 3600 \text{ Wh}$

**Step 2: Total Daily Energy Demand**

$$
E_{req} = E_1 + E_2 + E_3 = 2400 + 960 + 3600 = 6960 \text{ Wh} = 6.96 \text{ kWh/day}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate PV Array Area**

Substituting the available solar resource, the module conversion efficiency, and the system performance ratio into the standard PV sizing equation.

$$
A_{PV} = \dfrac{E_{req}}{G \times \eta_{PV} \times PR}
$$

$$
A_{PV} = \dfrac{6.96}{5 \times 0.17 \times 0.75} = \dfrac{6.96}{0.6375} = 10.92 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 4: Calculate Battery Bank Capacity**

Accounting for depth-of-discharge limits and round-trip efficiency at the DC bus voltage for 1-day autonomy.

$$
C_{bat} = \dfrac{E_{req} \times 1}{V_{sys} \times DOD \times \eta_{bat}}
$$

$$
C_{bat} = \dfrac{6960}{48 \times 0.7 \times 0.85} = \dfrac{6960}{28.56} = 243.7 \text{ Ah}
$$

**Recommended Standard Size:** $C_{bat,std} = 250 \text{ Ah}$ at 48 V (4 × 12 V, 250 Ah batteries in series).

**Total Battery Energy:** $48 \text{ V} \times 250 \text{ Ah} = 12 \text{ kWh}$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**(a)** Explain with a block diagram how renewable energy sources are integrated with electric vehicle charging infrastructure to form a sustainable transportation-energy system. Discuss the role of Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G) technology. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A small wind turbine of rotor diameter 3 m operates at an average wind speed of 10 m/s. The power coefficient is 0.38 and the generator + power electronics efficiency is 0.92. Air density is 1.225 $\text{kg/m}^3$. Calculate: (i) swept area, (ii) available wind power, (iii) electrical power output, (iv) verify Betz limit compliance, and (v) annual energy generation assuming a capacity factor of 0.22. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Integrated Renewable-EV Architecture:**

A sustainable transportation-energy system integrates three subsystems:

1. **Renewable Generation Subsystem:** Solar PV arrays and small wind turbines generate clean DC/AC power at distributed locations (parking lots, highways, residential areas).

2. **EV Charging Infrastructure:** Includes Level 1 (slow AC), Level 2 (fast AC), and DC fast chargers. Smart chargers with bidirectional capability support V2G.

3. **Energy Storage & Grid Interface:** Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS) buffer the intermittency of renewables and provide backup.

**Block Diagram (Verbal Description):**
```
[Solar PV + Wind] → [DC Bus] → [Inverter] → [AC Bus] ⇄ [Grid]
                                          ↓
                                [Smart Charger (Bidirectional)]
                                          ↓
                                   [EV Battery / V2G]
                                          ↓
                                   [Battery Storage]
```

**Role of V2G Technology:**
- **Grid Stabilization:** Aggregated EVs provide frequency regulation and voltage support.
- **Renewable Integration:** EVs store excess solar/wind generation during off-peak and discharge during peaks.
- **Economic Benefit:** EV owners earn revenue from grid services; utilities defer infrastructure upgrades.
- **Energy Security:** In islanded microgrids, EVs form a virtual power plant for resilience.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Step 1: Swept Area**

$$
A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (3)^2}{4} = 7.069 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2: Available Wind Power**

$$
P_{wind} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho_{air} A v^3
$$

$$
P_{wind} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 7.069 \times (10)^3
$$

$$
P_{wind} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 7.069 \times 1000 = 4329.7 \text{ W} \approx 4.33 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3: Electrical Power Output**

$$
P_{elec} = P_{wind} \times C_p \times \eta_g
$$

$$
P_{elec} = 4329.7 \times 0.38 \times 0.92 = 1513.7 \text{ W} \approx 1.51 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 4: Betz Limit Verification**

$$
\dfrac{C_p}{0.593} = \dfrac{0.38}{0.593} = 0.641 = 64.1\%
$$

Since **$C_p = 0.38 < 0.593$ (Betz limit)**, the design is **physically realizable**. ✓

**Step 5: Annual Energy Generation**

$$
E_{annual} = P_{elec} \times CF \times 8760
$$

$$
E_{annual} = 1.514 \times 0.22 \times 8760 = 2918 \text{ kWh/year} \approx 2.92 \text{ MWh/year}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing W and kW, or m/s and km/h. Always normalize units **before** substitution.
> 2. **Forgetting Performance Ratio (PR):** Many students compute $A_{PV} = E_{req}/(G \times \eta_{PV})$ and ignore PR — losing **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Betz Limit Misapplication:** Writing $C_p$ should be > 0.593 is a fatal error. The Betz limit states $C_p$ **cannot exceed** 0.593.
> 4. **V2G Confusion:** Many students describe V2G as only "EV charging from grid" — it must be **bidirectional**.
> 5. **Autonomy Factor:** Standalone battery sizing must include autonomy days; missing this costs **2–3 marks**.
> 6. **Standard Sizes:** Final answer must be rounded up to commercially available sizes (next standard module/battery).

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember** ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ☐ **EV&RT Applications** span **transport, stationary, industrial, and grid** domains.
- ☐ **Standalone PV sizing formula:** $A_{PV} = E_{req}/(G \times \eta_{PV} \times PR)$.
- ☐ **Battery sizing formula:** $C_{bat} = (E_{req} \times \text{Autonomy})/(V_{sys} \times DOD \times \eta_{bat})$.
- ☐ **Wind power equation:** $P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A v^3 C_p \eta_g$.
- ☐ **Betz Limit:** $C_p^{max} = 0.593$ (16/27) — never exceed.
- ☐ **LHV of Hydrogen:** $33.33 \text{ kWh/kg}$; efficiency chain: Electrolysis × FC.
- ☐ **Performance Ratio (PR):** $0.7$–$0.85$ for grid-tied PV.
- ☐ **V2G** = bidirectional EV ↔ grid power flow for ancillary services.
- ☐ **Capacity Factor (CF):** Wind = $0.20$–$0.40$; Solar = $0.15$–$0.25$.
- ☐ **Standard grid emission factor (India):** $0.82 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{/kWh}$ (CEA).
- ☐ **LCOE benchmark (2024 India):** Solar = ₹3–5/kWh; Wind = ₹3–6/kWh.
- ☐ **Energy equivalence:** $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \text{ MJ}$.
- ☐ **PM-KUSUM Scheme:** Solar agricultural pumps target — key Indian policy for renewable application.
- ☐ **Microgrid components:** Generation (PV/Wind) + Storage (Battery/H₂) + Loads + EMS.
- ☐ **FCEV applications:** Long-haul trucks, buses, forklifts, trains (corridor-based refueling).
- ☐ **Net-metering** reduces residential solar payback to ~5 years.
- ☐ **EV charging levels:** L1 (3–7 kW), L2 (7–22 kW), L3 DCFC (50–350 kW).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wind Energy - Introduction - Wind Turbine Types (HAWT and VAWT) - Wind Power Curve - Betz's Law - Power from a Wind Turbine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET742 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES AND RENEWABLE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Wind Energy - Introduction - Wind Turbine Types (HAWT and VAWT) - Wind Power Curve - Betz's Law - Power from a Wind Turbine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Wind Energy — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wind Energy** is the form of **kinetic energy** possessed by moving air masses (wind) that can be captured and converted into useful mechanical work or — through a coupled **electric generator** — into **electrical energy**. A device that performs this energy conversion is called a **Wind Turbine** (or Wind Energy Conversion System, WECS).

Mathematically, the kinetic energy of a moving air parcel of mass $m$ moving with velocity $v$ is:

$$E_{kinetic} = \frac{1}{2} m v^{2}$$

Since the air is constantly flowing, what matters to the engineer is not the *total* energy in a single packet, but the **power flux** — the rate at which kinetic energy crosses a unit area perpendicular to the wind direction. This is the foundation of **Betz's Law** and wind turbine design.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Wind Exists (Atmospheric Perspective):** Wind is fundamentally the atmosphere attempting to **equalize pressure differences** caused by uneven solar heating of the Earth's surface. This natural phenomenon is renewable, ubiquitous, and — most importantly for KTU's renewable technologies syllabus — directly exploitable for clean electricity generation.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Understanding Wind Energy in Plain English

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The "River of Air"**
> Imagine wind as an invisible **river flowing across the sky**. Just like a watermill extracts energy from a flowing river by placing a paddle wheel in its path, a wind turbine extracts energy from the "river of air" by placing **aerodynamic blades** in the wind's path. The faster the river (higher wind speed), and the larger the paddle wheel (greater swept area), the more energy you can extract. However — and this is critical — a paddle wheel placed perfectly perpendicular to a river **cannot extract 100% of the water's energy**, because doing so would stop the water entirely (and a stopped flow carries no energy out). This is exactly why **Betz's Law** tells us that no turbine can extract more than **59.3 %** of the wind's kinetic energy.

A simple, real-world example: a small rooftop wind turbine on a coastal home in Kerala generating just enough electricity to charge batteries or run lights. Multiply that by hundreds of turbines in a wind farm near **Kanjikode (Palakkad)** or **Ramakkalmedu**, and you have utility-scale power generation feeding the KSEB grid.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Physical Constants for Wind Energy Calculations:**
> - **Air density (ρ):** Standard value = **1.225 kg/m³** (at sea level, 15 °C, 1 atm). At higher altitudes or hotter locations, ρ decreases.
> - **Swept area (A):** For a horizontal-axis turbine, $A = \pi R^{2}$ (m²), where $R$ is the rotor radius.
> - **Wind speed (v):** Measured in **m/s** at hub height (typically 80–120 m for utility-scale turbines).
> - **Betz limit (C_p,max):** 16/27 ≈ **0.5926** (or **59.3 %**).
> - **Power output (P):** Measured in **Watts (W)** or **kilowatts (kW)** or **megawatts (MW)**.

**Standard Performance Metrics Used by KTU Examiners:**
- **Power Coefficient (C_p):** Fraction of available wind power converted to mechanical power.
- **Tip Speed Ratio (TSR or λ):** Ratio of blade tip speed to free-stream wind speed.
- **Cut-in, Rated, and Cut-out Wind Speeds:** Define the operating envelope of a turbine.

---

## 1.4 Why Wind Energy Matters in the KTU 2024 Scheme

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus places this topic under **PEAET742 (Electric Vehicles and Renewable Technologies)** specifically because wind energy is a **bulk renewable resource** that complements **solar PV** in hybrid EV charging stations, microgrids, and grid-connected renewable systems. Expect theory questions (definition, Betz's law, derivations) and numerical problems (power from wind, tip speed ratio, Betz efficiency) in both **Continuous Evaluation (CE)** and **End Semester Examinations (ESE)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Wind Turbine Types — HAWT vs. VAWT

Wind turbines are broadly classified into two major categories based on the orientation of their **rotor axis** relative to the wind direction. This classification is a **direct KTU syllabus topic** and is frequently asked in Part A (3-mark) questions.

### 2.1.1 Horizontal Axis Wind Turbine (HAWT)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** A **HAWT** is a wind turbine in which the **rotor shaft (main shaft) is oriented horizontally** — i.e., **parallel to the ground** — and the rotor blades rotate in a **vertical plane** that is **perpendicular to the wind direction**.

**Key Engineering Features of HAWTs:**
- The **rotor axis is horizontal**, parallel to the Earth's surface.
- Requires a **yaw mechanism** (yaw drive + yaw motor) to keep the nacelle pointed into the wind as direction changes.
- **Upwind** design: rotor faces the wind before the tower (most common).
- **Downwind** design: rotor is on the leeward side of the tower (less common, used in some designs).
- The **generator and gearbox** are housed in the **nacelle** on top of the tower.
- Typically has **2 or 3 blades** (3-bladed is the industry standard).
- Tall tower to access higher, less turbulent wind.

**Sub-categories of HAWTs:**
- **Dutch Windmill type** (old, multi-bladed, low RPM, high torque — used historically for water pumping).
- **Propeller-type / Modern HAWT** (3 blades, upwind, variable pitch, used for electricity generation).
- **Multi-blade (American / Farm) type** — used for water pumping, not electricity.

**Engineering Real-World Utility:**
- All modern utility-scale wind farms (e.g., **Muppandal Wind Farm, Tamil Nadu**; **Jaisalmer Wind Park, Rajasthan**; offshore farms in the UK and Germany) use 3-bladed upwind **HAWTs** because of their superior aerodynamic efficiency, lower cut-in speed, and ability to be pitched for power regulation.

---

### 2.1.2 Vertical Axis Wind Turbine (VAWT)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** A **VAWT** is a wind turbine in which the **rotor shaft is oriented vertically** — i.e., **perpendicular to the ground** — and the rotor blades rotate in a **horizontal plane** about a vertical axis.

**Key Engineering Features of VAWTs:**
- The **rotor axis is vertical**, perpendicular to the ground.
- **No yaw mechanism required** — the turbine accepts wind from **any horizontal direction** (omnidirectional).
- Generator and gearbox can be placed at **ground level** (simpler maintenance, no nacelle on a tall tower).
- Generally **lower tip speeds**, lower aerodynamic efficiency, and lower C_p than HAWTs.
- Operates at lower wind speeds; suitable for **urban, rooftop, and small-scale applications**.

**Sub-categories of VAWTs:**
- **Savonius Turbine:** Drag-based, S-shaped or split-S rotors. Simple, self-starting, low efficiency (~10–15 %).
- **Darrieus Turbine:** Lift-based, uses curved (troposkein) or straight blades. Requires external push to start.
- **Giromill (H-rotor):** Straight-bladed Darrieus variant with variable pitch.

**Engineering Real-World Utility:**
- VAWTs are used in **urban wind energy harvesting**, **rooftop installations**, and **hybrid systems** where wind direction is highly variable. They are a research interest in building-integrated wind systems.

---

### 2.1.3 HAWT vs. VAWT — Comparative Matrix (High-Yield for KTU)

> [!NOTE]
> **The following comparison is a frequently asked KTU question — both as 3-mark short notes and as 7-mark comparative essays in Part B.**

| Parameter | HAWT (Horizontal Axis) | VAWT (Vertical Axis) |
|---|---|---|
| Rotor axis orientation | Horizontal (parallel to ground) | Vertical (perpendicular to ground) |
| Yaw mechanism | Required | Not required |
| Wind direction dependency | Must face wind | Omnidirectional |
| Self-starting ability | Good (3-blade propeller) | Poor (Darrieus needs boost) |
| Power coefficient (C_p) | High (0.35–0.45 typical) | Low (0.15–0.30 typical) |
| Maintenance access | Difficult (nacelle on tower) | Easy (ground level) |
| Cut-in wind speed | Lower | Higher |
| Tower shadow / cyclic stresses | Present | Reduced |
| Suitability | Utility-scale, wind farms | Urban, rooftop, small-scale |
| Example installations | Muppandal, Jaisalmer | Building-integrated prototypes |

---

## 2.2 Wind Power Curve — Understanding Turbine Performance Envelope

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** The **Wind Power Curve** of a wind turbine is a **graphical representation of the electrical (or mechanical) power output** of the turbine as a function of the **free-stream wind speed** at hub height, under standard atmospheric conditions (air density = 1.225 kg/m³, horizontal, non-turbulent flow).

A wind power curve is the **engineering fingerprint** of a turbine — manufacturers (e.g., **Vestas, Siemens Gamesa, GE Renewable, Suzlon**) provide this curve in their datasheets, and it is the basis for **Annual Energy Production (AEP)** calculations in wind farm design.

### 2.2.1 The Four Critical Wind Speeds in a Power Curve

The wind power curve is divided into **five distinct operating regions**, each defined by a critical wind speed:

> [!NOTE]
> **Region 1 — Below Cut-in Speed (v < v_cut-in):**
> The wind does **not** contain enough energy to overcome the turbine's internal friction and generator losses. **Power output = 0**. The turbine is **idle** (braked or feathered).

> [!NOTE]
> **Region 2 — Between Cut-in and Rated Speed (v_cut-in ≤ v < v_rated):**
> The turbine is operational. Power output **rises rapidly** (approximately **cubic** with wind speed) until the **rated power** is reached. The control system is in **partial load / torque control** mode. $P \propto v^{3}$ (ideal region).

> [!NOTE]
> **Region 3 — Between Rated and Cut-out Speed (v_rated ≤ v < v_cut-out):**
> The turbine output is **deliberately held constant** at the **rated power** by the pitch control system (variable pitch blades turn to "spill" excess wind). This protects the generator and gearbox from overloading.

> [!NOTE]
> **Region 4 — Above Cut-out Speed (v ≥ v_cut-out):**
> The turbine is **shut down and braked** to prevent structural damage. **Power output = 0**. Typical cut-out speed is **25 m/s** (≈ 90 km/h).

> [!NOTE]
> **Region 0 — At and Above Survival Speed:**
> The turbine is locked in survival mode (rotor parked, blades feathered). Survival speeds are typically 60–80 m/s.

### 2.2.2 Typical Numerical Values (Modern 3 MW HAWT)

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Cut-in wind speed | 3 m/s |
| Rated wind speed | 12 m/s |
| Cut-out wind speed | 25 m/s |
| Survival wind speed | 70 m/s |
| Rated power | 3 MW |
| Rotor diameter | 110–130 m |

---

## 2.3 Betz's Law — The Fundamental Limit of Wind Energy Extraction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Betz's Law (1926, Albert Betz):** *No wind turbine can convert more than **16/27 (≈ 59.3 %)** of the kinetic energy of the wind into mechanical energy turning the rotor.* The factor **16/27** is called the **Betz coefficient** or **maximum power coefficient (C_p,max)**. It is a **purely theoretical upper bound** derived from conservation of mass and momentum — independent of turbine design.

**Why the limit is not 100 %:**
If a turbine extracted 100 % of the wind's kinetic energy, the air would have to come to a **complete stop** behind the rotor. But if the air is stationary downstream, it would **block the incoming wind** — a contradiction (continuity of mass flow). The optimum is reached when the air exits the turbine at exactly **1/3 of the upstream wind speed**, yielding the **16/27** maximum.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is a high-density summary of every formula you need for KTU ESE and internal assessments on this topic. Memorize it.**

| Equation | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Power in wind (total available) | $P_{wind} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3}$ | Total kinetic power flux through swept area A | W (Watts) |
| Swept area of HAWT rotor | $A = \pi R^{2}$ | Area swept by rotor blades | m² |
| Power extracted by turbine | $P_{turbine} = C_{p} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3}$ | Useful mechanical power at shaft | W |
| Betz's Law (max C_p) | $C_{p,\max} = \tfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926$ | Theoretical upper bound | dimensionless |
| Betz maximum extractable power | $P_{Betz} = \tfrac{16}{27} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3} = \tfrac{8}{27} \rho A v^{3}$ | Maximum realizable mechanical power | W |
| Power coefficient (general) | $C_{p} = \tfrac{P_{turbine}}{P_{wind}} = \tfrac{2 P_{turbine}}{\rho A v^{3}}$ | Fraction of wind power captured | dimensionless |
| Tip speed ratio (TSR) | $\lambda = \tfrac{\omega R}{v} = \tfrac{\pi R n}{30 v}$ | Blade tip speed / free-stream wind speed | dimensionless |
| Wind energy per year (Weibull distribution basis) | $E = P_{rated} \cdot \text{capacity factor} \cdot 8760$ | Annual energy production approximation | kWh/year |
| Air density (ideal gas) | $\rho = \tfrac{P}{R_{specific} T}$ | Density as function of pressure, temperature | kg/m³ |
| Thrust coefficient | $C_{T} = \tfrac{F_{thrust}}{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^{2}}$ | Ratio of thrust force to dynamic pressure force | dimensionless |
| Rotor solidity | $\sigma = \tfrac{N c}{2 \pi R}$ | Total blade chord / circumference | dimensionless |
| Induced velocity factor (axial) | $a = \tfrac{v_{induction}}{v}$ | Fraction of wind speed lost at rotor | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip — Common Substitution Errors:**
> - Use **v³** (NOT v²) for wind power.
> - Use **A = πR²** (NOT A = 2πR) for the swept area.
> - **ρ = 1.225 kg/m³** unless the problem specifies a different value.
> - Convert wind speed from **km/h to m/s** by dividing by 3.6 (a classic KTU trap).

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles in this topic are not just textbook concepts — they underpin:
- **Wind farm site selection** (using Weibull distribution and AEP calculations).
- **Turbine blade aerodynamic design** (BEM theory, lift/drag coefficients).
- **Pitch and yaw control algorithms** in modern SCADA systems.
- **Hybrid renewable systems** (e.g., wind + solar PV + battery for EV charging stations).
- **Offshore wind** (floating HAWTs, deeper water installations).
- **Micro wind turbines** (urban, rooftop, telecom tower applications).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Convention:** When asked to "derive" any of the following, you must show **every algebraic step** and state the underlying physical principle (conservation of mass, conservation of momentum, or energy balance) at each stage. The derivations below are written to exactly match the expected KTU 2024 solution format.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Power Available in the Wind (P_wind)

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Principle Used:** *Kinetic Energy of a moving mass per unit time (Power) = Rate of kinetic energy flux across a cross-section.*

### Step 1 — Define the Mass Flow Rate of Air

Consider wind of velocity $v$ (m/s) flowing perpendicularly through a circular cross-section of area $A$ (m²). The air density is $\rho$ (kg/m³).

The **mass flow rate** ($\dot{m}$), i.e., the mass of air passing through area $A$ per second, is the volume flow rate times density.

Volume flow rate = Area × velocity = $A \cdot v$ (m³/s).

Therefore, mass flow rate:

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A \cdot v \quad [\text{kg/s}]$$

### Step 2 — Write the Kinetic Energy of This Air Parcel Per Second

The kinetic energy of a mass $m$ moving with velocity $v$ is $\tfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$.

The **power** is the kinetic energy per unit time. Replacing $m$ with $\dot{m} \cdot t$ and dividing by $t$:

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot (\dot{m}) \cdot v^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot (\rho A v) \cdot v^{2}$$

### Step 3 — Final Expression

$$\boxed{P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3} \quad [\text{Watts}]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight:** The cubic dependence on $v$ is the reason why **doubling the wind speed increases the available power by 8 times**. This is why site selection prioritizes locations with consistently high mean wind speeds, and why small variations in wind speed translate to large variations in power output. This is also why modern wind turbines are placed on **tall towers** (80–120 m) to access faster, less turbulent wind layers.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Power Extractable by a Wind Turbine (Betz's Law)

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Principles Used:** (1) Conservation of Mass, (2) Conservation of Momentum (Euler's turbine equation), (3) Energy Balance between upstream and downstream of the rotor.

### Step 1 — Set Up the Control Volume

Consider a horizontal-axis wind turbine with:
- Upstream (free-stream) wind speed: $v_{1}$
- Wind speed at the rotor plane: $v$
- Downstream (wake) wind speed: $v_{2}$
- Rotor swept area: $A$

Assume:
- The control volume is incompressible.
- Air density $\rho$ is constant.
- The flow is steady, one-dimensional, and axial.
- The pressure far upstream and far downstream equals the undisturbed atmospheric pressure $p_{atm}$.

### Step 2 — Conservation of Mass (Continuity Equation)

The mass flow rate through the rotor must equal the mass flow rate in the upstream and downstream cross-sections (assuming the same area A, since the air stream tube contracts slightly after the rotor — this is the **actuator disk model**):

$$\dot{m} = \rho A v = \rho A_{1} v_{1} = \rho A_{2} v_{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **In a more refined model:** the stream tube expands upstream and contracts downstream, but the mass flow rate through the rotor disc is uniform.

### Step 3 — Conservation of Momentum (Thrust Equation)

The force on the air (thrust) equals the rate of change of momentum:

$$F = \dot{m} (v_{1} - v_{2}) = \rho A v (v_{1} - v_{2})$$

### Step 4 — Apply Bernoulli's Equation Upstream and Downstream of the Rotor

Since both $v_1$ and $v_2$ are at the same atmospheric pressure $p_{atm}$, Bernoulli's equation between upstream and downstream of the rotor gives:

$$p_{atm} + \frac{1}{2} \rho v_{1}^{2} = p_{-} + \frac{1}{2} \rho v^{2} = p_{+} + \frac{1}{2} \rho v_{2}^{2}$$

where $p_{-}$ and $p_{+}$ are the pressures just upstream and downstream of the rotor disk. The pressure drop across the rotor is:

$$p_{-} - p_{+} = \frac{1}{2} \rho (v_{2}^{2} - v_{1}^{2})$$

### Step 5 — Force Exerted by the Rotor on the Air (from Pressure Drop)

The thrust force also equals the pressure drop times area:

$$F = (p_{-} - p_{+}) \cdot A = \frac{1}{2} \rho A (v_{1}^{2} - v_{2}^{2})$$

### Step 6 — Equate the Two Expressions for Force

From Steps 3 and 5:

$$\rho A v (v_{1} - v_{2}) = \frac{1}{2} \rho A (v_{1}^{2} - v_{2}^{2})$$

Divide both sides by $\rho A$ (non-zero):

$$v (v_{1} - v_{2}) = \frac{1}{2} (v_{1}^{2} - v_{2}^{2}) = \frac{1}{2} (v_{1} - v_{2})(v_{1} + v_{2})$$

### Step 7 — Solve for the Rotor Plane Velocity v

Since $v_{1} \neq v_{2}$ (otherwise no power is extracted), divide both sides by $(v_{1} - v_{2})$:

$$v = \frac{1}{2} (v_{1} + v_{2})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Result:** The wind speed at the rotor plane is the **arithmetic mean** of the upstream and downstream wind speeds. This is a fundamental result in actuator disk theory.

### Step 8 — Power Extracted by the Rotor

The power extracted equals the force on the rotor times the velocity at the rotor:

$$P_{extracted} = F \cdot v = \frac{1}{2} \rho A (v_{1}^{2} - v_{2}^{2}) \cdot v$$

Substitute $v = \tfrac{1}{2}(v_{1} + v_{2})$:

$$P_{extracted} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A (v_{1}^{2} - v_{2}^{2}) \cdot \frac{1}{2}(v_{1} + v_{2})$$

Factor $(v_{1}^{2} - v_{2}^{2}) = (v_{1} - v_{2})(v_{1} + v_{2})$:

$$P_{extracted} = \frac{1}{4} \rho A (v_{1} - v_{2})(v_{1} + v_{2})^{2}$$

### Step 9 — Define the Induction Factor (a)

Define the **axial induction factor** $a$ such that:

$$v = v_{1}(1 - a)$$

This means the rotor slows the wind to a fraction $(1 - a)$ of its upstream value. By momentum conservation, the downstream velocity is:

$$v_{2} = v_{1}(1 - 2a)$$

This follows from $v = \tfrac{1}{2}(v_1 + v_2)$ ⇒ $v_2 = 2v - v_1 = 2v_1(1-a) - v_1 = v_1(1 - 2a)$.

### Step 10 — Express Power Extracted in Terms of a

Substitute into the power equation:

$$P_{extracted} = \frac{1}{4} \rho A \cdot v_{1}[(1-a) - (1-2a)] \cdot v_{1}^{2}[(1-a) + (1-2a)]^{2}$$

Simplify term-by-term:

- $v_1 - v_2 = v_1[(1-a) - (1-2a)] = v_1 \cdot a$
- $v_1 + v_2 = v_1[(1-a) + (1-2a)] = v_1(2 - 3a)$

Therefore:

$$P_{extracted} = \frac{1}{4} \rho A \cdot (a v_{1}) \cdot v_{1}^{2}(2 - 3a)^{2} = \frac{1}{4} \rho A v_{1}^{3} \cdot a(2 - 3a)^{2}$$

### Step 11 — Write the Power Coefficient C_p

The power coefficient is the ratio of extracted power to available wind power:

$$C_{p} = \frac{P_{extracted}}{P_{wind}} = \frac{\tfrac{1}{4} \rho A v_{1}^{3} \cdot a(2 - 3a)^{2}}{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho A v_{1}^{3}} = \frac{a(2 - 3a)^{2}}{2}$$

$$\boxed{C_{p}(a) = \frac{1}{2} a (2 - 3a)^{2}}$$

### Step 12 — Maximize C_p with Respect to a

Take the derivative and set to zero:

$$\frac{dC_{p}}{da} = \frac{1}{2} \left[ (2 - 3a)^{2} + a \cdot 2(2 - 3a)(-3) \right] = 0$$

Simplify:

$$\frac{dC_{p}}{da} = \frac{1}{2} (2 - 3a)\left[(2 - 3a) - 6a\right] = \frac{1}{2}(2 - 3a)(2 - 9a) = 0$$

Either $2 - 3a = 0$ (which gives $a = 2/3$ — but this would stop the rotor and is unphysical) or:

$$2 - 9a = 0 \Rightarrow a = \frac{2}{9}$$

### Step 13 — Substitute a = 2/9 Back into C_p

$$C_{p,\max} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{2}{9} \cdot \left(2 - 3 \cdot \frac{2}{9}\right)^{2}$$

Compute inside the bracket:

$$2 - \frac{6}{9} = 2 - \frac{2}{3} = \frac{6 - 2}{3} = \frac{4}{3}$$

So:

$$C_{p,\max} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{2}{9} \cdot \left(\frac{4}{3}\right)^{2} = \frac{1}{9} \cdot \frac{16}{9} = \frac{16}{81}$$

Wait — let me re-check the algebra. The standard derivation yields $C_{p,\max} = 16/27$. Let me re-examine the steps.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Correction to the standard derivation:** In the power equation, the correct expression is $P = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v (v_1^2 - v_2^2)$, not $\frac{1}{4}$. Let me redo cleanly:

Correct derivation:
- $F = \frac{1}{2} \rho A (v_1^2 - v_2^2)$
- $P = F \cdot v = \frac{1}{2} \rho A (v_1^2 - v_2^2) \cdot v$
- With $v = \frac{1}{2}(v_1+v_2)$: $P = \frac{1}{4} \rho A (v_1^2 - v_2^2)(v_1+v_2) = \frac{1}{4} \rho A (v_1-v_2)(v_1+v_2)^2$

So $P_{extracted} = \frac{1}{4} \rho A (v_1-v_2)(v_1+v_2)^2$

The available power is $P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v_1^3$

$$C_p = \frac{\tfrac{1}{4}(v_1-v_2)(v_1+v_2)^2}{\tfrac{1}{2} v_1^3} = \frac{(v_1-v_2)(v_1+v_2)^2}{2 v_1^3}$$

Using $v_1 - v_2 = 2a v_1$ and $v_1 + v_2 = v_1(2-2a)$:

$$C_p = \frac{(2a v_1)(v_1(2-2a))^2}{2 v_1^3} = \frac{2a v_1 \cdot v_1^2 (2-2a)^2}{2 v_1^3} = a(2-2a)^2 = 4a(1-a)^2$$

Now maximize $C_p = 4a(1-a)^2$:

$$\frac{dC_p}{da} = 4[(1-a)^2 + a \cdot 2(1-a)(-1)] = 4(1-a)[(1-a) - 2a] = 4(1-a)(1-3a) = 0$$

So $a = 1/3$ (the non-trivial solution).

Substitute $a = 1/3$:

$$C_{p,\max} = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{4}{3} \cdot \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{9} = \frac{16}{27}$$

$$\boxed{C_{p,\max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926 \text{ or } 59.3\%}$$

This is the **Betz Limit**.

### Step 14 — Downstream Velocity at Betz Limit

$$v_2 = v_1(1 - 2a) = v_1\left(1 - \frac{2}{3}\right) = \frac{v_1}{3}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **This confirms the earlier analogy:** the air exits the rotor at exactly **one-third** of its upstream speed. If it were reduced to zero (100 % extraction), no air could leave — a physical contradiction.

### Step 15 — Maximum Extractable Power (Betz Power)

$$P_{Betz} = \frac{16}{27} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3} = \frac{8}{27} \rho A v^{3} \quad [\text{Watts}]$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A HAWT has a rotor diameter of 80 m. Wind speed at hub height is 12 m/s. Air density is 1.225 kg/m³. Calculate: (a) the total power available in the wind, (b) the maximum extractable power at Betz limit, and (c) the actual power extracted if $C_p = 0.40$.

### Solution:

**Given:** $D = 80$ m ⇒ $R = 40$ m, $v = 12$ m/s, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³.

**Swept area:**
$$A = \pi R^{2} = \pi \cdot (40)^{2} = 1600\pi \approx 5026.55 \text{ m}^{2}$$

**(a) Power in wind:**
$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 1.225 \cdot 5026.55 \cdot (12)^{3}$$

Compute step by step:
- $v^3 = 12^3 = 1728$ m³/s³
- $\frac{1}{2} \cdot 1.225 = 0.6125$
- $0.6125 \cdot 5026.55 \approx 3079.76$
- $3079.76 \cdot 1728 \approx 5,321,825$ W

$$\boxed{P_{wind} \approx 5.32 \text{ MW}}$$

**(b) Betz Maximum Power:**
$$P_{Betz} = \frac{16}{27} \cdot 5.32 \text{ MW} \approx 0.5926 \cdot 5.32 \approx 3.15 \text{ MW}$$

**(c) Actual Power at C_p = 0.40:**
$$P_{actual} = 0.40 \cdot 5.32 \text{ MW} \approx 2.13 \text{ MW}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Realism:** A real 80 m rotor turbine in 12 m/s wind typically produces around 2–2.5 MW, which matches our C_p = 0.40 estimate. Modern utility turbines achieve C_p = 0.45–0.50 at optimal TSR.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Variables, Units & Typical Ranges

| Variable | Symbol | SI Unit | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Air density | $\rho$ | kg/m³ | 1.0 – 1.4 |
| Rotor radius | $R$ | m | 1 – 100 |
| Swept area | $A$ | m² | 3 – 30,000 |
| Wind speed | $v$ | m/s | 3 – 25 |
| Power coefficient | $C_p$ | dimensionless | 0 – 0.593 (max 16/27) |
| Tip speed ratio | $\lambda$ | dimensionless | 1 – 10 |
| Rotor speed | $\omega$ | rad/s | 0.5 – 4 |
| Tip speed | $v_{tip} = \omega R$ | m/s | 30 – 90 |
| Cut-in / Rated / Cut-out | — | m/s | 3 / 12 / 25 (typical) |
| Thrust coefficient | $C_T$ | dimensionless | 0 – 0.89 (Betz) |
