# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The following questions follow the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern** for a 14-mark module question with internal choice. Model answers are **complete and examiner-ready**. Distribution of marks follows KTU convention.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]

**Q: Define a Horizontal Axis Wind Turbine (HAWT). List any two of its advantages over a Vertical Axis Wind Turbine (VAWT).**

**Model Answer:**

> A **Horizontal Axis Wind Turbine (HAWT)** is a wind turbine in which the **rotor shaft (main axis) is oriented horizontally** — i.e., parallel to the ground — and the rotor blades rotate in a vertical plane that is perpendicular to the wind direction. The generator and gearbox are housed in a **nacelle** mounted on top of a tall tower, and a **yaw mechanism** keeps the rotor pointed into the wind.

**Two advantages of HAWT over VAWT:**
1. **Higher aerodynamic efficiency** — modern 3-bladed HAWTs achieve power coefficients (C_p) of 0.40–0.50, significantly higher than the typical 0.15–0.30 of VAWTs.
2. **Lower cut-in wind speed** — HAWTs start generating power at lower wind speeds, making them more productive in typical wind regimes.

*(Any two valid advantages from the HAWT/VAWT comparison table may be awarded full marks.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern]

**Q: State Betz's Law. What is the value of the Betz coefficient, and what does it physically signify?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Betz's Law** states that *no wind turbine, regardless of design, can convert more than **16/27 (≈ 59.3 %)** of the kinetic energy of the wind into useful mechanical work at the rotor shaft.*

> The factor **16/27 ≈ 0.5926** is called the **Betz coefficient** or the **maximum power coefficient (C_p,max)**. It is a **purely theoretical upper bound** derived from the conservation of mass, momentum, and energy applied to an ideal actuator disc.

> **Physical significance:** If a turbine were to extract 100 % of the wind's kinetic energy, the air would have to come to a complete stop behind the rotor. This would create a "blockage" preventing new air from entering — a physical contradiction. The optimum is achieved when the air exits at exactly **one-third** of its upstream velocity, yielding the 16/27 limit. Real turbines operate below this limit, with C_p values typically between 0.35 and 0.50.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE convention, students answer **one full question** of 14 marks from a choice of two. Each question is divided into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / June 2024 Pattern]

**Q: (a)** Derive an expression for the power available in the wind passing through a swept area $A$ at velocity $v$. Explain why the dependence is cubic and not quadratic. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wind turbine has a rotor diameter of 60 m and operates at a wind speed of 10 m/s. The air density is 1.225 kg/m³. Calculate: (i) the total power available in the wind, (ii) the maximum extractable power at Betz limit, and (iii) the actual power output if the power coefficient C_p = 0.45. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Define the air mass flow rate (1 mark):**
Consider a stream of air flowing perpendicularly through a cross-sectional area $A$ with velocity $v$. The **volume flow rate** is $A \cdot v$ (m³/s). The **mass flow rate** is the volume flow rate multiplied by the air density $\rho$:

$$\dot{m} = \rho A v \quad [\text{kg/s}]$$

**Step 2 — Kinetic energy of a moving body (1 mark):**
The kinetic energy of a body of mass $m$ moving at velocity $v$ is:

$$E_{k} = \frac{1}{2} m v^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Power = rate of energy flow (2 marks):**
Power is energy per unit time. Substituting the mass flow rate $\dot{m}$ in place of $m$:

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \dot{m} \cdot v^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot (\rho A v) \cdot v^{2}$$

$$\boxed{P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3} \quad [\text{Watts}]}$$

**Step 4 — Why cubic, not quadratic? (3 marks):**
The dependence is **cubic** because the power depends on **two factors, both of which depend on velocity**:
- The **mass flow rate** itself increases **linearly** with $v$ (more air passes per second).
- The **kinetic energy per unit mass** increases **quadratically** with $v$ ($\tfrac{1}{2}v^2$).
- The product of these two factors gives a **cubic** dependence: $v \cdot v^{2} = v^{3}$.

This is why **doubling the wind speed yields 8× the power** — a critical insight for wind farm site selection.

---

#### Model Answer — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Rotor diameter $D = 60$ m ⇒ Radius $R = 30$ m
- Wind speed $v = 10$ m/s
- Air density $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³
- Power coefficient $C_p = 0.45$

**Step 1 — Swept area (1 mark):**
$$A = \pi R^{2} = \pi \times (30)^{2} = 900\pi \approx 2827.43 \text{ m}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Total power available in the wind (2 marks):**
$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 2827.43 \times (10)^{3}$$
$$P_{wind} = 0.6125 \times 2827.43 \times 1000 = 1,731,801 \text{ W} \approx 1.732 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum extractable power (Betz limit) (2 marks):**
$$P_{Betz} = \frac{16}{27} \times P_{wind} = 0.5926 \times 1.732 \text{ MW} \approx 1.027 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 4 — Actual power output (2 marks):**
$$P_{actual} = C_{p} \times P_{wind} = 0.45 \times 1.732 \text{ MW} \approx 0.779 \text{ MW} = 779 \text{ kW}$$

**Final Summary:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Swept Area A | 2827.43 m² |
| Power in wind | 1.732 MW |
| Betz max power | 1.027 MW |
| Actual power (C_p = 0.45) | 0.779 MW |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Model Question]

**Q: (a)** With the help of a clear block diagram, explain the construction and working of a modern Horizontal Axis Wind Turbine (HAWT). Compare HAWT and VAWT on the basis of any four parameters. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define a Wind Power Curve. Sketch and explain its different operating regions, clearly indicating the cut-in, rated, and cut-out wind speeds. Why is the power held constant between rated and cut-out wind speeds? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — HAWT Construction (3 marks):**

A modern Horizontal Axis Wind Turbine (HAWT) consists of the following major components:
- **Rotor with three blades** (typically composite-fiberglass or carbon-fiber) — designed aerodynamically with a twist along the span.
- **Hub** — connects the blades to the main shaft and contains the **pitch control mechanism** to vary blade angle.
- **Main (low-speed) shaft** — rotates at rotor speed (10–20 rpm for utility turbines).
- **Gearbox** — steps up the rotation speed to ~1500 rpm suitable for the generator.
- **Generator** — usually a doubly-fed induction generator (DFIG) or permanent magnet synchronous generator (PMSG).
- **Nacelle** — the housing on top of the tower that contains all the above components.
- **Yaw system** — motor and drive that rotates the nacelle to face the wind.
- **Tower** — typically 80–120 m tall, tubular steel or concrete.
- **Control system** — anemometer, wind vane, SCADA, and pitch/yaw controllers.

**Step 2 — Working (2 marks):**
Wind strikes the rotor blades, generating aerodynamic lift that causes the rotor to spin. The rotor transfers torque to the gearbox, which steps up the speed. The high-speed shaft drives the generator, which produces electricity. The output is conditioned by power electronics and fed to the grid via a step-up transformer. The yaw motor continuously orients the nacelle into the wind, and the pitch system adjusts blade angle to control power.

**Step 3 — HAWT vs. VAWT comparison (2 marks):**

| Parameter | HAWT | VAWT |
|---|---|---|
| Yaw mechanism | Required | Not required |
| Power coefficient (C_p) | High (0.35–0.50) | Low (0.15–0.30) |
| Maintenance access | Difficult (nacelle high) | Easy (ground level) |
| Wind direction | Unidirectional | Omnidirectional |

*(Any four valid parameters accepted.)*

---

#### Model Answer — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Wind Power Curve Definition (1 mark):**

> The **Wind Power Curve** is a graphical representation of the **electrical power output** of a wind turbine as a function of the **free-stream wind speed** at hub height, measured under standard conditions (air density 1.225 kg/m³, horizontal, non-turbulent flow).

**Step 2 — Operating Regions (4 marks):**

The wind power curve is divided into **five regions**:

| Region | Wind Speed Range | Power Output | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $v < v_{cut-in}$ | 0 W | Turbine idle; not enough wind to overcome losses |
| 1 | $v_{cut-in} \leq v < v_{rated}$ | Rises cubically (P ∝ v³) | Partial load operation; torque control active |
| 2 | $v_{rated} \leq v < v_{cut-out}$ | Constant at P_rated | Full load; pitch control limits power |
| 3 | $v \geq v_{cut-out}$ | 0 W (shut down) | Braking engaged; turbine safe-mode |
| 4 | $v \geq v_{survival}$ | 0 W (parked) | Survival mode; blades fully feathered |

**Step 3 — Why Power is Held Constant Between Rated and Cut-out (2 marks):**

> Between the **rated wind speed** (e.g., 12 m/s) and the **cut-out wind speed** (e.g., 25 m/s), the available wind power would theoretically increase as $v^3$, exceeding the generator's rated capacity and risking **mechanical overload, electrical damage, and structural failure**. To prevent this, the **pitch control system** rotates the blades about their longitudinal axis to **"spill" excess aerodynamic energy** — effectively reducing the angle of attack so the blades capture only enough power to maintain the rated output. This protects the gearbox, generator, and tower structure from overloading during high-wind conditions.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Wind Energy Problems (Examiner's Perspective):**
> 1. **Forgetting the cube on $v$** — Writing $P = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^{2}$ instead of $v^{3}$ is the **single most common error** in KTU answers. This can cost up to 3–4 marks.
> 2. **Using diameter instead of radius** — The swept area formula is $A = \pi R^{2}$, NOT $A = \pi D^{2}$. Forgetting to halve the diameter loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Incorrect unit conversion for wind speed** — Many KTU problems quote wind speed in **km/h**. The conversion is $v_{m/s} = v_{km/h} / 3.6$. Forgetting this is a guaranteed mark loss.
> 4. **Confusing Betz's coefficient with turbine efficiency** — Betz's 59.3 % is the **absolute theoretical ceiling**, NOT a typical value. Real turbines achieve 35–50 %.
> 5. **Skipping the induction-factor derivation** — In Betz derivation questions, if you do not define and use the axial induction factor $a$ (or the equivalent $v = (v_1 + v_2)/2$ step), you will not get the 16/27 result and may lose 3–4 marks.
> 6. **Power curve regions mis-ordered** — Students often mix up cut-in and cut-out, or forget to mention the **survival region**. Ensure you know all five regions in order.
> 7. **Forgetting units in the final answer** — Always state the unit (W, kW, or MW). Numerical answers without units lose 0.5–1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this section for last-minute revision before the KTU ESE.**

**Core Concepts:**
- Wind energy = kinetic energy of moving air; converted to electricity via a wind turbine (WECS).
- **HAWT**: horizontal rotor axis, requires yaw, high C_p, utility-scale. **VAWT**: vertical rotor axis, no yaw, omnidirectional, lower C_p, urban/small-scale.
- HAWT sub-types: Upwind (most common), Downwind, Dutch, Multi-blade (American).
- VAWT sub-types: Savonius (drag), Darrieus (lift), Giromill (H-rotor).

**Key Formulas (Memorize):**
- $P_{wind} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3}$ (W)
- $A = \pi R^{2}$ (m²)
- $P_{turbine} = C_p \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3}$ (W)
- $C_{p,\max} = \tfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926$ (Betz Limit, 59.3 %)
- $P_{Betz,\max} = \tfrac{8}{27} \rho A v^{3}$ (W)
- $\lambda = \tfrac{\omega R}{v} = \tfrac{\pi R n}{30 v}$ (TSR, dimensionless)
- $\rho_{std} = 1.225$ kg/m³ (at sea level, 15 °C)
- Conversion: $v_{m/s} = v_{km/h} / 3.6$

**Wind Power Curve — Five Regions:**
- Region 0: Below cut-in (no power).
- Region 1: Cut-in to rated (cubic rise).
- Region 2: Rated to cut-out (constant rated power).
- Region 3: Above cut-out (shutdown).
- Region 4: Survival (parked, blades feathered).

**Betz's Law — Key Insights:**
- Derived from conservation of mass and momentum (actuator disc theory).
- Maximum C_p occurs at induction factor $a = 1/3$.
- At Betz limit, downstream wake velocity is $v_2 = v_1/3$.
- Betz limit is design-independent — applies to ALL wind turbines.

**Numerical Problem Solving — Standard Steps:**
1. Identify $R$ or $D$ and compute $A = \pi R^2$.
2. Apply $P_{wind} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^3$.
3. Apply $P_{turbine} = C_p \times P_{wind}$ (or $P_{Betz} = 16/27 \times P_{wind}$).
4. State final answer with units.

**High-Yield Keywords for KTU Answers:**
- Actuator disc model
- Stream tube
- Axial induction factor
- Yaw control, Pitch control
- Cut-in, Rated, Cut-out, Survival wind speeds
- Tip speed ratio (TSR)
- Power coefficient (C_p)
- Thrust coefficient (C_T)
- Weibull distribution
- Capacity factor
- Annual Energy Production (AEP)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tidal Energy - Principle of Tidal Power
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET742 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES AND RENEWABLE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Tidal Energy - Principle of Tidal Power

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tidal Energy** is a form of **Renewable Ocean Energy** that harnesses the kinetic and potential energy associated with the periodic rise (flood) and fall (ebb) of sea water levels caused by the **gravitational interaction** between the Earth, the Moon, and the Sun.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, tidal energy is broadly classified into two harvesting categories:

1. **Tidal Range Energy** (Potential Energy) — Captured from the vertical head difference between high and low tides using a barrage or lagoon.
2. **Tidal Stream Energy** (Kinetic Energy) — Captured from horizontal water currents using submerged turbines (similar to underwater wind turbines).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Ocean as a Slow Battery

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine the ocean as a *giant, slow-charging battery* that the Moon charges twice a day. Every ~12 hours and 25 minutes, the Moon pulls billions of tons of seawater upwards on one side of the Earth and creates a balancing bulge on the opposite side. When this water is trapped behind a dam and released through a turbine, it generates clean electricity — just like letting water out of a hydroelectric reservoir, except the "reservoir" refills itself predictably thanks to celestial mechanics.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following standard physical constants and engineering metrics are **essential** for tidal power calculations:
>
> - **Density of seawater:** $\rho \approx 1025\ \text{kg/m}^3$
> - **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$
> - **Mean tidal period (semi-diurnal):** $T \approx 12\ \text{h}\ 25\ \text{min} = 44700\ \text{s}$
> - **Mean tidal period (full cycle, high-to-high):** $T \approx 24\ \text{h}\ 50\ \text{min} = 89400\ \text{s}$
> - **Mean tidal range (global average):** $R \approx 0.5 - 1.0\ \text{m}$
> - **Viable tidal range for power plants:** $R \geq 5\ \text{m}$ (economically feasible)
> - **Gravitational constant:** $G = 6.674 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{N·m}^2/\text{kg}^2$

### 1.4 Origin of Tides — Why the Sea Level Changes

Tides are produced primarily by the **differential gravitational pull** of the Moon and the Sun on different parts of the Earth. The Moon is dominant because, despite being far smaller in mass, it is much closer to the Earth.

The tidal acceleration produced by the Moon is given by:

$$a_{\text{tidal}} = \frac{2 \cdot G \cdot M_{\text{moon}} \cdot R_{\text{Earth}}}{d^3}$$

where $d$ is the Earth–Moon distance ($\approx 3.844 \times 10^8\ \text{m}$) and $R_{\text{Earth}}$ is the Earth's radius.

> [!NOTE]
> The **Sun's tidal force is ~46% of the Moon's** tidal force. When Sun, Earth, and Moon align (new moon / full moon), the forces add up producing **Spring Tides** (largest range). When they form a right angle (quarter moons), the forces partially cancel producing **Neap Tides** (smallest range).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Tidal Patterns

- **Semi-Diurnal Tides:** Two high tides and two low tides of nearly equal amplitude every lunar day (most common, e.g., Atlantic coast of Europe).
- **Diurnal Tides:** One high tide and one low tide per lunar day (rare, e.g., parts of the Gulf of Mexico).
- **Mixed Semi-Diurnal Tides:** Two unequal high and low tides per day (e.g., Indian coast, Pacific coast of North America).

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Behind Tidal Power Generation

- **Step 1 — Storage:** During flood tide, water fills an enclosed coastal basin through sluice gates, raising the water level inside the basin.
- **Step 2 — Head Creation:** When high tide is reached, sluice gates are closed. As the sea level outside drops during ebb tide, a difference in water level (head, $H$) is created.
- **Step 3 — Energy Conversion:** The trapped water is released through low-head **bulb or Kaplan turbines** coupled to generators, producing electricity.
- **Step 4 — Reverse Pumping (optional):** In two-way systems, turbines can be reversed at the next flood tide to extract energy again.

### 2.3 Why Use Low-Head Kaplan Turbines?

> [!NOTE]
> Unlike conventional hydropower (heads of 50–500 m), tidal heads are typically only **3–10 m**. Therefore, **bulb-type Kaplan turbines** with large diameter and adjustable blades are used to extract maximum power at low heads with high discharge.

### 2.4 Tidal Power Plant Components

- **Barrage / Dam:** A barrier built across an estuary to impound water and create a head difference.
- **Sluice Gates:** Large gates to allow free flow during filling and emptying phases (minimizing head loss).
- **Bulb / Kaplan Turbines:** Reversible low-head turbines for energy conversion.
- **Generators:** Synchronous generators (usually coupled via a gearbox or direct-drive).
- **Transformers and Switchyard:** To step up voltage for grid transmission.
- **Navigational Locks:** To allow ship passage.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility and Engineering Significance

- Tidal energy is **highly predictable** (unlike solar and wind) — tides can be forecast centuries in advance.
- Power plant **lifespan is 75–100 years**, much longer than wind or solar installations.
- Carbon emissions are near zero during operation.
- It complements other renewables by providing **base-load-like firm power**.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes **all KTU 2024 high-yield formulas** for Tidal Energy. Note: $\vert$ symbols are written as `\vert` to preserve table syntax.

| **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tidal Potential Energy | $E_p = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot R^2$ | Energy stored in tidal basin (between high and low tide) | Joules (J) |
| Average Tidal Power | $P_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{E_p}{T}$ | Average power over a tidal period | Watts (W) |
| Tidal Period (semi-diurnal) | $T = 12\ \text{h}\ 25\ \text{min} = 44700$ | One high-to-low or low-to-high cycle | seconds (s) |
| Tidal Period (full cycle) | $T = 24\ \text{h}\ 50\ \text{min} = 89400$ | One high-to-high cycle | seconds (s) |
| Tidal Prism Volume | $V = A \cdot R$ | Total volume of water exchanged | m³ |
| Tidal Stream Power (per unit area) | $P/A = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot v^3$ | Available kinetic power density | W/m² |
| Tidal Stream Power (extracted) | $P = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot v^3 \cdot C_p$ | Extracted power with coefficient $C_p$ | W |
| Maximum Power Coefficient (Betz) | $C_{p,\max} = \tfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$ | Theoretical upper limit | dimensionless |
| Gravitational Tidal Acceleration | $a_{\text{tidal}} = \dfrac{2 \cdot G \cdot M \cdot R_E}{d^3}$ | Tidal acceleration due to a celestial body | m/s² |
| Spring Tidal Range | $R_{\text{spring}} = R_{\text{moon}} + R_{\text{sun}}$ | Constructive interference (Moon + Sun) | meters (m) |
| Neap Tidal Range | $R_{\text{neap}} = R_{\text{moon}} - R_{\text{sun}}$ | Destructive interference (Moon − Sun) | meters (m) |
| Turbine Discharge | $Q = A_{\text{turbine}} \cdot v_{\text{water}}$ | Volume flow rate through turbine | m³/s |
| Electrical Energy per Cycle | $E = P_{\text{avg}} \cdot T$ | Energy generated per tidal cycle | Wh or J |

> **Key Engineering Reality:** The above formulas assume **ideal conditions** (no friction, no leakage, perfect head). In practice, real-world plant efficiency is **$\eta \approx 0.20 - 0.30$** of the theoretical maximum. Always multiply theoretical values by an **overall plant efficiency** in KTU exam numerical problems.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Potential Energy of a Tidal Basin

**Problem Setup:** Consider a coastal tidal basin of surface area $A$ (m²) and tidal range $R$ (m). We want to find the total potential energy released when the water level in the basin drops from its maximum to its mean level (or equivalently, when it rises from mean to maximum).

**Conceptual Foundation:** The potential energy stored in a body of water is the work done by gravity as that water falls through a vertical height. For a thin horizontal slice of water of thickness $dh$ at a height $h$ above the lowest level, the mass is $\rho \cdot A \cdot dh$, and its potential energy (relative to the bottom) is:

$$dE = (\rho \cdot A \cdot dh) \cdot g \cdot h$$

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

Step 1 — Set up the differential energy expression. The energy contributed by a thin horizontal slice of water at height $h$ above the mean level is:

$$dE = \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot h \cdot dh$$

Step 2 — Integrate from $h = 0$ (lowest water level, i.e., mean low tide) to $h = R$ (highest water level, i.e., mean high tide):

$$E_p = \int_{0}^{R} \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot h \cdot dh$$

Step 3 — Perform the integration. The antiderivative of $h$ is $\tfrac{h^2}{2}$:

$$E_p = \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot \left[ \frac{h^2}{2} \right]_{0}^{R}$$

Step 4 — Apply the limits of integration:

$$E_p = \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot \left( \frac{R^2}{2} - \frac{0^2}{2} \right)$$

Step 5 — Simplify to obtain the **final closed-form expression**:

$$\boxed{E_p = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot R^2}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Notice the quadratic dependence on $R$. This means **doubling the tidal range quadruples the energy output**. This is why tidal plants are viable only at sites with exceptionally high tidal ranges (e.g., Gulf of Kutch in India, Bay of Fundy in Canada, La Rance in France).

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Average Tidal Power

**Conceptual Foundation:** The average power is simply the total energy divided by the time over which it is delivered.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

Step 1 — Use the potential energy from Derivation 1:

$$E_p = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot R^2$$

Step 2 — Divide by the tidal period $T$. For a single fill-to-empty cycle, $T = 44700$ s (semi-diurnal). For a complete cycle (high-to-high), $T = 89400$ s.

Step 3 — Write the average power expression:

$$P_{\text{avg}} = \frac{E_p}{T}$$

Step 4 — Substitute the energy expression:

$$\boxed{P_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot R^2}{2 \cdot T}}$$

Step 5 — Include the overall plant efficiency $\eta$ for real-world applications:

$$P_{\text{actual}} = \eta \cdot \frac{\rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot R^2}{2 \cdot T}$$

> **Real-World Utility:** This formula is used in **site feasibility studies** for proposed tidal power plants. Engineers first measure the local tidal range $R$ and basin area $A$ from satellite data, then plug these values in to estimate theoretical power output. The actual output is obtained by multiplying by an efficiency factor (typically 20–30%).

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Energy Extracted Per Tidal Cycle (Two-Way Generation)

**Conceptual Foundation:** A two-way (double-effect) tidal plant generates power during **both** flood and ebb tides. The total energy extracted per full cycle (high → low → high) is approximately twice the single-effect value, but with reduced head in each direction.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

Step 1 — Energy generated during ebb (high → low) tide:

$$E_{\text{ebb}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot R^2 \cdot \eta$$

Step 2 — Energy generated during flood (low → high) tide (similar magnitude):

$$E_{\text{flood}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot R^2 \cdot \eta$$

Step 3 — Total energy per full cycle (high → high):

$$E_{\text{total}} = E_{\text{ebb}} + E_{\text{flood}} = \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot R^2 \cdot \eta$$

Step 4 — Average power per full cycle (using $T = 89400$ s):

$$\boxed{P_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot R^2 \cdot \eta}{T}}$$

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A tidal power plant has a basin area of $A = 1.0 \times 10^6\ \text{m}^2$ and an average tidal range of $R = 8\ \text{m}$. Calculate:
> (a) the total potential energy available per cycle,
> (b) the average power output assuming an overall plant efficiency of $\eta = 0.25$ and a semi-diurnal tidal period of $T = 44700\ \text{s}$.

**Given Data:**

$$A = 1.0 \times 10^6\ \text{m}^2, \quad R = 8\ \text{m}, \quad \rho = 1025\ \text{kg/m}^3, \quad g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2, \quad \eta = 0.25, \quad T = 44700\ \text{s}$$

**Part (a) — Potential Energy per Cycle:**

Step 1 — Apply the formula $E_p = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot R^2$:

$$E_p = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 1025 \cdot 9.81 \cdot (1.0 \times 10^6) \cdot (8)^2$$

Step 2 — Compute intermediate values:

$$E_p = 0.5 \cdot 1025 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 10^6 \cdot 64$$

Step 3 — Evaluate the product:

$$E_p = \frac{1025 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 64 \cdot 10^6}{2}$$

$$E_p = \frac{643,737,600 \times 10^6}{2}$$

$$\boxed{E_p = 3.219 \times 10^{11}\ \text{J} \approx 321.9\ \text{GJ}}$$

**Part (b) — Average Power Output:**

Step 1 — Use the formula $P_{\text{avg}} = \eta \cdot \dfrac{E_p}{T}$:

$$P_{\text{avg}} = 0.25 \cdot \frac{3.219 \times 10^{11}}{44700}$$

Step 2 — Compute the division:

$$P_{\text{avg}} = 0.25 \cdot 7.20 \times 10^6$$

Step 3 — Final result:

$$\boxed{P_{\text{avg}} = 1.80 \times 10^6\ \text{W} = 1.80\ \text{MW}}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** A 1 km² basin with an 8 m tidal range produces only ~1.8 MW of average power. This illustrates why tidal energy has a relatively **low energy density** compared to fossil fuels and why very large basin areas are needed for commercial-scale generation.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Tidal Power Plant Schemes

| **Scheme Type** | **Head Source** | **Conversion Method** | **Bidirectional?** | **Storage?** | **Environmental Impact** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Tidal Barrage | Vertical tidal range | Bulb / Kaplan turbines in dam | Yes (in double-effect) | Indirect (basin) | Moderate to High |
| Tidal Lagoon | Vertical tidal range (artificial) | Bulb turbines in perimeter wall | Yes | Yes (continuous) | Low to Moderate |
| Tidal Stream | Horizontal current | Submerged axial-flow turbines | No (unidirectional) | None | Low |
| Dynamic Tidal Power | Long coastal dam + phase difference | Kaplan turbines | Continuous | Continuous | High (experimental) |

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Tidal Power Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[OCEAN SIDE HIGH TIDE] --> B[SLUICE GATE OPEN DURING FLOOD]
    B --> C[TIDAL BASIN FILLS UP]
    C --> D[SLUICE GATE CLOSED AT HIGH TIDE]
    D --> E[OCEAN LEVEL FALLS DURING EBB]
    E --> F[HEAD DIFFERENTIAL CREATED H = 3 TO 10 M]
    F --> G[TURBINE GATE OPENS]
    G --> H[BULB KAPLAN TURBINE SPINS]
    H --> I[GENERATOR PRODUCES ELECTRICITY]
    I --> J[STEP UP TRANSFORMER]
    J --> K[TRANSMISSION LINE TO GRID]
    H --> L[REVERSE PUMPING MODE OPTIONAL]
    L --> C
    I --> M[GRID DISTRIBUTION NETWORK]
```

### 4.2 Functional Flow of Tidal Energy Conversion

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[MOON AND SUN GRAVITY] --> M2[OCEAN TIDAL RISE AND FALL]
    M2 --> M3[HEAD OR KINETIC ENERGY]
    M3 --> M4[HYDRAULIC TURBINE]
    M4 --> M5[ROTATIONAL MECHANICAL ENERGY]
    M5 --> M6[GENERATOR]
    M6 --> M7[ELECTRICAL ENERGY AC]
    M7 --> M8[TRANSFORMER]

```

### 4.3 Comparative Process Diagram — Single vs Double Effect

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[START OF CYCLE] --> P2{HIGH TIDE REACHED}
    P2 --> P3[CLOSE SLUICE GATES]
    P3 --> P4[WAIT FOR OCEAN TO FALL]
    P4 --> P5{HEAD EXCEEDS MINIMUM}
    P5 -- YES --> P6[OPEN TURBINE GATES EBB GENERATION]
    P5 -- NO --> P5
    P6 --> P7[LOW TIDE REACHED]
    P7 --> P8[OPEN SLUICE GATES REVERSE FLOW]
    P8 --> P9[FLOOD TIDE FILLS BASIN]
    P9 --> P10[CLOSE SLUICE GATES AGAIN]
    P10 --> P11[WAIT FOR OCEAN TO FALL]
    P11 --> P5
```

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
*Define tidal energy. List the three different patterns of tides observed around the world.*

**Model Answer:**

Tidal energy is a form of renewable ocean energy that is generated by exploiting the **periodic rise and fall of sea water levels** caused by the gravitational attraction of the Moon and the Sun on the Earth's oceans.

The three patterns of tides are:

- **Semi-Diurnal Tides:** Two high tides and two low tides of nearly equal amplitude per lunar day (most common).
- **Diurnal Tides:** Only one high tide and one low tide per lunar day.
- **Mixed Semi-Diurnal Tides:** Two high and two low tides per day, but with significant differences in amplitudes.

| Gravitational Force Effect | Constructive superposition — Sun and Moon pull in same direction | Destructive superposition — Sun and Moon pull in perpendicular directions |
| Tidal Range | Maximum (largest difference between high and low tide) | Minimum (smallest difference between high and low tide) |
| Frequency per Month | Twice (full moon and new moon) | Twice (first and third quarter) |

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **working principle of a tidal barrage power plant**. Discuss its main components and their functions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A tidal power plant has a basin area of $A = 2.5 \times 10^6\ \text{m}^2$ and a mean tidal range of $R = 6\ \text{m}$. Calculate the **(i)** total potential energy available per tidal cycle and **(ii)** the average power output, assuming an overall efficiency of 0.25 and a semi-diurnal tidal period of $T = 44700\ \text{s}$. Take $\rho = 1025\ \text{kg/m}^3$ and $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

A **tidal barrage** is a dam-like structure built across an estuary or bay that captures the difference in water level between the ocean and an enclosed basin to generate electricity.

**Components and Functions:**

- **Barrage Dam:** The main structure across the estuary that separates the basin from the open sea.
- **Sluice Gates:** Large gates on the barrage that are opened during flood tide to allow free inflow of seawater (minimizing head loss) and closed at high tide.
- **Bulb / Kaplan Turbines:** Low-head, reversible turbines installed in the barrage wall that convert the head of water into mechanical rotation.
- **Synchronous Generators:** Coupled to the turbines to produce AC electrical power.
- **Transformers and Switchyard:** Step up the generator voltage to transmission levels.
- **Navigational Locks:** Allow ships to cross the barrage safely.

**Working Principle:**

During **flood tide** (rising sea level), the sluice gates are opened, allowing the basin to fill freely with seawater. At **high tide**, the sluice gates are closed. As the sea level outside the barrage begins to drop during **ebb tide**, a water level difference (head, typically 3–10 m) develops across the barrage. Once the head exceeds the minimum operational value, the turbine gates are opened, and water flows from the basin back into the sea through the **Kaplan turbines**, spinning them and driving the generators.

In **double-effect (bidirectional)** plants, the turbines are also operated during flood tide, extracting energy in the reverse direction.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $A = 2.5 \times 10^6\ \text{m}^2$, $R = 6\ \text{m}$, $\eta = 0.25$, $T = 44700\ \text{s}$, $\rho = 1025\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$.

**Part (i) — Potential Energy:**

$$E_p = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot R^2$$

$$E_p = 0.5 \cdot 1025 \cdot 9.81 \cdot (2.5 \times 10^6) \cdot (6)^2$$

$$E_p = 0.5 \cdot 1025 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 2.5 \times 10^6 \cdot 36$$

$$E_p = 0.5 \cdot 905,128,500$$

$$\boxed{E_p = 4.526 \times 10^{8}\ \text{J} \approx 452.6\ \text{MJ}}$$

**Part (ii) — Average Power:**

$$P_{\text{avg}} = \eta \cdot \frac{E_p}{T} = 0.25 \cdot \frac{4.526 \times 10^8}{44700}$$

$$P_{\text{avg}} = 0.25 \cdot 1.0125 \times 10^4$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{avg}} = 2531\ \text{W} \approx 2.53\ \text{kW}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The very low power output (only ~2.5 kW) for such a large basin area confirms why tidal energy is **site-specific** and requires exceptionally large basins and high tidal ranges to be commercially viable.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **origin of tides** due to the gravitational interaction of the Earth, Moon, and Sun. Derive the expression for tidal acceleration. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **tidal stream energy conversion system**. Compare it with tidal barrage systems in terms of advantages and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Origin of Tides:**

Tides are the periodic rise and fall of sea levels caused by the **gravitational pull of the Moon and Sun** on the Earth's oceans. Although the Sun is much more massive, the Moon's tidal effect is about **2.2 times stronger** than the Sun's because the Moon is much closer to the Earth.

**Derivation of Tidal Acceleration:**

Consider a point P on the Earth's surface at distance $r$ from the center, with the Moon at distance $d$ from the Earth's center. The gravitational acceleration at point P due to the Moon is:

$$g_{\text{point}} = \frac{G \cdot M_{\text{moon}}}{(d - r)^2}$$

The gravitational acceleration at the center of the Earth is:

$$g_{\text{center}} = \frac{G \cdot M_{\text{moon}}}{d^2}$$

The **tidal acceleration** is the difference between these two:

$$a_{\text{tidal}} = g_{\text{point}} - g_{\text{center}} = \frac{G \cdot M_{\text{moon}}}{(d-r)^2} - \frac{G \cdot M_{\text{moon}}}{d^2}$$

Since $r \ll d$, we can use the binomial approximation $\dfrac{1}{(d-r)^2} \approx \dfrac{1}{d^2} \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{2r}{d}\right)$:

$$a_{\text{tidal}} = \frac{G \cdot M_{\text{moon}}}{d^2} \cdot \left[ \left(1 + \frac{2r}{d}\right) - 1 \right]$$

$$\boxed{a_{\text{tidal}} = \frac{2 \cdot G \cdot M_{\text{moon}} \cdot r}{d^3}}$$

This shows that the tidal force is **inversely proportional to the cube** of the distance from the disturbing body. Hence the Moon (closer) exerts a stronger tidal force than the Sun (farther), despite its much smaller mass.

**Model Solution for (b):**

A **tidal stream energy system** is similar to an underwater wind farm. It uses **submerged axial-flow turbines** (similar to wind turbines) placed in regions where tidal currents are fast and consistent.

**Block Diagram of Components:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[TIDAL CURRENT IN OCEAN] --> T2[GUIDE VANES DIRECT FLOW]
    T2 --> T3[ROTOR BLADES SPIN]
    T3 --> T4[GEARBOX OR DIRECT DRIVE]
    T4 --> T5[GENERATOR IN NACELLE]
    T5 --> T6[UNDERSEA CABLE]
    T6 --> T7[OFFSHORE SUBSTATION]
    T7 --> T8[GRID TRANSMISSION]
```

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Tidal Stream | Tidal Barrage |
|---|---|---|
| Energy Source | Kinetic (horizontal current) | Potential (vertical head) |
| Infrastructure | Submerged turbines + seabed mounts | Large dam across estuary |
| Capital Cost | Lower | Very High |
| Environmental Impact | Low (fish-friendly designs possible) | High (affects sediment, fish migration) |
| Visual Impact | None (fully submerged) | Major (visible dam) |
| Power Predictability | Excellent (twice-daily cycles) | Excellent |
| Commercial Maturity | Pilot stage | Mature (La Rance, Sihwa Lake) |
| Suitable Sites | Narrow straits, coastal channels | Wide estuaries with high tidal range |

**Advantages of Tidal Stream:**

- **Lower environmental disruption** — no dam required.
- **Modular installation** — turbines can be added incrementally.
- **Fish-friendly designs** — slow-rotating blades minimize harm.
- **No visual impact** — completely submerged.

**Limitations of Tidal Stream:**

- **Site-specific** — requires fast tidal currents (>2 m/s).
- **Underwater maintenance** is expensive and weather-dependent.
- **Marine growth (biofouling)** on blades reduces efficiency over time.
- **Unproven at large commercial scale** (most are <10 MW).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to square the tidal range $R$** — Students often write $E = \rho g A R$ instead of $E = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho g A R^2$. This **single mistake loses 3 marks** out of 7 in numerical problems. Always derive or re-check the formula before substituting values.
>
> 2. **Using the wrong tidal period** — $T = 12\ \text{h}\ 25\ \text{min} = 44700\ \text{s}$ for a single semi-diurnal cycle. Using $T = 24\ \text{hours}$ (solar day) instead of $T = 12.42\ \text{hours}$ (lunar half-day) is a very common error.
>
> 3. **Confusing the Betz limit formula** — Tidal stream power is proportional to $v^3$ (cube of velocity), NOT $v^2$. Markers specifically check this distinction.
>
> 4. **Omitting the efficiency factor $\eta$** — Theoretical energy ≠ actual energy. Real plants operate at $\eta = 0.20 - 0.30$. Examiners will deduct 1–2 marks if $\eta$ is ignored.
>
> 5. **Forgetting units in the final answer** — Always write the unit (W, kW, MW, J, GJ) explicitly. Marks are often awarded separately for correct units.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Tidal Energy: Principle of Tidal Power**
>
> - **Definition:** Tidal energy is harvested from the periodic rise and fall of seawater due to gravitational forces of the Moon and Sun.
> - **Tidal acceleration formula:** $a_{\text{tidal}} = \dfrac{2 G M r}{d^3}$ — varies inversely as the **cube of distance**.
> - **Moon's tidal force is ~2.2 times** stronger than the Sun's tidal force.
> - **Three tide patterns:** Semi-diurnal, Diurnal, Mixed semi-diurnal.
> - **Spring Tides:** Sun + Moon aligned → maximum tidal range.
> - **Neap Tides:** Sun + Moon at 90° → minimum tidal range.
> - **Tidal period (semi-diurnal):** 12 h 25 min = **44700 s**.
> - **Tidal period (full cycle):** 24 h 50 min = **89400 s**.
> - **Potential Energy of basin:** $E_p = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho g A R^2$ — quadratic in $R$.
> - **Average Power:** $P_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{\rho g A R^2}{2 T}$.
> - **Tidal stream power density:** $\tfrac{1}{2} \rho v^3$ — **cubic in velocity**.
> - **Betz limit for tidal turbines:** $C_{p,\max} = 0.593$.
> - **Minimum viable tidal range:** ~**5 m** (economic threshold).
> - **Seawater density:** $\rho = 1025\ \text{kg/m}^3$ (slightly higher than freshwater).
> - **Main turbine type:** Reversible **bulb / Kaplan turbine** (low-head, high-discharge).
> - **Three plant types:** Tidal Barrage, Tidal Lagoon, Tidal Stream.
> - **Major global sites:** La Rance (France), Sihwa Lake (South Korea), Bay of Fundy (Canada), Gulf of Kutch (India — proposed).
> - **Plant lifespan:** 75–100 years (longer than wind/solar).
> - **Tidal energy is predictable** centuries in advance (major advantage over solar/wind).
> - **Real plant efficiency:** 20–30% of theoretical maximum.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Components of Tidal Power Plant (TPP)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET742 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES AND RENEWABLE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Components of Tidal Power Plant (TPP)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 4 — Components of a Tidal Power Plant (TPP)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Tidal Power Plant (TPP)** is a renewable energy conversion installation that exploits the periodic rise (flood) and fall (ebb) of sea levels caused by the gravitational interaction between the Earth, Moon, and Sun, to drive low-head hydraulic turbines coupled to electrical generators. The **potential energy** stored between the high tide and low tide water levels in an enclosed coastal basin is converted, via a **tidal barrage** (or **tidal fence / tidal stream** device), into **mechanical shaft power** and finally into **grid-grade electrical energy**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEAET742 — Module 4):** Students must clearly distinguish between the **three (3) architectural variants** of TPP:
> 1. **Tidal Barrage Plant** (uses a dam across an estuary; potential energy conversion).
> 2. **Tidal Stream Plant** (no dam; kinetic energy conversion in open water).
> 3. **Tidal Lagoon Plant** (artificial offshore impoundment; potential energy).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (The "Bathtub Power Plant" Picture)

Imagine your home **overhead water tank**. The plumber fills it to a high level at night. During the day, the water flows down through a small turbine to run a tiny generator that charges your phone. That turbine does not "create" water — it harvests the **height difference** between the tank (high) and the tap (low).

A tidal power plant operates on the **exact same principle**, except the "tank" is the ocean itself:
- The **Moon** acts as the "plumber", lifting millions of tonnes of seawater twice a day to a high level (high tide).
- The **coastal barrage** acts as the "tank wall", trapping this elevated water.
- The **sluice gate** acts as the "tap", releasing the trapped water through a turbine when the outside sea level drops (low tide).
- The **turbine + generator** acts as the phone charger, converting falling water into electricity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Tidal power is essentially **stored solar gravitational energy** — it is inexhaustible on human timescales, with a **tidal cycle of ≈ 12 hours 25 minutes** (two high + two low tides per lunar day).

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics (Board Exam Essentials)

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Density of seawater | $\rho$ | **1025** (often rounded to 1030) | kg/m³ |
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81** | m/s² |
| Tidal range (typical) | $h$ | 5 – 10 (utilisable ≥ 5) | m |
| Turbine efficiency | $\eta_t$ | 0.85 – 0.90 | — |
| Generator efficiency | $\eta_g$ | 0.95 – 0.98 | — |
| Combined plant efficiency | $\eta_{overall}$ | **0.30 – 0.45** | — |
| Capacity factor (TPP) | $CF$ | 0.25 – 0.40 | — |

> [!TIP]
> **Board Tip:** A tidal plant is only **technically and economically viable** when the average tidal range exceeds **5 metres**. Below this, the extractable head is too small to justify the civil-engineering cost of the barrage.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Anatomy — The Six (6) Major Subsystems of a TPP

A Tidal Power Plant is not just a "dam + turbine". KTU examiners expect a **system-level breakdown** into the following functional blocks:

### A. Civil / Structural Block
1. **Tidal Barrage (Dam)**
   - Massive concrete/stone barrier built across a tidal estuary or bay.
   - Holds back water at high tide, releases it at low tide to create a head difference.
   - Houses the powerhouse, sluice gates, and ship locks.

2. **Sluice Gates (Filling & Emptying Gates)**
   - Large adjustable gates embedded in the barrage wall.
   - Operate in two phases:
     - **Flooding Phase:** Gates open at high tide → basin fills from the sea.
     - **Generation Phase:** Gates close → water is held. When sea level falls, gates on the turbine side open → water rushes out through turbines.
   - Typical sluice discharge: $Q_{sluice} = C_d \cdot A_g \cdot \sqrt{2gh}$

3. **Powerhouse (Turbine House)**
   - Reinforced concrete structure embedded in the barrage.
   - Houses the turbines, generators, control panels, and draft tubes.
   - Designed to withstand bidirectional (ebb + flood) flow.

### B. Electro-Mechanical Conversion Block
4. **Turbine**
   - **Low-head, high-flow** axial flow machines.
   - Three principal types used in TPPs:
     - **Bulb Turbine** (most common; generator enclosed in a watertight bulb in the water passage).
     - **Rim Turbine** (generator on the rim, no bulb obstruction).
     - **Straflo Turbine** (generator above the runner, in the flow path).

5. **Generator**
   - Usually a **synchronous generator** (to match grid frequency and supply reactive power).
   - Rated at 3-phase, 50 Hz, with step-up transformer at the output.
   - Capacity: typically **20 – 250 MW per unit** (e.g., La Rance: 240 MW).

### C. Hydraulic Block
6. **Draft Tube and Penstock Assembly**
   - **Penstock** carries water from the basin to the turbine inlet.
   - **Draft tube** discharges water back to the sea at low velocity (recovers kinetic + pressure head — the **velocity head recovery** concept).

### D. Environmental / Auxiliary Block
7. **Fish Pass / Fish Lift**
   - Allows migratory fish to bypass the barrage (ecological compliance).
8. **Navigation Lock**
   - Permits ship movement between sea and basin.
9. **Switchyard & Grid Interface**
   - Step-up transformers, circuit breakers, and grid synchronisation equipment.

---

## 2.2 Operational Modes of a Tidal Power Plant

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Most Repeated Question:** "Explain the working of a tidal power plant in single-effect and double-effect modes with neat diagrams."

### Mode 1 — Single-Effect (One-Way Generation)
- Generation occurs in **only one direction** of flow (either ebb-only OR flood-only).
- **Ebb-mode (most common):** Water trapped at high tide → released at low tide → generates power on the way out.
- **Flood-mode:** Water enters the basin at high tide → generates on the way in.
- 4 operational phases per tidal cycle:
  1. **Filling** (sluice open, turbine idle)
  2. **Holding** (sluice closed, awaiting head)
  3. **Generation** (turbine open, water flows out)
  4. **Waiting** (basin low, sea high — reset)

### Mode 2 — Double-Effect (Two-Way / Bidirectional Generation)
- Generation occurs in **both directions** — water is used to generate power whether it is flowing in OR out.
- Requires **reversible (bi-directional) bulb turbines** with adjustable blade pitch.
- Doubles the daily energy yield, but increases mechanical complexity and capital cost.

### Mode 3 — Double-Basin Scheme (Continuous Generation)
- Uses **two adjacent basins** at different levels.
- One basin fills at high tide, the other empties at low tide; turbines run **continuously** between the two basins.
- Provides nearly **24/7 power** (very few such plants exist — only conceptual/test installations).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Mantra:** Memorise formulas **(1) – (5)** below. They cover ~85% of numerical questions asked from this module.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Available **Potential Energy** in basin | $E_p = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho \, g \, A \, h^2$ | $\rho$ = density, $g$ = gravity, $A$ = basin area, $h$ = tidal range | Joules (J) |
| 2 | **Average Power** (over a half-cycle $T/2$) | $P_{avg} = \dfrac{E_p}{T/2} = \dfrac{\rho g A h^2}{T}$ | $T$ = tidal period (≈ 44,712 s for semi-diurnal) | Watts (W) |
| 3 | Turbine shaft power | $P_{turbine} = \rho g Q h \cdot \eta_t$ | $Q$ = discharge, $\eta_t$ = turbine efficiency | W |
| 4 | **Electrical output** (gross) | $P_{elec} = \rho g Q h \cdot \eta_t \cdot \eta_g$ | $\eta_g$ = generator efficiency | W |
| 5 | Kinetic (tidal-stream) power | $P_{stream} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^3 C_p$ | $v$ = stream velocity, $C_p$ = power coefficient (≤ 0.593, Betz) | W |
| 6 | Useful discharge through turbine | $Q = C_d A_t \sqrt{2gh}$ | $C_d$ = coefficient of discharge, $A_t$ = turbine area | m³/s |
| 7 | Sluice gate discharge | $Q_s = C_s A_s \sqrt{2gh}$ | $C_s$ ≈ 0.6, $A_s$ = sluice opening area | m³/s |
| 8 | Head available | $H = h_{basin} - h_{sea}$ | time-dependent difference | m |
| 9 | Annual energy | $E_{annual} = P_{avg} \times 8760 \times CF$ | $CF$ = capacity factor | kWh |

> [!TIP]
> **Critical board-exam substitution:** For a **semi-diurnal** tide, $T = 12.42 \text{ hours} = 44,712 \text{ s}$. For full cycle, double it.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Tidal plants serve three engineering purposes beyond electricity:
1. **Base-load renewable** — predictable twice-daily cycle (unlike solar/wind).
2. **Coastal protection** — barrage acts as a breakwater during storms.
3. **Multi-use infrastructure** — fish farming, tourism, road/rail crossings on barrage.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Confusing **tidal range** ($h$ = vertical difference between high and low tide) with **head available** ($H$ = instantaneous difference between basin and sea level). The head $H$ is **time-varying**, while $h$ is the peak design parameter.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Theoretical Energy Stored in a Tidal Basin

### Statement
Derive an expression for the total potential energy released by a tidal basin of surface area $A$ when the water level falls by a tidal range $h$ from high tide to low tide.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Consider an elemental water slab of thickness $dy$ at a height $y$ above the low-tide level.**

The mass of this elemental slab is:
$$
dm = \rho \cdot A \cdot dy
$$

**Step 2 — The potential energy of this slab (relative to the low-tide datum) is:**
$$
dE = dm \cdot g \cdot y = \rho \, g \, A \, y \, dy
$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from the low-tide level ($y = 0$) to the high-tide level ($y = h$):**
$$
E_p = \int_{0}^{h} \rho \, g \, A \, y \, dy
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integral (constant $\rho g A$ pulled out):**
$$
E_p = \rho \, g \, A \int_{0}^{h} y \, dy = \rho \, g \, A \left[ \dfrac{y^2}{2} \right]_{0}^{h}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the limits:**
$$
E_p = \rho \, g \, A \cdot \dfrac{h^2}{2}
$$

**Final Result:**
$$
\boxed{E_p = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho \, g \, A \, h^2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The energy scales with the **square** of the tidal range. Doubling $h$ gives **4×** the energy — this is why even a 1-m increase in tidal range at a site is commercially transformative.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Average Power Output Over a Half-Cycle

### Statement
Obtain the average theoretical power extractable from a single-basin, single-effect tidal plant over a half-tidal cycle of period $T/2$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Total energy released in one emptying phase (from high to low tide):**
$$
E_p = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho \, g \, A \, h^2
$$

**Step 2 — Time taken for this phase:**
$$
\Delta t = \dfrac{T}{2}
$$
where $T$ is the full tidal period (≈ 12.42 h for semi-diurnal).

**Step 3 — Average power definition:**
$$
P_{avg} = \dfrac{E_p}{\Delta t} = \dfrac{E_p}{T/2}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $E_p$:**
$$
P_{avg} = \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{2} \rho \, g \, A \, h^2}{T/2} = \dfrac{\rho \, g \, A \, h^2}{T}
$$

**Step 5 — Insert real-world efficiencies** (turbine + generator + civil losses):
$$
P_{net} = \eta_{overall} \cdot \dfrac{\rho \, g \, A \, h^2}{T}
$$

**Final Result:**
$$
\boxed{P_{net} = \eta_{overall} \cdot \dfrac{\rho \, g \, A \, h^2}{T}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Board Note:** If a question states "with efficiency $\eta$", always multiply the raw formula by $\eta$ and explicitly state the assumption: *"Assuming $\eta_{overall} = \eta_t \times \eta_g \times \eta_{mechanical}$"*.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style — 7 Mark)

**Question:** A tidal power plant has a basin area of $9 \text{ km}^2$ and an average tidal range of $7 \text{ m}$. The turbine-generator combined efficiency is $0.30$. The tidal period is $12.42 \text{ hours}$. Calculate:
- (a) The total potential energy per tidal cycle.
- (b) The average power output.
- (c) The annual energy production in kWh.

### Solution

**Given:**
- $A = 9 \text{ km}^2 = 9 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^2$
- $h = 7 \text{ m}$
- $\eta = 0.30$
- $T = 12.42 \text{ h} = 12.42 \times 3600 = 44,712 \text{ s}$
- $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Part (a) — Potential energy per half-cycle:**

$$
E_p = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho g A h^2
$$

$$
E_p = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times (9 \times 10^6) \times (7)^2
$$

$$
E_p = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 9 \times 10^6 \times 49
$$

Step-wise numerical evaluation:
$$
1025 \times 9.81 = 10,055.25
$$
$$
10,055.25 \times 9 \times 10^6 = 9.0497 \times 10^{10}
$$
$$
9.0497 \times 10^{10} \times 49 = 4.4344 \times 10^{12}
$$
$$
E_p = 0.5 \times 4.4344 \times 10^{12} = 2.2172 \times 10^{12} \text{ J}
$$

$$
\boxed{E_p \approx 2.22 \times 10^{12} \text{ J} = 2.22 \text{ TJ}}
$$

**Part (b) — Average power output:**

$$
P_{net} = \eta \cdot \dfrac{\rho g A h^2}{T} = \eta \cdot \dfrac{2 E_p}{T}
$$

$$
P_{net} = 0.30 \times \dfrac{2 \times 2.2172 \times 10^{12}}{44,712}
$$

$$
P_{net} = 0.30 \times \dfrac{4.4344 \times 10^{12}}{44,712} = 0.30 \times 9.916 \times 10^{7}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{net} \approx 2.97 \times 10^{7} \text{ W} \approx 29.7 \text{ MW}}
$$

**Part (c) — Annual energy in kWh:**

Energy per tidal cycle (2 high tides, 2 low tides = 2 useful generation phases per day; but conventionally 1 generation per 12.42 h):
$$
E_{per\,cycle} = P_{net} \times T_{cycle} = 2.97 \times 10^7 \text{ W} \times 12.42 \text{ h}
$$
$$
E_{per\,cycle} = 3.69 \times 10^8 \text{ Wh} = 3.69 \times 10^5 \text{ kWh}
$$

Number of cycles per year:
$$
N = \dfrac{8760 \text{ h}}{12.42 \text{ h}} \approx 705.3
$$

Annual energy:
$$
E_{annual} = 3.69 \times 10^5 \times 705.3 \approx 2.6 \times 10^8 \text{ kWh}
$$

$$
\boxed{E_{annual} \approx 260 \text{ GWh/year}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Bulb vs Rim vs Straflo Turbine (Board-Comparison Favourite)

| Feature | Bulb Turbine | Rim Turbine | Straflo Turbine |
|---|---|---|---|
| Generator location | Inside bulb in water passage | On rim, outside flow | In the flow passage above runner |
| Flow obstruction | Moderate (bulb) | Low | Higher |
| Maintenance access | Difficult (submerged) | Easy (outside) | Moderate |
| Cost | High | Moderate | Moderate |
| Cavitation risk | Low | Moderate | Low |
| Best for | Large heads, big flow | Medium flow | Compact installations |
| Used in | La Rance (France) | Annapolis (Canada) | Experimental |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Tidal Barrage Power Plant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[SEA TIDE\nRising and falling water] --> B[SLUICE GATES\nFilling emptying control]
    B --> C[TIDAL BASIN\nReservoir high tide water]
    C --> D[PENSTOCK\nHigh pressure water conduit]
    D --> E[TURBINE\nBulb Rim Straflo low head]
    E --> F[DRAFT TUBE\nVelocity head recovery]
    F --> G[SEA\nDischarge back to ocean]
    E --> H[GENERATOR\nSynchronous AC 50 Hz]
    H --> I[STEP UP TRANSFORMER\nVoltage step up]
    I --> J[SWITCHYARD\nCircuit breakers]
    J --> K[GRID\nTransmission line]
    L[CONTROL ROOM\nSCADA and operation] -.controls.-> B
    L -.controls.-> E
    M[FISH PASS\nEcological migration] -.parallel.-> B
    N[NAVIGATION LOCK\nShip transit] -.parallel.-> B
```

## 4.2 Operational Sequence Flowchart — Single-Effect Ebb-Mode Generation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Stage 1 FLOODING\nHigh tide approaching\nSluice gates open\nBasin fills with seawater] --> S2[Stage 2 HOLDING\nHigh tide reached\nSluice gates close\nBasin traps water]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3 GENERATION\nSea level falling\nTurbine gates open\nWater flows sea to basin]
    S3 --> S4[Stage 4 WAITING\nLow tide reached\nTurbines closed\nBasin at minimum level]
    S4 --> S5{Next tide cycle\nbegins in 6 hours}
    S5 --> S1
```

## 4.3 Cross-Sectional Schematic — Bulb Turbine in Tidal Barrage

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[BASIN SIDE\nHigh water level] --> P2[INTAKE\nTrash rack guides water]
    P2 --> P3[BULB TURBINE\nRunner with adjustable blades]
    P3 --> P4[DRAFT TUBE\nDiffuser recovers pressure]
    P4 --> P5[SEA SIDE\nLow water level]
    P3 --> P6[GUIDE VANE\nWicket gate controls flow]
    P6 --> P3
    P7[GENERATOR\nInside bulb sealed housing] -.mechanical shaft.-> P3
    P8[STEP UP TX\nAnd switchgear] --> P7
    P9[GRID\nPower evacuation]
    P8 --> P9
```

## 4.4 System-Level Energy Conversion Chain (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E1[GRAVITATIONAL\nPotential Energy\nE = mgh] --> E2[HYDRAULIC\nKinetic Energy\nE = 0.5 mv squared]
    E2 --> E3[MECHANICAL\nShaft Power\nP = Torque omega]
    E3 --> E4[ELECTRICAL\nAC Power\nP = sqrt 3 V I cos phi]
    E4 --> E5[GRID\nDelivered Energy\nE in kWh]
    T1[eta turbine 0.90] -.-> E2
    T2[eta generator 0.97] -.-> E3
    T3[eta transformer 0.99] -.-> E4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** Each stage has an efficiency multiplier. The product of all stage efficiencies gives the **overall plant efficiency** $\eta_{overall}$. This is why a real TPP delivers only **30–45%** of the raw tidal energy — every conversion step has losses.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Q: List the major components of a tidal power plant and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Tidal Barrage (Dam):** A barrier across an estuary that traps water at high tide and creates a head difference.
2. **Sluice Gates:** Adjustable gates that fill the basin at high tide and hold water for generation.
3. **Turbine (Bulb/Rim/Straflo):** Converts hydraulic energy of flowing water into mechanical shaft rotation.
4. **Generator:** Synchronous AC machine that converts mechanical rotation into 3-phase electrical energy.
5. **Transformer & Switchyard:** Steps up voltage for grid transmission and provides protection.
6. **Draft Tube & Penstock:** Guides water to/from the turbine and recovers kinetic energy at discharge.

> [!NOTE]
> Listing 4 components with functions = full 3 marks. State functions in **one crisp line** each.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Q: Differentiate between single-effect and double-effect tidal power generation schemes.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Single-Effect | Double-Effect |
|---|---|---|
| Generation direction | One-way only (ebb or flood) | Bi-directional (ebb + flood) |
| Energy per cycle | Lower (1 generation phase) | Higher (2 generation phases) |
| Turbine type | Unidirectional bulb | Reversible/pitch-regulated bulb |
| Capital cost | Lower | Higher (≈ 1.3–1.5×) |
| Operational complexity | Simple | Complex control system |
| Example | La Rance (initial) | La Rance (modern mode) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rule (Mandatory):** In Part B, students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B (Internal Choice). Both options are provided below for practice.

---

### 📘 QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]`

**Q: (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a tidal barrage power plant. List all major components and describe any **two** of them in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A tidal power plant has a basin area of $5 \text{ km}^2$ and a tidal range of $6 \text{ m}$. Calculate: (i) Total potential energy available per tidal cycle. (ii) Average power output if the combined efficiency is 0.35. (iii) Annual energy production in MWh. Take $T = 12.42 \text{ h}$, $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Working Principle:**
A tidal barrage power plant exploits the **periodic difference in water level** between the sea and an enclosed coastal basin. The operational sequence in **ebb-generation (single-effect) mode** is:
1. **Flooding:** As the tide rises, sluice gates are opened; water enters the basin from the sea.
2. **Holding:** At high tide, sluice gates close; the basin is filled to peak level.
3. **Generation:** As the sea level falls below the basin level, a head difference $H$ develops. Turbine inlet valves open; water flows from the basin to the sea through the turbine, producing electricity.
4. **Waiting:** At low tide, the cycle resets.

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1)

**Detailed description of two components:**

**1. Tidal Barrage (Dam):**
- A massive civil structure (concrete, rock-fill) built across a tidal estuary.
- Houses **sluice gates, powerhouse, navigation lock, and fish pass**.
- Designed to withstand bidirectional hydrostatic pressure.
- Typical length: 0.5 – 5 km; construction cost is the **single largest investment** in a TPP.

**2. Bulb Turbine:**
- An **axial-flow, low-head, high-discharge** machine.
- The generator is enclosed in a **streamlined, watertight bulb** placed directly in the water passage.
- Runner has **4–5 adjustable blades** (Kaplan-type) for efficiency at varying heads.
- Operates bi-directionally in double-effect mode.
- Efficiency: 88–92%.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $A = 5 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^2$, $h = 6 \text{ m}$, $\eta = 0.35$, $T = 12.42 \text{ h} = 44,712 \text{ s}$, $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

**(i) Potential energy per cycle:**

$$
E_p = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho g A h^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times (5 \times 10^6) \times 36
$$

Stepwise:
$$
1025 \times 9.81 = 10,055.25
$$
$$
10,055.25 \times 5 \times 10^6 = 5.0276 \times 10^{10}
$$
$$
5.0276 \times 10^{10} \times 36 = 1.8099 \times 10^{12}
$$
$$
E_p = 0.5 \times 1.8099 \times 10^{12} = 9.05 \times 10^{11} \text{ J}
$$

$$
\boxed{E_p \approx 9.05 \times 10^{11} \text{ J} \approx 905 \text{ GJ}}
$$

**(ii) Average power output:**

$$
P_{net} = \eta \cdot \dfrac{\rho g A h^2}{T} = \eta \cdot \dfrac{2 E_p}{T}
$$

$$
P_{net} = 0.35 \times \dfrac{2 \times 9.05 \times 10^{11}}{44,712}
$$

$$
P_{net} = 0.35 \times \dfrac{1.81 \times 10^{12}}{44,712} = 0.35 \times 4.048 \times 10^7
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{net} \approx 1.42 \times 10^7 \text{ W} \approx 14.2 \text{ MW}}
$$

**(iii) Annual energy production in MWh:**

$$
E_{annual} = P_{net} \times 8760 \text{ h} = 14.2 \text{ MW} \times 8760 \text{ h}
$$

$$
E_{annual} = 124,392 \text{ MWh} \approx 1.24 \times 10^5 \text{ MWh}
$$

$$
\boxed{E_{annual} \approx 1.24 \times 10^5 \text{ MWh/year}}
$$

---

### 📗 QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the **single-effect** and **double-effect** modes of tidal power generation. Compare their energy output and operational complexity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a block diagram, describe the **tidal stream (tidal current) power plant**. How does it differ from a tidal barrage plant? Derive the expression for power extracted. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Single-Effect Mode:**
- Generation occurs in **one direction only** — typically during the **ebb tide** (water flowing out of basin).
- 4 phases: Flooding → Holding → Generation → Waiting.
- Energy per cycle: $E_{single} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho g A h^2 \times \eta$.
- **Advantage:** Simple control, lower cost. **Disadvantage:** Power available only for ~6 h per cycle.

**Double-Effect Mode:**
- Generation occurs in **both directions** — ebb AND flood.
- Requires **bi-directional turbines** with reversible runner blades.
- Total energy per cycle: $E_{double} \approx 2 \times E_{single}$ (theoretical upper limit).
- **Advantage:** Nearly doubles energy yield, shorter idle periods. **Disadvantage:** Higher turbine cost, complex pitch control, higher maintenance.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Single-Effect | Double-Effect |
|---|---|---|
| Phases per cycle | 1 generation | 2 generations |
| Energy yield | $E$ (base) | $\approx 1.6E - 1.9E$ |
| Turbine complexity | Simple | High (pitch + reversible) |
| Civil cost | Same | Same |
| Control complexity | Low | High (SCADA-based) |
| Example | Early La Rance | Modern La Rance |

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Tidal Stream Power Plant:**
- A **dam-less** tidal plant that extracts **kinetic energy** from fast-flowing tidal currents.
- Uses **underwater rotors/turbines** (similar to wind turbines) anchored to the seabed.
- Examples: SeaGen (N. Ireland), MeyGen (Scotland).

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to Mermaid block — same chain as Section 4.4 but starting from $E = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$)

**Derivation of extracted power:**

Consider a stream of velocity $v$ and cross-sectional area $A$ intercepted by the turbine.

**Step 1 — Mass flow rate through the swept area:**
$$
\dot{m} = \rho A v
$$

**Step 2 — Kinetic energy flux (power in the stream):**
$$
P_{stream} = \dfrac{1}{2} \dot{m} v^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^3
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the **Betz limit** — only a fraction $C_p$ of this can be extracted (max $C_p = 16/27 \approx 0.593$):**
$$
\boxed{P_{extracted} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^3 C_p}
$$

**Step 4 — Including mechanical + electrical efficiencies:**
$$
P_{elec} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^3 C_p \cdot \eta_t \cdot \eta_g
$$

**Differences from Tidal Barrage:**

| Aspect | Tidal Barrage | Tidal Stream |
|---|---|---|
| Energy form | Potential (head) | Kinetic (velocity) |
| Civil structure | Large dam required | None (open sea) |
| Environmental impact | High (estuary alteration) | Low (localised) |
| Capacity factor | 0.25 – 0.40 | 0.30 – 0.45 |
| Siting constraint | Tidal range ≥ 5 m | Stream velocity ≥ 2 m/s |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks on TPP questions (KTU 2024 pattern):**
>
> 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Writing $E_p$ in Joules when the question asks in kWh. Conversion: $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \times 10^6 \text{ J}$.
> 2. **Forgetting the efficiency factor:** Always include $\eta_{overall}$ in the final answer unless the question explicitly says "theoretical". A bare formula without $\eta$ loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing $T$ and $T/2$:** Full tidal period $T = 12.42$ h; **generation phase** = $T/2 = 6.21$ h. Many students use 24 h or 6 h — both are wrong.
> 4. **Wrong $\rho$ value:** Use $\rho_{seawater} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (NOT 1000). Examiners specifically test this.
> 5. **Diagram deficiency:** Not labelling **bidirectional flow arrows** in the basin during single/double-effect mode loses 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Skipping the basin-area datum:** When computing energy, the basin area $A$ is constant ONLY if the basin has vertical walls. For natural basins, $A$ varies with $h$ — this is rarely asked, but if mentioned in a question, do not ignore it.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute revision? Read ONLY this section.** Covers 90% of exam questions from "Components of Tidal Power Plant".

- ✅ A **Tidal Power Plant** converts the **gravitational potential energy** of seawater into electrical energy using a **barrage + low-head turbine + generator**.
- ✅ **Six major components** of a TPP: **Barrage, Sluice Gates, Powerhouse (Turbine), Generator, Draft Tube, Switchyard/Grid**.
- ✅ **Auxiliary components:** **Fish pass, Navigation lock, Control room (SCADA)**.
- ✅ **Three architectural variants:** **Tidal Barrage, Tidal Stream, Tidal Lagoon**.
- ✅ **Operating modes:** **Single-effect (one-way)**, **Double-effect (bi-directional)**, **Double-basin (continuous)**.
- ✅ **Three turbine types:** **Bulb** (most common), **Rim** (easy maintenance), **Straflo** (compact).
- ✅ **Master Formula:** $E_p = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho g A h^2$ — energy scales with **$h^2$**.
- ✅ **Average Power Formula:** $P_{avg} = \eta \cdot \dfrac{\rho g A h^2}{T}$ with $T = 12.42 \text{ h}$ for semi-diurnal cycle.
- ✅ **Tidal stream power:** $P = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^3 C_p$ (Betz limit: $C_p \leq 0.593$).
- ✅ **Minimum viable tidal range:** $h \geq 5 \text{ m}$ for economic operation.
- ✅ **Standard values to memorise:** $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $T = 12.42 \text{ h} = 44,712 \text{ s}$.
- ✅ **Typical overall efficiency:** $\eta_{overall} = 0.30 - 0.45$ (because of $\eta_t \times \eta_g \times$ civil losses).
- ✅ **Key advantage:** Predictable, twice-daily renewable (good for grid stability).
- ✅ **Key disadvantages:** High capital cost, environmental impact on estuaries, intermittent within each 6-hour window.
- ✅ **Reference plants:** **La Rance (France, 240 MW)** — world's first large TPP; **Sihwa Lake (South Korea, 254 MW)** — current largest.
- ✅ **Capacity factor reminder:** A tidal plant with $P_{rated} = 100 \text{ MW}$ actually delivers $\approx 30 \text{ MW}$ on average — keep this in mind for energy calculations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Tidal Power Plants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET742 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES AND RENEWABLE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Classification of Tidal Power Plants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tidal Power Plants** are a class of renewable energy conversion systems that harness the **kinetic and potential energy** of ocean tides — caused by the periodic gravitational interaction between the **Earth, Moon, and Sun** — and convert it into **electrical energy** using hydraulic turbines coupled to electrical generators.

The classification of tidal power plants is based on the **method of energy capture** and the **physical configuration** used to extract the predictable rise and fall of seawater. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, tidal power plants are broadly classified into four major categories based on the engineering principle employed to intercept the tidal energy flux.

### 1.2 The Four Main Classifications

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4 — Core Classification Taxonomy:**
> 1. **Tidal Barrage Power Plants** (Barrier / Dam-type)
> 2. **Tidal Stream (Current) Power Plants** (Kinetic / Free-flow)
> 3. **Tidal Lagoon Power Plants** (Enclosed coastal impoundments)
> 4. **Dynamic Tidal Power (DTP) Plants** (Phase-differential coastal dams)

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **💡 Plain-English Intuition (The "Water Wheel" Analogy):**
> Imagine the ocean as a giant water tank that **rises and falls twice a day** (like a bathroom float tank). Engineers have figured out clever ways to "catch" this moving water and spin turbines:
> - **Tidal Barrage** = Building a wall across a river mouth and installing water wheels in the wall — like a hydroelectric dam, but the "river" is the tide.
> - **Tidal Stream** = Placing underwater windmills directly in fast tidal currents — the ocean's "underwater wind."
> - **Tidal Lagoon** = Building a coastal swimming-pool-like structure connected to the sea, then letting water spin turbines as it flows in and out.
> - **Dynamic Tidal Power** = A long coastal wall that exploits the natural phase difference of tides along its length — like building a staircase where water naturally falls from one step to the next.

### 1.4 Why Classification Matters in Engineering

Each class has distinct:
- **Capital cost** profiles
- **Site suitability** constraints
- **Turbine technology** (bulb, rim, Savonius, Gorlov, vertical-axis)
- **Grid integration** challenges
- **Environmental impact** footprint

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Tidal Metrics Used in This Module:**
> - **Tidal Range (h):** typically **2 m – 10 m** at prospective sites.
> - **Basin Area (A):** measured in **km²**.
> - **Seawater Density (ρ):** **1025 kg/m³** (standard value, not 1000).
> - **Gravitational Acceleration (g):** **9.81 m/s²**.
> - **Tidal Cycle Period:** semi-diurnal ≈ **12 h 25 min**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification Tree — Detailed Logic

#### **Class 1: Tidal Barrage Power Plants**

A **tidal barrage** is a dam-like barrier constructed across a tidal estuary or bay, fitted with **low-head hydraulic turbines**, **sluice gates**, and **ship locks**.

**Sub-classification of Tidal Barrages:**

**(a) Single-Basin Single-Effect (Ebb Generation):**
- Water flows in freely through sluice gates during **flood tide** (high tide filling).
- At high tide, sluice gates close, creating a **head difference**.
- Water is released through turbines during **ebb tide** (outgoing), generating power in **one direction only** (~4–5 h/day).

**(b) Single-Basin Double-Effect (Ebb + Flood Generation):**
- Turbines are **reversible** (pump-turbines).
- Power is generated **both during flood and ebb** tides.
- Operational time increases to **~8–10 h/day**, but with lower heads in each phase.

**(c) Double-Basin (Linked Basin / Two-Pool):**
- Two interconnected basins with **slightly offset filling/emptying cycles**.
- **Continuous power generation** is possible (~24 h coverage), as one basin always has a usable head.
- Higher capital cost due to two dams, two sets of sluices.

**(d) Multi-Basin / Hybrid Barrage Systems:**
- Three or more basins with **staggered operating phases**.
- Maximum continuous output, very high civil cost.

#### **Class 2: Tidal Stream (Current) Power Plants**

No barrage is built. Instead, **submerged turbines** are mounted on the seabed in regions of high tidal current velocity (typically **> 2 m/s**). Equivalent to underwater wind turbines.

**Turbine Topologies Used:**
- **Horizontal-axis (HAWT)** — three-bladed, pitch-controlled (most common).
- **Vertical-axis (Gorlov Helical, Savonius)** — omnidirectional, no yaw mechanism.
- **Cross-flow / Darrieus-type** — suitable for bi-directional tidal flows.

**Site Examples:** Strangford Lough (UK), MeyGen (Scotland), OpenHydro (France).

#### **Class 3: Tidal Lagoon Power Plants**

A **tidal lagoon** is an **artificial enclosure built along a coastline** (not across an estuary) using **rockfill embankments** or **concrete retaining walls**. Turbines are housed in the embankment wall. The enclosed area acts as the "basin."

**Advantages over Barrages:**
- No need to dam an estuary → **less ecological disruption** (fish migration, sediment flow preserved).
- Flexible siting on open coastlines.
- Easier regulatory approval.

**Example:** **Swansea Bay Tidal Lagoon** (proposed 320 MW, UK) — world's first full-scale.

#### **Class 4: Dynamic Tidal Power (DTP)**

A theoretical concept (proposed by **Kees Hulsbergen, 1997**). A very long dam (~50–100 km) is built **perpendicular to the coast**, extending into deep water. It exploits the **natural phase difference of the tidal wave** along the dam's length, creating a continuous head difference on one side of the dam.

- Continuous generation is theoretically possible.
- Civil cost is the **primary showstopper** — no commercial DTP exists yet.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **#** | **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Applicable To** |
|------|---------------|-------------|-----------|-------------------|
| 1 | Available potential energy in a basin | $E = \frac{1}{2} \rho g A h^{2}$ | Joules | Barrages, Lagoons |
| 2 | Average tidal power (single-basin, ebb) | $P_{avg} = 0.225 \times A^{2} \times h^{2}$ | MW (if $A$ in km², $h$ in m) | Single-basin barrage |
| 3 | Maximum instantaneous power (single basin) | $P_{max} = \frac{1}{2} \rho g A h^{2} / T$ | W | Single-basin barrage |
| 4 | Tidal stream kinetic power (per unit area) | $P/A = \frac{1}{2} \rho v^{3}$ | W/m² | Tidal stream |
| 5 | Tidal stream turbine shaft power | $P_{turb} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A_{rot} C_{p} v^{3}$ | W | Tidal stream |
| 6 | Head developed across barrage | $H = h \cdot \sin(\omega t)$ | m | Barrage, Lagoon |
| 7 | Turbine discharge (under head H) | $Q = C_{d} A_{turb} \sqrt{2gH}$ | m³/s | All |
| 8 | Tubular turbine power | $P = \eta \rho g Q H_{net}$ | W | All |
| 9 | Tidal cycle period (semi-diurnal) | $T = 12.42$ | hours | All |
| 10 | Power coefficient (Betz limit for cross-flow) | $C_{p,max} = 16/27 \approx 0.593$ | dimensionless | Tidal stream |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Legend of Symbols:**
> $\rho$ = seawater density (**1025 kg/m³**), $g$ = 9.81 m/s², $A$ = basin/rotor area, $h$ = tidal range, $v$ = current velocity, $C_p$ = power coefficient, $C_d$ = discharge coefficient, $\eta$ = turbine-generator efficiency, $T$ = tidal period, $\omega$ = tidal angular frequency.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **UK (Sihwa Lake, South Korea — 254 MW)** is the world's largest operational tidal barrage.
- **France (La Rance — 240 MW)** is the oldest commercial tidal barrage (1966).
- **Scotland (MeyGen — 6 MW, expandable to 86 MW)** is the world's largest tidal stream array.
- Tidal is **highly predictable** (centuries in advance) — unlike solar/wind.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Available Tidal Energy in a Basin (Single-Basin Barrage)

**Statement:** Derive the expression for the **total potential energy** stored in a single-basin tidal system of area $A$ and tidal range $h$.

**Step 1 — Identify the energy element.**
Consider a thin horizontal slice of water of thickness $dz$ at height $z$ above the mean tide level. The volume of this slice is:

$$dV = A \, dz$$

**Step 2 — Compute the potential energy of the slice.**
The potential energy of a mass $dm$ raised to height $z$ is $dm \cdot g \cdot z$. With $dm = \rho \, dV = \rho A \, dz$:

$$dE = g z \, \rho A \, dz = \rho g A z \, dz$$

**Step 3 — Integrate over the tidal range (from 0 to h).**
The total energy above mean tide level is obtained by integrating $z$ from $0$ to $h$:

$$E = \int_{0}^{h} \rho g A z \, dz$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integral.**

$$E = \rho g A \left[ \frac{z^{2}}{2} \right]_{0}^{h} = \rho g A \cdot \frac{h^{2}}{2}$$

$$\boxed{E = \frac{1}{2} \rho g A h^{2}}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**
The energy scales as the **square of the tidal range**. Doubling the tidal range quadruples the stored energy. This is why sites with $h \geq 5$ m are economically preferred (e.g., Bristol Channel, Bay of Fundy, Sihwa Lake).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Average Tidal Power (Single-Basin, Ebb-Generation)

**Statement:** Derive the empirical power formula $P_{avg} = 0.225 \cdot A^{2} \cdot h^{2}$ (MW) used by KTU examiners.

**Step 1 — Substitute standard values into the energy expression.**
